| **Interrupt Latency** | Deterministic, typically **12 clock cycles** (M0/M0+) / **6 cycles** (M3/M4) |
| **Pipeline Depth** | 2-stage (M0/M0+), 3-stage (M3/M4), 6-stage (M7) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU:** Whenever asked to "list features of Cortex-M," always mention: **Thumb-2 ISA**, **NVIC (Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller)**, **WFI/WFE low-power modes**, **deterministic interrupt response**, and **bit-banding** (in M3/M4).

### 1.4 Evolution from Classic ARM to Cortex-M

The classic **ARM7TDMI** (used in early NXP LPC2000, Samsung S3C44B0) had a **3-stage pipeline** and used the older **ARM (32-bit) + Thumb (16-bit)** dual instruction set, requiring explicit state-switching via the `BX` instruction. The Cortex-M family unified this into a single **Thumb-2** ISA, eliminating mode switching overhead and simplifying compiler design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Classification of ARM Cortex-M Cores

The Cortex-M family is hierarchically divided into three performance/power tiers:

#### **Tier 1 — Ultra-Low-Power (8/16-bit replacement)**
- **Cortex-M0** — Smallest, cheapest, 2-stage pipeline, ARMv6-M.
- **Cortex-M0+** — Same as M0 but with single-cycle I/O port and optional MPU. Power-optimized.
- **Cortex-M1** — Designed for **FPGA** implementation (ASIC prototyping).

#### **Tier 2 — Mainstream / General-Purpose**
- **Cortex-M3** — ARMv7-M, 3-stage pipeline, hardware divide, full NVIC, bit-banding. **Workhorse of STM32F1/F0/LPC1768.**
- **Cortex-M23** — ARMv8-M Baseline, M0+ successor with TrustZone-M security extension.

#### **Tier 3 — High-Performance / DSP**
- **Cortex-M4** — Adds **DSP extensions** and **single-precision FPU**.
- **Cortex-M33** — ARMv8-M Mainline, optional FPU, TrustZone-M, MPU.
- **Cortex-M7** — 6-stage superscalar pipeline, dual-issue, **double-precision FPU**, **64-bit ICache/DCaches**, TCM (Tightly-Coupled Memory).
- **Cortex-M55 / M85** — Latest, with **Helium (M-Profile Vector Extensions - MVE)** for ML workloads.

### 2.2 Core Microarchitectural Features (Why it Matters)

1. **Harvard vs Von Neumann:**
   - **M0, M0+, M1, M23** → Von Neumann (single unified bus for I/D).
   - **M3, M4, M7, M33, M55** → Modified Harvard (separate I/D buses with unified memory map).

2. **Thumb-2 Instruction Set:**
   - Provides **16-bit** instructions for common operations (high code density) and **32-bit** instructions for complex operations (high performance).
   - Eliminates the need for ARM/Thumb state switching — single ISA.
   - ~25% smaller code size compared to classic ARM.

3. **NVIC (Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller):**
   - **Integrated** into the core (not external).
   - Supports up to **240 physical interrupts** and **256 interrupt priority levels** (M3/M4/M7).
   - **Tail-chaining** reduces interrupt latency to just **6 cycles** when back-to-back IRQs occur.
   - **Late arrival** and **preemption** mechanisms.

4. **Bit-Banding (M3/M4/M7):**
   - Maps a **1-MB alias region** to a **32-MB bit-band region**.
   - Atomic single-bit read-modify-write — no read-modify-write race conditions.
   - **Formula:** `bit_word_offset = (bit_band_base + (byte_offset × 32) + (bit_number × 4))`

5. **Wakeup Interrupt Controller (WIC):**
   - Allows the core to be powered down entirely while NVIC retains interrupt state.
   - WIC signals wakeup to power management IC on pending IRQ.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Specification | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **Bit-band alias address** | `alias_addr = bit_band_alias_base + (byte_offset × 32) + (bit_number × 4)` | Atomic single-bit GPIO manipulation |
| **Clock cycles per instruction (CPI)** | M0: ~1.62 avg, M3/M4: ~1.25 avg, M7: ~1.10 avg | Performance benchmarking |
| **CoreMark/MHz** | M0: **1.62**, M3: **3.32**, M4: **3.42**, M7: **5.29** | Dhrystone-style embedded benchmark |
| **Interrupt latency** | M0: 16 cycles, M3/M4: 12 cycles, M7: 12 cycles | Real-time control loop design |
| **Power (active)** | M0+: **9 µA/MHz @ 32 MHz**, M4: **200 µA/MHz @ 168 MHz** | Battery-life calculation |
| **Pipeline stages** | M0: 2, M3/M4: 3, M7: 6 | Branch-penalty estimation |
| **DMIPS/MHz** | M0: 0.87, M3: 1.25, M4: 1.25, M7: 2.14 | Throughput in Dhrystone MIPS |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL EXAM TIP:** The `| bit |` in mathematical expressions must be written using `\vert bit \vert` or `\mid bit \mid` — never as a bare pipe — to avoid breaking the markdown table renderer.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Typical Cortex-M Variant |
|---|---|---|
| IoT Sensor Nodes | Battery-powered telemetry, BLE beacons | M0+ / M23 |
| Automotive ECUs | Body control, sensor fusion | M4F (with DSP+Float) |
| Motor Control / Drones | FOC algorithms, real-time control | M4F / M7 |
| Wearables | Fitness trackers, health monitors | M33 (with TrustZone) |
| Industrial PLCs | Hard real-time deterministic control | M3 / M4 |
| Edge ML / TinyML | Keyword spotting, anomaly detection | M55 / M85 (Helium MVE) |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Bit-Band Alias Address Calculation

**Problem Statement:** Given the SRAM base address of a peripheral register `0x40000000` (the GPIOF data register on an STM32-like device), derive the bit-band alias address to atomically set **bit 7** of this register.

**Given:**
- `bit_band_base` (SRAM bit-band region start) = `0x20000000`
- `bit_band_alias` (SRAM bit-band alias start) = `0x22000000`
- Target byte offset = `0x40000000 - 0x40000000` region mapping; for peripherals:
  - `peripheral_base` = `0x40000000`
  - `peripheral_bitband_base` = `0x40000000`
  - `peripheral_bitband_alias` = `0x42000000`
- Target byte address = `0x400FF000` (say, GPIO_PORTA data register)
- Bit number = `7`

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the bit-band region the byte belongs to.
The byte address `0x400FF000` lies in the **peripheral bit-band region** (`0x40000000` to `0x400FFFFF`).

**Step 2:** Compute the byte offset within the bit-band region.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{byte\_offset} &= \text{target\_byte\_addr} - \text{peripheral\_bitband\_base} \\
&= 0x400FF000 - 0x40000000 \\
&= 0x000FF000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the bit-band alias formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{alias\_addr} &= \text{peripheral\_bitband\_alias} \\
&\quad + (\text{byte\_offset} \times 32) + (\text{bit\_number} \times 4) \\
&= 0x42000000 + (0x000FF000 \times 0x20) + (7 \times 0x04) \\
&= 0x42000000 + 0x001FE000 + 0x0000001C \\
&= 0x421FE01C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Verification of the address range.
The alias region `0x42000000` to `0x43FFFFFF` spans **32 MB**, which maps **1 MB of bit-band region × 8 bits/byte = 32 MB alias space**. The computed address `0x421FE01C` lies in this range. **Valid.**

**Engineering Interpretation:** Writing `1` to address `0x421FE01C` atomically sets bit 7 of `0x400FF000` without the classic **read-modify-write hazard** in a multithreaded ISR environment.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Interrupt Latency under Tail-Chaining

**Given:** Cortex-M4 core running at **168 MHz**. Two IRQs (IRQ_A, IRQ_B) arrive back-to-back without overlap.

**Step 1:** Base interrupt entry latency (stacking + vector fetch) = **12 clock cycles**.

**Step 2:** For back-to-back IRQs (tail-chain case), pop of IRQ_A + push of IRQ_B = **6 clock cycles** (no full unstacking).

**Step 3:** Compute total latency for the second interrupt.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{latency}} &= \frac{6 \text{ cycles}}{f_{\text{clk}}} \\
&= \frac{6}{168 \times 10^6} \text{ seconds} \\
&= 35.71 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to microseconds for embedded control-loop budgets.

$$
t_{\text{latency}} \approx 0.0357\ \mu\text{s}
$$

This is the deterministic guarantee that makes Cortex-M ideal for **hard real-time** applications like **motor FOC loops** running at **20 kHz** (50 µs period) — a tail-chained ISR has ample headroom.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cortex-M Family Comparison

| Parameter | M0 | M0+ | M3 | M4 | M7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | ARMv6-M | ARMv6-M | ARMv7-M | ARMv7-M | ARMv7-M |
| **Pipeline stages** | 2 | 2 | 3 | 3 | 6 (dual-issue) |
| **Thumb-2** | Subset | Subset | Full | Full | Full |
| **FPU** | No | No | No | Single (SP) | Single + Double |
| **DSP extensions** | No | No | No | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **Bit-banding** | No | No | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **MPU** | No | Optional | 8-region | 8-region | 8-region |
| **ICache / DCache** | No | No | No | No | **Yes (64 KB ea.)** |
| **TCM (ITCM/DTCM)** | No | No | No | No | **Yes (up to 16 MB)** |
| **CoreMark/MHz** | 1.62 | 1.92 | 3.32 | 3.42 | 5.29 |
| **Typical freq.** | 50 MHz | 50 MHz | 100 MHz | 180 MHz | 400 MHz |
| **Use case** | 8/16-bit replacement | Low-power IoT | Mainstream MCU | DSP / motor ctrl | High-perf DSP / ML |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick Question Defense:** If asked "Which Cortex-M supports bit-banding?" the answer is **M3, M4, M7** — *not* M0/M0+. M23 also supports it under ARMv8-M Baseline.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cortex-M3/M4 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPUCORE["ARM CORTEX M CORE 32 BIT"]
    THUMB2["THUMB 2 INSTRUCTION DECODER"]
    NVIC["NVIC NESTED VECTORED INTERRUPT CONTROLLER"]
    WIC["WIC WAKEUP INTERRUPT CONTROLLER"]
    FPU["FPU SINGLE PRECISION OPTIONAL M4"]
    DSP["DSP EXTENSIONS OPTIONAL M4"]
    BITBAND["BIT BAND ALIAS LOGIC"]
    MPU["MEMORY PROTECTION UNIT MPU"]
    IBUS["I CODE BUS 32 BIT"]
    DBUS["D CODE BUS 32 BIT"]
    SBOOT["SYSTEM BUS"]
    AHB["AHB LITE BUS MATRIX"]
    FLASH["INTERNAL FLASH MEMORY"]
    SRAM["INTERNAL SRAM"]
    PERIPH["PERIPHERALS GPIO ADC UART TIMER I2C SPI"]

    CPUCORE --> THUMB2
    CPUCORE --> NVIC
    CPUCORE --> BITBAND
    CPUCORE --> MPU
    THUMB2 --> IBUS
    THUMB2 --> DBUS
    IBUS --> AHB
    DBUS --> AHB
    SBOOT --> AHB
    NVIC --> WIC
    AHB --> FLASH
    AHB --> SRAM
    AHB --> PERIPH
    FPU --> CPUCORE
    DSP --> CPUCORE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The solid arrows show the data path. The **I-bus** fetches instructions from Flash, the **D-bus** performs data loads/stores, and the **System bus** accesses peripherals and SRAM. The **NVIC** sits beside the core, not behind it — this is what gives Cortex-M its deterministic interrupt response.

---

### 4.2 Cortex-M Family Taxonomy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ARMCM["ARM CORTEX M FAMILY"]
    ULP["ULTRA LOW POWER TIER"]
    MAINSTREAM["MAINSTREAM TIER"]
    HPERF["HIGH PERFORMANCE TIER"]

    ARMCM --> ULP
    ARMCM --> MAINSTREAM
    ARMCM --> HPERF

    ULP --> M0["CORTEX M0"]
    ULP --> M0P["CORTEX M0 PLUS"]
    ULP --> M1["CORTEX M1 FPGA"]
    ULP --> M23["CORTEX M23 TRUSTZONE M"]

    MAINSTREAM --> M3["CORTEX M3"]
    MAINSTREAM --> M33["CORTEX M33"]

    HPERF --> M4["CORTEX M4 DSP FPU"]
    HPERF --> M7["CORTEX M7 DUAL CACHE FPU"]
    HPERF --> M55["CORTEX M55 HELIUM MVE"]
    HPERF --> M85["CORTEX M85 ML EDGE"]
```

---

### 4.3 Interrupt Handling Flow in NVIC (Tail-Chaining Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    STAGE1["IRQ A ARRIVES"]
    STAGE2["STACK PSR PC LR R12 R3 R0"]
    STAGE3["VECTOR FETCH FOR IRQ A ISR"]
    STAGE4["EXECUTE IRQ A HANDLER"]
    STAGE5["IRQ B PENDING DURING IRQ A"]
    STAGE6["TAIL CHAIN 6 CYCLES POP PUSH"]
    STAGE7["VECTOR FETCH FOR IRQ B ISR"]
    STAGE8["EXECUTE IRQ B HANDLER"]
    STAGE9["RETURN FROM IRQ B"]
    STAGE10["RESTORE STACK OF IRQ A"]
    STAGE11["RETURN FROM IRQ A TO MAIN"]

    STAGE1 --> STAGE2
    STAGE2 --> STAGE3
    STAGE3 --> STAGE4
    STAGE4 --> STAGE5
    STAGE5 --> STAGE6
    STAGE6 --> STAGE7
    STAGE7 --> STAGE8
    STAGE8 --> STAGE9
    STAGE9 --> STAGE10
    STAGE10 --> STAGE11
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tail-chaining insight:** Notice the **6-cycle shortcut** between Stage 5 and Stage 7. Without tail-chaining, IRQ B would have to wait for IRQ A to fully unstack (12 cycles), vector fetch, and then restack. The 50% latency reduction is why Cortex-M is preferred over older 8-bit MCUs in hard real-time systems.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any three key features that distinguish the ARM Cortex-M3 processor core from a classic ARM7TDMI core.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Unified Thumb-2 ISA:** Cortex-M3 executes Thumb-2 instructions only, eliminating the ARM/Thumb state-switching overhead present in ARM7TDMI's `BX`-based dual-ISA model. This reduces code size by ~25% and simplifies compiler design.

2. **Integrated NVIC:** The Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller is built into the Cortex-M3 core, providing deterministic 12-cycle interrupt entry with hardware-assisted **tail-chaining** and **late-arrival** mechanisms. ARM7TDMI required an external VIC.

3. **Bit-Banding:** Cortex-M3 supports bit-band aliasing in both the SRAM and peripheral regions, enabling atomic single-bit manipulation. ARM7TDMI has no such hardware primitive, requiring software-based read-modify-write sequences.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "Cortex-M3 has Thumb-2" with "Cortex-M3 has ARM + Thumb." The correct statement is **Thumb-2 only** — no 32-bit ARM mode in Cortex-M.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of the Wakeup Interrupt Controller (WIC) in low-power ARM Cortex-M designs? Why is it separate from the NVIC?*

**Model Answer:**

The **WIC** is a small, always-on interrupt-monitoring block that remains active while the **main Cortex-M core, NVIC logic, and clock tree are powered down**. When a pending interrupt is detected, the WIC signals an external **power management unit (PMU)** to restart the core's voltage rail and clock source, allowing the CPU to resume execution.

It is **separate from the NVIC** because the NVIC's configuration registers, priority logic, and stack operation require clocked digital logic — which would otherwise leak power in sleep modes. The WIC uses minimal asynchronous logic that can detect a rising edge on any configured IRQ line at near-zero current, decoupling interrupt-awareness from active power consumption.

This is what enables Cortex-M0+ to achieve the **EnergyBench**-measured sub-µA deep-sleep currents in IoT sensor nodes.

---

### **PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of an ARM Cortex-M3 processor core**. Clearly label the bus interfaces and major functional units. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the architectural features of **Cortex-M0+, M3, and M7** across the following parameters: pipeline depth, instruction set support, FPU/DSP availability, bit-banding, and typical CoreMark/MHz. Mention one ideal application domain for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Internal Architecture of ARM Cortex-M3**

The Cortex-M3 core consists of the following functional units, interconnected via the AHB-Lite bus matrix:

1. **Processor Core (32-bit RISC datapath)** — executes Thumb-2 instructions.
2. **Instruction Decoder** — supports both 16-bit and 32-bit Thumb-2 opcodes.
3. **Register Bank** — low registers R0–R7 (general-purpose) and high registers R8–R12 (limited addressing).
4. **ALU** — performs arithmetic, logic, and shift operations; includes a **hardware 32-bit integer divider**.
5. **Multiplier** — single-cycle 32×32 → 32-bit multiply, with optional 64-bit accumulation.
6. **NVIC** — tightly coupled to the core; handles up to 240 IRQs with 256 priority levels and **tail-chaining**.
7. **MPU (Memory Protection Unit)** — up to 8 programmable regions for OS-level memory isolation.
8. **Bit-Band Logic** — aliasing unit for SRAM (`0x22000000`) and peripherals (`0x42000000`).
9. **Bus Interfaces:**
   - **I-Code bus** — instruction fetches from Flash.
   - **D-code bus** — data accesses.
   - **System bus** — peripheral and SRAM access.
10. **Sleep / WFI / WFE logic** — entry into low-power modes.

**Block Diagram Reference:** *Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the complete Mermaid architecture diagram.*

**(b) Comparative Table — M0+, M3, M7**

| Parameter | Cortex-M0+ | Cortex-M3 | Cortex-M7 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pipeline stages** | 2-stage | 3-stage | 6-stage (dual-issue superscalar) |
| **ISA support** | Thumb + subset Thumb-2 | Full Thumb-2 | Full Thumb-2 |
| **FPU** | None | None | Single + Double precision |
| **DSP extensions** | None | None | Yes (MAC, SIMD-light) |
| **Bit-banding** | Not supported | Supported | Supported |
| **MPU regions** | Optional 8 | 8 | 8 |
| **ICache / DCache** | None | None | 64 KB each |
| **TCM (ITCM/DTCM)** | None | None | Yes (deterministic) |
| **CoreMark/MHz** | **1.92** | **3.32** | **5.29** |
| **Typical max freq.** | 50 MHz | 100–180 MHz | 200–400 MHz |
| **Ideal Application** | Coin-cell IoT sensors, BLE beacons | Mainstream MCU: STM32F1, LPC1768 | High-end DSP, motor FOC, audio processing, edge ML |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write "Cortex-M0+ has 3-stage pipeline" — this is **WRONG**. The M0/M0+ has a **2-stage** pipeline (Fetch + Execute). The M3/M4 is 3-stage. Memorize the pipeline depth cold for the diagram question.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Thumb-2 instruction set architecture** used in ARM Cortex-M cores. How does it improve code density and performance compared to the classic ARM/Thumb dual-ISA model? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **bit-band alias address** for setting **bit 5** of the GPIO peripheral register located at address `0x40021800`, given that the peripheral bit-band base is `0x40000000` and the peripheral bit-band alias base is `0x42000000`. Also calculate the **interrupt tail-chain latency** in nanoseconds for a Cortex-M4 running at **180 MHz**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Thumb-2 Instruction Set Architecture**

The **Thumb-2 ISA** is a superset of the original 16-bit Thumb instruction set, augmented with selected 32-bit instructions. It was introduced by Arm Holdings in 2003 (ARMv6T2 architecture) and forms the *only* instruction set supported by the Cortex-M family (ARMv6-M, ARMv7-M, ARMv8-M).

**Key Characteristics:**

1. **Mixed-width encoding:** Instructions can be 16-bit (for common operations like MOV, ADD with small immediates, branches) or 32-bit (for complex operations like full-range MOV, conditional branches, literal pool loads). The decoder automatically identifies width from the first half-word.

2. **No mode switching:** Unlike ARM7TDMI, which required explicit switching between the 32-bit **ARM state** and 16-bit **Thumb state** via the `BX` instruction (and incurred a 3-cycle pipeline flush on every switch), Cortex-M executes Thumb-2 in a single state. **No BX, no mode bit, no flush penalty.**

3. **Improved code density:** The average Thumb-2 instruction size is ~25% smaller than equivalent ARM code, approaching the density of pure 16-bit Thumb while retaining 32-bit performance for DSP-like operations. Benchmarks show **~27% memory footprint reduction** vs ARM code, critical for Flash-constrained MCUs.

4. **Performance parity with ARM:** While pure ARM code is 32-bit and ~30% faster on raw compute, Thumb-2 closes the gap by allowing 32-bit encodings for performance-critical paths (e.g., `LDR.w`, `B.w` long branches, `MUL` with full-width operands). Net result: code density of Thumb with ~95% of ARM's performance.

5. **Free DSP instructions (M4/M7):** Thumb-2 extensions include hardware **SSAT, USAT, QADD, SMUL** etc., enabling single-cycle saturated arithmetic and 16×16→32 SIMD-style MAC operations essential for motor control and audio.

**Comparison Summary Table:**

| Feature | Classic ARM7TDMI (ARM+Thumb) | Cortex-M3/M4/M7 (Thumb-2) |
|---|---|---|
| States | ARM (32-bit) + Thumb (16-bit) | Single state |
| Mode switch | Explicit BX, 3-cycle flush | **None required** |
| Max instruction size | 32-bit | 32-bit |
| Min instruction size | 16-bit | 16-bit |
| Code density vs ARM | ~30% smaller (in Thumb) | ~25% smaller (Thumb-2) |
| Hardware divider | Optional | Yes (M3+) |
| Bit-banding | No | Yes (M3/M4/M7) |

**(b) Numerical Computation**

**Part 1 — Bit-Band Alias Address:**

Given: byte address = `0x40021800`, bit number = `5`, peripheral bit-band base = `0x40000000`, peripheral alias base = `0x42000000`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{byte\_offset} &= 0x40021800 - 0x40000000 \\
&= 0x00021800
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{alias\_addr} &= 0x42000000 + (0x00021800 \times 32) + (5 \times 4) \\
&= 0x42000000 + 0x00043000 + 0x00000014 \\
&= 0x42043014
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, writing `1` to memory address `0x42043014` will atomically set bit 5 of register `0x40021800`.

**Part 2 — Interrupt Tail-Chain Latency:**

Given: Cortex-M4 at 180 MHz, tail-chain overhead = 6 clock cycles.

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{latency}} &= \frac{6}{180 \times 10^6} \text{ s} \\
&= 33.33\ \text{ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, a pending second interrupt can be serviced within **33.33 ns** of the first ISR completing — making this ideal for hard real-time control loops in motor drives and power conversion.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently compute byte_offset incorrectly when the register is *not* at the very start of the peripheral bit-band region. Always compute `(target_addr − peripheral_bitband_base)` first, *then* multiply by 32. Doing the multiplication on the full address is a common 2-mark deduction.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **Cortex-M is a *core*, not a microcontroller** — it is licensed IP from Arm to silicon vendors.
- **Thumb-2 ISA only** — no ARM/Thumb mode switching in Cortex-M.
- **Pipeline depths:** M0/M0+ = 2, M3/M4 = 3, M7 = 6 (dual-issue).
- **NVIC is integrated** — deterministic 12-cycle entry, 6-cycle tail-chain.
- **Bit-banding supported on M3, M4, M7** (NOT on M0/M0+).
- **FPU + DSP available on M4 (SP only) and M7 (SP + DP)**.
- **Cortex-M0+ and M23 target ultra-low-power** — ~32 µW/MHz.
- **Wakeup Interrupt Controller (WIC)** allows NVIC to monitor IRQs while the core is powered down.
- **Cortex-M7 has ICache, DCache, and TCM** — unique among M-series for deterministic high-speed DSP.
- **The Cortex-M family uses a 4-GB unified memory map** divided into code, SRAM, peripheral, external RAM, and system regions.
- **CoreMark/MHz is the gold benchmark** — M7 ≈ 5.29 is roughly 3.3× the M0's 1.62.
- **Bit-band alias formula:** `alias = bitband_alias_base + (byte_offset × 32) + (bit × 4)` — memorize.
- **Interrupt tail-chain = 6 cycles** is the secret to Cortex-M's deterministic real-time behavior.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to the Cortex-M23 and Cortex-M33 Processors and the Armv8-M Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to the Cortex-M23 and Cortex-M33 Processors and the Armv8-M Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Armv8-M Architecture — Formal Definition

The **Armv8-M architecture** is the latest generation of ARM's M-profile architecture, designed specifically for **microcontroller-class** and **deeply embedded** applications. It introduces the **AArch32 execution state** for M-profile processors (the A-profile uses AArch64), backward compatibility with Armv6-M and Armv7-M, and the revolutionary **TrustZone for ARMv8-M** security extension.

The Armv8-M architecture is partitioned into two sub-profiles:

| Sub-profile | Purpose | Sample Processor |
|-------------|---------|------------------|
| **Armv8-M Baseline** | Ultra-low power, minimal gate count | Cortex-M23 |
| **Armv8-M Mainline** | High performance, rich features | Cortex-M33 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The two sub-profiles of Armv8-M are **Baseline** (Cortex-M23) and **Mainline** (Cortex-M33). The Mainline adds DSP, FPU, and full MPU support; the Baseline is a strict superset of Armv6-M (Cortex-M0/M0+).

### 1.2 The Cortex-M23 Processor — Definition

The **ARM Cortex-M23** is the smallest and most energy-efficient processor in the Cortex-M family. It implements the **Armv8-M Baseline** sub-profile, uses a **2-stage pipeline**, and is intended as a drop-in upgrade for legacy Cortex-M0/M0+ designs. It is the **first** Cortex-M processor to introduce **TrustZone for ARMv8-M** at the entry level.

### 1.3 The Cortex-M33 Processor — Definition

The **ARM Cortex-M33** is a **mid-range**, mainstream processor implementing the **Armv8-M Mainline** sub-profile. It features a configurable **3-stage pipeline** (with branch speculation), an optional **single-precision FPU**, optional **DSP extensions**, an **8-region MPU**, and **TrustZone for ARMv8-M** security. It is positioned as the successor to Cortex-M3/M4.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Scoops" Ice-Cream Shop

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Real-World):**
> Imagine a bank vault system. The **Cortex-M23** is like a *single-locked drawer* — it gives you one secure compartment (TrustZone) for your valuables while keeping everything else simple and cheap. The **Cortex-M33** is like a *full multi-locker vault* — it has the same secure compartment *plus* extra drawers (DSP, FPU, MPU) for organizing money, jewellery, and documents efficiently.
>
> Both run on the same **Armv8-M instruction grammar** (the "banking rules"), but the M33 vault is more powerful. The M23 vault is more affordable. Choosing between them is like choosing between a scooter and a motorcycle — both get you there, but one is built for cost-efficiency, the other for performance.

### 1.5 Key Architectural Innovations in Armv8-M

1. **TrustZone for ARMv8-M** — Hardware-level security partitioning into *Secure* and *Non-Secure* worlds.
2. **Enhanced MPU** — Up to **16 regions** on Mainline (8 on Baseline).
3. **Improved Interrupt Handling** — **Tail-chaining** of interrupts with lower latency than Armv7-M.
4. **Coprocessor Interface** — Custom instruction extensions via the ARM Custom Datapath Extension (CDE) on M33.
5. **Stack Pointer Banking** — Hardware-managed **MSP** (Main) and **PSP** (Process) Stack Pointers.
6. **A32 and T32 Instruction Set** — No A64 in M-profile (unlike A-profile).
7. **Wake-up Interrupt Controller (WIC)** — Ultra-low-power sleep mode support.

### 1.6 Physical / Standard Metrics Summary

| Parameter | Cortex-M23 | Cortex-M33 |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Architecture | Armv8-M Baseline | Armv8-M Mainline |
| Pipeline Stages | **2** | **3** (configurable) |
| DMIPS/MHz | **0.99** | **1.50** |
| CoreMark/MHz | **2.50** | **3.86** |
| Max Interrupt Lines | **32** (NVIC) | **240** (NVIC) |
| MPU Regions | **0 or 8** (optional) | **0 or 8** (optional, configurable to 16) |
| FPU | **No** | **Optional (SP-FPU)** |
| DSP Extensions | **No** | **Optional** |
| TrustZone-M | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Bit-banding | **No** (removed) | **No** (removed) |
| AHB-Lite Bus Width | **32-bit** | **32-bit** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Armv8-M Architecture Profile Hierarchy

Armv8-M is structured as a **layered compatibility model**. Understanding this hierarchy is critical for KTU questions on architectural evolution:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           Armv8-M (M-profile, AArch32)          │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │       Armv8-M Mainline (Cortex-M33)       │  │
│  │   ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐ │  │
│  │   │    Armv8-M Baseline (Cortex-M23)    │ │  │
│  │   │   ┌──────────────────────────────┐  │ │  │
│  │   │   │   Armv6-M (Cortex-M0/M0+)    │  │ │  │
│  │   │   └──────────────────────────────┘  │ │  │
│  │   └─────────────────────────────────────┘ │  │
│  └───────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Backward Compatibility Rule:** Every Cortex-M33 is functionally a superset of a Cortex-M23, which is in turn a functional superset of a Cortex-M0/M0+. Code compiled for an older core **runs unchanged** on a newer one (subject to memory map and peripheral availability).

### 2.2 TrustZone for ARMv8-M — Operation Logic

TrustZone partitions the **entire system** (not just the core) into two worlds:

- **Secure World** — Trusted firmware, key storage, secure I/O.
- **Non-Secure World** — Application code, RTOS, third-party libraries.

The partitioning is enforced by:
1. **NS-bit in bus transactions** — Every memory access carries a "Non-Secure" flag on the system bus (e.g., AHB5).
2. **SAU (Security Attribution Unit)** — A configurable unit inside the core that defines which memory regions are Secure/Non-Secure/Non-Secure Callable (NSC).
3. **Secure Gate (SG)** instructions — A special branch instruction `SG` placed in NSC regions that allows controlled entry into Secure functions.

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

1. At reset, the processor boots in the **Secure state**.
2. The SAU is configured by Secure firmware to mark certain memory regions as Non-Secure.
3. The application binary is loaded into the Non-Secure region.
4. When Non-Secure code calls a Secure API, it must first branch to a **veneer** located in an **NSC** region.
5. The `SG` instruction transitions the core to **Secure** state.
6. On return, the core returns to Non-Secure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why TrustZone on Cortex-M23?** Even tiny IoT devices need security. A connected temperature sensor must protect its cryptographic keys from firmware attacks — TrustZone-M provides this at near-zero cost overhead.

### 2.3 The Cortex-M33 Pipeline

The Cortex-M33 implements a **3-stage pipeline**:

| Stage | Operation |
|-------|-----------|
| **F (Fetch)** | Instruction fetched from I-Code bus |
| **D (Decode / Issue)** | Decoded; operands read from register file |
| **E (Execute)** | ALU operation, branch resolution, memory access on D-Code bus |

Branch speculation may be **enabled** or **disabled** by the implementer. With speculation enabled, the Fetch stage predicts the branch outcome to keep the pipeline full, increasing performance at the cost of slightly higher power.

### 2.4 Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (NVIC)

Both M23 and M33 integrate the **NVIC** for low-latency interrupt handling:

- **Cortex-M23:** Up to **32 external interrupts** (IRQ0–IRQ31) + 1 NMI + 16 system exceptions.
- **Cortex-M33:** Up to **240 external interrupts** + 1 NMI + 16 system exceptions.

**Interrupt latency is deterministic and short:**

$$ t_{latency} = N_{cycles} \times T_{clk} $$

where $N_{cycles}$ is typically **12 clock cycles** for tail-chained interrupts and **6 cycles** for late-arriving interrupts.

### 2.5 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Value or Unit |
|--------------|---------------------|---------------|
| $\text{CoreMark/MHz}$ | Performance metric | M23: **2.50**; M33: **3.86** |
| $\text{DMIPS/MHz}$ | Dhrystone performance | M23: **0.99**; M33: **1.50** |
| $f_{clk}$ | System clock frequency | Typically up to **50–180 MHz** |
| $t_{int}$ | Interrupt latency | **12 cycles** (tail-chain) |
| $N_{IRQ}$ | Number of IRQ lines | M23: 32; M33: 240 |
| $N_{MPU}$ | MPU regions | M23: 0/8; M33: 0/8/16 |
| $\text{SP}$ variants | Stack pointers | MSP + PSP (banked) |
| $\text{Pipeline depth}$ | Stages | M23: 2; M33: 3 |
| $\text{TrustZone}$ | Security extension | Yes (both) |
| $\text{FPU precision}$ | Floating-point | M33: **Single (SP-FPU)** |
| $R_{priv}$ | Privilege levels | Handler & Thread modes |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IoT Edge Nodes (M23):** Battery-powered sensors, BLE beacons, wearables.
- **Secure Motor Control (M33):** Drone flight controllers with DSP+security.
- **Smart Meters:** TrustZone protects cryptographic keys for billing integrity.
- **Automotive ECUs:** M33's DSP enables sensor-fusion at low latency.
- **Medical Wearables:** Low-power ECG patches using M23's deep sleep modes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Performance Ratio M33 / M23

The KTU examiner often asks for the **performance ratio** between two cores. Let us derive the DMIPS ratio mathematically.

$$
R_{DMIPS} = \frac{\text{DMIPS/MHz}_{M33}}{\text{DMIPS/MHz}_{M23}}
$$

Substituting ARM-published values:

$$
R_{DMIPS} = \frac{1.50}{0.99}
$$

Performing the long division:

$$
\begin{aligned}
1.50 \div 0.99 &= \frac{150}{99} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{50}{33} \\[4pt]
&= 1.5151\ldots
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{R_{DMIPS} \approx 1.52}
$$

> The Cortex-M33 delivers approximately **1.52× the DMIPS performance** of the Cortex-M23 at the same clock frequency.

### 3.2 Derivation: CoreMark Ratio

$$
R_{CM} = \frac{\text{CoreMark/MHz}_{M33}}{\text{CoreMark/MHz}_{M23}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{CM} &= \frac{3.86}{2.50} \\[4pt]
&= 1.544
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{CM} \approx 1.54}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Interrupt Latency in Real Time

Given a system clock $f_{clk} = 50\,\text{MHz}$ on a Cortex-M33 with tail-chained interrupt latency of **12 cycles**, find the latency in nanoseconds.

$$
T_{clk} = \frac{1}{f_{clk}} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^6} = 20\,\text{ns}
$$

$$
t_{latency} = N_{cycles} \times T_{clk} = 12 \times 20\,\text{ns}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{latency} = 240\,\text{ns}}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Cortex-M23 vs Cortex-M33 vs Cortex-M4

> [!NOTE]
> The Cortex-M4 is the **Armv7-M** predecessor. Comparing it with the M23 and M33 shows the **evolution** of M-profile design.

| Feature | Cortex-M4 (Armv7-M) | Cortex-M23 (Armv8-M Base) | Cortex-M33 (Armv8-M Main) |
|---------|--------------------|----------------------------|-----------------------------|
| ISA | Thumb-2 | A32/T32 (Armv8-M) | A32/T32 (Armv8-M) |
| Pipeline | 3-stage | **2-stage** | **3-stage** |
| FPU | Optional SP | **No** | **Optional SP** |
| DSP | Yes | **No** | **Optional** |
| MPU | Optional 8 | Optional 0/8 | Optional 0/8/16 |
| TrustZone | No | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Bit-banding | Yes | **No** | **No** |
| CoreMark/MHz | 3.40 | 2.50 | 3.86 |
| DMIPS/MHz | 1.25 | 0.99 | 1.50 |

### 3.5 Derivation: Effective Performance at 100 MHz

If both cores are clocked at $f_{clk} = 100\,\text{MHz}$, the effective CoreMark is:

**Cortex-M23:**

$$
CM_{M23} = 2.50 \times 100 = 250\,\text{CoreMark}
$$

**Cortex-M33:**

$$
CM_{M33} = 3.86 \times 100 = 386\,\text{CoreMark}
$$

Difference:

$$
\Delta CM = 386 - 250 = 136\,\text{CoreMark}
$$

Percentage gain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{gain} &= \frac{136}{250} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= 54.4\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\%\text{gain} \approx 54.4\%}
$$

### 3.6 KTU Analytical Reasoning Chain

For any "Compare M23 and M33" question, apply this four-step chain:

1. **Identify sub-profile** — Baseline (M23) vs Mainline (M33).
2. **List common features** — TrustZone, A32/T32, NVIC, WIC, AHB-Lite.
3. **List distinguishing features** — FPU, DSP, MPU region count, pipeline depth, CoreMark.
4. **Conclude with application** — M23 for ultra-low-power IoT; M33 for secure DSP/motor control.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cortex-M33 Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Cortex-M33 Core"] --> B["3 Stage Pipeline Fetch Decode Execute"]
    A --> C["Register Bank R0 to R12, MSP, PSP, LR, PC, PSR"]
    A --> D["NVIC Up to 240 IRQs"]
    A --> E["Optional SP FPU Single Precision"]
    A --> F["Optional DSP SIMD Instructions"]
    A --> G["Optional MPU 0 8 or 16 Regions"]
    A --> H["SAU Security Attribution Unit"]
    A --> I["WIC Wakeup Interrupt Controller"]
    A --> J["AHB Lite System Bus"]
    A --> K["Optional ETM Trace Macrocell"]
    A --> L["Optional MPU TrustZone for ARMv8 M"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cortex-M23 Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M["Cortex-M23 Core"] --> N["2 Stage Pipeline Fetch and Execute"]
    M --> O["Register Bank R0 to R12, MSP, PSP, LR, PC, PSR"]
    M --> P["NVIC 32 IRQs"]
    M --> Q["Optional MPU 0 or 8 Regions"]
    M --> R["SAU Security Attribution Unit"]
    M --> S["WIC Wakeup Interrupt Controller"]
    M --> T["AHB Lite System Bus"]
    M --> U["TrustZone for ARMv8 M Baseline"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flow — TrustZone State Transition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Reset State Secure"] --> B["Boot Secure Firmware"]
    B --> C["SAU Configuration"]
    C --> D["Branch to Non Secure Application"]
    D --> E["Call Secure API via SG Instruction"]
    E --> F["Enter Secure Handler"]
    F --> G["Return to Non Secure"]
    G --> D
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Memory Map with TrustZone Attribution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CODE["Code Region 0x00000000 to 0x1FFFFFFF"]
        C1["Secure Flash 0x00000000 to 0x000FFFFF"]
        C2["Non Secure Flash 0x00100000 to 0x1FFFFFFF"]
    end
    subgraph SRAM["SRAM 0x20000000 to 0x3FFFFFFF"]
        S1["Secure RAM 0x20000000 to 0x2001FFFF"]
        S2["Non Secure RAM 0x20020000 to 0x3FFFFFFF"]
    end
    subgraph PERI["Peripherals 0x40000000 to 0x5FFFFFFF"]
        P1["Secure Peripherals 0x40000000 to 0x4000FFFF"]
        P2["Non Secure Peripherals 0x40010000 to 0x5FFFFFFF"]
    end
    subgraph NSC["NSC Region Veneer Table 4 byte aligned"]
        N1["SG Instructions only"]
    end
```

### 4.5 Mermaid — Pipeline Comparison M23 vs M33

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M23["Cortex M23 Two Stage"]
        F1["F Fetch"] --> E1["E Execute"]
    end
    subgraph M33["Cortex M33 Three Stage"]
        F2["F Fetch"] --> D2["D Decode"]
        D2 --> E2["E Execute"]
    end
```

### 4.6 Mermaid — Architectural Evolution Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Armv6 M"] --> B["Armv8 M Baseline"]
    A --> C["Armv7 M"]
    B --> D["Armv8 M Mainline"]
    C --> E["Cortex M3 M4 M7"]
    D --> F["Cortex M33"]
    B --> G["Cortex M23"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define Armv8-M architecture. List its two sub-profiles.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Armv8-M architecture** is the latest M-profile architecture from ARM Holdings, designed for embedded and IoT microcontrollers. It introduces **TrustZone for ARMv8-M** hardware security while maintaining backward compatibility with the earlier Armv6-M and Armv7-M architectures. It supports only the **AArch32** execution state.

The two sub-profiles are:
1. **Armv8-M Baseline** — implemented by the **Cortex-M23** processor.
2. **Armv8-M Mainline** — implemented by the **Cortex-M33** processor.

**(3 Marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*List any three features of the Cortex-M23 processor.*

**Model Answer:**

1. Implements **Armv8-M Baseline** sub-profile.
2. **2-stage pipeline** with up to **32 interrupts** in the NVIC.
3. **TrustZone for ARMv8-M** with **Security Attribution Unit (SAU)**.
4. Optional **MPU** with up to **8 regions**.
5. **0.99 DMIPS/MHz** and **2.50 CoreMark/MHz** performance.

*(Any three — 3 Marks)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **Armv8-M architecture** in detail. Discuss its two sub-profiles and the relationship with the earlier M-profile architectures. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **internal architecture of the Cortex-M33 processor**. List at least **six** key features. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Armv8-M Architecture (7 Marks)**

The Armv8-M architecture is the **8th-generation M-profile** from ARM, released to bring **TrustZone security** and **enhanced performance** to deeply embedded systems.

**Key Elements:**

1. **AArch32 Execution State** — Unlike A-profile (Armv8-A uses AArch64), M-profile remains 32-bit-only. The instruction sets **A32** (formerly ARM) and **T32** (formerly Thumb) are supported.

2. **Two Sub-profiles:**
   - **Armv8-M Baseline:** Minimum feature set, drop-in replacement for Cortex-M0/M0+. Adds TrustZone. Implemented by **Cortex-M23**.
   - **Armv8-M Mainline:** Adds optional **FPU**, **DSP**, full **MPU**, and richer interrupt model. Implemented by **Cortex-M33**.

3. **Backward Compatibility:** An M-profile core can be either Armv6-M, Armv7-M, Armv8-M Baseline, or Armv8-M Mainline. A Mainline processor executes Baseline code unchanged.

4. **TrustZone for ARMv8-M:** Hardware partitioning into Secure and Non-Secure states. Implemented via:
   - **NS-bit** on the bus (AHB5).
   - **SAU** for memory attribution.
   - **SG instruction** as a Secure gateway.

5. **Improved NVIC:** Lower interrupt latency (12 cycles tail-chain), Wake-up Interrupt Controller (WIC) for ultra-low-power sleep.

**(b) Cortex-M33 Internal Architecture (7 Marks)**

The Cortex-M33 core consists of:

- **3-stage pipeline** (F → D → E) with optional branch speculation.
- **Register bank** — R0–R12, banked **MSP/PSP**, **LR**, **PC**, **PSR**.
- **NVIC** with up to **240 interrupts** and **16 system exceptions**.
- **Optional SP-FPU** (IEEE 754 single-precision).
- **Optional DSP/SIMD** extensions.
- **Optional MPU** with **8 or 16 regions**.
- **SAU** for TrustZone-M.
- **AHB-Lite** system interface.
- **Optional ETM** for instruction trace.

**Six Key Features:**
1. TrustZone for ARMv8-M hardware security.
2. 1.50 DMIPS/MHz.
3. Configurable 8/16-region MPU.
4. Wake-up Interrupt Controller.
5. Single-precision FPU.
6. DSP/SIMD instructions for signal processing.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Compare the **Cortex-M23** and **Cortex-M33** processors across **at least eight parameters**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe **TrustZone for ARMv8-M** with a neat diagram. Explain the role of the **Security Attribution Unit (SAU)** and the **SG instruction**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparison M23 vs M33 (7 Marks)**

| Parameter | Cortex-M23 | Cortex-M33 |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Architecture | Armv8-M Baseline | Armv8-M Mainline |
| Pipeline | 2-stage | 3-stage |
| CoreMark/MHz | 2.50 | 3.86 |
| DMIPS/MHz | 0.99 | 1.50 |
| NVIC IRQs | 32 | 240 |
| MPU | 0/8 | 0/8/16 |
| FPU | None | Optional SP |
| DSP | None | Optional |
| TrustZone | Yes | Yes |
| Target Use | Ultra-low-power IoT | Secure DSP/MCU |

**(b) TrustZone for ARMv8-M (7 Marks)**

TrustZone partitions the system into two execution worlds:
- **Secure World** — privileged, trusted code, keys, secure I/O.
- **Non-Secure World** — application code, RTOS, third-party libraries.

**Components:**

1. **NS-bit in bus protocol (AHB5):** Every bus transfer carries a Non-Secure indicator that peripherals and memory controllers use to gate access.

2. **SAU (Security Attribution Unit):** A small in-core unit defining memory regions as **Secure**, **Non-Secure**, or **Non-Secure Callable (NSC)**. The SAU typically supports **up to 8 regions** and is configured by Secure firmware at boot.

3. **NSC (Non-Secure Callable) Region:** A special region containing **veneer functions** that act as gateways into Secure code.

4. **SG (Secure Gateway) Instruction:** A pseudo-instruction placed in NSC regions. When Non-Secure code calls a Secure function, the call is routed through an NSC veneer containing the **SG** instruction. The SG instruction transitions the core from Non-Secure to Secure state.

5. **State transition flow:** Non-Secure → call veneer in NSC → SG → Secure handler → return → Non-Secure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Students often confuse **NS-bit on the bus** with the **security attribute stored in memory**. The NS-bit is a **transaction-level signal**, not a stored bit in flash.
> 2. Do **not** confuse **NSC** with **Secure Callable**. NSC is a *memory region type*; SG is the *instruction* that allows entry.
> 3. In TrustZone comparisons, students forget that the **MPU and SAU are separate units** — the MPU manages *privileged* vs *unprivileged* access, while the SAU manages *Secure* vs *Non-Secure* access.
> 4. The Cortex-M23 does **not** have a hardware FPU — claiming "M23 has FPU" will cost full marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Armv8-M** is the M-profile architecture version 8 — supports AArch32 only (no AArch64 in M-profile).
- **Two sub-profiles:** **Baseline (M23)** and **Mainline (M33)**. Mainline ⊇ Baseline.
- **Cortex-M23:** 2-stage pipeline, 32 IRQs, optional 8-region MPU, **no FPU, no DSP**, **2.50 CoreMark/MHz**.
- **Cortex-M33:** 3-stage pipeline, 240 IRQs, optional 8/16-region MPU, **optional FPU + DSP**, **3.86 CoreMark/MHz**.
- **TrustZone for ARMv8-M** is a *system-wide* security model, not a core-only feature. It uses **SAU + SG + NS-bit on AHB5**.
- **Bit-banding is removed** in Armv8-M (use bit-band alias region or atomic bit-banding via exclusive accesses).
- **Wake-up Interrupt Controller (WIC)** enables deep-sleep wake-up without NVIC clocking — vital for IoT.
- **Stack Pointer Banking:** Both cores have **MSP** (Main) and **PSP** (Process); Thread mode can use either, Handler mode uses MSP.
- **Cortex-M33 is a drop-in replacement for Cortex-M3/M4** with added TrustZone.
- **Pipeline rule of thumb:** More stages = higher clock frequency, slightly higher power per instruction; fewer stages = lower power, lower peak frequency.
- **For KTU numericals:** DMIPS/MHz and CoreMark/MHz are **per-MHz** metrics — multiply by clock frequency to get total performance.
- **Interrupt latency formula:** $t_{latency} = N_{cycles} \times T_{clk}$; typical value is **12 cycles** (tail-chained).
- **MPU region sizing:** Regions must be **power-of-two aligned** to 32 bytes minimum.
- **TrustZone-M ≠ TrustZone-A:** TrustZone-A uses EL3 and a Secure Monitor; TrustZone-M uses Handler mode transition via SG.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ARM Core Features: Registers, Memory, and Bus Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** ARM Core Features: Registers, Memory, and Bus Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ARM (Advanced RISC Machine)** is a family of **32-bit Reduced Instruction Set Computer (RISC)** processor cores characterized by a **Load/Store architecture**, a **uniform register file**, and a **modular bus infrastructure** standardized around the **AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture)** specification.

The ARM core, as prescribed in the KTU 2024 Microcontrollers syllabus, is built on three foundational pillars:

1. **Register Bank Architecture** – A large, multi-mode register file containing **37 physical 32-bit registers** (in the classic ARM7TDMI core), of which only **31 are usable at any instant** depending on the processor mode. The remaining registers handle banking for **FIQ, IRQ, Supervisor, Abort, Undefined, and System/User modes**.

2. **Memory Subsystem** – A single, **linear 4 GB address space** ($2^{32}$ bytes) with a **little-endian** default byte ordering, accessible through a unified memory map that maps Flash, SRAM, peripherals, and on-chip memory into specific address regions.

3. **Bus Architecture** – A hierarchical, standardized bus matrix implementing the **AMBA** specification, typically featuring:
   - **AHB (Advanced High-performance Bus)** for high-speed CPU, memory, and DMA traffic.
   - **APB (Advanced Peripheral Bus)** for low-bandwidth peripherals.
   - **ASB (Advanced System Bus)** as an alternative to AHB in older designs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The current module explicitly emphasizes the **differences between RISC and CISC**, the **register banking mechanism across privileged modes**, the **memory map of LPC2148 (ARM7TDMI-S)**, and the **AMBA bus hierarchy**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Factory

To grasp the ARM core intuitively, imagine a **highly automated smart factory** (the processor core):

| Factory Element | ARM Equivalent | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Foreman's Desk (work-in-progress papers) | **Registers (R0–R15)** | Ultra-fast scratchpad used for live calculations |
| Central Warehouse | **Main Memory (Flash/SRAM)** | Bulk storage of instructions and data |
| High-Speed Pneumatic Tubes (airmail) | **AHB** | Fast delivery between CPU, RAM, and DMA |
| Slow Conveyor Belts | **APB** | Delivery of small packages to peripheral workstations (UART, timers) |
| Security Badge System | **CPSR / SPSR** | Determines who (which mode) may enter the foreman's office |
| Reception Desk Switching | **Bus Matrix / Arbiter** | Routes packages between tubes and belts without collisions |

> The **foreman** (CPU) needs to do his math on the **desk (registers)** for speed. When he needs a blueprint, the **pneumatic tube (AHB)** fetches it instantly from the **warehouse (RAM)**. Routine supplies for the assembly line (peripherals) trickle in via the **slow conveyor (APB)**. This two-tier speed is the heart of ARM's power efficiency.

### 1.3 Salient ARM Core Features (Quick Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> These seven features are the most frequently tested in KTU Part A questions.

- **Load/Store Architecture** — Arithmetic/Logic operations operate **only on registers**, never directly on memory.
- **Fixed-Length 32-bit Instructions** — Simplifies decoding (CISC uses variable-length).
- **37 Physical Registers (ARM7TDMI)** — Of which **31 visible** to any mode; **6 reserved** for hardware.
- **CPSR & SPSR** — **Current Program Status Register** and **Saved Program Status Register** for mode control.
- **Uniform 4 GB Address Space** — Same address space for code, data, and peripherals.
- **AMBA Standardization** — Modular, reusable bus IP.
- **Thumb-2 (in newer cores)** — Compresses code to 16-bit instructions for density.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Register File — A Layered View

The ARM register file is not a flat array. It is a **banked, mode-dependent structure** that physically changes which physical silicon cells are exposed when the processor enters a privileged mode.

#### 2.1.1 Visible Registers per Mode

| Mode | Visible GPRs (R0–R12) | Banked Registers (R13, R14) | Special SPSR |
|---|---|---|---|
| **User (usr)** | R0–R12 | R13_usr, R14_usr | None |
| **System (sys)** | R0–R12 | R13_usr, R14_usr | None |
| **Supervisor (svc)** | R0–R12 | R13_svc, R14_svc | SPSR_svc |
| **FIQ (fiq)** | R0–R7, R8_fiq–R12_fiq (banked) | R13_fiq, R14_fiq | SPSR_fiq |
| **IRQ (irq)** | R0–R12 | R13_irq, R14_irq | SPSR_irq |
| **Abort (abt)** | R0–R12 | R13_abt, R14_abt | SPSR_abt |
| **Undefined (und)** | R0–R12 | R13_und, R14_und | SPSR_und |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** The **System mode** uses the *User mode* bank (R13_usr, R14_usr) but with *privileged* access to CPSR. There is **no SPSR for User or System modes**.

#### 2.1.2 R15 — The Program Counter (PC)

R15 is a special register that holds the **current instruction address plus 8 bytes** in ARM state (the pipeline is 3 stages: Fetch, Decode, Execute). When read, it returns:
$$ \text{PC}_{\text{read}} = \text{Address of current instruction} + 8 $$

In Thumb state:
$$ \text{PC}_{\text{read}} = \text{Address of current instruction} + 4 $$

#### 2.1.3 R13 — Stack Pointer (SP) and R14 — Link Register (LR)

- **R13 (SP):** Points to the **top of the active stack**. Each privileged mode has its own banked SP, enabling nested interrupts without corrupting the main stack.
- **R14 (LR):** On a **Branch with Link (BL)** instruction, R14 receives the **return address** (PC + 4) so the subroutine can return via `MOV PC, LR`.

#### 2.1.4 The CPSR and SPSR Layout

The **Current Program Status Register** is a 32-bit register with the following canonical layout:

| Bit(s) | Field | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 31 – 28 | **N, Z, C, V** | Condition flags (Negative, Zero, Carry, oVerflow) |
| 27 – 8 | Reserved / Extension (J in ARMv5+) | |
| 7 | **I** | IRQ disable (1 = masked) |
| 6 | **F** | FIQ disable (1 = masked) |
| 5 | **T** | Thumb state bit |
| 4 – 0 | **M[4:0]** | Processor mode (e.g., 0b10011 = Supervisor) |

**Mode encoding values (KTU High-Yield):**

| Mode | M[4:0] | Binary |
|---|---|---|
| User | 0b10000 | 0x10 |
| FIQ | 0b10001 | 0x11 |
| IRQ | 0b10010 | 0x12 |
| Supervisor | 0b10011 | 0x13 |
| Abort | 0b10111 | 0x17 |
| Undefined | 0b11011 | 0x1B |
| System | 0b11111 | 0x1F |

### 2.2 Memory Architecture

ARM uses a **modified Von Neumann** bus interface for the classic ARM7 (single address and data bus shared), but modern ARM cores (Cortex-M) use a true **Harvard** bus structure (separate I-code and D-code buses to Flash). The KTU syllabus (LPC2148) primarily concerns the **Von Neumann** external view with internal caches (in some cores) creating a Harvard-like behavior.

#### 2.2.1 The 4 GB Linear Address Map (LPC2148 / Generic ARM7)

The full 32-bit address space is partitioned as follows (high-yield for KTU):

| Address Range | Size | Typical Device |
|---|---|---|
| 0x0000 0000 – 0x0007 FFFF | 512 KB | **On-chip Flash** (LPC2148) |
| 0x4000 0000 – 0x400F FFFF | 1 MB | **On-chip SRAM** |
| 0xE000 0000 – 0xEFFF FFFF | 256 MB | AHB peripherals (Vectored Interrupt Controller, etc.) |
| 0xF000 0000 – 0xFFFF FFFF | 256 MB | APB peripherals (UART, Timers, I²C, SPI) |
| 0x8000 0000 – 0xDFFF FFFF | 1.5 GB | External memory (when remapped) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boot Remap:** On reset, address **0x0000 0000** is **aliased to Flash** (boot block) so the processor can fetch the reset vector. The **MEMMAP register** in System Control Block allows remapping to internal RAM or external memory.

#### 2.2.2 Endianness and Alignment

- **Default:** Little-endian (LSB at lowest address).
- **Big-endian:** Selectable via hardware pin BIGEND or CPSR.E bit (ARMv6+).
- **Alignment:** Word access must be on a 4-byte boundary ($A \mod 4 = 0$); halfword on 2-byte ($A \mod 2 = 0$). Misaligned access causes exceptions on classic ARM.

### 2.3 Bus Architecture — The AMBA Hierarchy

The **Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture (AMBA)** is the de-facto on-chip bus standard for ARM SoCs.

#### 2.3.1 The Two-Tier AMBA Model

The bus matrix consists of three principal bus types:

| Bus | Width | Purpose | Typical Devices |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AHB** | 32/64/128-bit | High-bandwidth, pipelined, burst transfers | CPU, DMA, RAM, external memory |
| **ASB** | 32-bit | Alternative to AHB (older, unidirectional) | Deprecated in new designs |
| **APB** | 8/16/32-bit | Low-power, simple, non-pipelined | UART, Timers, GPIO, I²C, SPI, ADC |

#### 2.3.2 AHB Key Features (Frequently Examined)

- **Burst transfers** of 4, 8, 16 beats (INCR, WRAP4, INCR4, etc.).
- **Split transactions** to release bus during long slave latency.
- **Bus matrix** with multiple masters and slaves, supporting **parallel paths** (e.g., CPU can fetch from Flash while DMA writes to RAM).
- **Arbitration:** Fixed-priority or round-robin, with a **HBUSREQ / HLOCK** handshake.

#### 2.3.3 APB Bridge

The **AHB-to-APB bridge** is the only device allowed to act as the sole master on the APB. All APB peripherals are slaves. The bridge translates high-speed AHB bursts into a simple **PSEL, PENABLE, PWRITE, PWDATA, PRDATA** protocol with no burst support.

### 2.4 KTU Formula & Concept Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative relationship the KTU examiner expects you to recognize. Use `\vert` for absolute-value separators so the table does not break.

| Concept | Expression / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Addressable memory | $2^{32}$ bytes | Equals **4 GB** |
| PC read (ARM state) | $A_{current} + 8$ | Bytes |
| PC read (Thumb state) | $A_{current} + 4$ | Bytes |
| Word alignment rule | $\text{addr} \mod 4 = 0$ | 32-bit access |
| Halfword alignment rule | $\text{addr} \mod 2 = 0$ | 16-bit access |
| Total physical registers (ARM7) | $37$ | $31$ user-visible + $6$ reserved |
| General purpose regs (R0–R12) | $13$ | User & System share |
| LR receive value (BL) | $A_{PC} + 4$ | Saved in R14 on call |
| Interrupt latency (min) | $\approx 7$ cycles | Classic ARM7 pipeline (Fetch/Dec/Exe) |
| Mode bits M[4:0] | $5$ bits | $2^{5} = 32$ possible modes |
| Condition codes (NZCV) | $4$ bits | Bits 31–28 of CPSR |
| Byte order (default) | Little-endian | LSB at lowest address |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Register banking** enables **deterministic, low-latency interrupt handling** — the IRQ handler immediately has its own R13/R14, so the compiler does not need a prologue to save user-mode stack pointers.
- **AMBA standardization** allows chip designers to drop in third-party IP blocks (e.g., a UART from Synopsys) without custom bus glue logic — a 70%+ reduction in design time.
- **Little-endian byte order** matches the dominant desktop/server architecture (x86) and most network protocols, simplifying data exchange.
- **The unified 4 GB address space** allows **Position-Independent Code** and flat memory models used in RTOS contexts (e.g., FreeRTOS, μC/OS-II).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section mathematically walks through three high-yield KTU derivations: **PC offset behavior**, **register banking under nested interrupts**, and **AMBA address decoding**.

### 3.1 Derivation: Why PC Reads `Current + 8` in ARM State

#### 3.1.1 The 3-Stage Pipeline

The classic ARM7 pipeline has three stages:
- **Stage 1 (F):** Fetch — instruction is read from memory.
- **Stage 2 (D):** Decode — operands are read from the register file.
- **Stage 3 (E):** Execute — ALU / shift / address generation.

At any given cycle, **three consecutive instructions** are simultaneously in the pipeline.

#### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Logic

Let $A_{n}$ denote the address of instruction $n$. We wish to find the value of R15 when instruction $n$ is being **executed** (in stage E).

**Step 1:** At cycle $k$, the processor is fetching instruction at address $A_{n+2}$.
- Reason: Two instructions $(n+1, n+2)$ are behind instruction $n$ in the pipeline.

**Step 2:** At cycle $k$, the processor is decoding instruction at address $A_{n+1}$.
- Reason: One instruction behind in stage D.

**Step 3:** At cycle $k$, the processor is executing instruction at address $A_{n}$.
- This is the current instruction. We want to know what R15 reports now.

**Step 4:** R15 is updated **at fetch time**. Therefore, by the time instruction $n$ is in the **Execute** stage, the value of R15 (the PC) has already advanced to the address of the instruction **two ahead** in the pipeline.
- The fetch stage is currently fetching $A_{n+2}$, so R15 reads as $A_{n+2}$.

**Step 5:** Since each instruction is 4 bytes (32-bit) in ARM state:
$$ A_{n+2} = A_{n} + (2 \cdot 4) = A_{n} + 8 $$

**Step 6:** Therefore, the **R15 read value** when instruction $n$ executes is:
$$ \text{R15}_{\text{read}} \;\vert_{\text{ARM}} = A_{n} + 8 $$

**Step 7:** In **Thumb state**, instructions are 16-bit (2 bytes). Two pipeline steps ahead is therefore:
$$ \text{R15}_{\text{read}} \;\vert_{\text{Thumb}} = A_{n} + (2 \cdot 2) = A_{n} + 4 $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Summary:**
> $$ \boxed{\text{R15}_{\text{ARM}} = A_{current} + 8 \quad ; \quad \text{R15}_{\text{Thumb}} = A_{current} + 4} $$

#### 3.1.3 Analytical Use — Branching

When we execute `SUB PC, PC, #4` after an LDR/STR using PC-relative addressing, we correct the pipeline offset. The compiler uses:
$$ \text{Actual target} = \text{Stated target} - 8 \quad (\text{ARM}) $$

### 3.2 Derivation: Register Banking During Nested Interrupts

#### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

Consider the following scenario: A User-mode program is running, an IRQ arrives, an FIQ arrives inside the IRQ, and the FIQ routine itself calls a Supervisor routine (e.g., SWI).

#### 3.2.2 Banking Decision Matrix

We model which physical R13 and R14 are in use at each point:

| Stage | Active Mode | Visible R13 | Visible R14 | SPSR Holding CPSR From |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | User | R13_usr | R14_usr | — |
| 2 | IRQ | **R13_irq** | **R14_irq** | User |
| 3 | FIQ | **R13_fiq** | **R14_fiq** | IRQ |
| 4 | Supervisor (SWI) | **R13_svc** | **R14_svc** | FIQ |

**Step-by-step logic:**

**Step 1:** Initial state — User mode uses R13_usr / R14_usr.

**Step 2:** IRQ exception occurs. Hardware automatically:
- Copies CPSR into SPSR_irq.
- Sets CPSR mode bits to `0b10010` (IRQ).
- Sets `I` bit in CPSR (masks further IRQ).
- Saves `PC + 4` into R14_irq.
- Forces PC to the IRQ vector (`0x0000 0018`).

**Step 3:** The IRQ handler now sees **only** R13_irq and R14_irq. The User-mode SP/LR are **invisible** (but physically intact).

**Step 4:** FIQ occurs. The new set R13_fiq / R14_fiq come online. SPSR_fiq holds the *current* CPSR (which is the IRQ's CPSR).

**Step 5:** Inside FIQ, an SWI is invoked. R13_svc / R14_svc activate; SPSR_svc holds the FIQ CPSR.

**Step 6:** On return, the inverse sequence restores R14 → PC, SPSR → CPSR in each layer. The User-mode stack pointer is **never** explicitly preserved — it is **physically undisturbed** throughout.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total visible registers at the deepest nesting point (SVC):** 13 (R0–R12 of user set) + R13_svc + R14_svc + PC + CPSR + SPSR_svc = **18 registers of 32 bits = 576 bits of instantaneous state**.

### 3.3 Derivation: AMBA Address Decoding

The AHB decoder must map a 32-bit `HADDR` to a unique slave select (HSELx). Typical decode regions for LPC2148:

#### 3.3.1 The Decoder Equation

For a slave occupying a region of size $2^{n}$ bytes, the HSEL is active when:
$$ \text{HSEL}_x = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \text{HADDR}[31:n] = \text{Base}[31:n] \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$

#### 3.3.2 Worked Example — SRAM on AHB

Suppose SRAM starts at `0x4000 0000` and is **64 KB** in size.

**Step 1:** Convert size to a power of 2.
$$ 64 \text{ KB} = 64 \times 2^{10} = 2^{16} \text{ bytes} $$

**Step 2:** Therefore $n = 16$. The decoder ignores bits [15:0] of HADDR.

**Step 3:** The base address in binary is:
$$ \text{Base}[31:0] = 0x40000000 = 0100\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000_2 $$

**Step 4:** HSEL_SRAM is asserted when the top 16 bits of HADDR equal the top 16 bits of the base:
$$ \text{HSEL}_{\text{SRAM}} = (\text{HADDR}[31:16] = 0x4000) $$

**Step 5:** Full address range that activates this HSEL:
$$ \text{HADDR} \in [0x4000\,0000,\; 0x4000\,FFFF] $$

#### 3.3.3 The General Decoding Rule

| Region Size $S$ | $n = \log_2 S$ | Address Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| 16 MB | 24 | Base + 0x00FFFFFF |
| 1 MB | 20 | Base + 0x000FFFFF |
| 64 KB | 16 | Base + 0x0000FFFF |
| 16 KB | 14 | Base + 0x00003FFF |
| 4 KB | 12 | Base + 0x00000FFF |

> [!NOTE]
> AMBA regions **must** be power-of-two aligned. Misaligned decode regions cause bus faults.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mode Transitions

| Trigger | Old Mode | New Mode | M[4:0] New | I-bit | SPSR written? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reset | Any | Supervisor | 0b10011 | 1 | No (SPSR is invalid) |
| IRQ | Any but FIQ | IRQ | 0b10010 | 1 | Yes |
| FIQ | Any | FIQ | 0b10001 | 1 | Yes |
| SWI / SVC | Any | Supervisor | 0b10011 | — | Yes |
| Data abort | Any | Abort | 0b10111 | 1 | Yes |
| Undefined instr. | Any | Undefined | 0b11011 | 1 | Yes |
| MSR CPSR (privileged) | Current | Specified | Variable | Specified | No |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ARM Core Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the major functional units of the ARM7 core and the AMBA bus matrix, showing how requests flow from master to slave.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ARM_CORE[ARM7TDMI CPU Core]
    CORE_REG[37-Physical-Register File]
    CPSR_REG[CPSR and Banked SPSRs]
    DECODER[Instruction Decoder]

    AHB_MASTER1[AHB Master Port CPU]
    AHB_MASTER2[AHB Master Port DMA Controller]
    ARB[AHB Arbiter]
    DECODE[AHB Address Decoder]
    SPLITTER[AHB Bus Matrix Splitter]

    MEM_FLASH[Flash Memory 512KB]
    MEM_SRAM[SRAM 32KB]
    EXT_MEM[External Memory Controller]

    BRIDGE[AHB to APB Bridge]
    APB_PERIPH1[APB UART0 UART1]
    APB_PERIPH2[APB Timer0 Timer1]
    APB_PERIPH3[APB I2C SPI GPIO]
    APB_PERIPH4[APB ADC PWM RTC]

    ARM_CORE --> CORE_REG
    ARM_CORE --> CPSR_REG
    ARM_CORE --> DECODER
    ARM_CORE --> AHB_MASTER1
    DECODER --> AHB_MASTER1

    AHB_MASTER1 --> ARB
    AHB_MASTER2 --> ARB
    ARB --> SPLITTER
    SPLITTER --> DECODE

    DECODE --> MEM_FLASH
    DECODE --> MEM_SRAM
    DECODE --> EXT_MEM
    DECODE --> BRIDGE

    BRIDGE --> APB_PERIPH1
    BRIDGE --> APB_PERIPH2
    BRIDGE --> APB_PERIPH3
    BRIDGE --> APB_PERIPH4

    CPSR_REG -.Controls Mode.-> ARM_CORE
    CORE_REG -.Bank Switch on Mode.-> CPSR_REG
```

### 4.2 Register Banking Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph USR_MODE[User Mode]
        U_R0[R0]
        U_R1[R1]
        U_R2[R2]
        U_R12[R12]
        U_R13[R13_usr]
        U_R14[R14_usr]
    end

    subgraph FIQ_MODE[FIQ Mode]
        F_R0[R0 to R7 shared]
        F_R8[R8_fiq]
        F_R9[R9_fiq]
        F_R10[R10_fiq]
        F_R11[R11_fiq]
        F_R12[R12_fiq]
        F_R13[R13_fiq]
        F_R14[R14_fiq]
        F_SPSR[SPSR_fiq]
    end

    subgraph IRQ_MODE[IRQ Mode]
        I_R13[R13_irq]
        I_R14[R14_irq]
        I_SPSR[SPSR_irq]
    end

    subgraph SVC_MODE[Supervisor Mode]
        S_R13[R13_svc]
        S_R14[R14_svc]
        S_SPSR[SPSR_svc]
    end

    subgraph ABT_MODE[Abort Mode]
        A_R13[R13_abt]
        A_R14[R14_abt]
        A_SPSR[SPSR_abt]
    end

    subgraph UND_MODE[Undefined Mode]
        X_R13[R13_und]
        X_R14[R14_und]
        X_SPSR[SPSR_und]
    end

    SYS_R13[R13_sys identical to R13_usr]
    SYS_R14[R14_sys identical to R14_usr]

    F_R0 -.shared.-> U_R0
```

> [!NOTE]
> Only the FIQ mode banks **R8–R12** (giving it 7 extra private registers). All other privileged modes bank **only R13, R14**, and have their **own SPSR**.

### 4.3 AMBA Two-Tier Bus Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MASTERS_LAYER[AHB Master Layer]
        M1[CPU]
        M2[DMA Engine]
    end

    subgraph ARBITRATION_LAYER[AHB Arbitration and Decode]
        ARB[Bus Arbiter]
        DEC[Address Decoder HSEL generator]
    end

    subgraph AHB_SLAVES[AHB Slaves High Speed]
        S1[Boot Flash 512KB]
        S2[SRAM 32KB]
        S3[External Memory Bus]
    end

    subgraph BRIDGE_NODE[AHB to APB Bridge]
        BRG[Single APB Master]
    end

    subgraph APB_SLAVES[APB Slaves Low Speed]
        P1[UART0 and UART1]
        P2[Timer0 and Timer1]
        P3[SPI and I2C]
        P4[ADC and PWM]
    end

    M1 --> ARB
    M2 --> ARB
    ARB --> DEC
    DEC --> S1
    DEC --> S2
    DEC --> S3
    DEC --> BRG
    BRG --> P1
    BRG --> P2
    BRG --> P3
    BRG --> P4
```

### 4.4 Memory Map Block Diagram (LPC2148)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MEM[32-bit Address Space 4GB]
    MEM --> B0[0x00000000 Flash 512KB]
    MEM --> B1[0x40000000 SRAM 32KB]
    MEM --> B2[0x7FD00000 Reserved]
    MEM --> B3[0x80000000 External Memory]
    MEM --> B4[0xE0000000 AHB Peripherals VIC]
    MEM --> B5[0xE0020000 GPIO]
    MEM --> B6[0xF0000000 APB Bridge and VPB Peripherals]
    MEM --> B7[0xFFFFFFFF Top of Space]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

*Explain the significance of the CPSR register in the ARM7 processor. List its mode-encoding for Supervisor and IRQ modes.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Current Program Status Register (CPSR)** is a 32-bit register that holds the current operating state of the ARM7 core. It contains the following key fields:

1. **Condition flags** (N, Z, C, V) in bits [31:28] — indicate the result of the most recent arithmetic/logic operation.
2. **Interrupt mask bits** — I (bit 7) masks IRQ; F (bit 6) masks FIQ.
3. **Thumb state bit** (T, bit 5) — 0 = ARM state, 1 = Thumb state.
4. **Mode bits** (M[4:0], bits [4:0]) — encode the current processor mode.

**Mode encodings required:**
- **Supervisor (SVC):** `M[4:0] = 0b10011` (decimal **19**)
- **IRQ:** `M[4:0] = 0b10010` (decimal **18**)

The CPSR is **only fully accessible in privileged modes**; in User mode, only the condition flags are readable. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for fields, 1 mark for mode values.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

*List the seven processor modes of ARM7 and state which mode has banked registers R8–R12.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **seven processor modes** of the ARM7 core are:

| # | Mode | Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | User | **usr** |
| 2 | Fast Interrupt Request | **fiq** |
| 3 | Interrupt Request | **irq** |
| 4 | Supervisor | **svc** |
| 5 | Abort | **abt** |
| 6 | Undefined | **und** |
| 7 | System | **sys** |

The **FIQ mode (fiq)** is the **only mode that banks R8, R9, R10, R11, and R12** in addition to R13, R14. This provides 7 extra private registers, allowing the FIQ handler to start processing critical data immediately without saving User-mode registers — a key reason FIQ is preferred for time-critical interrupts. (1 mark for list, 1 mark for FIQ identification, 1 mark for rationale.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), typically **7 + 7 marks**, with **internal choice within a part**.

---

#### 5.2.1 Question A — Register Architecture & CPSR (14 marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **register organization of ARM7** across all processor modes. Highlight the role of R13, R14, R15, and the SPSR. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A program is executing in **User mode** with R13_usr = `0x4000 0200` and R14_usr = `0x0000 10A0`. An **IRQ interrupt** occurs during execution of an instruction at address `0x0000 1040`.
- (i) What value gets loaded into R14_irq?
- (ii) What address does the PC jump to (assuming standard ARM7 vector table)?
- (iii) What value of CPSR is copied to SPSR_irq?
- (iv) After the IRQ handler finishes and executes `SUBS PC, LR_irq, #4` followed by `MOVS PC, LR_svc` (an inner Supervisor call is pending), what is the final User-mode CPSR N, Z, C, V bits? (Assume the IRQ handler ends with NZCV = `0xF0000000` and the SVC handler ends with NZCV = `0x80000000`.) **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

A complete diagram should show:

- **31 user-visible 32-bit registers** in ARM7TDMI.
- **R0–R12** unbanked (visible to all modes), except **R8–R12 banked in FIQ mode only**.
- **R13 (SP) and R14 (LR)** banked per mode: usr/sys share one set; fiq, irq, svc, abt, und each have their own set. That is **6 banked pairs** + 1 shared pair = 7 physical R13 + 7 physical R14, but only 2 are visible at a time.
- **R15 (PC)** — single physical register, but reads as `current + 8` in ARM state.
- **CPSR** — single physical register, one per processor.
- **SPSR** — one per privileged mode (5 SPSRs: fiq, irq, svc, abt, und). No SPSR for User or System.

**Roles:**
- **R13 (Stack Pointer):** Points to the top of the active stack; banked to allow independent stacks per privileged mode.
- **R14 (Link Register):** Receives return address on `BL` / subroutine call or exception entry; on IRQ, R14_irq = PC + 4.
- **R15 (Program Counter):** Holds the address of the next instruction to fetch; pipeline offset of +8 in ARM state.
- **SPSR:** "Save" copy of CPSR taken at the moment of exception entry. On exception return (via `MOVS PC, LR` or `SUBS PC, LR, #4`), SPSR is atomically restored to CPSR.

Marks breakdown: 1.5 for the diagram, 1.5 for R13/R14/R15, 1.5 for SPSR, 1 for R8–R12 banked in FIQ, 1.5 for full coverage of all 7 modes.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) R14_irq value:**

On IRQ entry, R14_irq is loaded with `PC + 4` (the address of the instruction that would have been executed if the IRQ had not occurred):
$$ \text{R14\_irq} = 0x0000\,1040 + 4 = 0x0000\,1044 $$

(1 mark)

**(ii) PC jump target:**

The standard ARM7 vector table has the **IRQ vector at offset 0x18** from the base address. After the boot remap, the vector base is `0x0000 0000`, so:
$$ \text{PC} = 0x0000\,0000 + 0x18 = 0x0000\,0018 $$

(1 mark)

**(iii) SPSR_irq value:**

The CPSR at the moment of interrupt is **copied unchanged** to SPSR_irq. We are not given the User CPSR explicitly, so we write:
$$ \text{SPSR\_irq} = \text{CPSR}_{\text{User at time of IRQ}} $$

(1 mark)

**(iv) Final User-mode NZCV bits — two-stage exception return:**

**Step 1:** The IRQ handler executes and ends with CPSR.NZCV = `0xF0000000`.

**Step 2:** The IRQ handler performs `SUBS PC, LR_irq, #4`. This causes the SPSR_irq to be restored to CPSR — but this is the SPSR that was saved when the IRQ was entered, i.e., the **User CPSR at IRQ time**, **not** the IRQ's current NZCV. The `SUBS` updates the **restored** CPSR's NZCV, but those new flags are the **result of the subtraction**, not the IRQ's `0xF0000000`.

**Step 3:** However, the problem asks for the *final* User-mode NZCV. So we must trace the full chain.

- After the IRQ return (`SUBS PC, LR_irq, #4`), CPSR reflects **User-at-IRQ-time** flags (not specified; we call it NZCV_U1).
- The User code briefly resumes and re-enters SVC via SWI. At that moment, CPSR (with NZCV_U1) is saved to SPSR_svc.
- Inside SVC, NZCV becomes `0x80000000`, meaning N=1, Z=0, C=0, V=0.
- SVC returns via `MOVS PC, LR_svc`. This restores SPSR_svc (which is NZCV_U1) back to CPSR. The `MOVS` updates NZCV with the result of the move (zeroes), so the final CPSR.NZCV = `0x00000000`.

**Final answer:**
$$ \text{NZCV}_{\text{User, final}} = 0x0000\,0000 \quad (\text{all flags cleared}) $$

(3 marks — 1 for IRQ return mechanism, 1 for SVC return mechanism, 1 for final numerical result)

---

#### 5.2.2 Question B — Memory Map & AMBA Bus Architecture (14 marks)

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Draw and explain the **AMBA bus architecture** of a typical ARM7-based microcontroller. Compare AHB and APB in terms of (i) bus width, (ii) pipelining, (iii) burst support, and (iv) typical peripherals. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The **LPC2148** has Flash memory mapped from `0x0000 0000` to `0x0007 FFFF` and SRAM from `0x4000 0000` to `0x4000 7FFF`.
- (i) Compute the size of Flash and SRAM in bytes and KB.
- (ii) An AHB slave occupies exactly one region. A second AHB slave is to be added with a base address of `0xE000 0000` and a size of **1 MB**. Write the full HSEL equation for both slaves and state the address range of the new slave. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

The AMBA architecture has two bus tiers (see Section 4.3 diagram).

**Comparison table:**

| Feature | AHB | APB |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Bus width | 32 / 64 / 128-bit | 8 / 16 / 32-bit |
| (ii) Pipelining | Yes (address + data phases overlapped) | No (simple 2-phase, no pipelining) |
| (iii) Burst support | Yes (4, 8, 16-beat bursts: INCR4, WRAP8 etc.) | No (single-beat only) |
| (iv) Typical peripherals | RAM, Flash, DMA, external memory bus | UART, Timers, GPIO, I²C, SPI, ADC, PWM, RTC |

**AHB features (1 mark each):**
- Multiple bus masters with arbitration.
- Split and retry transactions for slow slaves.
- Bus matrix for parallel master-to-slave paths.

**APB features (1 mark each):**
- Single master (the AHB-to-APB bridge).
- Latched address and control — low power.
- No response signal needed; no bursts.

**Mark distribution:** 1.5 for diagram, 2 for comparison table, 2 for AHB features, 1.5 for APB features.

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) Flash and SRAM size calculations:**

**Flash range:** `0x0000 0000` to `0x0007 FFFF`
$$ \text{Size}_{\text{Flash}} = 0x0007\text{FFFF} - 0x0000\,0000 + 1 = 0x0008\,0000 \text{ bytes} $$

$$ \text{Size}_{\text{Flash}} = 0x8000 = 8 \times 16^3 = 8 \times 4096 = 32\,768 \text{ bytes} $$

$$ \text{Size}_{\text{Flash}} = 32\,768 \div 1024 = \mathbf{32 \text{ KB}} $$

(1.5 marks)

**SRAM range:** `0x4000 0000` to `0x4000 7FFF`
$$ \text{Size}_{\text{SRAM}} = 0x4000\,7FFF - 0x4000\,0000 + 1 = 0x0000\,8000 \text{ bytes} $$

$$ \text{Size}_{\text{SRAM}} = 0x8000 = 32\,768 \text{ bytes} = \mathbf{16 \text{ KB}} $$

Wait — re-evaluating: `0x7FFF - 0x0000 = 0x7FFF`, so size = `0x8000` = 32 KB. The range `0x4000 0000–0x4000 7FFF` covers **32 KB** of SRAM. (The data sheet says 32 KB on the LPC2148; this matches.)

(1.5 marks)

**(ii) HSEL equation for the new 1 MB slave:**

**Step 1:** Compute $n$ for the 1 MB region:
$$ 1 \text{ MB} = 2^{20} \text{ bytes} \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = 20 $$

**Step 2:** The base address in binary (32 bits, top 20 bits significant):
$$ \text{Base} = 0xE000\,0000 = 1110\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000_2\ \text{(top 12 bits shown)} $$

Full 20 bits: `0xE0000` = `1110 0000 0000 0000 0000`

**Step 3:** HSEL equation:
$$ \text{HSEL}_{\text{new}} = \bigl(\text{HADDR}[31:20] = 0xE00\bigr) $$

**Step 4:** Address range:
$$ \text{HADDR} \in [0xE000\,0000,\ 0xE00F\,FFFF] $$

(4 marks: 1 for n, 1 for base binary, 1 for HSEL equation, 1 for full range)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Many students forget the `+1` when computing the size of a closed range `[start, end]`. If a region is from `0x0000` to `0x0007 FFFF`, the size is `0x0008 0000` bytes, not `0x0007 FFFF`.
> - The HSEL equation ignores the **lower n bits** of HADDR. Forgetting to write `[31:n]` (i.e., truncating) loses 1 mark.
> - AMBA regions **must** be naturally power-of-two aligned. A request for 1 MB starting at `0xE000 0000` is OK because `0xE0000` is 20-bit aligned, but a base of `0xE000 1000` would be a 1 MB but misaligned — the address decoder must reject it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid revision checklist — read this in the last 10 minutes before the exam.**

**Registers (ARM7):**
- 37 physical registers; 31 visible; 6 reserved for hardware.
- R0–R12: general purpose (unbanked except R8–R12 in FIQ).
- R13 (SP) and R14 (LR): banked in all privileged modes.
- R15 (PC) reads as `current + 8` (ARM) or `+ 4` (Thumb).
- R14 holds return address on BL: `LR = PC + 4`.
- CPSR holds N, Z, C, V (bits 31–28), I (bit 7), F (bit 6), T (bit 5), M[4:0] (bits 4–0).
- 5 SPSRs (no User / System SPSR).
- FIQ is the only mode that banks R8–R12 (7 extra private registers).
- 7 modes: usr, fiq, irq, svc, abt, und, sys.

**Mode encodings (M[4:0]):**
- usr = `0b10000`, fiq = `0b10001`, irq = `0b10010`, svc = `0b10011`, abt = `0b10111`, und = `0b11011`, sys = `0b11111`.

**Memory:**
- 4 GB linear address space ($2^{32}$).
- Default little-endian.
- Word access needs 4-byte alignment; halfword needs 2-byte alignment.
