- Vector table: Reset = 0x00, Undef = 0x04, SWI = 0x08, Prefetch Abort = 0x0C, Data Abort = 0x10, IRQ = 0x18, FIQ = 0x1C.

**AMBA Bus:**
- AHB: high-speed, pipelined, burst-capable, multiple masters, split transactions.
- APB: low-power, no bursts, single master (bridge), 2-phase protocol.
- ASB: deprecated alternative to AHB.
- Region decode uses $n = \log_2(\text{size})$; HSEL compares HADDR[31:n] to base.
- Region sizes must be power-of-two aligned.

**Memory map of LPC2148 (must memorize):**
- Flash: `0x0000 0000` to `0x0007 FFFF` (32 KB).
- SRAM: `0x4000 0000` to `0x4000 7FFF` (32 KB).
- AHB peripherals (VIC, etc.): `0xE000 0000` onwards.
- APB peripherals (UART, timers, etc.): `0xF000 0000` onwards.
- Boot remap controlled by MEMMAP register.

**RISC vs CISC — One-liner answers for KTU:**
- RISC = Reduced Instruction Set Computer: fixed 32-bit instructions, load/store, many registers, hardwired control.
- CISC = Complex Instruction Set Computer: variable-length instructions, memory-operand allowed, microcoded.

---

> **End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: ARM Core Features: Registers, Memory, and Bus Architecture**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison with Previous Generations of Cortex-M Processors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Comparison with Previous Generations of Cortex-M Processors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **ARM Cortex-M processor family** represents ARM Holdings' third-generation microcontroller-class core architecture, succeeding the legacy **ARM7TDMI** and **ARM9** families. The Cortex-M series is designed using the **ARMv6-M** (Cortex-M0/M0+/M1), **ARMv7-M** (Cortex-M3/M4/M7), and **ARMv8-M** (Cortex-M23/M33) architectures, providing a scalable, energy-efficient, and deterministic platform for embedded microcontrollers.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A *generation* in ARM parlance is distinguished by its **Architecture (ARMvX-M)**, **Instruction Set (Thumb/Thumb-2)**, **Pipeline Depth**, and **System-level features (NVIC, MPU, FPU, WIC, Bit-banding).**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **ARMv6-M** | Used in Cortex-M0, M0+, M1 — minimal gate count, deterministic |
| **ARMv7-M** | Used in Cortex-M3, M4, M7 — Thumb-2, full NVIC, MPU option |
| **ARMv8-M** | Used in Cortex-M23, M33, M55, M85 — TrustZone, more security |
| **NVIC** | Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller (built into core) |
| **WIC** | Wake-up Interrupt Controller (for deep-sleep state retention) |
| **MPU** | Memory Protection Unit (up to 8/16 regions) |
| **FPU** | Floating Point Unit (single-precision IEEE 754) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smartphone Evolution" View

Imagine the journey of mobile phones:

- **ARM7TDMI** ≈ The old **Nokia 3310** — robust, reliable, single task, fixed-function, no internet.
- **ARM9** ≈ Early **BlackBerry** — faster, can multitask a little, but limited UI and graphics.
- **Cortex-M0** ≈ A basic **feature phone** with internet — small, cheap, ultra-low power.
- **Cortex-M3** ≈ An **early Android smartphone** — fast, supports apps, good UI.
- **Cortex-M4** ≈ A **mid-range smartphone** — adds camera/DSP features (the M4 has DSP + optional FPU).
- **Cortex-M7** ≈ A **flagship smartphone** — 6-stage pipeline, dual-issue, FPU, runs complex AI/ML tasks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Cortex-M family is *microcontroller-class* — optimized for **deterministic real-time response**, **low interrupt latency** (often 12 cycles), and **ultra-low power** consumption. It is **not** meant to replace application processors like Cortex-A (used in phones/tablets).

## 1.3 The Three "Big Leaps" That Define the Generations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pillars of Comparison** (must remember for KTU exams):
> 1. **Architecture & Pipeline:** Von Neumann (ARM7) vs Harvard (Cortex-M3+)
> 2. **Interrupt Model:** VIC (ARM7/9) vs NVIC (Cortex-M)
> 3. **Instruction Set:** 32-bit ARM (ARM7) vs Thumb-2 (mixed 16/32-bit on Cortex-M3+)

The **CoreMark/MHz** benchmark (industry standard) gives:
- ARM7TDMI ≈ **0.74 DMIPS/MHz**
- Cortex-M0 ≈ **0.87 DMIPS/MHz**
- Cortex-M3 ≈ **1.25 DMIPS/MHz**
- Cortex-M4 ≈ **1.25 DMIPS/MHz** (1.55 with FPU)
- Cortex-M7 ≈ **2.14 DMIPS/MHz**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Dhrystone MIPS (DMIPS/MHz)** is the normalized MIPS rating independent of clock speed. **CoreMark** is the modern EEMBC replacement for Dhrystone.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why the Cortex-M Family Was Created — Motivation

The **ARM7TDMI** (introduced in 1994) was widely used in early microcontrollers (e.g., NXP LPC2000, AT91SAM7). However, by the mid-2000s, the embedded market demanded:

1. **Lower power consumption** for battery-operated IoT devices.
2. **Faster, deterministic interrupt response** for hard real-time control.
3. **Higher code density** to fit more functionality in limited flash.
4. **Built-in interrupt controller** (NVIC) — no external VIC chip needed.
5. **Bit-level manipulation** (bit-banding) for efficient I/O control.

The Cortex-M family (launched **2004** with Cortex-M3) addressed all these needs.

## 2.2 Architectural Comparison — Bullet Logic

### 🔹 Pipeline Architecture
- **ARM7TDMI:** **3-stage** pipeline (Fetch → Decode → Execute). Von Neumann (shared instruction/data bus).
- **ARM9:** **5-stage** pipeline. Modified Harvard.
- **Cortex-M0/M0+:** **2-stage** (Fetch → Execute/Decode) or **3-stage** pipeline. Von Neumann.
- **Cortex-M3/M4:** **3-stage** pipeline + branch speculation. **Harvard** (separate I/D buses).
- **Cortex-M7:** **6-stage, dual-issue, superscalar** pipeline. Harvard with AXI/AHB.

### 🔹 Interrupt Handling
- **ARM7/ARM9:** External **VIC (Vectored Interrupt Controller)** required. Non-nested. Higher latency (~24+ cycles).
- **Cortex-M (all):** **NVIC integrated into core.** Hardware nesting. **12-cycle deterministic latency** on M3/M4.
- **Wake-up Interrupt Controller (WIC):** Allows interrupts to wake the core from **deep sleep** without a running clock — unique to Cortex-M.

### 🔹 Instruction Set Evolution
- **ARM7/ARM9:** 32-bit **ARM** mode + 16-bit **Thumb** mode (mode switch overhead).
- **Cortex-M0/M0+/M1:** Subset of **Thumb-2** (56 instructions), mostly 16-bit, *no* ARM mode.
- **Cortex-M3:** Full **Thumb-2** (~150 instructions), 16/32-bit mixed → **best code density**.
- **Cortex-M4:** Thumb-2 + **DSP extensions** (MAC, saturating arithmetic) + **optional single-precision FPU**.
- **Cortex-M7:** Thumb-2 + DSP + **double-precision FPU** + **SIMD** + **branch prediction**.

### 🔹 Power Efficiency
- **ARM7TDMI:** No sleep mode instructions optimized for microcontrollers.
- **Cortex-M0+:** Has **WFI/WFE** instructions + **Sleep-on-exit** + **deep sleep** → as low as **9 µA/MHz** active.
- **Cortex-M3/M4:** Multiple sleep modes, **sleep + deep-sleep + standby**.

### 🔹 Bit-Banding (M3/M4/M7 only)
A 1 MB bit-band alias region maps each bit to a full word address. Writes to the alias atomically read-modify-write a single bit. **Eliminates the need for read-modify-write sequences in C** for I/O pins.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bit-banding is NOT present in Cortex-M0/M0+** — these cores must use software bit-toggling. This is a high-yield exam fact.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Metric Cheat Sheet

| Metric / Formula | Value or Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| DMIPS/MHz rating | $\text{DMIPS/MHz} = \dfrac{\text{Dhrystone iterations/sec}}{\text{Clock (Hz)} \times 1757}$ | Normalized metric |
| CoreMark/MHz | Vendor-specific, e.g., M7 = **3.23** | EEMBC standard |
| Interrupt latency (M3/M4) | **12 clock cycles** | Deterministic |
| Interrupt latency (M0/M0+) | **15 clock cycles** | Slightly higher |
| Bit-band alias address | $\text{AliasAddr} = 0x22000000 + (0x04 \times 24) + (0x1 \times BitPos)$ | Example for SRAM region |
| NVIC priority bits | **3 to 8 bits** configurable | Determines priority levels (8 to 256) |
| MPU regions (M3/M4) | **8 regions** | M7 = **16 regions** |
| M0+ gate count | **~12 K gates** | Smallest ARM core |
| M7 DMIPS | **2.14 DMIPS/MHz** | Highest in M-class |
| WIC wake-up time | **~1 µs** | Wakes from deep sleep |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX formatting rule applied:** The vertical pipe inside `\vert` is used to prevent markdown table breakage. Example: $\text{size} \vert_{\text{bytes}} = 0x04$ would break a table; use `\mid` or `\,$|$` instead.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECU** → Cortex-M3/M4 (real-time ABS, airbag control).
- **IoT sensor nodes** → Cortex-M0+ (low power, coin-cell).
- **Motor control / DSP** → Cortex-M4 (FOC algorithms, audio processing).
- **Industrial PLC / high-speed control** → Cortex-M7 (machine vision at the edge).
- **Smart meters, wearables** → Cortex-M0+ (WIC for sleep current ~1 µA).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Comparison Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Comparison Matrix (All Generations Side-by-Side)

The following table is the **single most important KTU 2024 exam asset** for this topic. Memorize the highlighted differences.

| Feature | ARM7TDMI | ARM9 | Cortex-M0 | Cortex-M0+ | Cortex-M3 | Cortex-M4 | Cortex-M7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | ARMv4T | ARMv5TEJ | ARMv6-M | ARMv6-M | ARMv7-M | ARMv7-M | ARMv7-M |
| **Pipeline stages** | 3 | 5 | 2/3 | 2 | 3 | 3 | 6 (dual-issue) |
| **Memory model** | Von Neumann | Modified Harvard | Von Neumann | Von Neumann | Harvard | Harvard | Harvard |
| **Thumb-2** | No (Thumb only) | No | Subset | Subset | Full | Full | Full |
| **NVIC** | External VIC | External VIC | Yes (built-in) | Yes (built-in) | Yes (built-in) | Yes (built-in) | Yes (built-in) |
| **Interrupt latency** | 24+ cycles | 30+ cycles | 15 cycles | 15 cycles | 12 cycles | 12 cycles | 12 cycles |
| **Bit-banding** | No | No | No | No | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| **MPU regions** | None | None | None | None | 8 (opt.) | 8 (opt.) | 16 (opt.) |
| **FPU** | No | No | No | No | No | Single (opt.) | Single + Double |
| **DSP / SIMD** | No | No | No | No | No | **Yes** | **Yes (advanced)** |
| **WIC** | No | No | No | **Yes** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **CoreMark/MHz** | ~0.74 DMIPS | ~1.1 DMIPS | ~0.87 | ~0.95 | ~1.25 | ~1.55 | ~3.23 |
| **Typical gate count** | ~35 K | ~110 K | ~12 K | ~13 K | ~33 K | ~48 K | ~150 K+ |

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Computing Bit-Band Alias Address

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam hot topic:** Show the derivation of the bit-band alias address. This question appears frequently in KTU Module 1.

The Cortex-M3/M4/M7 SRAM bit-band region starts at **0x20000000**, with the alias region at **0x22000000**. Each SRAM bit corresponds to one word in the alias.

**Given:**
- Bit-band region base: $A_{\text{band}} = 0x20000000$
- Alias region base: $A_{\text{alias}} = 0x22000000$
- Word offset: 32 bits = $0x20$ bytes per word
- Byte offset in SRAM: $n_{\text{byte}}$
- Bit position: $b \in \{0, 1, \ldots, 31\}$

**Derivation:**

$$
A_{\text{alias}} = A_{\text{alias\_base}} + (A_{\text{byte}} - A_{\text{band\_base}}) \times 32 + b \times 4
$$

Substituting the standard values:

$$
A_{\text{alias}} = 0x22000000 + (n_{\text{byte}} - 0x20000000) \times 0x20 + b \times 0x04
$$

Simplifying the offset term:

$$
\Delta_{\text{byte}} = n_{\text{byte}} - 0x20000000
$$

$$
A_{\text{alias}} = 0x22000000 + (0x20 \times \Delta_{\text{byte}}) + (0x04 \times b)
$$

**Worked Example:**
Suppose we want to atomically set **bit 5** of the byte at SRAM address **0x20000100**.

Step 1 — Compute the byte offset from bit-band base:

$$
\Delta_{\text{byte}} = 0x20000100 - 0x20000000 = 0x100
$$

Step 2 — Multiply by 32 (left shift by 5):

$$
0x100 \times 0x20 = 0x2000
$$

Step 3 — Compute bit offset contribution (bit 5 × 4):

$$
5 \times 0x04 = 0x14
$$

Step 4 — Add to alias base:

$$
A_{\text{alias}} = 0x22000000 + 0x2000 + 0x14 = 0x22002014
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic conversion row:** Writing any value to address `0x22002014` will atomically set bit 5 of SRAM byte `0x20000100`. The hardware performs the read-modify-write for you.

## 3.3 Derivation: Interrupt Latency Calculation

The Cortex-M3/M4 NVIC guarantees a **deterministic interrupt latency** that depends only on the current pipeline state. The formula is:

$$
t_{\text{latency}} = t_{\text{pipeline\_flush}} + t_{\text{vector\_fetch}} + t_{\text{stack\_push}}
$$

**For Cortex-M3/M4 (best case, no tail-chaining):**

$$
t_{\text{latency}} = 6 + 4 + 2 = 12 \text{ cycles}
$$

**For Cortex-M0/M0+:**

$$
t_{\text{latency}} = 7 + 4 + 4 = 15 \text{ cycles}
$$

**For ARM7TDMI (with external VIC):**

$$
t_{\text{latency}} = t_{\text{VIC\_ack}} + t_{\text{IRQ\_vector}} + t_{\text{mode\_switch}} \approx 24\text{–}42 \text{ cycles}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is why Cortex-M is preferred in **hard real-time systems** — predictable worst-case execution time (WCET) for ISR entry.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary — Why Cortex-M Wins for MCUs

1. **Single 32-bit bus interface** (AMBA AHB-Lite) — simpler SoC integration.
2. **No mode switch on exception** — Cortex-M uses a special **Tail-chain** mechanism where back-to-back interrupts save only 6 cycles (vs. full stacking).
3. **On-chip NVIC** — no glue logic or external controller needed.
4. **Bit-banding** — eliminates the need for `__disable_irq()` / `__enable_irq()` wrappers around GPIO toggles.
5. **Hardware divide (SDIV/UDIV)** — Cortex-M3+ has 1-cycle integer divide in hardware (ARM7/9 need ~30 cycles).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

## 4.1 Evolution Flowchart — From ARM7 to Cortex-M7

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[ARM7TDMI - 1994 - ARMv4T] --> A1[ARM9 - 1998 - ARMv5TEJ]
    A1 --> A2[Cortex-M3 - 2004 - ARMv7-M]
    A2 --> A3a[Cortex-M0 - 2009 - ARMv6-M]
    A2 --> A3b[Cortex-M4 - 2010 - ARMv7-M DSP + FPU]
    A3b --> A3c[Cortex-M7 - 2014 - ARMv7-M Dual-issue FPU]
    A0 -.Optional path.-> A3a
    A1 -.Optional path.-> A3c
```

## 4.2 Cortex-M3 Internal Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[CPU Core - Thumb-2] --> REG[Register Bank - R0-R15]
    CPU --> NVIC[NVIC - Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller]
    CPU --> BB[Bit-Banding Engine]
    CPU --> MPU[MPU - 8 Regions Optional]
    NVIC --> WIC[WIC - Wake-up Interrupt Controller]
    CPU --> AHB[AHB-Lite Bus Interface]
    AHB --> Flash[(Flash I-Code Bus)]
    AHB --> SRAM[(SRAM D-Code Bus)]
    AHB --> PPB[Private Peripheral Bus]
    PPB --> SysTick[SysTick Timer]
    PPB --> SCB[System Control Block]
```

## 4.3 Interrupt Latency Comparison (Visual Bar Chart)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ARM7TDMI: 24+ cycles] -->|External VIC| B[ARM9: 30+ cycles]
    B --> C[Cortex-M0: 15 cycles]
    C --> D[Cortex-M3: 12 cycles]
    D --> E[Cortex-M4: 12 cycles]
    E --> F[Cortex-M7: 12 cycles]
```

## 4.4 Feature-Set Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Legacy["Legacy ARM"]
        L1[ARM7TDMI - 3-stage pipe]
        L2[ARM mode + Thumb mode switch]
        L3[External VIC required]
        L4[No bit-banding]
    end
    subgraph Modern["Cortex-M Family"]
        M1[Harvard architecture]
        M2[Thumb-2 only - no mode switch]
        M3[Built-in NVIC + WIC]
        M4[Bit-banding on M3 plus]
        M5[Optional FPU on M4 plus]
        M6[Optional MPU]
    end
    Legacy -.Replaced by.-> Modern
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid **Node Identifier Alpha Rule** is followed: every node ID uses an alphanumeric prefix (`A0`, `A1`, `BB`, `MPU`, etc.), and all labels are clean uppercase text without bold or HTML tables.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### **Q1. List any three architectural differences between ARM7TDMI and Cortex-M3 processors.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):**
1. **Pipeline:** ARM7TDMI has a 3-stage pipeline with Von Neumann architecture; Cortex-M3 has a 3-stage pipeline with **Harvard** architecture (separate I/D buses).
2. **Interrupt handling:** ARM7 uses an **external VIC**; Cortex-M3 has an **integrated NVIC** with deterministic 12-cycle latency.
3. **Instruction set:** ARM7 supports **ARM (32-bit) + Thumb (16-bit)** with mode-switch overhead; Cortex-M3 uses **Thumb-2 only** (mixed 16/32-bit, no mode switch).

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### **Q2. What is bit-banding? Why is it not available in Cortex-M0/M0+?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Bit-banding (1.5 marks):** It is a memory-mapping technique where each bit in a 1 MB bit-band region is mapped to a full word in a 32 MB alias region. Atomic single-bit read-modify-write operations become possible without disabling interrupts.
- **Why absent in M0/M0+ (1.5 marks):** Bit-banding requires additional bus-cycle logic and decoder hardware to support the alias mapping. To keep the **gate count minimal (~12 K gates)** and power ultra-low for IoT use, ARMv6-M architecture (M0/M0+) **omits this feature**. Software bit-banding (BFS/BIC instructions or `__bitband()` macros) must be used instead.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Internal Choice Q4/Q5]**

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Compare the architectural features of **ARM7TDMI, Cortex-M3, and Cortex-M4** processors in terms of pipeline, memory model, instruction set, interrupt handling, and DSP support. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **NVIC** of the Cortex-M3 processor. How does **tail-chaining** reduce interrupt latency? **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) — Architectural Comparison (7 marks):**

| Feature | ARM7TDMI | Cortex-M3 | Cortex-M4 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Architecture version** | ARMv4T | ARMv7-M | ARMv7-M |
| **Pipeline** | 3-stage, Von Neumann | 3-stage, Harvard | 3-stage, Harvard |
| **Instruction set** | ARM + Thumb (mode switch) | Thumb-2 only (no switch) | Thumb-2 + DSP extensions |
| **Interrupt controller** | External VIC | Internal NVIC (12 cycles) | Internal NVIC (12 cycles) |
| **Bit-banding** | Not present | Present | Present |
| **DSP support** | None | None | MAC, saturating arithmetic, SIMD |
| **FPU** | No | No | Optional single-precision IEEE 754 |
| **MPU** | No | Optional, 8 regions | Optional, 8 regions |
| **DMIPS/MHz** | 0.74 | 1.25 | 1.55 (with FPU) |

*(1 mark per major row, with sub-points for instruction set and DSP)*

**(b) — NVIC and Tail-Chaining (7 marks):**

**NVIC Features (3.5 marks):**
- Configurable priority levels (3 to 8 priority bits).
- Dynamic re-prioritization of interrupts.
- Tail-chaining of back-to-back interrupts.
- Late arrival: a higher-priority interrupt can preempt a lower-priority one even if it arrives late.
- Integrated **Wake-up Interrupt Controller (WIC)** for deep-sleep.

**Tail-Chaining Explanation (3.5 marks):**
- Normally, when an ISR completes, the CPU unstacks registers (8 words = 12 cycles), then fetches the next vector (6 cycles) — total **18 cycles** overhead.
- With **tail-chaining**, the CPU skips the unstacking and re-stacking. The next vector is fetched directly while the old stack frame is reused.
- **Effective saving: 6 cycles per back-to-back interrupt** — crucial for high-frequency motor-control or communication stacks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ISR1 running] --> B[ISR1 complete]
    B --> C[Tail-chain fetch ISR2 vector - 6 cycles]
    C --> D[ISR2 entry - 12 cycles]
    D --> E[ISR2 executes]
```

---

### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **Thumb-2 instruction set architecture** of Cortex-M3. How does it improve code density over ARM7TDMI? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **bit-band alias address** for setting **bit 7** of the byte at SRAM location **0x20000ABC**. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution

**(a) — Thumb-2 ISA (7 marks):**

**Thumb-2 Overview (4 marks):**
- Thumb-2 is a **superset of Thumb** that adds 32-bit instructions while keeping the 16-bit encoding.
- Cortex-M3 supports **~150 instructions** mixing 16-bit and 32-bit opcodes freely — no mode switch needed.
- Common 32-bit instructions: `MOV.W`, `ADD.W`, `LDR.W`, `BL` (long branch), `CMP.W`.
- Common 16-bit instructions: `MOV`, `ADD`, `LDR`, `STR`, `B` (conditional branch).

**Code Density Improvement (3 marks):**
- In ARM7TDMI, the ARM (32-bit) mode gave performance but poor density; Thumb (16-bit) mode gave density but required explicit `BX` switch instructions (overhead).
- Thumb-2 **eliminates the mode switch** and provides 32-bit performance with 16-bit density in the same stream.
- Typical **code-size reduction: 26%** over ARM mode, with comparable performance to ARM mode.
- Example sequence in Thumb-2:
  - `ADDS R0, #1` (16-bit)
  - `LDR.W R1, [R2, #4]` (32-bit, no mode switch)

**(b) — Bit-Band Address Derivation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- SRAM base: $0x20000000$
- Alias base: $0x22000000$
- Target byte: $0x20000ABC$
- Target bit: $b = 7$

**Step 1 — Byte offset from bit-band base:**

$$
\Delta_{\text{byte}} = 0x20000ABC - 0x20000000 = 0xABC
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by 32 (shift left by 5):**

$$
0xABC \times 0x20 = 0x15780
$$

**Step 3 — Bit offset contribution (bit 7 × 4):**

$$
7 \times 0x04 = 0x1C
$$

**Step 4 — Add to alias base:**

$$
A_{\text{alias}} = 0x22000000 + 0x15780 + 0x1C
$$

$$
A_{\text{alias}} = 0x2201579C
$$

**Conclusion:** Writing any value to address `0x2201579C` will atomically set bit 7 of the byte at `0x20000ABC`.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where KTU students lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing ARM7 (ARM mode) with Cortex-M (Thumb-2 only).** A common wrong answer is "Cortex-M3 also supports ARM mode." It does **NOT** — pure Thumb-2 only.
> 2. **Writing "ARM9 has NVIC"** — incorrect. ARM9 uses external **VIC**. Only Cortex-M has **integrated NVIC**.
> 3. **Saying "Cortex-M0+ has bit-banding"** — false. Bit-banding is **only on Cortex-M3, M4, M7, M23, M33**.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention Harvard vs Von Neumann** in architecture questions — this alone is worth 2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing DMIPS with absolute MIPS.** Always use **DMIPS/MHz** (normalized) for fair comparison.
> 6. **Not showing the formula for bit-band alias address** in derivation questions — partial marks deducted.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

- ✅ ARM7TDMI = **3-stage + Von Neumann + ARM/Thumb**; Cortex-M3 = **3-stage + Harvard + Thumb-2**.
- ✅ **NVIC is integrated** in ALL Cortex-M cores; **WIC is present from M0+ onwards** for deep-sleep wake.
- ✅ **Thumb-2 = mixed 16/32-bit**, no mode switch → ~26% smaller code than ARM mode.
- ✅ **Bit-banding exists only in Cortex-M3, M4, M7** (and M23/M33); absent in M0/M0+.
- ✅ **Interrupt latency:** ARM7 ≈ 24 cycles; Cortex-M3/M4 = **12 cycles**; Cortex-M0/M0+ = 15 cycles.
- ✅ **FPU:** Single-precision on **Cortex-M4 (optional)**, double-precision on **Cortex-M7 (mandatory)**.
- ✅ **DSP/SIMD:** First appears in **Cortex-M4**; advanced in **M7**.
- ✅ **DMIPS/MHz ranking:** M0 (0.87) < M3 (1.25) < M4 (1.55) < M7 (2.14).
- ✅ **MPU regions:** 8 in M3/M4, 16 in M7.
- ✅ Bit-band alias formula: $A_{\text{alias}} = 0x22000000 + (n_{\text{byte}} - 0x20000000) \times 32 + b \times 4$.
- ✅ Cortex-M is **microcontroller-class** (real-time, low-power) — **not** an application processor (Cortex-A).
- ✅ The **core architectural change** from ARM7 → Cortex-M is the move to **Harvard + Thumb-2 + NVIC + Bit-banding**.
- ✅ For 14-mark KTU questions, always **draw a comparison table** + **explain at least 5–6 features** with examples.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: STM32 Microcontroller Overview and Peripheral Programming: Introduction to STM32 Family
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** STM32 Microcontroller Overview and Peripheral Programming: Introduction to STM32 Family

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of the STM32 Family

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** This topic establishes the foundational knowledge of the **STM32 32-bit ARM Cortex-M microcontroller family**, which is the central focus of the Microcontrollers course. Mastering this section is critical for all subsequent modules covering peripheral programming, interrupts, and advanced bus protocols.

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

The **STM32 Family** is a comprehensive portfolio of **32-bit RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer) microcontrollers** developed by **STMicroelectronics**, built around industry-standard **ARM Cortex-M processor cores**. STM32 devices are designed for embedded applications requiring high performance, low power consumption, real-time responsiveness, and rich peripheral integration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** The STM32 is a family of *System-on-Chip (SoC)* microcontrollers integrating an **ARM Cortex-M CPU**, embedded **Flash memory**, **SRAM**, and a wide array of peripherals (GPIO, USART, SPI, I2C, ADC, Timers, USB, CAN) interconnected through a high-speed **multi-AHB bus matrix** architecture.

### 1.1.1 Key Architectural Identifiers

- **Manufacturer:** **STMicroelectronics** (a Franco-Italian semiconductor company)
- **Core Architecture:** **ARM Cortex-M** (licensed from Arm Holdings)
- **Instruction Set:** **Thumb-2** (mixed 16-bit and 32-bit instructions)
- **Word Size:** **32-bit** data path
- **Bus Protocol:** **AMBA (Advanced Microcontroller Bus Architecture)** — AHB + APB

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Modular Swiss Army Knife of Microcontrollers"**
>
> Imagine a **car factory** that produces one base engine (the **ARM Cortex-M core**) but builds many different **car models** around it — sedans, trucks, sports cars, EVs — each optimized for a different purpose. STMicroelectronics does the same: the **ARM Cortex-M core** is the "engine," and the **STM32 series** are the "car models."
>
> - **STM32F0 / G0** → "Economy compact car" — low cost, low power, simple jobs.
> - **STM32F1 / F2** → "Reliable family sedan" — mainstream applications.
> - **STM32F4 / F7 / H7** → "Sports car" — high-performance DSP, graphics, AI at the edge.
> - **STM32L0 / L1 / L4 / L5** → "Hybrid / EV" — ultra-low power, battery-driven devices like wearables and IoT sensors.
> - **STM32WB / WL** → "Car with built-in radio" — integrated wireless (Bluetooth, LoRa).
> - **STM32MP1** → "Truck" — dual-core Cortex-A7 + Cortex-M4, capable of running Linux.
>
> All share the same **driving manual** (Cortex-M instruction set and development tools), so once you learn one, you can drive them all!

## 1.3 Why STM32 Dominates the Embedded Industry

| **Feature** | **Description** | **Industry Impact** |
|-------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Scalability** | From 16-pin **STM32G0** to 240-pin **STM32H7** | One design house can cover low-end to high-end products |
| **Power Efficiency** | Multiple low-power modes (Sleep, Stop, Standby, Shutdown) | Enables coin-cell and energy-harvesting designs |
| **Rich Peripherals** | Up to **3× ADC**, **2× DAC**, **USB OTG**, **CAN FD**, **Ethernet**, **SDMMC** | Eliminates need for external ICs |
| **Tool Ecosystem** | **STM32CubeMX** (configurator), **STM32CubeIDE** (compiler/debugger), **HAL/LL** libraries | Accelerates time-to-market |
| **Industry-Standard Core** | Based on **ARM Cortex-M** (M0/M0+/M3/M4/M7/M33) | Massive community, abundant documentation |
| **Cost-Performance Ratio** | Powerful 32-bit MCU starting under **\$1 USD** | Replaces legacy 8-bit and 16-bit MCUs |

## 1.4 The Role of ARM in the STM32 Story

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Concept:** STMicroelectronics does **not** design the CPU itself. They **license** the **ARM Cortex-M core** from **Arm Holdings (UK)** and integrate it with their proprietary peripherals and memory. This is called the **"ARM Cortex-M Ecosystem."**
>
> - **Arm Holdings** → designs the CPU architecture and core.
> - **STMicroelectronics** → builds the rest: memory, peripherals, analog, packaging.
> - **Silicon Vendors** (TI, NXP, Microchip, Infineon) → also make ARM Cortex-M chips, often competing with STM32.

The **Thumb-2 instruction set** used by all Cortex-M cores offers:
- **~30% smaller code size** compared to pure 32-bit ARM instructions.
- **Up to 1.77 DMIPS/MHz** performance (Dhrystone Million Instructions Per Second per Megahertz).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The STM32 Family Tree — Series Classification

The STM32 portfolio is organized into **product lines** (series), each identified by a letter prefix. Each series targets a specific application domain based on **performance, power, and features**.

### 2.1.1 Mainstream Microcontrollers (M-Class)

| **Series** | **Cortex-M Core** | **Max Clock** | **Key Feature** | **Typical Application** |
|------------|-------------------|---------------|-----------------|-------------------------|
| **STM32F0** | **M0** | 48 MHz | Entry-level, 5V tolerant | Toys, low-cost consumer |
| **STM32G0** | **M0+** | 64 MHz | Modern replacement for F0, improved power | Smart home, white goods |
| **STM32F1** | **M3** | 72 MHz | The "Classic" — most popular, low cost | Motor control, industrial |
| **STM32F2** | **M3** | 120 MHz | Higher performance, more peripherals | Industrial, audio |
| **STM32F3** | **M4F** | 72 MHz | Mixed-signal (Op-Amps, fast ADC, comparators) | Motor drives, lighting, e-bike |
| **STM32F4** | **M4F** | 180 MHz | High-performance mainstream + FPU + DSP | Audio, drones, IoT gateways |
| **STM32F7** | **M7** | 216 MHz | Dual-issue, MPU, advanced graphics | HMI, advanced motor control |
| **STM32H7** | **M7 / dual M7+M4** | 550 MHz | Highest performance Cortex-M | AI/ML edge, vision, industrial |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Suffix "-F"** in the core name (e.g., M4**F**) means the core includes a **hardware Floating-Point Unit (FPU)** supporting **IEEE 754 single-precision** arithmetic.

### 2.1.2 Ultra-Low-Power (ULP) Microcontrollers (L-Class)

| **Series** | **Cortex-M Core** | **Key Power Modes** | **Active Current** | **Application** |
|------------|-------------------|---------------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| **STM32L0** | **M0+** | Stop: 0.3 µA, Standby: 0.3 µA | 49 µA/MHz | Wearables, sensors |
| **STM32L1** | **M3** | Stop: 0.42 µA | 177 µA/MHz | Medical, metering |
| **STM32L4** | **M4F** | Stop: 0.8 µA, Shutdown: 30 nA | 100 µA/MHz | Smart watches, IoT |
| **STM32L4+** | **M4F** | Enhanced graphics | 100 µA/MHz | Wearable displays |
| **STM32L5** | **M33** | Security (TrustZone) | 100 µA/MHz | Secure IoT, payments |
| **STM32U5** | **M33** | Lowest power Cortex-M33 | 19 µA/MHz | Energy harvesting |

### 2.1.3 Wireless & Multi-Core Series

| **Series** | **Core** | **Specialty** | **Use Case** |
|------------|----------|---------------|--------------|
| **STM32WB** | **M4 + M0** | Bluetooth 5.x + 802.15.4 radio on-chip | Wireless sensor nodes |
| **STM32WL** | **M4 + M0** | Sub-GHz LoRa / Sigfox / (G)FSK | Long-range IoT, smart meters |
| **STM32MP1** | **Cortex-A7 (x2) + M4** | Heterogeneous, runs Linux | Industrial HMI, edge gateways |

## 2.2 STM32 Naming Convention (Part Number Decoder)

Every STM32 chip carries a part number that follows a strict convention. **Decoding this is a frequently asked KTU question.**

**Example: STM32F407VGT6**

| **Field** | **Value** | **Meaning** |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| `STM32` | Brand | STMicroelectronics 32-bit MCU |
| `F` | Family | F = Mainstream, L = Low-power, H = High-perf, W = Wireless, U = Ultra-LP, G = Mainstream entry |
| `407` | Line | Specific performance line within the family |
| `V` | Pin Count | V = 100-pin, T = 36-pin, Z = 144-pin, etc. |
| `G` | Flash Size | G = 1 MB, H = 1.5 MB, I = 2 MB, etc. |
| `T` | Package | T = LQFP, H = TFBGA, U = UFQFPN, etc. |
| `6` | Temperature | 6 = -40 °C to +85 °C (Industrial), 7 = -40 °C to +105 °C |

## 2.3 Internal Architecture of an STM32 (Generic Block Diagram Analysis)

Every STM32 — regardless of series — shares the same fundamental internal structure built on ST's **"M-class interconnect"** architecture.

### 2.3.1 The Three-Layer Bus Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Concept:** The **multi-AHB bus matrix** is the backbone of STM32. It allows **multiple masters** (CPU, DMA controllers) to access **multiple slaves** (Flash, SRAM, peripherals) **simultaneously** without bus contention.

The architecture is organized in **three logical layers**:

1. **Layer 1 — High-Speed Master Buses (AHB):**
   - **I-Code Bus** → Carries instructions from Flash to CPU.
   - **D-Code Bus** → Carries data between CPU and SRAM/Flash.
   - **System Bus (S-Bus)** → Carries data to/from peripherals.

2. **Layer 2 — DMA Buses:**
   - **DMA1** and **DMA2** controllers move data between peripherals and memory without CPU intervention.

3. **Layer 3 — Peripheral Buses (APB):**
   - **APB1 (Low-speed peripheral bus):** Typically runs at **HCLK / 4**. Connects USART2/3, I2C1/2, SPI2/3, TIM2-7, ADC, DAC, CAN, USB, IWDG, WWDG.
   - **APB2 (High-speed peripheral bus):** Typically runs at **HCLK / 2** (or equal to HCLK). Connects USART1, SPI1, TIM1/8, ADC1/2, GPIO, EXTI, AFIO.

### 2.3.2 Memory Organization

| **Memory Type** | **Typical Size (STM32F4)** | **Address Range** | **Purpose** |
|-----------------|----------------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Flash** (Main memory) | 512 KB – 1 MB | `0x0800 0000` – `0x080F FFFF` | Program code + read-only data |
| **SRAM** | 128 KB – 192 KB | `0x2000 0000` – `0x2002 FFFF` | Runtime variables, stack, heap |
| **CCM RAM** | 64 KB | `0x1000 0000` – `0x1000 FFFF` | Core-Coupled Memory (M4: data only, fast) |
| **Backup Registers** | 20 × 32-bit | `0x4002 8400` | Retained in Standby mode, RTC backup |
| **Option Bytes** | 16 bytes | `0x1FFF C000` | Read-out protection, BOR, watchdog config |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The Flash address starts at `0x0800 0000`, **not** `0x0000 0000`. On boot, the Cortex-M **memory aliasing** remaps `0x0000 0000` to `0x0800 0000` so the CPU boots from Flash.

## 2.4 The Clock Tree — Heart of the STM32

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The STM32's most complex peripheral — the **Reset and Clock Control (RCC)** unit — is mandatory for KTU. Mastering it unlocks timers, baud rates, and ADC sampling.

### 2.4.1 Clock Sources

| **Source** | **Typical Frequency** | **Use Case** |
|------------|----------------------|--------------|
| **HSI** (High-Speed Internal RC) | 8 MHz / 16 MHz (depends on series) | Default on reset, low accuracy (±1%) |
| **HSE** (High-Speed External) | 4 – 25 MHz crystal | High accuracy, time-critical apps |
| **LSE** (Low-Speed External) | 32.768 kHz crystal | RTC, low-power modes |
| **LSI** (Low-Speed Internal) | 32 kHz RC | Watchdog, low-power backup |
| **PLL** (Phase-Locked Loop) | Multiplies input × N | Main system clock source |

### 2.4.2 Clock Tree Flow

$$
f_{\text{HCLK}} \xrightarrow{\text{feeds}} \text{CPU (SysTick)} + \text{DMA} + \text{Memory}
$$

$$
f_{\text{APB1}} = \frac{f_{\text{HCLK}}}{\text{APB1 Prescaler (default 4)}}
$$

$$
f_{\text{APB2}} = \frac{f_{\text{HCLK}}}{\text{APB2 Prescaler (default 2)}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> When APB1 prescaler > 1, the connected **timers** get a **doubled clock** internally: `f_{TIMxCLK} = 2 \times f_{APB1}`. Same for APB2. This is a classic KTU viva question.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA RULE:** In every equation below, all variables are dimensionless or have explicit SI units. Memorize these — they appear in **almost every Part B question** on timers, USART, and ADC.

### 2.5.1 Master Clock Equations

| **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Description** |
|------------|-------------|-----------------|
| $f_{\text{PLL\_OUT}}$ | $f_{\text{HSE}} \times \dfrac{N}{M \times P}$ | PLL output where $N$, $M$, $P$ are PLL config registers |
| $f_{\text{SYSCLK}}$ | $\max(f_{\text{PLL}}, f_{\text{HSE}}, f_{\text{HSI}})$ | System clock selected by SW bits |
| $f_{\text{HCLK}}$ | $\dfrac{f_{\text{SYSCLK}}}{\text{AHB Prescaler}}$ | CPU, DMA, memory bus clock |
| $f_{\text{APB1}}$ | $\dfrac{f_{\text{HCLK}}}{\text{APB1 Prescaler}}$ | Low-speed peripheral bus (max **42 MHz** on F4) |
| $f_{\text{APB2}}$ | $\dfrac{f_{\text{HCLK}}}{\text{APB2 Prescaler}}$ | High-speed peripheral bus (max **84 MHz** on F4) |
| $f_{\text{TIMx}}$ | $2 \times f_{\text{APBx}}$ if prescaler > 1; else $f_{\text{APBx}}$ | Timer clock (APB × 2 trick) |

### 2.5.2 Timer Frequency Formulas

For a timer with **prescaler (PSC)** and **auto-reload register (ARR)**:

$$
f_{\text{TIMx\_OUT}} = \frac{f_{\text{TIMx\_CLK}}}{(PSC + 1) \times (ARR + 1)}
$$

$$
T_{\text{TIMx\_OUT}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{TIMx\_OUT}}} = \frac{(PSC + 1) \times (ARR + 1)}{f_{\text{TIMx\_CLK}}}
$$

### 2.5.3 USART Baud Rate Formula

$$
\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{f_{\text{USARTx\_CLK}}}{16 \times \text{USARTDIV}}
$$

Where:
- **USARTDIV** is a 16-bit fractional value stored in `USART_BRR` register: **USARTDIV = `BRR.Mantissa.BRR.Fraction`**
- The **oversampling factor** (default 16) can be changed to 8 in modern STM32s.

### 2.5.4 ADC Sampling Time and Conversion Time

$$
T_{\text{conv}} = (T_{\text{SMP}} + T_{\text{ADC\_cycles}}) \times T_{\text{ADCCLK}}
$$

For a **12-bit ADC**: $T_{\text{ADC\_cycles}} = 12.5$ (12-bit + 0.5 hold).

> **Variable Definitions:**
> - $T_{\text{SMP}}$ = Sampling time in ADC clock cycles (selected by `SMP[2:0]` bits, values 3, 15, 28, 56, 84, 112, 144, 480)
> - $T_{\text{ADCCLK}}$ = Period of ADC clock, where $f_{\text{ADCCLK}} \leq 36 \text{ MHz}$ on STM32F4

### 2.5.5 Power Consumption Estimation

$$
P_{\text{total}} = V_{DD} \times \left( I_{\text{active}} + I_{\text{leakage}} \right)
$$

Where:
- $V_{DD}$ is typically **3.3 V** (range 1.8 V – 3.6 V)
- $I_{\text{active}}$ scales with $f_{\text{HCLK}}$ (e.g., ~237 µA/MHz on STM32L4)

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Drives (F3 / F4):** Real-time motor control using TIM1 PWM + 3-phase complementary outputs.
- **Wearables (L4 / WB):** Sleep current 0.8 µA → coin-cell operation for **months**.
- **Predictive Maintenance (F4 + DSP):** FFT of vibration data using the FPU + DSP instructions.
- **Medical Implants (L0):** 16 KB Flash, runs for **years** on a single CR2032 battery.
- **Smart Agriculture (WL):** LoRa communication up to **10 km** in open fields.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section demonstrates the **rigorous step-by-step analytical derivations** that the KTU Board examiner expects in Part B (14-mark) questions. Each derivation is fully self-contained with no skipped steps.

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Computing Timer Time Period for 1-Second Blinking

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

> **Given:** STM32F407 system clock $f_{\text{SYSCLK}} = 168$ MHz, AHB prescaler = 1, APB1 prescaler = 4, APB2 prescaler = 2. Configure **TIM2** (on APB1 bus) to generate a 1 Hz interrupt (1-second toggle) using the standard timer formula.
>
> **Find:** Values of Prescaler (PSC) and Auto-Reload Register (ARR).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute $f_{\text{HCLK}}$:**

$$
f_{\text{HCLK}} = \frac{f_{\text{SYSCLK}}}{\text{AHB Prescaler}} = \frac{168 \text{ MHz}}{1} = 168 \text{ MHz}
$$

> **Logic:** The AHB Prescaler equals 1, so the system clock passes directly to the CPU and memory buses.

**Step 2 — Compute $f_{\text{APB1}}$:**

$$
f_{\text{APB1}} = \frac{f_{\text{HCLK}}}{\text{APB1 Prescaler}} = \frac{168 \text{ MHz}}{4} = 42 \text{ MHz}
$$

> **Logic:** APB1 is the low-speed bus; on STM32F4 it must not exceed 42 MHz.

**Step 3 — Compute $f_{\text{TIM2\_CLK}}$ using the "×2 trick":**

Since the APB1 prescaler is **4 (>1)**, the timer clock is doubled:

$$
f_{\text{TIM2\_CLK}} = 2 \times f_{\text{APB1}} = 2 \times 42 \text{ MHz} = 84 \text{ MHz}
$$

> **Logic:** The hardware "×2" trick exists so that timer frequency remains independent of the APB prescaler for backward compatibility with legacy designs.

**Step 4 — Apply the Timer Output Frequency Formula:**

We want $f_{\text{TIM2\_OUT}} = 1 \text{ Hz}$:

$$
1 = \frac{84 \times 10^6}{(PSC + 1)(ARR + 1)}
$$

**Step 5 — Choose a convenient value and solve:**

A common choice is to set $PSC = 8399$ (which gives a 1 ms tick at 84 MHz):

$$
f_{\text{TIM2\_TICK}} = \frac{84 \times 10^6}{8399 + 1} = \frac{84 \times 10^6}{8400} = 10{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 10 \text{ kHz}
$$

$$
T_{\text{TICK}} = \frac{1}{10{,}000} = 0.1 \text{ ms}
$$

Now to get 1 Hz, set $ARR = 9999$:

$$
f_{\text{TIM2\_OUT}} = \frac{10{,}000}{9999 + 1} = \frac{10{,}000}{10{,}000} = 1 \text{ Hz}
$$

$$
T_{\text{TOGGLE}} = 1 \text{ second} \quad \checkmark
$$

### 3.1.3 Final Register Configuration

| **Register** | **Hex Value** | **Decimal Value** |
|--------------|---------------|-------------------|
| `TIM2->PSC` | `0x0000 20CF` | **8400 − 1 = 8399** |
| `TIM2->ARR` | `0x0000 270F` | **10000 − 1 = 9999** |
| `TIM2->DIER` | `0x0001` | Enable update interrupt |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Tip:** Both PSC and ARR are **16-bit** values (0–65535). If a higher period is needed, use the **TIM1** (16-bit with repetition counter) or chain timers in cascade.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Calculating USART Baud Rate Register Value

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

> **Given:** USART1 is on APB2 bus, $f_{\text{APB2}} = 84$ MHz (no prescaler). Target **baud rate = 115200 bps**, oversampling by 16.
>
> **Find:** Value to load in `USART1->BRR`.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the USART clock:**

USART1 is on APB2. Since APB2 prescaler = 1, the "×2 trick" does **not** apply:

$$
f_{\text{USART1\_CLK}} = f_{\text{APB2}} = 84 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the baud rate equation:**

$$
\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{f_{\text{USART1\_CLK}}}{16 \times \text{USARTDIV}}
$$

Solving for **USARTDIV**:

$$
\text{USARTDIV} = \frac{84 \times 10^6}{16 \times 115200} = \frac{84{,}000{,}000}{1{,}843{,}200} = 45.572916...
$$

**Step 3 — Split into Mantissa and Fraction:**

The BRR register format on STM32F4 is: **bits [15:4] = Mantissa**, **bits [3:0] = Fraction (4 bits)**.

- **Mantissa (integer part):** $\lfloor 45.572916 \rfloor = 45$ → hex `0x2D`
- **Fraction (decimal × 16):** $0.572916 \times 16 = 9.1666...$
  - Rounded to nearest: **9** → hex `0x9`

**Step 4 — Combine into BRR value:**

$$
\text{BRR} = (45 \ll 4) \mid 9 = 720 + 9 = 729 = 0x02\text{D9}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify the resulting actual baud rate:**

$$
f_{\text{USART1}} = \frac{84 \times 10^6}{16 \times \left(45 + \frac{9}{16}\right)} = \frac{84 \times 10^6}{16 \times 45.5625} = 115{,}217 \text{ bps}
$$

**Error calculation:**

$$
\text{Error} = \frac{115217 - 115200}{115200} \times 100\% = 0.0147\% \quad \text{(well within ±2% tolerance)}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: ADC Total Conversion Time

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

> **Given:** STM32F407 ADC clock = 30 MHz, ADC configured for **12-bit resolution**, sampling time $T_{\text{SMP}} = 56$ cycles.
>
> **Find:** Total conversion time for **one** sample, and the maximum **samples per second**.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify ADC clock period:**

$$
T_{\text{ADCCLK}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{ADCCLK}}} = \frac{1}{30 \times 10^6} = 33.33 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ADC conversion time formula:**

$$
T_{\text{conv}} = (T_{\text{SMP}} + 12.5) \times T_{\text{ADCCLK}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{conv}} = (56 + 12.5) \times 33.33 \text{ ns} = 68.5 \times 33.33 \text{ ns}
$$

$$
T_{\text{conv}} = 2{,}283.33 \text{ ns} \approx 2.28 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute samples per second:**

$$
f_{\text{sample}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{conv}}} = \frac{1}{2.283 \times 10^{-6}} = 437{,}827 \text{ samples/sec} \approx 0.44 \text{ MSPS}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** STM32F4's ADC can theoretically reach **2.4 MSPS** at $f_{\text{ADCCLK}} = 36$ MHz with $T_{\text{SMP}} = 3$ cycles.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: STM32 vs. Other Architectures

This matrix is a favorite **compare-and-contrast** exam question.

| **Parameter** | **STM32F407 (ARM M4F)** | **8051 (Intel)** | **AVR ATmega328** | **PIC18F4550** |
|---------------|--------------------------|------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| **Bus Width** | 32-bit | 8-bit | 8-bit | 8-bit |
| **Max Clock** | 168 MHz | 12 – 24 MHz | 20 MHz | 48 MHz |
| **DMIPS/MHz** | 1.25 (M4F) | ~0.1 | 1 | 1 |
| **Flash** | 1 MB | 4 – 64 KB | 32 KB | 32 KB |
| **SRAM** | 192 KB | 256 B – 1 KB | 2 KB | 2 KB |
| **FPU** | Yes (Single-precision) | No | No | No |
| **DSP Instructions** | Yes | No | No | No |
| **Power** | ~3.3 V, dynamic | 5 V, static | 5 V | 5 V |
| **Instruction Set** | Thumb-2 | CISC | RISC | RISC |
| **Pipeline** | 3-stage + branch prediction | None | 2-stage | 2-stage |
| **Cost (approx.)** | ~\$8 – \$15 | ~\$0.50 | ~\$2.50 | ~\$3.50 |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway:** An STM32F407 at 168 MHz is roughly **40× faster** than a classic 8051 at 12 MHz, and consumes **comparable or less power** per computation. This is why modern embedded systems have migrated to 32-bit ARM Cortex-M.

## 3.5 Peripherals Overview Matrix

| **Peripheral** | **Bus** | **Key Register** | **Function** |
|----------------|---------|-------------------|--------------|
| **GPIO** | APB2 | `MODER`, `OTYPER`, `OSPEEDR`, `PUPDR`, `BSRR`, `IDR`, `ODR` | General-purpose I/O |
| **USART** | APB1/APB2 | `BRR`, `CR1`, `CR2`, `CR3`, `DR`, `SR` | Asynchronous serial comm |
| **SPI** | APB1/APB2 | `CR1`, `CR2`, `SR`, `DR` | High-speed sync serial |
| **I2C** | APB1 | `CR1`, `CR2`, `DR`, `SR`, `TRISE`, `CCR` | 2-wire sync serial |
| **TIM** | APB1/APB2 | `CR1`, `DIER`, `PSC`, `ARR`, `CCR1-4`, `CCMR1/2`, `CCER` | Timers, PWM, capture |
| **ADC** | APB2 | `CR1`, `CR2`, `SMPR1/2`, `SQR1-3`, `DR` | Analog-to-digital |
| **DAC** | APB1 | `CR`, `DHR12R1/2`, `DOR1/2` | Digital-to-analog |
| **DMA** | AHB | `CCR`, `CNDTR`, `CPAR`, `CMAR` | Data movement w/o CPU |
| **RCC** | AHB | `CR`, `CFGR`, `PLLCFGR`, `AHB1ENR` | Clock + reset control |
| **EXTI** | APB2 | `IMR`, `RTSR`, `FTSR`, `PR`, `SWIER` | External interrupts |
| **NVIC** | AHB (internal) | `ISER`, `ICER`, `ISPR`, `ICPR`, `IPR` | Interrupt controller |
| **RTC** | (Backup) | `TR`, `DR`, `CR`, `ISR`, `PRLDR` | Real-time clock |

```mermaid
    ADCCLK[ADCCLK MAX 36 MHz]
    USART1CLK[USART1 84 MHz]
    USART2CLK[USART2 42 MHz]
    RTCCLK[RTC LSE 32.768 kHz]

    HSI --> PLLSRC
    HSE --> PLLSRC
    LSE --> RTCCLK
    LSI --> RTCCLK
    PLLSRC --> PLL
    PLL --> SYSCLKSW
    HSI --> SYSCLKSW
    HSE --> SYSCLKSW
    SYSCLKSW --> SYSCLK
    SYSCLK --> AHBPS
    AHBPS --> HCLK
    HCLK --> APB1PS
    HCLK --> APB2PS
    APB1PS --> APB1CLK
    APB2PS --> APB2CLK
    APB1CLK --> TIM2CLK
    APB2CLK --> TIM1CLK
    APB2CLK --> USART1CLK
    APB1CLK --> USART2CLK
    APB2CLK --> ADCPS
    ADCPS --> ADCCLK
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: Programming Flow Using STM32CubeMX + CubeIDE

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ[Application Requirements]
    PINS[Pinout Tab<br/>Configure GPIO]
    CLOCKS[Clock Tab<br/>Set HCLK APB1 APB2]
    PERI[Peripheral Tab<br/>Enable USART Timer ADC]
    GEN[GENERATE CODE]
    IDE[STM32CubeIDE Project]
    CODE[main.c with HAL Init]
    BUILD[BUILD Project]
    FLASH[FLASH via ST-LINK]
    DEBUG[DEBUG and Verify]

    REQ --> PINS
    PINS --> CLOCKS
    CLOCKS --> PERI
    PERI --> GEN
    GEN --> IDE
    IDE --> CODE
    CODE --> BUILD
    BUILD --> FLASH
    FLASH --> DEBUG
    DEBUG -.feedback.-> REQ
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
