# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Question 1: Define the STM32 microcontroller family. Mention the manufacturer and the core architecture. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Set A]**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The **STM32** is a family of **32-bit RISC microcontrollers** manufactured by **STMicroelectronics**, built around the **ARM Cortex-M processor core** licensed from Arm Holdings. The family spans from low-cost Cortex-M0+ chips (STM32G0) to high-performance Cortex-M7 dual-core devices (STM32H7), with key features including Thumb-2 instruction set, multi-AHB bus matrix, integrated Flash/SRAM, and a rich set of peripherals (GPIO, USART, SPI, I2C, ADC, Timers, USB, CAN). STM32 is widely used in industrial control, IoT, motor drives, and consumer electronics. **(3 marks)**

### **Question 2: List any three different STM32 series with their Cortex-M core and one distinguishing feature. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

> **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per row):**
>
> | **Series** | **Cortex-M Core** | **Distinguishing Feature** |
> |------------|-------------------|----------------------------|
> | STM32F0 | M0 | Lowest cost, 5V-tolerant I/O, ideal for white goods |
> | STM32F4 | M4F | High performance, 180 MHz, hardware FPU + DSP, common in drones & audio |
> | STM32L4 | M4F | Ultra-low power (100 µA/MHz), Stop mode 0.8 µA, used in wearables |
> | STM32WB | M4 + M0 | Integrated Bluetooth 5 wireless SoC |
> | STM32H7 | M7 (550 MHz) | Highest performance Cortex-M with MPU, used in edge AI |

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Every Part B question offers **internal choice** — you may answer **either** "Question A" **or** "Question B". Both questions are designed to assess CO2 of the syllabus at **Apply + Analyze** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): STM32 Architecture, Clock Tree & Timer Configuration**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2, Set B]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **internal architecture of the STM32F4 microcontroller**. Identify the **buses** and explain how the **multi-AHB bus matrix** enables concurrent peripheral access. **[(7 marks)]**
>
> **(b)** For an STM32F407 system with $f_{\text{SYSCLK}} = 168$ MHz, AHB prescaler = 1, APB1 prescaler = 4, derive the values of **PSC** and **ARR** required to configure **TIM3** to generate a **500 ms delay**. Assume TIM3 is on APB1. **[(7 marks)]**

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The **STM32F407** internal architecture is built on ST's **multi-AHB bus matrix**, which is the backbone that allows **multiple bus masters** to communicate with **multiple slaves** simultaneously.

**1. CPU Core — ARM Cortex-M4F** [1 mark]
- 32-bit, Thumb-2 ISA, hardware FPU (single precision), DSP extensions, NVIC, MPU optional.

**2. Three Buses from CPU to Bus Matrix** [1 mark]
- **I-Code Bus** — Instruction fetches from Flash, optimized for low-latency read.
- **D-Code Bus** — Data and debug access to Flash/SRAM.
- **System Bus (S-Bus)** — Data access to peripherals and SRAM.

**3. Multi-AHB Bus Matrix** [2 marks]
- Connects **4 masters** (CPU via 3 buses + DMA1 + DMA2) to **multiple slaves** (Flash interface, SRAM, AHB-to-APB bridges, GPIO ports).
- Provides **parallelism**: e.g., CPU can fetch from Flash while DMA transfers data from USART to SRAM — no bus contention.
- Supports **burst transfers, bus parking, and round-robin** arbitration for fairness.

**4. Peripheral Buses (APB)** [1 mark]
- **APB1 Bridge (42 MHz max):** USART2/3, I2C1/2, SPI2/3, TIM2-7, CAN, USB, DAC, IWDG.
- **APB2 Bridge (84 MHz max):** USART1/6, SPI1, TIM1/8, ADC1-3, GPIOA-I, EXTI, AFIO.

**5. Memories** [1 mark]
- **Flash (1 MB)** at `0x0800 0000`, **SRAM (192 KB)** at `0x2000 0000`, **CCM RAM (64 KB)** at `0x1000 0000`, **Backup Registers (20 × 32-bit)** for RTC retention.

**6. NVIC and EXTI** [1 mark]
- **NVIC** handles nested interrupts with priority grouping.
- **EXTI** maps GPIO pins to interrupt lines.

**Concurrent Access Example (Why bus matrix matters):**
While the CPU executes instructions from Flash via the I-Code bus, the DMA controller can transfer ADC samples from ADC1's data register to SRAM via the System bus — both happen in parallel, doubling system throughput.

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Compute $f_{\text{HCLK}}$:**

$$
f_{\text{HCLK}} = \frac{168 \text{ MHz}}{1} = 168 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $f_{\text{APB1}}$:**

$$
f_{\text{APB1}} = \frac{168 \text{ MHz}}{4} = 42 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the "×2 trick" for TIM3 (APB1 prescaler > 1):**

$$
f_{\text{TIM3\_CLK}} = 2 \times 42 \text{ MHz} = 84 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 4 — Required timer period = 500 ms:**

$$
f_{\text{desired}} = \frac{1}{0.5} = 2 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the formula:**

$$
2 = \frac{84 \times 10^6}{(PSC + 1)(ARR + 1)}
$$

$$
(PSC + 1)(ARR + 1) = 42{,}000{,}000
$$

**Step 6 — Choose a convenient PSC. Set $PSC = 8399$ for 1 ms ticks:**

$$
(8400)(ARR + 1) = 42{,}000{,}000
$$

$$
ARR + 1 = 5{,}000
$$

$$
\boxed{ARR = 4999, \quad PSC = 8399}
$$

**Step 7 — Verify:**

$$
T = \frac{(8400)(5000)}{84 \times 10^6} = \frac{42{,}000{,}000}{84{,}000{,}000} = 0.5 \text{ s} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): STM32 Family, Peripherals & Naming Convention**

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2, Set B (Internal Choice Alternative)]**
>
> **(a)** Classify the **STM32 family** into its major series. Compare the **mainstream (F-class)**, **ultra-low-power (L-class)**, and **high-performance (H-class)** series in terms of: Cortex-M core, maximum clock, and one typical application. **[(7 marks)]**
>
> **(b)** Decode the part number **STM32F407VGT6** in detail, identifying the family, line, pin count, Flash size, package, and temperature grade. List the **buses** to which the following peripherals are connected: **USART1, USART2, I2C1, ADC1, TIM1, TIM3**. **[(7 marks)]**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

| **Aspect** | **Mainstream (F-class)** | **Ultra-Low-Power (L-class)** | **High-Performance (H-class)** |
|------------|--------------------------|--------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Representative Series** | STM32F0, F1, F2, F3, F4, F7 | STM32L0, L1, L4, L5, U5 | STM32H7 |
| **Cortex-M Core** | M0 / M0+ / M3 / M4F / M7 | M0+ / M3 / M4F / M33 | M7 (single or dual-core) |
| **Max Clock** | 48 – 216 MHz | 32 – 80 MHz | Up to **550 MHz** |
| **FPU/DSP** | M4F/M7 have FPU + DSP | M4F has FPU + DSP | M7 has **double-precision FPU** + DSP |
| **Active Current** | ~200 µA/MHz | ~20 – 100 µA/MHz | ~300 µA/MHz |
| **Stop Mode Current** | ~10 – 50 µA | **~0.3 – 0.8 µA** | ~100 µA |
| **Typical Application** | Motor control (F3/F4), industrial automation (F1) | Wearables, IoT sensors, smart meters, medical | AI/ML edge inference, vision processing, HMI |

**(7 marks — 2 marks for classification, 3 marks for the table, 2 marks for the application examples)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Part 1: Decoding STM32F407VGT6** [4 marks]

| **Field** | **Value** | **Meaning** |
|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| Prefix | STM32 | STMicroelectronics 32-bit MCU |
| Family letter | F | **Mainstream** series |
| Line | 407 | High-performance line, Cortex-M4F, 168 MHz, 1 MB Flash |
| Pin count letter | V | **100 pins** (LQFP) |
| Flash size letter | G | **1 MB** |
| Package letter | T | **LQFP (Low-profile Quad Flat Pack)** |
| Temperature grade | 6 | **−40 °C to +85 °C** (Industrial) |

**Part 2: Peripheral-to-Bus Mapping** [3 marks]

| **Peripheral** | **Bus** | **Reason** |
|----------------|---------|------------|
| **USART1** | **APB2** | High-speed peripheral bus, 84 MHz max |
| **USART2** | **APB1** | Low-speed bus, 42 MHz max |
| **I2C1** | **APB1** | Low-speed serial protocol |
| **ADC1** | **APB2** | High-speed analog with 12-bit resolution |
| **TIM1** | **APB2** | Advanced timer, paired with APB2 |
| **TIM3** | **APB1** | General-purpose timer, paired with APB1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes:**
>
> 1. **Naming Convention:** Students often confuse the **line number** (`407`) with the package — they are **different fields**. The `407` indicates the **product line** (CPU class, Flash range, peripherals), not the pin count.
> 2. **Bus Mapping:** A very common error is to say "**USART1 is on APB1**" — this is **wrong**. On STM32F4, USART1/USART6 are on **APB2**, while USART2/3 are on **APB1**. Memorize the rule: *Odd-numbered USARTs and high-speed peripherals are on APB2.*
> 3. **Timer Clock "×2 Trick":** When asked for $f_{\text{TIMx\_CLK}}$, students forget the **doubling** when the APB prescaler > 1, leading to **wrong PSC/ARR** and an entire 7-mark part going wrong. Always state explicitly: *"Since APBx prescaler is N > 1, the timer clock is 2 × $f_{\text{APBx}}$."*
> 4. **Register Names:** Use **STM32 datasheet notation** (e.g., `GPIOA->MODER`, `RCC->AHB1ENR`, `TIM2->PSC`) — not generic 8051-style `TRISx` or `PORTx` notation.
> 5. **FPU Presence:** Always clarify that the **F suffix** (M4F, M7) means **FPU + DSP instructions**. Bare M4 has neither.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this on the last night before the exam):**

**1. STM32 Brand and Origin**
- Manufacturer: **STMicroelectronics** (HQ: Geneva, Switzerland)
- Core: **ARM Cortex-M** licensed from Arm Holdings (UK)
- First released: **2007**
- ISA: **Thumb-2** (variable 16/32-bit instructions)

**2. The Five Main Categories of STM32**
- **Mainstream (F-class):** F0, G0, F1, F2, F3, F4, F7 — balanced cost/performance
- **Ultra-Low-Power (L/U-class):** L0, L1, L4, L5, U5 — battery-powered
- **High-Performance (H-class):** H7 — 550 MHz, AI/ML edge
- **Wireless (W-class):** WB (BLE 5), WL (LoRa)
- **Heterogeneous (MP-class):** MP1 — A7 + M4, runs Linux

**3. Cortex-M Core Variants**
- **M0 / M0+:** Lowest power, no FPU, 32-bit interface
- **M3:** Mainstream, no FPU, has bit-banding
- **M4F:** Adds FPU + DSP, MPU optional
- **M7:** 6-stage pipeline, double-precision FPU, L1 cache
- **M33:** TrustZone security, M-class successor to M3/M4

**4. Naming Convention — Memorize this format**
`STM32<FAMILY><LINE><PINS><FLASH><PACKAGE><TEMP>`

**5. Bus Matrix — Memorize the rule**
- **AHB** (high speed): CPU, DMA, Flash, SRAM
- **APB1** (≤42 MHz on F4): TIM2-7, USART2/3, I2C, SPI2/3, DAC, USB, CAN
- **APB2** (≤84 MHz on F4): USART1/6, SPI1, TIM1/8, ADC, GPIO, EXTI

**6. Key Memory Addresses (STM32F4)**
- Flash: `0x0800 0000` (booted from `0x0000 0000`)
- SRAM1: `0x2000 0000`
- CCM RAM: `0x1000 0000`

**7. Clock Tree Quick Rules**
- **HSI** = 8 MHz RC, fast start, low accuracy
- **HSE** = 4–25 MHz crystal, high accuracy
- **PLL** multiplies HSE to 168 MHz
- **APB×2 trick:** Timer clock = 2× APBx when prescaler > 1

**8. Timer Formula (BURN INTO MEMORY)**
$$
f_{\text{OUT}} = \frac{f_{\text{TIM\_CLK}}}{(PSC+1)(ARR+1)}
$$

**9. USART Baud Rate Formula**
$$
\text{USARTDIV} = \frac{f_{\text{USART\_CLK}}}{16 \times \text{Baud}}
$$
**BRR = (Mantissa × 16) + Fraction**

**10. Tool Chain (KTU Practical Exam)**
- **STM32CubeMX** → graphical configurator
- **STM32CubeIDE** → Eclipse-based compiler + debugger
- **HAL** (Hardware Abstraction Layer) → high-level portable APIs
- **LL** (Low-Layer) → register-level, faster but more code
- **ST-LINK/V2** → programmer + debugger
- **Serial Wire Debug (SWD)** → 2-pin debug interface (SWDIO, SWCLK)

**11. Compare-Contrast Anchors for Viva**
- STM32 vs 8051: 32-bit, 168 MHz vs 8-bit, 12 MHz
- ARM Cortex vs AVR: 32-bit Thumb-2 vs 8-bit RISC
- FPU presence: M4F/M7 = Yes, M0/M3 = No
- Flash vs SRAM: Flash holds code (non-volatile), SRAM holds runtime data (volatile)

**12. Common Exam Traps to Avoid**
- ❌ Writing "USART1 is on APB1" → ✅ It's on **APB2** (F4-series).
- ❌ Forgetting the **×2 trick** on timer clock.
- ❌ Confusing **BRR mantissa** (integer) with **fraction** bits [3:0].
- ❌ Loading `PSC = 8400` instead of `PSC = 8399` (off-by-one error).
- ❌ Using **Arduino-style `pinMode()`** terminology in STM32 exam — use **HAL_GPIO_Init()** or register-level `MODER` configuration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: STM32U575 Features and Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** STM32U575 Features and Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **STM32U575** is a **32-bit ultra-low-power microcontroller unit (MCU)** from **STMicroelectronics**, belonging to the **STM32U5 series**. It is built on the **ARM Cortex-M33** processor core and is fabricated using a **40 nm CMOS process technology**, specifically engineered for **energy-constrained embedded applications** that demand a balance between high computational throughput, rich peripheral integration, and aggressive power efficiency.

The STM32U575 is positioned in the *mainstream ultra-low-power* tier of the STM32U5 family. It supports the **Armv8-M architecture** with **TrustZone** hardware isolation, a **single-precision Floating Point Unit (FPU)**, and a **Memory Protection Unit (MPU)**. It is designed to comply with industry-grade **security certifications (SESIP, PSA Level 3, FIPS 140-3)** and is suited for **IoT nodes, wearable medical devices, smart sensors, and industrial control terminals**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> For Module 2, the focus is on understanding the **architectural features, memory organization, peripheral set, and low-power modes** of modern 32-bit microcontrollers, with the STM32U575 as the reference platform.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the STM32U575 as a **highly efficient, modern electric vehicle (EV)**:

- The **ARM Cortex-M33 CPU** is the **electric motor** — small, efficient, yet powerful enough to drive the whole system at 160 MHz when needed.
- The **multiple power modes (Run, Stop, Standby, Shutdown)** are the **driving modes** — *Sport* (Run), *Eco* (Low-Power Run), *Parked-Engine-Off* (Standby), and *Locked-Unplugged* (Shutdown) — each consuming drastically different levels of battery.
- The **TrustZone** is like a **two-compartment security vault** in the car — sensitive operations (e.g., OTA firmware updates, cryptographic keys) run in the *secure world*, while regular dashboard functions run in the *normal world*.
- The **integrated peripherals** (ADC, DAC, USB, CAN, Timers) are the **onboard systems** — *infotainment*, *climate control*, *battery management* — all sharing one compact chassis (the silicon die).

This single-chip "system" can run for **months on a coin-cell battery** while periodically waking up to perform sensing, processing, and wireless transmission.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> Memorize these **board-exam favourites**:

| Parameter | Value (Bolded for Recall) |
|---|---|
| Core | **ARM Cortex-M33** |
| Max Clock Frequency | **160 MHz** |
| Process Node | **40 nm CMOS** |
| Flash Memory | **Up to 4 MB** |
| SRAM | **Up to 2.5 MB** |
| Operating Voltage | **1.71 V – 3.6 V** |
| Temperature Range | **-40 °C to +85 °C / +125 °C** |
| ADC Resolution | **12-bit (16-bit with HW oversampling)** |
| DAC Resolution | **12-bit** |
| CoreMark Score | **~ 240 CoreMark** |
| Active Power (Run Mode) | **~ 19 µA/MHz** |
| Shutdown Current | **~ 30 nA** |

## 1.4 Block-Level Introduction

```
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
|                       STM32U575 SoC                           |
|                                                               |
|   +-------------+    +-------------+    +----------------+    |
|   | Cortex-M33  |    |   FPU + MPU |    |   TrustZone    |    |
|   |   160 MHz   |----|  + DSP Ext. |----|   (Secure/     |    |
|   |             |    |             |    |   Normal World)|    |
|   +-------------+    +-------------+    +----------------+    |
|                                                               |
|   Memory:   4 MB Flash | 2.5 MB SRAM | 4 KB BKPSRAM          |
|   Security: AES-256 | PKA | OTFDEC | RNG | Flash RDP        |
|   Comms:    USB-PD | CAN-FD | I2C | SPI | USART | LPUART     |
|   Analog:   3x ADC (12-bit) | 2x DAC (12-bit) | 2x OPAMP     |
|   Timers:   16/32-bit | LPTIM | RTC | SysTick | IWDG         |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
```

> The block diagram above shows the *intuitive system architecture* of the STM32U575. Each block will be expanded in the following sections.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Specification Sheet

## 2.1 Core Processor Subsystem

- **Core:** ARM **Cortex-M33** with **Armv8-M** architecture.
- **Pipeline:** 3-stage in-order pipeline (Fetch, Decode, Execute).
- **FPU:** Single-precision IEEE 754 compliant floating point unit.
- **DSP:** Single Instruction Multiple Data (SIMD) instructions for DSP workloads.
- **MPU:** 8 regions (optional, configurable for both secure and non-secure worlds).
- **TrustZone:** Hardware-isolated *secure* and *non-secure* execution domains.
- **NVIC:** Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller with up to **91 maskable interrupt channels** and **16 priority levels**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Cortex-M33?** It is the *smallest, most energy-efficient* Arm core that supports **TrustZone** and the **Helium (M-Profile Vector Extension - MVE)** family of DSP instructions. This makes it ideal for always-on, battery-operated IoT edge devices.

## 2.2 Memory Architecture

The STM32U575 implements a **multi-bank memory architecture** for low-power, concurrent operation:

- **Flash Memory:** Up to **4 MB** with *dual-bank* support, allowing *read-while-write (RWW)* operations.
- **SRAM:** Up to **2.5 MB** partitioned into:
  - **SRAM1** (main system RAM)
  - **SRAM2** (parity-checked, usable as backup)
  - **SRAM3** (for LP peripherals)
  - **SRAM4** (parity-checked, for low-power run modes)
- **Backup SRAM:** **4 KB** retained in **Standby** and **VBAT** mode.
- **QUAD-SPI / OSPI Interface:** For external memory expansion (PSRAM, NOR/NAND Flash).

> [!NOTE]
> The **multi-bank SRAM** allows different SRAM sections to operate in different voltage/power domains, enabling fine-grained power gating without losing data.

## 2.3 Power Supply and Low-Power Modes

The STM32U575 uses an **integrated SMPS (Switched-Mode Power Supply)** alongside a linear regulator, achieving high efficiency across load conditions.

- **VCORE range:** **1.0 V – 1.4 V** (dynamically scalable via software).
- **D1 / D2 / D3 voltage domains** for power gating peripherals.

| Power Mode | Typical Current | CPU State | Peripherals | Wake-up Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Run** | ~ 19 µA/MHz | Active | Full | N/A |
| **Low-Power Run** | ~ 110 µA | Active (low freq) | Most | N/A |
| **Sleep** | ~ 7 mA | Stopped, peripherals ON | All | Any IRQ |
| **Low-Power Sleep** | ~ 1.5 mA | Stopped | LP peripherals | LPUART, LPTIM, RTC |
| **Stop 0 / 1 / 2** | ~ 7 µA / 5 µA / 1.1 µA | Stopped, RAM retained | LP peripherals | RTC, EXTI, LPUART |
| **Standby** | ~ 350 nA | Powered off, RTC running | RTC + backup regs | RTC, IWDG, NRST |
| **Shutdown** | ~ 30 nA | Full power off | None | NRST, WKUP pins |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** A battery-powered **smart water meter** using STM32U575 in **Stop 2 mode** with periodic RTC wake-up can operate for **> 10 years** on a single lithium AA cell.

## 2.4 Analog Peripherals

- **3 × 12-bit ADCs** (5 Msps each, up to **16-bit effective resolution** via hardware oversampling).
- **2 × 12-bit DACs** (1 Msps, with sample-and-hold).
- **2 × Ultra-Low-Power Comparators.**
- **2 × Operational Amplifiers** (PGA mode capable, up to 16x gain).
- **1 × Digital Temperature Sensor.**
- **Vrefint / Vbat / Vref+ internal voltage references.**

## 2.5 Digital Communication Peripherals

| Peripheral | Count | Highlight |
|---|---|---|
| **USB 2.0 FS** | 1 | With **USB Type-C / Power Delivery** |
| **CAN-FD** | 1 | For automotive/industrial buses |
| **I2C** | Up to 6 | Standard (1 Mbit/s), Fast-mode Plus |
| **SPI** | Up to 5 | Up to 80 Mbit/s |
| **U(S)ART** | Up to 6 | Including ISO 7816, IrDA, LIN |
| **LPUART** | 1 | Active in Stop modes |
| **SAI** | 1 | Serial Audio Interface |
| **SDMMC** | 1 | For SD/SDIO/MMC cards |
| **FDCAN** | 1 | Controller Area Network, Flexible Data-rate |

## 2.6 Timers and Control Peripherals

- **16-bit timers** — General purpose, with PWM, input capture, and one-pulse modes.
- **32-bit timers** — For high-resolution timing.
- **Low-Power Timers (LPTIM)** — **6 × LPTIM** can run in **Stop** modes.
- **RTC** — Real-Time Clock with calendar, alarm, and timestamp functions.
- **IWDG / WWDG** — Independent and Window Watchdog timers.
- **SysTick** — 24-bit system timer (Cortex-M33 standard).
- **HRTIM** — High-Resolution Timer for power conversion applications.

## 2.7 Security Subsystem

- **TrustZone for Armv8-M** — Hardware isolation.
- **AES-256** Hardware Cryptographic Accelerator.
- **PKA (Public Key Accelerator)** — RSA, ECC up to 640 bits.
- **OTFDEC (On-The-Fly Decryption)** — For external memory encryption.
- **True Random Number Generator (TRNG)** — NIST SP 800-22 compliant.
- **Unique 96-bit device ID.**
- **RDP (Readout Protection)** Levels 0, 1, 2.
- **Secure Firmware Install (SFI)** and **Secure Boot**.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Specification Cheat Sheet

> **Exam Quick-Reference Table:**

| Feature | STM32U575 Value |
|---|---|
| Core | ARM Cortex-M33 |
| Max Frequency | **160 MHz** |
| Process Technology | 40 nm |
| Flash | 1 MB – 4 MB |
| SRAM | 786 KB – 2512 KB |
| Operating Voltage | 1.71 V – 3.6 V |
| Core Voltage | 1.0 V – 1.4 V |
| Lowest Power Mode | Shutdown (~ 30 nA) |
| ADC | 3 × 12-bit (16-bit OS) |
| DAC | 2 × 12-bit |
| USB | 2.0 FS + Type-C PD |
| CAN | CAN-FD |
| Crypto | AES-256, PKA, OTFDEC, TRNG |
| Architecture | Armv8-M with TrustZone |
| Temperature | -40 °C to +85 °C (or +125 °C) |
| Packages | LQFP64/100/144, UFBGA132/176, WLCSP |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Applications

The STM32U575 is widely used in:

1. **Wearable Health Monitors** — Continuous heart-rate, SpO2, and body-temperature sensing.
2. **Smart Energy Meters** — Decade-long battery life with low-power sensing.
3. **Industrial IoT Sensor Hubs** — CAN-FD connected, secure over-the-air updates.
4. **Smart Home Controllers** — Zigbee/BLE friendly interfaces.
5. **Asset Trackers** — GPS + accelerometer with deep-sleep duty cycling.
6. **Automotive Body Controllers** — LIN, CAN-FD, and secure boot.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Performance Derivations

This section is dedicated to **analytical derivations, performance calculations, and reasoned analysis** relevant to the STM32U575. The derivations below are typical questions that test your understanding of power-performance trade-offs — a high-weightage area in KTU board exams.

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Power Consumption in Duty-Cycled Operation

In a typical IoT application, the MCU does not run continuously. It spends most of its time in a low-power mode and periodically wakes up to perform a task. We can compute the *average current draw* and consequently the *battery lifetime*.

> **Step 1: Define the duty cycle.**
> Let $T_{active}$ be the active time per cycle and $T_{sleep}$ be the sleep time per cycle.

$$T_{cycle} = T_{active} + T_{sleep}$$

> **Step 2: Define the duty cycle ratio $D$.**

$$D = \frac{T_{active}}{T_{cycle}}$$

> **Step 3: Identify the currents in each phase.**
> $I_{active}$ — current drawn in active (Run) mode.
> $I_{sleep}$ — current drawn in low-power mode (e.g., Stop 2).

> **Step 4: Compute the average current.**

$$I_{avg} = D \cdot I_{active} + (1 - D) \cdot I_{sleep}$$

> **Step 5: Compute the battery life.**
> For a battery of capacity $C_{bat}$ (in mAh):

$$t_{life} \, [hours] = \frac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}}$$

> **Engineering Use:** This formula is the foundation of *energy budgeting* in low-power embedded design. Designers use it to determine how long a sensor node will run on a given battery.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Active Energy Per Processing Task

> **Step 1:** Suppose the CPU runs at frequency $f_{CPU}$ (in Hz) and executes $N$ instructions per task, where each instruction requires an average of $CPI$ cycles.

$$T_{task} = \frac{N \cdot CPI}{f_{CPU}}$$

> **Step 2:** The energy consumed per task is:

$$E_{task} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{active} \cdot T_{task}$$

> **Step 3:** Using $I_{active} = \alpha \cdot f_{CPU}$ (where $\alpha$ is in µA/MHz):

$$E_{task} = V_{DD} \cdot \alpha \cdot f_{CPU} \cdot \frac{N \cdot CPI}{f_{CPU}}$$

> **Step 4:** The $f_{CPU}$ cancels, revealing that the energy per instruction is **constant** for a given process and voltage:

$$E_{task} = V_{DD} \cdot \alpha \cdot N \cdot CPI$$

> **Conclusion:** The energy per task is **independent of the clock speed** in the active region (assuming all else is constant). This is the well-known *energy proportionality* property exploited by DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling). However, the **average power** and **time** still scale with $f_{CPU}$, which is why designers must trade off throughput vs. energy.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: ADC Oversampling Resolution Boost

The STM32U575 ADCs can be hardware-oversampled to enhance resolution beyond the native 12-bit.

> **Step 1:** The general formula relating oversampling factor and resolution gain is:

$$\Delta ENOB = \frac{1}{2} \log_{2}(OSR)$$

where $OSR$ is the oversampling ratio.

> **Step 2:** For an oversampling ratio of $OSR = 256$:

$$\Delta ENOB = \frac{1}{2} \log_{2}(256) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 8 = 4 \, \text{bits}$$

> **Step 3:** The effective number of bits (ENOB) becomes:

$$ENOB = N_{native} + \Delta ENOB = 12 + 4 = 16 \, \text{bits}$$

> **Conclusion:** With $OSR = 256$, the STM32U575 ADC achieves **16-bit effective resolution**, suitable for precision sensor measurements (e.g., strain gauges, thermocouples).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Interrupt Latency

The Cortex-M33 in STM32U575 uses a *tail-chaining* mechanism for low interrupt latency.

> **Step 1:** Base interrupt latency is **12 clock cycles** for the Cortex-M33 to stack the context and fetch the vector.

$$t_{latency} = \frac{12}{f_{CPU}}$$

> **Step 2:** For $f_{CPU} = 160 \, \text{MHz}$:

$$t_{latency} = \frac{12}{160 \times 10^{6}} = 75 \, \text{ns}$$

> **Step 3:** With **zero-wait-state Flash** and proper *vector tail-chaining*, the worst-case interrupt latency is **< 100 ns**. This makes the STM32U575 suitable for **real-time motor control** and **high-bandwidth digital signal processing**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — STM32U575 vs Other STM32 Families

> **Comparison Table (Frequently asked in KTU):**

| Feature | STM32F4 (Cortex-M4) | STM32L4 (Cortex-M4) | **STM32U575 (M33)** | STM32H7 (Cortex-M7) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Core | M4 (no TrustZone) | M4 | **M33 + TrustZone** | M7 |
| Max Frequency | 180 MHz | 80 MHz | **160 MHz** | 480 MHz |
| Shutdown Current | ~ 1 µA | ~ 30 nA | **~ 30 nA** | ~ 2 µA |
| Run Power (µA/MHz) | ~ 100 | ~ 100 | **~ 19** | ~ 110 |
| FPU | Single | Single | **Single** | Double |
| Security | Basic RDP | RDP | **TrustZone + AES + PKA** | TrustZone (M7) |
| Best For | Mid-power DSP | Low-power apps | **Secure ultra-low power** | High-performance DSP |

> **Insight:** The STM32U575 is uniquely positioned as a **secure ultra-low-power** MCU — a category the F4, L4, and H7 do not optimally address together.

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Battery Life Estimation for a Sensor Node

> **Scenario:** A smart water meter wakes every 30 seconds, samples an ADC for 1 ms, transmits via LPUART for 50 ms, and then enters Stop 2 mode. Compute the average current and battery life.

> **Step 1: Active duration per cycle.**

$$T_{active} = 1 \, \text{ms} + 50 \, \text{ms} = 51 \, \text{ms} = 0.051 \, \text{s}$$

> **Step 2: Sleep duration.**

$$T_{sleep} = 30 \, \text{s} - 0.051 \, \text{s} = 29.949 \, \text{s}$$

> **Step 3: Duty cycle.**

$$D = \frac{0.051}{30} = 0.0017 \, (0.17\%)$$

> **Step 4: Average current.**
> Take $I_{active} = 7 \, \text{mA}$ (Run mode at 160 MHz with peripherals), and $I_{sleep} = 1.1 \, \mu\text{A}$ (Stop 2 mode).

$$I_{avg} = (0.0017 \cdot 7000 \, \mu A) + (0.9983 \cdot 1.1 \, \mu A)$$

$$I_{avg} = 11.9 \, \mu A + 1.098 \, \mu A \approx 13 \, \mu A$$

> **Step 5: Battery life on a 2400 mAh lithium AA cell.**

$$t_{life} = \frac{2400 \, \text{mAh}}{0.013 \, \text{mA}} \approx 184615 \, \text{hours}$$

$$t_{life} \approx 21.07 \, \text{years}$$

> **Conclusion:** Even with conservative estimates, the STM32U575 enables a sensor to run for **> 20 years** on a single AA battery — a hallmark achievement of *ultra-low-power design*.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Top-Level MCU Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    core["CORTEX M33 CPU\n160 MHz with FPU MPU TrustZone"]
    secure["SECURE WORLD\nTrustZone Isolation"]
    normal["NORMAL WORLD\nApplication Code"]
    
    core --> secure
    core --> normal
    
    secure --> aes["AES 256 HW Accelerator"]
    secure --> pka["PKA Public Key"]
    secure --> trng["TRNG Random Source"]
    secure --> otfdec["OTFDEC On The Fly Decryption"]
    secure --> boot["SECURE BOOT SFI"]
    
    normal --> flash["FLASH MEMORY\nUp to 4 MB Dual Bank"]
    normal --> sram1["SRAM1 Main"]
    normal --> sram2["SRAM2 Parity"]
    normal --> sram3["SRAM3 LP Peripherals"]
    normal --> sram4["SRAM4 LP Run"]
    normal --> bkpsram["BKPSRAM 4 KB Retained"]
    normal --> qspi["QSPI OSPI Ext Memory IF"]
    
    core --> clk["CLOCK TREE\nHSI MSI HSE PLL LSE LSI"]
    core --> pwr["POWER MANAGEMENT\nSMPS LDO D1 D2 D3 Domains"]
    
    normal --> timers["TIMER BANK\n16 BIT 32 BIT LPTIM RTC SYSTICK HRTIM"]
    normal --> analog["ANALOG SUBSYSTEM\nADC1 ADC2 ADC3 DAC1 DAC2 OPAMP COMP"]
    normal --> comms["COMMS SUBSYSTEM\nUSART LPUART SPI I2C USB PD CAN FD SAI SDMMC"]
    normal --> gpio["GPIO BANK A B C D E F G H I"]
    
    pwr --> lpmodes["LP MODES\nRUN LP RUN SLEEP LP SLEEP\nSTOP 0 STOP 1 STOP 2\nSTANDBY SHUTDOWN"]
```

> This block diagram captures the *complete functional architecture* of the STM32U575. Use it as a mental model when answering diagram-based questions.

## 4.2 Power Mode State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> RUN
    RUN --> LP_RUN : Reduce clock
    RUN --> SLEEP : WFI or WFE
    LP_RUN --> LP_SLEEP : WFI or WFE
    SLEEP --> RUN : Interrupt wakeup
    LP_SLEEP --> LP_RUN : Interrupt wakeup
    SLEEP --> STOP0 : Enter low power
    SLEEP --> STOP1 : Enter low power
    SLEEP --> STOP2 : Enter low power
    STOP0 --> RUN : Wakeup event
    STOP1 --> RUN : Wakeup event
    STOP2 --> RUN : Wakeup event
    RUN --> STANDBY : PDDS bit set
    STANDBY --> RUN : WKUP pin or RTC alarm
    STANDBY --> SHUTDOWN : Deeper standby
    SHUTDBACKUP --> [*]
    SHUTDOWN --> RUN : NRST or WKUP pins
```

## 4.3 Memory Map Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    addr["ADDRESS SPACE\n0x0000 0000 to 0xFFFF FFFF"] --> f1["FLASH MAIN BANK1\n0x0800 0000\n1 MB"]
    f1 --> f2["FLASH MAIN BANK2\nUp to 3 MB"]
    f1 --> sram1["SRAM1\n0x2000 0000\n0 KB to 768 KB"]
    sram1 --> sram2["SRAM2\nParity checked"]
    sram2 --> sram3["SRAM3\nLP Peripherals"]
    sram3 --> sram4["SRAM4\nLP Run"]
    sram4 --> bkpsram["BKPSRAM 4 KB\n0x4003 6400"]
    bkpsram --> peri["PERIPHERAL BUS APB AHB\n0x4000 0000 to 0x5FFF FFFF"]
    peri --> ext["EXTERNAL MEMORY\nvia QSPI OSPI"]
    ext --> priv["PRIVATE BUS\nCPU Private Peripherals\n0xE000 0000 to 0xE00F FFFF"]
```

## 4.4 Interrupt & NVIC Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    irq["EXTERNAL INTERRUPTS\nUp to 91 Channels"] --> nvic["NVIC CONTROLLER\n16 Priority Levels"]
    nvic --> cpu["CORTEX M33 CPU\nTail Chain Preemption"]
    
    cpu --> vec["VECTOR TABLE\nStack and Fetch in 12 Cycles"]
    vec --> handler["ISR HANDLER\nService Routine"]
    handler --> rdy["RETURN TO MAIN\nor Tail Chain Next IRQ"]
```

## 4.5 ADC Oversampling Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensor["ANALOG SENSOR\nSignal 0 to 3.3 V"] --> mux["ANALOG MUX\nChannel Selection"]
    mux --> adc12["12 BIT SAR ADC\n5 Msps"]
    adc12 --> oversamp["HARDWARE OVERSAMPLER\nSum and Shift"]
    oversamp --> result["ENHANCED RESOLUTION\nUp to 16 Bit"]
    result --> dma["DMA CHANNEL\nto Memory or Register"]
    dma --> cpu["CORTEX M33\nApplication Read"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **For 3-mark questions:** Always redraw the high-level block diagram (Section 4.1) and label the **CPU, Memory, Peripherals, and Power domains** — this is the most common KTU diagram question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. List the major features of the ARM Cortex-M33 core used in STM32U575.
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

The ARM Cortex-M33 core in STM32U575 provides the following key features:

1. **Architecture:** Based on the **Armv8-M** profile with backward compatibility to Armv6-M instructions.
2. **Pipeline:** 3-stage in-order pipeline (Fetch, Decode, Execute).
3. **TrustZone:** Hardware-isolated **secure and non-secure worlds** for security.
4. **FPU:** Single-precision IEEE 754 **floating-point unit**.
5. **DSP Extensions:** SIMD instructions for digital signal processing.
6. **MPU:** Optional **Memory Protection Unit** with 8 regions.
7. **NVIC:** Up to **91 interrupt channels** with **16 priority levels** and **tail-chaining**.
8. **Wake-up Controller:** Supports ultra-low-power sleep modes.
9. **Performance:** Achieves **1.5 DMIPS/MHz** and **4.09 CoreMark/MHz**.

> [!TIP]
> Mentioning **TrustZone + FPU + DSP + NVIC** in any combination of 4–5 features fetches full 3 marks.

---

### Q2. What are the different low-power modes available in STM32U575?
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The STM32U575 supports **seven low-power modes**, ordered by decreasing power consumption:

1. **Run mode** — Full performance, all peripherals active.
2. **Low-Power Run mode** — CPU runs at reduced frequency, low voltage.
3. **Sleep mode** — CPU stopped, all peripherals active.
4. **Low-Power Sleep mode** — CPU stopped, only low-power peripherals active.
5. **Stop 0 / Stop 1 / Stop 2 modes** — Most peripherals powered down, RAM retained, RTC running.
6. **Standby mode** — VCORE off, only RTC and backup registers retained; wake-up from RTC, IWDG, or NRST.
7. **Shutdown mode** — Lowest power (~ 30 nA), wake-up only via NRST or WKUP pins.

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts: (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student must answer **either full Question A or full Question B**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the architecture of the STM32U575 microcontroller with a neat block diagram. List the features of its processor core and memory subsystem. **(7 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Processor Core (3 marks):**

- The STM32U575 is built around the **ARM Cortex-M33** core, a 32-bit processor based on the **Armv8-M** architecture.
- It runs at a maximum clock frequency of **160 MHz** and delivers **1.5 DMIPS/MHz**.
- The core integrates a **single-precision FPU** and supports **DSP SIMD instructions**.
- A **TrustZone** extension provides **secure** and **non-secure** execution domains, isolating critical security code.
- The **NVIC** supports up to **91 interrupts** with **16 priority levels** and **hardware tail-chaining** for minimal interrupt latency.
- A **Memory Protection Unit (MPU)** with 8 regions is included for system reliability.

**2. Memory Subsystem (3 marks):**

- **Flash:** Up to **4 MB**, organized as **dual-bank** for read-while-write, with ECC for reliability.
- **SRAM:** Up to **2.5 MB** split into multiple sections:
  - **SRAM1** — main system RAM
  - **SRAM2** — parity-checked
  - **SRAM3** — for low-power peripherals
  - **SRAM4** — used during low-power run mode
- **Backup SRAM:** **4 KB** retained during Standby and VBAT modes.
- **External Memory Interface:** **OCTOSPI / QUADSPI** for PSRAM / NOR Flash / NAND Flash.

**3. Block Diagram (1 mark):**

*(Draw the block diagram from Section 4.1, labeling: Cortex-M33, Memory, Peripherals, Power, Security blocks.)*

#### (b) Discuss the various power modes of STM32U575. Compare Stop 2, Standby, and Shutdown modes in terms of current consumption, RAM retention, and wake-up sources. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Mode | Current | RAM Retention | Wake-up Source | Reset on Wake-up |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stop 2** | ~ 1.1 µA | Full RAM retained | RTC, EXTI, LPUART, LPTIM, IWDG | No (CPU continues) |
| **Standby** | ~ 350 nA | Only 4 KB BKPSRAM | RTC, IWDG, NRST, WKUP pins | Yes (cold reset) |
| **Shutdown** | ~ 30 nA | None (BKPSRAM optional) | NRST, WKUP pins | Yes (cold reset) |

- **Stop 2 Mode:** All **SRAM contents are retained**, peripherals in D3 domain stay functional, and the CPU resumes execution from where it stopped. The LPUART can wake the system upon receiving a character.
- **Standby Mode:** Most of the chip is powered down. Only the RTC, IWDG, backup SRAM, and select registers are alive. The system undergoes a **cold reset** upon wake-up; the application must reinitialize the system before continuing.
- **Shutdown Mode:** The **lowest** power mode. The internal voltage regulator is disabled. The optional backup SRAM (BKPSRAM) can be retained if *BRR* bit is set. Wake-up sources are restricted to **NRST** and **WKUP** pins.

> **Conclusion:** The mode choice is a **trade-off** between *power savings* and *wake-up latency / state retention*. Stop 2 is best for sub-second duty cycling; Standby for minutes-to-hours; Shutdown for year-long hibernation.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative to Question A*

#### (a) Describe the security features of the STM32U575. How does TrustZone provide isolation between secure and non-secure worlds? **(7 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Security Features (4 marks):**

- **TrustZone for Armv8-M:** Provides **hardware-enforced isolation** between secure and non-secure code. Sensitive code (e.g., cryptographic key handling) executes in the **Secure World**, while user applications run in the **Non-Secure World**.
- **AES-256 Hardware Accelerator:** Performs encryption/decryption with **low CPU overhead**, achieving Gbps throughput.
- **Public Key Accelerator (PKA):** Offloads **RSA, ECC, and Diffie-Hellman** operations from the CPU.
- **OTFDEC (On-The-Fly Decryption):** Decrypts external Flash content (via OSPI) **on the fly** as the CPU reads instructions or data.
- **True Random Number Generator (TRNG):** Generates **NIST-compliant** entropy for cryptographic operations.
- **Readout Protection (RDP):** Three levels (0, 1, 2) of Flash protection to prevent code extraction.
- **Secure Firmware Install (SFI):** Allows STMicroelectronics or OEMs to deliver encrypted firmware images.
- **Unique 96-bit Device ID + 128-bit Unique Key** for hardware root-of-trust.

**2. TrustZone Isolation (3 marks):**

- The Cortex-M33 adds a **single-bit security attribute (SAU/IDAU)** to every bus transaction. The **Secure Attribution Unit (SAU)** defines which memory regions are secure.
- The **Implementation Defined Attribution Unit (IDAU)** is set by ST to mark certain memory regions (e.g., Flash sector 0, SRAM4, secure peripherals) as secure.
- A **secure exception (SecureFault)** is generated if a non-secure access attempts to reach a secure region.
- **Secure peripherals** (e.g., AES, PKA, RNG) can be accessed only by secure code; non-secure code calls them via **secure function entry points (veneers)**.
- This isolation prevents malicious code from reading keys, modifying boot code, or leaking sensitive data.

---

#### (b) Explain the ADC features of STM32U575. With the help of suitable expressions, show how oversampling achieves 16-bit resolution. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. ADC Features (3 marks):**

- **3 × 12-bit SAR ADCs** (ADC1, ADC2, ADC3) with up to **5 Msps** sampling rate.
- **Up to 20 channels** in total, with **single-ended and differential** input modes.
- **Hardware Oversampling:** Improves effective resolution up to **16 bits**.
- **Injected and Regular** conversion modes.
- **Analog Watchdog:** Generates interrupts when the signal exceeds a threshold.
- **Multi-mode operation:** Dual/quadruple interleaved mode for higher throughput.
- **DMA support:** Transfers results to RAM automatically.
- **Low-power modes:** ADC can operate in **Run, Sleep, Stop 0, Stop 1, Stop 2** modes with proper clock configuration.

**2. Oversampling Derivation (4 marks):**

To improve the effective resolution, the ADC performs **$OSR$ samples** and **averages** them. The relationship between the oversampling ratio and the resolution gain is given by:

$$ENOB = N_{native} + \frac{1}{2} \log_2(OSR)$$

where $N_{native}$ is the native ADC resolution and $OSR$ is the oversampling ratio.

For the STM32U575, $N_{native} = 12$ bits.

- To achieve 14 bits:
  $$14 = 12 + \frac{1}{2} \log_2(OSR) \Rightarrow OSR = 2^4 = 16$$
- To achieve 16 bits:
  $$16 = 12 + \frac{1}{2} \log_2(OSR) \Rightarrow OSR = 2^8 = 256$$

Thus, with $OSR = 256$, the STM32U575 ADC delivers a **16-bit effective resolution** without any software intervention — fully implemented in hardware.

> **Conclusion:** Hardware oversampling enables the STM32U575 to use a **low-power 12-bit SAR ADC** while still achieving the precision required for **high-accuracy sensor measurements** (e.g., temperature, pressure, strain gauges).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't confuse the ADC native resolution (12-bit) with the oversampled resolution (16-bit).** Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 2. **In TrustZone questions, always mention the SAU and IDAU.** Simply writing "TrustZone provides security" will get you only 1 mark.
> 3. **For power-mode questions, always provide the typical current values (e.g., Stop 2 = 1.1 µA).** Vague answers lose marks.
> 4. **Memory map questions require correct base addresses.** 0x0800 0000 for Flash, 0x2000 0000 for SRAM.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Bullet Checklist:**

- **Core:** ARM Cortex-M33, 160 MHz, with **FPU + DSP + TrustZone + MPU + NVIC (91 IRQs)**.
- **Process:** **40 nm CMOS**, enabling ultra-low leakage current.
- **Flash:** Up to **4 MB**, dual-bank, RWW, with ECC.
- **SRAM:** Up to **2.5 MB** distributed as SRAM1, SRAM2, SRAM3, SRAM4, plus **4 KB BKPSRAM**.
- **External memory:** OCTOSPI / QUADSPI for PSRAM, NOR, NAND.
- **Voltage:** **1.71 V – 3.6 V** (I/O), **1.0 V – 1.4 V** (VCORE).
- **Power modes (7):** Run, Low-Power Run, Sleep, Low-Power Sleep, **Stop 0/1/2, Standby, Shutdown**.
- **Lowest current:** **Shutdown = ~ 30 nA**.
- **ADCs:** 3 × 12-bit, can be oversampled to **16-bit** using $OSR = 256$.
- **DACs:** 2 × 12-bit.
- **Comparators / Op-amps:** 2 each.
- **Communication:** USB 2.0 FS + Type-C PD, **CAN-FD**, 6× I2C, 5× SPI, 6× USART, 1× LPUART, 1× SAI, 1× SDMMC.
- **Timers:** 16/32-bit, **6 × LPTIM**, RTC, IWDG, WWDG, HRTIM, SysTick.
- **Security:** **AES-256, PKA, OTFDEC, TRNG, RDP, SFI, secure boot, TrustZone**.
- **Packages:** LQFP64, LQFP100, UFBGA132, LQFP144, UFBGA176, WLCSP56/90.
- **Typical application:** Battery-powered IoT sensor nodes, wearables, smart meters, secure industrial controllers.
- **Most-loved KTU question:** "Differentiate Stop 2, Standby, and Shutdown modes" — *practice this thoroughly*.
- **Energy-budget formula:** $I_{avg} = D \cdot I_{active} + (1-D) \cdot I_{sleep}$ — *used in derivations*.
- **ADC oversampling formula:** $ENOB = N_{native} + \frac{1}{2} \log_2(OSR)$ — *memorize and apply*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Management and Low-Power Features Libraries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Power Management and Low-Power Features Libraries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Power Management** in the context of ARM Cortex-M based microcontrollers (specifically the STM32 family used in the KTU PBCST504 syllabus) refers to the systematic control of clock domains, voltage domains, and peripheral activity to minimize the overall energy consumption ($E_{total}$) of the embedded system. It is implemented through dedicated hardware blocks like the **Power Control (PWR)** peripheral, the **Reset and Clock Control (RCC)** unit, and a layered firmware library (Standard Peripheral Library / HAL / LL).

**Low-Power Features Libraries** are the collection of Application Programming Interface (API) functions, configuration structures, and macros that abstract the register-level access required to place the microcontroller into predefined low-power states such as **Sleep**, **Stop** (Deep-Sleep), and **Standby** (Power-Down) modes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the 2024 NEP-aligned scheme, students are expected to understand *why* an embedded designer chooses a specific low-power mode, the wake-up latency trade-offs, and the register-level sequence (not just library calls) for state transitions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Building" Intuition

Imagine a large corporate office building representing the microcontroller:

| Building Element | Microcontroller Equivalent |
|---|---|
| **Lights & Air Conditioning** in unused rooms | Peripheral clocks (USART, ADC, TIM, SPI) |
| **Entire floor shut down** | Stop Mode (Deep Sleep) |
| **Building security on duty only** | Standby Mode (Power-Down, only RTC alive) |
| **Employees at desks but resting** | Sleep Mode (CPU stopped, peripherals can run) |
| **Power meter** | Current consumption $I_{DD}$ measured in $\mu A$ or $mA$ |

Just as a building manager turns off lights in empty rooms to save electricity, the **Power Management library** allows the firmware engineer to "switch off" unused clock trees, lower the core voltage through the **voltage regulator**, and freeze the CPU core — all while retaining enough logic to wake the system up via interrupts or external events.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Metric:** The standard indicator of low-power performance is the **current consumption** $I_{DD}$ in $\mu A/MHz$, and the **wake-up time** $t_{WU}$ in $\mu s$. For battery-operated IoT nodes, a current budget of **$< 1 \mu A$** in standby is the gold standard.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Power Reduction

1. **Clock Gating** — Disabling the clock signal to peripherals that are idle. The flip-flops retain their state but do not toggle, reducing dynamic switching power $P_{dyn} \propto C \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{CLK}$.
2. **Voltage Scaling / Regulation** — Using the embedded LDO (Low-Dropout) or SMPS (Switched-Mode Power Supply) to lower the core voltage $V_{CORE}$ during low-activity phases.
3. **Sleep Modes** — Powering down CPU core logic, SRAM banks, or entire logic domains while selectively retaining RAM content and RTC operation.

## 1.4 KTU Board Examiner's Perspective

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Evaluation), questions on this topic typically test:
> - Differentiation between **Sleep, Stop, and Standby** modes.
> - Identification of **wake-up sources** for each mode.
> - Power calculation using $P = V \cdot I$.
> - Register-level configuration (e.g., `PWR->CR`, `SCB->SCR`, `RCC->APBxENR`).

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Where Does Power Go?

In CMOS digital logic (the technology used in STM32 microcontrollers), the total power dissipation $P_{total}$ has two dominant components:

$$P_{total} = P_{static} + P_{dynamic}$$

The **dynamic power** (the dominant term during active operation) is given by:

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{CLK}$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = switching activity factor (typically **0.1 to 0.3** for average code).
- $C_{L}$ = load capacitance of the logic gate.
- $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage (typically **3.3 V**, regulated down to **1.2 V** for the core).
- $f_{CLK}$ = clock frequency (e.g., **72 MHz** for STM32F1, **180 MHz** for STM32F4).

The **static power** (leakage current) is:

$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leakage}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The quadratic dependence on $V_{DD}$ is why **voltage reduction** is the most effective single technique — halving $V_{DD}$ reduces dynamic power by **a factor of 4**.

## 2.2 The Three Low-Power Modes (KTU High-Yield)

The STM32 family implements three primary low-power modes, each offering a different trade-off between power savings and wake-up capability:

### Mode 1 — Sleep Mode (WFI / WFE)

- **CPU Core:** Stopped.
- **Peripherals:** Continue to operate if their clock is enabled.
- **SRAM Content:** Retained.
- **Wake-up Sources:** Any peripheral interrupt (EXTI, USART, TIM, etc.).
- **Wake-up Time:** $\approx$ **0 cycles** (immediate, since HSI oscillator is still running).
- **Typical Current:** $\approx$ **$10$–$50$ mA/MHz** depending on peripheral activity.

### Mode 2 — Stop Mode (Deep Sleep)

- **CPU Core:** Powered down.
- **All Peripheral Clocks:** Stopped.
- **SRAM Content:** Retained.
- **Wake-up Sources:** EXTI external interrupt, RTC alarm, USB wakeup, IWDG reset.
- **Voltage Regulator:** Can be in normal or low-power mode.
- **Wake-up Time:** $\approx$ **5–10 $\mu s$** (HSI startup time).
- **Typical Current:** $\approx$ **$10$–$20 \mu A$**.

### Mode 3 — Standby Mode (Power-Down)

- **CPU Core:** Powered down.
- **SRAM Content:** **LOST** (unless backup registers / RTC backup domain used).
- **Wake-up Sources:** WKUP pin, RTC alarm, IWDG reset, NRST pin.
- **Wake-up Time:** $\approx$ **$50$–$200 \mu s$** (full oscillator + regulator startup).
- **Typical Current:** $\approx$ **$1$–$2 \mu A$**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Parameter | Formula / Definition | Typical Value (STM32F4) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $P_{dyn}$ | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{CLK}$ | — | W |
| $P_{static}$ | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | — | W |
| Battery Life Estimate | $t_{bat} = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}}$ | CR2032: 220 mAh | hours |
| Average Current | $I_{avg} = I_{active} \cdot d + I_{sleep} \cdot (1-d)$ | — | A |
| Energy per Task | $E_{task} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{active} \cdot t_{task}$ | — | J |
| Voltage Scaling Gain | $\dfrac{P_{new}}{P_{old}} = \left(\dfrac{V_{new}}{V_{old}}\right)^2$ | — | ratio |
| Sleep Wake Latency | $t_{WU,sleep} \approx 0$ | 0 | cycles |
| Stop Wake Latency | $t_{WU,stop} \approx t_{HSI\_START}$ | $\sim 5$ | $\mu s$ |
| Standby Wake Latency | $t_{WU,standby} \approx t_{HSI} + t_{REG}$ | $\sim 200$ | $\mu s$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid:** "**S**leep = **S**ame SRAM, **S**ame clocks active; **S**top = **S**top clocks, **S**ave SRAM; **S**tandby = **S**witch off **S**RAM" (lossy).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **IoT Sensor Nodes (e.g., soil moisture sensor):** Spend 99.9% of time in Standby, wake every 15 minutes via RTC, take reading, transmit via LoRa, return to Standby. Battery life exceeds **5 years** on 2x AA cells.
2. **Wearable Fitness Bands:** Use Stop mode between heart-rate samples; only the RTC and EXTI remain active.
3. **Smart Metering:** Run CPU in active mode for **< 1 second** per day; rest in Deep-Sleep.
4. **Automotive ECU:** Use Sleep mode to allow CAN bus listening while ECU "engine off" — wakes on CAN frame.

## 2.5 Power Management Library Architecture

The STM32 Standard Peripheral Library (SPL) and HAL both expose a layered API:

```
msp.c  (Hardware-specific callbacks: HAL_MspInit)
   |
   v
stm32f4xx_hal_pwr.c   (PWR HAL driver)
   |
   v
stm32f4xx_pwr.c       (SPL register-level access)
   |
   v
PWR Registers (PWR->CR, PWR->CSR, SCB->SCR)
```

Key library functions for KTU:

| Function | Purpose | Header |
|---|---|---|
| `PWR_DeInit()` | Reset PWR peripheral to defaults | `stm32f4xx_pwr.h` |
| `PWR_BackupAccessCmd(ENABLE)` | Enable RTC backup register writes | `stm32f4xx_pwr.h` |
| `PWR_WakeUpPinCmd(ENABLE)` | Enable WKUP pin for Standby exit | `stm32f4xx_pwr.h` |
| `PWR_EnterSTOPMode()` | Enter Stop mode with regulator config | `stm32f4xx_pwr.h` |
| `PWR_EnterSTANDBYMode()` | Enter Standby mode | `stm32f4xx_pwr.h` |
| `__WFI()` / `__WFE()` | Wait-For-Interrupt / Wait-For-Event intrinsic | `core_cm4.h` |
| `HAL_PWR_EnterSLEEPMode()` | HAL Sleep mode entry | `stm32f4xx_hal_pwr.h` |
| `HAL_PWREx_EnterSTOPMode()` | HAL Stop mode entry | `stm32f4xx_hal_pwr.h` |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Battery Life from Duty-Cycled Power Profile

A remote sensor node operates with the following duty cycle:
- **Active phase:** $I_{active} = 15$ mA for $t_{active} = 100$ ms.
- **Standby phase:** $I_{standby} = 1.5 \mu A$ for $t_{standby} = 14.9$ s.
- **Period:** $T = t_{active} + t_{standby} = 15$ s.
- **Supply:** $V_{DD} = 3.0$ V (CR2032 coin cell), $C_{bat} = 220$ mAh.

**Step 1: Compute the duty cycle $d$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{t_{active}}{T} \\
  &= \frac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{15} \\
  &= 6.667 \times 10^{-3} \\
  &= 0.667 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the average current $I_{avg}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{avg} &= I_{active} \cdot d + I_{standby} \cdot (1 - d) \\
        &= (15 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (6.667 \times 10^{-3}) + (1.5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (1 - 6.667 \times 10^{-3}) \\
        &= 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.49 \times 10^{-6} \\
        &= 1.0149 \times 10^{-4} \text{ A} \\
        &= 101.5 \;\mu A
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the battery life $t_{bat}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{bat} &= \frac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}} \\
        &= \frac{220 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Ah}}{101.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}} \\
        &= 2167.5 \text{ hours} \\
        &= \frac{2167.5}{24} \text{ days} \\
        &= 90.3 \text{ days} \approx 3 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The active current is **$10{,}000\times$ larger** than the standby current, yet because the duty cycle is so small, the **average current is dominated by the standby leakage**. To extend battery life further, the designer must either reduce $I_{standby}$ (e.g., use a load switch to fully cut power) or shorten the active phase (e.g., use DMA instead of polled USART).

## 3.2 Derivation: Voltage Scaling Effect on Power

A Cortex-M4 core runs at $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V, $f_{CLK} = 168$ MHz. The designer activates voltage scaling to reduce $V_{DD}$ to **0.9 V** during idle periods.

**Step 1: Original power ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_{new}}{P_{old}} &= \left( \frac{V_{new}}{V_{old}} \right)^2 \\
                        &= \left( \frac{0.9}{1.2} \right)^2 \\
                        &= (0.75)^2 \\
                        &= 0.5625
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Power savings**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= P_{old} - P_{new} = P_{old} \cdot (1 - 0.5625) = 0.4375 \cdot P_{old}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** A **43.75%** reduction in dynamic power is achieved solely by reducing the core voltage by 25%, while keeping the clock frequency the same.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mode Selection Decision Tree

| Design Constraint | Recommended Mode | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Need immediate peripheral response, $I_{sleep} < 50$ mA OK | **Sleep** | Zero wake-up latency, peripherals alive |
| Need $I_{sleep} < 50 \mu A$, can tolerate 5–10 $\mu s$ wake | **Stop** | Clocks off, SRAM retained, fast wake |
| Need $I_{sleep} < 5 \mu A$, can lose SRAM contents | **Standby** | Maximum power savings, full reset on wake |
| Need RTC to keep ticking across sleep | **Stop or Standby** | RTC domain is always powered in $V_{BAT}$ |
| Need to wake from button press after days of inactivity | **Standby + WKUP pin** | Lowest possible standby current |
| Need to resume program execution state | **Sleep or Stop** | Standby causes a full POR (Power-On Reset) |
| Need to listen on UART for incoming data | **Sleep with USART interrupt** | Peripherals continue running in Sleep |

## 3.4 Register-Level Configuration Sequence (Stop Mode)

A KTU board question often asks for the exact register write sequence. The canonical sequence to enter Stop mode on STM32F4 is:

1. Select the regulator mode: Set `PWR->CR.PLDS = 0` (main regulator) or `PWR->CR.LPDS = 1` (low-power regulator).
2. Set the deep-sleep bit in Cortex-M4 System Control Register: `SCB->SCR.SLEEPDEEP = 1`.
3. Clear the PDDS bit (Stop mode, not Standby): `PWR->CR.PDDS = 0`.
4. Execute the Wait-For-Interrupt intrinsic: `__WFI();`.
5. After wake, the HSI is selected as system clock. Reconfigure the PLL if needed: `RCC->CFGR.SW = 0x02;`.

The complete C implementation:

```c
/* Enter Stop Mode with main regulator */
void Enter_StopMode(void) {
    /* 1. Select the voltage regulator mode */
    PWR->CR &= ~PWR_CR_LPDS;     /* Main regulator */
    
    /* 2. Set SLEEPDEEP bit in Cortex-M4 SCR */
    SCB->SCR |= SCB_SCR_SLEEPDEEP_Msk;
    
    /* 3. Clear PDDS bit -> Stop mode (not Standby) */
    PWR->CR &= ~PWR_CR_PDDS_Msk;
    
    /* 4. Ensure wake-up source is configured (e.g., EXTI0) */
    EXTI->PR = EXTI_PR_PR0;      /* Clear pending bit */
    
    /* 5. Request Wait-For-Interrupt */
    __WFI();
    
    /* ---- CPU Resumes Here After Wake ---- */
    /* 6. Restore system clock (HSI default after wake) */
    RCC->CR |= RCC_CR_HSION;
    while (!(RCC->CR & RCC_CR_HSIRDY));
    RCC->CFGR |= RCC_CFGR_SW_HSI;
    
    /* 7. Restore PLL if needed */
    RCC->PLLCFGR = /* original PLL config */;
    RCC->CR |= RCC_CR_PLLON;
    while (!(RCC->CR & RCC_CR_PLLRDY));
    RCC->CFGR |= RCC_CFGR_SW_PLL;
    
    /* 8. Clear SLEEPDEEP bit */
    SCB->SCR &= ~SCB_SCR_SLEEPDEEP_Msk;
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The `__WFI()` instruction is an ARM Cortex-M intrinsic that is a hint to the core to stop fetching instructions. The CPU resumes only when an interrupt of sufficient priority is asserted. This is a hardware-level mechanism, not a library function — but the *configuration* of which interrupt sources are active is done via the **PWR library**.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power Domain Architecture of STM32

The diagram below illustrates the major power domains of an STM32F4-series microcontroller. Each domain can be independently powered on or off to minimize quiescent current.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VDD 3.3V Main Supply] --> B[Voltage Regulator LDO or SMPS]
    B --> C[VCORE 1.2V Digital Domain]
    C --> C1[CPU Cortex M4 Core]
    C --> C2[SRAM 192KB]
    C --> C3[Peripheral Logic]
    A --> D[VDDA Analog Domain]
    D --> D1[ADC DAC Voltage Ref]
    A --> E[VBAT Battery Domain]
    E --> E1[RTC Real Time Clock]
    E --> E2[Backup Registers 20 x 32bit]
    A --> F[VREF Plus Reference]
    C3 --> G[AHB Bus Matrix 168 MHz]
    G --> G1[APB1 Peripherals 42 MHz]
    G --> G2[APB2 Peripherals 84 MHz]
    B -.->|Standby Mode| H[VCORE OFF]
    C1 -.->|Standby Mode| H
    C2 -.->|Standby Mode SRAM LOST| H
    E1 -.->|Always On| I[VBAT Active]
    E2 -.->|Always On| I
    E1 --> J[Wakeup Source to CPU]
    E2 --> K[NVIC Interrupt Router]
    C3 --> L[EXTI Lines 0 to 15]
    L --> M[WKUP Pin Logic]
    M --> N[CPU Reset on Standby Exit]
```

## 4.2 Low-Power Mode State Transition Diagram

This flowchart shows the legal transitions between Run, Sleep, Stop, and Standby modes, along with the wake-up events that trigger transitions.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Run
    Run --> Sleep : WFI executed with SLEEPDEEP equals 0
    Run --> Stop : WFI with SLEEPDEEP equals 1 and PDDS equals 0
    Run --> Standby : WFI with SLEEPDEEP equals 1 and PDDS equals 1
    Sleep --> Run : Any peripheral interrupt
    Stop --> Run : EXTI RTC alarm IWDG USB wake
    Standby --> Run : WKUP pin RTC alarm IWDG NRST
    note right of Standby : SRAM content is LOST on entry
    note right of Stop : SRAM content is RETAINED
    note right of Sleep : CPU halted peripherals continue
```

## 4.3 Library Layered Architecture

The following block diagram clarifies the relationship between the application code, the HAL/PWR library, the Standard Peripheral Library, and the physical hardware registers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Application Code main c] --> B[HAL PWR Driver stm32f4xx hal pwr c]
    A --> C[SPL PWR Driver stm32f4xx pwr c]
    B --> D[Configuration Structs PWR PVDTypeDef]
    C --> E[Register Definitions stm32f4xx.h]
    D --> E
    E --> F[PWR Control Register PWR CR]
    E --> G[PWR Control Status Register PWR CSR]
    E --> H[System Control Register SCB SCR]
    E --> I[NVIC Interrupt Enable ISER]
    F --> J[Voltage Regulator Logic]
    F --> K[WKUP Pin Logic]
    G --> L[Wakeup Flags WUF SBF]
    H --> M[Cortex M4 Core Logic]
    I --> N[CPU Interrupt Controller]
    J --> O[VCORE Domain]
    K --> P[Standby Exit Logic]
    L --> P
    M --> Q[Power State Transition]
    N --> Q
```

## 4.4 Clock Gating Functional Block Diagram

Clock gating is the first line of defense for power reduction. The block diagram below shows how the **Reset and Clock Control (RCC)** unit gates individual peripheral clocks.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[HSI 16 MHz Internal] --> B[RCC Clock Tree]
    A1[HSE 8 MHz Crystal] --> B
    A2[PLL 168 MHz] --> B
    B --> C[AHB Clock HCLK]
    C --> D[APB1 Bus 42 MHz]
    C --> E[APB2 Bus 84 MHz]
    D --> F1[TIM2 to TIM7]
    D --> F2[USART2 USART3]
    D --> F3[SPI2 I2C1 I2C2]
    D --> F4[ADC Common Interface]
    E --> G1[TIM1 TIM8 to TIM11]
    E --> G2[USART1 SPI1]
    E --> G3[ADC1 ADC2 ADC3]
    E --> G4[GPIOA GPIOB to GPIOI]
    D --> H1[Clock Enable Bit RCC APB1ENR]
    E --> H2[Clock Enable Bit RCC APB2ENR]
    H1 -->|Bit 0 equals 1| F1
    H1 -->|Bit 17 equals 1| F2
    H2 -->|Bit 14 equals 1| G2
    H2 -->|Bit 8 equals 1| G1
    H1 -->|Bit 0 equals 0| I1[Clock OFF Peripheral Frozen]
    H1 -->|Bit 17 equals 0| I2[USART2 Frozen]
    H2 -->|Bit 14 equals 0| I3[SPI1 Frozen]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Diagram Point:** When `RCC->APBxENR` bit is cleared, the peripheral does not lose its register contents — only its clock input is gated. The peripheral becomes "frozen" but resumes exactly where it left off once the clock is re-enabled. This is **different** from Standby mode, which causes a full reset of the logic domain.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]

**Differentiate between Sleep, Stop, and Standby low-power modes of an STM32 microcontroller. Mention the typical current consumption and wake-up time for each.**

**Model Answer:**

The STM32 microcontroller family provides three primary low-power modes with progressively higher power savings at the cost of functionality:

| Parameter | **Sleep Mode** | **Stop Mode** | **Standby Mode** |
|---|---|---|---|
| CPU Core | Stopped | Powered off | Powered off |
| Peripheral Clocks | Active | Stopped | Stopped |
| SRAM Contents | Retained | Retained | **LOST** |
| Voltage Regulator | ON | Low-power option | OFF |
| Typical Current | $\sim 10$–$50$ mA/MHz | $\sim 10$–$20 \mu A$ | $\sim 1$–$2 \mu A$ |
| Wake-up Time | $\sim 0$ cycles | $\sim 5$–$10 \mu s$ | $\sim 50$–$200 \mu s$ |
| Wake-up Sources | Any IRQ | EXTI, RTC, IWDG | WKUP, RTC, IWDG, NRST |

The choice depends on whether the application needs to retain RAM state, can tolerate latency, and the strict current budget.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

**Explain the role of the `PWR->CR` register in configuring low-power modes on STM32. List at least four important bits of this register.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Power Control Register (`PWR->CR`)** is the central configuration register for selecting the low-power mode and the voltage regulator behavior. Important bits include:

1. **LPDS (Bit 0):** Low-Power Deep-Sleep. When set, the voltage regulator enters low-power mode during Stop/Standby.
2. **PDDS (Bit 1):** Power-Down Deep-Sleep. When set, the controller enters **Standby** mode; when cleared, it enters **Stop** mode.
3. **CWUF (Bit 2):** Clear Wake-up Flag. Software must set this bit to clear the `WUF` flag before re-entering Standby.
4. **CSBF (Bit 3):** Clear Standby Flag. Clears the `SBF` flag indicating the source of last reset.
5. **PVDE (Bit 4):** Power Voltage Detector Enable. Activates the PVD that monitors $V_{DD}$ against a threshold.
6. **PLS[2:0] (Bits 5–7):** PVD Level Selection. Selects the threshold voltage (2.0 V to 2.9 V).

The standard sequence to enter **Stop mode** is: clear PDDS, configure LPDS, then `__WFI()`. To enter **Standby**: set PDDS, clear CWUF, set WUF, then `__WFI()`.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Sample Paper Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the various power domains of an STM32F4 microcontroller. Describe how the voltage regulator operates in Run, Stop, and Standby modes.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Design a battery-powered temperature logging node that wakes every 60 seconds, reads an I2C temperature sensor, stores the value in RAM, and returns to sleep. Calculate the average current consumption and expected battery life on a 1000 mAh Li-ion cell, given: $I_{active} = 20$ mA, $t_{active} = 50$ ms, $I_{sleep} = 2 \mu A$.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

The STM32F4 has four distinct power domains:

1. **$V_{DD}$ / $V_{SS}$ (Main Digital Domain):** Powers the I/O ring and the LDO input. Typical **3.3 V**.
2. **$V_{CORE}$ (Digital Core Domain):** Generated internally by the LDO/SMPS at **1.2 V**. Powers the Cortex-M4 core, SRAM, and digital peripherals.
3. **$V_{DDA}$ (Analog Domain):** Powers the ADC, DAC, and voltage reference. Must be present for analog accuracy.
4. **$V_{BAT}$ (Backup Domain):** Powers the RTC and backup registers. Supplied by a coin cell or the main $V_{DD}$ when present.

The **voltage regulator** (LDO on STM32F4) operates in three modes:

- **Run Mode (Normal):** LDO delivers full **1.2 V** at high current capability. Power consumption: $\sim 100 \mu A$ quiescent.
- **Stop Mode (Low-Power):** LPDS bit set, LDO enters low-drive mode delivering $\sim 1.0$ V to retain SRAM. Quiescent current drops to $\sim 10 \mu A$.
- **Standby Mode (OFF):** LDO is fully shut down. Only $V_{BAT}$ domain remains powered. Quiescent current drops to $\sim 1 \mu A$ from $V_{BAT}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Drawing a clear block diagram with **4 labelled blocks** (VDD, VCORE, VDDA, VBAT) and showing the LDO between $V_{DD}$ and $V_{CORE}$ is critical for full marks.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1: Compute the duty cycle $d$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 60 \text{ s} \\
t_{active} &= 50 \text{ ms} = 0.05 \text{ s} \\
d &= \frac{t_{active}}{T} = \frac{0.05}{60} = 8.333 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the average current $I_{avg}$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{avg} &= I_{active} \cdot d + I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - d) \\
        &= (20 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (8.333 \times 10^{-4}) + (2 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (0.99917) \\
        &= 1.667 \times 10^{-5} + 1.998 \times 10^{-6} \\
        &= 1.867 \times 10^{-5} \text{ A} \\
        &= 18.67 \;\mu A
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the battery life**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{bat} &= \frac{C_{bat}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{1000 \text{ mAh}}{0.01867 \text{ mA}} \\
        &= 53{,}562 \text{ hours} \\
        &= \frac{53{,}562}{24 \times 365} \text{ years} \\
        &= 6.11 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** With proper duty cycling, the node can operate for **over 6 years** on a single Li-ion cell — a dramatic improvement over continuous-run mode, which would last only **50 hours**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Examiner's Warning):** Students frequently forget to convert units consistently. If you use mA for current and mAh for capacity, the result is in **hours**. Mixing seconds and hours, or mA with A, will cost you 1–2 marks. Always write the unit next to the number.

### Question B — 14 Marks [Internal Choice Alternative]

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is clock gating? Explain how the `RCC->APBxENR` registers of the STM32 are used to disable individual peripheral clocks. Why is clock gating more efficient than turning off the peripheral's power supply?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write the complete C code (using STM32 HAL/PWR libraries) to configure the RTC wake-up timer to wake the microcontroller from **Standby mode** every 30 seconds, and toggle an LED after each wake-up.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Clock gating** is the technique of selectively enabling or disabling the clock signal supplied to a peripheral. In CMOS logic, a flip-flop that does not toggle consumes **near-zero dynamic power** (since $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$, and $f \to 0$ when the clock is gated). On the STM32, each peripheral is connected to either the **APB1** or **APB2** bus, and each has a dedicated enable bit in `RCC->APB1ENR` or `RCC->APB2ENR`. Setting the bit to 0 disables the clock; setting to 1 re-enables it.

**Why clock gating is preferred over power gating:**
1. **Speed of re-enable:** Clock gating takes **1 bus cycle** to re-enable. Power gating requires a full power-on transient ($\sim 10$–$100 \mu s$) plus re-initialization of the peripheral registers.
2. **State retention:** Clock gating preserves all peripheral registers and configuration. Power gating (Standby) loses the state.
3. **Simplicity of firmware:** No need to save/restore configuration; just toggle a bit.

**Example code to disable USART2 clock:**

```c
RCC->APB1ENR &= ~RCC_APB1ENR_USART2EN;  /* Disable USART2 clock */
```

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

```c
#include "stm32f4xx_hal.h"

RTC_HandleTypeDef hrtc;

void SystemClock_Config(void);
void RTC_Init(void);
void Enter_Standby_With_RTC_Wakeup(void);

int main(void) {
    HAL_Init();
    SystemClock_Config();
    RTC_Init();
    
    while (1) {
        /* Toggle LED to indicate wake-up */
        HAL_GPIO_TogglePin(GPIOA, GPIO_PIN_5);
        
        /* Enter Standby mode — wake after 30 seconds via RTC */
        Enter_Standby_With_RTC_Wakeup();
        
        /* On wake, code restarts from main() (full POR) */
    }
}

void RTC_Init(void) {
    /* 1. Enable PWR clock and backup domain access */
    __HAL_RCC_PWR_CLK_ENABLE();
    HAL_PWR_EnableBkUpAccess();
    
    /* 2. Select LSE (32.768 kHz) as RTC clock source */
    __HAL_RCC_LSE_CONFIG(RCC_LSE_ON);
    while (!__HAL_RCC_GET_FLAG(RCC_FLAG_LSERDY));
    
    /* 3. Configure RTC */
    hrtc.Instance = RTC;
    hrtc.Init.HourFormat = RTC_HOURFORMAT_24;
    hrtc.Init.AsynchPrediv = 127;     /* 32.768 kHz / 128 = 256 Hz */
    hrtc.Init.SynchPrediv = 255;      /* 256 Hz / 256 = 1 Hz */
    hrtc.Init.OutPut = RTC_OUTPUT_DISABLE;
    HAL_RTC_Init(&hrtc);
    
    /* 4. Configure Wakeup Timer for 30 seconds */
    HAL_RTCEx_SetWakeUpTimer(&hrtc, 30, RTC_WAKEUPCLOCK_CK_SPRE_16BITS);
}

void Enter_Standby_With_RTC_Wakeup(void) {
    /* 1. Enable wake-up pin (optional, for button wake) */
    HAL_PWR_EnableWakeUpPin(PWR_WAKEUP_PIN1);
    
    /* 2. Clear wake-up flag */
    __HAL_PWR_CLEAR_FLAG(PWR_FLAG_WU);
    __HAL_PWR_CLEAR_FLAG(PWR_FLAG_SB);
    
    /* 3. Enter Standby mode */
    HAL_PWR_EnterSTANDBYMode();
    
    /* Code never returns here — full reset on wake */
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout — Common KTU Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to call `HAL_PWR_EnableBkUpAccess()`** before writing to RTC registers. The backup domain is write-protected.
> 2. **Selecting wrong wake-up timer clock source** — `CK_SPRE` is the 1 Hz clock from the RTC prescaler, *not* the RTC timer clock.
> 3. **Failing to clear `WUF` and `SBF` flags** before re-entering Standby — the chip will not enter Standby if a wake-up flag is still pending.
> 4. **Expecting code to continue after `__WFI()` in Standby** — Standby causes a **full Power-On Reset**. Code restarts from `main()`. This is a major conceptual point that many students miss in KTU exams.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Power dissipation** in CMOS has two components: **dynamic** ($\alpha C V^2 f$) and **static** ($V I_{leak}$).
- **Three low-power modes** (in increasing power savings): **Sleep → Stop → Standby**.
- **Sleep mode** stops the CPU but keeps all peripheral clocks active. Wake-up latency: **0 cycles**.
- **Stop mode** stops all clocks but **retains SRAM**. Wake-up latency: **$\sim 5$–$10 \mu s$**.
- **Standby mode** shuts down the voltage regulator and **loses SRAM** contents. Wake-up latency: **$\sim 200 \mu s$**.
- The **voltage regulator** (LDO/SMPS) is controlled by `PWR->CR` bits `LPDS` and `PDDS`.
- The **deep-sleep entry** requires setting `SCB->SCR.SLEEPDEEP = 1` followed by `__WFI()` or `__WFE()`.
- **Wake-up sources:** Sleep (any IRQ), Stop (EXTI, RTC, IWDG, USB), Standby (WKUP pin, RTC, IWDG, NRST).
- **Clock gating** via `RCC->APBxENR` is the **fastest and simplest** method to save power when a peripheral is idle, with **zero state loss**.
- **Battery life formula:** $t_{bat} = \dfrac{C_{bat}}{I_{active} \cdot d + I_{sleep} \cdot (1 - d)}$.
- **Voltage scaling** can reduce dynamic power by up to **$V^2$** — most effective single optimization.
- **RTC backup domain** ($V_{BAT}$) remains powered in all low-power modes, including Standby.
- **Standby causes a full POR** — code restarts from `main()`. Configure all I/O and clocks again after wake-up.
- **PWR library functions:** `PWR_EnterSTOPMode()`, `PWR_EnterSTANDBYMode()`, `PWR_WakeUpPinCmd()`, `HAL_RTCEx_SetWakeUpTimer()`.
- **KTU mark-grabber trick:** Always mention **typical current values** (in $\mu A$) and **wake-up times** (in $\mu s$) when describing low-power modes — examiners reward quantitative answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Integrated Development Environment and HAL
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Integrated Development Environment and HAL

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Integrated Development Environment (IDE)

### Formal KTU-Style Definition
An **Integrated Development Environment (IDE)** is a unified software application that consolidates all the essential tools required for embedded firmware development into a single graphical interface. In the context of microcontrollers, an IDE bundles a **source code editor**, **cross-compiler**, **assembler**, **linker**, **loader**, **debugger**, **simulator/emulator interface**, and **project manager** to facilitate end-to-end firmware design, compilation, programming, and on-target debugging for a specific microcontroller family.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The course PBCST504 emphasizes the use of **STM32CubeIDE** (the official Eclipse-based IDE from STMicroelectronics) along with the **STM32 HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer)** libraries. Mastery of this toolchain is mandatory for all lab-based assessments and university practical examinations.

### Common IDEs Used in Microcontroller Programming (KTU Perspective)

| IDE | Vendor / Origin | Target MCU Family | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **STM32CubeIDE** | STMicroelectronics | STM32 (ARM Cortex-M) | **Primary IDE** in KTU syllabus |
| Keil µVision 5 | ARM / Keil | ARM Cortex-M, 8051 | Widely used industry tool |
| IAR Embedded Workbench | IAR Systems | ARM, MSP430, AVR, etc. | Commercial / industrial |
| MPLAB X IDE | Microchip | PIC, dsPIC, AVR (new) | Common in other universities |
| Arduino IDE | Open Source | AVR, ARM, ESP | Entry-level / hobbyist |
| PlatformIO | Open Source (VS Code) | Multi-architecture | Modern cross-platform |
| Atmel Studio (Microchip Studio) | Microchip | AVR, SAM (ARM) | Legacy but still in use |

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Chef's Kitchen" Intuition
> [!TIP]
> **Imagine an IDE as a fully equipped, modern restaurant kitchen:** The **editor** is your chopping board where you prepare ingredients (write code). The **compiler** is the gas stove that cooks the raw ingredients (translates C to assembly). The **linker** is the plating chef that combines multiple dishes (merges object files) into a single meal (executable). The **debugger** is the food critic that tastes the dish and tells you which spice is too much (finds runtime errors). The **programmer** is the waiter who delivers the final dish to the customer (flashes the firmware into the MCU). Without the IDE, you would need to buy all these tools from different shops and connect them manually — a tedious and error-prone process!

## 1.2 Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)

### Formal KTU-Style Definition
A **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)** is a software abstraction layer, generally implemented as a set of C functions, macros, data structures, and configuration templates, that hides the low-level hardware details (register addresses, bit positions, peripheral control logic) of a microcontroller behind a uniform, portable, and high-level Application Programming Interface (API). It allows the application developer to control peripherals (GPIO, UART, SPI, I2C, ADC, Timers, etc.) through standardized function calls without directly manipulating the underlying hardware registers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Definition (Standard Expected Answer):**
> *"HAL is a software component that provides a high-level interface between the application program and the physical hardware of a microcontroller, enabling portability, modularity, and faster development by eliminating direct register-level programming."*

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal TV Remote"
> [!TIP]
> **Think of HAL as a universal TV remote control.** Every TV (hardware) has different internal circuitry (registers), but your remote has **standard buttons**: Power, Volume Up/Down, Channel Change, Input Source. You don't need to know *how* the TV processes these signals — you just press the button (call the HAL function). The remote (HAL) translates your button press into the specific infrared signal (register manipulation) the TV understands. If you buy a different TV (migrate to a different MCU), the same remote buttons still work — the manufacturer has simply updated the internal translation table. This is **portability**!

### Why HAL Exists — The Three Pillars

1. **Portability** — Code written using HAL for STM32F4 can often be reused on STM32L4 with minimal changes (function names remain the same).
2. **Productivity** — A simple LED blink takes ~3 lines with HAL vs ~15 lines of register-level code.
3. **Readability** — `HAL_GPIO_WritePin(GPIOA, GPIO_PIN_5, GPIO_PIN_SET);` is self-documenting.

---

## 1.3 Position of HAL in the Embedded Software Stack
