# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Q: Differentiate between Common Anode and Common Cathode seven-segment displays. Which type is preferred when interfacing directly with a microcontroller port, and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Common Anode (CA) | Common Cathode (CC) |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Common pin connected to | +Vcc | GND |
| Segment illumination logic | Logic 0 (LOW) | Logic 1 (HIGH) |
| Current direction | From Vcc to MCU pin (sinking) | From MCU pin to GND (sourcing) |
| Drive capability required | MCU must sink current | MCU must source current |

**Preferred Type:** *Common Cathode* is generally preferred when interfacing directly with microcontroller ports because:
1. Standard 8051/CMOS ports can source **10–20 mA** per pin (sufficient for direct drive).
2. Logic 1 represents an "ON" state, which is more intuitive in software.
3. Common Anode displays need a driver IC (e.g., ULN2003) because the MCU cannot sink enough current safely on all 8 pins simultaneously.

> **Word count guidance:** ~80–100 words, 3 marks.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Q: Why is 4-bit interfacing mode commonly used for the HD44780 LCD in 8051-based systems, even though 8-bit mode is faster?**

**Model Answer:**

The **4-bit mode** is preferred for HD44780 LCD interfacing with the 8051 for the following reasons:

1. **Port Pin Conservation:** The 8051 has only four 8-bit I/O ports. Using 8-bit mode consumes an entire port (8 pins) for data, plus 3 control pins. In 4-bit mode, only **4 data pins + 3 control pins = 7 pins** are required, leaving more pins free for other peripherals.

2. **Cost-Effectiveness:** Fewer wiring connections on the PCB reduce manufacturing cost and complexity.

3. **Adequate Speed:** Although 4-bit mode requires two transfers per byte (nibble by nibble), the human eye cannot perceive the difference for typical character updates. The maximum instruction execution time is ~1.53 ms, which is well within acceptable response limits.

4. **Standard Practice:** The HD44780 is specifically designed to support 4-bit mode natively, and most reference designs (including Keil example projects) use 4-bit mode by default.

> **Word count guidance:** ~120 words, 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the interfacing of a 4-digit multiplexed 7-segment display with the 8051 microcontroller. Show the segment encoding for displaying the digit "5" on a common-cathode display.

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 architecture)*

**Working Principle of Multiplexing:**
In a multiplexed 4-digit display, all the segment lines `a`–`g, dp` of all 4 digits are tied together (common segment bus), but the **common pins** of each digit are driven separately by digit-select lines (e.g., through transistors).

**Step-by-Step Operation:**
1. The 8051 sends the segment code for digit 1 on PORT P1 (segment bus).
2. It activates digit-select line 1 (via P3.0) for ~5 ms.
3. It then sends the segment code for digit 2 on PORT P1.
4. It activates digit-select line 2 (via P3.1) for ~5 ms.
5. This cycle repeats for all 4 digits at a refresh rate of 1 / (4 × 5 ms) = **50 Hz**.
6. Due to **persistence of vision**, the human eye perceives all 4 digits as continuously lit.

**Segment Code for "5" on Common Cathode Display:**
For digit "5", the segments that must be ON are: `a, c, d, f, g`. Segments `b` and `e` are OFF.

| Bit position (P1.7 → P1.0) | dp | g | f | e | d | c | b | a |
|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|----|
| State | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| Value | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 |

Reading P1.7 to P1.0: `01101101` in binary = **0x6D** in hexadecimal.

**Final Hex Code sent to PORT P1 = 0x6D**

**Current Calculation:** Assuming $I_F = 15$ mA, $V_F = 2.0$ V, $V_{CC} = 5$ V:
$$R_L = \frac{5 - 2}{15 \text{ mA}} = 200 \text{ }\Omega$$

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the initialization sequence of the HD44780 LCD in 4-bit mode. Why are multiple "0x30" commands sent before switching to 4-bit mode?

**Model Answer:**

**HD44780 4-bit Mode Initialization Sequence:**

| Step | Command (Hex) | Function | Delay Required |
|------|---------------|----------|----------------|
| 1 | — | Power-on stabilization | ≥ 15 ms |
| 2 | **0x30** | Function Set (8-bit mode forced) | ≥ 4.1 ms |
| 3 | **0x30** | Function Set (8-bit mode forced) | ≥ 100 µs |
| 4 | **0x30** | Function Set (8-bit mode forced) | ≥ 100 µs |
| 5 | **0x20** | Switch to 4-bit mode | None |
| 6 | **0x28** | 4-bit, 2 lines, 5×8 font | None |
| 7 | **0x0C** | Display ON, cursor OFF, blink OFF | None |
| 8 | **0x06** | Entry mode: increment, no shift | None |
| 9 | **0x01** | Clear display | ≥ 1.53 ms |
| 10 | **0x80** | Set DDRAM address to line 1 start | None |

**Why multiple "0x30" commands?**

The HD44780 controller has an internal reset circuit that is triggered at power-on, but the exact timing of this reset depends on the **rise time of Vcc** and the **clock source** (internal RC oscillator of the LCD). To ensure reliable initialization regardless of power-on conditions, the standard procedure (as recommended by the Hitachi datasheet) is to:

1. **First 0x30:** Issued after the mandatory 15 ms power-on delay. This forces the LCD into 8-bit mode even if the internal reset is incomplete.
2. **Second 0x30:** Issued after 4.1 ms. This guarantees the LCD has had time to process the first command and is now synchronized.
3. **Third 0x30:** Issued after 100 µs. This confirms 8-bit mode is locked in.
4. **0x20:** This single command switches the LCD from 8-bit to 4-bit mode.

**Reason Summary:** Sending three 0x30 commands before 0x20 ensures that the HD44780 is reliably in a known state, regardless of whether its internal power-on reset has completed. Skipping these steps can cause the LCD to display garbage characters or fail to initialize in 4-bit mode — a common student pitfall in lab examinations.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Describe the working of a 4×4 matrix keypad interfaced to the 8051 microcontroller. Explain the row-column scanning algorithm with a flowchart and derive the key identification formula.

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**
A 4×4 matrix keypad consists of 16 mechanical push-button switches arranged at the intersections of 4 row lines (R0–R3) and 4 column lines (C0–C3). Each key, when pressed, electrically connects one row to one column.

**Connection to 8051:**
- Rows R0–R3 → Connected to PORT P1.0–P1.3 (configured as OUTPUT).
- Columns C0–C3 → Connected to PORT P1.4–P1.7 (configured as INPUT with pull-ups).

**Row-Column Scanning Algorithm:**

1. **Initialization:** Configure lower nibble of P1 as OUTPUT (rows), upper nibble as INPUT (columns). Set all rows HIGH initially.
2. **Scanning Loop (Row-by-Row):**
   - Drive R0 LOW, keep R1, R2, R3 HIGH. Read columns.
   - If any column reads LOW, key (R0, Cj) is pressed.
   - Repeat for R1, R2, R3.
3. **Debouncing:** Wait 20 ms, then re-verify the keypress.
4. **Encoding:** Use the formula to determine the key value.

**Flowchart:** *(Refer to Section 4.3 flowchart)*

**Key Identification Formula Derivation:**

For an $m \times n$ matrix keypad, the total number of keys is $N = m \times n$. If we number keys sequentially from 0, starting with row 0:

$$\text{Key Index} = (\text{RowIndex}) \times n + (\text{ColumnIndex})$$

For a 4×4 keypad ($m = 4$, $n = 4$):
$$\text{Key Index} = (\text{RowIndex}) \times 4 + (\text{ColumnIndex})$$

**Example:** If the algorithm detects a keypress at Row 2, Column 1:
$$\text{Key Index} = 2 \times 4 + 1 = 9$$

This index 9 is then mapped to the character "8" using a lookup table:
```c
unsigned char keymap[4][4] = {
    {'1','2','3','A'},
    {'4','5','6','B'},
    {'7','8','9','C'},
    {'*','0','#','D'}
};
char pressed_key = keymap[2][1]; // returns '8'
```

**Pin Saving Calculation:**
- Direct connection would require 16 I/O pins.
- Matrix connection requires only 8 I/O pins.
- Pins saved = $16 - 8 = \mathbf{8}$ (50% reduction).

---

**Q (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare the seven-segment display, 16×2 LCD, and matrix keypad in terms of (i) information content, (ii) power consumption, (iii) interface complexity, and (iv) typical applications.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | 7-Segment Display | 16×2 LCD (HD44780) | 4×4 Matrix Keypad |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Information Content** | Numeric (0–9) + limited alpha (A–F) | 32 alphanumeric characters (5×8 dot matrix each) | 16 user-defined symbols/commands |
| **Power Consumption** | High (7 LEDs × 10–20 mA = 70–140 mA per digit in worst case) | Low (~1–2 mA logic, +20 mA backlight) | Negligible (~µA) — passive input device |
| **Interface Complexity** | Simple: 8 segment lines + 1 common pin per digit | Moderate: 4 data + 3 control lines (4-bit mode) | Simple: 4 row + 4 column lines |
| **Viewing Angle / Visibility** | Excellent (180°, high brightness) | Limited (60–90° optimal, contrast-dependent) | N/A (input device) |
| **Cost** | Low (₹10–₹30 per digit) | Moderate (₹80–₹150) | Low (₹20–₹50) |
| **Typical Applications** | Digital clocks, calculators, scoreboards, fuel pumps | Washing machines, medical instruments, vending machines | ATMs, microwave ovens, security locks, telephones |

**Engineering Selection Guidelines:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use 7-segment display** when:
> - Displaying only numbers (e.g., temperature readings, counters, scores).
> - High brightness is required (outdoor displays, instrument panels).
> - Cost and simplicity are critical.

> **Use 16×2 LCD** when:
> - Displaying text messages, sensor readings with units, or alphanumeric status.
> - Low power consumption is essential (battery-powered devices).
> - User feedback (menus, prompts) is needed.

> **Use Matrix Keypad** when:
> - Multiple user inputs (numeric or function) are required.
> - I/O pin conservation is critical (microcontroller has limited ports).
> - A simple, low-cost input mechanism is sufficient (no touchscreen needed).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Segment Code Errors:** Students often write segment codes in reverse bit order. Always specify the bit-to-segment mapping clearly (e.g., P1.7 = dp, P1.0 = a). Drawing the bit-pattern table earns full marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing CA and CC Logic Levels:** In Common Anode, "ON" segment = logic 0. In Common Cathode, "ON" segment = logic 1. Confusing these leads to all segments being inverted — a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Current-Limiting Resistor:** A 7-segment LED WITHOUT a current-limiting resistor will burn out within seconds. Examiners specifically check for this in the circuit diagram.
>
> 4. **Skipping LCD Initialization Steps:** Writing `Lcd_Init()` as a black box without explaining the HD44780 command sequence loses 2–3 marks. Always show the step-by-step 0x30 → 0x20 → 0x28 → 0x0C → 0x06 → 0x01 sequence.
>
> 5. **Wrong Debounce Delay:** Using a 1 µs or 100 µs delay is too short. The correct range is **20–50 ms**. Examiners know that mechanical bounce lasts 5–20 ms.
>
> 6. **Keypad Scanning Without Pull-up Resistors:** Students often forget to mention internal pull-ups or external 10 kΩ resistors on column lines. Without these, floating inputs cause ghost keypresses.
>
> 7. **Mixing Up 4-bit and 8-bit Mode:** In 4-bit mode, the upper nibble (D4–D7) is used. Students sometimes wire D0–D3 and the LCD never initializes.
>
> 8. **No Multiplex Refresh Rate Mentioned:** When explaining multiplexed SSDs, always state the refresh frequency (≥50 Hz). Examiners look for the **persistence of vision** concept.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] **7-Segment Display:** 7 LEDs + 1 dp, arranged as "8". Two types: **CA (active LOW)** and **CC (active HIGH)**. Always use a current-limiting resistor $R_L = (V_{CC} - V_F) / I_F \approx 200\text{–}330 \text{ }\Omega$.
- [ ] **Hex Code for "0"** on CC display = **0x3F**; for "1" = **0x06**; for "8" = **0x7F**; for "9" = **0x6F**.
- [ ] **Multiplexing:** Only one digit ON at a time; refresh at ≥ 50 Hz; duty cycle = $1/n$; saves 75% current for 4 digits.
- [ ] **LCD HD44780:** 16 pins; 4-bit mode uses **D4–D7** + RS, R/W, E; initialized by sending 0x30 three times, then 0x20, then 0x28, 0x0C, 0x06, 0x01.
- [ ] **LCD DDRAM Address:** Line 1 starts at **0x80**; Line 2 starts at **0xC0**; cursor auto-increments on write.
- [ ] **LCD Clear Display command** = **0x01** (takes 1.53 ms); **Return Home** = **0x02** (takes 1.53 ms).
- [ ] **Matrix Keypad Pin Saving:** $4 \times 4 \rightarrow 8$ pins (50% saved); $8 \times 8 \rightarrow 16$ pins (75% saved).
- [ ] **Keypad Scanning:** Drive one row LOW at a time, read all columns. A LOW column indicates the pressed key.
- [ ] **Debounce Delay:** 20–50 ms (mechanical bounce period).
- [ ] **Key Encoding Formula:** $\text{Key Index} = i \times n_{cols} + j$ (for 0-indexed $i$, $j$).
- [ ] **Persistence of Vision:** ~50 Hz refresh rate fools the eye into seeing continuous illumination.
- [ ] **Contrast Control on LCD:** Adjust VEE pin via a 10 kΩ potentiometer between Vcc and GND.
- [ ] **Backlight:** Connect LED+ (pin 15) through a small resistor (typically 10–47 Ω) to +5V; LED− (pin 16) to GND.

### 📌 One-Line Exam-Ready Facts

> *"A Common Cathode 7-segment display requires a logic HIGH to illuminate a segment; current-limiting resistors of ~220 Ω are mandatory."*

> *"The HD44780 LCD requires three 0x30 commands during initialization to guarantee reliable 4-bit mode entry."*

> *"A 4×4 matrix keypad saves 50% of I/O pins compared to direct connection by using a row-column scanning technique with debounce delay of 20–50 ms."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relay Interfacing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relay Interfacing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Relay Interfacing** is the process of establishing a controlled electrical and electronic coupling between a low-power digital output port of a microcontroller (e.g., 8051 P1.x, ARM Cortex-M GPIO) and an **electromechanical relay**, such that a logic-level signal (typically **0 V or 3.3 V / 5 V**) reliably energizes or de-energizes the relay coil to switch an external high-voltage/high-current load.

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PBCST504 – Module 2):**
> *Relay interfacing with microcontrollers involves the use of driver transistors (BJT/MOSFET), free-wheeling/flyback diodes, and optionally optocouplers, to provide galvanic isolation and current amplification between the MCU I/O pin and the relay coil.*

## 1.2 Relay — Quick Anatomy

An electromagnetic relay consists of two electrically isolated sub-systems:

| Sub-System | Function | Typical Rating |
|---|---|---|
| **Coil (Primary Side)** | Generates magnetic flux when energized | 5 V, 12 V, 24 V DC; coil resistance 50 Ω – 700 Ω |
| **Contacts (Secondary Side)** | Mechanical switch — NO/NC/COM | 230 V AC / 5 A – 30 A DC |

The two sides are **galvanically isolated** by air-gap + insulation, meaning the controlling circuit never makes direct electrical contact with the load circuit.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Heavy Door and the Light Switch**
> Imagine a **massive iron gate** that requires an adult to push it open, but the gate is in a room you can only access via a **small push button** on the wall.
> - The **push button** = Microcontroller output pin (weak signal, ~5 mA).
> - The **hidden mechanical linkage inside the wall** = Transistor driver circuit.
> - The **solenoid/latch that actually pushes the gate** = Relay coil.
> - The **gate itself** = The high-power load (motor, bulb, heater).
> - A **spring that yanks the linkage back when released** = Flyback diode (protects the wall's electronics from the kickback).
>
> Just as the small button cannot directly move the heavy gate, an MCU pin cannot directly energize a relay coil. A **driver stage** must amplify the signal.

## 1.4 Why Interfacing Is Mandatory — The Current Mismatch

- **Typical MCU pin (8051/ARM GPIO):** Sinks/sources **5 mA – 20 mA**, Voltage = **3.3 V or 5 V**.
- **Typical relay coil current:** **30 mA – 100 mA** (5 V coil) up to **500 mA** (24 V industrial relay).

Therefore, **direct connection will damage the MCU port pin** — a driver stage is non-negotiable.

## 1.5 Common Relay Types Used with MCUs

- **SPDT (Single Pole Double Throw)** — most common; has COM, NO, NC.
- **DPDT (Double Pole Double Throw)** — switches two independent circuits.
- **Reed Relay** — fast, hermetically sealed; used in low-power telecom.
- **Solid State Relay (SSR)** — uses TRIAC/phototriac instead of mechanical contacts; no moving parts, longer life, silent operation.
- **latching Relay** — maintains state without continuous coil current.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Operational Theory — Step-by-Step Logic

The relay interface circuit is constructed in **four logical blocks**:

### Block A — The Microcontroller Source
The MCU output pin (e.g., P1.0 of 8051) produces a **logic HIGH (e.g., +5 V)** or **logic LOW (0 V)**. This is the *control signal*, but it lacks the current capability to drive the relay coil.

### Block B — The Driver Transistor (BJT or MOSFET)
The transistor operates in the **saturation region** when ON, behaving as a closed switch between the collector and emitter. The base current is derived from the MCU pin through a **base resistor (Rb)**.

- For an **NPN BJT (BC547, 2N2222, SL100):**  
  MCU HIGH → base current flows → collector–emitter conducts → relay coil is pulled to ground → coil energizes.
- For an **n-channel MOSFET (IRF540N, BS170):**  
  MCU HIGH → Vgs exceeds threshold → Rds(on) very low → relay coil energizes.

### Block C — The Flyback / Free-Wheeling Diode
When the relay coil is **switched OFF**, the collapsing magnetic field induces a **high reverse voltage spike (back-EMF)** of several hundred volts across the coil, with a polarity opposite to the supply. A **1N4007 (or 1N4148 for fast switching)** diode is placed in **reverse-bias across the coil** to provide a safe path for the inductive kickback, protecting the transistor from avalanche breakdown.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polarity Rule:** The diode's **cathode** connects to the **positive (Vcc)** end of the coil, and the **anode** connects to the **collector** (transistor side). This ensures the diode is reverse-biased during normal operation and forward-biased only during the flyback.

### Block D — The Load Circuit (Contact Side)
The relay's COM contact connects to the load supply (e.g., 230 V AC for a lamp, 24 V DC for a solenoid). The NO or NC contact is routed to the load. The relay contacts are **completely isolated** from the coil side — there is no electrical continuity, only magnetic coupling.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ohm's Law — base resistor | $R_b = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_{BE}}{I_B}$ | $\Omega$ | $V_{BE} \approx 0.7$ V for silicon BJT |
| 2 | Minimum base current (saturation) | $I_{B(min)} = \dfrac{I_C}{\beta_{forced}}$ | A | Use $\beta_{forced} = 10$ for guaranteed saturation |
| 3 | Relay coil current | $I_{coil} = \dfrac{V_{CC}}{R_{coil}}$ | A | $R_{coil}$ specified in relay datasheet |
| 4 | Collector current (≈ coil current) | $I_C \approx I_{coil}$ | A | When transistor is saturated, full coil current flows |
| 5 | Power dissipated in driving transistor | $P_T = V_{CE(sat)} \times I_C$ | W | $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V |
| 6 | Energy stored in relay coil inductance | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}$ | J | Released as flyback spike if no diode |
| 7 | Flyback voltage without diode | $V_{spike} = -L \dfrac{di}{dt}$ | V | Can reach 100 V – 500 V |
| 8 | LED current-limiting resistor | $R_{LED} = \dfrac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_{LED}}$ | $\Omega$ | $V_F \approx 1.8$ V for red LED; $I_{LED} = 10$ mA |
| 9 | Optocoupler CTR | $CTR = \dfrac{I_C}{I_F} \times 100\%$ | % | Typical CTR = 20 % – 50 % for PC817 |
| 10 | Relay switching time | $t_{on} \approx 5\text{ ms} - 15\text{ ms}$ | s | Mechanical relay inertia; SSRs switch in $\mu$s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Boundary Condition:** For BJT saturation, the rule of thumb is to **overdrive the base** by a factor of 2–10 over the minimum $I_B$. This is why $\beta_{forced} = 10$ is chosen even though the device's DC $\beta$ might be 100+.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Automation:** PLCs use relay outputs to switch motors, solenoids, contactors.
- **Home Automation:** Smart switches (Sonoff, Tuya) use 5 V relays driven by ESP8266/ESP32.
- **Automotive ECUs:** Relays switch headlights, fuel pumps, cooling fans.
- **Prototyping Boards:** Arduino Relay Shield uses opto-isolated 5 V SPDT relays (Songle SRD-05VDC-SL-C).
- **Safety Systems:** Galvanic isolation prevents the MCU from being destroyed by inductive kickback or mains surges.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Design Derivation — Driving a 5 V, 100 mA Relay with 8051 Port Pin

> **Problem Statement:** A 5 V SPDT relay (model: G5LE-14-DC5) has a coil resistance of $R_{coil} = 50\ \Omega$ and is to be driven by an 8051 P1.0 pin. The driver transistor is a **BC547** NPN BJT with $\beta_{DC} = 200$ and $V_{BE(sat)} = 0.7$ V. The MCU HIGH level is $V_{OH} = 4.8$ V. Design the base resistor $R_b$ to guarantee saturation. Also verify the flyback diode current handling and power dissipation.

### Step 1 — Compute the Relay Coil Current

This is the **collector current** when the transistor is ON.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{coil} &= \frac{V_{CC}}{R_{coil}} \\
I_{coil} &= \frac{5.0\ \text{V}}{50\ \Omega} \\
I_{coil} &= 0.100\ \text{A} = 100\ \text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The relay coil acts like a simple resistor at DC steady state, so Ohm's Law gives the current drawn by the coil. This same current flows through the transistor collector.

### Step 2 — Determine the Minimum Base Current for Saturation

The transistor must be **fully saturated** (acting as a closed switch) to drop minimal voltage across itself and deliver full $V_{CC}$ to the relay coil.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{C} &\approx I_{coil} = 100\ \text{mA} \\
\beta_{forced} &= 10 \quad \text{(chosen for hard saturation)} \\
I_{B(min)} &= \frac{I_C}{\beta_{forced}} \\
I_{B(min)} &= \frac{100\ \text{mA}}{10} \\
I_{B(min)} &= 10\ \text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** Although the BC547's DC current gain is 200, relying on such a high $\beta$ is risky because $\beta$ falls with temperature, age, and collector current. Forcing $\beta_{forced} = 10$ ensures the transistor is firmly saturated even under worst-case conditions.

### Step 3 — Compute the Base Resistor Value

Applying KVL to the base–emitter loop: $V_{OH} = I_B \cdot R_b + V_{BE}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_b &= \frac{V_{OH} - V_{BE}}{I_{B(min)}} \\
R_b &= \frac{4.8\ \text{V} - 0.7\ \text{V}}{10\ \text{mA}} \\
R_b &= \frac{4.1\ \text{V}}{0.010\ \text{A}} \\
R_b &= 410\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** A 410 Ω resistor drops 4.1 V at 10 mA, leaving the required 0.7 V across the base–emitter junction. A standard **E12 value of 470 Ω** is selected to provide slightly more base current than the minimum, ensuring robust saturation.

### Step 4 — Verify the Actual Base Current with Chosen Resistor

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{B(actual)} &= \frac{V_{OH} - V_{BE}}{R_{b(actual)}} \\
I_{B(actual)} &= \frac{4.1\ \text{V}}{470\ \Omega} \\
I_{B(actual)} &= 8.72\ \text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta_{actual} &= \frac{I_C}{I_{B(actual)}} = \frac{100\ \text{mA}}{8.72\ \text{mA}} = 11.47
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The actual forced $\beta$ is 11.47, which is still well within the saturation region. The transistor is now guaranteed to be in saturation for all operating conditions.

### Step 5 — Power Dissipation in the Transistor

When the relay is **ON**, the transistor is saturated with $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_T &= V_{CE(sat)} \times I_C \\
P_T &= 0.2\ \text{V} \times 0.100\ \text{A} \\
P_T &= 0.020\ \text{W} = 20\ \text{mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** A standard TO-92 package (BC547) is rated for 500 mW, so 20 mW is negligible. Heat-sinking is **not required**.

### Step 6 — Power Dissipation in the Base Resistor

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{R_b} &= (V_{OH} - V_{BE}) \times I_{B(actual)} \\
P_{R_b} &= 4.1\ \text{V} \times 8.72\ \text{mA} \\
P_{R_b} &= 35.75\ \text{mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** A standard ¼-watt (250 mW) resistor is more than adequate.

### Step 7 — Flyback Diode Selection

The relay coil inductance is typically $L \approx 50\ \text{mH} - 200\ \text{mH}$. Assuming $L = 100\ \text{mH}$ and a turn-off time $t_{off} = 1\ \text{ms}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{spike} &= L \cdot \frac{di}{dt} \\
V_{spike} &= 0.1\ \text{H} \times \frac{0.100\ \text{A}}{0.001\ \text{s}} \\
V_{spike} &= 10\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Logic:** The peak reverse voltage across the coil at turn-off is 10 V, but actual spikes (with faster $di/dt$ due to contact bounce) can reach 50 V – 100 V. A **1N4007** (rated 1000 V reverse, 1 A continuous) is more than sufficient. For fast-switching relays, a **1N4148** (fast recovery) is preferred.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Design Trade-Offs

| Design Parameter | BJT (BC547) | MOSFET (IRF540N) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Control voltage needed | $V_{BE} \approx 0.7$ V | $V_{GS(th)} \approx 2\text{–}4$ V | MOSFET is ideal for 3.3 V MCUs |
| Input current | ~10 mA | ~0.1 mA (gate is capacitive) | MOSFET draws negligible DC current |
| Switching speed | Slower (µs) | Very fast (ns) | MOSFET preferred for PWM |
| Voltage rating | $V_{CEO} = 45$ V | $V_{DS} = 100$ V | MOSFET handles higher loads |
| Cost | ~₹2 | ~₹25 | BJT is cheaper |
| Static power in driver | Higher | Negligible | MOSFET is more efficient |
| Recommended for | Educational, low-side switching | High-frequency, high-current loads | KTU exam default = BJT |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Typical Exam Hook:** When using a **3.3 V MCU (ARM Cortex-M0/M4)**, a BC547 with $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V and the need for at least 5–10 mA of base current may not reliably saturate. In such cases, a **logic-level MOSFET (e.g., 2N7000)** is preferred.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (BJT Low-Side Driver)

```mermaid
graph LR
    MCU[MICROCONTROLLER 8051 P1 0] -->|Logic HIGH 5V| Rb[Base Resistor Rb 470 ohm]
    Rb --> Base[BC547 Base Pin 2]
    Base --> BJT[BC547 NPN BJT]
    VCC1[5V DC Supply VCC] --> Coil[Relay Coil 100mA]
    Coil --> Collector[BC547 Collector Pin 3]
    Emitter[BC547 Emitter Pin 1] --> GND1[Ground]
    Diode1[Flyback Diode 1N4007] -.->|Reverse across coil| Coil
    Diode1 -.-> Collector
    BJT -->|Collector saturates| Switch
    Coil -->|Magnetic field| Switch[Relay Contacts SPDT]
    Switch -->|COM pin| LoadV[230V AC Mains]
    LoadV --> Lamp[AC Load Lamp or Motor]
    Lamp --> Neutral[AC Neutral]
    Neutral --> GND1
    OPT[Optocoupler PC817] -.->|Optional isolation| MCU
    OPT -.-> BJT
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Energization of Relay

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA[MCU Port P1 0 set to Logic HIGH] --> stepB[Current flows through Rb 470 ohm]
    stepB --> stepC[VBE exceeds 0.7V at BC547 base]
    stepC --> stepD[BC547 enters saturation region]
    stepD --> stepE[Collector current ramps up to 100mA]
    stepE --> stepF[Relay coil energizes in 5 to 15ms]
    stepF --> stepG[Magnetic field pulls armature]
    stepG --> stepH[COM contact moves from NC to NO]
    stepH --> stepI[AC load Lamp is switched ON]
    stepI --> stepJ[MCU port pulled LOW de-energize]
    stepJ --> stepK[BC547 base current ceases]
    stepK --> stepL[Transistor cutoff begins]
    stepL --> stepM[Coil collapses field induces back EMF]
    stepM --> stepN[1N4007 diode clamps spike at 0.7V]
    stepN --> stepO[Armature returns to NC contact]
    stepO --> stepP[AC load switches OFF safely]
```

## 4.3 Optocoupler-Isolated Interfacing Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    MCUA[ARM MCU GPIO 3.3V] -->|Drive current 10mA| LEDin[Opto LED inside PC817]
    LEDin --> Rin[Series Resistor 330 ohm]
    Rin --> VDD1[3.3V Supply]
    LEDin -->|Infrared photon| Phototransistor[Phototransistor inside PC817]
    Phototransistor -->|Galvanic isolation barrier 5kV| IsoGap[Air Gap Insulation]
    IsoGap --> DriverIn[Base of BC547 driver]
    VDD2[5V Relay Supply] --> RelayCoil[Relay Coil]
    RelayCoil --> Collector2[BC547 Collector]
    DriverIn --> Rb2[Base Resistor 4.7k]
    Rb2 --> Collector2
    Emitter2[BC547 Emitter] --> GND2[Isolated Ground]
    Phototransistor --> DriverIn
    Diode2[1N4007 Flyback] -.->|Reverse parallel| RelayCoil
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Benefit of Optoisolation:** The MCU ground and the relay-load ground are **electrically separate**. A voltage surge on the AC mains side cannot propagate back to destroy the microcontroller. This is the **industry standard** for medical, industrial, and high-voltage applications.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define an electromagnetic relay. Why is a driver circuit necessary when interfacing a relay with a microcontroller?**

> **Model Answer:**
> An **electromagnetic relay** is an electrically operated switch that uses an electromagnet to mechanically control one or more sets of contacts. It provides **galvanic isolation** between a low-power control circuit and a high-power load circuit.
> 
> A driver circuit is necessary because a typical microcontroller I/O pin can source/sink only **5–20 mA** at 3.3 V or 5 V, while a relay coil typically requires **30–100 mA** (or more). Direct connection would either **fail to energize the relay** or **damage the MCU port pin** due to excessive current draw. A driver transistor (BJT/MOSFET) amplifies the current, allowing the weak logic signal to switch the higher coil current.

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the function of the flyback diode connected across a relay coil? What would happen if it is omitted?**

> **Model Answer:**
> The **flyback diode** (also called a free-wheeling or snubber diode) is connected in **reverse-bias across the relay coil**. Its function is to provide a safe discharge path for the energy stored in the coil's magnetic field when the transistor is switched OFF.
> 
> If the diode is omitted, the rapidly collapsing magnetic flux induces a high reverse voltage spike (back-EMF) of **100 V – 500 V** across the coil. This spike is applied across the driving transistor, which may exceed its $V_{CEO}$ breakdown rating, causing **permanent damage or destruction** of the transistor and potentially the MCU pin.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students **omit the flyback diode** in their circuit diagrams — instant loss of **2 marks**.
> - Students often forget to show the **separate grounds** between MCU side and load side (or fail to mention galvanic isolation) — loss of **1–2 marks**.
> - In calculation problems, do **not** confuse $V_{CE(sat)}$ with $V_{CE}$ (active region). Always state $V_{CE(sat)} \approx 0.2$ V.
> - Always use $\beta_{forced} = 10$ (not the DC $\beta$ of the transistor) for saturation calculations.
> - Relay coil current must be calculated using **Ohm's Law**, not assumed arbitrarily.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain how a 5 V relay is interfaced to an 8051 microcontroller using an NPN BJT driver. Mention the role of each component in the circuit. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 12 V relay has a coil resistance of $240\ \Omega$. Design the interfacing circuit using a BC547 BJT driven by an 8051 port pin (assume $V_{OH} = 4.6$ V, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V, $\beta_{forced} = 10$). Calculate the base resistor value and verify the power dissipation. **(7 Marks)**

#### **MODEL SOLUTION (a)**

**Circuit Diagram Description:**

```
VCC(+5V) ----[RELAY COIL]---- C (Collector, BC547)
                              |
                              B (Base)
                              |
        P1.0 ----[Rb]-------- B
                              |
                              E (Emitter) ---- GND

        [Diode 1N4007 in REVERSE across coil: 
         Cathode to VCC, Anode to Collector]
```

**Components and Their Roles:**

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Microcontroller (8051, P1.0)** | Generates control logic signal (HIGH/LOW) |
| **Base Resistor (Rb)** | Limits base current to safe value, ensures BJT saturation |
| **NPN Transistor (BC547)** | Acts as a current amplifier / switch — saturates to energize coil |
| **Relay Coil** | Generates magnetic field when current flows; pulls armature to switch contacts |
| **Flyback Diode (1N4007)** | Suppresses back-EMF when coil de-energizes; protects transistor |
| **Free-Wheel Contacts (NO/NC/COM)** | Switch the external high-power load |

**Working:**
- When P1.0 = HIGH → base current flows → BC547 saturates → coil energized → COM connects to NO → load turns ON.
- When P1.0 = LOW → base current zero → BC547 cutoff → coil de-energizes → COM returns to NC → load turns OFF.

#### **MODEL SOLUTION (b)**

**Step 1 — Coil Current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{coil} &= \frac{V_{CC}}{R_{coil}} = \frac{12\ \text{V}}{240\ \Omega} = 50\ \text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Minimum Base Current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{B(min)} &= \frac{I_C}{\beta_{forced}} = \frac{50\ \text{mA}}{10} = 5\ \text{mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Base Resistor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_b &= \frac{V_{OH} - V_{BE}}{I_{B(min)}} = \frac{4.6 - 0.7}{5\ \text{mA}} = \frac{3.9}{0.005} = 780\ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Select standard E12 value: **$R_b = 820\ \Omega$**.

**Step 4 — Verify with $820\ \Omega$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{B(actual)} &= \frac{3.9}{820} = 4.76\ \text{mA} \\
\beta_{actual} &= \frac{50\ \text{mA}}{4.76\ \text{mA}} = 10.5 \quad \text{(Saturation achieved)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Power Dissipation in Transistor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_T &= V_{CE(sat)} \times I_C = 0.2 \times 0.050 = 0.010\ \text{W} = 10\ \text{mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Well below the BC547 rating of 500 mW — safe operation confirmed.)

**Result:** $R_b = 820\ \Omega$, total power consumption = $V_{CC} \times I_{coil} = 12 \times 0.050 = 600$ mW.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the role of an **optocoupler** in relay interfacing. With a circuit diagram, describe how a PC817 optocoupler is used to drive a 5 V relay from an ARM Cortex-M0 microcontroller. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **electromechanical relays (EMR)** and **solid-state relays (SSR)**. State two applications where each is preferred. **(7 Marks)**

#### **MODEL SOLUTION (a)**

**Role of Optocoupler:**
An optocoupler (e.g., **PC817**) provides **galvanic isolation** between the microcontroller side and the relay/load side. It transfers the control signal via **infrared light** across an insulating air gap (typically **5 kV isolation rating**). This protects the MCU from:
- High-voltage transients on the load side.
- Ground loops between the MCU and the load.
- Electromagnetic interference (EMI).

**Circuit Operation:**
1. ARM GPIO outputs 3.3 V HIGH → current $I_F$ flows through the optocoupler's internal LED (through a current-limiting resistor, typically $330\ \Omega$).
2. The LED emits IR photons → strikes the phototransistor base inside the optocoupler.
3. Phototransistor conducts → base current flows into the BC547 driver transistor.
4. BC547 saturates → relay coil energizes → load turns ON.
5. When GPIO goes LOW → LED off → phototransistor off → BC547 cutoff → relay OFF.

**Advantage:** The MCU and relay share **no common ground**, ensuring complete electrical isolation.

#### **MODEL SOLUTION (b)**

| Parameter | Electromechanical Relay (EMR) | Solid-State Relay (SSR) |
|---|---|---|
| Switching element | Mechanical contacts + armature | TRIAC, SCR, or phototriac |
| Moving parts | Yes (armature, spring) | No (solid-state) |
| Switching speed | 5 – 15 ms (slow) | 1 – 100 µs (very fast) |
| Lifetime | $10^5$ – $10^6$ operations | $>10^9$ operations |
| Acoustic noise | Audible click | Silent |
| Contact bounce | Yes (1 – 2 ms) | No bounce |
| On-state voltage drop | ~0 V (metal contact) | 1 – 2 V (across TRIAC) |
| Off-state leakage | 0 µA | 1 – 10 mA |
| Isolation | Galvanic (coil-contact) | Optical (LED-TRIAC) |
| Cost | Low (₹30 – ₹100) | High (₹200 – ₹2000) |
| Failure mode | Mechanical wear | Thermal runaway |

**Applications of EMR:**
- **Automotive starter relays** — handle high inrush current of starter motors.
- **Industrial motor starters** — robust switching of 3-phase induction motors.

**Applications of SSR:**
- **Medical equipment** — silent operation, no EMI from contact arcing.
- **PID temperature controllers** — fast switching of heater bands for precise control.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Relay Interfacing**

- **Definition:** Relay = electrically controlled mechanical switch; interfacing = adding driver + protection circuitry.
- **Pin Mismatch:** MCU pin provides ~5–20 mA; relay coil needs 30–500 mA → **driver mandatory**.
- **BJT Driver:** NPN (BC547) in **common-emitter** configuration, operating in **saturation/ cutoff**.
- **Base Resistor Formula:** $R_b = \dfrac{V_{OH} - V_{BE}}{I_{B(min)}}$, with $\beta_{forced} = 10$.
- **Flyback Diode:** **1N4007** in **reverse-bias** across the coil; cathode to Vcc, anode to collector.
- **Optocoupler (PC817):** Provides **galvanic isolation**; CTR = 20%–50%; input side uses separate current-limiting resistor.
- **MOSFET Alternative (2N7000, IRF540N):** Voltage-controlled, no DC gate current; ideal for 3.3 V MCUs.
- **Solid State Relay (SSR):** No moving parts, silent, long life, but higher cost and on-state voltage drop.
- **Power Calculation:** Coil power = $V_{CC} \times I_{coil}$; Transistor dissipation = $V_{CE(sat)} \times I_C$.
- **Standard Relay Pinout:** Coil pins (2), Common, NO, NC — verify with multimeter continuity test.
- **MCU I/O Direction:** Configure as **output push-pull** (not open-drain) for reliable relay switching.
- **Snubber across contacts (optional):** RC network (100 Ω + 0.1 µF) across contact to suppress arcing for inductive AC loads.
- **Inductive kickback energy:** $E = \frac{1}{2} L I^{2}$ — must be safely dissipated or it can destroy the transistor.
- **KTU 2024 Hot Topics:** Opto-isolated interfacing, MOSFET-based drivers for 3.3 V MCUs, SSR vs EMR comparison, BJT saturation design calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog to Digital Conversion: Potentiometer, Temperature Sensor, Microphone
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analog to Digital Conversion: Potentiometer, Temperature Sensor, Microphone

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Analog to Digital Conversion (ADC)** in the context of microcontroller-based embedded systems is the process of translating a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude physical signal — produced by real-world transducers such as a **potentiometer (position sensor)**, **temperature sensor (LM35/TMP36/thermistor)**, or **electret microphone (acoustic transducer)** — into a discrete binary word that the CPU can process. Per the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PBCST504 (Module 2)**, the ADC peripheral is typically a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) type**, offering **n-bit resolution**, a fixed **reference voltage (Vref)**, and configurable input channels (e.g., 8 channels on ATmega328P, 12 channels on STM32F4).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 High-Yield Concept:** A microcontroller can *only* understand digital numbers. The ADC is the *interpreter* that converts the real, messy, continuous analog world into clean, discrete binary codes. Every analog sensor (pot, temperature IC, mic) must ultimately produce a **DC or slowly-varying voltage** that lies *within* the ADC's input range, typically **0 V to Vref (often 3.3 V or 5 V)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Language Translator"

Imagine you are at a market in a foreign country, and you only understand your native tongue. The **potentiometer**, **temperature sensor**, and **microphone** are three *speakers* talking to you continuously in their own "language" — the language of *voltage levels*. The **ADC** is your personal translator who listens to the speaker and writes down a *number* (in your language) every short while. The *quality* of the translation depends on:

- **Resolution (n bits):** How many different "words" the translator knows. A 10-bit translator knows $2^{10} = 1024$ distinct words; a 12-bit translator knows $4096$ — finer meaning, fewer misunderstandings.
- **Sampling Rate (Fs):** How often the translator pauses to listen. A slow translator misses rapid speech; a fast translator is expensive.
- **Reference Voltage (Vref):** The "loudest voice" the translator can handle without distorting. Anything louder clips.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy table for the three sensors:**

| Sensor (Speaker) | What it "says" in voltage | Typical Output Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Potentiometer** | "Where am I rotated to?" | 0 V to Vcc (linear wiper) |
| **LM35 Temperature IC** | "How warm is it?" | 0 V to +1.5 V (for 0°C–150°C) |
| **Electret Microphone** | "How loud is the sound?" | mV-level AC, needs DC bias + amplifier |

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **ADC Resolution of common KTU-relevant MCUs:** ATmega328P → **10-bit**; ATmega32 → **10-bit**; MSP430 → **10/12-bit**; STM32F1 → **12-bit**; Arduino Uno → **10-bit** (default).
- **Standard Reference Voltage (Vref):** typically **5.0 V** (AVR) or **3.3 V** (ARM Cortex-M).
- **LSB (Least Significant Bit) weight:** $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ — the smallest analog voltage step the ADC can distinguish.
- **LM35 scale factor:** **10 mV/°C** (linear, no offset).
- **Speed of sound (for acoustic context):** $c \approx 343$ m/s at 20°C.
- **Nyquist sampling requirement:** $F_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ of the input signal.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept of SAR ADC (Step-by-Step)

A **Successive Approximation Register ADC** is the dominant architecture in modern microcontrollers because it offers a *predictable* $n$-clock conversion time and modest silicon area. The conversion proceeds in $n$ cycles:

1. **Sample & Hold (S/H):** An internal capacitor array is connected to the analog input via a switch. It charges to the input voltage $V_{in}$. The switch then opens, "freezing" $V_{in}$ — this is essential for accuracy during the bit-by-bit comparison.
2. **Internal DAC reset:** The SAR starts with the MSB (Most Significant Bit) = 1, all others = 0. The corresponding analog voltage from the internal DAC is $V_{DAC} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2}$.
3. **Comparator decision:** A high-gain comparator checks: Is $V_{in} \geq V_{DAC}$?
   - If **YES** → MSB remains 1 (DAC voltage retained for next cycle).
   - If **NO** → MSB is cleared to 0 (DAC voltage lowered).
4. **Bit-by-bit refinement:** The next bit is set to 1, a new $V_{DAC}$ is generated, comparison repeats. After $n$ cycles, the SAR holds the digital equivalent of $V_{in}$.
5. **EOC flag:** The End-of-Conversion flag is set; the CPU reads the result.

> [!TIP]
> **Why SAR for KTU boards?** It is the architecture used inside ATmega328P, ATmega32, STM32, and PIC. Examiners love asking how many clock cycles one conversion takes — the answer is always **n clock cycles** (plus acquisition time), so a 10-bit ADC at 1 MHz ADC clock needs $\approx 13\text{ }\mu s$ total.

### 2.2 Sensor-by-Sensor Interface Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Potentiometer as a Position/Voltage Sensor

A potentiometer is a **three-terminal passive resistor** with a wiper that slides along a resistive track. Wired as a **voltage divider**, it becomes a tunable analog voltage source.

- **Linear taper (most common):** $V_{out}$ is a *linear* function of wiper position $\theta$ (in fraction of full travel).
- **Log taper:** Used for audio volume controls (mimics human hearing).

**Connection to MCU:**
- Terminal 1 → GND
- Terminal 3 → Vcc (often 5 V)
- Wiper (Terminal 2) → ADC input pin (e.g., ADC0 on ATmega32)
- *No additional signal conditioning required* — the output is already a clean, low-impedance DC voltage between 0 V and Vcc.

**Real-world use:** Joystick X/Y axes, user knobs, calibration trims, battery voltage dividers.

#### 2.2.2 Temperature Sensors — Two Major Families

| Type | Example | Output Type | Output Equation | Interface |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Analog IC** | LM35, TMP36, LM34 | Analog voltage | $V = \text{scale} \times T$ | Direct to ADC |
| **Analog resistive** | NTC Thermistor (10kΩ) | Resistance → needs bias | Steinhart-Hart / β-equation | Voltage divider + ADC |

- **LM35:** $V_{out} = 10 \text{ mV/°C} \times T(°C)$, range 0°C to +150°C. At 25°C, $V_{out} = 250$ mV. No offset, calibrated in °C.
- **TMP36:** $V_{out} = 500 \text{ mV} + 10 \text{ mV/°C} \times T(°C)$, range -40°C to +125°C. Has a 500 mV offset to handle sub-zero.
- **Thermistor (NTC):** Resistance *decreases* with temperature. Used in a voltage divider: $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \dfrac{R_{therm}}{R_{therm} + R_{ref}}$. Needs the **Steinhart-Hart** or simpler **β-parameter equation** to extract temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Tip:** If asked to interface an LM35 to a 5 V MCU with 10-bit ADC, the **digital reading** at 25°C is $\dfrac{0.250}{5} \times 1023 \approx 51$. Always show the *unit conversion* explicitly in the solution.

#### 2.2.3 Electret Microphone — Acoustic-to-Electric Transducer

An **electret microphone** contains a permanently-charged dielectric (the *electret*) and a FET impedance converter. Key facts:

- It requires a **DC bias** through a pull-up resistor (typically 2.2 kΩ to 10 kΩ) on the output pin to power the internal FET.
- Its output is a **very small AC voltage** (typically 1–20 mV peak) riding on a DC bias of about Vcc/2.
- It is **not DC-coupled** to the MCU; you must AC-couple and amplify using an op-amp (e.g., LM358, MAX9812, or TLV320) before feeding into the ADC.
- The amplified output must remain within **0 V to Vref** — otherwise the op-amp saturates and the ADC clips.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sampling rate for audio:** Per Nyquist, to capture audio up to 20 kHz (full hearing range), the ADC must sample at $\geq 40$ kHz. For voice (4 kHz BW), $\geq 8$ kHz is sufficient. The built-in 10-bit ADC of ATmega328P at 15 kS/s is barely adequate for voice; the STM32 at 12-bit / 1 MSPS is far superior.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Digital Output Code** (unipolar) | $D = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^n - 1)\right)$ | integer in $[0,\; 2^n-1]$ |
| 2 | **Reconstructed Analog Voltage** | $V_{in} = \dfrac{D}{2^n - 1} \cdot V_{ref}$ | Volts (V) |
| 3 | **LSB (Resolution) Weight** | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | V per step |
| 4 | **Dynamic Range** | $DR = 6.02n + 1.76$ | dB |
| 5 | **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio** | $SQNR \approx 6.02n$ | dB |
| 6 | **Nyquist Rate** | $F_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz |
| 7 | **LM35 Transfer Function** | $T(°C) = \dfrac{V_{out}}{10 \text{ mV}}$ | °C |
| 8 | **TMP36 Transfer Function** | $T(°C) = \dfrac{V_{out} - 500 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mV}}$ | °C |
| 9 | **NTC Thermistor (β-equation)** | $\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{T_0} + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{R}{R_0}\right)$ | Kelvin |
| 10 | **Potentiometer Divider** | $V_{wiper} = V_{cc}\cdot\dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | V |
| 11 | **Microphone Preamp Gain** | $A_v = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ (non-inverting) | dimensionless |
| 12 | **Acoustic SPL → Voltage** (approx) | $V_{rms} = P_{ref}\cdot 10^{SPL/20} \cdot s$ | V (s = sensitivity) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL MARKDOWN RULE:** The formula sheet table above uses no `|` characters inside any cell — every absolute value, fraction, or separator is rendered using $\vert$ or `\` in LaTeX to avoid breaking table syntax.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Process Control:** Thermistors monitor reactor temperature; ADC digital codes feed a PID loop.
- **Consumer Audio:** Microphone arrays in smart speakers use multi-channel ADCs for beamforming.
- **Automotive:** Potentiometric throttle position sensors (TPS) are read by ECUs to adjust fuel injection.
- **Medical Devices:** LM35-based wearable thermometers convert body temperature to digital for Bluetooth transmission.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** Battery-powered sensor nodes (e.g., Arduino Nano + ESP8266) sample LM35 every few seconds, average, and transmit — ADC at the heart of every "smart thing".

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantization Resolution ($V_{LSB}$)

The ADC has $2^n$ distinct output codes (from $0$ to $2^n-1$) spread evenly across the input range $V_{ref}$. The width of one "step" — the smallest detectable change in input voltage — is therefore the total range divided by the number of steps.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{LSB} &= \dfrac{V_{ref} - 0}{2^n - 1} \\[4pt]
        &\approx \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \quad (\text{for large } n)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:**
- $V_{ref}$ is the *full-scale* span (0 V to $V_{ref}$).
- The number of *intervals* between $2^n$ codes is $(2^n - 1)$ — one fewer than the codes (e.g., 1023 intervals for 1024 codes).
- For $n \geq 8$, the difference between dividing by $(2^n - 1)$ vs. $2^n$ is less than 0.1%, so the approximation is standard in KTU derivations.

**Worked example (10-bit, $V_{ref} = 5$ V):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{LSB} &= \dfrac{5.0}{2^{10}} = \dfrac{5.0}{1024} \\[4pt]
        &= 4.8828 \text{ mV/step}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Converting ADC Code to Physical Temperature (LM35)

A 10-bit ADC reads $D = 248$ from an LM35 sensor. Reference $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V. Find the temperature in °C.

**Step 1 — Convert digital code to analog voltage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in} &= \dfrac{D}{2^n - 1} \cdot V_{ref} \\[4pt]
       &= \dfrac{248}{1023} \cdot 5.0 \\[4pt]
       &= 1.2121 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply LM35 transfer function ($10$ mV/°C, no offset):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \dfrac{V_{in}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}} \\[4pt]
  &= \dfrac{1.2121 \text{ V}}{0.010 \text{ V/°C}} \\[4pt]
  &= 121.21 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sanity check:** LM35 range is 0°C to +150°C; 121.21°C is within range. ✓

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — TMP36 with Negative-Temperature Capability

A 12-bit ADC on an STM32 ($V_{ref} = 3.3$ V) returns $D = 1500$ from a TMP36. Find the temperature.

**Step 1 — Voltage reconstruction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in} &= \dfrac{1500}{4095} \cdot 3.3 \\[4pt]
       &= 1.2088 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — TMP36 transfer function (with 500 mV offset):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \dfrac{V_{in} - 0.500}{0.010} \\[4pt]
  &= \dfrac{1.2088 - 0.500}{0.010} \\[4pt]
  &= \dfrac{0.7088}{0.010} \\[4pt]
  &= 70.88 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Thermistor Temperature from Voltage Divider

A 10 kΩ NTC thermistor with $R_0 = 10$ kΩ at $T_0 = 298$ K and $\beta = 3950$ K is in a divider with $R_{ref} = 10$ kΩ, $V_{cc} = 5$ V. The ADC reads $D = 614$ on a 10-bit ADC.

**Step 1 — Compute wiper voltage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{th} &= \dfrac{614}{1023} \cdot 5.0 \\[4pt]
       &= 3.001 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve voltage divider for thermistor resistance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{th} &= V_{cc} \cdot \dfrac{R_{th}}{R_{th} + R_{ref}} \\[4pt]
3.001 &= 5.0 \cdot \dfrac{R_{th}}{R_{th} + 10000} \\[4pt]
3.001(R_{th} + 10000) &= 5 R_{th} \\[4pt]
3.001 R_{th} + 30010 &= 5 R_{th} \\[4pt]
30010 &= 1.999 R_{th} \\[4pt]
R_{th} &= 15012.5 \text{ }\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply β-equation to extract temperature:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{1}{T} &= \dfrac{1}{T_0} + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{R_{th}}{R_0}\right) \\[4pt]
\dfrac{1}{T} &= \dfrac{1}{298} + \dfrac{1}{3950}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{15012.5}{10000}\right) \\[4pt]
\dfrac{1}{T} &= 0.003356 + 0.000253 \cdot 0.40547 \\[4pt]
\dfrac{1}{T} &= 0.003356 + 0.0001026 \\[4pt]
\dfrac{1}{T} &= 0.0034586 \\[4pt]
T &= 289.13 \text{ K} = 15.98 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is consistent with a thermistor whose resistance *increased* from 10 kΩ (cooler-than-room) to ~15 kΩ (15.98°C, below the 25°C reference). ✓

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Comparison Summary

| Parameter | Potentiometer | LM35 / TMP36 | Electret Microphone |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Signal type** | DC, clean | DC, clean | AC, low-amplitude, biased |
| **Output magnitude** | 0 to Vcc (full-scale) | 0–1.5 V (LM35) | 1–20 mV AC + Vcc/2 DC |
| **Conditioning needed** | None (low-Z) | None (low-Z, calibrated IC) | Bias resistor + AC-couple + op-amp |
| **ADC sampling rate** | Slow (<1 kHz) | Slow (<1 kHz) | Fast ($\geq 2 \times$ audio BW) |
| **Typical KTU application** | User input, joystick | Temp monitoring, IoT | Audio capture, sound level |
| **Resolution driver** | Mechanical resolution (often $> 8$ bits) | 10-bit adequate | $\geq 12$-bit preferred |
| **Major error source** | Contact noise, wear | Self-heating ($\sim$ 0.1°C) | Amplifier noise, clipping |

### 3.6 Derivation 5 — Required ADC Sampling Rate for Audio

To digitize speech (max frequency 4 kHz) and full music (max 20 kHz):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s(\text{voice}) &\geq 2 \times 4000 = 8000 \text{ samples/sec} \\[4pt]
F_s(\text{music}) &\geq 2 \times 20000 = 40000 \text{ samples/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

For ATmega328P ADC max clock = 200 kHz, with prescaler 16, ADC clock = 125 kHz, conversion time $\approx 13$ cycles → $F_s \approx 9.6$ kHz. *Sufficient for voice only.*

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 System-Level Signal-Flow Block Diagram (Mermaid)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the three analog sensors are interfaced through proper signal conditioning into a common SAR ADC, and then to the MCU's digital processing unit.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Potentiometer Wiper Voltage 0 to 5 V] --> CON1[Direct Coupling No Conditioning]
    A2[LM35 or TMP36 Output 0 to 1.5 V] --> CON2[Direct Coupling Optional RC Filter]
    A3[Electret Microphone mV-level AC] --> BIAS[DC Bias Resistor 2.2 kohm]
    BIAS --> ACCouple[AC Coupling Capacitor 10 uF]
    ACCouple --> PGA[Op-Amp Preamp Gain 50 to 100]
    PGA --> CLAMP[Anti-alias Low-Pass Filter]
    CON1 --> MUX
    CON2 --> MUX
    CLAMP --> MUX
    MUX[Analog Multiplexer 8 to 1 Channel Select] --> SHA[Sample and Hold Capacitor]
    SHA --> CMP[High Speed Comparator]
    VREF[Voltage Reference 5.0 V or 3.3 V] --> DAC[Internal R-2R DAC]
    DAC --> CMP
    CMP --> SAR[Successive Approximation Register 10 or 12 bit]
    SAR --> DATABUS[Parallel Digital Data Bus D0 to D9 or D0 to D11]
    DATABUS --> CPU[MCU Core ATmega328P or STM32]
    CPU --> DISPLAY[LCD or UART or LED Display Temperature Volume Position]
    CPU --> FLAG[ADC Interrupt Flag EOC ADIF Set]
```

### 4.2 SAR ADC Internal State Machine (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Sampling : Start Conversion ADSC equals 1
    Sampling --> Hold : Acquisition Time 1.5 ADC clocks
    Hold --> BitTest_N : Set MSB equals 1
    BitTest_N --> Compare : Compare Vin with VDAC
    Compare --> BitSet_1 : if Vin greater or equal VDAC
    Compare --> BitSet_0 : if Vin less than VDAC
    BitSet_1 --> NextBit : Move to next lower bit
    BitSet_0 --> NextBit : Clear current bit move to next
    NextBit --> Compare : If bits remaining greater than 0
    NextBit --> Complete : If all n bits tested
    Complete --> Idle : Set ADIF flag clear ADSC
    Complete --> [*] : Result read by CPU
```

### 4.3 Potentiometer Voltage Divider — Equivalent Schematic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VCC[Vcc 5 V] --> R1[Resistor R1 0 to 10 kohm]
    R1 --> WIPER
    WIPER{{Wiper Slider}}
    WIPER --> R2[Resistor R2 10 kohm to 0]
    R2 --> GND[GND 0 V]
    WIPER --> ADCPIN[MCU ADC Input Channel 0]
    ADCPIN -.->|Digital Code D| MCU[Microcontroller]
    MCU --> EQ[D = Vin divided by VLSB]
    EQ --> POS[Position theta percent = D divided by 1023 times 100]
```

### 4.4 LM35 Interface with ATmega32 (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LM35[LM35 Temperature Sensor Pin 1 Vcc Pin 2 Vout Pin 3 GND] --> VOUT[Analog Output 10 mV per degree C]
    VOUT --> ADC0[ATmega32 ADC0 Pin PA0]
    ADC0 --> SARADC[10 bit SAR ADC]
    VREF[External 5 V Reference on AREF Pin] --> SARADC
    SARADC --> REG[ADCH and ADCL Registers]
    REG --> CODE[10 bit Digital Code 0 to 1023]
    CODE --> FORM[Software Conversion T equals Code times 5.0 divided by 1023 times 100]
    FORM --> DISP[LCD Display 16x2 Temperature in degree C]
```

### 4.5 Electret Microphone Signal Chain (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SOUND[Acoustic Pressure SPL in dB] --> MIC[Electret Capsule 1 to 20 mV AC]
    MIC --> RB[Pull-up Resistor 2.2 kohm to Vcc]
    RB --> COUT[AC Output Node with Vcc by 2 DC bias]
    COUT --> CAP[DC Blocking Capacitor 10 uF]
    CAP --> AMP[Non-inverting Op-amp Gain 100]
    AMP --> FILTER[2nd Order Butterworth Low-pass 8 kHz]
    FILTER --> ADCPIN[MCU ADC Input]
    ADCPIN --> CPU[CPU calculates RMS and SPL]
    CPU --> OUTPUT[Serial Output or LED Bar Graph]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagrams in KTU exams:** The examiner awards marks for labeling the **signal direction** (sensor → conditioning → ADC → CPU), the **reference voltage path**, and the **resolution bits**. Always annotate the diagram with $n$, $V_{ref}$, and the sensor's transfer function during your answer.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC. Why is it preferred in microcontrollers?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A SAR ADC consists of a **Sample-and-Hold circuit**, an **internal DAC** (R-2R ladder), a **high-speed comparator**, and a **Successive Approximation Register**. The working steps are:

1. The input voltage $V_{in}$ is sampled and held on an internal capacitor.
2. The SAR sets the **MSB = 1**, generating a DAC voltage of $V_{ref}/2$.
3. The **comparator** checks if $V_{in} \geq V_{DAC}$. If yes, MSB remains 1; if no, it is reset to 0.
4. The next bit is tested similarly. After **n cycles**, the SAR holds the final n-bit digital code.
5. The **End-of-Conversion (EOC)** flag is set, and the CPU reads the result.

**Why preferred:** *Deterministic conversion time* ($n$ clock cycles), low power, small silicon area, and adequate speed for most sensor (kHz-range) applications — making it ideal for integration in MCUs like the ATmega32 and STM32. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Differentiate between LM35 and TMP36 temperature sensors in terms of output equation, range, and zero-°C offset.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | LM35 | TMP36 |
|---|---|---|
| Output equation | $V = 10 \text{ mV/°C} \times T$ | $V = 500 \text{ mV} + 10 \text{ mV/°C} \times T$ |
| 0°C output | **0 V** (no offset) | **500 mV** (offset) |
| Range | 0°C to +150°C | -40°C to +125°C |
| Negative temperatures | Cannot read below 0°C | Yes — the 500 mV offset allows sub-zero voltages |

The TMP36 can measure **sub-zero** temperatures because its non-zero offset shifts the entire output curve upward, ensuring the output voltage remains positive even at -40°C. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A, 14 Marks]**
> **Q.A.** (a) Explain, with a circuit diagram, the interfacing of an **LM35 temperature sensor** to an ATmega32 microcontroller. Show the register-level configuration for 10-bit single-ended conversion on ADC channel 0 with AVCC as reference.
> (b) The ATmega32 reads a digital value of **315** from the LM35. If $V_{ref} = AVCC = 5$ V, calculate (i) the analog input voltage, (ii) the temperature in °C, and (iii) the smallest temperature change detectable by the ADC.

#### Model Solution — (a) Interfacing [7 marks]

**1. Circuit Diagram Description (2 marks):**
- LM35 **Pin 1 (+Vs)** → +5 V.
- LM35 **Pin 2 (Vout)** → ATmega32 **PA0/ADC0** pin.
- LM35 **Pin 3 (GND)** → 0 V.
- ATmega32 **AVCC** → +5 V (decoupled with $10\text{ }\mu F \parallel 0.1\text{ }\mu F$).
- ATmega32 **AREF** pin → bypass capacitor to GND (since we use AVCC as ref, not external AREF).
- Optional $100\text{ nF}$ capacitor from PA0 to GND to suppress noise.

**2. Register Configuration (5 marks):**

| Register | Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `ADMUX` | `0b01000000` = `0x40` | Select ADC0 channel, AVCC as Vref, right-adjusted result |
| `ADCSRA` | `0b10000111` = `0x87` | Enable ADC (`ADEN=1`), start conversion (`ADSC=1`), prescaler = 128 (ADC clock = 125 kHz) |
| `ADCSRB` | `0x00` | Free-running mode disabled (single conversion) |
| `DIDR0` | `0x01` | Disable digital input buffer on ADC0 to reduce noise |

> Wait for `ADSC` to clear (conversion complete) or use the ADC interrupt (`ADIE=1`).

**C-code snippet (for context, ATmega32, register-level):**

```c
void ADC_Init() {
    ADMUX  = (1 << REFS0);            // AVcc reference, ADC0
    ADCSRA = (1 << ADEN) | (1 << ADPS2) | (1 << ADPS1) | (1 << ADPS0);
}
uint16_t ADC_Read(uint8_t ch) {
    ADMUX = (ADMUX & 0xF0) | (ch & 0x0F);
    ADCSRA |= (1 << ADSC);
    while (ADCSRA & (1 << ADSC));
    return ADC;
}
```

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical [7 marks]

**Given:** $D = 315$, $n = 10$, $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V, LM35 scale = 10 mV/°C.

**(i) Analog input voltage (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in} &= \dfrac{D}{2^n - 1} \cdot V_{ref} = \dfrac{315}{1023} \cdot 5.0 \\[4pt]
       &= 0.30792 \cdot 5.0 = 1.5396 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Temperature (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \dfrac{V_{in}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}} = \dfrac{1.5396}{0.010} = 153.96 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Note:** This is *outside* the LM35's specified 0–150°C range. The student should flag this as an *error condition* (sensor saturation or wrong wiring). **(1 mark reserved for comment)**

**(iii) Smallest detectable temperature change (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{LSB} &= \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \dfrac{5.0}{1024} = 4.8828 \text{ mV} \\[4pt]
\Delta T_{min} &= \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}} = \dfrac{4.8828 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}} = 0.488 \text{ °C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total: 14 marks** ✓

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]**
> **Q.B.** (a) With a labeled block diagram, explain the **complete signal chain** of interfacing an **electret microphone** to a microcontroller's ADC. State clearly the role of each block.
> (b) An op-amp-based preamplifier uses $R_f = 100$ kΩ and $R_{in} = 1$ kΩ in non-inverting configuration. The microphone outputs a $5$ mV RMS signal. Calculate: (i) the closed-loop voltage gain, (ii) the amplified output RMS voltage, (iii) the peak-to-peak voltage entering the ADC, and (iv) whether a 3.3 V ADC (12-bit) will clip this signal.

#### Model Solution — (a) Block Diagram Explanation [7 marks]

**Signal chain blocks (1.5 marks each):**

1. **Electret Microphone + Bias Resistor (2 kΩ to Vcc):** The microphone capsule contains a permanently-charged electret diaphragm and a FET source follower. The bias resistor supplies DC operating current to the internal FET and sets the DC output bias at approximately $V_{cc}/2$.
2. **AC Coupling Capacitor (10 µF):** Blocks the $V_{cc}/2$ DC bias, allowing *only* the AC audio signal to pass to the next stage. Without this, the DC level would shift the op-amp output out of its linear range.
3. **Non-Inverting Op-Amp Preamplifier (LM358 / MAX9812):** Boosts the tiny mV-level microphone output (typically 1–20 mV) to a volt-level signal usable by the ADC. Voltage gain $A_v = 1 + R_f / R_{in}$.
4. **Anti-Alias Low-Pass Filter (R-C or active 2nd-order Butterworth, $f_c = 8$ kHz for voice):** Removes frequency content above half the sampling rate to prevent aliasing artifacts.
5. **ADC + MCU:** The cleaned-up, amplified, anti-aliased signal is fed into the ADC channel; the MCU computes RMS / peak / SPL.

#### Model Solution — (b) Numerical [7 marks]

**Given:** $R_f = 100$ kΩ, $R_{in} = 1$ kΩ, $V_{in,rms} = 5$ mV, $V_{ref} = 3.3$ V, $n = 12$ bits.

**(i) Closed-loop voltage gain (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_v &= 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_{in}} = 1 + \dfrac{100\,000}{1000} = 1 + 100 = 101
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Amplified RMS voltage (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{out,rms} &= A_v \cdot V_{in,rms} = 101 \cdot 5 \text{ mV} = 505 \text{ mV}_{rms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Peak-to-peak voltage entering ADC (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{peak} &= V_{rms} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 0.505 \cdot 1.414 = 0.7141 \text{ V} \\[4pt]
V_{pp} &= 2 \cdot V_{peak} = 1.428 \text{ V}_{pp}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Clipping analysis (1 mark):**

Since $V_{pp} = 1.428$ V is well within the 0–3.3 V ADC input range, **no clipping occurs** for this microphone level. The headroom is $(3.3 - 1.428)/2 \approx 0.94$ V on each side — ample for louder inputs up to roughly 7× this SPL before clipping. **(Full credit: 1 mark)**

**Total: 14 marks** ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `(2^n - 1)` denominator in reconstruction:** Many students use $2^n$ instead. The correct *full-scale* code is $2^n - 1$ (e.g., 1023 for 10-bit, 4095 for 12-bit). Using $2^n$ gives a slight but real error and loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing LM35 and TMP36 offset:** Writing $T = V/10$ mV for a TMP36 yields incorrect negative values around 50°C. **Always state the offset** explicitly.
> 3. **Microphone interfacing — missing DC blocking:** Students often connect the mic output *directly* to the op-amp or ADC, ignoring the required AC coupling capacitor and bias resistor. This causes saturation; full marks require the *complete* signal chain.
> 4. **Not stating units in calculations:** "T = 153.96" loses the "°C". Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units in long answers.
> 5. **Ignoring ADC acquisition time:** A new conversion started *immediately* after the previous one may use a stale sample. Always add $\geq 1$ ADC clock delay after channel change (typically handled by setting `ADSC` and waiting).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **ADC core equation:** $D = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^n - 1)$ — memorize this in both directions.
- [x] **LSB weight:** $V_{LSB} = V_{ref}/2^n$ — defines smallest distinguishable voltage step.
- [x] **SAR ADC** uses $n$ clock cycles per conversion; preferred for low-power MCUs.
- [x] **Nyquist:** $F_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — critical for microphone/audio applications.
- [x] **Potentiometer** = direct voltage divider; output is **0 to $V_{cc}$**; no signal conditioning needed.
- [x] **LM35** = $10$ mV/°C, **0 mV offset**, range **0°C to 150°C** — cannot read negative temperatures.
- [x] **TMP36** = $10$ mV/°C, **500 mV offset**, range **-40°C to 125°C** — can read sub-zero.
- [x] **NTC Thermistor** = resistance-based; needs voltage divider and **β-equation** to extract T.
- [x] **Electret microphone** needs **DC bias resistor** (2.2 kΩ), **AC coupling capacitor** (10 µF), and **op-amp preamplification** before the ADC.
- [x] **ATmega32 ADC** = 10-bit, 8 channels, prescaler 128, $F_{ADC} = 125$ kHz, conversion time $\approx 13$ µs.
- [x] **STM32 ADC** = 12-bit, up to 16 channels, up to 1 MSPS — superior for audio.
- [x] **Anti-alias filter** is mandatory before any audio ADC; cutoff $\leq F_s/2$.
- [x] **Sensor power-on stabilization:** wait 10–100 ms after Vcc applied before reading LM35 / TMP36 (sensor startup transient).
- [x] **Register names to know (ATmega32):** `ADMUX`, `ADCSRA`, `ADCSRB`, `ADCH`/`ADCL`, `DIDR0`.
- [x] **Reference voltage choices:** internal bandgap (1.1 V), AVCC (5 V), or external AREF — must be stable, decoupled, and chosen to match the sensor's output range to maximize resolution.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital to Analog Conversion: Simple DAC Output Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Digital to Analog Conversion: Simple DAC Output Generation ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC):** A DAC is an electronic circuit (or embedded peripheral) that converts a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude **digital codeword** (typically a binary integer) into a corresponding **continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog voltage or current** proportional to the input digital value. In the context of the **8051 microcontroller** (the architecture of focus in Module 2 of PBCST504), a DAC is an **external interfacing device** because the standard 8051 does not contain an internal DAC. The digital output port (e.g., **Port 1** or **Port 2**) of the 8051 is connected to the input of an external DAC IC (such as the **DAC 0808** or **DAC 0832**), which then generates a proportional analog voltage. ### 1.2 Key Terminology | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Resolution (n)** | Number of input bits accepted by the DAC (e.g., 8-bit, 10-bit) | | **Full-Scale Output (V_FS)** | The maximum analog output when all input bits are HIGH (1111...1) | | **Step Size / LSB Voltage (V_LSB)** | The smallest analog increment produced by a 1-LSB change in digital input | | **MSB** | Most Significant Bit — contributes the largest analog weight | | **LSB** | Least Significant Bit — contributes the smallest analog weight | | **Reference Voltage (V_ref)** | The stable precision voltage source used as the analog reference | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The phrase *"Simple DAC Output Generation"* refers to the fundamental methods of generating an analog output from a microcontroller port — namely the **Weighted Resistor DAC**, the **R-2R Ladder DAC**, and **PWM-based DAC** (where a digital PWM waveform is filtered into a DC voltage). ### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a **staircase** leading up to a wall. Each step represents **one digital input code** (e.g., 00000000, 00000001, 00000010 ... 11111111). As you walk up the staircase: - The **height of each step** is identical — this represents the **step size (V_LSB)**. - The **total height** of the staircase from ground to top represents the **full-scale output (V_FS)**. - The **number of steps** is fixed (= 2ⁿ for an n-bit DAC) — this is the **resolution**. A DAC is essentially an "electronic staircase" — the digital input selects *which step* the output should sit at, producing a stair-stepped approximation of a smooth analog waveform (see Section 4 for the Mermaid visualization). Another analogy is a **water tap with discrete settings**: OFF, 1, 2, 3, ..., MAX. Each setting corresponds to a digital input, and the water flow (analog output) is proportional to the chosen setting. --- ### 1.4 Why is a DAC Needed? > [!IMPORTANT] > Microcontrollers operate in the **digital domain** (HIGH = 5V, LOW = 0V), but many real-world devices require **analog signals**: > - **DC motor speed controllers** (variable voltage → variable speed) > - **Audio amplifiers** (digital audio samples → analog sound waves) > - **Function generators** (digital codes → sine/triangle/square waves) > - **Temperature control systems** (digital setpoint → analog heater voltage) > - **CRT/LCD brightness control** (PWM output filtered to DC voltage) The DAC is the **bridge** between the digital brain (microcontroller) and the analog world (actuators, transducers, speakers). --- ### 1.5 Typical DAC ICs Used with 8051 | DAC IC | Resolution | Interface | Key Feature | |---|---|---|---| | **DAC 0808** | 8-bit | Parallel | High-speed, current output, requires external op-amp | | **DAC 0832** | 8-bit | Parallel with input latches | Double-buffered, can hold previous value | | **MPC3008** (ADC with internal DAC concept) | 8-bit | SPI | Successive approximation | | **MCP4725** | 12-bit | I²C | Modern, low-power, embedded in many ARM designs | For KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2, the focus is on **the mathematical principles of simple DAC output generation**, particularly the **R-2R Ladder** and **Weighted Resistor** configurations, as well as **PWM-to-analog conversion**. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Core Methods of Simple DAC Output Generation #### **Method 1: Weighted Resistor DAC (Binary-Weighted DAC)** This is the **simplest conceptually** but the **least practical** for high-resolution designs. Each bit of the digital input drives a switch that connects a binary-weighted resistor to either V_ref or ground. **Operation Principle:** - A digital input of `bₙ₋₁ bₙ₋₂ ... b₁ b₀` controls n switches. - The MSB (bₙ₋₁) is connected through resistor **R**. - The next bit (bₙ₋₂) is connected through resistor **2R**. - The LSB (b₀) is connected through resistor **2ⁿ⁻¹ · R**. - All resistors meet at a common **summing node** (the inverting input of an op-amp configured as a summing amplifier). **Output Voltage Formula:** $$V_{out} = -V_{ref} \cdot \left( \frac{b_0}{2^{n-1}R} + \frac{b_1}{2^{n-2}R} + \cdots + \frac{b_{n-1}}{R} \right) \cdot R_f$$ Or, more compactly: $$V_{out} = -\frac{V_{ref} \cdot R_f}{R} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} \frac{b_k}{2^{n-1-k}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why the negative sign?** The op-amp is in **inverting configuration**. The minus sign is dropped (or the op-amp is followed by a unity-gain inverter) to obtain a positive V_out. **Disadvantages:** - Resistor values span an enormous range (R, 2R, 4R, ..., 2ⁿ⁻¹R). - For 8-bit DAC: resistors range from R to 128R — difficult to fabricate on a single IC die. - Highly susceptible to temperature drift and absolute resistor tolerance. --- #### **Method 2: R-2R Ladder DAC (The Industry Standard)** The **R-2R Ladder** is the most widely manufactured DAC architecture because it uses only **two resistor values (R and 2R)**, which are easy to fabricate in matched pairs on a monolithic IC. **Operation Principle:** - The ladder consists of a repeating pattern: each bit switch either connects the rung to **V_ref** (bit = 1) or to **ground** (bit = 0). - The ladder is analyzed using **Thevenin equivalent** at each node — moving from the LSB end toward the MSB end. - The output is taken at the top of the ladder, optionally buffered by an op-amp. **Why "Ladder" is Superior:** - Only **two resistor values** are needed (R and 2R), making IC fabrication trivial. - Trimming and matching are much easier. - Easily extended to higher resolutions (10, 12, 14, 16 bits) without proportional cost increase. **Output Voltage Formula:** $$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n}$$ where **D** is the **decimal value of the digital input codeword**. > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **single most important formula** in KTU DAC problems. Memorize it: *V_out is linearly proportional to the digital code D, scaled by V_ref / 2ⁿ.* --- #### **Method 3: PWM-Based DAC (Software DAC)** The **8051 has no built-in DAC** but **does have timers** that can generate **Pulse-Width Modulated (PWM) waveforms** on any output pin. **Operation Principle:** - The microcontroller outputs a PWM square wave of fixed frequency (e.g., 1 kHz) but **variable duty cycle**. - The PWM signal is passed through an **external RC low-pass filter**. - The filter **averages** the PWM waveform, producing a DC voltage proportional to the duty cycle. **Output Voltage Formula:** $$V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \text{Duty Cycle} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{period}}$$ $$V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{\text{Compare Value}}{\text{Timer Period (ARR+1)}}$$ **Filter Design Rule:** The RC time constant must satisfy: $$\tau = R \cdot C \gg T_{period}$$ Typically, choose **τ ≈ 10 · T_period** to ensure >99% smoothing while allowing reasonable response time. | Method | Pros | Cons | |---|---|---| | Weighted Resistor | Conceptually simple | Wide resistor range, impractical for n > 4 | | R-2R Ladder | Only 2 resistor values, scalable, accurate | Requires precision resistors | | PWM + Filter | No external DAC IC needed, fully software | Lower bandwidth, requires filter, ripple | --- ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Unit / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2^n}$ | Output voltage for an ideal n-bit DAC | Volts (V); D is decimal code (0 to 2ⁿ−1) | | 2 | $V_{FS} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{2^n - 1}{2^n} \approx V_{ref}$ | Full-Scale Output | Volts (V); never quite reaches V_ref | | 3 | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Step Size (Resolution) | Volts per LSB | | 4 | $V_{out}(\%) = \dfrac{D}{2^n -
