1} \times 100$ | Output as percentage of full-scale | Percentage (%) | | 5 | $V_{out(PWM)} = V_{CC} \cdot \dfrac{T_{ON}}{T_{period}}$ | PWM-filtered DC output | Volts; 0 ≤ V_out ≤ V_CC | | 6 | $\tau = R \cdot C$ | Filter time constant | Seconds (s) | | 7 | $f_{-3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R C}$ | Filter cutoff frequency | Hertz (Hz); must be ≪ PWM frequency | | 8 | Settling time $\approx n \cdot \tau$ | Time for output to settle within 1 LSB | Seconds | | 9 | $\% \text{ Resolution} = \dfrac{1}{2^n} \times 100$ | Percentage resolution | 8-bit = 0.39%, 10-bit = 0.098% | | 10 | Output current (summing amp): $I_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \sum \dfrac{b_k}{2^{n-1-k} R}$ | R-2R ladder current output | Amperes (A) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip — Memorize Formulas 1, 2, and 3.** Almost every DAC problem reduces to: *compute V_out for a given digital input, or find the digital code for a desired V_out.* Use Formula 1 directly. --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications | Field | Application of DAC Output Generation | |---|---| | **Audio Engineering** | Digital audio players (MP3, FLAC) → DAC → speakers/headphones | | **Industrial Automation** | PLCs generate analog control signals (4–20 mA) using DACs to drive valves, motors | | **Telecommunications** | Direct Digital Synthesizers (DDS) generate sine waves using lookup tables + DAC | | **Medical Devices** | ECG simulators, infusion pumps (precise analog voltage → controlled flow rate) | | **Automotive** | Engine control units (ECUs) — throttle position, fuel injection timing | | **Robotics** | Servo motor control (PWM to analog, or direct DAC for voltage-mode servos) | | **Test & Measurement** | Function/arbitrary waveform generators, calibrators | --- ### 2.4 Resolution vs. Accuracy — A Critical Distinction > [!WARNING] > **Resolution ≠ Accuracy.** > - **Resolution** is the *smallest theoretical step* the DAC *can* produce (e.g., 19.5 mV for an 8-bit, 5V DAC). > - **Accuracy** is how *close* the actual output is to the *theoretical ideal* value, limited by resistor matching, offset, gain error, and linearity (INL, DNL). > - A 16-bit DAC with poor resistor matching can be *less accurate* than a well-designed 12-bit DAC. For KTU problems, assume **ideal DACs** unless stated otherwise. Real-world accuracy errors are covered in advanced courses. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: R-2R Ladder DAC Output Voltage (Full Exhaustive Steps) > **Problem Setup:** An 8-bit R-2R ladder DAC has a reference voltage **V_ref = 5.0 V**. The digital input code is **D = 10000000₂ = 128₁₀**. Derive the output voltage V_out. #### **Step 1 — State the General Output Equation** For an n-bit R-2R ladder DAC with reference voltage V_ref, the output voltage is derived from the principle that **each successive bit contributes exactly half the analog weight of the previous bit** (because of the 2:1 voltage division at every ladder rung). The general output voltage is: $$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} b_k \cdot 2^k = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \cdot D$$ where **D** is the **decimal equivalent** of the binary input word. **Explanation:** This formula comes from the fact that the MSB (bit position n−1) contributes V_ref/2 at the output, the next bit contributes V_ref/4, then V_ref/8, etc. Summing these binary-weighted contributions gives the general formula. --- #### **Step 2 — Substitute the Given Values** Given: - n = 8 bits - V_ref = 5.0 V - D = 10000000₂ = 128₁₀ Substituting: $$V_{out} = \frac{5.0}{2^8} \cdot 128$$ --- #### **Step 3 — Compute 2ⁿ Explicitly** $$2^8 = 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 \cdot 2 = 256$$ --- #### **Step 4 — Compute the Division V_ref / 2ⁿ** $$\frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5.0 \text{ V}}{256} = 0.01953125 \text{ V/LSB}$$ This value is the **step size (V_LSB)** = **19.53 mV per LSB**. --- #### **Step 5 — Multiply by the Digital Code D** $$V_{out} = 0.01953125 \text{ V/LSB} \cdot 128 \text{ LSB} = 2.5 \text{ V}$$ --- #### **Step 6 — Interpret the Result** $$V_{out} = 2.5 \text{ V} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2}$$ This makes **physical sense**: a digital code of exactly 128 (which is 2ⁿ/2 = 256/2) must produce an output of exactly **half of V_ref**. ✓ > [!NOTE] > **Cross-Check:** For a binary input of 10000000₂, only the MSB is active. The R-2R ladder acts as a precise 2:1 voltage divider, producing V_ref/2 = 2.5 V. The formula agrees with the physical circuit behavior. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Step Size and Full-Scale Output > **Problem:** For a 10-bit DAC with V_ref = 4.096 V, find: (a) Step size V_LSB, (b) Full-Scale Output V_FS, (c) Output voltage for input 0110011001₂. #### **Part (a) — Step Size** $$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{4.096}{2^{10}} = \frac{4.096}{1024} = 4.0 \text{ mV}$$ --- #### **Part (b) — Full-Scale Output** $$V_{FS} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{2^n - 1}{2^n} = 4.096 \cdot \frac{1023}{1024}$$ $$V_{FS} = 4.096 \cdot (0.9990234375) = 4.0920 \text{ V}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why not exactly 4.096 V?** Because the maximum digital code is 1023 (not 1024), the output is **one LSB short** of V_ref. For 10-bit systems, this difference is only 4 mV — negligible in practice. --- #### **Part (c) — Output for 0110011001₂** First, convert binary to decimal: $$D = 0 \cdot 2^9 + 1 \cdot 2^8 + 1 \cdot 2^7 + 0 \cdot 2^6 + 0 \cdot 2^5 + 1 \cdot 2^4 + 1 \cdot 2^3 + 0 \cdot 2^2 + 0 \cdot 2^1 + 1 \cdot 2^0$$ Breaking it down row-by-row: $$ \begin{aligned} D &= 0 \cdot 512 + 1 \cdot 256 + 1 \cdot 128 + 0 \cdot 64 + 0 \cdot 32 + 1 \cdot 16 + 1 \cdot 8 + 0 \cdot 4 + 0 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 1 \\ D &= 0 + 256 + 128 + 0 + 0 + 16 + 8 + 0 + 0 + 1 \\ D &= 256 + 128 + 16 + 8 + 1 \\ D &= 409_{10} \end{aligned} $$ Now compute V_out: $$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \cdot D = 4.0 \text{ mV} \cdot 409 = 1.636 \text{ V}$$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: PWM-to-Analog Conversion > **Problem:** An 8051 generates a PWM signal with period **T = 1 ms** and ON-time **T_ON = 0.4 ms** on a port pin powered by V_CC = 5.0 V. Compute the average DC voltage at the output of an ideal RC low-pass filter. #### **Step 1 — Compute Duty Cycle** $$\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{period}} = \frac{0.4 \text{ ms}}{1.0 \text{ ms}} = 0.4 = 40\%$$ #### **Step 2 — Compute Average Voltage** $$V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \text{Duty Cycle} = 5.0 \text{ V} \cdot 0.4 = 2.0 \text{ V}$$ #### **Step 3 — Choose Filter Components** For τ = 10 · T_period = 10 ms, with R = 10 kΩ: $$C = \frac{\tau}{R} = \frac{10 \text{ ms}}{10 \text{ k}\Omega} = \frac{0.01}{10{,}000} = 1.0 \text{ μF}$$ Filter cutoff frequency: $$f_{-3dB} = \frac{1}{2\pi R C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10{,}000 \cdot 1 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 15.9 \text{ Hz}$$ This is much less than the PWM frequency (1 kHz), so the ripple is heavily attenuated. ✓ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — KTU-Expected Calculation Patterns The table below consolidates the **most common KTU 2024 calculation scenarios** with worked examples. | S.No. | Scenario | Given | Find | Formula | Answer | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 8-bit DAC, V_ref = 5 V, D = 80H (128) | n, V_ref, D | V_out | $V_{ref} \cdot D / 2^n$ | 2.5 V | | 2 | 10-bit DAC, V_ref = 5 V, D = 200H (512) | n, V_ref, D | V_out | $V_{ref} \cdot D / 2^n$ | 2.5 V | | 3 | 12-bit DAC, V_ref = 4.096 V | n, V_ref | V_LSB | $V_{ref} / 2^n$ | 1.0 mV | | 4 | 8-bit DAC, V_ref = 10 V | n, V_ref | V_FS | $V_{ref} \cdot (2^n - 1)/2^n$ | 9.961 V | | 5 | Desired V_out = 3.0 V from 5 V 8-bit DAC | n, V_ref, V_out | D | $D = V_{out} \cdot 2^n / V_{ref}$ | 153.6 → 154 | | 6 | PWM 50% duty, V_CC = 3.3 V | Duty, V_CC | V_out (DC) | $V_{CC} \cdot \text{Duty}$ | 1.65 V | | 7 | PWM, V_out needed = 1.5 V, V_CC = 5 V | V_out, V_CC | Duty | $V_{out} / V_{CC}$ | 30% | | 8 | 8-bit DAC, % resolution | n | % | $100/2^n$ | 0.3906% | | 9 | R-2R ladder, 4-bit, all bits ON | n=4, D=15 | V_out (V_ref = 5) | $5 \cdot 15/16$ | 4.6875 V | | 10 | Weighted resistor, V_out, R, R_f | Component values | V_out | Summing-amp formula | Case-specific | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pattern Recognition Tip for KTU Exams:** 80% of DAC problems reduce to: > - *Find V_out for a given binary input* → use $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot D / 2^n$ > - *Find D for a desired V_out* → use $D = V_{out} \cdot 2^n / V_{ref}$ (round to nearest integer) > - *Find step size* → use $V_{LSB} = V_{ref} / 2^n$ > - *PWM question* → use $V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \text{Duty Cycle}$ ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Microcontroller Interfaced to a DAC ```mermaid flowchart LR CPU["8051 CPU"] --> PORT["Port 1 or Port 2
8-bit Digital Output"] LATCH["Latch / Data Bus
Buffer"] PORT --> LATCH LATCH --> DAC["DAC IC
Example DAC0808 / DAC0832"] VREF["Vref
Precision 5.0V Source"] --> DAC DAC --> ITOV["Current to Voltage
Converter Op-Amp"] ITOV --> OUT["Vout
Analog Signal"] FILTER["RC Low-Pass
Filter optional"] --> OUT ``` > [!NOTE] > **Architecture Notes:** The 8051 sends a digital byte over its parallel port → the DAC IC converts it to a proportional current → an op-amp converts current to voltage → (optional) RC filter smooths any switching noise. The output drives an analog load such as a motor, lamp, or audio amplifier. --- ### 4.2 R-2R Ladder DAC Internal Structure (Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB B0["Bit 0 LSB"] --> SW0["Switch b0"] B1["Bit 1"] --> SW1["Switch b1"] B2["Bit 2"] --> SW2["Switch b2"] B3["Bit 3 MSB"] --> SW3["Switch b3"] SW0 --> R0["R"] SW1 --> R1["2R"] SW2 --> R2["2R"] SW3 --> R3["2R"] R0 --> NODE0["Node A"] R1 --> NODE0 NODE0 --> R2X["R"] R2X --> NODE1["Node B"] R2 --> NODE1 NODE1 --> R3X["R"] R3X --> NODE2["Node C"] R3 --> NODE2 NODE2 --> OPAMP["Summing Op-Amp
Unity Gain Buffer"] VREF["Vref Source"] --> OPAMP OPAMP --> VOUT["Vout = Vref x D / 2 to the n"] GND["Ground"] -.Switch b=0.-> SW0 GND -.Switch b=0.-> SW1 GND -.Switch b=0.-> SW2 GND -.Switch b=0.-> SW3 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Each bit switch routes its ladder rung to either V_ref (bit = 1) or ground (bit = 0). The ladder's natural voltage division produces binary-weighted analog voltages at each node, which the op-amp sums and buffers to produce V_out. --- ### 4.3 PWM-to-Analog Conversion Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR TIMER["8051 Timer
Mode 1 or Mode 2"] --> PWM["PWM Waveform
Variable Duty Cycle"] PWM --> RC["RC Low-Pass Filter
R and C"] RC --> DC["Smoothed DC Voltage
Vout = VCC x Duty"] DC --> LOAD["Analog Load
Motor, Lamp, Heater"] ``` --- ### 4.4 Output Transfer Characteristic (Ideal DAC) ```mermaid flowchart LR A["00000000
0"] --> V0["0.000 V"] B["00000001
1"] --> V1["19.5 mV"] C["10000000
128"] --> V2["2.500 V"] D["11111111
255"] --> V3["4.980 V"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **For an 8-bit DAC with V_ref = 5 V:** > - Code 00H → V_out = 0 V (ground) > - Code 01H → V_out = 19.5 mV (one LSB step) > - Code 80H → V_out = 2.5 V (mid-scale) > - Code FFH → V_out = 4.980 V (one LSB shy of V_ref) --- ### 4.5 Weighted Resistor DAC Structure (Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TB B0["b0 LSB"] --> R0["2 to n-1 times R
Highest Resistance"] B1["b1"] --> R1["2 to n-2 times R"] B2["b(n-2)"] --> R2["2R"] B3["b(n-1) MSB"] --> R3["R
Lowest Resistance"] R0 --> SUM["Summing Junction
Op-Amp Inverting Input"] R1 --> SUM R2 --> SUM R3 --> SUM RFB["Rf Feedback Resistor"] --> SUM VREF["Vref"] --> R0 VREF --> R1 VREF --> R2 VREF --> R3 SUM --> OPAMP["Op-Amp
Summing Configuration"] OPAMP --> VOUT["Vout proportional to Digital Code"] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why R-2R is preferred over Weighted Resistor:** For 8-bit, weighted resistors range from R to 128R — a 128:1 spread. R-2R ladder uses only R and 2R — a 2:1 spread — making IC fabrication vastly easier. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the term *Resolution* of a DAC. For an 8-bit DAC with V_ref = 5 V, calculate the step size (V_LSB). **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Resolution of a DAC is defined as the **smallest change in the analog output voltage** that can be distinguished when the digital input changes by **one LSB (1 Least Significant Bit)**. It is determined by the number of input bits *n* and is often expressed as a percentage. **Mathematical expression (1 mark):** $$\text{Resolution (V)} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$ **Calculation (1 mark):** $$V_{LSB} = \frac{5.0 \text{ V}}{2^8} = \frac{5.0}{256} = 0.01953 \text{ V} = 19.53 \text{ mV}$$ Hence, the step size is **19.53 mV**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the **Full-Scale Output Voltage (V_FS)** of an n-bit DAC? Compute V_FS for a 10-bit DAC with V_ref = 4.096 V. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** The Full-Scale Output Voltage is the **maximum analog output voltage** produced by a DAC when its digital input is set to all 1s (i.e., D = 2ⁿ − 1). **Formula (1 mark):** $$V_{FS} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{2^n - 1}{2^n}$$ **Calculation (1 mark):** $$V_{FS} = 4.096 \cdot \frac{2^{10} - 1}{2^{10}} = 4.096 \cdot \frac{1023}{1024} = 4.096 \cdot 0.99902 = 4.0920 \text{ V}$$ Hence, V_FS ≈ **4.0920 V** (slightly less than V_ref by one LSB = 4 mV). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Two Part-B questions from each module appear in the End Semester Exam, and the student answers **one** (Module Internal Choice). Each question has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the interfacing of an 8-bit DAC (DAC 0808) to the 8051 microcontroller. List the control signals required and describe the data flow. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An 8-bit R-2R ladder DAC has V_ref = 5.0 V. Find the analog output voltage for the following digital input codes: **(i)** 00000000, **(ii)** 10000000, **(iii)** 11111111. Also calculate the step size. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question A:** **(a) Interfacing 8-bit DAC to 8051 (7 marks):** > **Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** The 8051 Port 1 (P1.0 to P1.7) is connected to the 8-bit data inputs of the DAC 0808 (pins A1 through A8). The DAC 0808 is a **current-output** DAC, so an external op-amp (configured as a current-to-voltage converter) is connected to its I_out pin. The op-amp output produces the analog V_out. A precision **+5.0 V reference** is applied to V_ref+ of the DAC 0808. > **Control Signals (2 marks):** The DAC 0808 requires the following control signals: - **V_ref+ (Pin 14):** Connected to a stable +5.0 V reference (often derived from a zener diode or precision reference IC like LM336). - **V_ref− (Pin 15):** Connected to analog ground (AGND). - **I_out (Pin 4):** Analog current output; connected to the inverting input of the op-amp. - **I_out (complement, Pin 2):** Connected to analog ground. - **V_EE (Pin 3):** Connected to −12 V (or −5 V) supply to bias the internal current switches. - **V_CC (Pin 13):** Connected to +5 V digital supply. > **Data Flow Description (2 marks):** The 8051 CPU executes an instruction such as: ``` MOV P1, #data_byte ; load digital code to Port 1 ``` The 8-bit data byte is latched on Port 1 pins. The DAC 0808 continuously reads the data and sinks a current proportional to the digital value at the I_out pin. The op-amp converts this current to a voltage using V_out = I_out × R_f. The resulting V_out is the analog equivalent of the digital code. --- **(b) R-2R Ladder Output Voltages (7 marks):** **Given:** n = 8, V_ref = 5.0 V **Step size (2 marks):** $$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5.0}{256} = 19.53 \text{ mV/LSB}$$ **Formula (1 mark):** $$V_{out} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} \cdot D$$ **Calculations (4 marks):** **(i) For D = 00000000₂ = 0₁₀:** $$V_{out} = \frac{5.0}{256} \cdot 0 = 0.0 \text{ V}$$ **(ii) For D = 10000000₂ = 128₁₀:** $$V_{out} = \frac{5.0}{256} \cdot 128 = 2.5 \text{ V}$$ **(iii) For D = 11111111₂ = 255₁₀:** $$V_{out} = \frac{5.0}{256} \cdot 255 = 4.9805 \text{ V}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Compare the **Weighted Resistor DAC** and the **R-2R Ladder DAC** in terms of construction, accuracy, and ease of fabrication. Which is preferred for n > 6 bits and why? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A microcontroller generates a PWM waveform with frequency 2 kHz and duty cycle 35% on a port pin (V_CC = 5 V). The output is connected to an RC low-pass filter with R = 10 kΩ. **(i)** Find the average DC output voltage after filtering. **(ii)** Calculate the required capacitance C to ensure good smoothing (assume τ = 10 · T). **(iii)** If the desired DC output is 2.5 V, what duty cycle is required? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question B:** **(a) Comparison of DAC Types (7 marks):** | Parameter (1 mark) | Weighted Resistor DAC (2 marks) | R-2R Ladder DAC (2 marks) | |---|---|---| | **Construction** | Resistor values: R, 2R, 4R, ..., 2ⁿ⁻¹R | Only two values: R and 2R | | **Number of resistor values** | n different values | 2 values | | **Range of resistance** | 2ⁿ⁻¹ : 1 (e.g., 128:1 for 8-bit) | 2 : 1 | | **IC fabrication difficulty** | Very hard for n > 4 (large spread) | Trivial — easy to match | | **Accuracy** | Limited by R, 2R, 4R, ... matching | Limited by R, 2R matching only | | **Cost** | High for high resolution | Low, scales with n | | **Speed** | Faster (parallel switches) | Slightly slower (ladder capacitance) | | **Typical use** | Educational / low-bit (≤ 4-bit) | Industry standard, 8 to 16-bit ICs | **Conclusion (2 marks):** > The **R-2R Ladder DAC** is preferred for n > 6 bits because: > - It uses only **two resistor values (R and 2R)**, which can be easily fabricated with tight matching on a monolithic IC. > - The resistor spread is only **2:1**, eliminating the wide tolerance issues of weighted-resistor designs. > - It scales efficiently to 10, 12, 14, or 16-bit resolution without proportional cost increase. > - Real-world examples (DAC 0808, DAC 0832, AD558, AD7524) all use R-2R architecture. --- **(b) PWM-to-Analog Conversion (7 marks):** **Given:** - f_PWM = 2 kHz → T = 1/f = 0.5 ms - Duty cycle = 35% = 0.35 - V_CC = 5 V - R = 10 kΩ **(i) Average DC Output Voltage (2 marks):** $$V_{out} = V_{CC} \cdot \text{Duty Cycle} = 5.0 \cdot 0.35 = 1.75 \text{ V}$$ **(ii) Required Capacitance C (3 marks):** For good smoothing, τ = 10 · T: $$\tau = 10 \cdot T = 10 \cdot 0.5 \text{ ms} = 5.0 \text{ ms} = 0.005 \text{ s}$$ $$C = \frac{\tau}{R} = \frac{0.005}{10{,}000} = 5.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ F} = 0.5 \text{ μF}$$ **Verification — Cutoff Frequency (optional):** $$f_{-3dB} = \frac{1}{2\pi R C} = \frac{1}{2\pi \cdot 10{,}000 \cdot 0.5 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 31.8 \text{ Hz}$$ This is ≪ 2 kHz, so the filter heavily attenuates the PWM ripple. ✓ **(iii) Duty Cycle for V_out = 2.5 V (2 marks):** $$\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{CC}} = \frac{2.5}{5.0} = 0.5 = 50\%$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert binary to decimal** before applying the V_out formula. The formula $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot D / 2^n$ requires **D in decimal**, not binary. **Lose 1–2 marks.** > > 2. **Confusing V_FS with V_ref.** V_FS = V_ref · (2ⁿ−1)/2ⁿ, which is **one LSB short of V_ref**, NOT equal to V_ref. **Lose 1 mark.** > > 3. **Unit errors in V_LSB calculation.** Always state the unit (mV or V) explicitly. A bare number "0.0195" is ambiguous. **Lose 0.5–1 mark.** > > 4. **PWM problem — ignoring the filter design.** Many students compute V_out but forget the filter component calculation. KTU often asks both parts. **Lose 2–3 marks.** > > 5. **Inverting the formula** when asked "what digital code produces a given V_out?" The correct inversion is D = V_out · 2ⁿ / V_ref, and the result must be **rounded to the nearest integer** (since D must be an integer code). **Lose 1 mark for not rounding.** > > 6. **Sign convention in op-amp DACs.** The summing amplifier output is **inverted** (negative). Forgetting the sign shows incomplete understanding. **Lose 1 mark.** > > 7. **Forgetting the "simple DAC output generation" framework.** KTU Module 2 expects knowledge of *all three methods* — Weighted Resistor, R-2R Ladder, and PWM. Restricting the answer to only one method may lose comparison-marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2: Simple DAC Output Generation** ### **Core Concepts** - ☑ **DAC definition:** Digital codeword → continuous analog voltage/current - ☑ **Three simple methods:** Weighted Resistor, R-2R Ladder, PWM + RC filter - ☑ **Resolution** = smallest step = V_ref / 2ⁿ (in volts) or 1/2ⁿ × 100 (in %) - ☑ **Full-Scale Output (V_FS)** = V_ref · (2ⁿ − 1) / 2ⁿ (one LSB shy of V_ref) - ☑ **Step Size (V_LSB)** = V_FS / (2ⁿ − 1) ≈ V_ref / 2ⁿ ### **The Master Formula (Memorize!)** - ☑ $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2^n}$ — where D is the **decimal** value of the binary input ### **Practical Knowledge** - ☑ **R-2R Ladder** is the industry standard (uses only 2 resistor values) - ☑ **Weighted Resistor** is conceptually simple but impractical for n > 4 - ☑ **PWM + RC filter** = software DAC when no external DAC IC is available - ☑ Filter design rule: τ = R·C should be ≈ 10 × PWM period for good smoothing - ☑ 8051 has **no internal DAC** — must use external ICs like DAC 0808 / DAC 0832 ### **Quick Reference Values (Memorize)** - ☑ 8-bit DAC, V_ref = 5 V → V_LSB = 19.53 mV, V_FS = 4.98 V - ☑ 10-bit DAC, V_ref = 4.096 V → V_LSB = 4.0 mV, V_FS = 4.092 V - ☑ 12-bit DAC, V_ref = 4.096 V → V_LSB = 1.0 mV, V_FS = 4.095 V - ☑ Code 80H (128) on 8-bit DAC → always produces V_ref / 2 (mid-scale) - ☑ Code FFH (255) on 8-bit DAC → always produces V_FS (one LSB shy of V_ref) ### **KTU Exam Quick Wins** - ☑ **Always convert binary to decimal** before applying the formula - ☑ **Always show units** in the final answer (V, mV, μF, kHz, ms) - ☑ **State the formula** explicitly before substituting values - ☑ For PWM, **show the duty cycle calculation** before computing V_out - ☑ Mention **R-2R advantages** (only 2 resistor values) in comparison questions - ☑ Remember the **inverting op-amp sign** in summing-amp DAC outputs > [!NOTE] > **Final Word:** The DAC topic is **highly scoring** in KTU exams because it is mathematically deterministic. With 3 core formulas (V_out, V_FS, V_LSB) and 3 methods (Weighted, R-2R, PWM), you can solve >90% of expected questions. Practice **5–10 numerical problems** covering all three methods, and you will be well-prepared for both Part A and Part B questions.

topics.title: Generating a Sine Wave
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Generating a Sine Wave

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sine Wave Generation (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **Sine Wave Generator** in a microcontroller-based system is a digital signal processing technique wherein a pre-computed set of discrete amplitude values representing one full cycle of a sinusoidal function (sinusoidal lookup table / LUT) is sequentially fed to a **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** at regular time intervals governed by a hardware timer. The DAC reconstructs the analog sine waveform, whose **frequency** is controlled by the rate at which LUT samples are output (sample rate), and whose **amplitude resolution** is governed by the DAC bit-width.

In the KTU 2024 Microcontrollers syllabus (Module 2: Peripherals & Signal Generation), generating a sine wave is the canonical application problem used to teach:
1. DAC interfacing with a microcontroller (e.g., 8051, ARM LPC2148, STM32).
2. The concept of a **lookup table** in embedded C.
3. Timer-driven sample-rate control.
4. The mathematical relationship between sample count `N`, sampling frequency `f_s`, and output frequency `f_out`.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Rotating Lighthouse Beam"**
> Imagine a lighthouse whose beam sweeps in a circle, but you only photograph it at **36 fixed moments** around the circle. The 36 photographs, when flipped through in sequence at a steady speed on a film reel, make the beam *appear* to rotate smoothly. The lighthouse is the true **continuous sine wave**; the 36 photographs are the **discrete LUT samples**; and the film reel's speed is the **timer-driven sample rate**. If you flip faster, the apparent rotation frequency increases — exactly like raising `f_out` by increasing the sample rate `f_s`.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The key engineering metrics (always highlighted in **bold** in KTU answers) are:

- **N** → Number of samples per complete sine cycle (typically **36, 48, 50, 64, 100, or 256**).
- **f_s** → Sampling frequency (Hz) — the rate at which DAC receives new values.
- **f_out** → Output sine wave frequency (Hz).
- **Δt** → Time between two consecutive samples = **1 / f_s**.
- **DAC resolution** → Number of bits, e.g., **8-bit** (256 levels), **10-bit** (1024 levels), **12-bit** (4096 levels).
- **V_ref** → Reference voltage of the DAC (e.g., **+3.3 V** or **+5 V**).

The fundamental governing relation is universally written as:

$$f_{out} = \frac{f_s}{N}$$

This single equation is the **heart of every KTU sine-wave generation numerical**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How the System Works

A microcontroller generates a sine wave in **five structured steps**:

1. **Pre-compute the LUT** (offline, before main loop):
   - Discretize one cycle of `sin(θ)` into `N` equally spaced points, where `θ = 0°, 360°/N, 2·360°/N, …, (N−1)·360°/N`.
   - Scale each value to the DAC's integer range `[0, 2^n − 1]`, where `n` is DAC bit-width.
   - Store the `N` integers in a `const` array in program memory (Flash/ROM).

2. **Initialize the DAC**:
   - Configure the DAC pin/port as analog output.
   - Set `V_ref` if externally pin-strapped.

3. **Initialize the timer**:
   - Program the timer to overflow at frequency `f_s`, generating one **interrupt per sample period** `Δt = 1 / f_s`.
   - On 8051, this uses Timer mode 1 (16-bit); on LPC2148/STM32, this uses Match Register or PWM/Update interrupts.

4. **ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) — output the next sample**:
   - On each timer tick, fetch `LUT[i]`, write it to the DAC register, and increment the index `i = (i + 1) mod N`.
   - The repetition modulus `N` is what makes the wave **periodic**.

5. **Analog reconstruction**:
   - The DAC's zero-order-hold staircase is smoothed by an external low-pass **RC filter** (or op-amp active filter) to recover a clean sinusoid.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Typical KTU Use |
|---|--------------------|------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Output frequency | $f_{out} = \dfrac{f_s}{N}$ | Direct question — compute `N` or `f_s` |
| 2 | Sampling period | $\Delta t = \dfrac{1}{f_s} = \dfrac{T_{out}}{N}$ | Relate timer reload to output period |
| 3 | LUT value (unipolar, `n`-bit DAC) | $DAC\_val = \left\lfloor \dfrac{(2^n - 1)}{2} \cdot \big[1 + \sin(\theta_k)\big] + 0.5 \right\rfloor$ | Compute the integer code at angle `θ_k` |
| 4 | LUT value (bipolar, centered at 0) | $DAC\_val = \left\lfloor V_{mid} + A \cdot \sin(\theta_k) + 0.5 \right\rfloor$ | When output swing is $\pm A$ around `V_mid` |
| 5 | Timer reload (8051, 12 MHz crystal, Mode 1) | $Reload = 65536 - \dfrac{12 \times 10^6}{12 \cdot f_s} = 65536 - \dfrac{10^6}{f_s}$ | Compute TH0/TL0 hex values |
| 6 | Step size (variable-frequency mode) | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{2^{32} \cdot f_{out}}{f_{clock}}$ | DDS (Direct Digital Synthesis) extension |
| 7 | Frequency resolution | $\Delta f = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{2^{32}}$ | DDS resolution |
| 8 | Quantization noise step | $q = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ | LSB size of DAC |
| 9 | Nyquist constraint | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{out}^{max}$ | Anti-alias requirement |
| 10 | Reconstructed amplitude (post-filter) | $V_{pk} \approx \dfrac{(2^{n-1} - 1) \cdot V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$ | Peak voltage after reconstruction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** When writing the LUT formula, KTU examiners expect the **"+ 0.5"** rounding term and the explicit mention of `floor()` (or integer cast). Forgetting the `+ 0.5` is the single most common mark-losing error in numericals.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Sine wave generation from a microcontroller is the foundational building block of:
- **Audio synthesizers** and tone generators.
- **Function/arbitrary waveform generators (AWGs)**.
- **Power inverter SPWM control** (sinusoidal PWM for motor drives).
- **Acoustic / ultrasonic transducers** (e.g., 40 kHz sonar ping).
- **DTMF tone generation** in telephone/GSM modules.
- **Software-Defined Radio (SDR)** DDS-based local oscillators.

The same LUT-plus-timer architecture scales from low-cost 8051 chips to high-performance 32-bit ARM/DSP platforms — the math does not change, only the timer reload arithmetic scales with the system clock.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Master Output-Frequency Equation

We begin with the definition of the sampling period:

$$\Delta t = \frac{1}{f_s}$$

The time required to output one **full** sine cycle (all `N` samples once) is the output period:

$$T_{out} = N \cdot \Delta t = \frac{N}{f_s}$$

Taking the reciprocal of both sides gives the output frequency:

$$f_{out} = \frac{1}{T_{out}} = \frac{f_s}{N}$$

**Conversion logic / physical meaning:**
- Doubling the sample rate (running the timer twice as fast) → doubles the output frequency.
- Doubling `N` (finer angular resolution) → halves the output frequency for a fixed `f_s`.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Computing the LUT Value for an `n`-bit Unipolar DAC

A unipolar DAC (output range `0 V` to `V_ref`) can only emit **non-negative** codes `0, 1, 2, …, (2^n − 1)`. The continuous `sin(θ)` oscillates in `[-1, +1]`, so we must **bias and scale** it into the DAC's code range.

**Step 1 — Normalize the sine:**
The bipolar sine `sin(θ)` lies in `[-1, +1]`. Adding `1` shifts it to `[0, 2]`. Dividing by `2` maps it to `[0, 1]`.

$$\text{normalized}(\theta) = \frac{1 + \sin(\theta)}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Scale to DAC full-scale:**
The DAC's full-scale code is `(2^n − 1)`, so multiply by it:

$$DAC_{ideal}(\theta) = \left(\frac{2^n - 1}{2}\right) \cdot \big[1 + \sin(\theta)\big]$$

**Step 3 — Integerize with rounding (the "+ 0.5 trick"):**
The DAC accepts only integer codes. We round to the nearest integer by adding `0.5` then truncating (floor):

$$DAC\_val(\theta) = \left\lfloor \left(\frac{2^n - 1}{2}\right) \cdot \big[1 + \sin(\theta)\big] + 0.5 \right\rfloor$$

**Step 4 — Example check for n = 8, θ = 90°:**
- `sin(90°) = 1`
- `(2^8 − 1)/2 = 127.5`
- `127.5 × (1 + 1) = 255`
- `255 + 0.5 = 255.5`, floor → **`255`**
- The DAC outputs `255/255 × V_ref = V_ref` (the sine peak). ✔

**Step 5 — Example check for n = 8, θ = 270°:**
- `sin(270°) = −1`
- `127.5 × (1 + (−1)) = 0`
- `0 + 0.5 = 0.5`, floor → **`0`**
- The DAC outputs `0 V` (the sine trough). ✔

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Timer Reload Value for the 8051 (12 MHz Crystal)

A standard 8051 system uses a **12 MHz crystal**, but the internal machine-cycle clock is the crystal divided by 12, i.e., **1 MHz**. One timer increment therefore takes **1 µs**.

**Step 1 — Required timer increments per sample period:**
If we need to output a sample every `Δt` seconds, the timer must count up `Δt` increments of 1 µs each. The number of timer counts is:

$$Counts_{\Delta t} = \frac{\Delta t}{1\,\mu s} = \frac{1}{f_s \cdot 1\,\mu s} = \frac{10^6}{f_s}$$

**Step 2 — Subtract from the 16-bit full-scale (Mode 1):**
Timer 0/1 in Mode 1 is a 16-bit up-counter that overflows from `65535` to `0000`. The reload value is therefore:

$$Reload = 65536 - Counts_{\Delta t} = 65536 - \frac{10^6}{f_s}$$

**Step 3 — Splitting into TH0 and TL0 (high and low bytes):**

$$\begin{aligned}
TL0 &= \text{Reload} \;\&\; 0x00FF \\
TH0 &= (\text{Reload} \;\gg\; 8) \;\&\; 0x00FF
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (f_s = 10 kHz, generate a 200 Hz sine using N = 50):**
- `Counts = 10^6 / 10000 = 100`
- `Reload = 65536 − 100 = 65436`
- `65436 in hex = 0xFF9C`
- `TL0 = 0x9C`, `TH0 = 0xFF`

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Variable-Frequency Mode (DDS Step Size)

When the application demands **smoothly variable frequency** (e.g., a music synthesizer), the LUT is indexed by an **accumulator** `φ`, not by a fixed counter.

**Step 1 — Define a 32-bit phase accumulator `φ`:**
It increments by a **tuning word** `M` on every clock tick:

$$\phi_{k+1} = (\phi_k + M) \mod 2^{32}$$

**Step 2 — The LUT index is the upper bits of `φ`:**
If the LUT has `N` entries, the index is:

$$i = \left\lfloor \frac{\phi}{2^{32} / N} \right\rfloor$$

**Step 3 — Output frequency relationship:**
On average, `M` of the `2^32` possible phase values are consumed per clock, so the LUT is traversed `M / 2^32` times per clock cycle. Multiplying by the clock `f_{clk}`:

$$f_{out} = \frac{M \cdot f_{clk}}{2^{32}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for M (the KTU-favorite rearrangement):**

$$M = \left\lfloor \frac{f_{out} \cdot 2^{32}}{f_{clk}} + 0.5 \right\rfloor$$

**Step 5 — Frequency resolution:**
The smallest non-zero `M` is 1, giving the smallest frequency step:

$$\Delta f = \frac{f_{clk}}{2^{32}}$$

This is the **engineering reason** 32-bit accumulators are standard in DDS chips like the AD9850.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Matrix (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An 8051 system (f_osc = 11.0592 MHz) is required to generate a **500 Hz sine wave** using an 8-bit DAC. The LUT has **N = 100** samples per cycle. Compute (a) the required sampling frequency, (b) the timer reload value, and (c) the first four LUT values.

**Solution — Part (a) Sampling frequency:**

$$f_s = N \cdot f_{out} = 100 \times 500 = 50{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 50 \text{ kHz}$$

**Solution — Part (b) Timer reload:**
For 8051, machine-cycle clock = `11.0592 MHz / 12 = 0.9216 MHz`, so one tick = `1 / 0.9216 MHz ≈ 1.085 µs`. Number of counts per sample period:

$$Counts = f_{mc} / f_s = 921600 / 50000 = 18.432$$

Since timer counts must be integer, we take `Counts ≈ 18` (the ±0.4% error is acceptable in 8051 designs; precise designs use an even multiple of `12 / f_osc`).

$$Reload = 65536 - 18 = 65518 = 0xFFEE$$

$$\begin{aligned}
TL0 &= 0xEE \\
TH0 &= 0xFF
\end{aligned}$$

**Solution — Part (c) First four LUT values (k = 0, 1, 2, 3):**

For `n = 8`, `(2^n − 1)/2 = 127.5`. The angles are `θ_k = 360°·k/100`.

$$\begin{aligned}
k=0:\quad \theta = 0°,\;\; DAC\_val &= \lfloor 127.5 \cdot (1+0) + 0.5 \rfloor = \lfloor 128.0 \rfloor = 128 \\
k=1:\quad \theta = 3.6°,\;\; \sin(3.6°) \approx 0.0628 \\
DAC\_val &= \lfloor 127.5 \cdot (1.0628) + 0.5 \rfloor = \lfloor 135.51 + 0.5 \rfloor = 136 \\
k=2:\quad \theta = 7.2°,\;\; \sin(7.2°) \approx 0.1253 \\
DAC\_val &= \lfloor 127.5 \cdot (1.1253) + 0.5 \rfloor = \lfloor 143.48 + 0.5 \rfloor = 143 \\
k=3:\quad \theta = 10.8°,\;\; \sin(10.8°) \approx 0.1874 \\
DAC\_val &= \lfloor 127.5 \cdot (1.1874) + 0.5 \rfloor = \lfloor 151.39 + 0.5 \rfloor = 151
\end{aligned}$$

The four values `[128, 136, 143, 151]` form the **rising quarter** of the sine wave, starting at the mid-rail (since unipolar DACs cannot output negative voltages, the sine is biased up by `V_ref/2`).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Sine Wave Generation Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LUT[ROM LUT of N sine samples] --> IDX[Index Pointer i]
    IDX --> DACINT[DAC Data Register Write]
    DACINT --> DAC8[8 bit or 12 bit DAC]
    DAC8 --> LPF[RC Low Pass Filter]
    LPF --> AMP[Op Amp Buffer Amplifier]
    AMP --> OUT[Reconstructed Sine Wave Output]
    TIMER[Hardware Timer at f sub s Hz] --> ISR[Timer ISR Routine]
    ISR --> IDX
    ISR --> TIMER
    CTRL[Microcontroller CPU] --> LUT
    CTRL --> TIMER
    CTRL --> DACINT
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **CPU** initializes the LUT pointer, the timer, and the DAC once at startup.
- The **timer** fires the ISR at exactly `f_s` Hz — this is the heartbeat of the system.
- Inside the ISR, the next sample is read from the **ROM LUT**, written to the **DAC data register**, and the index `i` is incremented modulo `N`.
- The **DAC** emits a staircase analog voltage; the **RC low-pass filter** smooths the staircase into a clean sinusoid.
- The **op-amp buffer** provides current drive and isolates the filter from the load.

## 4.2 ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Timer Overflow Interrupt Fires] --> SAVE[Push ACC and PSW onto stack]
    SAVE --> READ[Read LUT index i from RAM]
    READ --> OUTDAC[Write LUT of i to DAC register]
    OUTDAC --> INC[Increment i by 1]
    INC --> TEST{Is i equal to N}
    TEST -- Yes --> RESET[Reset i to 0]
    TEST -- No  --> SKIP[Keep i unchanged]
    RESET --> RETURN
    SKIP --> RETURN
    RETURN[Pop ACC and PSW, Return from ISR]
```

**Why the wrap-around matters:**
The `if (i == N) i = 0;` test is what makes the output **periodic**. Forgetting the wrap-around causes the LUT pointer to walk off the end of the array, fetch garbage, and the sine wave collapses into a flat line or noise — a classic KTU lab troubleshooting item.

## 4.3 Full System Schematic (Block Form)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MCU[Microcontroller 8051 or ARM]
        CPU1[CPU Core]
        T0[Timer 0]
        LUTMEM[Flash ROM Holding Sine LUT]
        DACREG[DAC Data Register]
    end
    subgraph ANALOG[Analog Reconstruction Section]
        DACCHIP[DAC0808 or MCP4725]
        RCFILTER[RC Low Pass Filter]
        OPAMP[Op Amp LM358 Buffer]
    end
    subgraph LOAD[Output Stage]
        SPEAKER[Speaker or Oscilloscope]
    end
    CPU1 --> T0
    T0 -- Interrupt every 1 over f sub s --> CPU1
    CPU1 -- Reads --> LUTMEM
    LUTMEM -- Returns LUT of i --> CPU1
    CPU1 -- Writes --> DACREG
    DACREG -- Digital code --> DACCHIP
    DACCHIP -- Staircase analog --> RCFILTER
    RCFILTER -- Smooth sinusoid --> OPAMP
    OPAMP --> SPEAKER
```

**Engineering insight from the schematic:**
- The **digital** and **analog** domains are physically separated at the DAC chip boundary. This is the *only* point in the system where the signal changes from discrete-time discrete-amplitude to continuous-time continuous-amplitude.
- The **timer-driven ISR** is what makes the timing deterministic — software `delay()` loops would drift with compiler optimization and are explicitly forbidden in KTU model answers for waveform generation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

### Q1. What is a sine wave lookup table? Why is it used in microcontroller-based sine wave generation?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A sine wave **lookup table (LUT)** is a pre-computed array of `N` integer values that represent the amplitude of one complete cycle of `sin(θ)`, sampled at equal angular intervals and scaled to the DAC's code range. It is used because **(1)** computing `sin(θ)` in real-time using series expansions would consume excessive CPU cycles and introduce jitter, **(2)** an array lookup is a single-cycle operation, making the timing deterministic and ISR-friendly, and **(3)** it decouples the analog waveform precision from the CPU's floating-point capability — the math is done once at design time. The LUT is stored in **Flash/ROM** (declared `const`) to preserve RAM.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q2. State and explain the relation between sampling frequency, number of samples per cycle, and output sine wave frequency.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The fundamental relation is `f_out = f_s / N`, where `f_s` is the sampling frequency (Hz), `N` is the number of LUT samples per complete sine cycle, and `f_out` is the output frequency. This means the output period equals the time to play all `N` samples once: `T_out = N · Δt = N / f_s`. For example, with `N = 50` and `f_s = 10 kHz`, the output is `200 Hz`. To **raise** the frequency one can either **increase `f_s`** (run the timer faster) or **decrease `N`** (use a coarser table).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, ESE Module 2, Q7]** — *14 marks*

### ⭐ Question A — Design of a 1 kHz Sine Generator using 8051 and DAC0808

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain how a sine wave is generated using a microcontroller, DAC, and lookup table. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An 8051 system (crystal = **11.0592 MHz**) must generate a **1 kHz sine wave** using a 100-sample LUT. Calculate: (i) the required sampling frequency, (ii) the Timer 0 reload value in Mode 1, and (iii) the first three LUT values for an 8-bit unipolar DAC with `V_ref = 5 V`. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The system has **four functional blocks** (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Microcontroller (8051)** — hosts the LUT in ROM and the timer that drives the sample clock.
2. **LUT generation (offline)** — for each of the `N = 100` angles `θ_k = k·(360°/100)`, the value `sin(θ_k)` is computed, scaled to `[0, 255]` using the standard formula:
$$DAC\_val(k) = \left\lfloor 127.5 \cdot [1 + \sin(\theta_k)] + 0.5 \right\rfloor$$
and stored in a `const unsigned char sine_lut[100]` array.

3. **Timer 0 in Mode 1** — reloaded to overflow at the sampling frequency `f_s = N · f_out = 100 · 1000 = 100 kHz`. On overflow, an ISR is entered.

4. **ISR logic:**
   - Read `sine_lut[i]`.
   - Write it to the **DAC0808** data port (P1 of 8051).
   - Increment `i = (i + 1) mod 100`.

5. **DAC0808 + smoothing** — the DAC converts the 8-bit code to a 0–5 V staircase. An **RC low-pass filter** (cutoff ≈ `2 · f_out = 2 kHz`) smooths it into a clean sinusoid. An op-amp buffer (LM358) provides load isolation.

The **timing is deterministic** because the timer hardware — not a software delay — schedules each sample. The **wave is periodic** because `i` is taken modulo `N`.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Sampling frequency:**

$$f_s = N \cdot f_{out} = 100 \times 1000 = 100{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 100 \text{ kHz}$$

**(ii) Timer 0 reload value:**
Machine-cycle clock = `11.0592 MHz / 12 = 0.9216 MHz` ⇒ one tick = `1.085 µs`. Required counts per sample period:

$$Counts = \frac{0.9216 \times 10^6}{100{,}000} = 9.216$$

Take the integer floor: `Counts = 9` (≈ 2.4% error; for production designs we pick a crystal whose `/12` divides exactly into `f_s`).

$$Reload = 65536 - 9 = 65527 = 0xFFE7$$

$$\begin{aligned}
TH0 &= 0xFF \\
TL0 &= 0xE7
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) First three LUT values (k = 0, 1, 2):**
For `n = 8`, scale factor `= 127.5`. Angles `θ_k = k·3.6°`.

$$\begin{aligned}
k=0:\; \theta=0°,\; \sin=0 &\Rightarrow \lfloor 127.5 \cdot 1.0 + 0.5 \rfloor = \mathbf{128} \\
k=1:\; \theta=3.6°,\; \sin\approx 0.0628 &\Rightarrow \lfloor 127.5 \cdot 1.0628 + 0.5 \rfloor = \mathbf{136} \\
k=2:\; \theta=7.2°,\; \sin\approx 0.1253 &\Rightarrow \lfloor 127.5 \cdot 1.1253 + 0.5 \rfloor = \mathbf{143}
\end{aligned}$$

These correspond to output voltages of `[2.51 V, 2.67 V, 2.80 V]` on the 0–5 V scale — the rising first quadrant of the sine wave biased at the mid-rail of `2.5 V`.

---

### ⭐ Question B — Alternative Module Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, ESE Module 2, Q8 Alternate]**

**(a)** Explain the DDS (Direct Digital Synthesis) method of sine wave generation. Derive the relation between the tuning word `M` and output frequency. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An LPC2148 ARM7 system uses a **30 MHz** APB clock and a 32-bit phase accumulator to drive a 256-entry sine LUT. If the desired output frequency is **150 Hz**, calculate (i) the tuning word `M`, (ii) the frequency resolution, and (iii) the LUT index corresponding to an accumulated phase `φ = 0x4000_0000`. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Direct Digital Synthesis (DDS)** generates a variable-frequency sine wave without changing any hardware — the entire system is digital until the final DAC.

The architecture has **three blocks**:
1. **Phase Accumulator** — a 32-bit register `φ` that increments by a **tuning word** `M` on every clock tick: `φ ← (φ + M) mod 2^32`.
2. **LUT + DAC** — the upper bits of `φ` index a sine LUT, whose output is fed to a DAC.
3. **LPF** — smooths the DAC staircase.

**Derivation of the output-frequency relation:**
Each clock tick advances the phase by `M / 2^32` of one full cycle. In one second (i.e., `f_{clk}` ticks), the phase completes `M · f_{clk} / 2^32` full cycles. Therefore:

$$f_{out} = \frac{M \cdot f_{clk}}{2^{32}}$$

**Key advantage:** `M` is a **software register**, so changing `M` instantly changes the output frequency with no analog retuning. The frequency resolution is `Δf = f_{clk} / 2^32`, which for `f_{clk} = 30 MHz` gives `Δf ≈ 0.00698 Hz` — sub-centihertz precision.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Tuning word for f_out = 150 Hz:**

$$M = \left\lfloor \frac{f_{out} \cdot 2^{32}}{f_{clk}} + 0.5 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{150 \times 4{,}294{,}967{,}296}{30 \times 10^6} + 0.5 \right\rfloor$$

$$M = \left\lfloor \frac{644{,}245{,}094{,}400}{30{,}000{,}000} + 0.5 \right\rfloor = \lfloor 21474.836 + 0.5 \rfloor = \mathbf{21475}$$

**(ii) Frequency resolution:**

$$\Delta f = \frac{f_{clk}}{2^{32}} = \frac{30 \times 10^6}{4{,}294{,}967{,}296} \approx 6.984 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Hz}$$

**(iii) LUT index for φ = 0x4000_0000:**
`0x4000_0000` = `2^30` in decimal. The LUT has `N = 256` entries, so the index is:

$$i = \left\lfloor \frac{\phi}{2^{32} / N} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2^{30}}{2^{32} / 256} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{2^{30} \times 256}{2^{32}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{256}{4} \right\rfloor = \mathbf{64}$$

The 64th entry of a 256-point sine table corresponds to phase `64/256 · 360° = 90°`, where `sin(90°) = 1` (the peak of the wave). The DAC code would be **255** for an 8-bit unipolar DAC.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Most Commonly Lose Marks in "Sine Wave Generation" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "+ 0.5" rounding term** in the LUT formula → off-by-one errors in the DAC code → loss of 1–2 marks in every numerical.
> 2. **Confusing `f_s` with `f_out`** in the master equation `f_out = f_s / N` → the entire numerical collapses. Always re-state: *"N samples per cycle, output = sample rate divided by N."*
> 3. **Forgetting to wrap the index** (`i = (i+1) % N`) in the ISR code → marks deducted for "non-periodic output" reasoning.
> 4. **Using a software `delay()` instead of a timer ISR** → marks deducted under "explain how deterministic timing is achieved." KTU expects the timer-interrupt mechanism by name.
> 5. **Writing `f_out = N · f_s`** (inverted) — the most catastrophic single error; zero marks for the final answer in many valuation schemes.
> 6. **Omitting the LPF** in the block diagram → in a 7-mark "with neat block diagram" sub-question, this omission costs a full sub-part.
> 7. **Confusing bipolar vs. unipolar DAC scaling** — for an 8-bit DAC, peak is `255` (unipolar) or `+127 / -128` (bipolar/sign-magnitude). Read the question stem carefully.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Generating a Sine Wave" (Module 2)**
>
> - **Master equation:** $f_{out} = f_s / N$. Memorize this; it appears in nearly every question.
> - **LUT formula (unipolar `n`-bit DAC):** $DAC\_val = \lfloor 127.5 \cdot (1 + \sin\theta) + 0.5 \rfloor$ for `n = 8`. For general `n`, use `(2^n − 1)/2`.
> - **Mid-rail biasing:** a unipolar DAC cannot output negative voltage, so the sine is shifted up by `V_ref/2`. The mid-rail code is `2^(n−1)`.
> - **Timer reload (8051, Mode 1, 12 MHz crystal):** $Reload = 65536 - 10^6 / f_s$. Always subtract; the timer counts UP to overflow.
> - **Wrap-around is mandatory:** the LUT index must use modulo `N` arithmetic, otherwise the wave is not periodic.
> - **Use timer ISR, not delays:** deterministic timing is the entire point of using a hardware timer.
> - **LPF is essential:** the DAC's staircase output must be filtered to recover a clean sinusoid; cutoff ≈ `2·f_out` is a good rule of thumb.
> - **DDS extension:** when the frequency must vary smoothly, replace the fixed index with a 32-bit phase accumulator and tuning word `M = round(f_out · 2^32 / f_clk)`.
> - **Nyquist criterion:** `f_s ≥ 2 · f_out`. In practice, use `N ≥ 50` for an acceptably smooth wave; `N = 100` or `256` is the KTU-favorite range.
> - **DAC resolution rule of thumb:** doubling `n` (e.g., 8 → 10 bit) reduces quantization noise by ≈ 6 dB but does NOT change the output frequency.
> - **Common pitfall to avoid:** `f_out = N · f_s` — this is the inverted (and wrong) form. Always write the master equation in words: *"output frequency is the sample rate divided by the number of samples per cycle."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Audio Signal Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Audio Signal Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Audio Signal Generation** in the context of embedded systems and microcontrollers refers to the systematic synthesis of time-varying electrical waveforms whose frequency components lie within the audible spectrum of **20 Hz to 20 kHz**, produced by a programmable digital processor (such as the 8051, ARM Cortex-M, or AVR family) and converted into an analog signal capable of driving a transducer (speaker, buzzer, or headphone).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, audio signal generation in microcontrollers is achieved through three principal hardware-software mechanisms:

1. **Direct Digital Synthesis (DDS)** – using a DAC to output a digitally stored waveform.
2. **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) followed by low-pass filtering** – a DAC-less technique where duty cycle is modulated to approximate an analog waveform.
3. **Software Square-Wave / Timer-Interrupt Generation** – toggling a GPIO pin at audio frequencies, typically used for buzzers, DTMF tones, and melodies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 2 focus is on **hardware peripherals of the 8051 family** (Timer/Counters, Serial Port, Interrupts) and their application in real-time signal generation. Audio signal generation is a direct application of **Timer-based square wave generation** and **PWM using timers**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Finger on a Doorbell" Model

Imagine you are ringing an old mechanical doorbell. You press the button rapidly. To a person listening from outside, the individual clicks blur into a continuous "buzz." 

Now, if you press the button **faster or slower**, the buzz changes pitch. If you press it for **longer or shorter durations** (changing the duty cycle), the loudness character changes. 

**The microcontroller is the finger. The output pin is the button. The speaker is the listener.** By precisely controlling *how fast* (frequency) and *how long* (duty cycle / amplitude) the pin is toggled, we generate audible signals — from simple beeps to complex music.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** Any digital waveform — square, sine, triangular, or arbitrary — can be reconstructed as audio if its **spectral energy falls within 20 Hz – 20 kHz** and is delivered with sufficient power to a transducer.

## 1.3 Standard Audio Metrics (KTU Board-Expected Values)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Audible Bandwidth | **20 Hz – 20 kHz** | Human hearing range |
| Standard Sampling Rate (CD Quality) | **44.1 kHz** | Nyquist minimum is 40 kHz for 20 kHz audio |
| Telephone Audio | **8 kHz** | Narrowband speech |
| Hi-Fi Audio | **48 kHz / 96 kHz** | Studio/professional |
| Bit Depth | **8 / 16 / 24-bit** | Dynamic range (SNR ≈ 6.02N + 1.76 dB) |
| Speaker Impedance | **8 Ω / 4 Ω** | Drives amplifier matching |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Overview of Microcontroller Audio Generation

The complete audio generation chain in an embedded system consists of **four sequential blocks**:

1. **Waveform Memory / Algorithm** – Stores sample values (sine lookup table) or computes them in real-time.
2. **Digital Output Stage** – Timer-driven GPIO toggling OR PWM duty cycle modulation.
3. **Reconstruction Filter** – Low-pass RC or active filter to remove high-frequency PWM carrier.
4. **Amplifier + Transducer** – Amplifies filtered signal to drive a speaker.

## 2.2 Method 1 — Square Wave Generation Using 8051 Timers

The **8051 Timer in Mode 1 (16-bit)** is the most commonly tested method in KTU exams. The timer overflows at a programmed value, fires an interrupt, and the ISR toggles a port pin.

### Timer Reload Calculation Logic

To generate a square wave of frequency `f_out`, the timer must overflow **every half-period**, so:

$$T_{out} = \frac{1}{f_{out}}$$

$$T_{half} = \frac{T_{out}}{2} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot f_{out}}$$

The number of timer counts required per overflow is:

$$N_{counts} = \frac{T_{half}}{T_{machine}}$$

Where the **machine cycle period** for a standard 8051 is:

$$T_{machine} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

Therefore, the **timer reload value** (TH0 for high byte) is:

$$\text{Timer Value} = 2^{16} - N_{counts} = 65536 - \frac{f_{osc}}{24 \cdot f_{out}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Anchor:** This is the single most-tested equation in Module 2 audio/timer questions. Memorize the 24 in the denominator — it comes from $12 \text{ (clocks/machine cycle)} \times 2 \text{ (half-period)} = 24$.

## 2.3 Method 2 — PWM-Based Audio Generation

A **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** signal with a fixed frequency but **variable duty cycle** can, when low-pass filtered, produce a varying DC voltage. By rapidly updating the duty cycle in a pattern matching a desired waveform (e.g., sine), the filtered output reconstructs the original audio.

### Duty Cycle to Equivalent Voltage

For a PWM signal with amplitude $V_{CC}$ and duty cycle $D$:

$$V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{CC}$$

Where:

$$D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{period}}$$

For sine wave generation via PWM, the instantaneous duty cycle follows:

$$D(t) = 0.5 + 0.5 \cdot \sin(2 \pi f_{out} t)$$

## 2.4 Method 3 — DAC-Based Direct Audio Output

A **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** accepts an N-bit digital word and outputs a proportional analog voltage. Common microcontrollers (e.g., ARM LPC2148) include built-in DACs. The output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = \frac{D_{in}}{2^{N}} \cdot V_{ref}$$

For sine wave generation, a **lookup table** stored in code memory is fed to the DAC at the required sampling rate $f_s$:

$$\text{Table}[i] = 127 + 127 \cdot \sin\left(\frac{2 \pi i}{M}\right), \quad i = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, M-1$$

Where `M` is the number of samples per cycle. By KTU convention, **M = 32 or M = 64** for a sine table (trade-off between resolution and memory).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory for KTU Board Exam** — these formulas are the **only ones** needed to solve any audio generation problem in Module 2.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Machine Cycle Period | $T_{mach} = 12 / f_{osc}$ | Seconds |
| 2 | Timer Reload Value | $TH = 65536 - \dfrac{f_{osc}}{24 \cdot f_{out}}$ | Integer (16-bit) |
| 3 | PWM Average Voltage | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{CC}$ | Volts |
| 4 | Duty Cycle | $D = T_{ON} / T_{period}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 5 | DAC Output | $V_{out} = (D_{in}/2^N) \cdot V_{ref}$ | Volts |
| 6 | Nyquist Sampling | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz |
| 7 | Sine Sample Value | $V[i] = V_{mid} + A \cdot \sin(2\pi i/M)$ | Digital code |
| 8 | 8051 SQW Frequency | $f_{out} = f_{osc} / (24 \cdot (65536 - \text{Reload}))$ | Hz |
| 9 | RC Low-Pass Cutoff | $f_c = 1 / (2 \pi RC)$ | Hz |
| 10 | Quantization SNR | $SNR \approx 6.02N + 1.76$ | dB (N = bits) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Audio signal generation from microcontrollers powers:
- **Medical devices:** Heart-rate monitor beep, ventilator alarms
- **Automotive:** Reverse parking sensors, turn-tick alerts
- **Consumer electronics:** Doorbells, alarm clocks, washing machine beeps
- **Telephony:** DTMF dialing (Dual-Tone Multi-Frequency)
- **Industrial:** Machine fault sirens, process control buzzers
- **Educational toys:** Musical keyboards, learning aids

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note for KTU Students:** DTMF (Dual-Tone Multi-Frequency Signaling) is a frequently-asked application question. It uses **two simultaneous sine tones** — one from a low group (697, 770, 852, 941 Hz) and one from a high group (1209, 1336, 1477, 1633 Hz) — to represent digits 0–9, *, #.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Timer Reload Value for a Desired Audio Frequency

> **Problem Statement (KTU Style):** An 8051 microcontroller uses an **11.0592 MHz crystal**. Write a program to generate a **square wave of 1 kHz** on pin P1.0 using Timer 0 in Mode 1. Calculate the value to be loaded into TH0.

### Step 1 — Determine the Machine Cycle Frequency

The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by 12 to get the machine cycle frequency:

$$f_{mach} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12}$$

$$f_{mach} = 921{,}600 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Machine Cycle Period

$$T_{mach} = \frac{1}{f_{mach}} = \frac{1}{921{,}600}$$

$$T_{mach} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.085 \text{ μs}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Required Square-Wave Period

For a target output frequency of 1 kHz:

$$T_{out} = \frac{1}{f_{out}} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Half-Period (Toggle Interval)

Since the pin must toggle once per half-period (to produce a full cycle in two toggles):

$$T_{half} = \frac{T_{out}}{2} = \frac{1 \text{ ms}}{2} = 500 \text{ μs} = 500 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Required Number of Timer Counts

$$N_{counts} = \frac{T_{half}}{T_{mach}} = \frac{500 \times 10^{-6}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$N_{counts} = 460.8 \approx 461 \text{ counts}$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Timer Reload Value

Since Timer 0 is in **16-bit Mode 1**, it counts up from the loaded value to **65536** ($2^{16}$) and overflows. The reload value is:

$$\text{Reload} = 65536 - N_{counts} = 65536 - 461$$

$$\text{Reload} = 65075$$

### Step 7 — Split into TH0 and TL0 Bytes

$$TH0 = \frac{\text{Reload}}{256} = \frac{65075}{256} = 254.20 \rightarrow \text{Floor} = 254 = \text{0xFE}$$

$$TL0 = \text{Reload} \mod 256 = 65075 - (254 \times 256) = 65075 - 65024 = 51 = \text{0x33}$$

### Step 8 — Verification

$$N_{counts} = 65536 - 65075 = 461$$

$$f_{out} = \frac{f_{osc}}{24 \cdot N_{counts}} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{24 \times 461}$$

$$f_{out} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{11064} = 999.65 \text{ Hz} \approx 1 \text{ kHz} \quad \checkmark$$

### Final 8051 Assembly Code (KTU Exam Answer)

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 000BH              ; Timer 0 ISR vector
        CPL P1.0               ; Toggle P1.0
        MOV TH0, #0FEH         ; Reload high byte
        MOV TL0, #033H         ; Reload low byte
        RETI
MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #01H         ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
        MOV TH0,  #0FEH
        MOV TL0,  #033H
        SETB TR0               ; Start Timer 0
        SETB ET0               ; Enable Timer 0 interrupt
        SETB EA                ; Enable global interrupt
HERE:   SJMP HERE              ; Idle loop
        END
```

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: PWM Low-Pass Filter Cutoff for Audio Reconstruction

> **Problem Statement:** A 8051 generates a PWM signal at **10 kHz switching frequency** with duty cycle modulated to follow a **500 Hz sine wave**. Design a first-order RC low-pass filter to recover the sine wave. The speaker impedance is 8 Ω.

### Step 1 — Identify the Spectral Components

The PWM signal has energy at:
- DC component (proportional to mean duty cycle)
- Fundamental of 500 Hz (the desired audio)
- Switching harmonics at 10 kHz, 20 kHz, 30 kHz, …

### Step 2 — Set the Filter Cutoff

A common design rule is to place the cutoff **one decade above the highest audio frequency** but **one decade below the switching frequency**:

$$f_{c} = \sqrt{f_{audio} \cdot f_{sw}} = \sqrt{500 \times 10000} = \sqrt{5 \times 10^6} \approx 2236 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 3 — Choose Capacitor and Compute Resistor

Choose $C = 0.1 \text{ μF}$ (standard value). Then:

$$R = \frac{1}{2 \pi f_c C} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 2236 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R = \frac{1}{1.4049 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 711.7 \text{ Ω}$$

Choose the nearest standard value: **R = 680 Ω**.

### Step 4 — Verify the Filter Response

The attenuation of the 10 kHz switching component:

$$|H(j\omega)| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (10000/2236)^2}}$$

$$|H| = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 20}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{21}} = 0.218$$

So the 10 kHz ripple is reduced to ~22% of its original amplitude — adequate for a buzzer application but not for hi-fi.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Generation Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** Students are often asked to compare these methods. Memorize this table.

| Parameter | GPIO Toggle (SQW) | PWM + Filter | DAC Direct |
|---|---|---|---|
| Audio Quality | Poor (square wave) | Fair (depends on filter) | Excellent (true analog) |
| Hardware Needed | Speaker only | RC filter + amplifier | DAC + amplifier |
| 8051 Feasibility | Easy (no extra HW) | Easy (timer-based PWM) | Needs external DAC |
| Frequency Range | 20 Hz – 100 kHz | 20 Hz – 4 kHz | DC – 20 kHz |
| Waveform Flexibility | Square only | Arbitrary (slow) | Arbitrary (any) |
| Power Efficiency | Low (lots of harmonics) | Medium | High |
| KTU Typical Use | Buzzer, melody, DTMF | Tone generation | Hi-fi music playback |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Audio Generation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Microntroller Core] --> B[Timer or PWM Module]
    B --> C[Digital Output Pin]
    C --> D[Reconstruction Filter]
    D --> E[Audio Amplifier]
    E --> F[Speaker or Buzzer]
    A --> G[Lookup Table in ROM]
    G --> B
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram: 8051 Timer Interrupt-Driven SQW Generation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Power On Reset]) --> Init[Initialize Timer 0 in Mode 1]
    Init --> LoadTH[Load TH0 and TL0 with computed reload value]
    LoadTH --> StartT[Set TR0 to start timer]
    StartT --> EnableIE[Enable ET0 and EA interrupts]
    EnableIE --> Loop[Main loop - Idle]
    Loop --> WaitTF{Timer Overflow?}
    WaitTF -- No --> Loop
    WaitTF -- Yes --> ISR[Enter ISR at 000BH]
    ISR --> Toggle[CPL P1.0 to toggle pin]
    Toggle --> ReloadT[Reload TH0 and TL0]
    ReloadT --> Return[RETI]
    Return --> Loop
    style Start fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ISR fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Toggle fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: PWM-to-Audio Reconstruction Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sine Sample Table in Code Memory] --> B[Update PWM Duty Cycle]
    B --> C[PWM Output Pin]
    C --> D[RC Low-Pass Filter]
    D --> E[Buffered Amplifier]
    E --> F[8 Ohm Speaker]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Architecture Flow: DTMF Tone Generation (Application-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    KeyPress[Keypad Key Press Detected] --> Decode[Decode Row and Column]
    Decode --> RowTone[Select Low Group Frequency]
    Decode --> ColTone[Select High Group Frequency]
    RowTone --> Mixer[Sum Two Tones Digitally]
    ColTone --> Mixer
    Mixer --> Timer1[Timer 1 generates Low Tone]
    Mixer --> Timer2[Timer 2 generates High Tone]
    Timer1 --> OutputPin[Output to Speaker]
    Timer2 --> OutputPin
    style KeyPress fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Mixer fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style OutputPin fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.5 Memory Map: Sine Lookup Table Layout (M = 32 Samples)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[ROM Address 0x0200] --> B[Sample 0: Sine 0 deg = 127]
    C[ROM Address 0x0201] --> D[Sample 1: Sine 11.25 deg = 152]
    E[ROM Address 0x0202] --> F[Sample 2: Sine 22.5 deg = 177]
    G[ROM Address 0x021F] --> H[Sample 31: Sine 348.75 deg = 102]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> The samples above are **8-bit unsigned values centered at 128** (for 0 to 3.3 V swing through a rail-to-rail amplifier). This is the standard KTU 2024 convention for sine table generation.
