# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Audio Signal Generation. Mention the audible frequency range.
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

Audio signal generation is the process of producing time-varying electrical waveforms whose frequency components lie in the audible band, using a microcontroller's timer, PWM, or DAC peripherals, and delivering them to a transducer (speaker or buzzer).

The audible frequency range for humans is **20 Hz to 20 kHz**. Signals below 20 Hz are called infrasonic and above 20 kHz are ultrasonic; both are inaudible.

A typical audio generation chain in a microcontroller system consists of:
- **Waveform source** (lookup table, algorithm, or timer)
- **Digital output stage** (GPIO, PWM, or DAC)
- **Reconstruction filter** (low-pass RC or active)
- **Amplifier and transducer** (speaker, 8 Ω typically)

---

### Question 2: What is DTMF? How is it generated using microcontrollers?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

**DTMF (Dual-Tone Multi-Frequency)** is a signaling standard used in telephony where each digit (0–9, *, #, A–D) is represented by **two simultaneous pure tones** — one from a low-frequency group and one from a high-frequency group.

| Group | Frequencies (Hz) |
|---|---|
| Low group | **697, 770, 852, 941** |
| High group | **1209, 1336, 1477, 1633** |

**Generation in a microcontroller:**
1. Two timers (e.g., Timer 0 and Timer 1 of 8051) are programmed to overflow at the rates of the two required frequencies.
2. The two timer outputs are combined (e.g., through a summing amplifier) to produce the composite DTMF signal.
3. The composite signal is amplified and fed to the earpiece or speaker.

For example, pressing the digit **'5'** generates **770 Hz + 1336 Hz** simultaneously.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** An 8051 system uses a **12 MHz crystal oscillator**. Design a program to generate a **2 kHz square wave** on pin P1.7 using Timer 1 in Mode 1. Compute the timer reload values with full derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat block diagram how a **PWM signal followed by a low-pass filter** can be used to generate a sine wave audio signal. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

**Step 1: Machine cycle frequency**

$$f_{mach} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12} = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{12} = 1 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 2: Machine cycle period**

$$T_{mach} = \frac{1}{f_{mach}} = 1 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 3: Required square wave period**

$$T_{out} = \frac{1}{2000} = 500 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 4: Half-period (toggle interval)**

$$T_{half} = 250 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 5: Required timer counts**

$$N_{counts} = \frac{250 \text{ μs}}{1 \text{ μs}} = 250$$

**Step 6: Timer reload value**

$$\text{Reload} = 65536 - 250 = 65286$$

**Step 7: Split into bytes**

$$TH1 = 65286 / 256 = 255 = \text{0xFF}$$

$$TL1 = 65286 - (255 \times 256) = 65286 - 65280 = 6 = \text{0x06}$$

**Step 8: Program**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 001BH              ; Timer 1 ISR vector
        CPL P1.7               ; Toggle P1.7
        MOV TH1, #0FFH
        MOV TL1, #006H
        RETI
MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #10H         ; Timer 1, Mode 1
        MOV TH1, #0FFH
        MOV TL1, #006H
        SETB TR1
        SETB ET1
        SETB EA
HERE:   SJMP HERE
        END
```

#### Model Solution (b)

A PWM-based audio generation system works on the principle that a **low-pass filter** converts a varying duty cycle PWM signal into a proportional analog voltage. By rapidly updating the duty cycle to follow a desired waveform (e.g., sine wave) and filtering, the audio signal is reconstructed.

**Block Diagram Description:**

1. **Microcontroller + Sine Lookup Table** — A precomputed table of M samples of one full sine cycle is stored in code memory. For example, for M = 32 samples of a sine wave: `sine_table[i] = 127 + 127 * sin(2πi/32)`.

2. **PWM Generator (Timer)** — A high-frequency timer (e.g., 20 kHz to 100 kHz) is used. The timer's compare register is updated at a rate $f_s$ (e.g., 8 kHz) with the next sample value from the sine table.

3. **PWM Output Pin** — The PWM output is a rectangular wave whose duty cycle is proportional to the desired instantaneous sine amplitude.

4. **Reconstruction Low-Pass Filter** — A first-order RC filter (e.g., R = 1 kΩ, C = 0.1 μF, $f_c \approx 1.6$ kHz) removes the high-frequency PWM carrier and passes the audio envelope.

5. **Audio Amplifier** — An op-amp based amplifier (e.g., LM386) boosts the filtered signal to drive a speaker.

**Mathematical Relation:** The instantaneous duty cycle follows:

$$D(t) = 0.5 + 0.5 \cdot \sin(2 \pi f_{out} t)$$

The filtered output voltage is:

$$V_{out}(t) = V_{CC} \cdot D(t) = \frac{V_{CC}}{2} \left[ 1 + \sin(2 \pi f_{out} t) \right]$$

The DC component $\frac{V_{CC}}{2}$ is blocked by a coupling capacitor, leaving the pure sine wave.

**Advantages:** No external DAC needed; works on any microcontroller with a timer.
**Disadvantages:** Requires filter hardware; limited by PWM resolution and switching frequency.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Direct Digital Synthesis (DDS) technique** for audio signal generation using a DAC. Mention the role of the lookup table and sampling theorem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 with **11.0592 MHz crystal** is used to generate a **musical note A4 (440 Hz)** on a buzzer using Timer 0. Calculate the timer reload value and write the 8051 assembly program. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (a)

**Direct Digital Synthesis (DDS)** is a technique for generating arbitrary analog waveforms (typically sinusoidal) from a single fixed-frequency clock by reading precomputed sample values from a lookup table and feeding them to a DAC at a precisely-timed rate.

**Block Diagram:**

```
Phase Accumulator → Lookup Table (ROM) → DAC → Low-Pass Filter → Output
        ↑                                                           
   Tuning Word (M)                                                   
```

**Working Principle:**

1. **Phase Accumulator** — A digital counter that adds a **tuning word M** to its current value on every clock cycle. The accumulator size is typically 32 bits (in commercial DDS chips) or smaller in microcontrollers.

2. **Lookup Table** — Stores one full cycle of the desired waveform (usually sine) at high resolution. For a sine wave with M_samples points:
   
   $$\text{Table}[i] = \frac{2^N - 1}{2} \cdot \left[ 1 + \sin\left(\frac{2 \pi i}{M_{samples}}\right) \right]$$

3. **DAC** — Converts the digital sample value to a corresponding analog voltage. The output of an N-bit DAC is:

   $$V_{out} = \frac{D_{in}}{2^N} \cdot V_{ref}$$

4. **Reconstruction Filter** — A low-pass filter with cutoff just above the maximum output frequency removes spectral images and smooths the staircase waveform.

5. **Output Frequency Equation:**

$$f_{out} = \frac{M \cdot f_{clk}}{2^n}$$

Where `n` is the accumulator width, `M` is the tuning word, and $f_{clk}$ is the clock frequency.

**Role of the Sampling Theorem (Nyquist):**

For faithful reproduction, the output frequency must satisfy:

$$f_{out} \leq \frac{f_{clk}}{2}$$

In practice, the low-pass filter cutoff is set at 40% of the clock frequency to allow a realistic roll-off.

**Applications:** Function generators, software-defined radio, musical synthesizers, communications.

#### Model Solution (b)

**Step 1: Machine cycle frequency**

$$f_{mach} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12} = 921{,}600 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2: Period of 440 Hz note**

$$T_{out} = \frac{1}{440} = 2.2727 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3: Half-period**

$$T_{half} = 1.1364 \text{ ms} = 1136.36 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 4: Required timer counts**

$$N_{counts} = \frac{1136.36 \text{ μs}}{1.085 \text{ μs}} \approx 1047.4 \rightarrow 1047$$

**Step 5: Timer reload value**

$$\text{Reload} = 65536 - 1047 = 64489$$

**Step 6: Split into bytes**

$$TH0 = 64489 / 256 = 251.91 \rightarrow 251 = \text{0xFB}$$

$$TL0 = 64489 - (251 \times 256) = 64489 - 64256 = 233 = \text{0xE9}$$

**Step 7: Program**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 000BH              ; Timer 0 ISR
        CPL P1.0               ; Toggle buzzer pin
        MOV TH0, #0FBH
        MOV TL0, #0E9H
        RETI
MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #01H         ; Timer 0, Mode 1
        MOV TH0, #0FBH
        MOV TL0, #0E9H
        SETB TR0
        SETB ET0
        SETB EA
HERE:   SJMP HERE
        END
```

**Verification:**

$$f_{out} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{24 \times 1047} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{25128} = 440.12 \text{ Hz} \quad \checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 
> **1. Confusing half-period and full-period:** A 1 kHz signal needs toggling at 500 Hz, so the timer overflow interval is **0.5 ms**, not 1 ms. Marks lost = 1–2.
> 
> **2. Using 12 instead of 24 in the denominator:** The denominator must be **24 × (65536 − Reload)**, not 12. The factor 12 is the clock divisor; the factor 2 is for half-period. Forgetting the factor 2 gives exactly half the required frequency.
> 
> **3. Not splitting TH and TL correctly:** Students often write only TH0. Always show the byte split.
> 
> **4. Wrong vector address for ISR:** Timer 0 is at 000BH, Timer 1 is at 001BH. Mixing these up is a frequent error.
> 
> **5. Forgetting to enable interrupts:** SETB EA and SETB ET0 (or ET1) are mandatory; otherwise the ISR never executes.
> 
> **6. Floating-point timer values:** If the reload value is not an integer, **truncate** (floor) it, not round. This gives a slightly lower actual frequency.
> 
> **7. Audio signal chain missing amplifier:** A bare microcontroller pin cannot drive an 8 Ω speaker — the current is insufficient. Always show an amplifier stage (e.g., LM386, TDA7052) before the speaker.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Audio Signal Generation**

- **Audible range:** 20 Hz to 20 kHz. Signals outside this are inaudible.
- **Three core methods:** GPIO toggle (timer), PWM + filter, DAC direct.
- **8051 Timer Mode 1 (16-bit):** Most commonly used for tone generation.
- **Key formula:** $f_{out} = f_{osc} / [24 \times (65536 - \text{Reload})]$.
- **DDS uses:** Phase accumulator + lookup table + DAC.
- **Nyquist theorem:** Sampling rate $f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max}$.
- **DTMF:** Two simultaneous tones — low group (697/770/852/941 Hz) + high group (1209/1336/1477/1633 Hz).
- **PWM low-pass filter cutoff:** Place $f_c$ between audio band and switching frequency (geometric mean is a good starting point).
- **Speaker driving:** Always use an amplifier — microcontroller pins deliver only a few mA.
- **ISR vector addresses:** Timer 0 = 000BH, Timer 1 = 001BH.
- **Crystal choice:** 11.0592 MHz is preferred because it generates exact baud rates for serial communication and clean timer intervals.
- **Quantization SNR:** $6.02N + 1.76$ dB for an N-bit system.
- **Sine lookup table:** Center at 128 (for 8-bit unsigned), use M = 32 or 64 samples.
- **Duty cycle to voltage:** $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_{CC}$.
- **DAC output:** $V_{out} = (D_{in}/2^N) \cdot V_{ref}$.

> **End of Module 2 — Audio Signal Generation Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupt Handling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Interrupt Handling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interrupt Handling** in a microcontroller is the **hardware- and software-driven mechanism** by which the CPU is **temporarily suspended** from its current sequential execution of the main program in response to an **asynchronous internal or external event**, transfers control to a dedicated **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, executes the event-specific response, and then **returns seamlessly** to the exact point in the main program where the suspension occurred.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for the **8051 Microcontroller** (the canonical device studied under PBCST504), interrupt handling involves a tightly choreographed interaction between:

- The **Interrupt Request (IRQ)** lines (Hardware pins like `INT0`, `INT1`).
- The **Interrupt Enable (IE)** Special Function Register (SFR at address `0xA8`).
- The **Interrupt Priority (IP)** SFR (at address `0xB8`).
- The **TCON** register (at address `0x88`, controlling external interrupt trigger types).
- The **Interrupt Vector Table** (fixed, hard-wired code memory addresses).
- The **Program Counter (PC)** and **Stack Pointer (SP)** for context preservation.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Doorbell in a Study Room" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a student (CPU) deeply engrossed in solving math problems (executing the main program) inside a closed study room.**

| Real-World Element | Microcontroller Equivalent |
|---|---|
| 📚 The student solving math | The CPU executing the main program |
| 🚪 The closed door | The CPU executing code uninterrupted |
| 🔔 A doorbell rings | An **Interrupt Request (IRQ)** signal arrives |
| 🛎️ Doorbell wired to a light | The **Interrupt Flag (TF0, IE0, etc.)** is set in `TCON` |
| 🪟 Student hears bell, leaves a bookmark | CPU completes the current instruction, **saves the PC** on the stack |
| 🚶 Walks to the door (door = ISR address) | CPU fetches the **ISR vector address** from the Vector Table |
| 🤝 Handles the visitor | Executes the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** |
| 🔙 Returns to the same math problem | Executes **`RETI`**, **restores PC**, resumes main program |

This analogy makes it instantly clear: the CPU **does not abandon** its work — it **pauses, services, and resumes** with surgical precision.

## 1.3 Why Interrupt Handling is a Syllabus Highlight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme places **Interrupt Handling** as a **high-weightage, high-Cognitive-Level** topic in Module 2 because it is the **backbone of real-time embedded systems**. Without interrupts, a microcontroller would waste thousands of CPU cycles in **busy-wait polling loops**, making it impossible to handle real-world events like sensor triggers, UART data arrival, or timer overflows in real time.

## 1.4 The 8051 Interrupt Source Map (Standard KTU Vocabulary)

The 8051 has **6 interrupt sources** (5 in original 8051, 6 in 8052). These are the official names the examiner expects:

1. **Reset** (Highest priority, non-maskable, vector `0x0000`)
2. **External Interrupt 0** — `INT0` (Pin P3.2, vector `0x0003`)
3. **Timer 0 Overflow** — `TF0` (vector `0x000B`)
4. **External Interrupt 1** — `INT1` (Pin P3.3, vector `0x0013`)
5. **Timer 1 Overflow** — `TF1` (vector `0x001B`)
6. **Serial Port (TX/RX)** — `TI/RI` (vector `0x0023`)

> Each vector address is **8 bytes apart** in code memory — only enough space for a small ISR. Longer ISRs require an **AJMP** or **LJMP** to the actual service code.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown: The 6-Step Interrupt Handling Sequence

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When an interrupt occurs and is **enabled** and **not masked by a higher-priority interrupt**, the 8051 hardware **automatically** performs the following sequence. This is a **guaranteed KTU exam question**.

### Step 1 — Interrupt Detection & Flag Setting
- The event sets the corresponding **interrupt flag bit** in the `TCON` or `SCON` register.
- Example: External interrupt on `INT0` sets the `IE0` bit in `TCON`.

### Step 2 — Enable Verification
- The CPU checks the corresponding **enable bit** in the **`IE` register** (address `0xA8`).
- If the bit is `0`, the interrupt is **masked** and the flag is left pending (ignored).

### Step 3 — Global Enable Check
- The **EA (Enable All) bit** in `IE` (bit 7) must be set to `1` for **any** interrupt to be recognized.
- This is the **master switch** for the entire interrupt system.

### Step 4 — Priority Arbitration
- The CPU consults the **`IP` register** (address `0xB8`) to determine if this interrupt has **high or low priority**.
- If two interrupts occur **simultaneously**, the higher-priority one is serviced first.
- The **default natural priority order** (high → low) is: **Reset > INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial**.

### Step 5 — Hardware-Automatic Context Save
- The CPU completes the **current instruction** (no mid-instruction interruption).
- It then **automatically pushes the 16-bit Program Counter (PC)** onto the **stack** (pointed to by `SP`).
- This is what enables the **`RETI`** instruction to return to the exact point later.

### Step 6 — Vector Fetch & ISR Execution
- The CPU loads the **vector address** corresponding to the interrupt into the PC.
- Execution continues from that vector address — this **IS** the ISR.
- The ISR ends with the special instruction **`RETI`** (Return from Interrupt), which:
  - **Pops the PC** from the stack → resumes main program.
  - **Signals the interrupt controller** that servicing is complete (re-enables lower-priority interrupts).

## 2.2 The `IE` Register (Interrupt Enable) — Full Bit Map

| Bit | Name | Function | KTU Mnemonic |
|-----|------|----------|--------------|
| 7 | `EA` | **Global Enable** — must be 1 for any interrupt | `EA = 1` |
| 6 | — | **Reserved** (undefined on original 8051) | — |
| 5 | — | **Reserved** | — |
| 4 | `ES` | **Enable Serial Port** interrupt | `ES = 1` |
| 3 | `ET1` | **Enable Timer 1** overflow interrupt | `ET1 = 1` |
| 2 | `EX1` | **Enable External Interrupt 1** | `EX1 = 1` |
| 1 | `ET0` | **Enable Timer 0** overflow interrupt | `ET0 = 1` |
| 0 | `EX0` | **Enable External Interrupt 0** | `EX0 = 1` |

> **KTU Examiner Tip:** To enable **only** the Timer 0 interrupt, the value loaded into `IE` is:  
> `IE = 0x82` → Binary `1000 0010` → `EA=1, ET0=1`, all others 0.

## 2.3 The `IP` Register (Interrupt Priority) — Full Bit Map

| Bit | Name | Function |
|-----|------|----------|
| 7–5 | — | Reserved |
| 4 | `PS` | Serial Port priority (1 = high) |
| 3 | `PT1` | Timer 1 priority (1 = high) |
| 2 | `PX1` | External Int 1 priority (1 = high) |
| 1 | `PT0` | Timer 0 priority (1 = high) |
| 0 | `PX0` | External Int 0 priority (1 = high) |

> A "high priority" interrupt **can preempt** a low-priority ISR. Two "high priority" interrupts follow the **natural priority order**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **essential numerical relationships and timing values** for interrupt handling in the 8051. These are **directly testable** in KTU exams.

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit | Engineering Utility |
|-----------|-----------------|------|---------------------|
| **Interrupt Vector Spacing** | $\Delta_{\text{vector}} = 8$ | bytes | Distance between consecutive ISR vector addresses in code memory |
| **Vector Address** | $V_n = 0x0003 + 8(n-1)$ for $n=1\ldots 6$ | hex | Direct lookup of ISR entry point |
| **PC Save Size** | $L_{\text{PC}} = 16$ | bits | Length of Program Counter pushed on stack |
| **Stack Growth** | $SP \leftarrow SP - 2$ | bytes | Stack pointer behavior on interrupt entry |
| **RETI Pop Size** | $SP \leftarrow SP + 2$ | bytes | Stack pointer behavior on interrupt exit |
| **Interrupt Latency** | $t_{\text{latency}} = T_{\text{inst}} \times C_{\text{cycle}}$ | seconds | Time from IRQ to first ISR instruction |
| **Min Latency (8051)** | $t_{\text{latency,min}} = 3 \text{ to } 8$ | machine cycles | Includes instruction completion + stack push |
| **Max ISR Frequency (8-byte slot)** | — | — | Slot only holds ~3–4 short instructions; use LJMP |
| **EA = 1 Condition** | $EA = 1 \;\wedge\; E_{\text{source}} = 1$ | logical AND | Both must be true for interrupt acceptance |
| **Edge/Level Trigger** | $\text{IT0, IT1} \in \{0,1\}$ | bit | `0` = level-triggered, `1` = falling-edge-triggered |

## 2.5 Edge-Triggered vs. Level-Triggered: A Critical Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> This is a **favourite KTU Part A question**. Many students confuse the two.

| Mode | Control Bit | Behavior | When to Use |
|------|-------------|----------|-------------|
| **Level-Triggered** | `IT0 = 0` (or `IT1 = 0`) | Interrupt is generated as long as the pin is held **LOW** | When the external device holds the line low until serviced |
| **Edge-Triggered** | `IT0 = 1` (or `IT1 = 1`) | Interrupt is generated on the **falling edge** (HIGH→LOW transition) | For pulse-type events (e.g., a button press) |

> **Hardware Auto-Clear:** In edge-triggered mode, the `IE0`/`IE1` flag is **automatically cleared** when the ISR is vectored. In level-triggered mode, the flag is **not** auto-cleared — the ISR must clear the source.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs:** Interrupt-driven crank-shaft angle sensors.
- **Medical Devices:** ECG monitors using timer interrupts for sampling.
- **Industrial PLCs:** External interrupts for emergency-stop buttons.
- **IoT Devices:** UART receive interrupts for asynchronous wireless module data.
- **Robotics:** Encoder pulse counting via external interrupts for precise motor control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Interrupt Vector Address Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 8051 interrupt vector table is **hard-wired** in ROM. The KTU examiner often asks students to **derive** a vector address or explain the **8-byte spacing**.

### Given:
- The 8051 reset vector address: $V_{\text{reset}} = \text{0x0000}$
- The vector address of the **$n$-th** interrupt source in the standard 8051 table.
- Each vector slot is **8 bytes** wide.

### Derivation:

$$
V_n = V_{\text{reset}} + (8 \text{ bytes}) \times (n - 1)
$$

where $n = 1$ corresponds to External Interrupt 0.

### Step-by-step evaluation for all 6 sources:

$$
V_1 = 0x0000 + 8 \times 0 = \text{0x0003} \quad (\text{External Int 0})
$$

$$
V_2 = 0x0000 + 8 \times 1 = \text{0x000B} \quad (\text{Timer 0 Overflow})
$$

$$
V_3 = 0x0000 + 8 \times 2 = \text{0x0013} \quad (\text{External Int 1})
$$

$$
V_4 = 0x0000 + 8 \times 3 = \text{0x001B} \quad (\text{Timer 1 Overflow})
$$

$$
V_5 = 0x0000 + 8 \times 4 = \text{0x0023} \quad (\text{Serial Port})
$$

> **Textual note:** The Reset vector is the very first address `0x0000` (4 bytes for LJMP), and each subsequent vector is offset by exactly **8 bytes**, because the 8051 instruction set needs at least 8 bytes to safely contain a 3-byte `LJMP` to a full-sized ISR located elsewhere in code memory.

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Interrupt Latency

The **minimum interrupt latency** $t_{\text{latency,min}}$ is the time elapsed between the **physical arrival of the IRQ signal** and the **execution of the first instruction inside the ISR**.

### Given:
- 8051 oscillator frequency: $f_{\text{osc}} = 12 \text{ MHz}$
- One machine cycle: $T_{\text{mc}} = 12 / f_{\text{osc}}$
- Worst-case remaining cycles of the current instruction: $C_{\text{max}} = 4$ (e.g., for `MUL AB` or `DIV AB`)
- Cycles for hardware context save (PC push): $C_{\text{push}} = 2$
- Cycles for vector fetch: $C_{\text{vector}} = 2$

### Derivation:

$$
T_{\text{mc}} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \, \mu s
$$

$$
t_{\text{latency,max}} = T_{\text{mc}} \times (C_{\text{max}} + C_{\text{push}} + C_{\text{vector}})
$$

$$
t_{\text{latency,max}} = 1 \, \mu s \times (4 + 2 + 2) = 8 \, \mu s
$$

> **Textual note:** This **8 µs upper bound** is why the 8051 is unsuitable for sub-microsecond real-time control — but it is **fast enough** for almost all human-interface and industrial control applications.

## 3.3 Derivation: IE Register Value for a Specific Combination

**Problem:** Configure the 8051 to enable **only** External Interrupt 0 (`INT0`) and the **Serial Port** interrupt, with all other sources disabled. Compute the value to be written into the `IE` register.

### Solution:

| Bit Position | Bit 7 (EA) | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 (ES) | Bit 3 (ET1) | Bit 2 (EX1) | Bit 1 (ET0) | Bit 0 (EX0) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Value | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |

Converting the 8-bit binary `10010001` to hexadecimal:

$$
\text{IE} = (1 \times 2^7) + (0 \times 2^6) + (0 \times 2^5) + (1 \times 2^4) + (0 \times 2^3) + (0 \times 2^2) + (0 \times 2^1) + (1 \times 2^0)
$$

$$
\text{IE} = 128 + 16 + 1 = 145_{10}
$$

$$
\text{IE} = \text{0x91}
$$

> **Textual note:** The C-style instruction is `IE = 0x91;` — a routine KTU Part A problem.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Trigger Methods

| Criterion | Level-Triggered (`ITx = 0`) | Edge-Triggered (`ITx = 1`) |
|-----------|----------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Activation** | Pin held LOW | Falling edge (HIGH→LOW) |
| **Flag IE0/IE1** | NOT auto-cleared by hardware | Auto-cleared on ISR vector fetch |
| **ISR re-entry risk** | Possible if pin stays LOW | None — pulse is latched |
| **Typical use** | Wired-OR shared interrupt lines | Single discrete event (button, sensor pulse) |
| **Software overhead** | Higher (must clear source) | Lower (hardware clears) |
| **Power noise immunity** | Lower | Higher |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Polling vs. Interrupt

| Parameter | Polling | Interrupt |
|-----------|---------|-----------|
| **CPU Utilization** | Low (busy-wait) | High (runs main code) |
| **Response Time** | Variable, depends on loop | Bounded (latency ≤ 8 µs on 8051/12 MHz) |
| **Code Complexity** | Simple | Moderate (ISR + vector setup) |
| **Suitability for Multi-Event** | Poor | Excellent |
| **Real-Time Guarantee** | Weak | Strong |
| **Power Consumption** | Higher | Lower (CPU can idle or sleep) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🖼️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Complete 8051 Interrupt Handling Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Program Execution] --> B{IRQ Signal Arrives?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C[Set Interrupt Flag in TCON or SCON]
    C --> D{Source Enable Bit = 1?}
    D -- No --> E[Ignore - Flag Remains Pending]
    E --> A
    D -- Yes --> F{Global EA = 1?}
    F -- No --> G[Masked - CPU Ignores]
    G --> A
    F -- Yes --> H{Priority Check vs IP Register}
    H --> I[Complete Current Instruction]
    I --> J[Push 16-bit PC onto Stack SP minus 2]
    J --> K[Load Vector Address into PC]
    K --> L[Execute ISR from Vector Table]
    L --> M{ISR Code Complete?}
    M -- No --> L
    M -- Yes --> N[Execute RETI Instruction]
    N --> O[Pop PC from Stack SP plus 2]
    O --> P[Re-enable Lower Priority Interrupts]
    P --> A
```

> **Textual note:** The diagram shows the **closed-loop resumption** — the CPU returns to the main program at the exact instruction after the one that was interrupted, thanks to the PC being saved on the stack.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Interrupt Source Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ExternalWorld[External Hardware Sources]
        EXT0[INT0 Pin P3.2]
        EXT1[INT1 Pin P3.3]
    end
    subgraph InternalSources[Internal Peripheral Sources]
        T0[Timer 0 Overflow TF0]
        T1[Timer 1 Overflow TF1]
        UART[Serial Port TI or RI]
    end
    subgraph ControlRegisters[8051 Control Logic]
        TCON[TCON Register - Flags and Trigger Type]
        SCON[SCON Register - Serial Flags]
        IEreg[IE Register - Enable Mask]
        IPlog[IP Register - Priority]
        EA[EA Global Bit]
    end
    subgraph VectorTable[Code Memory Vector Table]
        V0[0x0000 Reset]
        V1[0x0003 INT0 ISR]
        V2[0x000B T0 ISR]
        V3[0x0013 INT1 ISR]
        V4[0x001B T1 ISR]
        V5[0x0023 Serial ISR]
    end
    EXT0 --> TCON
    EXT1 --> TCON
    T0 --> TCON
    T1 --> TCON
    UART --> SCON
    TCON --> IEreg
    SCON --> IEreg
    EA --> IEreg
    IEreg --> IPlog
    IPlog --> V0
    IPlog --> V1
    IPlog --> V2
    IPlog --> V3
    IPlog --> V4
    IPlog --> V5
```

> **Textual note:** This block diagram shows that **all six sources** converge through the **IE → IP → Vector Table** pipeline before being serviced. This is the canonical architecture students are expected to draw in the exam.

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Timer 0 Interrupt Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as 8051 CPU
    participant Timer as Timer 0 Hardware
    participant TCON as TCON Register
    participant IE as IE Register
    participant Stack as Stack Memory
    participant ISR as Timer 0 ISR Code
    CPU->>Timer: Start Timer 0 in Mode 1
    loop Counting Up
        Timer->>Timer: Increment TH0 TL0
    end
    Timer->>TCON: Overflow Sets TF0 Flag
    TCON->>IE: Request Interrupt ET0 and EA
    IE-->>CPU: Interrupt Accepted
    CPU->>CPU: Complete Current Instruction
    CPU->>Stack: Push PC onto Stack
    CPU->>ISR: Jump to Vector 0x000B
    ISR->>TCON: Clear TF0 Flag Manually
    ISR->>Timer: Reload TH0 TL0 for Next Cycle
    ISR->>CPU: Execute RETI
    CPU->>Stack: Pop PC from Stack
    CPU->>CPU: Resume Main Program
```

> **Textual note:** Note that unlike external interrupts, **timer and serial interrupts require the ISR to manually clear the flag** (`TF0 = 0`). Forgetting this is a classic KTU exam pitfall (see Section 5 warning).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** 
**Define an Interrupt. List the interrupts available in the 8051 Microcontroller with their vector addresses.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** An interrupt is an **external or internal event** that temporarily halts the main program execution, causing the CPU to execute a special function called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. After the ISR completes (via the `RETI` instruction), the CPU resumes the main program from the point of interruption.

**List of 8051 Interrupts with Vector Addresses (2 marks):**

| # | Interrupt Source | Flag | Vector Address | Type |
|---|------------------|------|---------------|------|
| 1 | Reset | — | `0x0000` | Non-maskable, Highest Priority |
| 2 | External Interrupt 0 (`INT0`) | `IE0` | `0x0003` | Maskable, External |
| 3 | Timer 0 Overflow | `TF0` | `0x000B` | Maskable, Internal |
| 4 | External Interrupt 1 (`INT1`) | `IE1` | `0x0013` | Maskable, External |
| 5 | Timer 1 Overflow | `TF1` | `0x001B` | Maskable, Internal |
| 6 | Serial Port (TI/RI) | `TI` / `RI` | `0x0023` | Maskable, Internal |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** 
**Differentiate between edge-triggered and level-triggered interrupts in the 8051.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Level-Triggered (`ITx = 0`) | Edge-Triggered (`ITx = 1`) |
|---------|----------------------------|-----------------------------|
| **Activation Condition** | Pin held LOW | Falling edge (HIGH → LOW transition) |
| **Flag Cleared By** | **Software** (ISR must clear the source) | **Hardware automatically** on ISR entry |
| **Re-trigger** | Continuous if pin stays LOW | Only on next falling edge |
| **Typical Use** | Wired-OR shared interrupt lines | Single discrete events (buttons, pulses) |

> **Control Bits:** `IT0` (TCON.0) and `IT1` (TCON.2) select the trigger type for `INT0` and `INT1` respectively.

---

## 📗 PART B — ESE Module Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `RETI` instruction** — Students often end the ISR with `RET`. This will NOT correctly restore the interrupt enable logic, and the system will hang or behave erratically. Always use `RETI`.
> 2. **Forgetting to clear timer/serial flags** — `TF0`, `TF1`, `TI`, and `RI` are **NOT** auto-cleared by the 8051 hardware (unlike `IE0`/`IE1` in edge mode). The ISR MUST clear them, or the interrupt will retrigger infinitely.
> 3. **Not setting the global `EA` bit** — Even if individual sources are enabled, the CPU will ignore all interrupts if `EA = 0`.
> 4. **Confusing 8-byte vector spacing** — Beginners often think the entire ISR must fit in 8 bytes. The correct method is to place an `LJMP` at the vector address to the real ISR.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** 
**Explain the complete interrupt handling sequence in the 8051 microcontroller. Describe the role of the `IE` and `IP` registers with their bit formats.**

**Model Answer:**

**Interrupt Handling Sequence (4 Marks):**

When an interrupt request (IRQ) is generated:

1. **The interrupt flag is set** in `TCON` (for INT0/INT1/T0/T1) or `SCON` (for serial).
2. **The CPU checks the corresponding enable bit** in the `IE` register — if disabled, the interrupt is ignored.
3. **The global `EA` bit** in `IE` is checked — if `EA = 0`, **all** interrupts are masked.
4. **Priority arbitration** is performed using the `IP` register. Simultaneous interrupts are resolved using either IP-defined priority or the **natural priority order** (Reset > INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial).
5. **The CPU completes the current instruction** (no mid-instruction interruption).
6. **The 16-bit PC is automatically pushed onto the stack** (pointed to by `SP`). This is the **hardware context save**.
7. **The vector address** corresponding to the interrupt is loaded into the PC.
8. **The ISR executes** from the vector address.
9. The ISR ends with the **`RETI`** instruction, which **pops the PC from the stack** and signals the interrupt controller that service is complete.
10. **Main program execution resumes** at the exact instruction following the interrupted one.

**`IE` Register Bit Format (1.5 Marks):**

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | `EA` | — | — | `ES` | `ET1` | `EX1` | `ET0` | `EX0` |
| Function | Global Enable | Reserved | Reserved | Serial Enable | Timer 1 Enable | Ext Int 1 Enable | Timer 0 Enable | Ext Int 0 Enable |

- `EA = 1` → master switch.
- Each `Ex` bit = 1 → that source is unmasked.

**`IP` Register Bit Format (1.5 Marks):**

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|-----|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Name | — | — | — | `PS` | `PT1` | `PX1` | `PT0` | `PX0` |
| Function | Reserved | Reserved | Reserved | Serial Pri | Timer 1 Pri | Ext Int 1 Pri | Timer 0 Pri | Ext Int 0 Pri |

- A `1` in any bit → that source is **high priority** and can preempt low-priority ISRs.
- Two high-priority sources → tie broken by **natural priority order**.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** 
**Write an 8051 C program to generate a square wave of 1 kHz on pin P1.0 using Timer 0 interrupts. Assume a crystal frequency of 11.0592 MHz. Show all SFR configurations.**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Required Timer Delay Calculation (1 Mark):**

Square wave frequency = 1 kHz → Period = 1 ms → Half-period (toggle time) = **0.5 ms = 500 µs**.

**Step 2 — Machine Cycle Calculation (1 Mark):**

$$
T_{\text{mc}} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Required Timer Count (1 Mark):**

$$
N = \frac{500 \, \mu s}{1.085 \, \mu s} \approx 461 \text{ counts}
$$

$$
\text{Timer Initial Value} = 65536 - 461 = 65075 = \text{0xFE33}
$$

So: `TH0 = 0xFE`, `TL0 = 0x33`.

**Step 4 — Complete Program (4 Marks):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit WAVE = P1^0;   // Output pin for square wave

void timer0_isr() interrupt 1   // Interrupt vector 1 = Timer 0
{
    TH0 = 0xFE;       // Reload high byte
    TL0 = 0x33;       // Reload low byte
    WAVE = ~WAVE;     // Toggle P1.0 to generate square wave
}

void main(void)
{
    TMOD = 0x01;      // Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit timer)
    TH0  = 0xFE;      // Initial high byte
    TL0  = 0x33;      // Initial low byte
    ET0  = 1;         // Enable Timer 0 interrupt
    EA   = 1;         // Enable global interrupts
    TR0  = 1;         // Start Timer 0
    
    while(1);         // Main program does nothing - idle loop
}
```

> **Explanation of key lines:**
> - `interrupt 1` → links this function to vector `0x000B` (Timer 0).
> - `TMOD = 0x01` → Mode 1 = 16-bit timer with software-controlled reload.
> - `ET0 = 1; EA = 1;` → Two-step interrupt enable.
> - `TR0 = 1` → Starts the timer hardware.
> - The `while(1)` loop keeps the CPU busy, but interrupts preempt it every 500 µs to toggle the pin.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** 
**Explain the concept of interrupt priority in the 8051. How is it implemented using the `IP` register? What is the default natural priority order?**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of Interrupt Priority (2 Marks):**

When **two or more interrupt requests arrive at the same time**, or when an interrupt occurs **while another ISR is executing**, the 8051 must decide which one to service first. This decision is governed by the **Interrupt Priority** mechanism, which is a **two-level** (high / low) scheme implemented via the `IP` register.

**Implementation Using `IP` Register (3 Marks):**

The `IP` register (address `0xB8`) contains 5 user-accessible bits, each corresponding to one maskable interrupt source:

- A bit set to **`1`** → that interrupt is **high priority**.
- A bit set to **`0`** → that interrupt is **low priority**.

**Priority Arbitration Rules:**

1. A **high-priority** interrupt can **interrupt (preempt)** a low-priority ISR.
2. A **low-priority** interrupt **cannot** preempt a high-priority ISR.
3. If two interrupts of the **same priority** occur simultaneously, the **natural priority order** is used as a tie-breaker.
4. **Reset** has the highest natural priority and is **non-maskable** — it preempts everything.

**Default Natural Priority Order (2 Marks)** — From highest to lowest:

$$
\text{Reset} \; > \; \text{INT0} \; > \; \text{Timer 0} \; > \; \text{INT1} \; > \; \text{Timer 1} \; > \; \text{Serial Port}
$$

> **Example:** If `IP = 0x04` (binary `00000100`, so `PX1 = 1`), External Interrupt 1 is bumped to high priority. Now it can preempt Timer 0 even if Timer 0's ISR is running.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** 
**Design an 8051-based system where an external interrupt (`INT0`) from a push-button is used to count pulses and display the count on Port P2. Write the C program and explain the configuration of `TCON` and `IE` registers for falling-edge triggering.**

**Model Answer:**

**Design Overview (1 Mark):**
- The push-button is connected to **`INT0` (P3.2)**.
- Each **press of the button** generates a falling edge → triggers `INT0`.
- The ISR increments a global counter.
- The main program **continuously** displays the counter value on **Port P2** (8 LEDs).

**`TCON` Configuration for Falling-Edge Trigger (2 Marks):**

The `TCON` register (address `0x88`) controls the external interrupt trigger type:

| Bit | Name | Required Value | Reason |
|-----|------|----------------|--------|
| `TCON.0` (`IT0`) | `IT0` | `1` | Falling-edge triggered |
| `TCON.1` (`IE0`) | `IE0` | Auto-cleared | Hardware clears the flag on ISR entry |

So the relevant bit setup is: `IT0 = 1` → set `TCON = TCON \;\vert\; 0x01`.

**`IE` Configuration (2 Marks):**

To enable only External Interrupt 0:

$$
\text{IE} = 0x81 \quad (\text{binary } 10000001)
$$

This sets `EA = 1` (global) and `EX0 = 1` (External Int 0 enable); all other sources remain masked.

**Complete C Program (2 Marks):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

unsigned char count = 0;   // Global pulse counter (8-bit)

void ext_int0_isr() interrupt 0   // Vector 0 = INT0
{
    count++;              // Increment count on each button press
}

void main(void)
{
    IT0 = 1;              // Configure INT0 for falling-edge trigger
    EX0 = 1;              // Enable External Interrupt 0
    EA  = 1;              // Enable global interrupts
    
    P2 = 0x00;            // Initialize Port 2 to 0
    
    while(1)
    {
        P2 = count;       // Continuously display count on Port 2
    }
}
```

> **Explanation of key lines:**
> - `interrupt 0` → links function to vector `0x0003` (External Int 0).
> - `IT0 = 1` → falling-edge detection on `INT0`.
> - `EX0 = 1; EA = 1;` → two-step enable.
> - The `count` variable is declared `unsigned char` (8-bit) → displays 0 to 255 on P2.
> - In edge-triggered mode, the `IE0` flag is **hardware-cleared** on ISR entry, so no manual flag clearing is needed (unlike timer/serial interrupts).

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this high-density summary for **last-minute revision** before the KTU exam.

### ⚡ Key Concepts (One-Liners)

- **Interrupt** = asynchronous event that preempts the CPU, executes an ISR, and resumes the main program.
- **ISR** = Interrupt Service Routine — the dedicated function executed in response to an interrupt.
- **RETI** = the only correct way to exit an ISR (NOT `RET`).
- **EA** = global master enable — must be `1` for any interrupt to fire.
- **Vector Address** = hard-wired ROM address where the ISR for each interrupt begins.
- **Polling** = CPU keeps checking a flag in a loop (wasteful); **Interrupt** = hardware notifies CPU (efficient).

### ⚡ Critical Numbers to Memorize

- 8051 has **6 interrupt sources** (5 maskable + Reset).
- Vector address spacing = **8 bytes**.
- Vector table starts at `0x0003` (Reset at `0x0000`).
- 8-bit `IE` register at address **`0xA8`**.
- 8-bit `IP` register at address **`0xB8`**.
- 8-bit `TCON` register at address **`0x88`**.

### ⚡ High-Frequency Exam Triggers

1. **Calculate IE register value** for a given combination of enabled interrupts.
2. **Identify the vector address** for a given interrupt source.
3. **Edge vs. Level** triggering — know the control bit (`IT0` / `IT1`) and auto-clear behavior.
4. **Natural priority order** — memorize: Reset > INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial.
5. **Write an 8051 C ISR** using `interrupt <vector_number>` keyword.
6. **Explain why `RETI` differs from `RET`** — `RETI` re-enables the interrupt controller.
7. **Calculate timer reload value** for a given square wave frequency using timer interrupts.

### ⚡ Common Student Mistakes

- ❌ Ending ISR with `RET` instead of `RETI`.
- ❌ Forgetting to clear `TF0` / `TF1` / `TI` / `RI` flags inside the ISR.
- ❌ Setting `EX0 = 1` but leaving `EA = 0` — interrupts never fire.
- ❌ Believing the entire ISR must fit in 8 bytes (it doesn't — use `LJMP`).
- ❌ Confusing the IP register bits (memorize the 5 bits: `PX0`, `PT0`, `PX1`, `PT1`, `PS`).

### ⚡ Golden Formula

$$
\text{Vector Address of Source } n \;=\; 0x0003 + 8 \times (n - 1)
$$

> **Last-line of defence:** If you remember **only one thing** about interrupt handling, remember that the **PC is saved on the stack** and **restored by `RETI`** — this single fact explains 80% of interrupt behavior.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timer and Counter Applications: Basic Timer Configuration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Timer and Counter Applications: Basic Timer Configuration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** A **Timer** in the 8051 microcontroller is a dedicated on-chip hardware peripheral that increments an internal register on every **machine cycle** (oscillator period ÷ 12) or on every external pulse transition, depending on its configuration. The **Counter** is the same hardware block used in "Counter Mode," where the register is incremented by **external events** applied to dedicated pins (T0 for Timer 0, T1 for Timer 1) instead of the internal oscillator.

The 8051 contains **two 16-bit Timer/Counter registers** — **Timer 0** and **Timer 1** — each composed of two 8-bit Special Function Registers (SFRs):

| Timer | High Byte | Low Byte | External Pin | Control Bit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Timer 0** | `TH0` | `TL0` | **P3.4 (T0)** | `TR0` |
| **Timer 1** | `TH1` | `TL1` | **P3.5 (T1)** | `TR1` |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Bucket and Tap" Analogy (Plain-English Intuition):**
> Imagine a **bucket** (the timer register `THx:TLx`) of capacity **65,536 drops** (for Mode 1, since it is 16-bit: $2^{16} = 65{,}536$). A **tap** drips water into the bucket at a **fixed rate** — one drop every **machine cycle (1.085 µs** for an **11.0592 MHz crystal**). When the bucket **overflows** (passes 65,536 and rolls back to 0), an **overflow flag** (`TF0` or `TF1`) is raised — like a "buzzer" that tells the CPU: *"Hey, the time interval you asked for is over!"*
>
> If you want a **shorter delay**, you **pre-fill** the bucket so it has to receive fewer drops before overflowing. This pre-filled value is called the **Initial (Reload) Value**.

- **Timer Mode** = internal tap dripping at a known, fixed rate (suitable for **time delays**).
- **Counter Mode** = counting external pulses (e.g., **rotations of a wheel**, products on a conveyor belt) on pin T0/T1.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Highlighted

> [!NOTE]
> - **Machine Cycle Frequency:** $f_{MC} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12} = \dfrac{11.0592 \text{ MHz}}{12} = 921.6 \text{ kHz}$.
> - **Machine Cycle Period (one tick):** $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}} = 1.085\,\mu s$.
> - **Maximum Count (16-bit):** $2^{16} = \mathbf{65{,}536}$ states (0 to 65,535).
> - **Maximum Delay (Mode 1, no prescaler):** $65{,}536 \times 1.085\,\mu s \approx \mathbf{71.1 \text{ ms}}$.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture: The Two Key SFRs That Govern Timer Operation

#### 2.1.1 The `TMOD` Register (Timer MODE Register) — Address **89H**

`TMOD` is **not bit-addressable**; it is configured by writing the whole byte. Its upper nibble controls **Timer 1**, and its lower nibble controls **Timer 0**.

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Symbol** | **GATE** | **C/T̅** | **M1** | **M0** | **GATE** | **C/T̅** | **M1** | **M0** |
| **Controls** | Timer 1 | Timer 1 | Timer 1 | Timer 1 | Timer 0 | Timer 0 | Timer 0 | Timer 0 |

**Bit-by-Bit Meaning:**

- **GATE (1 bit per timer):** When `GATE = 1`, the timer is enabled **only when both** the `TRx` software bit **AND** the external `INTx` pin are HIGH (hardware-software AND). When `GATE = 0`, the timer is enabled by `TRx` alone (software control only).
- **C/T̅ (Counter/Timer Select):**
  - `C/T̅ = 0` → **Timer Mode** (counts internal machine cycles).
  - `C/T̅ = 1` → **Counter Mode** (counts external pulses on T0/T1 pin).
- **M1, M0 (Mode Select Bits):** These select one of four operating modes (see Section 2.2).

#### 2.1.2 The `TCON` Register (Timer CONtrol Register) — Address **88H**

`TCON` **IS bit-addressable** (unlike `TMOD`). It contains both timer-control bits and external interrupt-edge flags.

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Symbol** | **TF1** | **TR1** | **TF0** | **TR0** | **IE1** | **IT1** | **IE0** | **IT0** |

- **TR0 / TR1 (Timer Run Control Bits):** Software START/STOP switches. Set to `1` to RUN, `0` to HALT.
- **TF0 / TF1 (Timer Overflow Flags):** Set **automatically by hardware** when the timer rolls over. **Must be cleared by software** before the next run.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the distinction matters (KTU Board Favourite):** Examiners often ask — *"To start Timer 0 in software-controlled mode, which bits of which registers must be set?"* The exact answer is: configure `TMOD` (mode, C/T̅, GATE) and then set `TR0 = 1` in `TCON`.

---

### 2.2 The Four Operating Modes of the Timer

| M1 | M0 | Mode | Description | Width | Max Count | Auto-Reload? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | **Mode 0** | 13-bit Timer (8048 compatibility) | 13-bit | $2^{13} = 8192$ | ❌ No |
| 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | 16-bit Timer (Most commonly used) | 16-bit | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | ❌ No |
| 1 | 0 | **Mode 2** | 8-bit Auto-Reload Timer | 8-bit | $2^8 = 256$ | ✅ **Yes** (`THx → TLx`) |
| 1 | 1 | **Mode 3** | Split Timer (Timer 0 only) | 8-bit + 8-bit | Special | ❌ No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Mode Comparison:**
> - **Mode 1** is used when a **precise, one-shot delay** is needed; the programmer must **manually reload** `THx:TLx` after each overflow.
> - **Mode 2** is used when a **continuous, jitter-free periodic delay** is needed (e.g., generating a **baud rate for Serial Communication**, which KTU Module 3 covers). The value in `THx` is **auto-copied** to `TLx` on overflow.
> - **Mode 3** "freezes" Timer 1 (it acts as if `TR1 = 0`) and splits Timer 0 into two independent 8-bit timers (`TL0` controlled by `TR0`, `TH0` controlled by `TR1`).

---

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic: How to Configure a Timer (KTU Lab & Exam Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> The standard 8051 "Timer Configuration Recipe" (memorize this — it is the most frequently asked KTU 14-mark problem structure):

1. **Load `TMOD`** with the mode word (M1, M0, C/T̅, GATE for both timers).
2. **Load initial values** into `THx` (high byte) and `TLx` (low byte) — derived from the desired delay.
3. **Start the timer** by setting the run bit (`TR0 = 1` for Timer 0, or `TR1 = 1` for Timer 1) in `TCON`.
4. **Wait for the overflow flag** (`TF0 = 1` for Timer 0) — polled in a loop, OR use an **interrupt**.
5. **Stop the timer** (`TRx = 0`).
6. **Clear the overflow flag** (`TFx = 0`).
7. **Reload** the initial value (mandatory in Mode 1; automatic in Mode 2).

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Machine Cycle Period | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | µs | For 11.0592 MHz → **1.085 µs** |
| 2 | Required Count Value | $N = \dfrac{T_{desired}}{T_{MC}}$ | dimensionless | Total ticks needed |
| 3 | **Initial Value (16-bit, Mode 1)** | $IV = 2^{16} - N = 65{,}536 - N$ | decimal / hex | Convert to `THx = IV/256`, `TLx = IV mod 256` |
| 4 | **Initial Value (8-bit, Mode 2)** | $IV = 2^8 - N = 256 - N$ | decimal / hex | Loaded into `THx`; `TLx` auto-receives it |
| 5 | Delay Equation (Mode 1) | $T = (65{,}536 - IV) \times 1.085\,\mu s$ | µs | Inverse of equation (3) |
| 6 | Delay Equation (Mode 2) | $T = (256 - IV) \times 1.085\,\mu s$ | µs | For periodic, auto-reload |
| 7 | Boundary — Max Delay (Mode 1) | $T_{max} = 65{,}536 \times T_{MC}$ | µs | ≈ 71.1 ms @ 11.0592 MHz |
| 8 | Boundary — Min Delay (Mode 1) | $T_{min} = 1 \times T_{MC}$ | µs | One tick (≈ 1.085 µs) |
| 9 | Counter Mode Trigger | External pulse on **P3.4 (T0)** / **P3.5 (T1)** | — | Set `C/T̅ = 1` |
| 10 | Overflow Flag | `TFx = 1` when roll-over occurs | — | Must be cleared by software |

> [!WARNING]
> **Latex Safety Note:** Absolute-value symbols like $\vert x \vert$ are written as `\vert` (or `\mid`) inside tables to avoid breaking markdown syntax. Do NOT use the bare `|` character.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Industrial Process Control:** Timers trigger periodic sensor sampling (e.g., reading a temperature sensor every 100 ms).
> - **Automotive ECU:** Counters tally wheel rotations for Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) and odometer logic.
> - **Embedded Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS):** The **System Tick** of an RTOS is generated by a **Mode 2 auto-reload timer** at a fixed frequency (e.g., 1 kHz).
> - **Digital Signal Generation:** Precise square waves (e.g., for buzzer tone generation) rely on timed toggle of an output pin inside the overflow ISR.
> - **Serial Communication (UART):** Timer 1 in **Mode 2** is the standard baud-rate generator in 8051 (used in KTU Module 3).

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked-Out Derivation #1: Mode 1 Delay Calculation

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Standard):**
> An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz** crystal. Configure **Timer 0 in Mode 1** to generate a delay of **exactly 50 ms**. Calculate the values to be loaded into `TH0` and `TL0`. Write the relevant 8051 assembly snippet.

#### Step 1 — Convert the desired delay into "number of timer ticks"

Each tick = 1 machine cycle = $1.085\,\mu s$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{T_{desired}}{T_{MC}} \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}} \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{50{,}000\,\mu s}{1.085\,\mu s} \\[6pt]
  &= 46{,}082.95 \text{ ticks}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical interpretation:** Since we cannot have a fractional tick, we round to the **next whole number** of ticks (rounding up ensures the delay is **at least** 50 ms, never shorter):

$$
N_{rounded} = 46{,}083 \text{ ticks}
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the Initial (Reload) Value

In Mode 1, the timer is a 16-bit up-counter that overflows at $2^{16} = 65{,}536$. The number of ticks the timer actually counts is $(65{,}536 - IV)$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
IV &= 2^{16} - N_{rounded} \\[4pt]
   &= 65{,}536 - 46{,}083 \\[4pt]
   &= 19{,}453
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Split `IV` into High Byte (`TH0`) and Low Byte (`TL0`)

$$
\begin{aligned}
TH0 &= \left\lfloor \dfrac{IV}{256} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{19{,}453}{256} \right\rfloor = 75 \quad (\text{decimal}) = \mathbf{4BH} \\[6pt]
TL0 &= IV \bmod 256 = 19{,}453 - (75 \times 256) = 19{,}453 - 19{,}200 = 253 \quad (\text{decimal}) = \mathbf{FDH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification (sanity check):**
$$
(75 \times 256) + 253 = 19{,}200 + 253 = 19{,}453 \;\;\checkmark
$$

#### Step 4 — Compute the *Actual* Delay Generated

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{actual} &= (65{,}536 - 19{,}453) \times 1.085\,\mu s \\[4pt]
           &= 46{,}083 \times 1.085\,\mu s \\[4pt]
           &= 50{,}000.055\,\mu s \approx \mathbf{50.000 \text{ ms}} \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — 8051 Assembly Code (Model Solution)

```asm
        MOV  TMOD, #01H     ; Timer 0, Mode 1, Software Control, Timer Mode
        MOV  TH0,  #4BH     ; Load high byte
        MOV  TL0,  #FDH     ; Load low byte
        SETB TR0            ; Start Timer 0

AGAIN:  JNB  TF0, AGAIN     ; Wait here until overflow flag TF0 = 1
        CLR  TR0            ; Stop the timer
        CLR  TF0            ; Clear the overflow flag (software MUST do this)
        ; ---- 50 ms delay is now complete ----
        ; (For REPEATED delay, reload TH0/TL0 again)
```

---

### 3.2 Worked-Out Derivation #2: Mode 2 Auto-Reload Counter

> **Problem:**
> Configure **Timer 1 in Mode 2** to count exactly **100 external pulses** arriving on pin **P3.5 (T1)**. Find the value to be loaded into `TH1`. When the 100th pulse arrives, generate a brief HIGH pulse on **P1.0**.

#### Step 1 — Choose Mode & C/T̅ bit

- We need an **8-bit counter with auto-reload** → **Mode 2** (M1 = 1, M0 = 0).
- We need **external pulse counting** → set `C/T̅ = 1`.
- We do not need hardware gating → `GATE = 0`.
- Timer 1 is in the **upper nibble** of `TMOD` (bit positions 4–7).

The `TMOD` value becomes:
- Lower nibble (Timer 0): `0000` (Timer 0 not used).
- Upper nibble (Timer 1): `1100` = `0xC`.

So $\text{TMOD} = \mathbf{0xC0}$.

#### Step 2 — Calculate the Initial Value for `TH1`

For a counter, $N$ is the number of external pulses (100). In Mode 2 (8-bit):

$$
\begin{aligned}
IV &= 2^8 - N \\[4pt]
   &= 256 - 100 \\[4pt]
   &= 156 \quad (\text{decimal}) \\[4pt]
   &= \mathbf{9CH} \quad (\text{hex})
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Behaviour of Auto-Reload

After the **100th pulse**, `TL1` overflows ($256 \rightarrow 0$). Hardware **automatically copies `TH1` → `TL1`**, so the next 100 pulses can be counted without software intervention — and `TF1` is set.

#### Step 4 — 8051 Assembly Code

```asm
        MOV  TMOD, #0C0H    ; Timer 1, Mode 2, Counter Mode
        MOV  TH1,  #9CH     ; Auto-reload value (100 pulses)
        MOV  TL1,  #9CH     ; Initial value for the FIRST cycle
        SETB TR1            ; Start Counter 1

WAIT:   JNB  TF1, WAIT      ; Wait for the 100th pulse (overflow)
        CLR  TR1            ; Stop counter
        CLR  TF1            ; Clear overflow flag
        SETB P1.0           ; Generate HIGH pulse on P1.0
        ACALL DELAY_PULSE   ; (small software delay for pulse width)
        CLR  P1.0           ; Bring P1.0 LOW
        SETB TR1            ; Restart counter for the NEXT 100 pulses
        SJMP WAIT
```

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mode-Selection Decision Table

| Requirement | Recommended Mode | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Single precise delay (e.g., debounce) | **Mode 1** | Full 16-bit range; manual reload acceptable |
| Continuous, jitter-free periodic interrupt (e.g., RTOS tick) | **Mode 2** | Auto-reload, no jitter between cycles |
| Maximum range delay up to ~71 ms | **Mode 1** | 16-bit range is largest |
| Exact 1-ms tick generation for a clock | **Mode 1 with IV = 65536 – 921** | $921 \times 1.085\,\mu s \approx 999.4\,\mu s$ |
| Counting rotation pulses of a wheel | **Counter Mode (`C/T̅=1`)** | Counts external events, not time |
| Need TWO independent 8-bit timers | **Mode 3 (Timer 0 only)** | Splits Timer 0 into `TL0` + `TH0` |
| Baud-rate generation for UART | **Timer 1, Mode 2** | Standard 8051 UART practice |

---

### 3.4 Numerical Edge-Case: Maximum & Minimum Delays (Mode 1)

#### Maximum Delay

For **IV = 0000H** (timer starts at 0 and counts the full 65,536 ticks):

$$
T_{max} = (65{,}536 - 0) \times 1.085\,\mu s = 71{,}106.56\,\mu s \approx \mathbf{71.1 \text{ ms}}
$$

#### Minimum Delay

For **IV = FFFFH** (timer starts at max and counts only 1 tick to overflow):

$$
T_{min} = (65{,}536 - 65{,}535) \times 1.085\,\mu s = 1 \times 1.085\,\mu s = \mathbf{1.085\,\mu s}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Trap Question:** *"What is the value of `TH0:TL0` to generate the maximum possible delay in Mode 1?"* — Correct Answer: **`TH0 = 00H, TL0 = 00H`** (NOT FFFFH; FFFFH gives the **minimum** delay).

---

### 3.5 Generating Delays > 71.1 ms: The "Software Loop" Technique

Since Mode 1 maxes out at ~71.1 ms per overflow, longer delays are generated by **nesting a software loop around the timer overflow check**.

```asm
        MOV  R7, #10        ; Outer loop count = 10
        MOV  TMOD, #01H     ; Timer 0, Mode 1
        MOV  TH0, #00H
        MOV  TL0, #00H      ; Max delay per overflow

BACK:   SETB TR0
HERE:   JNB  TF0, HERE
        CLR  TR0
        CLR  TF0
        DJNZ R7, BACK       ; Repeat 10 times → ~711 ms total
```

**Total delay** $= 10 \times 71.1 \text{ ms} \approx \mathbf{711 \text{ ms}}$.

### 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Mode Comparison of Timer Operation

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> IDLE: Power ON
    IDLE --> LOAD_TMOD: Programmer writes TMOD
    LOAD_TMOD --> LOAD_VALUE: Programmer writes THx and TLx
    LOAD_VALUE --> RUNNING: Set TRx equals 1
    RUNNING --> RUNNING: Increment on every machine cycle or external pulse
    RUNNING --> OVERFLOW: Count reaches 65536 Mode 1 or 256 Mode 2
    OVERFLOW --> SET_FLAG: Hardware sets TFx equals 1
    SET_FLAG --> NOTIFY_CPU: CPU polls or ISR fires
    NOTIFY_CPU --> CLEAR_FLAG: Software clears TFx
    CLEAR_FLAG --> STOPPED: Set TRx equals 0
    STOPPED --> LOAD_VALUE: Manual reload for Mode 1
    STOPPED --> RUNNING: Auto reload for Mode 2
    STOPPED --> [*]: Program ends
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Timer mode and Counter mode of the 8051 microcontroller. Which SFR bit is used to switch between them?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Timer Mode | Counter Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Source of increment** | Internal **machine cycles** (1.085 µs ticks from oscillator ÷ 12) | **External pulses** on pin **P3.4 (T0)** or **P3.5 (T1)** |
| **Use case** | Generating precise **time delays** | Counting **external events** (e.g., revolutions, products) |
| **Control bit** | `C/T̅ = 0` (cleared) | `C/T̅ = 1` (set) |

The switching bit is the **`C/T̅` bit** in the `TMOD` register. (3 marks)

---

#### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the four operating modes of the 8051 timer. Which mode is most commonly used for baud-rate generation in serial communication, and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four modes are:
1. **Mode 0** — 13-bit timer.
2. **Mode 1** — 16-bit timer.
3. **Mode 2** — 8-bit **auto-reload** timer.
4. **Mode 3** — Split timer (Timer 0 only).

**Mode 2** is used for baud-rate generation because its **8-bit auto-reload** feature (where `TH1` is automatically copied to `TL1` on overflow) produces a **jitter-free, continuous periodic overflow** that is essential for accurate UART timing. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Internal Choice Rule:** In KTU End-Semester Examinations, Part B Module questions carry 14 marks and offer an internal choice. The student must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Timer/Counter logic** of the 8051 microcontroller. Discuss the role of the `TMOD` and `TCON` registers in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz** crystal. Write an 8051 assembly program to generate a square wave of **5 kHz** frequency on pin **P1.0** using **Timer 0 in Mode 1**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Block Diagram & Register Roles (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram (text-rendered for answer script):**

```
        ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
        │  8051 Timer/Counter Logic        │
        │                                  │
        │  f_OSC ──►[÷12]──► Machine Cycle │
        │                       │          │
        │                  ┌────┴─────┐    │
        │  P3.4 (T0) ────►│  C/T̅ mux │    │
        │  P3.5 (T1) ────►│ (per T)  │    │
        │                  └────┬─────┘    │
        │                       ▼          │
        │               ┌──────────────┐   │
        │               │  THx : TLx   │   │
        │               │ (16-bit reg) │   │
        │               └──────┬───────┘   │
        │                      ▼           │
        │               ┌──────────────┐   │
        │               │  Overflow    │   │
        │               │  → set TFx   │   │
        │               └──────────────┘   │
        │                                  │
        │  TMOD controls: mode, C/T̅, GATE  │
        │  TCON controls: TRx (run), TFx    │
        └──────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Role of `TMOD` (3 marks):**
- Located at SFR address **89H**, **not bit-addressable**.
- Upper nibble → Timer 1; lower nibble → Timer 0.
- Bits: `GATE`, `C/T̅`, `M1`, `M0` for each timer.
- `M1 M0` → selects Mode 0, 1, 2, or 3.
- `C/T̅` → 0 = Timer, 1 = Counter.
- `GATE` → 1 = hardware-software AND control via `INTx` pin; 0 = software control only via `TRx`.

**Role of `TCON` (3 marks):**
- Located at SFR address **88H**, **bit-addressable**.
- Bits `TR0`/`TR1` → software start/stop for the corresponding timer.
- Bits `TF0`/`TF1` → overflow flags (set by hardware, cleared by software).
- Remaining bits (`IE0`, `IT0`, `IE1`, `IT1`) relate to external interrupts, not timers (1 mark for distinguishing them).

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Square Wave Generation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Determine the half-period delay.**

A 5 kHz square wave has period $T = 1/5000 = 200\,\mu s$. The ON time = OFF time = $100\,\mu s$ each (the timer toggles `P1.0` after each half-cycle).

**Step 2 — Compute the Initial Value for 100 µs half-period.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 92.17 \approx 92 \text{ ticks} \\[6pt]
IV &= 65{,}536 - 92 = 65{,}444 \\[6pt]
TH0 &= 65{,}444 / 256 = 255 \quad (\text{decimal}) = \mathbf{FFH} \\[4pt]
TL0 &= 65{,}444 \bmod 256 = 65{,}444 - (255 \times 256) = 65{,}444 - 65{,}280 = 164 \quad (\text{decimal}) = \mathbf{A4H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Assembly Program (continuous square wave on P1.0):**

```asm
        ORG  0000H
        SJMP MAIN
        ORG  0030H

MAIN:   MOV  TMOD, #01H     ; Timer 0, Mode 1, software control
AGAIN:  MOV  TH0, #0FFH     ; Load high byte (255)
        MOV  TL0, #0A4H     ; Load low byte (164)
        SETB TR0            ; Start Timer 0

HERE:   JNB  TF0, HERE      ; Wait for 100 µs overflow
        CLR  TR0            ; Stop timer
        CLR  TF0            ; Clear overflow flag
        CPL  P1.0           ; Toggle P1.0 (ON↔OFF)
        SJMP AGAIN          ; Repeat forever

        END
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Forgetting to **clear `TF0` in software** after detecting overflow — *deduct 1 mark*. (The 8051 does NOT auto-clear `TF0` in polled mode; only inside an ISR does it auto-clear for some interrupt modes, but explicitly clearing is the safe, mark-winning practice.)
> 2. Loading **wrong values** for `TH0` and `TL0` (e.g., swapping high/low bytes) — *deduct 2 marks*.
> 3. Forgetting that the timer is reloaded **inside the loop** for Mode 1 — *deduct 1 mark*.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **four modes of operation** of the 8051 timer with the help of diagrams. Compare **Mode 1** and **Mode 2** in terms of reload mechanism and range. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 microcontroller with a **12 MHz** crystal is required to generate a delay of **1 ms** using **Timer 1 in Mode 2**. Calculate the value to be loaded into `TH1` and write the assembly program. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Four Modes Comparison (7 marks)**

**Mode-wise description (5 marks):**

| Mode | Width | Counting Range | Overflow at | Reload? | Block Diagram Sketch |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mode 0** | 13-bit (5 of `TLx` + 8 of `THx`) | 0 to 8191 | 8192 | ❌ Manual | 13-bit up-counter using `THx:5-bits of TLx` |
| **Mode 1** | 16-bit | 0 to 65,535 | 65,536 | ❌ Manual | Full 16-bit: `THx` + `TLx` |
| **Mode 2** | 8-bit (with `THx` shadow) | 0 to 255 | 256 | ✅ **Auto** (THx → TLx) | `TLx` is the counter; `THx` holds the reload value |
| **Mode 3** | Two 8-bit timers (T0 only) | 0 to 255 each | 256 each | ❌ Manual | `TL0` + `TH0` become independent; Timer 1 freezes |

**Mode 1 vs Mode 2 — Key Differences (2 marks):**

| Property | Mode 1 | Mode 2 |
|---|---|---|
| **Bit width** | 16-bit (large range) | 8-bit (small range) |
| **Max count** | 65,536 | 256 |
| **Reload mechanism** | **Manual** by software after each overflow | **Automatic** (hardware copies `THx → TLx`) |
| **Jitter** | Higher (gap between overflow and reload) | Negligible (back-to-back reload) |
| **Typical use** | One-shot precise delay | Periodic, continuous applications (baud rate, RTOS tick) |

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Mode 2 Delay at 12 MHz (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute the machine cycle period for 12 MHz crystal.**

$$
T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}} = \dfrac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} = 1\,\mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of ticks needed for 1 ms.**

$$
N = \dfrac{T_{desired}}{T_{MC}} = \dfrac{1000\,\mu s}{1\,\mu s} = 1000 \text{ ticks}
$$

**Step 3 — Check feasibility in Mode 2.**

Mode 2 can count **at most 256 ticks**. Since 1000 > 256, the **1 ms delay cannot be achieved in a single Mode-2 cycle**. We must use a **software loop counter**.

Let the loop repeat $k$ times. Then total ticks = $k \times (256 - IV)$. We choose a convenient `IV` and compute $k$:

Choose $IV = 256 - 250 = 6$ (i.e., each loop iteration = 250 µs, near 1 ms).

$$
k = \left\lceil \dfrac{1000}{250} \right\rceil = 4 \text{ iterations}
$$

Total delay = $4 \times 250 = 1000\,\mu s = 1 \text{ ms} \;\;\checkmark$

So: $\mathbf{TH1 = TL1 = 06H}$, and a **register (e.g., R3) holds the loop count = 04H**.

**Step 4 — Assembly Program.**

```asm
        MOV  TMOD, #20H     ; Timer 1, Mode 2, software control
        MOV  TH1,  #06H     ; Auto-reload value
        MOV  TL1,  #06H     ; First-cycle initial value
        MOV  R3,   #04H     ; Loop count = 4

LOOP:   SETB TR1            ; Start Timer 1
WAIT:   JNB  TF1, WAIT      ; Wait for 250 µs overflow
        CLR  TR1
        CLR  TF1
        DJNZ R3, LOOP       ; Repeat 4 times → 1 ms total
        ; ---- 1 ms delay is now complete ----
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Q.B):**
> 1. **Crystal-frequency trap:** Students often blindly use $T_{MC} = 1.085\,\mu s$ (the 11.0592 MHz value). For 12 MHz, the correct value is **1 µs**. Wrong assumption = *lose 2 marks*.
> 2. **Range trap:** Forgetting that Mode 2 is only 8-bit (max 256 ticks). At 1 µs/tick, max Mode 2 delay = 256 µs, so 1 ms requires a **loop**. Missing this loop = *lose 2 marks*.
> 3. **Reload trap:** For Mode 2, only `TH1` is the reload source. `TL1` is the live counter. Students sometimes write `MOV TL1, #06H` inside the loop — wrong, since the hardware already does it. *Lose 1 mark for redundancy.*
> 4. **Flag-clear trap:** Not clearing `TF1` after the wait loop = *lose 1 mark*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density, KTU 2024 Pattern):**

- ✅ The 8051 has **two 16-bit timers**: **Timer 0** (controlled by `TR0`/`TF0`, external pin **P3.4**) and **Timer 1** (controlled by `TR1`/`TF1`, external pin **P3.5**).
- ✅ Each timer's high byte is `THx` and low byte is `TLx`. The full 16-bit count = `THx × 256 + TLx`.
- ✅ **`TMOD` (89H)** = **not bit-addressable**; **`TCON` (88H)** = **bit-addressable**.
- ✅ **`C/T̅ = 0` → Timer Mode** (counts machine cycles). **`C/T̅ = 1` → Counter Mode** (counts external pulses on T0/T1).
- ✅ **`GATE = 1`** → timer runs only when `TRx = 1` **AND** `INTx = 1` (hardware gating). **`GATE = 0`** → software gating only.
- ✅ **Mode 0:** 13-bit (legacy). **Mode 1:** 16-bit (most common, manual reload). **Mode 2:** 8-bit **auto-reload** (best for periodic). **Mode 3:** split timer (Timer 0 only).
- ✅ **Standard KTU delay formulas:**
  - $T_{MC} = 12 / f_{OSC}$
  - $IV_{16} = 65{,}536 - (T_{desired} / T_{MC})$
  - $IV_{8} = 256 - (T_{desired} / T_{MC})$
- ✅ **Maximum delay (Mode 1, 11.0592 MHz)** = **~71.1 ms**. Use a **software loop** for longer delays.
- ✅ **Minimum delay (Mode 1)** = **1.085 µs** (one tick, when `IV = FFFFH`).
- ✅ **Maximum delay (Mode 1, 12 MHz crystal)** = $65{,}536 \times 1\,\mu s =$ **65.536 ms**.
- ✅ **Always** clear the overflow flag (`TFx = 0`) in software before re-using the timer.
- ✅ **Always** reload `THx` and `TLx` after each overflow in **Mode 1** (manual reload is mandatory).
- ✅ **Mode 2 reload** is **automatic** — `THx` is copied to `TLx` by hardware on every overflow.
- ✅ The 8051 **does NOT auto-reload in Mode 1**. The single most common bug in KTU scripts is forgetting this.
- ✅ For **baud-rate generation (UART)**, KTU Module 3 uses **Timer 1, Mode 2** (auto-reload).
- ✅ To **start** a timer: set the `TRx` bit in `TCON` (`SETB TR0` or `SETB TR1`).
- ✅ To **stop** a timer: clear the `TRx` bit (`CLR TR0` or `CLR TR1`).

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU Examiner's Mantra:**
> *"If a 14-mark question asks for a delay calculation, the examiner awards marks in this order: (1) correct crystal analysis and machine cycle period — 2 marks, (2) correct tick count N — 2 marks, (3) correct Initial Value IV — 2 marks, (4) correct THx/TLx split — 1 mark, (5) correct TMOD byte — 2 marks, (6) syntactically correct assembly with flag clear & reload — 5 marks. Skipping any of these costs marks proportionally."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timers as Counters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Timers as Counters ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the **Intel 8051 microcontroller architecture**, each of the two internal **16-bit Timer/Counter hardware units (Timer 0 and Timer 1)** is a dual-function peripheral. The mode of operation is selected via the **C/T̄ (Counter/Timer) control bit** located in the **TMOD (Timer Mode) register**. When the C/T̄ bit is set to logic **1**, the hardware unit is configured as a **Counter**, meaning it increments its internal register pair (**THx:TLx**) in response to **external negative-edge (1-to-0) transitions** sampled on dedicated input pins — **T0 (P3.4)** for Timer 0 and **T1 (P3.5)** for Timer 1. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PBCST504 / Module 2:** > The Timer block in the 8051 is a **dual-purpose unit** (Timer OR Counter). The C/T̄ bit in TMOD is the *single switch* that determines whether the unit measures **internal machine cycles (Timer mode)** or **external events on a port pin (Counter mode)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **toll booth counter**: - A worker sits at a booth and presses a clicker every time a car passes. - The clicker mechanically advances by **+1 per car**. - The toll booth counter does not care *how long* it takes between cars — it only counts **how many cars** have passed. Now replace the toll booth with the 8051: - The **port pin (P3.4 or P3.5)** is the "car sensor" (e.g., a limit switch, a photo-interrupter, an opto-coupler output). - Each time the pin **transitions from 1 → 0 (a falling edge)**, the internal register **increments by 1**. - The 8051 does not know *when* the events happen — it simply **counts the number of events** that occurred. > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Summary:** A Counter counts *things* (events) arriving at a pin. A Timer counts *time* using the internal oscillator. Both use the **same hardware** — only the **C/T̄ bit** and the **clock source** change. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Value | Significance | |---|---|---| | **Maximum count value (unsigned)** | **65,536** | $2^{16}$ (FFFFH → 0000H rollover) | | **Counter width** | **16 bits** | THx (8 bits) + TLx (8 bits) | | **Triggering edge** | **1 → 0 (falling/negative)** | Sampled on P3.4 (T0) / P3.5 (T1) | | **Minimum pulse width (high)** | **1 machine cycle** | ≈ 1.085 µs @ 11.0592 MHz crystal | | **Minimum pulse width (low)** | **1 machine cycle** | Same as above | | **Oscillator frequency (typical)** | **11.0592 MHz** | Standard KTU lab crystal | | **Machine cycle period** | **12 / f_osc** | 12 oscillator periods = 1 machine cycle | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Architecture: How the Counter Works The 8051's Timer/Counter block is built from a chain of hardware flip-flops forming a **16-bit up-counter**. The source of the increment clock is governed by a **multiplexer** steered by the C/T̄ bit: - **C/T̄ = 0 (Timer mode):** Multiplexer routes the **internal machine cycle clock** → increments every machine cycle. - **C/T̄ = 1 (Counter mode):** Multiplexer routes the **external pin (T0 or T1)** → increments on every 1→0 transition. **Step-by-step logic flow when configured as a Counter:** 1. **Pin sampling:** The 8051's input synchronizer samples the T0/T1 pin across two machine cycles to **debounce and synchronize** the asynchronous external signal. 2. **Edge detection:** A negative-edge detector outputs one pulse per 1→0 transition. 3. **Register increment:** This pulse is routed (if C/T̄ = 1) to the **TLx register**, incrementing it by 1. 4. **Overflow propagation:** When TLx rolls over from **FFH → 00H**, it triggers an increment of **THx**. Thus the pair behaves as a 16-bit counter. 5. **Overflow flag:** When THx rolls over from **FFH → 00H**, the **TFx (Timer Overflow Flag)** is set to 1, which can raise an **interrupt** (if enabled via IE register) or be polled by software. 6. **Run control:** Counting is active only when the **TRx (Timer Run) bit in TCON** is set to 1. ### 2.2 The C/T̄ Bit — The Master Switch The **TMOD register** is an 8-bit, non-bit-addressable SFR where each timer occupies 4 bits: | Bit 7 (GATE) | Bit 6 (C/T̄) | Bit 5 (M1) | Bit 4 (M0) | ← Timer 1 | |---|---|---|---|---| | Bit 3 (GATE) | Bit 2 (C/T̄) | Bit 1 (M1) | Bit 0 (M0) | ← Timer 0 | | Bit | Name | Function | |---|---|---| | **C/T̄** | Counter / Timer select | **1 = Counter mode**, 0 = Timer mode | | **GATE** | Software/Hardware control | 1 = hardware-controlled by INTx pin, 0 = software-controlled by TRx | | **M1, M0** | Mode select | 00=Mode 0 (13-bit), 01=Mode 1 (16-bit), 10=Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload), 11=Mode 3 (split) | > [!NOTE] > For Counter mode in KTU exams, **Mode 1 (16-bit) and Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** are most frequently tested. Mode 1 gives a maximum count of 65,536. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Count value (desired events)** | $N$ | pulses | Integer ≥ 1 | | 2 | **Initial (reload) value, 16-bit** | $\text{TVAL} = 2^{16} - N = 65536 - N$ | decimal | THx = high byte, TLx = low byte | | 3 | **Initial value, 8-bit auto-reload** | $\text{TVAL} = 256 - N$ | decimal | Loaded into THx; TLx starts here | | 4 | **Max events before overflow (Mode 1)** | $N_{\max} = 2^{16} = 65536$ | pulses | From 0000H to FFFFH then overflow | | 5 | **Max events before overflow (Mode 2)** | $N_{\max} = 2^{8} = 256$ | pulses | Auto-reload mode | | 6 | **Initial value byte split** | $\text{THx} = \lfloor \text{TVAL}/256 \rfloor,\ \text{TLx} = \text{TVAL} \bmod 256$ | hex | Use modulo 256 | | 7 | **Pins used for external events** | T0 = **P3.4**, T1 = **P3.5** | — | Dedicated alternate functions | | 8 | **Triggering edge** | **1 → 0 (negative edge)** | — | Hardware sensitive to falling edge | | 9 | **Event frequency maximum** | $f_{\text{ext}} \le \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{24}$ | Hz | ≈ 460.8 kHz @ 11.0592 MHz | | 10 | **Overflow flag location** | **TF0 (TCON.5)** or **TF1 (TCON.7)** | — | Set automatically by hardware | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Trick:** In Counter mode, the value loaded into the timer registers is **NOT a time**, it is a **count of events remaining until overflow**. This is the #1 conceptual confusion point in KTU exams. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications - **Production line counters** (counting bottles, screws, packets on a conveyor) - **RPM / speed measurement** (counting pulses from an optical encoder in a fixed window) - **Frequency measurement** (counting pulses for a known gate time) - **Anemometers & flow meters** (counting rotations of a cup or turbine) - **People counters** at doors (IR beam break sensors) - **Vending machine coin acceptors** (pulse-per-coin output) - **Stepper motor position tracking** (closed-loop via encoder feedback) ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Initial Value and Number of Events **Given:** A 16-bit counter in Mode 1, starting from an initial 16-bit value $\text{TVAL}$, counts $N$ external events and then overflows. **Step 1 — Recognize the counting mechanism:** Each external pulse on P3.4 (or P3.5) increments the 16-bit register pair THx:TLx by 1.
