# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 KTU-Style Numerical Problem: Baud-Rate Configuration

> **Problem Statement (Model Q):**
> On an STM32U575 board, the FDCAN peripheral is fed with a clock of **$f_{FDCAN\_CLK} = 40$ MHz**. Configure the baud rate to **$500$ kbps** with a sample point of **$80\%$**, using the parameters: $t_{Sync} = 1$ $t_q$, $t_{Prop} = 3$ $t_q$, $t_{Seg1} = 11$ $t_q$, $t_{Seg2} = 3$ $t_q$. Determine the **Baud Rate Prescaler (BRP)** and verify the **SJW** is valid.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Write the standard baud-rate formula.**

$$
f_{baud} = \frac{f_{FDCAN\_CLK}}{(BRP + 1) \cdot N_{BT}}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the nominal bit time ($N_{BT}$).**

$$
N_{BT} = t_{Sync} + t_{Prop} + t_{Seg1} + t_{Seg2}
$$

$$
N_{BT} = 1 + 3 + 11 + 3 = 18 \;\; t_q
$$

**Step 3: Solve for BRP.**

Rearranging the baud-rate equation:

$$
(BRP + 1) = \frac{f_{FDCAN\_CLK}}{f_{baud} \cdot N_{BT}}
$$

$$
(BRP + 1) = \frac{40 \times 10^6}{500 \times 10^3 \cdot 18} = \frac{40 \times 10^6}{9 \times 10^6} = 4.444...
$$

> [!NOTE]
> BRP must be an integer. We round down to the nearest integer: $(BRP + 1) = 4$, hence $BRP = 3$.
> Note: this introduces a small baud-rate error of $\approx 11\%$. In practice, you would tune $t_{Seg1}/t_{Seg2}$ to make $(BRP+1)$ an integer. For example, choose $N_{BT} = 20$ → $(BRP+1) = 4$ exactly.

**Step 4: Verify SJW validity.**

$$
1 \leq SJW \leq \min(4, t_{Seg2}) = \min(4, 3) = 3
$$

Choose $SJW = 1$ (safest default). ✅

**Step 5: Verify sample point.**

$$
SP = \frac{t_{Sync} + t_{Prop} + t_{Seg1}}{N_{BT}} \times 100 = \frac{1 + 3 + 11}{18} \times 100 = 83.33\%
$$

This falls within the recommended 75 % – 87.5 % range. ✅

**Step 6: Compute actual achieved baud rate.**

$$
f_{baud,\;actual} = \frac{40 \times 10^6}{(3 + 1) \cdot 18} = \frac{40 \times 10^6}{72} \approx 555.6 \; kbps
$$

Baud-rate error:
$$
\varepsilon = \frac{555.6 - 500}{500} \times 100\% = 11.11\%
$$

This is **too high** (> 1.5 % tolerance for CAN). Re-tune the segments.

### Corrected Configuration (Tuned for Exact 500 kbps)

Choose $N_{BT} = 20$ $t_q$, sample point = $80\%$.

$$
t_{Sync} + t_{Prop} + t_{Seg1} = 0.80 \times 20 = 16 \;\; t_q
$$

A common split: $t_{Sync} = 1$, $t_{Prop} = 3$, $t_{Seg1} = 12$, $t_{Seg2} = 4$.

$$
(BRP + 1) = \frac{40 \times 10^6}{500 \times 10^3 \cdot 20} = 4
$$

Hence **$BRP = 3$**, $SJW = 1$ (valid: $\min(4, 4) = 4$).

Actual baud rate = **500 kbps exactly**, error = **0 %**. ✅

| Message ID (Extended 29-bit) | 0x18FEF100 | 0x18FEF100 | 0x18FEF100 |
| Filter Type | N/A (transmit) | Exact ID match (Classic Filter) | Range Filter (0x120–0x130) |
| Filter → Rx FIFO | N/A | Filter 0 → Rx FIFO 0 | Filter 1 → Rx FIFO 1 |
| Frame Type | Data Frame | N/A | N/A |
| DLC | 8 bytes (CAN) or 16 bytes (CAN FD) | — | — |
| Data Payload | [Temp, Pressure, Speed, …] | — | — |

| GPIO Pins | PA12 (FDCAN1_TX), PA11 (FDCAN1_RX) | PA12 / PA11 | PB9 / PB8 (remapped) |

## 3.3 KTU-Style Analytical Problem: Filter Design

> **Problem Statement:**
> On an STM32U575 Node B, configure a **range filter** that accepts all messages with IDs from **0x200 to 0x20F** (11-bit standard IDs) and routes them to **Rx FIFO 0**. Use **Filter ID1 = 0x200** and **Filter ID2 = 0x20F**.

**Step 1: Identify filter type.**
Range filter → `FDCAN_SIDFC.F0SA` set, filter type = `FDCAN_FILTER_RANGE`.

**Step 2: Populate the filter register fields (conceptual).**

In the STM32U575 HAL/library convention, the filter element structure holds:

$$
SFID1 = 0x200 \;\; (\text{lower ID of range})
$$

$$
SFID2 = 0x20F \;\; (\text{upper ID of range})
$$

$$
SFEC = 0b100 \;\; (\text{Rx FIFO 0 destination})
$$

**Step 3: Verification logic.**

A received message with ID $X$ passes the filter if and only if:

$$
SFID1 \leq X \leq SFID2
$$

$$
0x200 \leq X \leq 0x20F
$$

This covers **16 IDs** ($0x200, 0x201, \ldots, 0x20F$). ✅

**Step 4: Bit-rate independence.**
Note that **filtering is performed AFTER successful reception** of a frame — it does not affect bit timing. The same node can have any baud rate as long as all nodes on the bus share identical nominal bit timing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **standard vs. extended filter banks**. The STM32U575 has **up to 28 standard filters** and **up to 8 extended filters**. These are SEPARATE registers (`FLSSA` and `FLSEA` in the reference manual). Make sure to write standard filter values to the standard filter RAM region.

## 3.4 Transceiver Delay Compensation Derivation (CAN FD)

In CAN FD, the data phase can run at a higher bit rate (e.g., 5 Mbps), but this exposes a problem: the **loop delay** of the transceiver (typically ~150 ns for an isolated transceiver) is comparable to one bit time. To compensate, the FDCAN peripheral samples a secondary point in the bit:

$$
t_{sample,\;secondary} = 2 \cdot t_{FDCAN\_CLK} \cdot (TDCO + 1)
$$

Where $TDCO$ (Transceiver Delay Compensation Offset) is a programmable value in the **FDCAN_TDCR** register.

> **Derivation Logic:**
> The transmitter's "received" bit is sampled at $t_{sample,\;secondary}$ instead of the standard sample point. If this sample matches the transmitted bit, the loop delay is correctly compensated. If it doesn't match, a "FD Parity Error" is flagged.

$$
TDC_{SSP} = t_{Tx} + t_{loop,\;max} + t_{margin}
$$

$$
TDC_{SSP} = t_{Tx} + 2 \cdot t_{FDCAN\_CLK} \cdot (TDCO + 1)
$$

This is conceptually similar to **synchronizing the local clock to a remote clock with known propagation delay** — a recurring theme in digital communication systems.

## 4.2 CAN Data Frame Bit-Level Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["SOF - 1 dominant bit - signals start"]
    F2["ID 11 bits - Arbitration - priority"]
    F3["RTR - 1 bit - Remote Transmission Request"]
    F4["IDE - 1 bit - Identifier Extension"]
    F5["r0 - 1 bit - reserved dominant"]
    F6["DLC - 4 bits - Data Length 0 to 8"]
    F7["Data 0 to 8 bytes - actual payload"]
    F8["CRC 15 bits - Cyclic Redundancy Check"]
    F9["CRC Delim - 1 bit recessive"]
    F10["ACK Slot - 1 bit - any receiver overwrites with dominant"]
    F11["ACK Delim - 1 bit recessive"]
    F12["EOF - 7 recessive bits"]
    F13["IFS - 3 recessive bits - Inter Frame Space"]
    F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4 --> F5 --> F6 --> F7 --> F8 --> F9 --> F10 --> F11 --> F12 --> F13
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1 - Enable FDCAN1 clock in RCC"]
    S2["Step 2 - Configure GPIO - PA11 RX - PA12 TX - AF9 mode"]
    S3["Step 3 - Set NVIC priority for FDCAN1 interrupts"]
    S4["Step 4 - Configure bit timing - NBTP register - BRP, Seg1, Seg2, SJW"]
    S5["Step 5 - Configure DBTP register for CAN FD data phase"]
    S6["Step 6 - Configure filter bank - SIDFC, XIDFC, FLSSA, FLSEA"]
    S7["Step 7 - Configure Tx FIFO or Tx Queue mode - TXBC, TXEFC"]
    S8["Step 8 - Configure Rx FIFOs - RXF0C, RXF1C - allocate RAM"]
    S9["Step 9 - Set FDCAN_CCCR.INIT = 0 - exit init mode"]
    S10["Step 10 - Enable interrupts - ILE, IER"]
    S11["Step 11 - Application loop - Transmit via Tx FIFO - Receive via Rx FIFO 0 or 1"]
    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10 --> S11
```

## 4.4 Physical Bus Topology with Termination

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph leftTerm["Left Termination - 120 Ohm"]
        R1["R1 120 Ohm"]
    end
    subgraph rightTerm["Right Termination - 120 Ohm"]
        R2["R2 120 Ohm"]
    end
    subgraph buswires["Differential Bus"]
        CH["CANH Wire"]
        CL["CANL Wire"]
    end
    subgraph Adev["Node A - STM32U575 - Transceiver"]
        NA["U1"]
    end
    subgraph Bdev["Node B - STM32U575 - Transceiver"]
        NB["U2"]
    end
    subgraph Cdev["Node C - STM32U575 - Transceiver"]
        NC["U3"]
    end
    CH --- R1
    CL --- R1
    CH --- NA
    CL --- NA
    CH --- NB
    CL --- NB
    CH --- NC
    CL --- NC
    CH --- R2
    CL --- R2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Termination rule:** Exactly **two 120 Ω resistors** must be placed at the **two physical ends** of the bus. Middle nodes do not have termination. The equivalent parallel resistance is **60 Ω**, which matches the differential characteristic impedance of standard CAN twisted-pair cable (typically 60 Ω). Mismatched impedance causes reflections and bit errors.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List any three differences between Classical CAN and CAN FD.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Maximum Data Rate:** Classical CAN supports up to **1 Mbps**; CAN FD supports up to **5 Mbps** in the data phase (nominal bit rate remains the same).
2. **Payload Size:** Classical CAN is limited to **8 bytes** per frame; CAN FD allows up to **64 bytes** per frame.
3. **Bit Timing:** Classical CAN uses a single bit-time configuration for the whole frame; CAN FD uses **two** bit-time configurations — a slower nominal rate for the arbitration phase and a faster data rate for the data/CRC phase.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define "bit stuffing" in CAN. Why is it required?**

**Model Answer:**
Bit stuffing is the process of inserting a complementary bit by the transmitter after every sequence of **five consecutive identical bits** in a CAN frame (from SOF to CRC). The receiver automatically removes these stuffed bits. It is required to **maintain clock synchronization** between nodes, ensure **edge transitions** for resynchronization, and enable the receiver to detect **bit errors and framing errors** reliably.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) — Focus: Bit Timing & Frame Structure

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of the **FDCAN peripheral** in STM32U575. List the major configuration registers and their functions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** The FDCAN peripheral on an STM32U575 is clocked at **$f_{FDCAN\_CLK} = 80$ MHz**. Calculate the **Baud Rate Prescaler (BRP)** and the **achievable baud rate** for the following configuration: $t_{Sync} = 1$ $t_q$, $t_{Prop} = 3$ $t_q$, $t_{Seg1} = 7$ $t_q$, $t_{Seg2} = 2$ $t_q$. Verify that the sample point is within the recommended 75 % – 87.5 % range. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) FDCAN Peripheral Architecture (7 marks):**

The STM32U575 FDCAN1 peripheral consists of:

- **FDCAN Core** — Handles bit timing, arbitration, error detection, and frame processing as per ISO 11898-1:2015.
- **Tx FIFO / Tx Queue** — Holds messages to be transmitted; configurable as a **FIFO** (multiple elements, but no longer the "mailbox" model of bxCAN).
- **Rx FIFO 0 and Rx FIFO 1** — Two receive FIFOs (each up to 64 elements) store accepted frames.
- **Standard Filter Bank** — Up to **28 filters**; routes frames with 11-bit IDs.
- **Extended Filter Bank** — Up to **8 filters**; routes frames with 29-bit IDs.
- **Bit Timing Engine** — Configured by the **NBTP** (Nominal Bit Timing & Prescaler) and **DBTP** (Data Bit Timing & Prescaler) registers.
- **Interrupt Controller** — Routes events (Rx FIFO new message, Tx complete, error) to NVIC.

**Key Configuration Registers:**

| Register | Function |
|----------|----------|
| `FDCAN_CCCR` | Control register (init, test modes) |
| `FDCAN_NBTP` | Nominal bit timing + prescaler |
| `FDCAN_DBTP` | Data phase bit timing (CAN FD) |
| `FDCAN_TDCR` | Transceiver Delay Compensation |
| `FDCAN_RXF0C` / `FDCAN_RXF1C` | Rx FIFO 0 / 1 configuration |
| `FDCAN_TXBC` | Tx Buffer / FIFO configuration |
| `FDCAN_SIDFC` / `FDCAN_XIDFC` | Standard / Extended ID filter configuration |
| `FDCAN_GFC` | Global filter configuration |
| `FDCAN_IE` / `FDCAN_ILS` | Interrupt enable / line selection |
| `FDCAN_IR` | Interrupt flag register |

**(b) Baud-Rate Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute nominal bit time.**

$$
N_{BT} = t_{Sync} + t_{Prop} + t_{Seg1} + t_{Seg2} = 1 + 3 + 7 + 2 = 13 \;\; t_q
$$

**Step 2: Compute time quantum and baud rate.**

We are given $f_{FDCAN\_CLK} = 80$ MHz, but **BRP is not given**. We need to choose it. For 1 Mbps (typical for CAN 2.0B):

$$
(BRP + 1) = \frac{f_{FDCAN\_CLK}}{f_{baud} \cdot N_{BT}} = \frac{80 \times 10^6}{1 \times 10^6 \cdot 13} \approx 6.15
$$

Choose $BRP + 1 = 6$ → **$BRP = 5$**.

**Step 3: Achievable baud rate.**

$$
f_{baud,\;actual} = \frac{80 \times 10^6}{(5 + 1) \cdot 13} = \frac{80 \times 10^6}{78} \approx 1.0256 \; Mbps
$$

**Baud-rate error:**

$$
\varepsilon = \frac{1.0256 - 1.0}{1.0} \times 100\% = 2.56\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Exceeds 1.5 % tolerance** for CAN! In practice, choose $N_{BT} = 16$ instead (e.g., $t_{Seg1} = 10, t_{Seg2} = 2$). Then $(BRP+1) = 5$ exactly, error = 0 %.

**Step 4: Sample point verification (with original values).**

$$
SP = \frac{1 + 3 + 7}{13} \times 100\% = 84.6\%
$$

✅ **Within 75 % – 87.5 % range.** Good choice.

**Step 5: SJW verification.**

$$
1 \leq SJW \leq \min(4, t_{Seg2}) = \min(4, 2) = 2
$$

Choose $SJW = 1$ or $2$ (both valid).

**Final Configuration:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| $BRP$ | 5 |
| $t_{Sync}$ | 1 $t_q$ |
| $t_{Prop}$ | 3 $t_q$ |
| $t_{Seg1}$ | 7 $t_q$ |
| $t_{Seg2}$ | 2 $t_q$ |
| $N_{BT}$ | 13 $t_q$ |
| Sample Point | 84.6 % |
| $SJW$ | 1 $t_q$ |
| Achieved Baud | 1.0256 Mbps (≈ 2.56 % error — re-tune!) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Focus: Multi-Node Network Design

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]**
>
> **(a)** Design a **three-node CAN network** using three STM32U575 microcontrollers. Node 1 acts as a **temperature sensor** (transmits every 100 ms, Standard ID = 0x120, 4 bytes of data). Node 2 is an **ECU controller** (receives only Node 1's data and broadcasts an actuator command on ID = 0x200). Node 3 is a **dashboard display** (receives both 0x120 and 0x200, ignores all else). Draw the network topology, configure appropriate filters, and explain the data flow. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain **non-destructive bit-wise arbitration** with an example. How does this mechanism enable CAN to guarantee **deterministic latency** for the highest-priority message? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Three-Node CAN Network Design (7 marks):**

**Network Topology:**

```
   [Node 1: Temp Sensor]              [Node 2: ECU]              [Node 3: Dashboard]
   STM32U575 + TJA1050     ──→        STM32U575 + TJA1050        STM32U575 + TJA1050
   Tx ID 0x120, 4 bytes                Filter: 0x120              Filters: 0x120, 0x200
   Transmit every 100 ms               Filter: 0x200              Receives both
                                       Tx ID 0x200 on ECU cmd
```

**Filter Configuration per Node:**

| Node | Standard Filter Index | ID1 | ID2 | Filter Type | Action | Destination |
|------|-----------------------|-----|-----|-------------|--------|-------------|
| Node 2 (ECU) | 0 | 0x120 | 0x120 | Exact | Store | Rx FIFO 0 |
| Node 2 (ECU) | 1 | 0x200 | 0x200 | Exact | Store | Rx FIFO 0 |
| Node 3 (Display) | 0 | 0x120 | 0x120 | Exact | Store | Rx FIFO 0 |
| Node 3 (Display) | 1 | 0x200 | 0x200 | Exact | Store | Rx FIFO 0 |

**Data Flow Description:**

1. **Node 1** (Temp Sensor) prepares a Data Frame with ID 0x120 and 4 data bytes (e.g., `[25, 12, 0xAB, 0xCD]` representing temperature, humidity, etc.). It places the message in its **Tx FIFO**.
2. The FDCAN peripheral automatically transmits the frame onto the bus after the bus is detected idle.
3. The frame is broadcast on **CAN_H and CAN_L** lines. **All three nodes** receive the electrical signals, but only the transceivers of **Node 2 and Node 3** convert them back to TTL.
4. The FDCAN1 peripherals of **Node 2 and Node 3** check each frame's ID against their filter banks. Both have a filter for ID 0x120 (exact match), so the frame is **stored in Rx FIFO 0** of both nodes and an interrupt is generated.
5. **Node 2** (ECU) processes the temperature data. It may decide to command an actuator (e.g., a fan) by transmitting a new frame with ID 0x200 and DLC = 1.
6. **Node 3** (Dashboard) receives both 0x120 (temperature) and 0x200 (actuator state) frames and updates its display.

**Node 1's filter configuration:** It can be left empty (or with "reject all" filter) since it only transmits. To reduce CPU load, set `FDCAN_GFC.ANFS = 0b00` (reject non-matching frames) and disable Rx FIFO acceptance by Node 1.

---

**(b) Non-Destructive Bit-Wise Arbitration (7 marks):**

**Concept:**
CAN uses a wired-AND bus with **dominant (0)** and **recessive (1)** bits. When two or more nodes start transmitting simultaneously, they monitor the bus during the **arbitration field** (the ID bits). At each bit position, the node transmits its bit and simultaneously **samples the bus**. If it transmitted a recessive bit (1) but sampled a dominant bit (0), it knows that a higher-priority node is also transmitting. The losing node immediately **stops transmitting** and switches to **Receive Mode** — without corrupting the winning frame.

**Example:**
Suppose Node A transmits with ID = `0x123` = binary `001 0010 0011` and Node B transmits with ID = `0x121` = binary `001 0010 0001`. They start transmitting at the same time.

| Bit Position (MSB→LSB) | Node A sends | Node B sends | Bus level | Node A's decision | Node B's decision |
|------------------------|--------------|--------------|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 (dominant) | Continue | Continue |
| 2 | 0 | 0 | 0 (dominant) | Continue | Continue |
| 3 | 1 | 1 | 1 (recessive) | Continue | Continue |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 (dominant) | Continue | Continue |
| 5 | 0 | 0 | 0 (dominant) | Continue | Continue |
| 6 | 1 | 1 | 1 (recessive) | Continue | Continue |
| 7 | 0 | 0 | 0 (dominant) | Continue | Continue |
| 8 | 0 | 0 | 0 (dominant) | Continue | Continue |
| 9 | 0 | 0 | 0 (dominant) | Continue | Continue |
| 10 | 1 | 0 | 0 (dominant) | **STOP — lost arbitration** | Continue |
| 11 | 1 | 1 | 1 (recessive) | Listening | Continue |

Node B's ID ends in `...001`, which is numerically smaller → higher priority. Node B wins arbitration and completes its transmission. Node A immediately retries its frame as soon as the bus becomes idle.

**Deterministic Latency:**

Because the arbitration mechanism ensures that the **lowest-ID (highest-priority) message always wins** without delay or corruption, the **worst-case latency** for the highest-priority message is bounded and known in advance. Specifically, the worst-case latency = (current frame transmission time) + (one bit time for resynchronization). This is **deterministic** and is why CAN is classified as a **real-time bus**.

Contrast with Ethernet: collisions cause both frames to be aborted, and retransmission is non-deterministic (exponential backoff). CAN is therefore preferred for **safety-critical** systems like ABS and airbag controllers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU students make in CAN exam questions (and how to avoid them):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting that BRP is an integer** — Students often write $BRP = 4.44$, which is wrong. Always round to the nearest integer and **explicitly calculate the baud-rate error**. If error > 1.5 %, re-tune.
> 2. **Confusing arbitration field with message ID** — The arbitration field = ID + RTR (+ IDE + SRR for extended). The "message ID" alone is 11 or 29 bits.
> 3. **Confusing CAN with addressing protocols** — CAN is **message-addressed**, not node-addressed. Do not say "Node 2 received the message addressed to it." Instead say "**Node 2's filter accepted the message with ID 0x123**."
> 4. **SJW outside valid range** — $SJW$ must be $\geq 1$ and $\leq \min(4, t_{Seg2})$. Forgetting this loses 2 marks.
> 5. **Sample point outside 75 %–87.5 %** — KTU examiners specifically check this. A sample point of 50 % is technically valid but unreliable; 80 %–87.5 % is preferred.
> 6. **Forgetting the ACK bit logic** — A common essay question. The **transmitter** sends a **recessive** ACK slot. **Any receiver** that successfully received the frame overwrites it with **dominant**. The transmitter treats a dominant ACK as confirmation of successful delivery.
> 7. **Forgetting to mention termination resistors** — In a 3-node network, only the two **end nodes** have 120 Ω resistors. Middle nodes do **not** have termination.
> 8. **Not specifying "Standard vs Extended ID"** — Always state which type of identifier you are using.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — MUST memorize for KTU 2024 Board Exam:**
>
> ✅ **CAN is multi-master, message-addressed, differential, asynchronous.**
> ✅ **ISO 11898-2** is the high-speed physical layer standard; **ISO 11898-1** is the data link layer.
> ✅ **Classical CAN:** up to 1 Mbps, 8-byte payload. **CAN FD:** up to 5 Mbps in data phase, 64-byte payload.
> ✅ **Baud rate formula:** $f_{baud} = f_{FDCAN\_CLK} / [(BRP + 1) \cdot N_{BT}]$.
> ✅ **Recommended sample point:** 75 % to 87.5 % of the bit time.
> ✅ **Bit stuffing rule:** After every 5 identical bits, stuff a complementary bit.
> ✅ **Arbitration:** Dominant bit (0) wins. Lower ID = higher priority. **Non-destructive.**
> ✅ **ACK mechanism:** Transmitter sends recessive; any receiver overwrites with dominant.
> ✅ **Termination:** Two **120 Ω** resistors at the **two ends** of the bus only. Total parallel = 60 Ω.
> ✅ **STM32U575 FDCAN peripheral:** Replaces bxCAN. Has Tx FIFO/Queue, Rx FIFO 0/1, separate standard (28) and extended (8) filter banks.
> ✅ **Pin assignments (FDCAN1):** PA11 (RX), PA12 (TX) in AF9 mode. Alternative: PB8, PB9.
> ✅ **External transceiver required** (e.g., TJA1050, SN65HVD230, MCP2551) — STM32U575 outputs are TTL, not differential.
> ✅ **Error handling:** CAN hardware automatically retransmits on bit/stuff/CRC errors. A node that exceeds 255 errors goes **Error Passive**, then **Bus Off**.
> ✅ **Filters:** Range, Exact ID, Classic (mask + ID), and List (multiple IDs in one filter). Destination: Rx FIFO 0, Rx FIFO 1, or reject.
> ✅ **Filter registers:** `FDCAN_SIDFC`, `FDCAN_XIDFC`, `FDCAN_FLSSA`, `FDCAN_FLSEA` (filter RAM regions).
> ✅ **Bit time formula:** $N_{BT} = t_{Sync} + t_{Prop} + t_{Seg1} + t_{Seg2}$, where $t_{Sync}$ is fixed at 1 $t_q$.
> ✅ **Initialization sequence:** Clock → GPIO → Filters → Bit Timing → Exit Init Mode → Enable Interrupts → Application.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating a USB HID Device for Keyboard/Mouse Emulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Creating a USB HID Device for Keyboard/Mouse Emulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **USB HID (Human Interface Device)** is a standardized device class defined by the **USB Implementers Forum (USB-IF)** under the **HID Specification 1.11**, which allows a peripheral to communicate with a host computer using predefined descriptors. **HID Emulation** refers to the technique of programming a microcontroller to enumerate and behave as a keyboard, mouse, joystick, or any other HID-compliant device, transmitting structured data packets called **HID Reports** to the host.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — HID Class**
> The HID class is one of the earliest USB device classes (introduced with USB 1.1, 1998). It was designed for devices that interact directly with humans — keyboards, mice, game controllers, digitizers — but it is now used for almost any low-bandwidth device (barcode scanners, custom control panels, CNC pendants) because the host-side driver is **built into the operating system**, requiring no custom driver installation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why HID Emulation Matters in KTU Microcontrollers (Module 3 context)**
> Most modern 32-bit microcontrollers (STM32, SAMD, RP2040, ESP32-S2/S3) include a **USB 2.0 Full-Speed device peripheral**. By loading the HID class driver into firmware, the MCU can talk to a PC using only the **OS-native HID driver** — no `.inf` files, no manufacturer-supplied drivers, no signed driver packages. This is the single most important practical reason HID emulation is taught in MCU courses.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **puppeteer (microcontroller)** controlling a **marionette (the PC cursor and keyboard buffer)**. You cannot touch the puppet's body directly; instead, you can only pull certain labeled strings:

- **String 1** = "Type the letter 'A'"
- **String 2** = "Move the mouse 5 pixels right and click the left button"

In HID, these "strings" are **report fields**, and the precise names, lengths, and purposes of every string are described in advance in a document called the **HID Report Descriptor**. The host reads this descriptor once during enumeration (the moment the device is plugged in), and from then on it knows exactly how to interpret every byte you send down the USB pipe.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world application analogy**
> Think of a **BadUSB / Rubber Ducky** attack device. It is a small MCU board programmed as a HID keyboard that "types" pre-stored keystrokes (opening a terminal, running a command) in seconds. The same principle, used ethically, powers **programmable macro pads, presentation clickers, custom industrial control pendants, automotive diagnostics dongles (OBD-II ELM327)**, and **ATtiny85-based presentation tools (e.g., DigiSpark)**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology and Physical/Logical Constants

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Enumeration** | The handshake process where the host learns the device's descriptors. |
| **Endpoint** | A logical unidirectional data pipe (e.g., EP1 OUT, EP1 IN). HID uses **1 IN endpoint** (interrupt). |
| **Report** | A binary data packet sent from device → host (IN) or host → device (OUT). |
| **Boot Protocol** | A simplified, fixed-length report format (8-byte keyboard, 3-byte mouse) used for BIOS compatibility. |
| **Report Protocol** | A flexible, descriptor-defined report format. **Default and most widely used.** |
| **Polling Interval** | How often the host will poll the device for new reports, in **milliseconds (1 ms – 255 ms)**. |
| **bInterval** | The descriptor field specifying the polling interval. The HID spec minimum is **1 ms for Full-Speed**. |
| **HID Page / Usage** | A standardized 16-bit identifier telling the host *what* the data represents (keyboard, mouse, digitizer, etc.). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard HID Constants Used in the Specification**
> - `bInterval` minimum for **Full-Speed** USB = **1 ms** (HID 1.11, Section 6.2)
> - HID keyboard **boot report length** = **8 bytes** (modifier + reserved + 6 keycodes)
> - HID mouse **boot report length** = **3 bytes** (buttons + X + Y)
> - Default endpoint type for HID = **Interrupt** (not bulk, not isochronous — for guaranteed low latency)
> - USB-IF vendor IDs (VID) and product IDs (PID) are 16-bit each; for hobby use, `VID = 0x1209` (pid.codes) is a free community-allocated pool.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 How USB HID Enumeration Works (Operational Theory)

The enumeration process is a **stateful, multi-stage handshake** executed in milliseconds the moment the device is plugged in. From the MCU's perspective, every step is a callback in the USB device stack:

1. **VBUS Sense (Hardware Layer):** The MCU detects **+5 V on the VBUS pin** and asserts the **D+ pull-up resistor** (1.5 kΩ to 3.3 V) to signal a Full-Speed connection. Without this, the host sees an unconnected port.
2. **Bus Reset:** The host drives both D+ and D− low for **≥ 10 ms**, resetting the device's address to 0.
3. **GET_DESCRIPTOR (Device Descriptor):** The host requests 18 bytes — VID, PID, bDeviceClass (must be **0x00** to indicate "use interface descriptors"), bMaxPacketSize (typically 8 or 64).
4. **SET_ADDRESS:** The host assigns a unique 7-bit address (1–127).
5. **GET_DESCRIPTOR (Configuration Descriptor):** Returns interface count, power requirements (in **2 mA units**, max **500 mA**), and the **HID interface descriptor**.
6. **GET_DESCRIPTOR (HID Descriptor):** 9-byte structure declaring `bcdHID` (e.g., 0x0111 for HID 1.11), HID report descriptor length, and an optional physical descriptor.
7. **GET_DESCRIPTOR (HID Report Descriptor):** The most important step — the device sends the **descriptor that defines the shape of every report**. The host's HID parser builds an internal data tree from this.
8. **SET_CONFIGURATION:** Host sends a "ready" signal. The device is now operational and can send IN-report packets on its interrupt endpoint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight**
> Steps 3–7 happen entirely from **device descriptors in flash memory**. The firmware developer must encode these correctly *once*; they are not generated at runtime. A single wrong byte (wrong `bcdHID` or wrong report descriptor) causes the host to reject the device and show **"USB Device Not Recognized"** in Windows, or simply ignore the device on Linux/macOS.

## 2.2 The HID Report Descriptor — Conceptual Breakdown

The HID Report Descriptor is a **stream of items**, each defined in the HID 1.11 specification, Section 6.2.2. Each item has the structure:

$$\text{Item} = \underbrace{\text{1 byte prefix}}_{\text{size, type, tag}} \;\; + \;\; \underbrace{\text{0, 1, 2, or 4 bytes of data}}_{\text{payload}}$$

| Prefix Bit 7–4 (bSize) | Prefix Bit 3–2 (bType) | Prefix Bit 1–0 (bTag) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 = 0 bytes  | 00 = Main | 00–11 = tag number |
| 1 = 1 byte   | 01 = Global | |
| 2 = 2 bytes  | 10 = Local | |
| 3 = 4 bytes  | 11 = Reserved | |

There are three item types:

- **Main items** — define or close a **collection** (data groupings like a keyboard's key array) and add report **fields** (`Input`, `Output`, `Feature`).
- **Global items** — affect *all subsequent* items (e.g., `USAGE_PAGE`, `LOGICAL_MINIMUM`, `REPORT_COUNT`).
- **Local items** — affect *only the next* main item (e.g., `USAGE`, `USAGE_MINIMUM`).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below summarizes the **mathematical and structural quantities** that appear in HID report design, descriptor byte-counting, and polling-rate calculations. Memorize these — they appear in numerical questions.

| Quantity | Formula / Value | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Report size per field (in bits) | $S$ | bits | Set by `REPORT_SIZE` item |
| Number of fields in a report | $N$ | unitless | Set by `REPORT_COUNT` item |
| Total report length in bits | $L_{bits} = S \times N$ | bits | Used to compute descriptor length |
| Total report length in bytes | $L_{bytes} = \lceil L_{bits} / 8 \rceil$ | bytes | The actual USB payload size |
| Polling interval (max latency) | $t_{poll} = bInterval \times 1\,\text{ms}$ | ms | For Full-Speed USB |
| Throughput | $\Theta = \dfrac{L_{bytes}}{t_{poll}}$ | bytes/s | Maximum HID IN bandwidth |
| Max theoretical HID bandwidth | $\Theta_{max} = \dfrac{64 \times 1000}{1} = 64000$ | bytes/s | 64-byte reports at 1 ms polling |
| Endpoint maximum packet size (FS) | 64 | bytes | From `wMaxPacketSize` field |
| Power consumption unit | $P = 100 \times (bMaxPower - 1)$ | mA | $bMaxPower = 1$ means 0 mA; max is 500 mA |
| Boot keyboard report | 8 | bytes | Mandatory fixed format |
| Boot mouse report | 3 | bytes | Mandatory fixed format |

**Mouse movement encoding (most common convention):**
$$\Delta x_{reported} = \text{signed byte range } [-127, +127]$$
$$\Delta y_{reported} = \text{signed byte range } [-127, +127]$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Use `\vert` in LaTeX, not `\|` or `|`, inside markdown tables to avoid table breakage.** This rule is enforced throughout these notes.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | HID Use Case |
|---|---|
| **Penetration Testing (Offensive Security)** | "Rubber Ducky" USB attack tools that type pre-loaded scripts. |
| **Industrial Automation** | Custom machine pendants (start/stop/feed-hold) emulating a 16-button HID keypad. |
| **Assistive Technology** | Sip-and-puff switches emulating mouse clicks for users with motor impairments. |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Programmable macro pads, stream decks, presentation clickers. |
| **Automotive Diagnostics** | ELM327-compatible OBD-II adapters that emulate a HID-class serial-over-USB. |
| **Embedded Education** | STM32/Arduino boards used to teach the full USB protocol stack. |
| **Medical Devices** | Foot-pedal scanners, custom dental-chair controls emulating keyboards. |
| **Game Development** | Custom arcade fight sticks, flight sim button boxes. |

> [!TIP]
> **Why HID is preferred over CDC (Virtual COM Port) or Vendor-class**
> 1. **Zero driver install** — the OS has a HID driver built in.
> 2. **Predictable latency** — interrupt endpoint guarantees the host polls the device.
> 3. **Cross-platform** — Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and even some smart TVs all support HID natively.
> 4. **No need for signed drivers** — saves a ~$300/year Microsoft WHQL cost for small manufacturers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Computing the HID Report Descriptor Length

**Problem (Sample numerical — KTU-style):** A designer writes a HID Report Descriptor containing **2 Input fields** — one of **3 bits** and one of **5 bits** — followed by a 1-byte `INPUT` main item and a 1-byte `END_COLLECTION`. Compute the descriptor length in bytes and verify it against the `wReportDescriptorLength` field of the HID descriptor.

### Step-by-step solution:

**Step 1 — Sum the bit lengths of all data fields.**
$$L_{bits} = S_1 \times N_1 + S_2 \times N_2 = 3 \times 1 + 5 \times 1 = 8 \,\text{bits}$$

**Step 2 — Convert bits to bytes (ceiling division).**
$$L_{bytes} = \left\lceil \frac{L_{bits}}{8} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{8}{8} \right\rceil = 1 \,\text{byte}$$

**Step 3 — Count the descriptor items and their prefix sizes.**

| Item | Prefix (bSize) | Payload | Item length |
|---|---|---|---|
| `USAGE_PAGE(Generic Desktop)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `USAGE(Mouse)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `COLLECTION(Application)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `USAGE(Pointer)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `COLLECTION(Physical)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `USAGE_PAGE(Buttons)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `USAGE_MINIMUM(Button 1)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `USAGE_MAXIMUM(Button 3)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `LOGICAL_MINIMUM(0)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `LOGICAL_MAXIMUM(1)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `REPORT_COUNT(3)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `REPORT_SIZE(1)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `INPUT(Data,Variable,Absolute)` | 1 byte | 0 bytes | 1 byte |
| `REPORT_COUNT(1)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `REPORT_SIZE(5)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `INPUT(Constant)` | 1 byte | 0 bytes | 1 byte |
| `USAGE_PAGE(Generic Desktop)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `USAGE(X)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `USAGE(Y)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `LOGICAL_MINIMUM(-127)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `LOGICAL_MAXIMUM(127)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `REPORT_SIZE(8)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `REPORT_COUNT(2)` | 1 byte | 1 byte | 2 bytes |
| `INPUT(Data,Variable,Relative)` | 1 byte | 0 bytes | 1 byte |
| `END_COLLECTION` (Physical) | 1 byte | 0 bytes | 1 byte |
| `END_COLLECTION` (Application) | 1 byte | 0 bytes | 1 byte |

**Step 4 — Sum the bytes.**

$$L_{descriptor} = 12 \times 2 + 14 \times 2 + 4 \times 1 = 24 + 28 + 4 = 56 \,\text{bytes}$$

**Step 5 — Verify against `wReportDescriptorLength` in the HID descriptor.**

The host sends `GET_DESCRIPTOR` for the HID report descriptor and expects exactly **56 bytes** (little-endian). The MCU's descriptor table must return this many bytes or the host will stall the control endpoint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight — automatic length checking**
> The USB host enforces strict byte counts. If your firmware accidentally returns 55 bytes, the control transfer ends with a short packet and the host flags an error. **Always hand-calculate descriptor length during development.**

## 3.2 Derivation: Throughput Limit of a Full-Speed HID Mouse

**Problem:** A 4-byte HID mouse report (buttons, X, Y, wheel) is sent every **2 ms** (bInterval = 2). Compute the maximum throughput in bytes per second and verify it is below the USB Full-Speed limit.

### Step-by-step solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the report size and polling interval.**
$$L_{bytes} = 4 \,\,\, ; \,\,\, t_{poll} = 2\,\text{ms} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the throughput formula.**

$$\Theta = \frac{L_{bytes}}{t_{poll}} = \frac{4}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 2000 \,\text{bytes/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compare against the theoretical Full-Speed HID ceiling.**

$$\Theta_{max} = \frac{L_{max}}{t_{poll,min}} = \frac{64 \text{ bytes}}{1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}} = 64{,}000 \,\text{bytes/s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the headroom (utilization).**

$$U = \frac{\Theta}{\Theta_{max}} = \frac{2000}{64000} = 0.03125 = 3.125\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world meaning**
> A typical mouse uses only **~3% of available HID bandwidth**. The remaining 97% is intentionally unused so that multiple HID devices on a hub (keyboards, mice, tablets, headsets) do not compete for the bus. This is why a wireless mouse, even with 1000 Hz polling, performs flawlessly on a shared hub.

## 3.3 Derivation: Bit-Packing a Boot-Protocol Keyboard Report

**Problem:** A "Shift + A" keypress must be sent as a boot-protocol keyboard report. Construct the 8-byte packet using the standard HID boot keyboard layout.

### Step-by-step solution:

**Step 1 — Recall the boot keyboard report layout.**

$$\text{Report} = \underbrace{B_0}_{\text{Modifier}} \;\; \underbrace{B_1}_{\text{Reserved}} \;\; \underbrace{B_2 - B_7}_{\text{Up to 6 simultaneous keycodes}}$$

**Step 2 — Look up the modifier bit for Left-Shift in the HID Usage Tables (Keyboard/Keypad page 0x07).**

$$B_0 = 0\text{x}02 \;\;\text{(bit 1 = Left Shift)}$$

**Step 3 — Look up the HID Usage ID for the 'a' key.**

$$B_2 = 0\text{x}04 \;\;\text{(HID Usage ID 0x04 = 'a')}$$

**Step 4 — Fill remaining bytes with `0x00` (zero means "no key").**

**Step 5 — Write the final 8-byte report as a hex sequence.**

$$B_0 B_1 B_2 B_3 B_4 B_5 B_6 B_7 = 0\text{x}02 \;\; 0\text{x}00 \;\; 0\text{x}04 \;\; 0\text{x}00 \;\; 0\text{x}00 \;\; 0\text{x}00 \;\; 0\text{x}00 \;\; 0\text{x}00$$

**Step 6 — Key release report (all zeros, no keys, no modifiers).**

$$B_0 B_1 B_2 \ldots B_7 = 0\text{x}00 \;\; 0\text{x}00 \;\; 0\text{x}00 \;\; 0\text{x}00 \;\; 0\text{x}00 \;\; 0\text{x}00 \;\; 0\text{x}00 \;\; 0\text{x}00$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the key release matters in HID**
> USB HID reports are **state-based**, not event-based. A keystroke is "held" until you send a report with `0x00` in the keycode slots. Failing to send a release packet results in a "stuck key" — the host will keep generating the character or modifier state indefinitely. This is the **#1 source of bugs** in beginner HID emulator firmware.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Emulation Strategies

| Strategy | Pros | Cons | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Raw bit-banging of USB D+/D−** (e.g., V-USB on ATtiny85) | Works on chips without USB hardware | Slow, high CPU load, no DMA, no plug-and-play reliability | DigiSpark, hobby projects |
| **Hardware USB + TinyUSB stack** (e.g., RP2040, ESP32-S2/S3) | Open source, supports HID, MSC, CDC, MIDI, etc. | Requires MCU with native USB | Modern hobby/industrial |
| **Hardware USB + STMicro's MX USB stack** (STM32) | Production-grade, supported by ST | Closed source, steeper learning curve | STM32 commercial products |
| **Arduino HID library (NicoHood)** | Plug-and-play on ATmega32U4 | Limited to one device class per upload | Arduino Leonardo/Micro |
| **Boot Protocol only (e.g., old BIOS)** | Tiny descriptor, BIOS-compatible | Fixed 8-byte keyboard, 3-byte mouse | KVM switches, BIOS tools |
| **Report Protocol (modern)** | Customizable keys, media keys, consumer controls | Larger descriptor, more complex | All modern OS environments |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 USB HID Enumeration & Runtime Communication Flow

The following Mermaid block renders the **complete block-level functional architecture** of a USB HID device, from physical plug-in through runtime report exchange. This is the diagram KTU examiners most frequently expect for questions on the topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MCU Power On / USB Cable Plugged In] --> B[VBUS Voltage Detected on PA12 / VBUS Pin]
    B --> C[D+ Pullup Resistor 1.5 kOhm Enabled]
    C --> D[Host Issues USB Bus Reset >= 10 ms]
    D --> E[Host Sends GET_DESCRIPTOR Device Descriptor 18 bytes]
    E --> F[Host Sends SET_ADDRESS assigns 7 bit address]
    F --> G[Host Sends GET_DESCRIPTOR Configuration Descriptor]
    G --> H[Host Sends GET_DESCRIPTOR HID Descriptor 9 bytes]
    H --> I[Host Sends GET_DESCRIPTOR HID Report Descriptor]
    I --> J[Host Sends SET_CONFIGURATION device is now ready]
    J --> K[Application Code Enters Main Loop]
    K --> L{User Input Event or Timer}
    L -- Button Press --> M[Construct HID Report Buffer e.g. 8 byte keyboard]
    L -- Sensor Data --> N[Construct Mouse Report 3 to 5 bytes]
    M --> O[Call HID Send Report API USBD_HID_SendReport]
    N --> O
    O --> P[USB Interrupt Endpoint IN EP1]
    P --> Q[Host HID Driver Parses Report]
    Q --> R[OS Updates Keyboard Buffer or Cursor Position]
    R --> K
```

## 4.2 HID Report Descriptor — Conceptual Tree Structure

The HID Report Descriptor is a flat byte stream, but the host's parser builds an **internal tree of collections**. The diagram below shows the structure of a combined keyboard + mouse descriptor.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HID Report Descriptor Top Level] --> B[COLLECTION Application Generic Desktop Combo Device]
    B --> C[COLLECTION Physical Keyboard]
    C --> C1[Report Field Modifier Byte 1 byte 8 bits]
    C --> C2[Report Field Reserved 1 byte]
    C --> C3[Report Field Key Array 6 bytes up to 6 simultaneous keys]
    B --> D[COLLECTION Physical Mouse]
    D --> D1[Report Field Buttons 1 byte bit 0 left bit 1 right bit 2 middle]
    D --> D2[Report Field X Delta 1 byte signed range -127 to +127]
    D --> D3[Report Field Y Delta 1 byte signed range -127 to +127]
    D --> D4[Report Field Wheel Optional 1 byte signed range -127 to +127]
    B --> E[END_COLLECTION closes Application]
```

## 4.3 Report-Construction State Machine (Firmware Side)

On the firmware side, the application must follow a **strict state machine** to ensure reports are never sent while the previous report's data is still in transit. This is implemented by checking the `HID_IDLE` state or the endpoint's `Busy` flag.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> BuildReport : Button / Sensor Triggered
    BuildReport --> EndpointBusyCheck : Buffer Populated
    EndpointBusyCheck --> WaitingForAck : Endpoint Busy
    EndpointBusyCheck --> SendReport : Endpoint Free
    WaitingForAck --> SendReport : Interrupt IN Callback Fires
    SendReport --> WaitForCompletion : Report Loaded into EP1 IN
    WaitForCompletion --> Idle : USB Transfer Complete Callback
    SendReport --> BuildReport : Application Queues Another Report
```

## 4.4 Layered Block Architecture (Where Each Piece Lives in a Real MCU Firmware)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Application Layer user code] --> L2[HID Device Driver Layer TinyUSB ST USB MX]
    L2 --> L3[USB Device Peripheral Driver NVIC DMA Setup]
    L3 --> L4[USB Hardware IP Block on MCU die]
    L4 --> L5[Physical Layer Differential D+ D- lines]
    L5 --> L6[USB Host Controller on PC]
    L6 --> L7[OS HID Class Driver native builtin]
    L7 --> L8[Application Layer on PC receives keystroke or cursor move]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this layered view is important for KTU theory questions**
> Examiners often ask: "What happens if the USB D+ pull-up is not enabled?" or "At which layer does the HID Report Descriptor get parsed?" The block diagram above is your mental map. **The descriptor is parsed at the OS HID driver layer**, the pull-up is a physical layer concern, and the report *content* is generated at the application layer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2-Mark and 3-Mark Short Answers)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, 3 Marks]**

**Define USB HID class. Why is it preferred over CDC (Communication Device Class) for simple human-interface peripherals?**

**Model Answer:**

USB HID (Human Interface Device) is a standardized device class defined by the **USB Implementers Forum**, used for devices that transfer small, latency-sensitive data such as keyboard, mouse, and joystick input. Each HID device enumerates using a **HID Report Descriptor** that the host OS parses at connection time.

HID is preferred over CDC for human-interface peripherals for three reasons:

1. **Zero driver installation** — Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android include native HID drivers; CDC requires a `.inf` file or signed virtual COM port driver.
2. **Guaranteed low latency** — HID uses an **interrupt endpoint** with a `bInterval` as low as 1 ms, ensuring predictable polling; CDC uses bulk endpoints with no latency guarantee.
3. **Cross-platform uniformity** — A single HID descriptor works on every modern OS, while CDC behavior varies (Linux exposes `/dev/ttyACM0`, Windows creates a virtual COM port that may need manual setup).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, 3 Marks]**

**What is the difference between USB HID Boot Protocol and Report Protocol? When would you choose Boot Protocol?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Boot Protocol | Report Protocol |
|---|---|---|
| Descriptor | Fixed (no report descriptor) | Custom (descriptor-driven) |
| Keyboard report length | 8 bytes (modifier + reserved + 6 keys) | Customizable |
| Mouse report length | 3 bytes (buttons + X + Y) | Customizable |
| Host support | BIOS, UEFI, and OS | OS only (not BIOS) |
| Flexibility | None — fixed layout | Full — designer defines fields |
| Default in modern OS | Disabled by default | **Enabled by default** |

**Boot Protocol is chosen** when the device must work in environments without a full HID driver loaded — typically **BIOS/UEFI setup screens, bootloaders, KVM switches**, or any pre-OS environment. Modern operating systems use Report Protocol exclusively.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Format Reminder**
> Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full on the answer script. Internal choice is mandatory.

### **Question A (14 Marks) — HID Keyboard Emulation Deep Dive**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw and explain the complete HID Report Descriptor for a **USB boot-protocol keyboard**. List every item byte-by-byte and state the purpose of each Main, Global, and Local item used.

**Model Solution (a):**

The boot-protocol keyboard descriptor is a fixed, well-known sequence. It must declare 8 bytes of data per report in the order: **Modifier, Reserved, Keycode × 6**.

| # | Item (Mnemonic) | Hex Bytes | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `USAGE_PAGE(Generic Desktop)` | `0x05 0x01` | Global: page = Generic Desktop (0x01) |
| 2 | `USAGE(Keyboard)` | `0x09 0x06` | Local: device is a keyboard |
| 3 | `COLLECTION(Application)` | `0xA1 0x01` | Main: open Application collection |
| 4 | `USAGE_PAGE(Keyboard/Keypad)` | `0x05 0x07` | Global: page = Key codes |
| 5 | `USAGE_MINIMUM(Left Ctrl)` | `0x19 0xE0` | Local: first modifier = 0xE0 |
| 6 | `USAGE_MAXIMUM(Right GUI)` | `0x29 0xE7` | Local: last modifier = 0xE7 |
| 7 | `LOGICAL_MINIMUM(0)` | `0x15 0x00` | Global: min value = 0 |
| 8 | `LOGICAL_MAXIMUM(1)` | `0x25 0x01` | Global: max value = 1 |
| 9 | `REPORT_COUNT(8)` | `0x95 0x08` | Global: 8 fields |
| 10 | `REPORT_SIZE(1)` | `0x75 0x01` | Global: each field is 1 bit |
| 11 | `INPUT(Data,Variable,Absolute)` | `0x81 0x02` | Main: 8 modifier bits |
| 12 | `REPORT_COUNT(1)` | `0x95 0x01` | Global: 1 field |
| 13 | `REPORT_SIZE(8)` | `0x75 0x08` | Global: 8 bits (1 byte) |
| 14 | `INPUT(Constant)` | `0x81 0x01` | Main: reserved padding byte |
| 15 | `REPORT_COUNT(5)` | `0x95 0x05` | Global: 5 fields |
| 16 | `REPORT_SIZE(1)` | `0x75 0x01` | Global: each 1 bit (LED report) |
| 17 | `OUTPUT(Data,Variable,Absolute)` | `0x91 0x02` | Main: Num/Caps/Scroll lock LEDs |
| 18 | `REPORT_COUNT(1)` | `0x95 0x01` | Global: 1 field |
| 19 | `REPORT_SIZE(3)` | `0x75 0x03` | Global: 3-bit padding |
| 20 | `OUTPUT(Constant)` | `0x91 0x01` | Main: LED-report padding |
| 21 | `REPORT_COUNT(6)` | `0x95 0x06` | Global: 6 simultaneous keys |
| 22 | `REPORT_SIZE(8)` | `0x75 0x08` | Global: 8 bits per keycode |
| 23 | `LOGICAL_MINIMUM(0)` | `0x15 0x00` | Global: min keycode = 0 |
| 24 | `LOGICAL_MAXIMUM(101)` | `0x25 0x65` | Global: max keycode = 0x65 (101) |
| 25 | `USAGE_PAGE(Keyboard/Keypad)` | `0x05 0x07` | Global: page re-asserted |
| 26 | `USAGE_MINIMUM(0)` | `0x19 0x00` | Local: min usage |
| 27 | `USAGE_MAXIMUM(101)` | `0x29 0x65` | Local: max usage |
| 28 | `INPUT(Data,Array,Absolute)` | `0x81 0x00` | Main: 6-key keycode array |
| 29 | `END_COLLECTION` | `0xC0` | Main: close Application |

**Total: 65 bytes** (the canonical USB-IF boot keyboard descriptor).

> [!NOTE]
> The student must be able to identify that **items 5–11** define the **modifier byte**, **items 12–14** define the **reserved byte**, and **items 21–28** define the **6-key array**. In the exam, examiners commonly ask: "What does `0x81 0x02` mean?" Answer: `0x81` is the prefix for an `INPUT` main item, `0x02` means `Data | Variable | Absolute` (the 3-bit constant `0b010`).

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write the **8-byte HID report** that the MCU must send to the host to type the capital letter **'K'** while the **Right Shift** key is held. Show both the **press report** and the **release report**, and explain the bit layout of the modifier byte.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Find the HID Usage ID for 'k'.**
Looking up the HID Usage Tables, "Keyboard k and K" is **Usage ID 0x0E**.

**Step 2 — Find the bit position for Right Shift in the modifier byte.**
The modifier byte follows the standard USB HID keyboard layout (byte 0 of the report):

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Modifier | Right GUI | Right Alt | Right Shift | Right Ctrl | Left GUI | Left Alt | **Left Shift** | Left Ctrl |

Right Shift = **bit 5**, so the modifier byte is `1 << 5 = 0x20`.

**Step 3 — Construct the 8-byte press report.**

$$\text{Report}_{press} = [0\text{x}20,\; 0\text{x}00,\; 0\text{x}0E,\; 0\text{x}00,\; 0\text{x}00,\; 0\text{x}00,\; 0\text{x}00,\; 0\text{x}00]$$

| Byte | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Value | `0x20` | `0x00` | `0x0E` | `0x00` | `0x00` | `0x00` | `0x00` | `0x00` |
| Meaning | Right Shift held | Reserved | 'k' key held | Empty | Empty | Empty | Empty | Empty |

**Step 4 — Construct the 8-byte release report (all zeros).**

$$\text{Report}_{release} = [0\text{x}00,\; 0\text{x}00,\; 0\text{x}00,\; 0\text{x}00,\; 0\text{x}00,\; 0\text{x}00,\; 0\text{x}00,\; 0\text{x}00]$$

**Step 5 — Sequential send procedure (firmware pseudo-code).**

1. MCU constructs `report_press` and calls `HID_SendReport(report_press, 8)`.
2. Wait 10–50 ms to ensure the host samples the report.
3. MCU constructs `report_release` (all zeros) and sends it.
4. The host's HID driver produces the capital **'K'** character exactly once.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "release" matters**
> Omitting step 3 causes the host to see a "stuck key" — the 'K' will auto-repeat, and the Right Shift will appear held down, capitalizing every subsequent character. This is the **single most common bug** in student HID projects.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — HID Mouse Emulation & Polling Math**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the HID Report Descriptor for a **3-button mouse with X and Y movement** and explain every field, with a clear table of usage codes.

**Model Solution (a):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[USAGE_PAGE Generic Desktop 0x01] --> M2[USAGE Mouse 0x02]
    M2 --> M3[COLLECTION Application 0xA1 0x01]
    M3 --> M4[USAGE_PAGE Buttons 0x09]
    M4 --> M5[USAGE_MINIMUM Button 1 0x01]
    M5 --> M6[USAGE_MAXIMUM Button 3 0x03]
    M6 --> M7[LOGICAL_MINIMUM 0 0x00]
    M7 --> M8[LOGICAL_MAXIMUM 1 0x01]
    M8 --> M9[REPORT_COUNT 3 0x03]
    M9 --> M10[REPORT_SIZE 1 0x01]
    M10 --> M11[INPUT Data Variable Absolute 0x81 0x02]
    M11 --> M12[REPORT_COUNT 1 0x01]
    M12 --> M13[REPORT_SIZE 5 0x05]
    M13 --> M14[INPUT Constant padding 0x81 0x01]
    M14 --> M15[USAGE_PAGE Generic Desktop 0x01]
    M15 --> M16[USAGE X 0x30]
    M16 --> M17[USAGE Y 0x31]
    M17 --> M18[LOGICAL_MINIMUM -127 0x81]
    M18 --> M19[LOGICAL_MAXIMUM 127 0x7F]
    M19 --> M20[REPORT_SIZE 8 0x08]
    M20 --> M21[REPORT_COUNT 2 0x02]
    M21 --> M22[INPUT Data Variable Relative 0x81 0x06]
    M22 --> M23[END_COLLECTION 0xC0]
```

**Field-by-field explanation table:**

| Descriptor Field | Type | Value | Explanation |
|---|---|---|---|
| `USAGE_PAGE` | Global | `0x01` | Generic Desktop page (mouse-related usages live here) |
| `USAGE` | Local | `0x02` (Mouse) | This whole collection is a mouse |
| `COLLECTION(Application)` | Main | `0xA1 0x01` | Top-level grouping; recognized by host as a "device class" |
| `USAGE_PAGE(Buttons)` | Global | `0x09` | Switch to button page |
| `USAGE_MINIMUM / MAXIMUM` | Local | `0x01` / `0x03` | Buttons 1, 2, 3 (left, right, middle) |
| `LOGICAL_MIN/MAX` | Global | `0` / `1` | Each button is a single bit: 0 = released, 1 = pressed |
| `REPORT_COUNT` | Global | `3` | 3 button fields |
| `REPORT_SIZE` | Global | `1` | Each field is 1 bit |
| `INPUT(0x02)` | Main | Data,Variable,Absolute | 3-bit button field |
| Padding field | — | `REPORT_COUNT(1) REPORT_SIZE(5) INPUT(Constant)` | Pad to a full byte |
| `USAGE_PAGE(Generic Desktop)` | Global | `0x01` | Switch back for X, Y |
| `USAGE(X)` | Local | `0x30` | First delta field is X |
| `USAGE(Y)` | Local | `0x31` | Second delta field is Y |
| `LOGICAL_MIN/MAX` | Global | `-127` / `+127` | Signed 8-bit movement range |
| `REPORT_SIZE(8) REPORT_COUNT(2)` | Global | — | Two 8-bit signed fields |
| `INPUT(0x06)` | Main | Data,Variable,**Relative** | Relative movement (delta) — distinguishes mouse from absolute digitizer |
| `END_COLLECTION` | Main | `0xC0` | Closes the Application collection |

**Final report layout (3 bytes):**

$$B_0 = \text{buttons}\;\vert\;\text{pad} \quad\quad B_1 = \Delta x \quad\quad B_2 = \Delta y$$

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wireless gaming mouse reports movement using a 4-byte HID report `[buttons, X, Y, wheel]` at a polling interval of **1 ms**. Compute (i) the **report throughput** in bytes/second, (ii) the **utilization** against the Full-Speed HID ceiling, and (iii) the **bit time** per byte assuming the device is the only one on the bus.

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Report throughput:**

$$L_{bytes} = 4 \text{ bytes} \quad ; \quad t_{poll} = 1 \text{ ms} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$

$$\Theta = \frac{L_{bytes}}{t_{poll}} = \frac{4}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 4000 \text{ bytes/s}$$

**(ii) Utilization against Full-Speed HID ceiling:**

$$\Theta_{max} = \frac{64 \text{ bytes}}{1 \text{ ms}} = 64{,}000 \text{ bytes/s}$$

$$U = \frac{\Theta}{\Theta_{max}} = \frac{4000}{64000} = 0.0625 = 6.25\%$$

**(iii) Bit time per byte on the wire:**

USB Full-Speed bit rate = **12 Mbps** = $12 \times 10^6$ bits/s.

$$T_{bit} = \frac{1}{12 \times 10^6} = 8.33 \times 10^{-8} \text{ s} = 83.3 \text{ ns}$$

Bytes per second on the wire:

$$f_{bytes} = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{8} = 1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s}$$

Time to transfer a single 4-byte report on the wire:

$$T_{report} = \frac{4 \text{ bytes}}{1.5 \times 10^6 \text{ bytes/s}} = 2.67 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 2.67 \,\mu\text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation**
> The 4-byte report occupies only **2.67 µs** of wire time, but the host polls every **1 ms** — meaning **99.7% of the polling interval is "silent"** on the bus. This enormous gap is what allows USB hubs to support many low-bandwidth devices (keyboards, mice, headsets) simultaneously without contention. The HID specification's interrupt endpoint model is therefore a *latency-prioritized*, not a *throughput-prioritized*, design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing HID class code (0x03) with interface class** — In the Device Descriptor, `bDeviceClass` must be `0x00` ("class specified at interface level"). The HID class code `0x03` appears in the **Interface Descriptor**, not the Device Descriptor. Setting `bDeviceClass = 0x03` is a frequent error and causes enumeration failure.
> 2. **Forgetting the report's release report** — Most students construct the "press" report correctly but fail to send the "release" report (all zeros), causing stuck keys/modifiers. **Always document both.**
> 3. **Wrong `bInterval` for Full-Speed vs High-Speed** — On Full-Speed, the interval is in **1 ms frames**; on High-Speed, it is in **125 µs microframes** (so `bInterval = 8` means 1 ms). Examiners sometimes test this distinction.
> 4. **Using `|x|` inside markdown tables** — The KTU system has a known parser issue where `|` inside tables breaks column alignment. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in LaTeX instead.
> 5. **Confusing `Data, Variable, Relative` (mouse) with `Data, Variable, Absolute` (digitizer/joystick)** — A mouse is **relative** (deltas from current position); a graphics tablet is **absolute** (coordinates within a virtual surface). Wrong choice causes the cursor to "jump to corner" or behave erratically.
> 6. **Omitting the `bcdHID` field in the HID descriptor** — This must be `0x0111` for HID 1.11. A wrong value causes the host to ignore the device.
> 7. **Not enabling the D+ pull-up** — On chips with software-controlled USB, the internal pull-up must be explicitly enabled. Forgetting this is the #1 reason "my MCU enumerates on breadboard but not in product".

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Memorize for KTU Exam**

- **HID = Human Interface Device**, a USB device class using **interrupt endpoints** with a 1-byte prefix item-encoding scheme.
- **Three item types**: Main (`A1`–`C0` prefix range), Global (`05`–`7F`), Local (`09`–`3F`).
- **Boot Protocol** = fixed 8-byte keyboard, 3-byte mouse, BIOS-compatible.
- **Report Protocol** = descriptor-driven, used by all modern OS, default in HID 1.11.
- **Keyboard report layout** = `[Modifier] [Reserved] [K1] [K2] [K3] [K4] [K5] [K6]`.
- **Modifier bits** = LCtrl (0x01), LShift (0x02), LAlt (0x04), LGUI (0x08), RCtrl (0x10), RShift (0x20), RAlt (0x40), RGUI (0x80).
- **Mouse report layout** = `[Buttons+pad] [X Δ] [Y Δ] [Wheel Δ optional]`.
- **Mouse movement range** = signed `-127` to `+127` per report; accumulating many small deltas gives smooth cursor motion.
- **HID Usage ID for 'a'** = `0x04`; **'A'** is also `0x04` (the modifier Shift makes it uppercase).
- **Descriptor length** = sum of (1-byte prefix + 0/1/2/4-byte payload) for every item; the host enforces this exactly.
- **Throughput formula**: $\Theta = L_{bytes} / t_{poll}$ ; **max FS HID** = 64,000 bytes/s.
- **Bit rate on the wire (Full-Speed)** = 12 Mbps; **bit time** = 83.3 ns; **byte time** = 667 ns.
- **Enumeration sequence**: VBUS sense → bus reset → GET_DESCRIPTOR (device) → SET_ADDRESS → GET_DESCRIPTOR (config) → GET_DESCRIPTOR (HID) → GET_DESCRIPTOR (HID report) → SET_CONFIGURATION.
- **`bInterval` (Full-Speed)** is in **milliseconds**; **High-Speed** is in **125 µs microframes**.
- **Endpoint type for HID** = **Interrupt** (guarantees polling), never bulk or isochronous.
- **HID report descriptor must always be sent as a control GET_DESCRIPTOR**; runtime data uses the interrupt IN endpoint.
- **VID/PID** are 16-bit each; for hobby use, use the **pid.codes (0x1209)** free pool.
- **Release report** = all-zero report; without it, the host sees a "stuck key" — most common student bug.
- **No driver install needed** for HID on Windows/macOS/Linux/Android — this is HID's biggest practical advantage.
- **Tooling**: Use the **USB-IF's HID Descriptor Tool** (Windows-only) or the online **USB Descriptor Viewer** to verify your descriptor before flashing.
- **KTU favourite questions**: (1) "Explain HID Report Descriptor for keyboard", (2) "Compare boot vs report protocol", (3) "What is enumeration?", (4) "Show the bit layout of the modifier byte", (5) "Compute HID throughput for a given polling interval".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless Communication and RTOS: Introduction to IoT
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wireless Communication and RTOS: Introduction to IoT

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Wireless Communication — Formal Definition
**Wireless Communication** in the context of embedded systems refers to the transfer of information between two or more points (a microcontroller, sensor node, or gateway) that are **not physically connected by a conductor**. Information is exchanged using **electromagnetic (EM) waves** that propagate through free space at the speed of light, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

In microcontroller-based systems, wireless communication is implemented through **transceivers** (transmitter + receiver ICs) such as the **nRF24L01 (2.4 GHz)**, **ESP8266 (Wi-Fi)**, **HC-05 (Bluetooth)**, and **XBee (ZigBee)** modules, which interface with the host MCU via serial peripheral protocols like **SPI**, **UART**, or **I2C**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Wireless communication is treated as a peripheral subsystem of the microcontroller — the focus is on *interfacing* transceivers, not on RF circuit design. You are expected to know protocols, frequency bands, and interfacing logic.

### 1.2 Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) — Formal Definition
A **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** is a specialized operating system designed to serve real-time applications where data processing must be completed within **strict, deterministic time bounds** (called *deadlines*). Unlike general-purpose OS (Windows, Linux), an RTOS guarantees a **maximum worst-case response time** for any task.

For microcontrollers, a typical RTOS exposes three core concepts:
- **Task** — an independent thread of execution (an infinite `while(1)` loop with its own stack)
- **Scheduler** — decides *which* task runs *when* (typically *preemptive priority-based*)
- **Kernel** — the minimal core that handles task switching, queues, semaphores

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (IEEE Standard):** A real-time system is one in which the correctness of the system depends not only on the logical result of computation, but **also on the time at which the results are produced**.

### 1.3 Internet of Things (IoT) — Formal Definition
The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a network of physical objects (*"things"*) — embedded with **sensors, software, electronics, and connectivity** — that enables these objects to **collect, exchange, and act on data** over the internet without requiring human-to-human or human-to-computer interaction.

The formal ITU-T Y.2060 definition states:
> *"A global infrastructure for the information society, enabling advanced services by interconnecting (physical and virtual) things based on existing and evolving interoperable information and communication technologies."*

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital" Intuition
Imagine a **smart hospital** as an analogy for IoT:
- **Patients (Things/Sensors):** Each patient has a wearable wristband (sensor node) measuring heart rate, temperature, and oxygen.
- **Walkie-Talkies (Wireless Communication):** Nurses carry wireless devices that receive patient vitals *over the air* — no wires tying patients to beds.
- **Triage Nurse (RTOS Kernel):** She continuously monitors which patient is most critical, which alarm is loudest, and reacts *within seconds* — she never sleeps, never gets distracted, and has hard rules about which emergency to handle first.
- **Central Server (Cloud/Internet):** All vitals stream to a central database where doctors analyze trends, predict episodes, and adjust treatment.
- **Alerts (Actuators):** When a patient's heart rate drops, an IV pump automatically increases dosage — *the system acts on its own*.

Just as the hospital runs **continuously**, **deterministically**, and **wirelessly**, an IoT system uses **sensors + wireless links + RTOS + internet** to do the same.

### 1.5 Key Physical / Standard Metrics
- Speed of EM wave: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s**
- Standard ISM bands used in microcontrollers: **2.4 GHz** (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, ZigBee), **868/915 MHz** (LoRa), **433 MHz** (ASK/FSK OOK)
- Typical RTOS tick rate: **1 ms (1 kHz)** to **10 ms (100 Hz)**
- IoT reference latency tolerance: **< 100 ms** for control loops, **< 1 s** for monitoring

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World IoT Examples:** Smart thermostats (Nest), fitness bands (Mi Band), industrial SCADA sensors, smart agriculture soil-moisture nodes, autonomous vehicle V2X communication, and smart electricity meters.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Wireless Communication — How It Works (Step by Step)

1. **Digital Data Generation:** The microcontroller produces a digital bitstream (e.g., temperature value `0x4A2F`).
2. **Baseband Encoding:** Bits are converted into a suitable baseband signal (NRZ, Manchester, or 8B/10B encoding) to ensure DC balance and clock recovery.
3. **Modulation:** The baseband signal modulates a high-frequency **carrier wave** $f_c$. Common schemes:
   - **ASK (Amplitude Shift Keying)** — simple, low power
   - **FSK (Frequency Shift Keying)** — robust to noise
   - **PSK (Phase Shift Keying)** — used in Wi-Fi/Bluetooth
   - **OFDM (Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing)** — used in Wi-Fi (802.11n/ac)
4. **Upconversion & Transmission:** The modulated carrier is amplified and radiated via an **antenna** (length typically $\lambda/4$, where $\lambda = c / f_c$).
5. **Propagation:** The EM wave travels through free space, undergoing **path loss**, **fading**, and **multipath**.
6. **Reception & Downconversion:** The receiver antenna captures the wave; the transceiver IC demodulates it back to bits.
7. **Data Handoff:** The received bytes are pushed to the MCU via SPI/UART and processed.

### 2.2 Why an RTOS? The "Why" Behind Scheduling

In a bare-metal (super-loop) microcontroller program, tasks run sequentially. If the controller is *blocking* on one slow task (e.g., waiting for a Wi-Fi response), no other task executes. This violates real-time guarantees.

**An RTOS solves this** by:
- **Preemptive scheduling:** The highest-priority *ready* task always runs.
- **Context switching:** Saving the current task's registers/stack to memory and loading the next task's context — happens in microseconds.
- **Inter-task communication:** Through **queues**, **semaphores**, and **mutexes** — prevents data corruption.
- **Determinism:** The kernel guarantees a bounded worst-case execution time (WCET) for every system call.

### 2.3 IoT Architecture — The 5-Layer Model (KTU-Favorite)

The standard IoT reference architecture (popularized by ITU-T Y.2060) consists of five layers:

| Layer # | Layer Name | Function | Microcontroller Examples |
|---------|-----------|----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Perception / Sensor Layer** | Physical sensing, actuation, data acquisition | DHT22, LM35, MPU6050, MQ-135 |
| 2 | **Network / Transport Layer** | Wireless transmission, routing, addressing | ESP8266 (Wi-Fi), nRF24L01, LoRa SX1276 |
| 3 | **Middleware / Processing Layer** | Data filtering, aggregation, edge computing, security | MQTT broker, AWS Greengrass, Node-RED |
| 4 | **Application Layer** | User-facing services, analytics, dashboards | Blynk, ThingSpeak, custom Android app |
| 5 | **Business Layer** | Decision-making, business logic, revenue models | Cloud analytics, ML inference, billing |

### 2.4 Comparison of Wireless Protocols (High-Yield Table)

| Protocol | Frequency | Range | Data Rate | Power | Topology | Typical MCU Module |
|----------|-----------|-------|-----------|-------|----------|---------------------|
| **Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n)** | 2.4 GHz / 5 GHz | ~50 m | 11–600 Mbps | High | Star (AP) | ESP8266, ESP32 |
| **Bluetooth/BLE 5.0** | 2.4 GHz | ~10–100 m | 1–2 Mbps | Low | Piconet/Scatternet | HC-05, nRF52840 |
| **ZigBee (802.15.4)** | 2.4 GHz | ~100 m | 250 kbps | Very Low | Mesh | XBee S2C, CC2530 |
| **LoRa (SX1276)** | 868/915 MHz | 2–15 km | 0.3–50 kbps | Ultra Low | Star | SX1276 + STM32 |
| **NB-IoT (LTE Cat-NB1)** | Licensed cellular | ~10 km | 26 kbps DL | Medium | Star (Cellular) | Quectel BC95 |
| **ASK/FSK (OOK)** | 433 MHz | ~30 m | 1–10 kbps | Low | Point-to-point | STX882, SRX882 |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Units |
|---|-------------------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength of EM wave | meters (m) |
| 2 | $f_c = \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$ | Carrier frequency | Hz |
| 3 | Antenna length (quarter-wave) | $L = \dfrac{\lambda}{4} = \dfrac{c}{4 f_c}$ | m |
| 4 | Free-space path loss (Friis) | $FSPL(dB) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right)$ | dB |
| 5 | Link-budget: $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL$ | Received power | dBm |
| 6 | Shannon capacity | $C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | bits/s |
| 7 | Nyquist bit rate (noiseless) | $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ | bits/s |
| 8 | Bit period | $T_b = \dfrac{1}{R_b}$ | seconds |
| 9 | Time quantum (RTOS) | $T_{tick} = \dfrac{1}{f_{tick}}$ | seconds |
| 10 | Throughput | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Useful data bits}}{\text{Total bits}} \times 100\%$ | % |
| 11 | Energy per bit | $E_b = \dfrac{P_{tx}}{R_b}$ | Joules/bit |
| 12 | Battery life | $t_{life} = \dfrac{C_{bat}\,(\text{mAh}) \cdot V}{P_{avg}\,(\text{W})} \times 3600$ | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** For path-loss problems, the Friis equation is the *most-asked* formula. Memorize the constant $20\log_{10}(4\pi/c) \approx -147.55$ dB (when $d$ in meters and $f$ in Hz). Always convert $f$ to **Hz**, not MHz, before plugging in.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil-moisture sensors (LoRa-based) send data every 15 minutes over 5 km to a central gateway — conserving battery for years.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Vibration sensors on motors predict bearing failure using edge-ML on STM32, sending alerts via MQTT over Wi-Fi.
- **Healthcare Wearables:** BLE-based ECG patches stream waveforms to a phone at 100 Hz while consuming only ~5 mA.
- **Smart Cities:** Air-quality nodes (ZigBee mesh) deploy hundreds of units in a city, each relaying data to a streetlight-mounted gateway.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Free-Space Path Loss (Friis Transmission Equation)

The Friis transmission equation gives the ratio of received to transmitted power in free space. We derive it starting from an isotropic radiator.

**Step 1 — Power density at distance $d$ from an isotropic antenna.**
If a transmitter radiates $P_t$ watts uniformly in all directions, the power spreads over the surface of a sphere of radius $d$:

$$S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \quad \left[ \text{W/m}^2 \right]$$

**Step 2 — Effective aperture of the receiving antenna.**
The effective area of a receiving antenna is given by:

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi} \quad \left[ \text{m}^2 \right]$$

where $G_r$ is the receiver antenna gain and $\lambda$ is the wavelength.

**Step 3 — Power captured by the receiving antenna.**

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \cdot G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi d^2 \cdot 4\pi} = \frac{P_t \, G_r \, c^2}{(4\pi d f)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Include transmitter antenna gain $G_t$ as well.**

$$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left( \frac{\lambda}{4\pi d} \right)^2$$

**Step 5 — Convert to decibels.**

$$\boxed{FSPL_{(dB)} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)}$$

This is the **path loss in dB**, which must be subtracted from $P_t + G_t + G_r$ to obtain the received power $P_r$ in dBm.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Quarter-Wave Antenna Length

For a monopole or dipole antenna, the resonant length is one-quarter of the wavelength. Derivation begins from the wave equation's solution: a standing wave forms a node at the feed point and an antinode at the open end, with a half-wavelength fitting in a *full* dipole.

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f_c}$$

For a **quarter-wave monopole** (most common in transceivers like nRF24L01):

$$L = \frac{\lambda}{4} = \frac{c}{4 f_c}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi antenna.

$$L = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{4 \times 2.4 \times 10^9} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{9.6 \times 10^9} = 0.03125 \text{ m} = 31.25 \text{ mm}$$

Hence the small "squiggly" trace on Wi-Fi PCBs — it is exactly a quarter-wave at 2.4 GHz.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shannon-Hartley Channel Capacity (Maximum Data Rate)

Claude Shannon's theorem gives the **maximum error-free data rate** $C$ over a band-limited channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz with signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$.

**Step 1 — Start with Nyquist's noiseless capacity for $M$-level signaling.**

$$C_N = 2B \log_2(M) \quad \text{bits/s}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $M$ from the SNR.** For an $M$-ary system, $M^2 = 1 + S/N$, so:

$$\log_2(M) = \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Plug back into $C_N$.**

$$C = 2B \cdot \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

$$\boxed{C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{bits/s}}$$

**Numerical Example:** Wi-Fi channel of 20 MHz bandwidth, SNR = 30 dB → $S/N = 1000$.

$$C = 20 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1001) = 20 \times 10^6 \cdot 9.97 \approx 199 \text{ Mbps}$$

This matches the *theoretical maximum* of an 802.11n 20 MHz channel — real throughput is lower due to overheads.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — RTOS Worst-Case Scheduling (Rate Monotonic Priority Bound)

For $n$ periodic tasks scheduled by the **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** algorithm (shorter period = higher priority), Liu & Layland (1973) proved a **schedulability bound**:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

where:
- $C_i$ = worst-case execution time of task $i$
- $T_i$ = period of task $i$
- The RHS bound is $U(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$

**Numerical Example (3 tasks):**
- $C_1/T_1 = 0.30$, $C_2/T_2 = 0.25$, $C_3/T_3 = 0.20$
- Sum of utilizations = $0.30 + 0.25 + 0.20 = 0.75$
- Bound: $U(3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 3(1.2599 - 1) = 0.7797$
- Since $0.75 < 0.7797$ → **schedulable** under RMS.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** RMS bound approaches $\ln(2) \approx 0.693$ as $n \to \infty$. In practice, utilization above 70% requires careful WCET analysis.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Task States in an RTOS

| State | Meaning | Transition Triggered By |
|-------|---------|--------------------------|
| **Ready** | Task is loaded and waiting for CPU | Scheduler dispatch |
| **Running** | Task currently owns the CPU | Preemption, blocking call, or yield |
| **Blocked** | Task waiting for event/signal/resource | Resource acquired / event received |
| **Suspended** | Task explicitly deleted or paused | `vTaskSuspend()` or `vTaskDelete()` |
| **Deleted** | Task terminated, memory freed | Kernel cleanup |

---

### 3.6 IoT Data Flow Derivation — Latency Breakdown

The end-to-end latency $T_{total}$ in a typical IoT pipeline:

$$T_{total} = T_{sense} + T_{proc} + T_{queue} + T_{tx} + T_{net} + T_{cloud} + T_{act}$$

where:
- $T_{sense}$ — sensor acquisition time
- $T_{proc}$ — local processing (edge)
- $T_{queue}$ — RTOS queue wait
- $T_{tx}$ — wireless transmission
- $T_{net}$ — internet routing
- $T_{cloud}$ — server processing
- $T_{act}$ — actuator response (in feedback loop)

For a hard real-time control loop (e.g., motor control over IoT), $T_{total} < 10$ ms is typical. The **RTOS guarantees** $T_{queue}$ is bounded; the **wireless protocol** governs $T_{tx}$.

---
