## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IoT 5-Layer Architecture (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PERCEPTION LAYER\nSensors and Actuators\nDHT22, LM35, MPU6050] --> B[NETWORK LAYER\nWireless Transceivers\nWiFi, BLE, LoRa, ZigBee]
    B --> C[TRANSPORT LAYER\nProtocols\nMQTT, CoAP, HTTP, AMQP]
    C --> D[PROCESSING LAYER\nEdge and Cloud\nAWS IoT, Azure, Greengrass]
    D --> E[APPLICATION LAYER\nUser Interface\nMobile App, Dashboard, Analytics]
    E --> F[BUSINESS LAYER\nDecision Support\nML Models, Billing, Reports]
    A -. feedback .-> F
    F -. actuator command .-> A
```

### 4.2 RTOS Task Scheduling State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created
    Created --> Ready: vTaskCreate
    Ready --> Running: Scheduler Dispatch
    Running --> Ready: Time Slice Expired
    Running --> Blocked: xQueueReceive Wait
    Blocked --> Ready: Event Signaled
    Running --> Suspended: vTaskSuspend
    Suspended --> Ready: vTaskResume
    Running --> [*]: vTaskDelete
```

### 4.3 Wireless Transceiver Interfacing with Microcontroller (SPI-based)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU[STM32 / ATmega328\nMicrocontroller] -->|SPI MOSI MISO SCK CS| TRX[nRF24L01 / SX1276\nTransceiver IC]
    MCU -->|GPIO CE and IRQ| TRX
    TRX -->|RF Signal 2.4 GHz| ANT[PCB Antenna\nlambda by 4]
    ANT -->|EM Waves| AIR((Free Space))
    AIR -->|EM Waves| ANT2[Remote Antenna]
    ANT2 --> TRX2[Remote Transceiver]
    TRX2 -->|SPI| MCU2[Remote MCU]
    MCU -. control flow .-> MCU2
```

### 4.4 IoT Data Flow with Edge-Cloud Split

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SENS[Sensor Node\nTemp, Humidity, Motion] --> EDGE[Edge Gateway\nRTOS-based MCU\nSTM32 + FreeRTOS]
    EDGE -->|Filter and Aggregate| CLOUD[Cloud Server\nAWS IoT Core]
    CLOUD --> APP[Mobile App and Dashboard]
    APP -->|User Command| CLOUD
    CLOUD -->|Control Signal| EDGE
    EDGE -->|PWM GPIO| ACT[Actuator\nRelay, Motor, LED]
    ACT -. feedback .-> SENS
```

### 4.5 Wireless Protocol Stack Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph WiFi["WI-FI STACK"]
        W1[Application HTTP] --> W2[TCP] --> W3[IP] --> W4[MAC CSMA CA] --> W5[PHY 2.4 GHz OFDM]
    end
    subgraph BLE["BLE STACK"]
        B1[GATT Profile] --> B2[ATT] --> B3[L2CAP] --> B4[Link Layer] --> B5[PHY 2.4 GHz GFSK]
    end
    subgraph LoRa["LORA STACK"]
        L1[Application Cayenne] --> L2[LoRaWAN MAC] --> L3[Class A B C] --> L4[PHY Chirp Spread 868 MHz]
    end
    subgraph ZigB["ZIGBEE STACK"]
        Z1[Application ZCL] --> Z2[APS] --> Z3[NWK Mesh] --> Z4[MAC 802.15.4] --> Z5[PHY 2.4 GHz OQPSK]
    end
```

### 4.6 RTOS vs Bare-Metal Super-Loop Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BAREMETAL["BARE METAL SUPER LOOP"]
        M1[Task 1 Sensor Read] --> M2[Task 2 Display Update] --> M3[Task 3 UART Send] --> M4[Delay ms] --> M1
    end
    subgraph RTOSVIEW["RTOS PREEMPTIVE"]
        R1[Task 1 Priority 3] 
        R2[Task 2 Priority 2] 
        R3[Task 3 Priority 1] 
        SCH[Scheduler Tick 1 ms] 
        SCH -. preempt .-> R1
        SCH -. preempt .-> R2
        SCH -. preempt .-> R3
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Recap:** In the bare-metal loop, **Task 3 must wait** until Tasks 1 and 2 complete. In the RTOS view, the scheduler preempts Tasks 1 and 2 *at every tick* if Task 3 has higher urgency — guaranteeing determinism.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define the Internet of Things (IoT). List any four characteristics of an IoT system.

**Model Answer:**
The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a network of interconnected physical devices — embedded with sensors, software, and communication hardware — that collect, exchange, and act on data over the internet with minimal human intervention.

**Four characteristics of an IoT system:**
1. **Connectivity** — Devices connect to the internet via Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, LoRa, ZigBee, or cellular.
2. **Sensing and actuation** — Physical parameters (temperature, motion, light) are sensed and converted to digital signals.
3. **Intelligence and identity** — Each device has a unique IP/MAC address and embedded intelligence to process data.
4. **Dynamic and self-adapting** — The system adapts to changing context (e.g., smart thermostat adjusting to occupancy).

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for characteristics)

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** Differentiate between a GPOS (General Purpose OS) and an RTOS in three points.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | GPOS (e.g., Windows, Linux) | RTOS (e.g., FreeRTOS, VxWorks) |
|-----------|------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Scheduling** | Optimizes for *throughput* and *fairness* | Optimizes for *determinism* and *deadlines* |
| **Latency** | Best-effort, no guarantee | Bounded worst-case response time |
| **Kernel size** | Large (MBs) | Tiny (KBs), fits in MCU flash |
| **Preemption** | Time-shared, may starve critical tasks | Strict priority, preempts immediately |
| **Example use** | Desktop, server | Embedded, IoT, automotive ECUs |

(3 marks: 3 valid points with correct distinction)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

**Q3. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Question A (14 Marks):** *With a neat block diagram, explain the five-layer architecture of IoT. Discuss any two wireless communication protocols used in IoT with their frequency, range, and typical applications.*

**(a) Five-Layer Architecture [7 Marks]**

The IoT architecture (ITU-T Y.2060) consists of five layers:

1. **Perception Layer (Sensor Layer):** The physical layer where sensors and actuators interact with the environment. Examples: temperature sensors (LM35), humidity (DHT22), gas sensors (MQ-135). Actuators include relays, motors, and LEDs.

2. **Network Layer (Transmission Layer):** Transmits data collected by the sensor layer to the processing layer using wireless protocols like Wi-Fi, ZigBee, LoRa, BLE, or wired Ethernet. Handles addressing, routing, and reliable delivery.

3. **Middleware / Processing Layer:** Performs data filtering, aggregation, storage, and edge analytics. Implements security (encryption, authentication). Examples: MQTT brokers, AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub.

4. **Application Layer:** Provides user-facing services and analytics. Examples: smart home apps, industrial dashboards, healthcare monitoring. Data is visualized, and notifications are sent.

5. **Business Layer:** Manages the overall IoT system, including business models, analytics, and decision-making. Often uses ML/AI to predict failures or optimize resources.

(7 marks: 1.5 marks per layer with examples + 0.5 for neat diagram)

**(b) Two Wireless Protocols [7 Marks]**

**Protocol 1: Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11 b/g/n)**
- **Frequency Band:** 2.4 GHz (and 5 GHz for 802.11ac/ax)
- **Range:** ~50 m indoor, ~200 m outdoor
- **Data Rate:** 11 Mbps (b), 54 Mbps (g), up to 600 Mbps (n)
- **Modulation:** DSSS for b, OFDM for g/n
- **Power:** High (~100 mA during TX, ESP8266)
- **Typical Application:** Home automation, IP cameras, video streaming IoT
- **Module:** ESP8266, ESP32

**Protocol 2: LoRa (Long Range) — SX1276**
- **Frequency Band:** 868 MHz (EU), 915 MHz (US), 433 MHz (Asia)
- **Range:** 2–15 km (line-of-sight)
- **Data Rate:** 0.3 kbps to 50 kbps (configurable via Spreading Factor)
- **Modulation:** Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)
- **Power:** Ultra-low (~10 mA TX, 10 nA sleep)
- **Typical Application:** Smart agriculture, smart metering, asset tracking
- **Module:** Semtech SX1276 + STM32

(7 marks: 3.5 marks per protocol — 0.5 for each of frequency, range, data rate, modulation, power, application, module)

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** *Explain the concept of an RTOS. With a state diagram, describe the various task states in an RTOS. Discuss Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) and its schedulability bound.*

**(a) RTOS Concept and Task States [7 Marks]**

A **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** is an OS that processes data within strict, deterministic time constraints (deadlines). In microcontrollers, popular RTOSes include **FreeRTOS**, **RTX**, **VxWorks**, and **Zephyr**.

**Task States (with diagram):**

1. **Created:** Task has been declared (Tcb allocated, stack allocated) but not yet active.
2. **Ready:** Task is ready to run, waiting for the CPU.
3. **Running:** Task is currently executing on the CPU.
4. **Blocked:** Task is waiting for an event (semaphore, queue, delay) — removed from ready list.
5. **Suspended:** Task is explicitly paused (`vTaskSuspend`).
6. **Deleted:** Task removed and resources freed.

The **scheduler** is invoked at every tick interrupt (e.g., 1 ms). It selects the highest-priority *ready* task and performs a **context switch** — saving the old task's PC, SP, and registers to its TCB (Task Control Block), and loading the new task's context.

(7 marks: 2 for RTOS concept, 3 for states with diagram, 2 for scheduler tick + context switch)

**(b) Rate Monotonic Scheduling [7 Marks]**

**Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** is a **fixed-priority, preemptive** scheduling algorithm proposed by Liu & Layland (1973).

- **Rule:** The task with the **shortest period $T_i$** receives the **highest priority**.
- **Rationale:** A task that runs more frequently (shorter period) must complete quickly, so it deserves higher priority.

**Schedulability Test (Necessary and Sufficient for RMS):**
A set of $n$ tasks is **guaranteed to be schedulable** under RMS if:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

where $C_i$ = worst-case execution time, $T_i$ = period.

**Bound values:**

| $n$ | $U(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ |
|-----|--------------------------|
| 1 | 1.000 |
| 2 | 0.828 |
| 3 | 0.779 |
| 4 | 0.756 |
| 5 | 0.743 |
| $\infty$ | $\ln(2) = 0.693$ |

**Example:** Three tasks with $C/T$ values 0.30, 0.25, 0.20. Sum = 0.75 < 0.779 → **schedulable**.

(7 marks: 2 for RMS rule, 3 for formula + table of bounds, 2 for numerical example)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in IoT/RTOS Questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Confusing "real-time" with "fast":** An RTOS is not necessarily *fast* — it is **deterministic**. A real-time system must complete work *within a deadline*, not at maximum speed.
> 2. **Wrong units in Friis equation:** Using frequency in MHz instead of Hz gives an answer off by a factor of $10^6$ — full 7 marks lost. **Always convert $f$ to Hz.**
> 3. **Missing antenna length explanation:** A 1-mark follow-up usually asks *why* the Wi-Fi antenna is ~31 mm long. Students who write only the formula lose this mark. State: *"It is a quarter-wave monopole resonant at 2.4 GHz."*
> 4. **RMS bound misuse:** The bound $U(n) = n(2^{1/n}-1)$ is a *sufficient* condition, not necessary. Tasks may still be schedulable above this bound — but you cannot *guarantee* it without response-time analysis.
> 5. **Protocol frequency confusion:** ZigBee, Wi-Fi, and Bluetooth all use **2.4 GHz ISM** — students sometimes wrongly claim ZigBee uses 868 MHz. ZigBee's underlying 802.15.4 PHY operates at 2.4 GHz globally; 868 MHz is **only for the European 802.15.4 variant** (rare in boards).
> 6. **Forgetting to draw the diagram:** A 1–2 mark deduction is common if the IoT architecture or RTOS state diagram is missing or unlabeled.
> 7. **Naming only the layer without examples:** In 5-layer architecture questions, each layer *must* include at least one protocol or sensor example for full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — IoT, Wireless, RTOS**

- **IoT Definition (ITU-T Y.2060):** A global infrastructure interconnecting physical and virtual things based on interoperable ICT.
- **5 Layers (Bottom-Up):** Perception → Network → Transport/Middleware → Application → Business. Memorize one example per layer.
- **Speed of EM wave:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s — used in *every* antenna and path-loss calculation.
- **Friis FSPL constant:** $20\log_{10}(4\pi/c) = -147.55$ dB (with $d$ in m, $f$ in Hz).
- **Quarter-wave antenna:** $L = c / (4 f_c)$. For 2.4 GHz → **31.25 mm**.
- **Shannon capacity:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — theoretical upper bound; real throughput is 50–70% of $C$.
- **RTOS vs GPOS:** RTOS is **deterministic**, **preemptive**, and **priority-based**; GPOS optimizes throughput, not deadlines.
- **Three core RTOS concepts:** Task, Scheduler, Kernel.
- **Five RTOS task states:** Created, Ready, Running, Blocked, Suspended, Deleted.
- **FreeRTOS** is the most widely used open-source RTOS for microcontrollers (ARM Cortex-M, AVR, ESP32).
- **RMS Rule:** Shorter period → Higher priority.
- **RMS Bound:** $U(n) = n(2^{1/n}-1)$; approaches 0.693 as $n \to \infty$.
- **Wi-Fi:** 2.4 GHz, 50 m, high power, IP-based, ESP8266 module.
- **BLE:** 2.4 GHz, 10 m, very low power, GATT profiles, nRF52840 module.
- **ZigBee:** 2.4 GHz, 100 m, mesh topology, IEEE 802.15.4, XBee S2C.
- **LoRa:** 868/915 MHz, 2–15 km, ultra-low power, Chirp Spread Spectrum, ideal for battery IoT.
- **MQTT vs HTTP:** MQTT is a lightweight **publish-subscribe** protocol over TCP — preferred for IoT; HTTP is **request-response**, heavy.
- **Edge computing** reduces cloud dependency by processing data locally on the RTOS-equipped MCU.
- **Determinism > Speed:** A real-time system is judged by *worst-case* latency, not average speed.

---

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Internet of Things (IoT)** is a paradigm that describes a network of physical objects (called *Things*) embedded with sensors, actuators, microcontrollers, software, and communication technologies, with the primary objective of enabling these objects to **collect, exchange, and act upon data** over the Internet without requiring direct human-to-human or human-to-computer interaction.

**IoT Architecture** is the systematic structural framework that defines how the heterogeneous components of an IoT system — namely, *sensing devices, communication networks, data processing platforms, and application services* — interact with each other to deliver intelligent, context-aware services. It is a layered reference model that abstracts the end-to-end data flow from a physical sensor in the field to a cloud-based dashboard or actuation command.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The official KTU Module 4 expects students to master the **reference layered architecture (3-layer / 4-layer / 5-layer models)**, the **functional components within each layer**, and the **standardized reference architectures** such as the *oneM2M* and *IoT World Forum (IoTWF)* 7-layer models. Direct definition questions on these models are routinely asked for 3 marks in Part A.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Smart Human Body" Model**
>
> Imagine a human body that can sense, communicate, and act on its own — that is essentially what an IoT system is.

| Human Body Counterpart | IoT Architectural Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Sense Organs** (eyes, ears, skin) | Sensors & Actuators (Perception Layer) | Detect physical phenomena: temperature, pressure, motion, light |
| **Nervous System** (nerves & spinal cord) | Communication Networks (Network Layer) | Carry the sensed signals toward a decision-making center |
| **Brain** (processing & memory) | Middleware / Cloud / Edge Computing (Processing Layer) | Analyse data, store it, derive decisions |
| **Actions / Speech** (muscle movement, talking) | Application Services (Application Layer) | Notify user, control a device, send alert to a mobile app |
| **Goals & Purpose of Life** | Business Layer | Manage overall system KPIs, ROI, evolution roadmap |

So when a *smart agriculture field* has a soil-moisture sensor (sense organ) detecting dryness, it sends a signal via a wireless module (nervous system) to a cloud server (brain). The brain decides irrigation is needed and commands a water pump (muscle) to switch on. The farmer (end user) is notified on a mobile app (speech). This is IoT in action — **Sense → Transmit → Think → Act → Serve**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Bold

- **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** — lightweight publish/subscribe protocol, port **1883 (TCP)**.
- **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** — RESTful protocol for constrained devices, port **5683 (UDP)**.
- **HTTP/HTTPS** — traditional web protocol used by many IoT cloud APIs.
- **6LoWPAN** — IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks.
- **Latency in IoT:** typically targeted between **<1 ms (tactile IoT)** and **seconds (monitoring IoT)**.
- **Power budget for IoT end nodes:** often between **<1 mW (energy harvesting)** and **<100 mW (battery)**.
- **Bandwidth range:** from **~250 kbps (LoRa)** to **>1 Gbps (5G/6G mMTC)**.

> [!TIP]
> A useful rule of thumb that examiners love: **"In IoT, latency is inversely proportional to power consumption, and proportional to bandwidth cost."** Memorizing this single sentence can win you easy marks in application-based questions.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Why We Need a Layered Architecture

A *monolithic* (single-block) IoT system becomes unmanageable when we scale from a single room (smart bulb) to a smart city (millions of streetlights). The layered approach offers:

1. **Abstraction** — each layer is unaware of the internal complexity of adjacent layers.
2. **Modularity** — any layer can be upgraded/replaced without disturbing the whole stack.
3. **Interoperability** — vendors can compete at a single layer while maintaining compatibility.
4. **Scalability** — millions of nodes can be added by simply extending the perception layer.
5. **Security Isolation** — encryption/authentication can be applied at the *correct* layer, not as a blanket.

## 2.2 The Three Foundational Architectural Models

### Model A — The Classical 3-Layer Model

| Layer | Name | Function | Key Components |
|---|---|---|---|
| **L1** | Perception Layer (Sensing) | Physical data acquisition & actuation | Sensors, actuators, RFID tags, microcontrollers, embedded firmware |
| **L2** | Network Layer (Transmission) | Reliable data transport across heterogeneous networks | Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, ZigBee, LoRa, cellular (4G/5G), gateways, routers |
| **L3** | Application Layer (Service) | Deliver service to the end user | Smart-home apps, dashboards, alert systems, control panels |

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation of 3-Layer Model:** It lacks explicit support for *data storage*, *analytics*, and *business logic*. Hence the 4-layer and 5-layer models evolved.

### Model B — The 4-Layer Reference Model (Most Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Layer | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **L1** | Sensing / Perception Layer | Captures physical phenomena via sensors & actuators |
| **L2** | Network Layer | Handles connectivity & data routing |
| **L3** | Middleware / Processing Layer | Storage, analytics, decision-making (edge/fog/cloud) |
| **L4** | Application Layer | Service delivery to end-users |

### Model C — The 5-Layer Comprehensive Model

| Layer | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **L1** | Perception (Objects) Layer | Physical sensing & actuation |
| **L2** | Network / Transport Layer | Wired / wireless communication, gateways |
| **L3** | Middleware Layer | Service-oriented management; data caching, filtering, aggregation |
| **L4** | Application Layer | Smart services (healthcare, agriculture, transport) |
| **L5** | Business Layer | Manages applications, business models, privacy, profit |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — IoT Reference Architecture

| Item | Description | Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| **3-Layer Model** | Perception + Network + Application | ★★★ |
| **4-Layer Model** | Adds Middleware/Processing | ★★★★★ |
| **5-Layer Model** | Adds Business Layer | ★★★★ |
| **IoTWF 7-Layer Model** | Edge → Communication → Cloud → Data → Application → Collaboration → Business | ★★★ |
| **oneM2M Standard** | Horizontal common service layer, ETSI / TIA / CCSA / TTA / TTC / ARIB | ★★★★ |
| **MQTT Protocol** | Pub/Sub, broker-based, TCP, port 1883 | ★★★★★ |
| **CoAP Protocol** | RESTful, UDP, port 5683, for constrained devices | ★★★★ |
| **AMQP Protocol** | Queuing protocol, TCP, port 5672 | ★★★ |
| **DDS Protocol** | Real-time pub/sub, no broker, peer-to-peer | ★★ |
| **6LoWPAN** | IPv6 over IEEE 802.15.4 | ★★★ |
| **Edge Computing** | Processing at the network edge (near sensor) | ★★★★ |
| **Fog Computing** | Intermediate layer between edge and cloud | ★★★ |
| **Cloud Computing** | Centralized, scalable, elastic storage & compute | ★★★★ |
| **Sensor** | Converts physical quantity → electrical signal | ★★★★★ |
| **Actuator** | Converts electrical signal → physical action | ★★★★ |
| **Gateway** | Bridges sensor network (e.g., ZigBee) ↔ Internet (TCP/IP) | ★★★★ |
| **RFID** | Radio-frequency identification for identification | ★★★ |
| **WSN** | Wireless Sensor Network — spatially distributed sensors | ★★★★ |
| **QoS Levels in MQTT** | 0 (At most once), 1 (At least once), 2 (Exactly once) | ★★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** A 4-layer IoT architecture powers a *smart irrigation system* where the perception layer (soil-moisture + temperature sensors + solenoid valve actuator) feeds data via LoRaWAN (network layer) to an AWS IoT Core platform (middleware layer) which runs a rule-engine, finally triggering a mobile notification (application layer) to the farmer.

## 2.4 Functional Block Decomposition of Each Layer

**Perception Layer — Detailed**
- Sensors (temperature, humidity, pressure, gas, motion, light, sound, biometric)
- Actuators (motors, relays, solenoids, LED drivers, buzzers)
- Embedded MCUs (Arduino, ESP32, STM32, MSP430, PIC, 8051)
- RFID readers & tags
- Power supply (battery, energy harvesting, mains)

**Network Layer — Detailed**
- Short-range: BLE, ZigBee, Z-Wave, NFC, RFID, Wi-Fi (802.11)
- Long-range: LoRa, SigFox, NB-IoT, LTE-M
- Cellular: 4G LTE, 5G mMTC / URLLC / eMBB
- Wired: Ethernet, RS-485, CAN, Modbus
- Routing: RPL (for 6LoWPAN), 6LoWPAN adaptation

**Middleware / Processing Layer — Detailed**
- Data filtering, aggregation, compression
- Stream processing engines (Apache Kafka, AWS Kinesis)
- Storage (time-series DBs: InfluxDB, TimescaleDB)
- Analytics (ML/AI inference, anomaly detection)
- Device management, identity & access control

**Application Layer — Detailed**
- Domain-specific apps: smart home, smart health, smart agriculture, industrial IoT (IIoT)
- Visualization: dashboards, mobile apps, AR/VR overlays
- Control logic: rule engines, digital twins

**Business Layer — Detailed**
- Business models (subscription, pay-per-use, freemium)
- Privacy & regulatory compliance (GDPR, India's DPDP Act 2023)
- SLA management
- ROI analytics

## 2.5 Key IoT Communication Protocols (Comparison)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU often asks: "Compare MQTT and CoAP."** Memorize this 6-point table.

| Parameter | MQTT | CoAP |
|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP | UDP |
| **Port** | 1883 / 8883 (TLS) | 5683 / 5684 (DTLS) |
| **Architecture** | Publish / Subscribe (Broker) | Request / Response (RESTful) |
| **Header Size** | 2 bytes (fixed) | 4 bytes (small) |
| **QoS** | 3 levels (0, 1, 2) | 2 levels (CON / NON) |
| **Best For** | Reliable, continuous telemetry | Constrained, lossy networks |

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of Layered IoT

- **Smart Cities** — traffic, lighting, waste management (5-layer model fits best).
- **Healthcare (IoMT)** — wearable ECG monitors, remote patient dashboards.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — predictive maintenance, SCADA integration.
- **Precision Agriculture** — drone-based field monitoring, automated irrigation.
- **Smart Home** — voice assistants, occupancy-driven HVAC.
- **Logistics & Supply Chain** — cold-chain monitoring, fleet tracking.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Example

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Data Flow in a Generic IoT Pipeline

The 4-layer architecture can be mapped to a deterministic data-flow model. Let us formalize the journey of a single sensor sample from field to dashboard.

> [!NOTE]
> Although IoT is a non-mathematical topic, the *latency budget* and *bandwidth sizing* sub-problems are extremely common in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions. We will therefore walk through one fully worked numerical.

### Step 1 — Define the Sensing Function

Let the perception-layer node sample a physical quantity $x(t)$ at a uniform sampling rate $f_s$.

$$x[n] = x(t) \big\vert_{t = nT_s}, \quad T_s = \frac{1}{f_s}$$

where $T_s$ is the sampling period. The number of samples generated per node per day is:

$$N_{day} = f_s \times 86400$$

### Step 2 — Quantize the Sample

Each sample is quantized to $B$ bits per reading. Total data generated per node per day:

$$D_{node} = N_{day} \times B \;\; \text{bits/day}$$

### Step 3 — Network-Layer Framing Overhead

The network layer adds protocol headers. If the total overhead per packet is $H$ bits and one packet carries $P$ useful payload bits, the effective data rate becomes:

$$R_{eff} = R_{link} \times \frac{P}{P+H}$$

where $R_{link}$ is the nominal physical-layer data rate.

### Step 4 — Middleware-Layer Compression

Let the compression ratio be $\rho \in (0,1]$, where $\rho = 1$ means no compression and $\rho = 0.1$ means 90% reduction.

$$D_{stored} = D_{node} \times \rho$$

### Step 5 — Application-Layer Display Latency

If the processing pipeline (sensing $\to$ transmit $\to$ analyze $\to$ display) has stages with delays $t_1, t_2, t_3, t_4$ respectively, the **end-to-end latency** is:

$$L_{E2E} = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"Identify which architectural layer a given protocol/component belongs to."* The trick is to map each item to the closest functional step. Sensors → Step 1; Wi-Fi/LoRa → Step 2; Cloud/Edge → Step 3; Dashboard → Step 4.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> [!TIP]
> **Question (Worked):** A smart factory has 500 sensor nodes. Each node samples 4 different parameters at 10 Hz, with 16-bit resolution. Compute (a) total raw data per day generated, (b) data after 80% compression at the middleware layer, (c) end-to-end latency if sensing delay = 5 ms, network delay = 20 ms, cloud processing delay = 100 ms, dashboard rendering delay = 50 ms. Express (a) and (b) in **GB/day**.

### Solution (Step-by-Step)

**Part (a) — Total Raw Data Per Day**

Samples per node per parameter per day:

$$N_{day} = f_s \times 86400 = 10 \times 86400 = 864{,}000 \text{ samples}$$

Total per node across 4 parameters:

$$N_{node} = 4 \times 864{,}000 = 3{,}456{,}000 \text{ samples/day}$$

Bits per node per day:

$$D_{node} = N_{node} \times B = 3{,}456{,}000 \times 16 = 55{,}296{,}000 \text{ bits/day}$$

For 500 nodes:

$$D_{raw} = 500 \times 55{,}296{,}000 = 27{,}648{,}000{,}000 \text{ bits/day}$$

Convert to GB/day using $1 \text{ GB} = 8 \times 10^9 \text{ bits}$:

$$D_{raw} = \frac{27.648 \times 10^9}{8 \times 10^9} = 3.456 \text{ GB/day}$$

**Part (b) — Data After 80% Compression**

Compression ratio $\rho = 1 - 0.80 = 0.20$ (i.e., data is reduced to 20% of original):

$$D_{stored} = 3.456 \times 0.20 = 0.6912 \text{ GB/day}$$

**Part (c) — End-to-End Latency**

$$L_{E2E} = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4 = 5 + 20 + 100 + 50 = 175 \text{ ms}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Summary:** A 500-node factory generates **3.456 GB/day** raw, which compresses to **0.6912 GB/day** at the middleware layer, with an end-to-end latency of **175 ms**. This is well within the typical real-time IIoT budget of **< 1 second**.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Layer Does "X" Belong To?

| Component / Protocol | Correct Layer | Common Mistake |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature sensor (LM35) | Perception | Sometimes wrongly placed in Network |
| ESP32 Wi-Fi module | Perception + Network (dual) | Often missed in Network only |
| MQTT Broker | Middleware / Application (overlap) | Confused with Network |
| AWS IoT Core | Middleware | Confused with Application |
| Mobile dashboard | Application | Confused with Business |
| Profit / ROI calculation | Business | Confused with Application |
| ZigBee coordinator | Network | Confused with Perception |
| InfluxDB | Middleware | Confused with Network |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the three layers of the classical IoT reference architecture. State ONE function of each layer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The classical IoT architecture is the **3-layer model** consisting of:

1. **Perception Layer (1 mark):** Also called the sensing layer. It identifies physical objects (sensors, RFID tags, actuators) and collects/processes data from the physical environment. Example: temperature sensor LM35.
2. **Network Layer (1 mark):** Responsible for transmitting the sensed data through wired or wireless networks (Wi-Fi, ZigBee, LoRa, cellular) to the processing centre. Example: 4G modem.
3. **Application Layer (1 mark):** Delivers application-specific services to the end user such as smart-home control, smart-health monitoring, or industrial dashboards.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write only "transmit" or "receive" for the Network Layer and earn only partial credit. Always mention *both* transmission and routing, and quote a *specific protocol* for the full 1 mark.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between MQTT and CoAP protocols. Mention any THREE points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per correct comparison):**

| Sl. | MQTT | CoAP |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uses **TCP** as the underlying transport. Port **1883**. | Uses **UDP** as the underlying transport. Port **5683**. |
| 2 | Follows **Publish / Subscribe** model with a central **broker**. | Follows **Request / Response** (RESTful) model, peer-to-peer. |
| 3 | Header is **2 bytes** fixed; designed for reliable, continuous telemetry. | Header is **4 bytes**; designed for highly constrained, lossy networks. |
| 4 | Three QoS levels: **0, 1, 2** (at-most/at-least/exactly-once). | Two message types: **CON** (confirmable) and **NON** (non-confirmable). |
| 5 | Suited for stable, broadband IoT links. | Suited for low-power, lossy IoT links. |

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Module Internal Choice Set 1]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **5-layer IoT architecture**. Identify the specific layer in which each of the following resides: (i) Soil-moisture sensor, (ii) LoRaWAN gateway, (iii) AWS IoT Core analytics, (iv) Mobile app, (v) ROI calculation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **functional components of the Middleware Layer** in detail. State TWO real-world use-cases that depend on middleware-layer processing. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

### Part (a) — 5-Layer IoT Architecture (7 marks)

**Diagram & Layer Identification (5 marks):**

The 5-layer IoT architecture consists of the following layers (from bottom to top):

**Layer 1 — Perception Layer (Objects / Sensors Layer) (1 mark)**
- Contains physical sensors, actuators, RFID tags, embedded microcontrollers.
- Item (i) — *Soil-moisture sensor* — resides here. Function: converts dielectric constant of soil into an analog voltage.

**Layer 2 — Network Layer (Transport Layer) (1 mark)**
- Handles data transmission via wired/wireless media, gateways, routers.
- Item (ii) — *LoRaWAN gateway* — resides here. Function: aggregates LoRa-modulated packets from end-nodes and forwards them to an IP-based network server.

**Layer 3 — Middleware Layer (Processing / Service Management) (1 mark)**
- Provides data caching, filtering, aggregation, analytics, security, and device management.
- Item (iii) — *AWS IoT Core analytics* — resides here. Function: ingests MQTT topics, runs rules engine, stores in time-series DB, executes ML inference.

**Layer 4 — Application Layer (1 mark)**
- Provides domain-specific services to end-users via dashboards, mobile apps, web portals.
- Item (iv) — *Mobile app* — resides here. Function: displays real-time soil moisture, sends push notifications.

**Layer 5 — Business Layer (1 mark)**
- Manages overall system goals, business models, profit, privacy, regulatory compliance.
- Item (v) — *ROI calculation* — resides here. Function: monetizes the data, computes crop-yield improvement per rupee invested.

**Neat block diagram (shown earlier in SECTION 4.3) — IoTWF 7-layer model can also be referenced.**

### Part (b) — Middleware Layer Functional Components (7 marks)

The Middleware Layer is the "brain" of the IoT system. Its functional components include:

1. **Data Filtering & Aggregation (1 mark)** — Removes redundant or noisy data. Example: averaging 10 successive temperature readings into one to reduce bandwidth.

2. **Data Storage (1 mark)** — Time-series databases (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB), NoSQL stores (MongoDB, Cassandra), or data lakes (AWS S3, Azure Data Lake).

3. **Analytics & Machine Learning (1 mark)** — Real-time and batch analytics. Examples: anomaly detection, predictive maintenance, demand forecasting.

4. **Stream Processing (1 mark)** — Frameworks like Apache Kafka, AWS Kinesis, RabbitMQ for real-time event handling.

5. **Device Management (1 mark)** — Provisioning, configuration, firmware updates (FOTA/SOTA), health monitoring of edge devices.

6. **Security & Access Control (1 mark)** — Authentication (X.509 certs, OAuth2.0), authorization (RBAC), encryption (TLS/DTLS).

7. **Decision / Rule Engine (1 mark)** — Executes pre-defined rules (e.g., "if moisture < 30% AND temperature > 35 °C, open valve") and triggers downstream actions.

**Two real-world use-cases dependent on middleware processing (within 7-mark scope):**
- **Smart Grid Energy Management** — middleware aggregates 1-minute readings from millions of smart meters, runs demand-prediction models, and dynamically adjusts pricing.
- **Remote Patient Monitoring** — middleware processes real-time ECG streams, applies ML-based arrhythmia detection, and alerts cardiologists within seconds.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B 14-mark):**
> 1. Students often *forget to map items to specific layers* in part (a) and merely list layer names — that caps your marks at ~3/7.
> 2. In part (b), students *omit a real-world use-case* and lose 1–2 marks. Always quote at least **one** numerical scenario (e.g., "10⁶ smart meters, 1-min cadence").
> 3. Don't draw the 3-layer model when the question explicitly asks for 5-layer — the mismatch costs you 2 marks outright.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Module Internal Choice Set 2]`

**(a)** Compare **MQTT and CoAP** protocols in terms of transport, architecture, header size, QoS, and typical use-cases. State THREE advantages of MQTT over HTTP for IoT. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **IoT reference architecture as proposed by the IoT World Forum (IoTWF)** with a neat block diagram. Identify the layer responsible for *data abstraction* and explain its role. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

### Part (a) — MQTT vs CoAP + MQTT vs HTTP (7 marks)

**MQTT vs CoAP — Comparison (4 marks: 0.8 × 5 rows)**

| Parameter | MQTT | CoAP |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | TCP | UDP |
| Port | 1883 / 8883 (TLS) | 5683 / 5684 (DTLS) |
| Architecture | Publish / Subscribe (Broker) | Request / Response (RESTful) |
| Header size | 2 bytes | 4 bytes |
| QoS | 0, 1, 2 | CON / NON |
| Use-case | Stable links, telemetry | Constrained, lossy links |

**Three advantages of MQTT over HTTP (3 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Lightweight overhead:** MQTT's 2-byte fixed header is far smaller than HTTP's variable-length header, which is critical for low-bandwidth IoT links.
2. **Asynchronous pub/sub model:** MQTT supports many-to-many messaging with a broker, whereas HTTP is strictly request/response and requires the client to poll — wasting power.
3. **Designed for unreliable networks:** MQTT offers three QoS levels and a keep-alive mechanism tailored for IoT, while HTTP assumes a relatively stable connection.
4. **Lower power consumption:** Persistent connection and binary framing mean fewer radio wake-ups, extending battery life by 3–10× in field deployments.

### Part (b) — IoTWF 7-Layer Reference Architecture (7 marks)

The **IoT World Forum (IoTWF) 7-layer model** was proposed by Cisco, IBM, and academic partners as an industry-grade reference architecture. (5 marks for diagram + 2 marks for explanation of "Data Abstraction" layer.)

The seven layers (bottom to top) are:

1. **Layer 1 — Physical Devices & Controllers:** Sensors, actuators, edge devices.
2. **Layer 2 — Connectivity:** Communication infrastructure (Wi-Fi, cellular, LoRa, ZigBee).
3. **Layer 3 — Edge / Fog Computing:** Local processing, filtering, decision-making near the data source.
4. **Layer 4 — Data Accumulation:** Event-based storage, streaming, message brokers.
5. **Layer 5 — Data Abstraction:** Data modelling, transformation, and federation across heterogeneous sources.
6. **Layer 6 — Application:** Smart services, dashboards, control systems.
7. **Layer 7 — Collaboration & Business Processes:** Cross-organisation processes, KPIs, monetisation.

**Data Abstraction Layer — Role (2 marks):**

The Data Abstraction Layer is responsible for:
- **Data modelling** — converting raw sensor streams into a common schema.
- **Data transformation** — normalising, cleaning, and enriching data.
- **Data federation** — providing a unified view across multiple storage systems (relational DBs, time-series DBs, data lakes).

It enables the Application Layer (Layer 6) to query the system in a *vendor-neutral* way, abstracting the underlying storage heterogeneity. Example: a smart-city dashboard queries "average PM2.5 across all wards" without knowing whether the data is in InfluxDB, Cassandra, or S3.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part B 14-mark):**
> 1. In part (a), students *quote only 2 advantages* of MQTT over HTTP — make it a habit to write at least **three crisp points**.
> 2. In part (b), many students confuse the *Data Accumulation* (Layer 4) and *Data Abstraction* (Layer 5) layers. Remember the mnemonic: **"Accumulate raw, Abstract clean."**
> 3. Skipping the diagram in part (b) costs a guaranteed 2 marks — always draw a labelled block diagram.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (read this 5 minutes before the exam):**

- ✅ IoT = network of *things* with sensors/actuators/MCUs that exchange data over the Internet with **minimal human intervention**.
- ✅ **3-Layer Model** = Perception + Network + Application (simplest, most common short answer).
- ✅ **4-Layer Model** = Adds Middleware/Processing between Network and Application.
- ✅ **5-Layer Model** = Adds Business Layer on top of the 4-layer model.
- ✅ **IoTWF 7-Layer Model** = Physical → Connectivity → Edge → Data Accumulation → Data Abstraction → Application → Collaboration/Business.
- ✅ **Perception Layer** = Sensors + Actuators + RFID + Embedded MCUs.
- ✅ **Network Layer** = Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, ZigBee, LoRa, 5G, gateways, routers.
- ✅ **Middleware Layer** = Storage + Analytics + Stream Processing + Device Mgmt + Security.
- ✅ **Application Layer** = Dashboards, mobile apps, smart services.
- ✅ **Business Layer** = ROI, regulations (GDPR, DPDP), business models.
- ✅ **MQTT** = TCP, port 1883, pub/sub, 2-byte header, 3 QoS levels.
- ✅ **CoAP** = UDP, port 5683, request/response, 4-byte header, CON/NON.
- ✅ **HTTP** = heavy, request/response, not ideal for constrained IoT.
- ✅ **Gateways** bridge short-range sensor protocols (ZigBee, BLE) to IP networks.
- ✅ **Edge Computing** = processing near the sensor; **Fog** = intermediate; **Cloud** = centralized.
- ✅ **Latency budget formula:** $L_{E2E} = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_4$.
- ✅ **Raw data sizing formula:** $D = f_s \times B \times \text{nodes} \times 86400 \text{ bits/day}$.
- ✅ **Compression formula:** $D_{stored} = D_{raw} \times \rho$, where $\rho \in (0,1]$.
- ✅ For **definition questions**, always quote *layer name + function + example* — the example is what fetches the third mark.
- ✅ For **comparison questions**, use a *tabular format with 4–5 parameters* — examiners award marks row-wise.
- ✅ Memorize the **port numbers** (MQTT 1883, CoAP 5683, HTTPS 443) — they are direct 1-mark win questions.
- ✅ When asked for a *diagram*, draw a **labelled flowchart with arrows** showing data direction; never omit the labels.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Layer-name + Function + Real-world Example"* = 3 marks in Part A. *"Tabular Comparison + Diagram + Use-Case"* = 14 marks in Part B. Stick to this template and you will comfortably score above 90% in this module.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Protocols: MQTT, CoAP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Protocols: MQTT, CoAP # 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Introduction to IoT Communication Protocols In the **Internet of Things (IoT)** ecosystem, microcontrollers and embedded devices rarely communicate in isolation. They exchange telemetry data (sensor readings), actuation commands, and control signals with cloud servers, gateways, or peer devices. Because traditional Internet protocols (like **HTTP/TCP**) are too heavy for resource-constrained 8-bit/32-bit MCUs (limited RAM, flash, and battery), specialized **lightweight application-layer protocols** are used. The two most important ones in the KTU 2024 Microcontroller syllabus are **MQTT** and **CoAP**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** > *Application-layer protocols* are standardized rules that govern the format, syntax, semantics, and synchronization of communication between two or more devices operating over a transport-layer medium. --- ## 1.2 MQTT — Message Queuing Telemetry Transport **MQTT** is a **lightweight, publish/subscribe-based messaging protocol** originally invented by Dr. Andy Stanford-Clark (IBM) and Arlen Nipper in **1999**. It was designed for **low-bandwidth, high-latency, unreliable networks** — the exact conditions found in IoT and M2M (Machine-to-Machine) communication. > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (ISO/IEC 20922):** > MQTT is a Client-Server publish/subscribe messaging transport protocol. It is designed to be open, simple, lightweight, and easy to implement. These characteristics make it ideal for use in constrained environments — for example, but not limited to, machine-to-machine (M2M) and Internet of Things (IoT) contexts. ### Conceptual Analogy — The Newspaper Stand 🗞️ Imagine a **newspaper stand** in your city: - **The Newspaper Company (Publisher)** prints the newspaper every morning. - **You (Subscriber)** decide to buy the *Times of India* or *The Hindu* — you do **not** talk to the writer directly. - The **Newspaper Delivery System (Broker)** sits in the middle. It accepts papers from publishers and hands them out to interested subscribers. In MQTT: - The **Publisher** = MCU/embedded device sending data (e.g., a temperature sensor). - The **Subscriber** = Backend server or dashboard wanting that data. - The **Broker** = A central server (e.g., **Mosquitto**, **HiveMQ**, **EMQX**) that decouples publishers from subscribers. The newspaper (message) is identified by a **Topic** (e.g., `"home/livingroom/temperature"`). Subscribers register their interest in a topic, and the broker ensures delivery. --- ## 1.3 CoAP — Constrained Application Protocol **CoAP** is a specialized web transfer protocol defined by the **IETF in RFC 7252 (2014)** for use with constrained nodes and constrained networks. It is designed to enable **RESTful communication** (similar to HTTP) but for tiny embedded devices. > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (RFC 7252):** > The Constrained Application Protocol (CoAP) is a specialized web transfer protocol for use with constrained nodes and constrained (e.g., low-power, lossy) networks. The nodes often have 8-bit microcontrollers with small amounts of ROM and RAM, while constrained networks such as IPv6 over Low-Power Wireless Personal Area Networks (6LoWPANs) often have high packet error rates and a typical throughput of 10s of kbit/s. ### Conceptual Analogy — The Walkie-Talkie 📻 Think of two friends using **walkie-talkies**: - One says, *"Get me the temperature, over!"* (this is a **GET request**). - The other replies, *"It's 25°C, over!"* (this is a **GET response**). In CoAP, devices speak to each other **directly**, in short bursts, without needing a central broker. It's **request-response**, like HTTP, but compressed to fit on tiny devices and transported over **UDP** (which is connectionless — quick to set up, low overhead). --- ## 1.4 Quick Reference at a Glance | Feature | **MQTT** | **CoAP** | |---|---|---| | **Architecture** | Publish / Subscribe | Request / Response (REST) | | **Transport Layer** | TCP (port 1883 / 8883 for TLS) | UDP (port 5683 / 5684 for DTLS) | | **Message Format** | Binary, ~2-byte fixed header | Binary, ~4-byte fixed header | | **Reliability** | 3 QoS levels (0, 1, 2) | Confirmable (CON) / Non-confirmable (NON) | | **Typical Use Case** | Telemetry, cloud dashboards | Resource-constrained sensor/actuator control | | **Standard Body** | OASIS / ISO/IEC 20922 | IETF RFC 7252 | | **Header Size** | 2 bytes minimum | 4 bytes minimum | | **Energy Efficiency** | Good (TCP keep-alive can be costly) | Excellent (UDP, low overhead) | | **Broker Required?** | Yes | No (but can use one) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Takeaway for KTU Exam:** MQTT = *TCP-based, broker-mediated, pub/sub* — best for **stable networks with cloud connectivity**. CoAP = *UDP-based, request/response* — best for **highly constrained, lossy networks** like 6LoWPAN/LoRa. --- # 📘 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 MQTT — Architecture & Components ### 2.1.1 Core Components 1. **Client**: Any device (MCU, server, mobile app) that runs the MQTT library. A client can be both a publisher and a subscriber simultaneously. 2. **Broker**: The central server. Maintains topic hierarchy, authenticates clients, and routes messages. Examples: *Mosquitto* (open-source), *EMQX*, *HiveMQ*. 3. **Topic**: A UTF-8 string identifier (hierarchical, separated by `/`). E.g., `factory/line1/motor3/temperature`. 4. **Message**: The actual payload (binary or text) carried on a topic. 5. **Session**: Persistent state maintained between client and broker (subscriptions, in-flight messages). ### 2.1.2 Topic Wildcards > [!NOTE] > MQTT supports two wildcards in subscriptions, but **NEVER in publishing**: > - **Multi-level wildcard**: `+` matches a single level. > E.g., `factory/+/temperature` matches `factory/line1/temperature` and `factory/line2/temperature`. > - **Multi-level wildcard**: `#` matches all remaining levels (must be the last character). > E.g., `factory/line1/#` matches `factory/line1/motor3/temperature` AND `factory/line1/motor3/vibration`. ### 2.1.3 Quality of Service (QoS) Levels QoS is the **agreement** between sender and broker (or broker and receiver) on the **guarantee of message delivery**. This is the most heavily tested MQTT concept in KTU. | QoS Level | Name | Guarantee | Mechanism | Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | **QoS 0** | At most once | Fire-and-forget | No ACK | Lossy data like periodic temp readings | | **QoS 1** | At least once | May duplicate | PUBACK handshake | Critical alerts (may arrive twice) | | **QoS 2** | Exactly once | No loss, no duplicate | 4-step handshake (PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP) | Billing, medical data | ### 2.1.4 The MQTT Control Packet Structure Every MQTT packet (e.g., CONNECT, PUBLISH, SUBSCRIBE) has a **Fixed Header** of just **2 bytes** minimum: - **Byte 1**: Control Packet Type (4 bits) + Flags (4 bits) - **Byte 2+**: Remaining Length (variable, 1–4 bytes using a continuation-bit scheme) **Fixed Header Diagram:** $$ \text{Fixed Header} = [\text{Byte 1: Type}\vert\text{Flags}] \;+\; [\text{Remaining Length (1–4 bytes)}] $$ The **PUBLISH** packet additionally has a **Variable Header** (Topic Name, Packet Identifier) and a **Payload** (up to 256 MB). ### 2.1.5 MQTT Connection Lifecycle (7 states) A client transitions through these states: 1. **Initial** (no connection) 2. **Connecting** (sent CONNECT) 3. **Connected** (received CONNACK) 4. **Disconnecting** (sent DISCONNECT) 5. **Disconnected** 6. **Sleeping** (in QoS 1/2 flow) 7. **Lost connection** (network failure — last will may trigger) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last Will and Testament (LWT)**: A message pre-registered by the client. If the broker detects an abnormal disconnection (no DISCONNECT packet, heartbeat lost), it **publishes the LWT message** on behalf of the dead client. Extremely useful for fault detection in IoT. ### 2.1.6 Keep-Alive (PINGREQ / PINGRESP) The client sends a **PINGREQ** packet at least once every *Keep-Alive interval* (set during CONNECT). The broker replies with **PINGRESP**. If 1.5× the keep-alive interval passes with no packet, the broker assumes the client is dead and triggers the LWT. --- ## 2.2 CoAP — Architecture & Components ### 2.2.1 Core Concepts - **Endpoints**: Each CoAP device is a *server* (with resources) and/or a *client* (consuming resources). - **Resource**: Identified by a URI, e.g., `coap://[2001:db8::1]:5683/sensors/temp`. Resources support CRUD via CoAP methods. - **Methods** (similar to HTTP): - `GET` — Retrieve resource - `POST` — Create resource / request execution - `PUT` — Update/create resource - `DELETE` — Remove resource ### 2.2.2 CoAP Message Format A CoAP message has a **4-byte fixed header**: $$ \text{CoAP Header} = [\text{Ver (2b)} \;|\; \text{T (2b)} \;|\; \text{TKL (4b)}] \;+\; [\text{Code (8b)}] \;+\; [\text{Message ID (16b)}] $$ Where: - **Ver** = Version (must be 1). - **T** = Type of message: `CON` (Confirmable), `NON` (Non-confirmable), `ACK` (Acknowledgement), `RST` (Reset). - **TKL** = Token Length (0–8 bytes). - **Code** = Class (3 bits) + Detail (5 bits). E.g., `0.01` = GET, `0.02` = POST, `2.04` = Changed. - **Message ID** = Used to match CON/ACK pairs and detect duplicates. Then follows: **Token** (0–8 bytes) + **Options** (URI-path, Content-Format, etc.) + **Payload marker** (`0xFF`) + **Payload**. ### 2.2.3 Message Types (T field) | Type | Value | Behaviour | |---|---|---| | **CON** | 0 | Confirmable. Receiver **must** send ACK or RST. Retransmit on timeout with exponential backoff. | | **NON** | 1 | Non-confirmable. Fire-and-forget. No ACK. | | **ACK** | 2 | Acknowledges a CON. Echoes the Message ID. | | **RST** | 3 | Reset. Indicates a CON was received but context was missing (e.g., empty ACK for unsolicited CON). | ### 2.2.4 CoAP Reliability Layer CoAP implements its own reliability on top of UDP: - For **CON** messages, the sender starts a retransmission timer (initial timeout = `ACK_TIMEOUT` = 2 seconds by default). - If no ACK arrives, it **exponentially backs off** (doubles) and retransmits up to `MAX_RETRANSMIT` (default 4) times. $$ \text{Retransmit Time} = \text{ACK\_TIMEOUT} \times 2^{(n-1)} \times \text{random}(1, 1.5) $$ where $n$ is the retransmission number. This is exactly the **exponential backoff** used in CSMA/CD Ethernet. ### 2.2.5 CoAP Observe (RFC 7641) A lightweight **publish/subscribe** mode layered on top of CoAP: - A client sends a `GET` with the `Observe` option. - The server keeps sending responses whenever the resource state changes. - Essentially, CoAP can mimic MQTT-like behavior with `Observe`. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Key Parameter / Equation | Default Value | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | MQTT Keep-Alive | $T_{keepalive}$ | Configurable (e.g., 60 s) | | 2 | Broker Disconnect Timeout | $1.5 \times T_{keepalive}$ | — | | 3 | MQTT Max Payload | $256 \; \text{MB}$ | — | | 4 | MQTT Fixed Header | $2 \; \text{bytes}$ (min) | — | | 5 | CoAP Header | $4 \; \text{bytes}$ (min) | — | | 6 | CoAP Port (no DTLS) | $5683$ | — | | 7 | CoAP Port (DTLS) | $5684$ | — | | 8 | CoAP Max Token Length | $8 \; \text{bytes}$ | — | | 9 | CoAP ACK Timeout | $T_{ACK\_TIMEOUT}$ | $2 \; \text{s}$ | | 10 | CoAP Retransmit Backoff | $T_{ACK\_TIMEOUT} \cdot 2^{(n-1)} \cdot R$ | $R \in [1, 1.5]$ random | | 11 | MQTT Port (no TLS) | $1883$ | — | | 12 | MQTT Port (TLS) | $8883$ | — | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **MQTT**: Used by **AWS IoT Core**, **Azure IoT Hub**, **Google Cloud IoT** for billions of device messages. Powering Facebook Messenger's push notifications, smart home platforms (Philips Hue), and Tesla telemetry. - **CoAP**: Used in **smart energy metering (HAN)**, **building automation (BACnet-over-CoAP)**, **industrial wireless sensor networks (ISA100.11a, WirelessHART)**, and **6LoWPAN** deployments. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** When choosing between MQTT vs CoAP for a microcontroller project (e.g., a smart agriculture node using ESP32), consider: > - **Network stability**: If using Wi-Fi with reliable TCP → MQTT is easier (mature libraries like PubSubClient). > - **Power constraints**: If using LoRa, Zigbee, or 6LoWPAN → CoAP over UDP saves ~50% header overhead. > - **Topology**: Many-to-one telemetry → MQTT. Peer-to-peer device control → CoAP. --- # 📘 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: MQTT PUBLISH Packet Size Calculation A common KTU problem: *"Calculate the total bytes used by an MQTT PUBLISH packet for a given topic and payload."* ### 3.1.1 Given Scenario A temperature sensor node publishes the value `{"t":25.4,"h":60}` (20 ASCII characters) to the topic `home/lab/sensor1/temp` (21 characters) with **QoS = 1** and a **16-bit Packet Identifier**. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Analysis **Step 1: Fixed Header** - Control Packet Type for PUBLISH = `0011` (binary) → High nibble = `0x3` → First byte = `0x30` (with QoS = 1 in flags: `0010`). - Remaining Length = size of (Variable Header + Payload). $$ \text{Remaining Length} = 2 + 21 + 2 + 20 = 45 \; \text{bytes} $$ > **Conversion logic:** Topic length (2 bytes, MSB first) + Topic string (21 bytes) + Packet ID (2 bytes) + Payload (20 bytes). Since $45 < 128$, the Remaining Length is encoded in **1 byte** (no continuation bit). **Step 2: Variable Header** - Topic Name Length: `0x00 0x15` (2 bytes → value 21). - Topic Name: `home/lab/sensor1/temp` (21 bytes). - Packet Identifier: `0x00 0x01` (2 bytes, present only for QoS 1 and 2). **Step 3: Payload** - The JSON string `{"t":25.4,"h":60}` (20 bytes). **Step 4: Total Packet Size** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Size} &= \text{Fixed Header} + \text{Remaining Length byte} + \text{Variable Header} + \text{Payload} \\ &= 1 + 1 + (2 + 21 + 2) + 20 \\ &= 47 \; \text{bytes} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Add the wrapping overhead:** Over TCP, this is then wrapped inside an IP + TCP + Ethernet frame (~54 bytes more). So on the wire, the actual frame is **~101 bytes** to send 20 bytes of useful data — this is why MQTT is still considered *lightweight* in the IoT context. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: CoAP Exponential Backoff Timing ### 3.2.1 Problem Setup A CoAP client sends a CON (confirmable) request to a CoAP server. The server is temporarily busy and does not reply with an ACK. Calculate the **retransmission schedule** for $n = 1$ to $4$, assuming `ACK_TIMEOUT = 2 s` and the random factor $R = 1.5$ (worst case). ### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Calculation **Step 1: Recall the formula** $$ T_{retransmit}(n) = T_{ACK\_TIMEOUT} \times 2^{(n-1)} \times R $$ **Step 2: Substitute $T_{ACK\_TIMEOUT} = 2$ s** $$ \begin{aligned} T_1 &= 2 \times 2^{0} \times R = 2 \times 1 \times 1.5 = 3.0 \; \text{s} \\ T_2 &= 2 \times 2^{1} \times R = 2 \times 2 \times 1.5 = 6.0 \; \text{s} \\ T_3 &= 2 \times 2^{2} \times R = 2 \times 4 \times 1.5 = 12.0 \; \text{s} \\ T_4 &= 2 \times 2^{3} \times R = 2 \times 8 \times 1.5 = 24.0 \; \text{s} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Total Time Before Giving Up** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{total} &= T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 \\ &= 3.0 + 6.0 + 12.0 + 24.0 \\ &= 45.0 \; \text{s} \end{aligned} $$ > **Engineering Insight:** This exponential backoff (with randomization $R \in [1, 1.5]$) prevents the **thundering herd problem** — multiple CoAP clients don't synchronize their retransmissions and overload a recovering server. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: MQTT QoS vs CoAP Confirmable | Criteria | MQTT QoS 1 | CoAP CON | |---|---|---| | **Header (Payload + Overhead)** | 2 + var | 4 + var | | **Acknowledgement Packet** | PUBACK (2 bytes) | ACK (4 bytes) | | **Total Round-trips** | 2 (PUBLISH → PUBACK) | 2 (CON → ACK) | | **Duplicate Detection** | Packet ID (16-bit) | Message ID (16-bit) | | **Retransmit Strategy** | At sender's discretion | Exponential backoff | | **Transport** | TCP (already reliable) | UDP (unreliable → CoAP adds reliability) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why CoAP needs its own reliability on UDP:** UDP has *no* guarantee of delivery, ordering, or duplicate protection. CoAP's CON/ACK mechanism plus the 16-bit Message ID provides the reliability that TCP would otherwise give MQTT for free. Hence CoAP's "Confirmable" mode is roughly equivalent to MQTT QoS 1 in semantics. --- ## 3.4 MQTT QoS 2 — The 4-Step Handshake (Analytical Breakdown) QoS 2 guarantees **exactly-once delivery** via a 4-part handshake: | Step | Packet | Direction | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **PUBLISH** | Client → Broker | Sender delivers message with Packet ID = X. | | 2 | **PUBREC** | Broker → Client | Broker acknowledges receipt of PUBLISH. | | 3 | **PUBREL** | Client → Broker | Client confirms release of original message. | | 4 | **PUBCOMP** | Broker → Client | Broker confirms completion. Both parties discard the message. | > **Why 4 steps?** If only 2 steps (like QoS 1) were used, network duplication could cause the message to be received twice. The PUBREL/PUBCOMP pair ensures the broker knows the client is done with the message and won't re-send it. --- # 📘 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 MQTT Publish/Subscribe Architecture (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PUB["Publisher Side"] P1["MCU Sensor 1
Temperature"] P2["MCU Sensor 2
Humidity"] end subgraph BROKER["MQTT Broker
e.g. Mosquitto"] T1["Topic Tree
home/lab/sensor1/temp"] T2["Topic Tree
home/lab/sensor2/humidity"] end subgraph SUB["Subscriber Side"] S1["Cloud Dashboard"] S2["Mobile App"] S3["Database Logger"] end P1 -- PUBLISH QoS 1 --> T1 P2 -- PUBLISH QoS 0 --> T2 T1 -- Push to interested subscribers --> S1 T1 -- Push to interested subscribers --> S3 T2 -- Push to interested subscribers --> S2 style P1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333 style P2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333 style S1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333 style S2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333 style S3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333 style T1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 style T2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** Sensors never talk to the dashboard directly. They hand the message to the broker, which forwards it. Adding/removing a subscriber requires **zero changes** to the publishers — this is the *decoupled* advantage of MQTT. --- ## 4.2 CoAP Request/Response Sequence (Mermaid) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant C as CoAP Client
(MCU Controller) participant S as CoAP Server
(Sensor Node) participant R as Resource
coap://[addr]/sensors/temp C->>S: CON GET (Message ID 0x7A3F) S->>R: Lookup resource R-->>S: Current value = 25.4 degC S-->>C: ACK 2.05 Content (Value: 25.4) Note over C,S: If ACK not received by C within ACK_TIMEOUT C->>S: CON GET (Retransmit, same Message ID) S-->>C: ACK 2.05 Content ``` > **Engineering Insight:** CoAP also supports **NON** (non-confirmable) messages where the client just sends a `GET` and **doesn't care** about the reply — useful for one-way telemetry when you can afford to lose a reading. --- ## 4.3 MQTT PUBLISH Packet — Bit-Level Anatomy (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Byte 0
0x30
Type=PUBLISH | Flags=0000
QoS0, No Retain, No Dup"] B["Byte 1
Remaining Length
e.g. 0x2D = 45"] C["Bytes 2-3
Topic Name Length
0x00 0x15 = 21"] D["Bytes 4-24
Topic Name
home/lab/sensor1/temp"] E["Bytes 25-26
Packet ID
0x00 0x01 (QoS 1+)"] F["Bytes 27-46
Payload
{'t':25.4,'h':60}"] G["Total = 47 bytes
Wrapped in TCP/IP = ~101 bytes on wire"] A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G ``` --- ## 4.4 CoAP Header — Bit-Level Anatomy (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Byte 0
VV TT LLLL
Ver=01 T=00 CON TKL=0100 (4B token)"] B["Byte 1
Code
Class.Detail e.g. 0.01 GET = 0x01"] C["Bytes 2-3
Message ID
0x7A3F (16-bit)"] D["Bytes 4-7
Token (TKL bytes)
0xA1 0xB2 0xC3 0xD4"] E["Bytes 8+
Options
Uri-Path: sensors/temp"] F["0xFF
Payload Marker"] G["Bytes after marker
Payload
25.4"] A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G ``` --- ## 4.5 Comparative Flowchart — When to Use MQTT vs CoAP ```mermaid flowchart TD A["IoT Project Decision:
Which Protocol?"] A --> B{"Network Type?"} B -- "Reliable TCP / Wi-Fi / Ethernet" --> C{"Need Pub/Sub
for many clients?"} B -- "Lossy 6LoWPAN / LoRa / Zigbee" --> D["Use CoAP
UDP-based, low overhead"] C -- "Yes" --> E["Use MQTT
TCP + Broker"] C -- "No, peer-to-peer control" --> F["Use CoAP Observe
or plain CoAP"] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333 style D fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333 style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333 style F fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333 ``` --- # 📘 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]** *List the three Quality of Service (QoS) levels in MQTT and explain what each guarantees.* **Model Answer:** 1. **QoS 0 — At most once:** Message is delivered with no acknowledgement. Best-effort, fire-and-forget. The broker/client does not retransmit. **Use case:** Lossy sensor telemetry where losing one reading is acceptable. 2. **QoS 1 — At least once:** Sender transmits the message until it receives a `PUBACK`. The receiver may receive duplicates if the original ACK was lost. **Use case:** Critical alerts where occasional duplicates are tolerable. 3. **QoS 2 — Exactly once:** A 4-step handshake (`PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP`) ensures the message is delivered exactly once with no duplicates. **Use case:** Billing, medical, or financial data. *(Examiner's note: 1 mark per level + 0.5 marks for an example each. Total = 3 marks.)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]** *Differentiate between MQTT and CoAP in terms of architecture and transport protocol.* **Model Answer:** | Aspect | MQTT | CoAP | |---|---|---| | **Architecture** | Publish / Subscribe (needs a broker) | Request / Response (RESTful, peer-to-peer) | | **Transport** | TCP (port 1883/8883) | UDP (port 5683/5684) | | **Reliability** | Built into TCP + QoS levels | Implemented at CoAP layer (CON/NON + ACK) | | **Header Size** | 2 bytes minimum | 4 bytes minimum | | **Typical Use** | Cloud telemetry, dashboards | Constrained WSN, M2M control | *(Examiner's note: 1 mark for architecture, 1 mark for transport, 1 mark for one more valid difference.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) ### **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]** **Q.A (a)** Explain the MQTT protocol architecture in detail. Describe the roles of the broker, publisher, subscriber, and topic. List the three QoS levels with their packet-level handshake. (7 marks) **Q.A (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the CoAP message format and its four message types. How does CoAP provide reliability over UDP? (7 marks) --- ### Model Solution — Q.A (a) **Architecture (3 marks):** MQTT follows a **publish/subscribe** model. Three main components: - **Publisher**: A client (e.g., a sensor MCU) that sends messages tagged with a topic. - **Subscriber**: A client that registers interest in one or more topics. - **Broker**: A central server (e.g., Mosquitto) that: - Accepts messages from publishers. - Filters messages by topic. - Distributes them to interested subscribers. - Maintains client sessions, handles LWT (Last Will and Testament), and provides authentication (username/password or TLS certificates). - **Topic**: A hierarchical UTF-8 string (e.g., `home/kitchen/temp`) used to route messages. Subscriptions can use `+` (single-level) and `#` (multi-level) wildcards. **QoS Levels (4 marks):** | QoS | Name | Guarantee | Handshake | |---|---|---|---| | **0** | At most once | Best-effort, no ACK | 1 packet: `PUBLISH` | | **1** | At least once | Guaranteed, may duplicate | 2 packets: `PUBLISH` → `PUBACK` | | **2** | Exactly once | Guaranteed, no duplicate | 4 packets: `PUBLISH` → `PUBREC` → `PUBREL` → `PUBCOMP` | **Connection Lifecycle (included for completeness):** The client starts in *Initial* state, sends `CONNECT` with client ID, keep-alive, and credentials. The broker replies with `CONNACK`. The client then maintains the session via periodic `PINGREQ/PINGRESP` packets. On graceful exit, it sends `DISCONNECT`. On abnormal exit, the broker publishes the LWT. --- ### Model Solution — Q.A (b) **CoAP Message Format (4 marks):** A CoAP message has a **4-byte fixed header** followed by a token, options, payload marker (`0xFF`), and payload. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Byte 0} &: \text{Ver (2b)} \;|\; \text{T (2b)} \;|\; \text{TKL (4b)} \\ \text{Byte 1} &: \text{Code (8b) = Class (3b).Detail (5b)} \\ \text{Bytes 2-3} &: \text{Message ID (16b)} \end{aligned} $$ **Four Message Types (2 marks):** - **CON (Confirmable) — T=0**: Receiver must send ACK or RST. Used for reliable messages. - **NON (Non-confirmable) — T=1**: Fire-and-forget. No ACK expected. - **ACK (Acknowledgement) — T=2**: Echoes the Message ID of the corresponding CON. - **RST (Reset) — T=3**: Indicates a CON was received in error (e.g., context missing). Acts like a negative ACK. **Reliability over UDP (1 mark):** CoAP implements its own reliability layer on top of UDP because UDP is connectionless and unreliable. When a CON is sent, the sender starts a retransmission timer initialized to `ACK_TIMEOUT` (default 2 s). If no ACK is received, the message is retransmitted with **exponential backoff** (doubling the timeout) plus a random jitter $R \in [1, 1.5]$. The default `MAX_RETRANSMIT = 4`, after which the client gives up. A 16-bit Message ID allows duplicate detection. --- ## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* ### **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]** **Q.B (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the structure of an MQTT PUBLISH packet (QoS 1). Calculate the total on-wire packet size for a PUBLISH carrying a 15-character topic and a 50-byte JSON payload. (7 marks) **Q.B (b)** Explain the CoAP **Observe** mechanism (RFC 7641). How does it enable a publish/subscribe-like behavior on top of a request/response protocol? Compare it with MQTT in terms of overhead and use case. (7 marks) --- ### Model Solution — Q.B (a) **Structure of MQTT PUBLISH packet (QoS 1) — 4 marks:** - **Byte 0**: `0x32` (Type = PUBLISH = 3 → `0011` in high nibble; Flags = `0010` for QoS 1). - **Byte 1**: Remaining Length (variable, encodes size of variable header + payload). - **Bytes 2-3**: Topic Name Length (2 bytes, MSB first) — value = 15 = `0x00 0x0F`. - **Bytes 4-18**: Topic Name (15 ASCII bytes). - **Bytes 19-20**: Packet Identifier (2 bytes) — required for QoS 1 and 2. - **Bytes 21-70**: Payload (50 bytes of JSON). **Size Calculation — 3 marks:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Variable Header} &= 2 + 15 + 2 = 19 \; \text{bytes} \\ \text{Remaining Length} &= 19 + 50 = 69 \; \text{bytes} \\ \text{Encoding of 69} &: \text{single byte } 0x45 \quad (\text{since } 69 < 128) \\ \text{Fixed Header} &= 1 + 1 = 2 \; \text{bytes} \\ \text{Total MQTT packet} &= 2 + 19 + 50 = 71 \; \text{bytes} \\ \text{TCP/IP + Ethernet wrapper} &\approx 54 \; \text{bytes} \\ \text{Total on the wire} &\approx 71 + 54 = 125 \; \text{bytes} \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion Logic:** For each step, the **MSB-first** network byte order is used. The Remaining Length is encoded using the **continuation-bit scheme** where the MSB = 1 means "more bytes follow"; since 69 fits in 7 bits, only 1 byte is needed. --- ### Model Solution — Q.B (b) **CoAP Observe — 4 marks:** CoAP Observe (RFC 7641) adds a **subscription mode** to CoAP: 1. A client sends a `GET` request with the `Observe` option (value = 0 to register). 2. The server responds with the current state and **remembers** this client. 3. Whenever the resource state **changes**, the server proactively sends a notification (response with the same Token, but the `Observe` option now contains a sequence number). 4. The client may cancel by sending a GET with `Observe` option cancelled. 5. If no notification arrives within ~24 hours, the registration expires and the client must re-register. **How it mimics pub/sub — 1 mark:** The client does not poll; the server pushes state changes. This decouples timing and saves bandwidth, similar to MQTT's broker model. **Comparison with MQTT — 2 marks:** | Aspect | MQTT | CoAP + Observe | |---|---|---| | **Architecture** | Native pub/sub | Request/response extended | | **Transport** | TCP | UDP | | **Overhead** | ~2-byte header + TCP overhead | ~4-byte header + UDP overhead | | **Broker Needed** | Yes (Mosquitto, HiveMQ) | No (server tracks clients) | | **Best For** | Stable cloud, many clients | Constrained WSN, low-power | --- ## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing MQTT QoS 1 and 2:** Many students write *"QoS 1 guarantees exactly-once delivery"* — **WRONG.** Only QoS 2 does. QoS 1 = *at least once* (may duplicate). > 2. **Forgetting the wildcard rule:** Students often think `+` and `#` can be used in *publishing* — they cannot. They are only for *subscriptions*. > 3. **Mixing up CoAP and MQTT ports:** CoAP uses UDP/5683 (no DTLS) and UDP/5684 (DTLS). MQTT uses TCP/1883 and TCP/8883. Confusing 5683 with 1883 is a frequent mark-loser. > 4. **Missing the Token field in CoAP:** The Token (0–8 bytes) is used to match a response to a request when concurrent transactions exist. Don't omit it from the diagram. > 5. **Skipping the LWT explanation:** When asked about MQTT fault detection, students forget the *Last Will and Testament* mechanism. It's a guaranteed 2-3 mark question in KTU. > 6. **CoAP reliability math:** Students often forget the **random jitter** $R \in [1, 1.5]$ in the exponential backoff formula. Always include it. --- ## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **MQTT** = Publish/Subscribe, **TCP-based**, uses a **central broker**, supports **3 QoS levels** (0, 1, 2), and offers **LWT** for fault detection. - **CoAP** = Request/Response, **UDP-based**, uses **4 message types** (CON, NON, ACK, RST), and implements its **own reliability** via exponential backoff. - MQTT default port = **1883** (no TLS) / **8883** (TLS). CoAP default port = **5683** (no DTLS) / **5684** (DTLS). - MQTT minimum header = **2 bytes**; CoAP minimum header = **4 bytes**. - **Wildcards** in MQTT: `+` (single level), `#` (multi-level) — *subscription only*. - **LWT** = Last Will and Testament — a pre-registered message published by the broker when a client disconnects abnormally. - **QoS 2** uses a 4-step handshake: `PUBLISH → PUBREC → PUBREL → PUBCOMP` for exactly-once delivery. - **CoAP Observe** = RFC 7641 feature that adds a pub/sub-like behavior on top of CoAP. - CoAP reliability backoff formula: $T_{retransmit}(n) = T_{ACK\_TIMEOUT} \times 2^{(n-1)} \times R$, where $R \in [1, 1.5]$ random. - **Choose MQTT** for: cloud-connected, TCP-based, many-to-one telemetry with pub/sub. - **Choose CoAP** for: constrained 6LoWPAN/LoRa networks, peer-to-peer RESTful communication, low-power devices. - **Topic** in MQTT = hierarchical string (e.g., `home/lab/sensor1/temp`). - **Resource** in CoAP = URI-based (e.g., `coap://[2001:db8::1]:5683/sensors/temp`). - **Message ID** in CoAP = 16-bit, used for matching CON ↔ ACK and duplicate detection. - **Packet ID** in MQTT = 16-bit, present only in QoS 1 and 2 packets. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Mantra:** *"MQTT = TCP + Broker + Pub/Sub. CoAP = UDP + Direct + Req/Res. Both are binary, lightweight, and designed for tiny MCUs — but they pick different trade-offs in the reliability-vs-overhead spectrum."* ---

topics.title: IoT Security Principles and Common Threats
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** IoT Security Principles and Common Threats

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Internet of Things (IoT) Security** is the umbrella discipline of cyber-physical protection strategies, cryptographic primitives, hardware-rooted trust mechanisms, and protocol-level safeguards engineered to preserve the **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (CIA Triad)** of resource-constrained embedded devices, the sensor data they generate, and the communication channels that interconnect them within an IoT ecosystem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PBCST504, Module 4):**
> The university expects students to articulate the **principles of IoT security** (CIA Triad, authentication, authorization, non-repudiation) and to enumerate the **common threat categories** (device tampering, eavesdropping, replay, man-in-the-middle, firmware hijack, DoS) faced by microcontroller-based edge nodes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Home" Bank Vault

Imagine an **automatic teller machine (ATM)** sitting in your living room. Every time you withdraw money, the ATM must (i) **verify it is really you** (Authentication), (ii) **hide your PIN from people shoulder-surfing behind you** (Confidentiality), (iii) **guarantee the dispensed cash amount is not tampered with mid-transit** (Integrity), and (iv) **remain operational even if 100 fake cards are inserted in one second** (Availability).

In the IoT world, a microcontroller-based node (e.g., a temperature sensor, smart lock, or wearable heart-rate monitor) plays the role of this ATM. The "money" is your sensor data and actuator commands. The "shoulder-surfer" is an attacker sniffing Wi-Fi traffic. The "fake cards" are malformed packets flooding the network. **IoT security is the vault door, the PIN pad, the dye-pack, and the queue management system — all rolled into the embedded firmware.**

## 1.3 The CIA Triad — Foundation of Every Security Policy

| Pillar | One-Line Definition | IoT-Specific Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Ensuring data is disclosed only to authorized entities | Preventing eavesdropping on wireless sensor telemetry |
| **Integrity** | Ensuring data is not altered in transit or at rest | Detecting bit-flips injected into a firmware update image |
| **Availability** | Ensuring systems remain accessible when needed | Defending a smart-grid node from Denial-of-Service (DoS) floods |

> [!NOTE]
> **Non-Repudiation** is often appended as a fourth pillar in IoT literature — it ensures a sender cannot later deny having issued a command (critical for medical IoT and industrial SCADA systems).

## 1.4 The Three Realms of IoT Security

1. **Device-Level Security** — physical tamper resistance, secure boot, hardware random number generators, Trusted Platform Modules (TPM).
2. **Communication-Level Security** — encryption (TLS/DTLS), authentication handshakes, message authentication codes.
3. **Cloud/Application-Level Security** — API gateways, role-based access control (RBAC), secure firmware Over-The-Air (OTA) pipelines.

**Standard benchmark metrics** to memorize for KTU viva: **AES-128** (128-bit key symmetric block cipher), **SHA-256** (256-bit cryptographic hash), **RSA-2048** (2048-bit asymmetric modulus), and **ECC-256** (equivalent to RSA-3072 in strength with smaller key).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Foundational IoT Security Principles

### 2.1.1 Principle 1 — *Defense in Depth*
A **layered security architecture** in which no single control failure compromises the entire system. Each layer (physical, hardware, firmware, network, application) is an independent failsafe.

> **Why it matters in microcontrollers:** A 32-bit MCU often lacks an MMU, so a single buffer overflow in a UART handler can grant shell access. Defense-in-depth ensures the Wi-Fi SoC, the crypto accelerator, and the secure element all enforce policies independently.

### 2.1.2 Principle 2 — *Least Privilege*
Every process, user, and device receives the **minimum set of permissions** required to perform its designated task. A temperature sensor firmware should never possess the privilege to flash the bootloader.

### 2.1.3 Principle 3 — *Zero Trust*
**Never trust, always verify.** Each request — even from a device already inside the LAN — must be authenticated, authorized, and logged. This is the architectural antithesis of the legacy "perimeter firewall" model.

### 2.1.4 Principle 4 — *End-to-End Encryption (E2EE)*
Data is encrypted at the originating sensor and decrypted only at the authorized sink. Intermediate gateways, brokers, and routers see only ciphertext.

### 2.1.5 Principle 5 — *Secure Boot & Measured Trust*
At power-on, a **hardware root of trust** (immutable ROM bootloader) cryptographically verifies the signature of every subsequent firmware stage before execution. A chain of hashes is built — if any link is broken, the device halts.

### 2.1.6 Principle 6 — *Resilience & Graceful Degradation*
A compromised or malfunctioning node must **fail safely** without cascading failures. Smart-grid inverters must disconnect cleanly; insulin pumps must revert to a safe delivery profile.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Typical Use in KTU Problems |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Password Entropy | $H = L \cdot \log_2(N)$ | Bits of strength in a password |
| 2 | Caesar Cipher Encryption | $C \equiv (P + K) \pmod{26}$ | Toy encryption example in viva |
| 3 | Caesar Cipher Decryption | $P \equiv (C - K) \pmod{26}$ | Reversing toy cipher |
| 4 | XOR Symmetric Property | $A \oplus B \oplus B = A$ | Stream-cipher & one-time-pad proof |
| 5 | Hash Avalanche Effect | $\Delta H \geq 50\%$ of output bits | SHA-256 design criteria |
| 6 | RSA Modulus Selection | $n = p \cdot q$ | Public-key generation |
| 7 | RSA Encryption | $C \equiv P^{e} \pmod{n}$ | Asymmetric encryption step |
| 8 | RSA Decryption | $P \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$ | Asymmetric decryption step |
| 9 | Time to Brute-Force | $T = \frac{2^{H}}{2 \cdot R}$ | Average work-factor for attacker |
| 10 | Birthday Attack Bound | $k \approx 1.177 \cdot \sqrt{N}$ | Hash collision probability threshold |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols used:**
> $H$ = entropy in bits, $L$ = password length, $N$ = alphabet/cardinality, $P$ = plaintext, $C$ = ciphertext, $K$ = key, $R$ = guesses per second, $p,q$ = large primes, $e,d$ = public/private exponents.

## 2.3 Cryptographic Primitives — The Three Pillars

### 2.3.1 Symmetric Encryption
A **single shared secret key** $K$ is used for both encryption and decryption. Examples: **AES-128**, **ChaCha20**. Fast, ideal for streaming sensor data on resource-constrained MCUs.

$$E_{K}(P) = C \quad \text{and} \quad D_{K}(C) = P$$

### 2.3.2 Asymmetric Encryption
