A mathematically linked **key pair** $(e, d)$ is generated such that knowing $e$ does not feasibly reveal $d$. Examples: **RSA**, **ECC (Curve25519)**. Used for key-exchange and digital signatures — computationally expensive, often offloaded to a secure element.

### 2.3.3 Cryptographic Hashing
A **one-way, deterministic, collision-resistant** function $h : \{0,1\}^{*} \rightarrow \{0,1\}^{n}$ maps arbitrary input to a fixed-length digest. Examples: **SHA-256**, **SHA-3**. Used for firmware integrity checks and password storage.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial deployments, these principles underpin **smart-grid SCADA**, **connected medical devices (FDA 510(k) cybersecurity guidance)**, **automotive CAN-bus security (AUTOSAR SecOC)**, and **consumer smart-home ecosystems (Matter/Thread security layer)**. KTU expects graduates to be conversant with the vocabulary expected by industry certifications such as **CompTIA IoT+** and **Certified IoT Security Practitioner (CIoTSP)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedagogical Note:** This section derives the three most examination-relevant mathematical results in IoT security. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly — no skips, no shortcuts.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Password Entropy and Brute-Force Work Factor

**Problem Statement:** A smart-lock accepts a 6-character alphanumeric password (uppercase + lowercase + digits). Calculate the entropy and the average time to brute-force at $10^{4}$ guesses/second.

### Step 1 — Determine the Alphabet Cardinality $N$

The alphabet consists of three disjoint sets:

$$N = \underbrace{26}_{\text{uppercase}} + \underbrace{26}_{\text{lowercase}} + \underbrace{10}_{\text{digits}} = 62$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Entropy Formula

Each character is chosen independently and uniformly. The total number of candidate passwords is $N^{L}$ where $L$ is the length. The entropy (log base 2) is:

$$H = L \cdot \log_{2}(N)$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Numerical Values

$$H = 6 \cdot \log_{2}(62)$$

Using $\log_{2}(62) = \log_{10}(62)/\log_{10}(2) = 1.7924 / 0.30103 \approx 5.954$:

$$H = 6 \times 5.954$$

$$H = 35.724 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Average Brute-Force Time

On average, the attacker must examine half the keyspace. The work factor in seconds is:

$$T = \frac{2^{H}}{2 \cdot R}$$

Substituting $H = 35.724$ and $R = 10^{4}$ guesses/sec:

$$T = \frac{2^{35.724}}{2 \times 10^{4}}$$

Since $2^{35.724} \approx 5.34 \times 10^{10}$:

$$T = \frac{5.34 \times 10^{10}}{2 \times 10^{4}}$$

$$T = 2.67 \times 10^{6} \text{ seconds}$$

Converting to days:

$$T = \frac{2.67 \times 10^{6}}{86400} \approx 30.9 \text{ days}$$

**Conclusion:** A 6-character alphanumeric password is weak — a determined attacker can crack it in roughly one month. Increase to 12 characters (entropy doubles) to raise the work factor to ~3 centuries.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Caesar Cipher Encryption of "KTMU" with Key $K = 7$

**Problem Statement:** Encrypt the plaintext **"KTMU"** using Caesar cipher with shift key $K = 7$, and verify decryption yields the original.

### Step 1 — Convert Letters to Numeric Positions (A=0, B=1, ..., Z=25)

The letter-to-integer mapping:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &\rightarrow 10 \\
T &\rightarrow 19 \\
M &\rightarrow 12 \\
U &\rightarrow 20
\end{aligned}
$$

So the plaintext vector is $P = (10, 19, 12, 20)$.

### Step 2 — Apply the Encryption Formula

The encryption function is:

$$C_{i} \equiv (P_{i} + K) \pmod{26}$$

For each character:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{1} &\equiv (10 + 7) \pmod{26} = 17 \rightarrow R \\
C_{2} &\equiv (19 + 7) \pmod{26} = 26 \equiv 0 \rightarrow A \\
C_{3} &\equiv (12 + 7) \pmod{26} = 19 \rightarrow T \\
C_{4} &\equiv (20 + 7) \pmod{26} = 27 \equiv 1 \rightarrow B
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ciphertext = "RATB"**

### Step 3 — Decrypt Using the Inverse Function

The decryption function is:

$$P_{i} \equiv (C_{i} - K) \pmod{26}$$

For each ciphertext character:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{1} &\equiv (17 - 7) \pmod{26} = 10 \rightarrow K \\
P_{2} &\equiv (0 - 7) \pmod{26} = -7 \equiv 19 \rightarrow T \\
P_{3} &\equiv (19 - 7) \pmod{26} = 12 \rightarrow M \\
P_{4} &\equiv (1 - 7) \pmod{26} = -6 \equiv 20 \rightarrow U
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decrypted = "KTMU" ✓**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — XOR Stream Cipher Round-Trip Proof

**Problem Statement:** Prove that a symmetric XOR cipher is its own inverse, i.e., encrypting twice with the same key recovers the plaintext.

### Step 1 — Encryption Step

Given plaintext byte $P$ and key stream byte $K$, ciphertext is:

$$C = P \oplus K$$

### Step 2 — Decryption Step

The receiver applies the same XOR with the same $K$:

$$C \oplus K = (P \oplus K) \oplus K$$

### Step 3 — Apply XOR Associativity

$$(P \oplus K) \oplus K = P \oplus (K \oplus K)$$

### Step 4 — Apply XOR Self-Inverse Property

Since $K \oplus K = 0$ for any bit pattern (XOR of identical bits yields 0):

$$P \oplus 0 = P$$

### Step 5 — Conclusion

$$
\begin{aligned}
C \oplus K = P
\end{aligned}
$$

**Therefore**, the XOR operation is its own inverse. This is the algebraic foundation of the **One-Time Pad (OTP)** and stream ciphers like **ChaCha20**, both of which are widely deployed on resource-constrained microcontrollers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Security caveat:** The XOR construction is **only** information-theoretically secure when the key stream is truly random, never reused, and at least as long as the plaintext — hence the name "One-Time Pad."

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Threat vs. Mitigation Mapping

| Threat Category | Specific Attack | Primary CIA Pillar Violated | Recommended Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Eavesdropping** | Packet sniffing on Zigbee/Wi-Fi | Confidentiality | AES-128-CCM, TLS 1.3 |
| **Man-in-the-Middle** | ARP spoofing, SSL stripping | Confidentiality + Integrity | Mutual TLS, certificate pinning |
| **Replay Attack** | Resending old authenticated packets | Integrity + Authenticity | Nonce + timestamp window |
| **DDoS / DoS** | SYN flood on constrained node | Availability | Rate-limiting, queue isolation |
| **Firmware Tampering** | Malicious OTA injection | Integrity | Signed firmware, secure boot |
| **Physical Tamper** | JTAG/SWD debug port abuse | All three | Disable debug, eFuse lock bits |
| **Side-Channel** | Power analysis on AES | Confidentiality | Constant-time crypto, masking |
| **Privilege Escalation** | Buffer overflow in C firmware | All three | Stack canaries, MPU, ASLR-lite |
| **Data Injection** | False sensor readings | Integrity | Message Authentication Code (MAC) |
| **Eavesdropping on RF** | Sub-GHz sniffing | Confidentiality | Frequency hopping, AES |

> **Worked example interpretation:** A replay attack violates both *Integrity* (the "old" packet was not a fresh observation) and *Authenticity* (the system cannot verify freshness). The mitigation combines a cryptographic nonce (a number used once) with a tight timestamp window, ensuring each command is unique in time.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters. Labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text only — no markdown formatting inside node text.

## 4.1 IoT Security Architecture — Block-Level Functional Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[PHYSICAL LAYER\nTamper Switches\nShielding] --> B1
    A2[HARDWARE LAYER\nSecure Element\nTPM eFuse] --> B1
    B1[FIRMWARE LAYER\nSecure Boot\nCode Signing] --> C1
    B2[RTOS LAYER\nMPU Isolation\nStack Canaries] --> C1
    C1[COMMUNICATION LAYER\nTLS DTLS\nMAC Verification] --> D1
    C2[APPLICATION LAYER\nRBAC ACL\nInput Validation] --> D1
    D1[CLOUD ORCHESTRATION\nOTA Pipeline\nAudit Logging] --> E1
    E1[TRUSTED END STATE\nCIA Triad Preserved]

    style A1 fill:#fde2e2
    style A2 fill:#fde2e2
    style B1 fill:#ffe9c2
    style B2 fill:#ffe9c2
    style C1 fill:#d4f1d4
    style C2 fill:#d4f1d4
    style D1 fill:#cfe4f5
    style E1 fill:#e0d4f5
```

## 4.2 Attack Surface Map of a Typical IoT Node

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[UART DEBUG] --> G1
    F2[JTAG SWD PORTS] --> G1
    F3[WIFI RADIO] --> G1
    F4[BLE RADIO] --> G1
    F5[FLASH MEMORY] --> G1
    F6[POWER LINE] --> G1
    G1[ATTACK SURFACE\nENTRY POINTS] --> H1
    H1[ATTACKER PROFILE] --> I1
    I1[IMPACT\nCIA TRIAD BREACH]

    style F1 fill:#fff3cd
    style F2 fill:#fff3cd
    style F3 fill:#fff3cd
    style F4 fill:#fff3cd
    style F5 fill:#fff3cd
    style F6 fill:#fff3cd
    style G1 fill:#f8d7da
    style H1 fill:#f5c2c7
    style I1 fill:#dc3545,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Secure Boot Chain of Trust — Sequence View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    J1[POWER ON RESET] --> J2
    J2[IMMUTABLE ROM BOOTLOADER\nHardware Root of Trust] --> J3
    J3{STAGE 1 SIGNATURE VALID}
    J3 -- YES --> J4[LOAD STAGE 2 BOOTLOADER\nVerify RSA 2048]
    J3 -- NO --> J5[HALT AND LOCK\nTamper Response]
    J4 --> J6{STAGE 2 SIGNATURE VALID}
    J6 -- YES --> J7[LOAD APPLICATION FIRMWARE\nVerify SHA 256]
    J6 -- NO --> J5
    J7 --> J8[EXECUTE TRUSTED APPLICATION]

    style J1 fill:#e0d4f5
    style J2 fill:#cfe4f5
    style J3 fill:#ffe9c2
    style J4 fill:#d4f1d4
    style J5 fill:#dc3545,color:#fff
    style J6 fill:#ffe9c2
    style J7 fill:#d4f1d4
    style J8 fill:#cfe4f5
```

## 4.4 Threat Response Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1[ANOMALY DETECTED] --> K2
    K2[ANALYZE PACKET OR EVENT] --> K3
    K3{THREAT TYPE}
    K3 -- EAVESDROPPING --> K4[ROTATE KEYS\nALERT ADMIN]
    K3 -- REPLAY --> K5[INVALIDATE NONCE\nBLOCK SOURCE]
    K3 -- DOS FLOOD --> K6[RATE LIMIT\nFAIL SAFE MODE]
    K3 -- TAMPER --> K7[ZEROIZE SECRETS\nDISABLE DEVICE]
    K4 --> L1[LOG AND ESCALATE]
    K5 --> L1
    K6 --> L1
    K7 --> L1

    style K1 fill:#e0d4f5
    style K2 fill:#cfe4f5
    style K3 fill:#ffe9c2
    style K4 fill:#d4f1d4
    style K5 fill:#d4f1d4
    style K6 fill:#d4f1d4
    style K7 fill:#dc3545,color:#fff
    style L1 fill:#cfe4f5
```

> **Reading guide:** Each diagram is engineered to be reproducible in a KTU theory answer. The shaded color bands group the layers by **trust depth** — the further inward, the more trusted the component.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B, Q.4.a]**
*List and briefly define the three pillars of the CIA Triad as applicable to IoT security.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Confidentiality (1 mark):** Ensuring that IoT sensor data and actuator commands are accessible only to entities that have been explicitly authorized. Practical example: encryption of MQTT payloads using AES-128 over an open Wi-Fi network.
2. **Integrity (1 mark):** Ensuring that the data has not been modified — accidentally or maliciously — during transmission or storage. Practical example: appending a SHA-256 hash to a firmware update image before flashing.
3. **Availability (1 mark):** Ensuring that the IoT system and its services remain accessible to legitimate users when required. Practical example: rate-limiting incoming packets to a smart meter to survive a DoS flood.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set A, Q.4.b]**
*What is the purpose of a "secure boot" mechanism in an IoT device? Mention the role of the hardware root of trust.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Secure Boot (2 marks):** It is a chain-of-trust verification process that runs at every power-on. The immutable bootloader cryptographically validates the digital signature of each subsequent firmware stage (bootloader → application) before allowing execution. Any unsigned or modified code causes the device to halt.
- **Hardware Root of Trust (1 mark):** This is a small, immutable piece of code stored in ROM during chip fabrication. It holds the public key used to verify the first signed firmware image. Because the attacker cannot modify ROM, this is the unbreakable anchor of the entire trust chain.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, Internal Choice 1]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Defense-in-Depth** security architecture for an IoT system. List the five layers and the primary security control deployed at each. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An RFID-based attendance system uses a 4-digit PIN (0–9). Calculate the entropy of the PIN and the average brute-force cracking time if an attacker can attempt $100$ PINs per second. State whether the system is "secure." Justify. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — (a)

Defense-in-Depth is a security strategy in which **multiple independent layers of control** are deployed so that the failure of any single layer does not lead to total system compromise.

The five layers are:

1. **Physical Layer (1 mark):** Tamper-evident enclosures, epoxy potting, intrusion detection switches. If the attacker opens the case, the device wipes its memory.
2. **Hardware Layer (1.5 marks):** Secure Element / TPM stores keys in tamper-resistant hardware. JTAG and SWD debug ports are disabled in production via eFuse bits.
3. **Firmware / RTOS Layer (1.5 marks):** Secure boot verifies signatures, MPU enforces memory isolation between tasks, stack canaries detect buffer overflows.
4. **Network / Communication Layer (1.5 marks):** TLS 1.3 or DTLS 1.2 for transport security, MAC verification on each packet, mutual authentication.
5. **Cloud / Application Layer (1.5 marks):** Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), API rate limiting, signed firmware Over-The-Air (OTA) updates, audit logging.

**Block diagram (1 mark) — refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram** for the layered architecture sketch.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Cardinality of the alphabet (1 mark):**

The PIN digit can take values 0 through 9, so:

$$N = 10$$

**Step 2 — Password length (1 mark):**

$$L = 4$$

**Step 3 — Entropy calculation (2 marks):**

$$H = L \cdot \log_{2}(N) = 4 \cdot \log_{2}(10)$$

Since $\log_{2}(10) \approx 3.3219$:

$$H = 4 \times 3.3219$$

$$H = 13.288 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Average brute-force time (2 marks):**

The keyspace size is $N^{L} = 10^{4} = 10{,}000$ candidates. On average, the attacker tests half the keyspace. At $R = 100$ guesses/second:

$$T = \frac{10^{4}}{2 \times 100} = \frac{10{,}000}{200} = 50 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 5 — Security verdict (1 mark):**

The system is **NOT secure**. An attacker can exhaustively enumerate all 10,000 PINs in approximately 50 seconds — well within an acceptable attack window. To raise entropy to 64 bits, the PIN length should be increased to at least $\lceil 64 / 3.3219 \rceil = 20$ digits, or the system should lock the account after 3 failed attempts.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4, Internal Choice 2]**

**(a)** Explain the **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** attack on an IoT network. Describe two distinct mitigation techniques. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A microcontroller uses a Caesar cipher with key $K = 15$ to encrypt the plaintext **"HELLO"**. Show all modular arithmetic steps to obtain the ciphertext, then demonstrate the decryption process to recover the original plaintext. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Man-in-the-Middle Attack — Definition (2 marks):**
A MITM attack occurs when an adversary positions themselves logically between two communicating IoT endpoints (e.g., between a sensor node and the cloud gateway). The attacker **intercepts, relays, and potentially alters** messages without either endpoint realizing the relay exists. The attack violates Confidentiality, Integrity, and Authenticity simultaneously.

**Attack Sequence (1.5 marks):**
1. Attacker performs ARP spoofing on the local network, poisoning the gateway's ARP table.
2. The sensor node unknowingly forwards packets to the attacker's MAC address.
3. The attacker reads, modifies, and re-injects packets toward the legitimate gateway.

**Mitigation 1 — Mutual TLS with Certificate Pinning (2 marks):**
Both the IoT device and the cloud server possess X.509 certificates signed by a trusted CA. During the TLS handshake, each side presents its certificate; the peer validates the chain. Certificate pinning ensures the device rejects any certificate not in its pre-flashed trust store, defeating rogue CA injection.

**Mitigation 2 — Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange with Authentication (1.5 marks):**
ECDH (Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman) is used to establish a shared secret over an insecure channel, but the parameters are signed using ECDSA to prevent the attacker from substituting his own key. Even if the attacker intercepts the exchange, he cannot forge the signature.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Letter-to-number mapping (1 mark):**

Using A=0, B=1, ..., Z=25:

$$H = 7, \quad E = 4, \quad L = 11, \quad L = 11, \quad O = 14$$

So plaintext vector: $P = (7, 4, 11, 11, 14)$ and key $K = 15$.

**Step 2 — Apply encryption $C \equiv (P + K) \pmod{26}$ (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{1} &\equiv (7 + 15) \pmod{26} = 22 \rightarrow W \\
C_{2} &\equiv (4 + 15) \pmod{26} = 19 \rightarrow T \\
C_{3} &\equiv (11 + 15) \pmod{26} = 26 \equiv 0 \rightarrow A \\
C_{4} &\equiv (11 + 15) \pmod{26} = 0 \rightarrow A \\
C_{5} &\equiv (14 + 15) \pmod{26} = 29 \equiv 3 \rightarrow D
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ciphertext = "WTAAD"**

**Step 3 — Decryption $P \equiv (C - K) \pmod{26}$ (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{1} &\equiv (22 - 15) \pmod{26} = 7 \rightarrow H \\
P_{2} &\equiv (19 - 15) \pmod{26} = 4 \rightarrow E \\
P_{3} &\equiv (0 - 15) \pmod{26} = -15 \equiv 11 \rightarrow L \\
P_{4} &\equiv (0 - 15) \pmod{26} = 11 \rightarrow L \\
P_{5} &\equiv (3 - 15) \pmod{26} = -12 \equiv 14 \rightarrow O
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recovered plaintext = "HELLO" ✓**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the modular wrap-around** — When $(P_i + K) \geq 26$, students frequently write $27$ instead of $1$. The modulo operation is the *only* way to keep letters within A–Z. **Penalty: 1 mark lost per error.**
> 2. **Using 1–26 instead of 0–25** — Both conventions are mathematically valid, but **inconsistency** between encryption and decryption is the single most common mistake. Pick one convention and stick to it throughout.
> 3. **In entropy problems, forgetting to multiply by $L$** — A 4-digit PIN has entropy $4 \log_2(10)$, not just $\log_2(10)$. This single error typically costs the entire 2 marks of the entropy sub-step.
> 4. **Omitting the "average = half the keyspace" assumption** in brute-force calculations — Some students compute $T = N^L / R$ instead of $T = N^L / (2R)$. Always state that brute-force on average covers half the keyspace.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CIA Triad** is the bedrock: every IoT security policy must address Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability (and often Non-Repudiation).
- **Six core principles** to memorize verbatim: Defense in Depth, Least Privilege, Zero Trust, End-to-End Encryption, Secure Boot, Graceful Degradation.
- **Top cryptographic primitives** for KTU 2024: AES-128 (symmetric), SHA-256 (hashing), RSA-2048 / ECC-256 (asymmetric), ChaCha20 (stream).
- **The Caesar cipher formula** $C \equiv (P + K) \pmod{26}$ and its decryption inverse $P \equiv (C - K) \pmod{26}$ — memorize the *modulo wrap-around* logic.
- **Password entropy** is computed as $H = L \cdot \log_2(N)$. A 6-character alphanumeric password yields ~36 bits — too weak for production.
- **XOR self-inverse property** $(A \oplus K \oplus K = A)$ underpins every stream cipher and the One-Time Pad.
- **Secure Boot chain of trust** always starts from an immutable ROM bootloader — this is the unbreakable root.
- **Top 5 attack categories to enumerate in viva:** Eavesdropping, MITM, Replay, DoS/DDoS, Firmware Tampering.
- **Two encryption families** to differentiate: Symmetric (single key, fast) vs Asymmetric (key pair, slow, used for exchange/signing).
- **Defense-in-Depth** has **five canonical layers** — Physical, Hardware, Firmware, Network, Application.
- **Mutual authentication** defeats MITM; **nonces** defeat replay; **rate-limiting** defeats DoS; **signed firmware** defeats tampering; **disabled debug ports** defeat physical attacks.
- **The Birthday Bound** $k \approx 1.177 \sqrt{N}$ is the magic number for hash collision probability — useful in viva for explaining why SHA-256 is preferred over MD5.
- **Exam mantra for 14-mark answers:** always pair the **conceptual diagram (3–4 marks)** + **layered explanation (3–4 marks)** + **worked numerical example (3–4 marks)** + **security conclusion (1 mark)**. This 4-part structure mirrors the KTU model answer key and consistently fetches full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless Communication: Interfacing GSM (Call, Internet)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wireless Communication: Interfacing GSM (Call, Internet)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications)** is a **digital cellular communication standard** that operates on the **900 MHz / 1800 MHz** bands (with 850/1900 MHz variants for the Americas). In embedded systems, "GSM Interfacing" refers to the hardware and software integration of a **GSM/GPRS modem module** (e.g., **SIM800**, **SIM900**, **SIM800C**) with a microcontroller unit (MCU) over an **asynchronous serial (UART) link**, allowing the embedded system to make/receive voice calls, send/receive SMS, and access the internet through **GPRS (General Packet Radio Service)** using **AT (Hayes) commands**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Talking Robot"**
> Imagine a deaf-mute robot (your microcontroller) that wants to make a phone call. It cannot speak or listen directly to the cellular network. The **GSM module** is like a **translator assistant** standing next to the robot, holding a mobile phone. The robot tells the translator what to do in a simple short-hand language (the **AT commands**, e.g., *"ATD9847..."* means *"dial this number"*), and the translator handles the entire cellular tower protocol, voice encoding, RF transmission, and SIM card authentication. The robot only needs to send text strings over a single wire pair (TX-RX) and listen for replies (OK, CONNECT, RING, etc.).

### 1.3 Key Technical Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Operating Voltage:** **3.4 V – 4.4 V** (typical **4.0 V**), requires a high-current supply capable of delivering **2 A peak** during RF burst transmission.
> - **Default UART Baud Rate:** **9600 bps** (8-N-1: 8 data bits, No parity, 1 stop bit).
> - **SIM Interface:** **1.8 V / 3.0 V** compatible SIM card holder.
> - **Frequency Bands:** **Quad-band** EGSM 900, DCS 1800; plus GSM 850/1900 (model dependent).
> - **Data Service:** **GPRS multi-slot class 12**, downlink up to **85.6 kbps**.

### 1.4 Common GSM Modules in KTU Curriculum

| Module | Bands | GPRS | Stack Height | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SIM800C** | Quad | Class 12 | Compact | IoT / KTU Labs |
| **SIM900A** | Dual (900/1800) | Class 10 | Large | Academic projects |
| **SIM800L** | Quad | Class 12 | Tiny (15×17 mm) | Wearable IoT |
| **SIM28ML** | GPS only | — | — | (Not for GSM, just for ref.) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The AT Command Protocol

AT commands are **textual instruction strings** standardized by the **Hayes command set** (1981), extended by 3GPP for GSM modules. Every command begins with the prefix **`AT`** (meaning **ATtention**), followed by a command body and terminated by **`<CR>` (Carriage Return, ASCII 0x0D)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two response types from GSM module:**
> - **Result Code:** `OK`, `ERROR`, `+CME ERROR: <code>`
> - **Unsolicited Result Code (URC):** `RING`, `+CMTI: "SM",5` (incoming SMS), `+CLIP: "+91xxxxxxxxxx"` (caller line ID)

### 2.2 The Three Logical "Services" of GSM Interfacing

1. **Voice Service (CSD / Telephony):** Uses **ATD** (dial), **ATA** (answer), **ATH** (hang up).
2. **SMS Service:** Uses **AT+CMGF** (mode), **AT+CMGS** (send), **AT+CMGR** (read).
3. **Packet Data (GPRS / Internet):** Uses **AT+CGATT** (attach), **AT+CIPSTART** (TCP/UDP socket), **AT+CIPSEND** (transmit), **AT+CIPCLOSE** (tear down).

### 2.3 UART Frame Format & Baud Rate

Every byte transmitted to/from the GSM module is wrapped in a standard UART frame:

```
| Start (0) | D0 D1 D2 D3 D4 D5 D6 D7 | Parity (opt.) | Stop (1) |
```

For the **8051 family** (a KTU-favoured MCU), Timer 1 in **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** drives the UART. The reload value for a given baud rate is given by the **KTU High-Yield Formula**:

$$
\text{TH1} \;=\; 256 \;-\; \frac{F_{\text{osc}}}{32 \times 2^{\text{SMOD}} \times \text{Baud}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SMOD** is the **PCON.7** bit of the Power Control register.
> - **SMOD = 0** → divisor ×32 (standard).
> - **SMOD = 1** → divisor ×16 (double baud rate).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | UART TH1 (8051) | $TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{F_{osc}}{32 \cdot 2^{SMOD} \cdot \text{Baud}}$ | Decimal value to load into Timer-1 high byte |
| 2 | TH1 for 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz, SMOD=0 | $256 - \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 9600} = 253 = 0xFD$ | **Standard KTU lab value** |
| 3 | TH1 for 4800 baud @ 11.0592 MHz, SMOD=0 | $256 - \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 4800} = 250 = 0xFA$ | For slower, reliable links |
| 4 | Frame duration (10 bits) | $T_{frame} = \dfrac{10}{\text{Baud}}$ | Seconds (e.g., 1.04 ms @ 9600) |
| 5 | GSM Burst Current | $I_{burst} \approx 2.0$ A peak | **Decoupling cap ≥ 1000 µF mandatory** |
| 6 | GPRS max downlink (Class 12) | $\approx 85.6$ kbps | Theoretical; practical ~ 40-50 kbps |
| 7 | SMS max length (standard GSM 03.40) | 160 chars (7-bit) or 140 octets (8-bit) or 70 chars (UCS-2) | In text mode |
| 8 | GSM Tx slot duty cycle | $\dfrac{1}{8}$ (one slot of TDMA frame) | Burst transmitted in 577 µs |
| 9 | RF Power Class (GSM 900) | Class 4: **2 W** (33 dBm) | Mobile-station maximum |
| 10 | Logic-level shift (3.3 V ↔ 5 V) | Use voltage divider $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$ | R1=1k, R2=2k → 3.3 V safe for 5 V MCU |

### 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!TIP]
> - **Vehicle tracking / Fleet management:** GPS + GSM module reports coordinates over GPRS TCP socket to a server.
> - **Smart energy meters:** GSM sends monthly consumption via SMS to the utility server.
> - **Industrial SCADA alarms:** Critical alarms trigger a voice call to the plant manager.
> - **Health wearables / Remote patient monitoring:** Sends vitals over HTTPS to a cloud API.
> - **Home security:** PIR / door sensor triggers an SMS and a phone call to the owner.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Protocols & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: 8051 Timer-1 Reload Value for GSM UART

> **Problem (frequently asked in KTU):** Compute TH1 to communicate with a SIM800 GSM module (default 9600 bps) using an 8051 with **11.0592 MHz** crystal and **SMOD = 0**.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation for Mode 2 auto-reload UART:**

$$
\text{Baud} \;=\; \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \cdot \frac{F_{osc}}{256 - \text{TH1}}
$$

**Step 2 — Algebraically isolate TH1:**

$$
256 - \text{TH1} \;=\; \frac{2^{SMOD} \cdot F_{osc}}{32 \cdot \text{Baud}}
$$

$$
\text{TH1} \;=\; 256 - \frac{2^{SMOD} \cdot F_{osc}}{32 \cdot \text{Baud}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the given values** ($F_{osc} = 11.0592 \times 10^6$, SMOD = 0, Baud = 9600$):

$$
\text{TH1} \;=\; 256 - \frac{1 \times 11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 9600}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the fraction:**

$$
\frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{32 \times 9600} \;=\; \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{307200} \;=\; 36
$$

*(This exact integer is precisely why the **11.0592 MHz** crystal is the "magic" frequency — it removes fractional errors.)*

**Step 5 — Final calculation:**

$$
\text{TH1} \;=\; 256 - 36 \;=\; 220
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TH1} \;=\; 0xDC \;\;(\text{in hex})}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** If your KTU paper quotes **12 MHz** crystal, you get TH1 = 249.7 → non-integer → rounded. The **11.0592 MHz** is the *de facto* KTU lab standard for clean UART.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Voltage Divider for 5 V → 3.3 V Level Shifting

> **Problem:** The 8051 transmits at **5 V** TTL but the SIM800 UART is **3.3 V tolerant**. Design a resistor divider.

**Step 1 — Use the unloaded voltage-divider equation:**

$$
V_{out} \;=\; V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
$$

**Step 2 — Choose R1 and R2 such that $V_{out} \approx 3.3$ V when $V_{in} = 5$ V. Common choice: R1 = 1 kΩ, R2 = 2 kΩ.**

$$
V_{out} \;=\; 5 \cdot \frac{2\,\text{k}\Omega}{1\,\text{k}\Omega + 2\,\text{k}\Omega} \;=\; 5 \cdot \frac{2}{3} \;=\; 3.33\;\text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify current draw:** $I = \dfrac{5}{3\,\text{k}\Omega} \approx 1.67$ mA (safe for MCU pin).

> [!WARNING]
> **Bidirectional line (RX/TX) is fine with a divider on TX, but the GSM-TX-to-MCU-RX line can be left direct because 3.3 V is already a valid logic HIGH for a 5 V CMOS 8051 (V_IH min ≈ 2.0 V).**

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Master AT-Command Sequence Table

| # | Task | MCU → GSM Command | Expected Reply (URC) | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Handshake test | `AT<CR>` | `OK` | Verify UART link |
| 2 | Module identity | `ATI<CR>` | `SIM800 R14.18` | Identify firmware |
| 3 | Set SMS to text mode | `AT+CMGF=1<CR>` | `OK` | Required before send |
| 4 | Send SMS to +91xxxxxxxxxx | `AT+CMGS="+91xxxxxxxxxx"<CR>Hello<Ctrl+Z>` | `+CMGS: 1`<CR>`OK` | Alert messages |
| 5 | Read SMS at index 1 | `AT+CMGR=1<CR>` | `+CMGR: "REC READ",...Hello` | Process commands |
| 6 | List all SMS | `AT+CMGL="ALL"<CR>` | List of indexes | Inbox polling |
| 7 | Delete SMS at index 1 | `AT+CMGD=1<CR>` | `OK` | Memory hygiene |
| 8 | Dial voice call | `ATD9847xxxxxx;<CR>` | `OK` / `BUSY` / `NO CARRIER` | Voice alerting |
| 9 | Answer incoming call | `ATA<CR>` | `OK` | Tele-control |
| 10 | Hang up | `ATH<CR>` | `OK` | Always hang up! |
| 11 | Enable caller ID | `AT+CLIP=1<CR>` | `OK` | Identify caller |
| 12 | Attach GPRS | `AT+CGATT=1<CR>` | `OK` | Pre-condition for TCP |
| 13 | Set APN (Airtel example) | `AT+CSTT="airtelgprs.com","",""<CR>` | `OK` | APN varies by SIM |
| 14 | Bring up wireless | `AT+CIICR<CR>` | `OK` | Wait 3-5 s |
| 15 | Get IP address | `AT+CIFSR<CR>` | `10.45.xx.xx` | Sanity check |
| 16 | Open TCP socket | `AT+CIPSTART="TCP","api.example.com",80<CR>` | `OK`<CR>`CONNECT OK` | HTTP channel |
| 17 | Send data | `AT+CIPSEND<CR>` (wait `>`) then payload | `SEND OK` | Push data |
| 18 | Close TCP | `AT+CIPCLOSE<CR>` | `CLOSE OK` | Free socket |
| 19 | Deactivate PDP | `AT+CIPSHUT<CR>` | `SHUT OK` | Clean disconnect |
| 20 | Signal strength | `AT+CSQ<CR>` | `+CSQ: 18,0` (0-31, 99=unknown) | Antenna tuning |

> [!TIP]
> **`<CR>` = ASCII 13 (0x0D)** in C, written as `'\r'`.
> **`<Ctrl+Z>` = ASCII 26 (0x1A)** in C, written as `0x1A`, marks end of SMS body.
> **Each step MUST end with a small `delay_ms(300)`** because the GSM module's internal processor is slow.

---

### 3.4 Exhaustive C-Code Skeleton (8051 / Keil)

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit RELAY  = P1^0;          // Output device
sbit SWITCH = P3^2;          // External interrupt for trigger
unsigned char i = 0;
unsigned char msg[40];

void uart_init(void)
{
    TMOD = 0x20;             // Timer-1, Mode-2 (auto-reload)
    TH1   = 0xFD;            // 9600 baud @ 11.0592 MHz
    SCON  = 0x50;            // 8-bit UART, REN enabled
    TR1   = 1;               // Start Timer-1
    IE    = 0x90;            // Enable serial interrupt
}

void tx(unsigned char ch)
{
    SBUF = ch;
    while (TI == 0);
    TI = 0;
}

void tx_str(unsigned char *s)
{
    while (*s) tx(*s++);
}

void delay(unsigned int d)
{
    while (d--);
}

void gsm_send_sms(unsigned char *num, unsigned char *body)
{
    tx_str("AT+CMGF=1\r");          delay(200);
    tx_str("AT+CMGS=\"");           tx_str(num);  tx_str("\"\r");
    delay(200);
    tx_str(body);
    tx(0x1A);                       // Ctrl+Z terminates SMS body
    delay(500);
}

void gsm_call(unsigned char *num)
{
    tx_str("ATD");  tx_str(num);  tx_str(";\r");
    delay(300);
    tx_str("ATH\r");                // Hang up after 2 s
    delay(2000);
}

void main(void)
{
    uart_init();
    tx_str("AT\r");                 delay(200);
    while (1);
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above is a faithful KTU-lab pattern: **Initialize UART → Issue `AT` handshake → Issue higher-level command → Add small delays between AT strings → Wait for `OK` before next command.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — MCU ↔ GSM Interfacing Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MCU1[8051 Microcontroller]
    PWR1[LM2596 Buck Regulator 4.0V 2A]
    LEVEL1[Voltage Divider 5V to 3.3V]
    UART1[UART TXD RXD 9600 bps]
    SIM1[SIM800C GSM Module]
    SIMCARD1[SIM Card Holder]
    ANT1[GSM Quad Band Antenna]
    NET1[Mobile Network Tower]
    SRV1[Cloud Server TCP HTTP]
    PHONE1[Remote Mobile Phone]

    PWR1 -->|4.0V 2A| SIM1
    MCU1 -->|TXD 5V| LEVEL1
    LEVEL1 -->|3.3V RXD| SIM1
    SIM1 -->|TXD 3.3V| MCU1
    SIM1 --> SIMCARD1
    SIM1 --> ANT1
    ANT1 <--> NET1
    NET1 --> SRV1
    NET1 --> PHONE1
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Voice Call Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start MCU Program] --> S2[UART Init 9600 bps]
    S2 --> S3[Send AT plus CR]
    S3 --> S4{Reply OK?}
    S4 -- No --> S5[Recheck wiring and baud]
    S4 -- Yes --> S6[Send ATD plus Number plus semicolon]
    S6 --> S7[Wait for OK or BUSY or NO CARRIER]
    S7 --> S8{Connected?}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9[Call active 30 s timer]
    S9 --> S10[Send ATH to hang up]
    S8 -- No --> S11[Retry or end]
    S10 --> S12[End]
    S11 --> S12
    S5 --> S12
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — GPRS Internet / HTTP Request Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[Start] --> G2[AT plus CR]
    G2 --> G3[AT plus CGATT equals 1 Attach GPRS]
    G3 --> G4[AT plus CSTT set APN]
    G4 --> G5[AT plus CIICR Bring up wireless]
    G5 --> G6[AT plus CIFSR Get IP]
    G6 --> G7[AT plus CIPSTART TCP server port 80]
    G7 --> G8{Connect OK?}
    G8 -- No --> G9[Reissue after delay 5 s]
    G8 -- Yes --> G10[AT plus CIPSEND wait for greater than prompt]
    G10 --> G11[Send HTTP GET request payload]
    G11 --> G12[Ctrl Z 0x1A terminate]
    G12 --> G13[Receive server reply via UART]
    G13 --> G14[AT plus CIPCLOSE]
    G14 --> G15[AT plus CIPSHUT]
    G15 --> G16[End]
    G9 --> G7
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Typical Hardware Pin Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PWR2[5V DC Adapter] --> REG2[LM2596 Step Down]
    REG2 -->|4.0V| GSM2[SIM800 Vbat]
    REG2 -->|3.3V LDO| MCU2[8051 Vcc]
    MCU2 -->|P3.1 TXD| DIV2[R1 1k and R2 2k Divider]
    DIV2 -->|3.3V RXD| GSM2
    GSM2 -->|TXD 3.3V direct| MCU2RX[8051 P3.0 RXD]
    GSM2 --> NET2[GSM Tower via Antenna]
    MCU2 -->|P1.0 Relay Driver| LOAD2[Buzzer and Lamp]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** Define GSM. List the four frequency bands supported by a quad-band SIM800 module.

**Model Answer:**
GSM (Global System for Mobile Communications) is a 2G digital cellular standard defined by ETSI/3GPP that uses TDMA/FDMA on the 900/1800 MHz bands. A **quad-band SIM800** supports **GSM 850 MHz, EGSM 900 MHz, DCS 1800 MHz, and PCS 1900 MHz**, making it usable worldwide.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** Differentiate between *result codes* and *unsolicited result codes (URC)* with one example each in GSM AT communication.

**Model Answer:**
A **result code** is a deliberate response to a command the MCU sent (e.g., `AT<CR>` → `OK` or `ERROR`). An **unsolicited result code (URC)** arrives *without* a preceding command, typically to notify an external event (e.g., `RING` for an incoming call, `+CMTI: "SM",3` for a new SMS). The MCU must poll or use interrupts to catch URCs.

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> The University allows a choice within a module. **Answer ANY ONE** of Q1 or Q2.

---

#### **QUESTION 1 (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **interfacing of a SIM800 GSM module with the 8051 microcontroller**. Mention the role of the level shifter and the power supply design. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Power Supply:** The SIM800 draws up to **2 A** during the RF burst (577 µs every 4.615 ms). A single 7805 linear regulator is *insufficient*. Use an **LM2596 switching buck regulator** set to **4.0 V** with a **1000 µF** decoupling capacitor and a **10 µF + 100 nF** decoupling network close to the Vbat pin.
2. **Serial Link:** SIM800's TX and RX pins operate at **3.3 V CMOS** levels. The 8051 UART runs at **5 V TTL**, so a **resistor divider (1 kΩ / 2 kΩ)** on the 8051-TXD line drops 5 V to ~3.3 V. The reverse direction (GSM-TXD → 8051-RXD) does *not* need shifting because 3.3 V is a valid logic HIGH for the 8051 (V_IH min ≈ 2.0 V).
3. **SIM Holder:** A 6-pin SIM holder with **1 µF + 100 nF** caps on VDD and on the I/O lines as required by the SIM specification.
4. **UART Init:** 8051 Timer-1 in **Mode 2**, **TH1 = 0xFD** for 9600 bps at 11.0592 MHz.
5. **Antenna:** A GSM quad-band **chip antenna or SMA whip**, with a matching 50 Ω microstrip line on the PCB.
6. **Status LEDs:** Connect the module's `NETLIGHT` pin to an LED to indicate registration status.

**Pin Map:**

| 8051 Pin | Connects to | Direction |
|---|---|---|
| P3.0 (RXD) | SIM800 TXD | Input to MCU |
| P3.1 (TXD) | R1 → R2 → SIM800 RXD | Output from MCU |
| GND | GND (single common ground) | — |
| Vcc (5V) | LM2596 input | — |
| LM2596 out (4.0V) | SIM800 Vbat | — |

**(b)** Write the **complete AT-command sequence** (with `OK` checks) to: (i) send an SMS "FIRE ALERT" to number 9847xxxxxx, and (ii) dial a voice call to the same number, then hang up after 5 seconds. Provide the equivalent C pseudocode. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (i) — SMS:**

```
AT\r                  → OK
AT+CMGF=1\r           → OK
AT+CMGS="9847xxxxxx"\r→ >
FIRE ALERT<Ctrl+Z>    → +CMGS: 1\r\nOK
```

**Model Solution (ii) — Voice Call:**

```
AT\r          → OK
ATD9847xxxxxx;\r → OK
delay(5000)   ; // allow 5 s of conversation
ATH\r         → OK
```

**C Pseudocode:**

```c
gsm_send_sms("9847xxxxxx", "FIRE ALERT");
delay_ms(1000);
gsm_call("9847xxxxxx");
delay_ms(5000);
gsm_hangup();
```

---

#### **QUESTION 2 (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the **GPRS attach and TCP connection procedure** for a SIM800 module. List the AT commands in correct order to open a TCP socket to `api.thingspeak.com` on port 80 and send an HTTP GET request to update a channel field. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Step | AT Command | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `AT` | Handshake |
| 2 | `AT+CGATT=1` | Attach to GPRS service |
| 3 | `AT+CSTT="airtelgprs.com","",""` | Set APN (network-dependent) |
| 4 | `AT+CIICR` | Bring up the wireless GPRS link (wait 3-5 s) |
| 5 | `AT+CIFSR` | Retrieve the assigned IP address |
| 6 | `AT+CIPSTART="TCP","api.thingspeak.com",80` | Open TCP socket |
| 7 | `AT+CIPSEND` (wait for `>`) | Start data mode |
| 8 | `GET /update?api_key=XXXXX&field1=25 HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: api.thingspeak.com\r\n\r\n` | HTTP request body |
| 9 | `0x1A` (Ctrl-Z) | Terminate request, wait for `SEND OK` |
| 10 | `AT+CIPCLOSE` | Close socket |
| 11 | `AT+CIPSHUT` | Deactivate PDP context |

**ThingSpeak Sample HTTP Request:**

```
GET /update?api_key=YOUR_KEY&field1=25 HTTP/1.1\r\n
Host: api.thingspeak.com\r\n
Connection: close\r\n
\r\n
```

**(b)** Design a complete **fire-alarm system using 8051 + SIM800 + LM35 temperature sensor**. Draw the interfacing diagram, write the algorithm, and show the main `while(1)` loop. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (textual):**

- **LM35** → ADC0804 → 8051 Port P0.
- **8051** → UART (P3.0/P3.1) → Level shifter → **SIM800**.
- **SIM800** → Antenna → GSM tower → User mobile (SMS + Call).

**Algorithm:**

1. Initialise UART at 9600 bps; initialise ADC.
2. Loop: read ADC value every 1 s; convert to °C ($T = V_{out} \times 100$).
3. If T > 60 °C: send SMS "FIRE DETECTED at Zone-1" and dial 9847xxxxxx.
4. Otherwise: print temperature on LCD; continue loop.

**Main Loop Skeleton:**

```c
while (1) {
    unsigned int adc = read_adc();
    float temp = adc * (5.0 / 255.0) * 100.0;
    if (temp > 60.0) {
        gsm_send_sms("9847xxxxxx", "FIRE DETECTED at Zone-1");
        gsm_call("9847xxxxxx");
    }
    lcd_show_temp(temp);
    delay_ms(1000);
}
```

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 reasons students LOSE marks in this question (KTU pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting `<CR> (\r)` at the end of every AT string** — the module silently ignores the command.
> 2. **Skipping the `delay_ms(300-500)` between AT commands** — the module's internal processor needs time.
> 3. **Not using `<Ctrl+Z> (0x1A)` to terminate the SMS body** — the SMS never gets sent.
> 4. **Connecting TX→TX and RX→RX** (cross-over forgotten). The correct cross is **MCU-TXD → GSM-RXD** and **MCU-RXD ← GSM-TXD**.
> 5. **Using 7805 linear regulator to power the GSM module** — it cannot supply the 2 A peak; the module resets every 4.6 ms.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] GSM is a **2G digital cellular** standard; SIM800 is the most common KTU-lab module.
- [x] **UART** is the physical link; **AT commands** are the logical link; **Hayes command set** is the syntax.
- [x] Default baud rate = **9600 bps, 8-N-1**; TH1 = **0xFD** for 11.0592 MHz crystal.
- [x] **Voice call:** `ATD<num>;`  ·  **Answer:** `ATA`  ·  **Hang up:** `ATH`.
- [x] **SMS text mode:** `AT+CMGF=1` → `AT+CMGS="num"\r<body>\x1A`.
- [x] **GPRS Internet:** `AT+CGATT=1` → `AT+CSTT="APN"` → `AT+CIICR` → `AT+CIFSR` → `AT+CIPSTART="TCP",host,port` → `AT+CIPSEND` → payload → `\x1A` → `AT+CIPCLOSE` → `AT+CIPSHUT`.
- [x] **Voltage level shift** is needed on MCU-TXD → GSM-RXD (5 V → 3.3 V), not the reverse.
- [x] **Power budget:** GSM Tx burst needs **2 A peak** — use a switching regulator (LM2596), not 7805.
- [x] **URCs** like `RING`, `+CMTI`, `+CLIP` arrive *without* being requested — handle them in serial interrupt.
- [x] **Every AT command must end with `<CR> (0x0D)`** and there **must be a 300-500 ms delay** between commands.
- [x] **SMS body ends with `Ctrl+Z (0x1A)`**, not ENTER.
- [x] **TCP/HTTP requires correct APN** for the SIM's operator (e.g., Airtel: `airtelgprs.com`, Jio: no APN, BSNL: `bsnlnet`).
- [x] **Differentiate AT vs AT+** — bare `AT` are Hayes basic; `AT+` are extended 3GPP commands.
- [x] **SIM800 supports only 2G/2.5G**; for 4G/5G IoT use SIM7600E or similar LTE modules (out of KTU Module-4 scope).
- [x] **Examiner's favourite follow-up:** "What will happen if you forget the terminating `<Ctrl+Z>`?" → *SMS will never be sent; module will time out and return `ERROR` after 30 s.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bluetooth Communication Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Bluetooth Communication Basics # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Bluetooth** is a short-range wireless personal area network (WPAN) technology standardized as **IEEE 802.15.1**, operating in the globally unlicensed **Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) radio band of 2.4 GHz to 2.4835 GHz**. It employs **Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum (FHSS)** with **79 channels** (each 1 MHz wide) and a hopping rate of **1600 hops/second** to provide robust, low-power, point-to-multipoint data and voice communication between embedded systems, microcontrollers, sensors, and mobile devices. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Microcontrollers course**, Bluetooth communication refers to the integration of a Bluetooth module (typically **HC-05** or **HC-06**) with a microcontroller (Arduino Uno, ESP32, ATmega328P, PIC, ARM Cortex-M, etc.) via the **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver Transmitter)** interface, enabling wireless serial data exchange with smartphones, tablets, laptops, and other embedded devices. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of Bluetooth as an "invisible, smart walkie-talkie" that automatically changes radio stations 1600 times every second to avoid interference.** Imagine you and your friend are trying to have a private conversation in a noisy cafeteria filled with other groups talking. The naive approach is to stay on one table (one frequency) — but others can overhear you, and your conversation gets drowned out. **Bluetooth's clever solution:** 1. You and your friend agree on a secret pattern of switching tables (frequency hopping) — switching **1600 times per second**! 2. The other groups cannot follow your pattern, so they cannot intercept or jam you. 3. If a particular table (frequency) is noisy, you will be off it in less than a millisecond. **Real-world Embedded Example:** When a smartphone sends a temperature reading to a microcontroller via a Bluetooth module (HC-05), the module: - Receives the UART data from the microcontroller's TX pin. - Encodes it into a Bluetooth packet. - Transmits it via 2.4 GHz radio, rapidly hopping between 79 frequencies. - The receiving device follows the *same* hopping pattern, decodes the packet, and delivers the data to its application layer. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | |---|---| | Frequency Band | **2.4 GHz – 2.4835 GHz** | | Number of RF Channels | **79** | | Channel Bandwidth | **1 MHz** | | Hop Rate | **1600 hops/second** | | Symbol Rate | **1 Msymbol/second** | | Modulation (Basic Rate) | **GFSK** (Gaussian Frequency Shift Keying) | | Typical Range (Class 2) | **10 meters** | | Output Power (Class 2) | **0 dBm (1 mW)** | | Range (Class 1) | **100 meters** | | Output Power (Class 1) | **20 dBm (100 mW)** | > [!NOTE] > **Note for KTU Board Exam:** Always remember the magic numbers — **79 channels**, **1 MHz spacing**, **1600 hops/sec**, and **2.4 GHz ISM band**. These appear repeatedly in KTU questions. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Bluetooth Protocol Stack (Logical Architecture) Bluetooth follows a layered protocol architecture that mirrors the **OSI model**. The KTU syllabus requires understanding the function of each layer and how it relates to a microcontroller's UART interface. ### Layered Breakdown (Bottom-Up) **1. Radio (Physical) Layer** - Responsible for transmitting and receiving radio signals in the 2.4 GHz ISM band. - Defines frequency, modulation (GFSK), and transmitter characteristics. - This is the *only* layer dealing with actual electromagnetic waves. **2. Baseband Layer** - Manages the **FHSS channel selection** (decides which of the 79 channels to use next). - Controls **piconet addressing** (device identification within a network). - Handles **packet assembly, error correction (FEC)**, and timing/synchronization. - Defines the **625 µs time slot** structure (TDD — Time Division Duplex). **3. Link Manager Protocol (LMP)** - Sets up, authenticates, and configures the link between two Bluetooth devices. - Manages **pairing, encryption key generation, and power mode control** (Sniff, Hold, Park). **4. Host Controller Interface (HCI)** - Standardized command interface between the host (e.g., microcontroller) and the Bluetooth controller chip. - Transports commands, events, and data via **UART, USB, or SPI**. **5. Logical Link Control and Adaptation Protocol (L2CAP)** - Provides **multiplexing** of higher-level protocols over a single baseband link. - Performs **packet segmentation and reassembly** (SAR). - Supports **QoS** (Quality of Service) for real-time traffic (e.g., voice). **6. Service Discovery Protocol (SDP)** - Allows devices to **discover available services** and their attributes on remote devices. - Essential for "what can this device do?" queries. **7. RFCOMM (Serial Port Emulation)** - Emulates an **RS-232 serial cable** over Bluetooth — this is why HC-05 modules behave like UART. - Provides up to 60 simultaneous emulated serial ports. **8. OBEX / Application Profiles** - Defines specific use-cases: **SPP** (Serial Port Profile), **HID** (Human Interface Device), **A2DP** (Audio), **HFP** (Hands-Free), etc. ## 2.2 The Piconet and Scatternet Architecture > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question** topic. Master the master-slave relationship and scatternet limits. ### Piconet (Basic Network Unit) - A piconet consists of **1 master** and up to **7 active slaves** (8 devices total active). - Up to **255 parked slaves** can be registered but inactive. - The master determines the **hopping sequence** and clock. - All slaves synchronize to the master's clock and hop in unison. ### Scatternet (Inter-Piconet Bridge) - A scatternet is formed when a device is a member of **two or more piconets**. - A device acting as a bridge is a **slave in one piconet** and **master in another**. - Maximum theoretical slave count: a device can be a slave in up to 10 piconets but master in only 1. ## 2.3 Frequency Hopping Mathematics Bluetooth's FHSS is pseudo-random. The **hopping sequence** is determined by the master's **BD_ADDR (48-bit Bluetooth Device Address)** and its **clock (28 bits)**. ### Hop Selection Kernel The current frequency channel is computed as: $$f_{n} = \left[ (C_{27} \oplus C_{k}) \bmod 79 \right] \text{ (with 23 hops excluded in some countries)}$$ Where: - $C_{27}$ is the 27th bit of the master's clock - $C_{k}$ represents a permutation of the 28-bit clock based on addressing inputs - $\oplus$ denotes XOR (modulo-2 addition) > [!NOTE] > **For KTU exams**, you do NOT need to derive the full 79-channel kernel. The key takeaway: hopping is **pseudo-random, deterministic to authorized devices, and unpredictable to interferers**, providing security and noise immunity. ## 2.4 Time Division Duplex (TDD) Structure Bluetooth uses **TDD** for half-duplex communication in basic rate: $$T_{slot} = 625 \, \mu s$$ A **full-duplex packet** occupies 1, 3, or 5 slots: $$T_{packet} = N \times 625 \, \mu s, \quad N \in \{1, 3, 5\}$$ | Packet Type | Slots Occupied | Duration | |---|---|---| | HV1 (High-quality Voice) | 1 | **625 µs** | | HV3 | 1 | **625 µs** | | DM1 / DH1 (Data Medium / High) | 1 | **625 µs** | | DM3 / DH3 | 3 | **1875 µs** | | DM5 / DH5 | 5 | **3125 µs** | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | |---|---|---| | Number of RF Channels | $N_{ch} = 79$ | Total hopping frequencies | | Channel Bandwidth | $BW = 1 \, MHz$ | Spacing between adjacent channels | | Hop Period | $T_{hop} = \dfrac{1}{1600} = 625 \, \mu s$ | Time per hop | | Slot Duration | $T_{slot} = 625 \, \mu s$ | Basic TDD unit | | Bit Rate (Basic Rate) | $R_b = 1 \, Mbps$ | Symbol rate using GFSK | | Bit Rate (EDR) | $R_{b,EDR} = 2 \text{ or } 3 \, Mbps$ | Enhanced Data Rate | | Band Edge | $f_{min} = 2.402 \, GHz$, $f_{max} = 2.480 \, GHz$ | Frequency range boundaries | | TX Power (Class 2) | $P_{TX} = 0 \, dBm$ | Typical HC-05 module output | | Max Active Devices per Piconet | $N_{active} = 1 \text{ master} + 7 \text{ slaves} = 8$ | Active membership | | Max Parked Devices | $N_{parked} = 255$ | Inactive but registered | | Coverage Range (Class 2) | $R \approx 10 \, m$ | Indoor, typical | | Raw Air Data Rate (DH5) | $R_{DH5} = 723.2 \, kbps$ | Maximum symmetric throughput | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Valuation Tip:** If asked to calculate throughput, always account for **overhead** (headers, acknowledgements, guard bands). The actual application-layer throughput of SPP over Bluetooth is typically **10–100 kbps**, far below the raw 1 Mbps. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Bluetooth is used in: - **Wireless sensor networks** (IoT, home automation) - **Wearable health devices** (heart-rate, fitness bands) - **Automotive infotainment** (hands-free calling, A2DP music) - **Industrial cable replacement** (replacing RS-232 in factories) - **Microcontroller-to-smartphone apps** (MIT App Inventor, Bluetooth Terminal) - **Beacons** for retail proximity marketing - **BLE Mesh** for large-scale smart lighting # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Theoretical Data Throughput of DH5 Packet The **DH5 (Data High rate, 5-slot)** packet is the largest basic-rate Bluetooth packet. KTU loves throughput calculations. ### Step 1: Identify Packet Structure A DH5 packet has the following layout: - **Access Code:** 72 bits (synchronization + channel identity) - **Packet Header:** 54 bits (logical transport address, type, flow, ARQN, SEQN, HEC) - **Payload:** 2744 bits (user data + 2/3 FEC for DH) - **Guard Band:** ~ 6 bits worth of time for frequency switching ### Step 2: Total Packet Duration in Bits and Time A 5-slot packet occupies 5 × 625 µs = 3125 µs. At 1 Msymbol/s with 1 bit/symbol: $$T_{packet} = 5 \times 625 \, \mu s = 3125 \, \mu s$$ The total transmitted bits in this time: $$N_{total} = R_b \times T_{packet} = 1 \times 10^6 \, bps \times 3125 \times 10^{-6} \, s$$ $$N_{total} = 3125 \, bits$$ ### Step 3: Subtract Overhead Overhead bits = Access Code + Header: $$N_{OH} = 72 + 54 = 126 \, bits$$ Net user payload: $$N_{payload} = N_{total} - N_{OH} = 3125 - 126 = 2999 \, bits$$ But the **standard-defined DH5 payload is 2744 bits** (some of the remaining time is for the next receiver's turnaround). We accept the standard value: $$N_{payload,DH5} = 2744 \, bits$$ ### Step 4: Compute Asymmetric Throughput (Asymmetric DH5) In asymmetric mode, a slave sends DH5 in **1 slot** and receives DH5 in **5 slots** per **6-slot cycle**: $$T_{cycle} = 6 \times 625 \, \mu s = 3750 \, \mu s$$ Forward (Master → Slave) throughput: $$R_{fwd} = \frac{2744 \, bits}{3750 \, \mu s} = 731.7 \, kbps \approx 723.2 \, kbps$$ Reverse (Slave → Master) throughput in asymmetric mode: $$R_{rev} = \frac{2744 \, bits}{6 \times 625 \, \mu s} \times \frac{1}{5} = 57.6 \, kbps$$ ### Step 5: Compute Symmetric Throughput (DH5 + DH5 symmetric) In symmetric mode, both directions use a 5-slot packet within a 10-slot cycle: $$T_{sym} = 10 \times 625 \, \mu s = 6250 \, \mu s$$ $$R_{sym} = \frac{2744}{6250 \, \mu s} = 439.04 \, kbps$$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** The maximum symmetric user throughput with basic-rate Bluetooth (DH5 packets) is approximately **433.9 kbps**, while the **asymmetric peak is 723.2 kbps** in the forward direction. These numbers are **KTU exam favourites**. ## 3.2 Derivation: Time to Hop Through All 79 Channels Given a hop rate of 1600 hops/sec: $$T_{full\_sweep} = \frac{N_{ch}}{R_{hop}} = \frac{79}{1600} = 0.049375 \, s$$ $$T_{full\_sweep} = 49.375 \, ms$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical meaning:** A Bluetooth device revisits each of the 79 channels approximately every **49.4 ms**. This ultra-fast hop rate is why Bluetooth is robust against narrowband interference (e.g., Wi-Fi channels 1, 6, 11). ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Bluetooth vs. Other Wireless Technologies | Feature | Bluetooth (BR/EDR) | BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy) | Wi-Fi (802.11n) | Zigbee (802.15.4) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Frequency | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 GHz | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 2.4 GHz | | Channels | 79 (1 MHz) | 40 (2 MHz) | 13–25 (variable) | 16 (2 MHz) | | Hopping | FHSS, 1600 hop/s | Adaptive FHSS | DSSS / OFDM | DSSS | | Range | 10 m (Class 2) | 50–100 m | 30–100 m | 10–100 m | | Peak Data Rate | 1–3 Mbps | 1–2 Mbps | 150–600 Mbps | 250 kbps | | Power | Medium | **Very Low** | High | Low | | Topology | Piconet / Scatternet | Star / Mesh | Star | Mesh | | Typical Use | Audio, file transfer | IoT, beacons | Internet, video | Sensor mesh | ## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Establishing a Bluetooth Connection (HC-05 + Microcontroller) This is a frequent **practical question** in KTU viva and labs. **Step 1 — Hardware Wiring (HC-05 to Arduino Uno)** | HC-05 Pin | Arduino Pin | Function | |---|---|---| | VCC | 5V | Power supply | | GND | GND | Ground | | TXD | RX (Pin 0) | Bluetooth TX → MCU RX | | RXD | TX (Pin 1) via voltage divider | Bluetooth RX ← MCU TX (logic-level shift to 3.3V) | | STATE / EN | (optional) | Indicates connection state | > [!WARNING] > **Critical:** HC-05 RXD pin is **3.3V logic level**. Connecting directly to a 5V Arduino TX pin may damage the module over time. Use a **voltage divider** (1 kΩ + 2 kΩ) or a logic-level converter. **Step 2 — Enter AT Command Mode** 1. Power off the HC-05. 2. Hold the small push-button on the HC-05 module. 3. Power it back on. The onboard LED will **blink slowly (≈ 2 sec period)**, indicating AT mode at default baud rate of **38400 bps**. 4. Open Arduino Serial Monitor at 38400 bps with "Both NL & CR" line ending. **Step 3 — Query Configuration** Send the following AT commands: ``` AT → returns OK AT+NAME? → returns +NAME:HC-05 AT+PSWD? → returns +PSWD:1234 (default PIN) AT+UART? → returns +UART:38400,0,0 AT+ROLE? → returns +ROLE:0 (0 = slave, 1 = master) AT+ADDR? → returns +ADDR:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx:xx ``` **Step 4 — Configure Module** ``` AT+NAME=KTUMCU → rename device AT+PSWD="0000" → change PIN AT+UART=9600,0,0 → set UART to 9600, no parity, 1 stop bit AT+ROLE=1 → make it a master (if needed) AT+CMODE=1 → connect to any device ``` **Step 5 — Normal Operation (Exit AT Mode)** 1. Cycle power without holding the button. 2. LED blinks **fast (≈ 0.3 sec period)** — searchable. 3. Pair from smartphone using PIN "1234" or "0000". 4. After pairing, LED becomes **steady ON** — connection established. 5. Open a Bluetooth Terminal app and send "AT" — if "OK" echoes back, the link is functional. **Step 6 — Data Exchange** ```cpp // Example: Arduino sketch void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); // USB serial Serial.println("Ready"); } void loop() { if (Serial.available()) { // data from USB char c = Serial.read(); // Bluetooth module transparently forwards UART data } // Data received from Bluetooth is auto-appearing on Serial } ``` > [!NOTE] > **The HC-05 is transparent — any data written to its TXD line is wirelessly transmitted, and any received wireless data appears on its RXD line as standard UART bytes.** The microcontroller does not need to know Bluetooth protocols; it just uses `Serial.read()` and `Serial.print()`. ## 3.5 Connection State Analysis (Inquiry, Paging, Connection) Bluetooth connection setup follows a defined state machine: 1. **Standby** — default low-power state, listening for inquiries. 2. **Inquiry** — master broadcasts an **ID packet** to discover slaves. Slave responds with **FHS packet** (its address, clock). 3. **Inquiry Scan** — slave periodically listens on a single hop frequency for inquiries. 4. **Paging** — master sends slave's access code (derived from BD_ADDR). Slave wakes and synchronizes. 5. **Page Scan** — slave scans for page messages addressed to it. 6. **Connection** — link established; piconet active; data exchange begins. 7. **Park / Sniff / Hold** — low-power sub-states of an existing connection. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why hopping helps here:** Inquiry uses 32 dedicated train frequencies; paging uses the slave's clock-derived sequence. This prevents collisions when many devices are in range. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Bluetooth Protocol Stack (Layered Architecture) ```mermaid graph TB APP1["Application Profiles
SPP, HID, A2DP"] APP2["OBEX / RFCOMM
Serial Port Emulation"] APP3["SDP
Service Discovery"] APP4["L2CAP
Multiplexing and SAR"] APP5["HCI
Host Controller Interface"] APP6["LMP
Link Manager Protocol"] APP7["Baseband
FHSS, Piconet, Packets"] APP8["Radio
2.4 GHz GFSK Transceiver"] APP1 --> APP2 --> APP3 --> APP4 --> APP5 --> APP6 --> APP7 --> APP8 ``` ## 4.2 Piconet and Scatternet Architecture ```mermaid graph TB subgraph PiconetA MA["Master A"] S1["Slave 1"] S2["Slave 2"] S3["Slave 3"] S4["Slave 4"] S5["Slave 5"] S6["Slave 6"] S7["Slave 7"] end subgraph PiconetB MB["Master B"] S8["Slave 8"] S9["Slave 9"] S10["Slave 10"] S1B["Bridge Device
Master A Slave in B"] end MA --- S1 MA --- S2 MA --- S3 MA --- S4 MA --- S5 MA --- S6 MA --- S7 MB --- S8 MB --- S9 MB --- S10 MB --- S1B MA --- S1B ``` > [!NOTE] > The **bridge device** `S1B` is a **slave** in Piconet A and a **master** in Piconet B. This forms a scatternet and is the basis of multi-hop Bluetooth networking. ## 4.3 HC-05 Module Block-Level Architecture ```mermaid graph LR MCU["Microcontroller
Arduino / ESP32 / PIC"] -->|UART TX 5V| LVC["Logic Level
Converter 5V to 3.3V"] LVC -->|3.3V TX| HCTX["HC-05 RXD Pin"] HCRX["HC-05 TXD Pin"] -->|3.3V RX| MCU VCC["5V Supply"] --> HCMOD["HC-05 Module
CSR BC417 + Flash"] HCMOD --> ANT["Chip Antenna
2.4 GHz"] HCMOD --> LED["Status LED
Fast/Slow/Steady"] HCMOD --> BT["Bluetooth Radio
FHSS 79 Channels"] BT -->|Wireless Link| PHONE["Smartphone /
Master Device"] ``` ## 4.4 Frequency Hopping Sequence Visualization ```mermaid graph LR F0["Slot 0
Channel 23"] --> F1["Slot 1
Channel 71"] F1 --> F2["Slot 2
Channel 12"] F2 --> F3["Slot 3
Channel 50"] F3 --> F4["Slot 4
Channel 5"] F4 --> F5["Slot 5
Channel 78"] F5 --> F6["Slot 6
Channel 33"] F6 --> F7["Slot 7
Channel 18"] ``` > [!NOTE] > The **hopping pattern is pseudo-random**, not sequential. It is determined by the master's BD_ADDR and clock, making interception extremely difficult. ## 4.5 Complete Data Flow: Sensor → Bluetooth → Smartphone ```mermaid graph LR SENSE["Sensor
Temperature LM35"] --> ADC["Microcontroller
ADC Input"] ADC --> MCU2["MCU Process
and Format"] MCU2 -->|UART 9600 bps| BTX["HC-05 TXD"] BTX -->|FHSS 2.4 GHz| BRX["Receiver BT
Module or Phone"] BRX --> APP["Mobile App
Bluetooth Terminal"] APP --> DISP["Display Value
on Screen"] ``` ## 4.6 Bluetooth Connection State Machine ```mermaid graph TB STANDBY["Standby State"] --> INQSCAN["Inquiry Scan"] STANDBY --> PAGESCAN["Page Scan"] INQSCAN --> INQUIRY["Inquiry
Broadcast ID"] INQUIRY --> STANDBY PAGESCAN --> PAGING["Paging
Connect to Slave"] PAGING --> CONNECTED["Connected
Active Piconet"] CONNECTED --> SNIFF["Sniff Mode
Low Duty Cycle"] CONNECTED --> HOLD["Hold Mode
Paused Connection"] CONNECTED --> PARK["Park Mode
Release Address"] SNIFF --> CONNECTED HOLD --> CONNECTED PARK --> STANDBY ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the frequency hopping spread spectrum technique used in Bluetooth. Why is it important?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Bluetooth operates in the 2.4 GHz ISM band using **79 RF channels** spaced 1 MHz apart. The transmitter rapidly switches between these channels at a rate of **1600 hops per second** (i.e., once every 625 µs) following a **pseudo-random hopping sequence** determined by the master's Bluetooth device address (BD_ADDR) and its clock. **Importance:** (1 mark) FHSS provides **robustness against narrowband interference** (e.g., from Wi-Fi, microwave ovens) — since the signal occupies each channel for only 625 µs, a jammed frequency causes minimal damage. (1 mark) It also provides **security and co-existence**, as unauthorized devices cannot follow the pseudo-random pattern. (1 mark) The fast hop rate combined with error correction (FEC) ensures reliable communication even in noisy industrial environments. ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between a Piconet and a Scatternet in Bluetooth. Mention the maximum number of active devices in each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Piconet | Scatternet | |---|---|---| | Definition | Basic Bluetooth network with one master and up to 7 active slaves | Interconnection of two or more piconets via a shared bridge device | | Active devices | **1 master + 7 slaves = 8** | Theoretically **up to 10 piconets** can be interlinked, with bridge devices being master in one and slave in another | | Hopping | All devices follow the master's hop sequence | Each piconet has its own master and hopping sequence | | Topology | Star | Mesh-like, with bridge nodes | (1 mark for piconet definition, 1 mark for scatternet definition, 1 mark for numerical limits) ## 5.2 PART B: Full-Question Choices (14 Marks Each) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a) Describe the Bluetooth protocol stack with a neat diagram. Explain the functions of the Baseband layer, LMP, L2CAP, and RFCOMM layers. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** The Bluetooth protocol stack follows a layered architecture analogous to the OSI model, designed to enable short-range wireless communication between devices. **1. Radio (Physical) Layer** — Handles the transmission and reception of 2.4 GHz radio waves using GFSK modulation. It defines the 79 RF channels, transmit power classes, and receiver sensitivity. **2. Baseband Layer** — The most critical lower layer. It: - Manages the **FHSS channel hopping** (1600 hops/sec). - Defines the **625 µs time slot** structure for TDD. - Assembles packets (Access Code + Header + Payload). - Handles **error detection/correction** (1/3 FEC, 2/3 FEC, ARQ). - Manages **piconet addressing** and clock synchronization. **3. Link Manager Protocol (LMP)** — Responsible for link setup, **authentication, encryption**, and control of low-power modes (Sniff, Hold, Park). It exchanges LMP PDUs between the link managers of master and slave. **4. Logical Link Control and Adaptation Protocol (L2CAP)** — Provides: - **Multiplexing** of multiple higher-layer protocols over a single baseband link using **Channel IDs (CIDs)**. - **Segmentation and Reassembly (SAR)** of large packets. - **QoS negotiation** for delay-sensitive traffic. - Connection-oriented and connectionless channels. **5. RFCOMM (Cable Replacement Protocol)** — Emulates a **9-pin RS-232 serial port** over the L2CAP layer. It is the protocol that enables the HC-05 module to behave transparently as a UART cable from the microcontroller's perspective. It supports up to 60 simultaneous emulated serial ports. **6. Service Discovery Protocol (SDP)** — Allows a Bluetooth device to **discover services** offered by another device, including service type, protocol stack, and attributes. **7. Application Profiles** — Define specific use cases: SPP (Serial Port Profile), HID (Human Interface Device), A2DP (Advanced Audio Distribution Profile), etc. **Diagram:** (Draw the layered stack as shown in Section 4.1) > [!NOTE] > **Allocation of marks (7 total):** Protocol stack diagram — 2 marks; Baseband functions — 2 marks; LMP, L2CAP, RFCOMM — 1 mark each. **(b) Explain with a neat block diagram how an HC-05 Bluetooth module is interfaced with an Arduino Uno microcontroller. Mention the AT commands required to configure the module for a 9600 bps baud rate and change its device name to "KTUMCU". (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Hardware Interfacing:** The HC-05 module communicates with the Arduino Uno via **UART (serial communication)**. The connection diagram is as follows: | HC-05 Pin | Arduino Uno Pin | Remarks | |---|---|---| | VCC | 5V | Power supply | | GND | GND | Common ground | | TXD | Pin 0 (RX) | Bluetooth TX → Arduino RX (direct 3.3V compatible) | | RXD | Pin 1 (TX) | Bluetooth RX ← Arduino TX (via voltage divider) | | EN / Key | Pin 9 (optional) | Hold LOW to enter AT command mode | > [!WARNING] > The HC-05 RXD pin is **3.3V logic level**. Direct connection to Arduino's 5V TX pin may damage the module. Use a **voltage divider**: TX → 1 kΩ → junction (HC-05 RX) → 2 kΩ → GND. This divides 5V to ~3.3V. **Steps to Configure HC-05 (AT Mode):** 1. **Disconnect VCC** from the HC-05. 2. **Hold the small push-button** on the HC-05 module. 3. **Reconnect VCC** while continuing to hold the button. 4. The onboard LED will **blink slowly (≈ 2 sec period)** indicating AT command mode. 5. In the Arduino IDE, upload an **empty sketch** (or one with `Serial.begin(38400)` and remove MCU-to-Bluetooth wiring for TX). 6. Open **Serial Monitor** at **38400 baud** with "Both NL & CR" line ending. 7. Send the following AT commands: ``` AT → Response: OK (test) AT+NAME=KTUMCU → Response: OK (sets device name to KTUMCU) AT+UART=9600,0,0 → Response: OK (sets baud rate 9600, no parity, 1 stop) AT+PSWD="1234" → Response: OK (sets pairing PIN) AT+ROLE=0 → Response: OK (sets as slave) ``` 8. **Cycle power** without holding the button. The LED will blink fast, indicating normal discoverable mode. 9. The module now communicates at **9600 bps** with name **"KTUMCU"**. **Arduino Sketch Outline:** ```cpp void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); // Match HC-05 baud rate } void loop() { if (Serial.available()) { char c = Serial.read(); // Forward to Bluetooth automatically (transparent) } } ``` > [!NOTE] > **Allocation of marks (7 total):** Interfacing diagram + pinout — 3 marks; AT mode entry procedure — 2 marks; AT commands and configuration — 2 marks. --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a) With a neat diagram, explain the Bluetooth piconet and scatternet architecture. Mention the role of the master, the maximum number of active and parked slaves, and how frequency synchronization is achieved. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Piconet Architecture:** A **piconet** is the fundamental building block of a Bluetooth network. It consists of one **master** device and up to **7 active slaves**, totaling 8 active devices. **Role of the Master:** - The master is the **clock reference** for the entire piconet. Its 28-bit native clock defines the timing. - The master **determines the FHSS hopping sequence** by feeding its BD_ADDR and clock into the hop selection kernel. - The master **controls channel access** (polling) using TDD. - The master manages **pairing, authentication, and link control** via LMP. **Active vs Parked Slaves:** - **Active slaves (max 7):** Have an **Active Member Address (AM_ADDR)** in the range 1–7. They can transmit and receive in the piconet. - **Parked slaves (max 255):** Have given up their AM_ADDR and only listen periodically. They can be reactivated by the master. - A piconet can therefore have **1 master + 7 active + 255 parked = 263 devices** total. **Frequency Synchronization:** All slaves in a piconet **synchronize their clocks to the master's clock** with a timing offset. The master transmits its access code, and slaves perform a **timing offset correction** based on the difference between expected and actual arrival. Once synchronized, every device uses the **master's BD_ADDR and the master's (offset-adjusted) clock** to compute the same hopping sequence, ensuring they all "meet" on the same frequency at the same time. **Scatternet Architecture:** A scatternet is formed when a device is a member of **two or more piconets simultaneously**. This device acts as: - A **slave in one piconet** (following that master's hopping sequence and clock). - A **master in another piconet** (providing its own clock to that piconet's slaves). The bridge device must **time-multiplex** its participation in each piconet. Maximum limits: a device can be a slave in up to **10 piconets** and master in only **1 piconet** at a time. > [!NOTE] > **Allocation of marks (7 total):** Piconet diagram — 2 marks; Master's role + active/parked limits — 2 marks; Synchronization — 1.5 marks; Scatternet — 1.5 marks. **(b) Calculate the maximum symmetric and asymmetric throughput of Bluetooth basic rate using DH5 packets. Given: bit rate = 1 Mbps, slot duration = 625 µs, DH5 payload = 2744 bits, and a 6-slot cycle. (7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Given:** - $R_b = 1 \text{ Mbps}$ - $T_{slot} = 625 \, \mu s$ - $N_{payload,DH5} = 2744 \text{ bits}$ - Cycle for asymmetric = 6 slots **Step 1: Asymmetric Throughput (Forward Direction)** In asymmetric mode, a single DH5 packet occupies **5 slots** and is acknowledged with a 1-slot return packet in the reverse direction, making a **6-slot cycle**: $$T_{cycle} = 6 \times 625 \, \mu s = 3750 \, \mu s$$ Forward throughput: $$R_{fwd} = \frac{N_{payload,DH5}}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{2744 \text{ bits}}{3750 \, \mu s}$$ $$R_{fwd} = 731.7 \times 10^3 \, bps \approx 723.2 \, kbps$$ **Step 2: Reverse Throughput (Asymmetric)** In the 6-slot cycle, only **1 slot** is available for the reverse (slave → master) direction. With a 1-slot DH1 packet carrying ~ 240 bits of payload: $$R_{rev} = \frac{240 \text{ bits}}{3750 \, \mu s} = 64 \, kbps$$ **Step 3: Symmetric Throughput** In symmetric mode, both directions use 5-slot DH5 packets within a **10-slot cycle**: $$T_{sym} = 10 \times 625 \, \mu s = 6250 \, \mu s$$ $$R_{sym} = \frac{2744 \text{ bits}}{6250 \, \mu s} = 439.04 \, kbps$$ **Step 4: Summary** | Mode | Throughput (kbps) | |---|---| | Asymmetric Forward (Master → Slave) | **723.2** | | Asymmetric Reverse (Slave → Master) | **57.6** | | Symmetric (both directions) | **433.9** | **Step 5: Application-Layer Note** After accounting for **L2CAP headers (4 bytes), RFCOMM overhead, and SDP/control traffic**, the actual user-available throughput is typically **80–100 kbps** for SPP — a key insight for KTU lab viva questions. > [!NOTE] > **Allocation of marks (7 total):** Time calculations — 2 marks; Asymmetric throughput — 2 marks; Symmetric throughput — 2 marks; Summary table — 1 mark. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Confusing the hop rate with the bit rate:** The hop rate is 1600 hops/sec, the bit rate is 1 Mbps. These are different. > 2. **Forgetting to subtract overhead:** Students often write "1 Mbps throughput" for Bluetooth, but with headers, FEC, and ARQ, application throughput is much lower. > 3. **Wrong number of channels:** Some students write 23 (this is the inquiry hop set) instead of 79 (the data channel set). > 4. **Misstating the piconet limit:** It is 1 master + 7 ACTIVE slaves, not 8 slaves. The 8 includes the master. > 5. **HC-05 logic level damage:** Skipping the voltage divider is a frequent practical error that costs full marks in lab evaluations. > 6. **Forgetting "Both NL & CR":** When sending AT commands from Arduino IDE Serial Monitor, the line ending must be set to "Both NL & CR" or commands are not recognized. > 7. **State of slow-blinking LED:** A 2-second period blink means AT mode at 38400 baud; a fast blink means discoverable mode. Students often mix these up in viva. ## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam** ### Core Concepts - Bluetooth = **IEEE 802.15.1**, short-range WPAN, **2.4 GHz ISM** band. - Originally developed by **Ericsson in 1994**; named after **King Harald Bluetooth** of Denmark. - Designed as a **cable replacement** technology for low-power, low-cost devices. ### Numerical Facts (Memorize!) - **79 RF channels**, each **1 MHz wide**, spanning **2.402 to 2.480 GHz**. - **1600 hops per second** → 625 µs per slot. - **1 Msymbol/s** basic rate using **GFSK** modulation. - **1 master + 7 active slaves = 8 active devices** per piconet. - **255 parked slaves** can be registered. - **Class 2 (most common):** 0 dBm, **10 m** range. - DH5 packet = 2744 bits payload, 5 slots = 3125 µs. ### Protocol Stack (Bottom-Up) Radio → Baseband → LMP → HCI → L2CAP → SDP → RFCOMM → Application Profiles. ### Piconet vs. Scatternet - **Piconet:** Star topology, single master, shared hop sequence. - **Scatternet:** Bridge device connects two piconets; bridge is slave in one, master in another. ### Practical Tips (HC-05) - Default baud rate: **38400 bps** in AT mode, **9600 bps** in data mode. - Default pairing PIN: **1234** or **0000**. - RXD pin is **3.3V logic** — use a **voltage divider** to interface with 5V MCUs. - **Slow-blinking LED = AT mode**, **fast-blink = discoverable**, **steady ON = connected**. ### Connection States Standby → Inquiry / Page Scan → Inquiry / Paging → Connected → (Sniff / Hold / Park). ### Frequently Asked Viva Questions - What is FHSS and why is it used? *(Noise immunity, security, co-existence.)* - Why 2.4 GHz? *(Globally unlicensed ISM band.)* - Difference between HC-05 and HC-06? *(HC-05 = master/slave, HC-06 = slave only.)* - Why is Bluetooth throughput much lower than 1 Mbps? *(Overhead, FEC, ARQ, headers.)* - What is BLE and how does it differ from classic Bluetooth? *(40 channels, GATT-based, ultra-low power, on-air time < 3 ms.)* ### Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid - Confusing 79 channels (data) with 32 inquiry hop frequencies. - Writing "8 slaves" instead of "7 slaves + 1 master." - Forgetting that HC-05 is a 3.3V device on the RXD line. - Missing the voltage divider in the circuit diagram — instant 0 marks for the practical. - Computing throughput without considering the ACK slot or cycle.

topics.title: LoRa Communication Basics and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** LoRa Communication Basics and Applications # 🔹 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **LoRa (Long Range)** is a **proprietary physical (PHY) layer spread-spectrum modulation technique** derived from **Chirp Spread Spectrum (CSS)** and developed by **Semtech Corporation**. It operates in the **sub-GHz Industrial, Scientific, and Medical (ISM) radio bands** and is engineered to enable **long-range, low-power, and low-data-rate wireless communication** for Internet-of-Things (IoT) and Machine-to-Machine (M2M) applications. The MAC-layer protocol that sits on top of LoRa PHY is **LoRaWAN** (maintained by the **LoRa Alliance**), which defines the network architecture, device addressing, security (AES-128), and adaptive data-rate control. | Layer | Protocol | Responsibility | |---|---|---| | Application | User / Cloud | Payload interpretation, dashboards | | MAC | **LoRaWAN** | Device classes, frame format, security, ADR | | PHY | **LoRa (CSS)** | Modulation, spreading, channel access | > [!NOTE] > **Kerala/India band context:** India operates LoRa in the **IN865–867 MHz** band (8 mandatory channels: `865.0625, 865.4025, 865.7425, 866.1425, 866.5425, 866.9425, 867.3425, 867.7425` MHz). The **maximum EIRP** allowed is **+30 dBm (1 W)** for outdoor deployments, and the **duty cycle** restriction is **≤ 10 %** per channel per hour (per Indian Wireless Planning & Coordination Wing norms). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Whistling Kettle" Mental Model Imagine you are standing at the far end of a football field trying to tell your friend a single digit (0–9).
