
**Step 3 — I²C read sequence (read time):**

```
START
→ Slave address W = 0xD0        [ACK]
→ Register pointer = 0x00       [ACK]   (point to seconds)
REPEATED START
→ Slave address R = 0xD1        [ACK]
← Seconds = 0x25                [ACK]   (master ACK)
← Minutes = 0x35                [ACK]
← Hours   = 0x14                [ACK]
← Day     = 0x03                [ACK]
← Date    = 0x25                [ACK]
← Month   = 0x12                [ACK]
← Year    = 0x24                [NACK]  (master NACK, last byte)
STOP
```

**Step 4 — Firmware-side BCD → decimal conversion on read:**

```c
uint8_t raw;
HAL_I2C_Mem_Read(&hi2c1, 0xD1, 0x00, 1, &raw, 1, 100);
uint8_t seconds = ((raw >> 4) & 0x07) * 10 + (raw & 0x0F);   // 0x25 → 25
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Masking the tens-of-seconds with `0x07` (not `0x0F`) is required because bit 7 of the seconds register is the **Clock Halt (CH)** flag — leaving it 1 stops the oscillator entirely.

**(7 marks — 2 for BCD conversion, 3 for write sequence, 2 for read sequence)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — re-read this section the morning of the exam.**

### 🔑 Software Timer Fundamentals
- Software timers are *kernel objects*, not hardware — they are driven by the **tick interrupt**.
- Created with `xTimerCreate()`; started with `xTimerStart()`.
- **`uxAutoReload`** selects one-shot (`pdFALSE`) vs auto-reload (`pdTRUE`).
- Callbacks execute in the **Timer Service Task context**, *not* in ISR context — safe to use queues/semaphores but must be short.
- The Timer Service Task is a single FreeRTOS task that processes *all* software timers — one slow callback delays every other timer.

### 🔑 Delay Functions
- `vTaskDelay(x)` — relative delay; drifts if used periodically.
- `vTaskDelayUntil(&last, x)` — absolute periodic delay; **drift-free**, use for periodic tasks.
- `pdMS_TO_TICKS(ms)` — always use this macro instead of hard-coding tick counts.
- Busy-wait loops (`for(i=0;i<1000000;i++);`) are **anti-patterns** in RTOS code — block the scheduler and break real-time guarantees.

### 🔑 RTC & Timekeeping
- **32.768 kHz** crystal ÷ $2^{15}$ = **1 Hz** (exact integer).
- DS1307 / DS3231 use **BCD** for time registers; convert in firmware.
- **I²C address 0x68** (7-bit) — write = 0xD0, read = 0xD1 on the bus.
- **CH bit (bit 7 of 0x00)** must be cleared or the RTC halts.
- **V_BAT** pin provides backup via coin cell; I²C interface disables in battery mode.
- DS3231 has integrated TCXO — accuracy ±2 ppm across temperature — preferred over DS1307 for new designs.
- **Unix epoch** = 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC; 32-bit `time_t` overflows in 2038 (Y2K38).

### 🔑 KTU-Favoured Exam Points
- Always quote the *formula* for ms → ticks: $\text{ticks} = \text{ms} \times \frac{\text{configTICK\_RATE\_HZ}}{1000}$.
- Quote the *derivation* of 1 Hz from 32.768 kHz — at least once in an exam answer.
- Show the I²C frame **byte-by-byte** for any RTC question — KTU rewards this.
- Mention **BOTH** the decimal-to-BCD and BCD-to-decimal directions.
- Distinguish `vTaskDelay` vs `vTaskDelayUntil` with the *drift* property in a table.
- Quote `configTICK_RATE_HZ` explicitly whenever discussing timer periods.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inter-task Communication: Queues and Semaphores
topics.content:
# Inter-task Communication: Queues and Semaphores > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Inter-task Communication: Queues and Semaphores --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Inter-Task Communication (ITC) — Formal Definition In a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) running on a microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4 with FreeRTOS), **Inter-Task Communication (ITC)** refers to the set of kernel-provided mechanisms that allow multiple concurrently executing tasks (TCBs) to safely exchange data and synchronize their execution. Because each task has its own stack and runs in its own context, they cannot directly share CPU registers or local variables — the kernel must mediate every interaction. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Inter-task communication is the *mechanism*, while synchronization is the *goal*. The two principal kernel constructs for this in FreeRTOS-based microcontrollers are **Queues** and **Semaphores**. The two fundamental problems in any multi-tasking firmware are: 1. **Data Transfer** — How does Task A pass a sensor reading to Task B without losing it or corrupting memory? 2. **Synchronization** — How does Task B know *when* new data is available, or how does Task A know that Task B has finished using a peripheral? Queues solve problem (1). Semaphores solve problem (2). --- ### 1.2 Queues — Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):** A **Queue** is a kernel-managed, thread-safe First-In-First-Out (FIFO) buffer that allows tasks or ISRs to send fixed-size data items to one or more receiving tasks through a single handle. Each FreeRTOS queue is characterized by two key parameters declared at creation time: - **Length** — the maximum number of items the queue can hold at any instant. - **Item Size** — the size (in bytes) of each individual item placed in the queue. A queue is therefore a *bounded, typed, atomic* communication channel. --- ### 1.3 Semaphores — Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):** A **Semaphore** is a kernel object containing a non-negative integer *count* and a *wait list*, used to provide signaling (task-to-task or ISR-to-task notification) and resource counting without transferring any data payload. There are three principal semaphore variants used in embedded RTOS design: | Variant | Core Purpose | Count Range | |---|---|---| | **Binary Semaphore** | Single-bit signal/event flag | **0 or 1** | | **Counting Semaphore** | Tracks multiple identical resources | **0 to N** | | **Mutex (Mutual Exclusion)** | Guards a single shared resource | **Locked (1) / Unlocked (0)** | --- ### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition **🧠 The Postal Mailbox Analogy (Queues)** Imagine a wall-mounted physical mailbox between two offices. Office A (the *producer task*) drops letters in. Office B (the *consumer task*) empties the mailbox into their inbox. The mailbox is a **queue**: - Letters are removed in the exact order they were inserted → **FIFO**. - The mailbox has a fixed capacity → **bounded length**. - Each letter is a fixed-size envelope → **fixed item size**. - If the mailbox is full, the postman must wait → **task blocks on full queue**. **🧠 The Parking Lot Analogy (Counting Semaphores)** A parking lot with **N spaces** displays a counter showing free spots. Each arriving car decrements the counter; each leaving car increments it. If the counter is **0**, a new car must wait at the gate. This is a **counting semaphore** — its integer *count* represents available resources. **🧠 The Bathroom Key Analogy (Mutexes)** A small office has one bathroom and a single key hanging on a hook. Only the person holding the key may enter. When they leave, they return the key to the hook. This is a **mutex** — it enforces mutual exclusion, and crucially, the key is *owned* by one task at a time. **🧠 The Doorbell Analogy (Binary Semaphores)** A doorbell (one button, one buzzer) is pressed by an ISR to signal a task that "an event has occurred." The button is **binary** — it is either pressed or not. Once the task services the event, the doorbell must be re-enabled. This is a **binary semaphore** used purely for *signaling*, not resource ownership. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet --- ## 2.1 Why ITC Is Needed — The Shared Memory Hazard In a preemptive RTOS, any task can be interrupted *at any instruction boundary* by a higher-priority task or an ISR. If two tasks read/write the same global variable without protection, the sequence shown below causes data corruption. Consider a 32-bit variable `sensorValue` updated by Task1 and read by Task2 on an 8-bit bus architecture where the read/write takes two instructions: | Step | Task1 (Writer) | Task2 (Reader) | Result | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | `MOV sensorValue_LOW, R0` | — | Partial write | | 2 | — *(preempted)* | `MOV R1, sensorValue_LOW` | Reads stale low byte | | 3 | — | `MOV R2, sensorValue_HIGH` | Reads new high byte | | 4 | `MOV sensorValue_HIGH, R1` | — | Mixed bytes | The reader obtains a **torn read** — half-old, half-new data. Queues and semaphores exist to prevent exactly this class of bug. --- ## 2.2 Queue Operational Model A FreeRTOS queue is implemented as a **ring buffer** with two indices and a thread-safe copy mechanism. Every `xQueueSend()` performs an *atomic byte-by-byte copy* from the caller's buffer into the queue's internal storage under a critical section. ### 2.2.1 Queue Creation A queue is created by a single call before the scheduler starts (or lazily from a task), and its handle is the only data the kernel needs to identify it. The relevant parameters are: - `uxQueueLength` → maximum number of items the queue can store simultaneously. - `uxItemSize` → byte-size of each item (e.g., 1 for `uint8_t`, 4 for `uint32_t`, 8 for a struct). ### 2.2.2 Queue Send (Post / Produce) A task or ISR places an item at the *tail* of the queue. The kernel copies the data into the queue's storage. Two behaviors exist: - **No-block** → returns immediately with success/fail. - **Block with timeout** → task is placed in the queue's *send-wait list* if the queue is full, and unblocked when a slot frees up or the timeout expires. ### 2.2.3 Queue Receive (Pend / Consume) A task removes the *head* item. If the queue is empty, the task blocks (optionally with timeout) until an item arrives. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** A queue is *the only* FreeRTOS primitive that actually moves data between tasks. A semaphore merely signals; it does not transfer payload bytes. --- ## 2.3 Semaphore Operational Model A semaphore's entire state is a single non-negative integer and a wait list. Three atomic operations exist: 1. **Take / Wait / P-operation** — Decrement the count. If the count is already 0, block the caller. 2. **Give / Post / Signal / V-operation** — Increment the count. If a task is waiting, unblock it (optionally waking the highest-priority waiter). 3. **Peek** — Read the count without modifying it (where supported). The crucial difference between a mutex and a binary semaphore is **ownership**: only the task that successfully took a mutex may give it. A binary semaphore may be given by *any* task or ISR, making it suitable for ISR-to-task signaling. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Parameter | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Queue memory footprint | $M_{queue} = uxQueueLength \times uxItemSize + K_{overhead}$ | Bytes; $K_{overhead}$ ≈ 80 B (FreeRTOS) | | 2 | Queue throughput bound | $\Theta_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{copy}}$ | Items/sec, where $t_{copy}$ is per-item copy time | | 3 | Semaphore count range (Binary) | $c \in \{0, 1\}$ | Discrete integer | | 4 | Semaphore count range (Counting) | $c \in \{0, 1, \dots, N\}$ | N = initial/max count | | 5 | Mean blocking time (M/G/1 approx.) | $T_{block} \approx \dfrac{\rho \cdot (S^2 / \mu + 1/\mu^2)}{2(1-\rho)}$ | $\rho$ = utilization, $S$ = service variance | | 6 | Priority inversion worst-case | $T_{inv} = T_{critical} + T_{noncrit\_hp}$ | Lower bound on blocking | | 7 | CPU utilization (basic) | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | $C_i$ = compute time, $T_i$ = period | | 8 | Tasks to wake (binary sem) | $n_{wake} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } c = 0 \text{ and waiters} \geq 1 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Boolean count | | 9 | Queue overflow check | $n_{items} \leq uxQueueLength$ | Strict inequality for safety | | 10 | ISR-safe API rule | Use `FromISR` suffix variants only inside ISRs | Hard real-time constraint | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** These relations are used in KTU lab assignments to size queues for UART DMA buffers, size semaphore counts for the number of simultaneously open file handles, and compute worst-case interrupt latency. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix --- ## 3.1 Derivation: Queue Memory Footprint and Buffering Capacity A common KTU question asks: *"Given a sensor that produces 16-bit ADC samples at 1 kHz, and a consumer task that bursts every 10 ms, design the queue length."* ### Step 1 — Establish the input rate The producer (ADC + ISR) deposits one item every 1 ms: $$f_{prod} = 1 \text{ kHz} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{prod} = \frac{1}{f_{prod}} = 1 \text{ ms}$$ ### Step 2 — Establish the consumer service time The consumer task runs every 10 ms and processes one item per run: $$T_{cons} = 10 \text{ ms} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_{cons} = 100 \text{ Hz}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute items produced per consumer cycle During one full 10 ms consumer period, the producer emits 10 items: $$N_{in} = \frac{T_{cons}}{T_{prod}} = \frac{10 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ ms}} = 10 \text{ items}$$ ### Step 4 — Compute items consumed per consumer cycle The consumer drains exactly 1 item per cycle: $$N_{out} = 1 \text{ item per cycle}$$ ### Step 5 — Compute the queue depth (Little's Law application) The steady-state queue depth is the difference between arrivals and departures, accumulated over the consumer's period: $$L_{queue} = (N_{in} - N_{out}) \times N_{cycles}^{accum} + \text{burst margin}$$ For a single steady cycle, the queue grows by 10 − 1 = 9 items per cycle. If the consumer could be delayed by up to 2 extra cycles (e.g., higher-priority task preempts it), the safe queue length is: $$L_{queue} = 9 + 9 \times 2 = 27 \text{ items} \Rightarrow \text{choose } uxQueueLength = 32$$ ### Step 6 — Compute the memory footprint Each item is 2 bytes (uint16_t). Using the formula from the cheat sheet: $$M_{queue} = uxQueueLength \times uxItemSize + K_{overhead}$$ $$M_{queue} = 32 \times 2 + 80 = 64 + 80 = 144 \text{ bytes}$$ ### Step 7 — Validate against the SRAM budget On a typical STM32 with 20 KB SRAM, this queue consumes less than 1 % of memory — well within the design budget. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Worst-Case Blocking Under Priority Inversion Consider three tasks: Task L (low priority) holds Mutex M; Task M (medium priority) becomes runnable; Task H (high priority) tries to take M. | Time | Event | Running Task | State of M | |---|---|---|---| | t0 | L takes M | L | locked | | t1 | H ready, blocks on M | L | locked, H waiting | | t2 | M ready, preempts L | M | locked, H waiting | | t3 | M finishes, L resumes | L | locked, H waiting | | t4 | L releases M, H unblocks | H → L | unlocked | The blocking time experienced by H is: $$T_{block}^{H} = T_{crit}^{L} + T_{noncrit}^{M}$$ where $T_{crit}^{L}$ is the critical section length of L and $T_{noncrit}^{M}$ is the time M runs while H is blocked. With **priority inheritance mutexes**, M's effective priority is temporarily raised to H's priority for the duration of L's critical section, so M cannot preempt L — eliminating the $T_{noncrit}^{M}$ term: $$T_{block,PI}^{H} = T_{crit}^{L}$$ This is the core benefit of the `xSemaphoreCreateMutex()` API versus `xSemaphoreCreateBinary()`. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Primitive | Requirement | Use Queue | Use Binary Sem | Use Counting Sem | Use Mutex | |---|---|---|---|---| | Need to pass actual data | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | | ISR-to-task event flag | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | | Task-to-task synchronization | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | | Guarding a shared resource | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | Pool of N identical resources | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | | Producer–consumer data stream | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | | Needs priority inheritance | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | Safe to call from ISR (`FromISR`) | ✅ send | ✅ give | ✅ give | ❌ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** A binary semaphore is *not* a mutex. Using a binary semaphore to guard a shared peripheral allows any task to `Give` it — leading to corrupted ownership and unbounded priority inversion. The exam answer must explicitly state this distinction. --- ## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Round-Trip Latency of a Queue Transaction A useful derivation for exam problems is the time from "producer calls Send" to "consumer returns from Receive": ### Step 1 — Producer side time The producer enters the kernel, copies the item into the ring buffer under a critical section, and exits: $$t_{send} = t_{enter} + n_{bytes} \cdot t_{copy} + t_{exit}$$ ### Step 2 — Wake-up latency If a consumer was blocked, the kernel sets it to *Ready*. Its actual start depends on the scheduler's tick resolution: $$t_{wake} \leq T_{tick}$$ ### Step 3 — Consumer side time When scheduled, the consumer executes `xQueueReceive`, which copies the item out: $$t_{recv} = t_{enter} + n_{bytes} \cdot t_{copy} + t_{exit}$$ ### Step 4 — Total round-trip $$\boxed{\,T_{RT} = t_{send} + t_{wake} + t_{recv}\,}$$ For a 4-byte item on a Cortex-M4 @ 72 MHz, the per-side copy is ~ 20 cycles ≈ 0.28 µs, so the total latency is dominated by $t_{wake}$, typically 1–10 µs in FreeRTOS configured with a 1 kHz tick. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics --- ## 4.1 High-Level Producer–Consumer Communication Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Producer Task
or ISR] -->|xQueueSend copy| Q[(FreeRTOS Queue
Ring Buffer)] Q -->|xQueueReceive copy| B[Consumer Task] Q -.->|block on full| A Q -.->|block on empty| B A -.->|FromISR variant| A ``` --- ## 4.2 Queue State Machine (Producer View) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 s1[Idle Task Running] --> s2[Call xQueueSend] s2 --> s3{Queue Full?} s3 -->|No| s4[Copy Item into Ring Buffer] s4 --> s5[Return pdPASS] s5 --> s1 s3 -->|Yes| s6{Block Allowed?} s6 -->|No| s7[Return errQUEUE_FULL] s7 --> s1 s6 -->|Yes| s8[Place Task in Send Wait List] s8 --> s9[Task Blocked] s9 --> s10{Timeout or Slot Free} s10 --> s4 s10 --> s11[Return pdFALSE Timeout] s11 --> s1 ``` --- ## 4.3 Semaphore Hierarchy and Usage Map ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Semaphore Family] --> B[Binary Semaphore
count is 0 or 1] S --> C[Counting Semaphore
count is 0 to N] S --> M[Mutex
count is 0 or 1
with priority inheritance] B --> B1[ISR to Task Signaling] B --> B2[Task to Task Sync] C --> C1[Resource Pool Management] C --> C2[Event Counters] M --> M1[Shared Peripheral Guard] M --> M2[Shared Memory Section] ``` --- ## 4.4 Priority Inversion Timeline (Without vs With Inheritance) ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant L as Task L (priority 1) participant M as Task M (priority 2) participant H as Task H (priority 3) participant K as Kernel Note over L,H: Without Priority Inheritance L->>K: Take Mutex H->>K: Take Mutex (blocks) M->>K: Preempt L M->>K: Run to completion L->>K: Release Mutex H->>K: Unblock and run Note over L,H: With Priority Inheritance L->>K: Take Mutex H->>K: Take Mutex (blocks) Note right of L: L's priority raised to 3 M->>K: Try preempt L K-->>M: Denied (L now priority 3) L->>K: Release Mutex L->>K: Priority restored to 1 H->>K: Unblock and run ``` --- ## 4.5 Functional Architecture: FreeRTOS IPC Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart TB APP[Application Layer] -->|API calls| KERN[FreeRTOS Kernel] KERN --> QENG[Queue Engine] KERN --> SENG[Semaphore Engine] KERN --> MUTX[Mutex Engine with PI] QENG --> RB[(Ring Buffer Pool)] SENG --> WL1[Wait List Binary] MUTX --> WL2[Wait List Mutex with Owner Tag] RB --> SRAM[(SRAM)] WL1 --> SRAM WL2 --> SRAM ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** **Differentiate between a Binary Semaphore and a Mutex. When would you choose one over the other in an embedded application?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Binary Semaphore** is a signaling primitive with a count of 0 or 1; it has *no ownership* — any task or ISR can `Give` it. It is used purely to signal an event (e.g., "ADC conversion complete" from an ISR to a processing task). A **Mutex** is a special binary semaphore with **ownership and priority inheritance**. Only the task that successfully called `xSemaphoreTake` may call `xSemaphoreGive`. It is used to protect a *shared resource* (e.g., a UART peripheral or a global linked list). **Choice rule:** Use a **binary semaphore** for ISR-to-task event signaling. Use a **mutex** whenever a critical section must be guarded against priority inversion. Using a binary semaphore in place of a mutex can cause unbounded priority inversion (1 mark for rule, 1 mark for ownership distinction, 1 mark for example). --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **List any three FreeRTOS queue APIs and state the role of the `xBlockTime` parameter.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. `xQueueCreate(uxQueueLength, uxItemSize)` — creates a queue and returns a handle (1 mark). 2. `xQueueSend(QueueHandle_t, void *pvItemToQueue, TickType_t xTicksToWait)` — posts an item to the tail; blocks up to `xBlockTime` ticks if the queue is full (1 mark). 3. `xQueueReceive(QueueHandle_t, void *pvBuffer, TickType_t xTicksToWait)` — pulls an item from the head; blocks up to `xBlockTime` ticks if the queue is empty (1 mark). `xBlockTime` specifies the maximum number of RTOS tick periods the calling task will sleep if the operation cannot complete immediately. A value of `0` makes the call non-blocking; `portMAX_DELAY` causes the task to wait indefinitely. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Queue-Based Design > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, Module 4]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the internal structure and operation of a FreeRTOS queue. Discuss what happens when (i) a task sends to a full queue, and (ii) a task receives from an empty queue. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An ADC interrupt service routine produces 12-bit samples at 5 kHz into a `uint16_t` queue. A consumer task processes samples every 50 ms in bursts. Compute (i) the minimum safe queue length for a 2-cycle consumer delay tolerance, and (ii) the SRAM consumed by the queue. Assume the kernel overhead constant is 80 bytes. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Internal structure and operation of a FreeRTOS queue (7 marks):** A FreeRTOS queue is a **ring buffer** of `uxQueueLength` slots, each of `uxItemSize` bytes, accompanied by head/tail indices and a wait list (1 mark). The structure contains: - A byte array for item storage. - `UBaseType_t uxHead`, `uxTail`, `uxMessagesWaiting`. - A linked list of tasks blocked on send (full) and receive (empty). **Send operation (1.5 marks):** The producer's data is byte-copied into `pcTail`. If `uxMessagesWaiting == uxQueueLength`, the task is moved from the Ready list into the queue's `xTasksWaitingToSend` list and the scheduler selects a new task. **Receive operation (1.5 marks):** The consumer's buffer is filled by byte-copying from `pcHead`. If `uxMessagesWaiting == 0`, the task is moved into `xTasksWaitingToReceive`. **Case (i) — Send to full queue (1.5 marks):** The sending task is added to the send wait list with a timeout of `xBlockTime`. The task becomes Blocked. When a receiver later frees a slot, the kernel checks the wait list (in priority order) and unblocks the highest-priority waiter, copies its item, and transitions it to Ready. If the timeout expires first, the call returns `errQUEUE_FULL`. **Case (ii) — Receive from empty queue (1.5 marks):** Symmetric to the above. The receiving task blocks on `xTasksWaitingToReceive` until a sender deposits an item, the timeout expires (returning `pdFALSE`), or the queue is deleted (returning `eQueueDeleted`). **Diagram (extra credit, not required for full marks):** A ring buffer with head/tail arrows, two wait lists, and arrows showing copy direction. --- **(b) Queue sizing calculation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Producer rate (1 mark):** $$f_{prod} = 5 \text{ kHz} \Rightarrow T_{prod} = \frac{1}{5000} = 0.2 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 2 — Consumer period (1 mark):** $$T_{cons} = 50 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 3 — Items produced per consumer cycle (1 mark):** $$N_{in} = \frac{T_{cons}}{T_{prod}} = \frac{50 \text{ ms}}{0.2 \text{ ms}} = 250 \text{ items}$$ **Step 4 — Net items accumulated per cycle (1 mark):** $$N_{net} = N_{in} - N_{out} = 250 - 1 = 249 \text{ items per cycle}$$ **Step 5 — Add 2-cycle delay tolerance (1 mark):** $$L_{queue} = N_{net} + 2 \times N_{net} = 3 \times 249 = 747 \text{ items}$$ **Step 6 — Round up to a power of 2 for the FreeRTOS macro `queueQUEUE_LENGTH` (1 mark):** $$uxQueueLength = 1024 \text{ (next power of 2 above 747)}$$ **Step 7 — SRAM footprint (1 mark):** $$M_{queue} = uxQueueLength \times uxItemSize + K_{overhead} = 1024 \times 2 + 80 = 2128 \text{ bytes} \approx 2.08 \text{ KB}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Semaphore-Based Design (Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern, Module 4 Alternative]** **(a)** Explain the three types of semaphores supported by FreeRTOS with one embedded-system example for each. Mention the API used to create each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Three tasks share a UART peripheral: Task H (high), Task M (medium), Task L (low). Task L holds a mutex for 20 ms. Task H tries to take the same mutex and blocks. If Task M requires 50 ms of CPU time and starts immediately after H blocks, compute (i) the blocking time of H *without* priority inheritance, and (ii) the blocking time of H *with* the priority-inheritance mutex. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Three semaphore types in FreeRTOS (7 marks):** 1. **Binary Semaphore (2 marks):** Count is restricted to 0 or 1. Used for ISR-to-task or task-to-task *signaling*, never for mutual exclusion. Example: An ADC end-of-conversion ISR `Gives` a binary semaphore; a processing task `Takes` it. API: `xSemaphoreCreateBinary()`. 2. **Counting Semaphore (2 marks):** Count can range from 0 to N. Used to manage a *pool of identical resources* or to count events. Example: A parking-lot controller counts free spaces; each arriving car `Takes`, each departing car `Gives`. API: `xSemaphoreCreateCounting(uxMaxCount, uxInitialCount)`. 3. **Mutex (2 marks):** A binary semaphore with **ownership** and **priority inheritance**. Used to guard a *single shared resource*. Example: Two tasks share a SPI bus; the bus is wrapped in a mutex. Only the task that took the mutex can give it. API: `xSemaphoreCreateMutex()`. (1 mark for the priority inheritance benefit.) --- **(b) Blocking time calculation (7 marks):** **Given:** - $T_{crit}^{L} = 20$ ms (L's critical section holding the mutex) - $T_{noncrit}^{M} = 50$ ms (M's CPU burst while H is blocked) - All other times negligible. **Step 1 — Blocking time *without* priority inheritance (3 marks):** Without PI, M (medium priority) can freely preempt L (low priority). Therefore H waits for both: $$T_{block}^{H} = T_{crit}^{L} + T_{noncrit}^{M}$$ $$T_{block}^{H} = 20 \text{ ms} + 50 \text{ ms} = 70 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 2 — Blocking time *with* priority inheritance (3 marks):** With PI, the moment H blocks on the mutex, the kernel raises L's effective priority to H's level. M can no longer preempt L. L runs to completion of its critical section (20 ms) and then releases the mutex. H unblocks immediately. $$T_{block,PI}^{H} = T_{crit}^{L} = 20 \text{ ms}$$ **Step 3 — Improvement factor (1 mark):** $$\eta = \frac{70}{20} = 3.5 \times \text{ reduction in worst-case blocking latency}$$ This demonstrates the engineering value of always using `xSemaphoreCreateMutex()` (and not `xSemaphoreCreateBinary()`) when guarding a shared resource. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. *Confusing binary semaphore with mutex:* Writing "binary semaphore can be used for mutual exclusion" costs 2 marks outright. The examiner will deduct for missing the ownership and priority-inheritance distinctions. > 2. *Forgetting `FromISR` suffix:* Using `xQueueSend` from inside an ISR is a hard fault in many ports. Always cite `xQueueSendFromISR` and `xSemaphoreGiveFromISR`. > 3. *Not sizing the queue length:* If a question gives a producer rate and consumer rate, the model answer must compute the depth — not merely say "create a queue." > 4. *Skipping the timeout value in API calls:* Examiner's keyword scan looks for `xBlockTime` or `xTicksToWait`; omitting the parameter loses 1 mark. > 5. *Power-of-2 rounding ignored:* FreeRTOS macros like `queueQUEUE_LENGTH` expect powers of 2. Examiners reward explicit rounding steps. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ A **queue** moves *data* between tasks/ISRs; a **semaphore** moves only a *signal* (a count change). - ✅ Every queue is **FIFO, bounded, and typed** by its `uxItemSize`. - ✅ A **binary semaphore** = 0/1 signaling flag, **no ownership**, callable from ISRs. - ✅ A **counting semaphore** = 0..N resource/event counter, used for pools. - ✅ A **mutex** = binary semaphore + **ownership** + **priority inheritance**; never call from an ISR. - ✅ Always use the **`FromISR` variant** of any API inside an interrupt service routine. - ✅ Queue length is sized by **Little's Law**: $L = (Rate_{in} - Rate_{out}) \times T_{delay} + \text{margin}$. - ✅ Blocking time is a **system-level performance metric** — minimize critical section length to reduce worst-case latency. - ✅ **Priority inheritance** is the only mechanism in FreeRTOS that bounds the duration of priority inversion. - ✅ Critical sections inside a mutex should contain **no blocking calls** (no `vTaskDelay`, no other `xQueueReceive`) — otherwise the system may deadlock. - ✅ The SRAM cost of an IPC object is `Length × ItemSize + ~80 B`; budget it into your linker script. - ✅ A `xBlockTime` of `0` makes the call non-blocking; `portMAX_DELAY` makes it wait forever. - ✅ Queues are *the* recommended mechanism for ISR-to-task data hand-off; semaphores are recommended for ISR-to-task *event* hand-off.

topics.title: Trust Zone Technology: Introduction to ARM Trust Zone
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Trust Zone Technology: Introduction to ARM Trust Zone

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ARM TrustZone Technology** is a hardware-enforced security extension integrated into the ARM architecture that partitions all System-on-Chip (SoC) hardware and software resources into two physically isolated execution environments: the **Secure World** and the **Non-Secure (Normal) World**. It enables a single physical processor core to operate as two virtual cores, with deterministic isolation enforced by hardware through a single additional bit — the **NS (Non-Secure) bit** — propagated across the AMBA AXI/AXI-4 bus fabric.

TrustZone was first introduced in **ARM1176JZ(F)-S** (2003) and is now a fundamental security primitive in **ARM Cortex-A** (application processors) and **ARM Cortex-M** (microcontroller profiles like Cortex-M23, M33, M55) series.

### Key Architectural Extensions Introduced by TrustZone
| Extension | Full Form | Function |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **NS bit** | Non-Secure bit | Added to bus transaction sideband signals (ARPROT, AWPROT) to tag access privilege |
| **TZASC** | TrustZone Address Space Controller | Dynamically partitions external DRAM into Secure/Non-Secure regions |
| **TZMA** | TrustZone Memory Adapter | Partitions small on-chip SRAM (e.g., last 4 KB/8 KB) into Secure regions |
| **TZPC** | TrustZone Protection Controller | Declares peripheral security attribution at SoC level |
| **TZIC** | TrustZone Interrupt Controller (Cortex-A) | Masks/routes interrupts to the correct world |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Two-Room Building

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of an SoC as a high-security office building with two floors:**

- The **Ground Floor (Non-Secure World)** is the public reception area. Visitors (untrusted apps, e.g., a web browser, a third-party game) can walk around freely, but the elevators and stairways to the top floor are physically locked.
- The **Top Floor (Secure World)** is the executive suite containing the vault, the biometric safe, and the master keys (cryptographic keys, DRM licenses, fingerprint templates, payment PINs).
- The **NS bit** is the security badge embedded in every visitor's ID card — embedded in every memory transaction. The door controllers (TZASC/TZPC) read the badge instantly and grant or deny access in **hardware, with zero software overhead**.
- The **SMC (Secure Monitor Call)** instruction is the only legal staircase between floors, guarded by a 24/7 security guard (Secure Monitor firmware running in EL3 / secure handler mode).

This physical isolation is critical because software-level security (sandboxing, OS user/kernel separation) can be **subverted by buffer overflows, rootkits, or kernel exploits**. TrustZone defeats such attacks by ensuring secure assets are *never* mapped into the Non-Secure memory map at all.

## 1.3 Industrial Significance

- **Standard metric:** TrustZone is present in **>10 billion deployed ARM cores** (as of ARM market data).
- **Mandatory in:** Mobile payments (Samsung Knox, Apple Secure Enclave uses a similar concept), DRM (Widevine L1, Microsoft PlayReady), automotive (AUTOSAR Classic, EVITA HSM), IoT firmware update verification.
- **Bold metric:** Hardware-level context switch between worlds typically occurs in **< 100 ns** on modern Cortex-A cores.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Execution Worlds — Operational Logic

TrustZone hardware uses a single state bit, but the operational impact spans the entire SoC fabric. The execution flow is governed by a hierarchical state model.

### 2.1.1 On Cortex-A (Application Processors)
The processor operates in one of four Exception Levels (EL0–EL3), combined with a security state:

| Exception Level | Non-Secure World | Secure World | Typical Software |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| EL0 | User App (Android) | Trusted App (KeyStore) | Applications |
| EL1 | Linux Kernel | Secure OS (OP-TEE) | OS Kernel |
| EL2 | Hypervisor | — | Virtualization |
| **EL3** | — | **Secure Monitor** | **World Switcher (only here)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Rule:** Only code executing in **EL3 (Secure Monitor)** can change the NS bit. User code, even in the Secure World, cannot arbitrarily reconfigure security boundaries.

### 2.1.2 On Cortex-M (Microcontroller Profile)
Cortex-M (M23, M33, M55) uses a *flat* security model — no EL hierarchy. Instead, security attribution is performed by:
- **SAU (Security Attribution Unit):** Up to **8 programmable regions**, defined by base address + size + security state.
- **IDAU (Implementation Defined Attribution Unit):** Vendor-fixed regions (e.g., flash, debug, OTP marked as Secure by default).
- A **single mode bit (MSPLIM / security attribute)** in the bus matrix determines access.

This makes Cortex-M TrustZone ideal for embedded MCUs (the focus of KTU Module 4).

## 2.2 The NS Bit — Bus Protocol Anatomy

The NS bit is the cornerstone. It rides along *every* AXI bus transaction as a sideband signal:

| AXI Channel | Sideband Signal | Width | Purpose |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Read Address | **ARPROT[1]** | 1 bit | 0 = Secure read, 1 = Non-Secure read |
| Write Address | **AWPROT[1]** | 1 bit | 0 = Secure write, 1 = Non-Secure write |

The receiving memory controller (TZASC) inspects the NS bit and either accepts or **bus-errors** the transaction in a single clock cycle. There is no fuzzy matching — it is binary and atomic.

## 2.3 The Secure Monitor Call (SMC) — World Switch Mechanism

To transition from Non-Secure → Secure World, software issues:

```assembly
SMC #imm16    ; on Cortex-A
```

This triggers an exception that traps to **EL3 (Secure Monitor)**, which:
1. Saves the Non-Secure context (banked registers, CPSR, SP).
2. Sets the **NS bit = 0** in the processor and bus matrix.
3. Restores the Secure World context.
4. Resumes execution in the Secure World.

> **Reverse direction (Secure → Non-Secure):** ERET (Exception Return) instruction with appropriate ELR/SPSR banked values.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical / Logical Expression | Unit / Notes |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| NS bit value | $\text{NS} \in \{0, 1\}$ where $0 = \text{Secure}, \; 1 = \text{Non-Secure}$ | Propagated on every AMBA AXI transaction |
| TZASC max regions (Cortex-A) | $N_{\text{TZASC}} = 8$ | Standard TZASC 380 variant |
| TZMA split granularity | $S_{\text{TZMA}} = 4 \text{ KB} \text{ or } 8 \text{ KB}$ | Last chunk of on-chip SRAM |
| SAU regions (Cortex-M) | $N_{\text{SAU}} = 8$ | Configurable via SAU_CTRL, SAU_RNR, SAU_RBAR, SAU_RLAR |
| Secure world context switch time | $t_{\text{switch}} < 100 \text{ ns}$ | Typical on Cortex-A53 @ 1 GHz |
| World switch triggering | $\text{SMC} \; \#\text{imm} \Rightarrow \text{exception to EL3}$ | Only legal bridge |
| IDAU security level (Cortex-M) | $L_{\text{IDAU}} \in \{\text{Secure}, \text{Non-Secure}, \text{NSC}\}$ | NSC = Non-Secure Callable |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mobile Payments:** Samsung Pay's tokenization runs in the Secure World; the Non-Secure Android OS never sees the raw card data.
- **DRM Content Protection:** Widevine L1 decrypts video frames inside TrustZone so that even a rooted Android cannot dump plaintext video.
- **Secure Boot:** The first-stage bootloader (BL1 in Samsung Knox) verifies the second-stage signature using a key fused in OTP marked Secure.
- **IoT Firmware Integrity:** Cortex-M33 with TrustZone isolates the cryptographic library (Secure) from the application logic (Non-Secure), preventing malware from exfiltrating the AES key.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Memory Region Security Classification Logic

The TrustZone Address Space Controller (TZASC) determines the security state of any incoming bus transaction using a deterministic lookup. Let us derive the decision function.

### 3.1.1 Formal Setup

Let an incoming AXI read transaction be characterized by:
- $A$ = address of the transaction (in bytes, 32-bit on Cortex-M, 32/64-bit on Cortex-A)
- $\text{NS}_{\text{req}}$ = Non-Secure bit signaled by the master on ARPROT[1]
- $R = \{R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_N\}$ = set of $N$ programmed TZASC regions, each defined by $(B_i, L_i, S_i)$ where $B_i$ = base, $L_i$ = limit, $S_i$ = security state

### 3.1.2 Region Match Condition

A transaction address $A$ falls within region $R_i$ if and only if:

$$
B_i \;\leq\; A \;\leq\; L_i
$$

### 3.1.3 Security Decision Function

The TZASC grants or denies access based on the following logic:

$$
\text{AccessGranted} \;=\; \begin{cases}
\text{TRUE}, & \text{if } A \in R_i \text{ and } S_i = \text{Secure (and NS}_{\text{req}} = 0\text{)} \\
\text{TRUE}, & \text{if } A \in R_j \text{ and } S_j = \text{Non-Secure (any NS}_{\text{req}}\text{)} \\
\text{FALSE}, & \text{if no region matches (default deny)}
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.1.4 Step-by-Step Numeric Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:** A TZASC is configured with two regions in an embedded SoC.
> - $R_1 = (0x1000\text{0000}, \; 0x1000\text{FFFF}, \; \text{Secure})$ — 64 KB secure key vault
> - $R_2 = (0x2000\text{0000}, \; 0x2007\text{FFFF}, \; \text{Non-Secure})$ — 512 KB application RAM
>
> An application at address $A = 0x1000\text{4A2C}$ issues a read with $\text{NS}_{\text{req}} = 1$.

**Step 1: Check $R_1$ containment.**

$$
B_1 = 0x10000000, \quad L_1 = 0x1000FFFF
$$

$$
0x10000000 \;\leq\; 0x10004A2C \;\leq\; 0x1000FFFF
$$

The lower bound holds trivially. The upper bound check:

$$
0x1000\text{FFFF} - 0x1000\text{4A2C} = 0x0000\text{B5D3} \;\geq\; 0
$$

Therefore, $A \in R_1$. ✓

**Step 2: Check security mismatch.**

$R_1$ is declared **Secure**, but $\text{NS}_{\text{req}} = 1$ (Non-Secure request).

**Step 3: Decision.**

$$
\text{AccessGranted} = \text{FALSE} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{TZASC raises a bus-error (SLVERR)}
$$

The Non-Secure application is denied. The transaction never reaches the physical memory, so the secure key is *invisible* to the Non-Secure world.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: World Switch Event Sequence

Below is the full chronological event sequence for a **Non-Secure → Secure World** transition (Cortex-A profile), useful for both KTU theory and numerical timing questions.

| Step | Event | Hardware State | Software State | Time (Relative) |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | Application executes `SMC #0` | EL1, NS=1 | Linux User request | $t_0$ |
| 2 | CPU latches SMC exception | EL1, NS=1 | Trap taken | $t_0 + 1$ cycle |
| 3 | Vector table dispatch to EL3 | EL3, NS=1 | Secure Monitor entry | $t_0 + 2$ cycles |
| 4 | Save NS=1 context (GPRs, SPSR, SP) | EL3, NS=1 | Context saved | $t_0 + \tau_{\text{save}}$ |
| 5 | Set SCR.NS=0 in CP15 | EL3, NS=0 | World flip | $t_0 + \tau_{\text{save}} + 1$ |
| 6 | Flush L1 cache (NS-tagged lines) | EL3, NS=0 | Cache cleaned | $t_0 + \tau_{\text{save}} + 1 + \tau_{\text{flush}}$ |
| 7 | Restore Secure context | EL3, NS=0 | Secure context loaded | $t_0 + \tau_{\text{switch}}$ |
| 8 | `ERET` to Secure EL1 | EL1, NS=0 | Secure OS resumes | $t_0 + \tau_{\text{switch}} + 1$ |

The total world-switch latency is bounded by:

$$
\tau_{\text{switch}} \;=\; \tau_{\text{save}} \;+\; \tau_{\text{flush}} \;+\; \tau_{\text{restore}}
$$

On a Cortex-A53 running at **1 GHz**, typical values are $\tau_{\text{save}} \approx 20$ ns, $\tau_{\text{flush}} \approx 30$ ns, $\tau_{\text{restore}} \approx 20$ ns, giving:

$$
\tau_{\text{switch}} \;\approx\; 20 + 30 + 20 \;=\; 70 \text{ ns}
$$

This is why TrustZone is used for latency-critical security functions like fingerprint matching on smartphones.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: SAU vs IDAU on Cortex-M

Since Module 4 of the KTU syllabus focuses on **microcontrollers** (Cortex-M), this is critical exam material.

| Attribute | SAU (Security Attribution Unit) | IDAU (Implementation Defined Attribution Unit) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Configurability | Software programmable | Hardware-fixed by vendor |
| Number of regions | **8** | Vendor-defined (e.g., 4–16) |
| Precedence | Lower (overridable) | Higher (overrides SAU) |
| Security states | Secure, Non-Secure, NSC | Secure, Non-Secure, NSC |
| Typical use | Application-defined partitions | Flash, OTP, debug interface |
| Access at reset | NS=0, all secure by default | Vendor-specific |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Precedence Rule (exam favorite):** If a memory address is marked Secure by the IDAU, **no SAU programming can make it Non-Secure**. This is the principle of *fail-secure defaults*.

The effective security state of an address $A$ is given by:

$$
S_{\text{eff}}(A) \;=\; \begin{cases}
S_{\text{IDAU}}(A), & \text{if defined} \\
S_{\text{SAU}}(A), & \text{otherwise} \\
\text{Secure}, & \text{if neither defines it (default deny / secure)}
\end{cases}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 SoC-Level TrustZone Block Architecture (Cortex-M33)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how TrustZone partitions a typical microcontroller SoC. The diagram uses only alphanumeric node IDs and clean uppercase labels per the engine constraints.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["ARM CORTEX M33 CPU"]
    SAU["SAU SECURITY ATTRIBUTION UNIT 8 REGIONS"]
    IDAU["IDAU VENDOR FIXED REGIONS FLASH OTP DEBUG"]
    AHB["AHB BUS MATRIX WITH NS BIT ARPROT AWPROT"]
    TZASC["TZASC EXTERNAL MEMORY CONTROLLER"]
    TZPC["TZPC PERIPHERAL PROTECTION CONTROLLER"]
    SRAM["SRAM SECURE BANK 0x30000000"]
    DRAM["EXTERNAL DRAM REGION A REGION B"]
    FLASH["INTERNAL FLASH SECURE BOOT 0x00000000"]
    PERIPH_S["PERIPHERAL SECURE AES RNG TRNG"]
    PERIPH_NS["PERIPHERAL NONSECURE UART GPIO TIMER"]
    APP_NS["NONSECURE APP RTOS THREADX"]
    TEE_S["SECURE TEE TFM OPTEE"]
    MONITOR["SECURE MONITOR HANDLER SG CALLER"]

    CPU --> SAU
    CPU --> IDAU
    CPU --> MONITOR
    MONITOR --> TEE_S
    MONITOR --> APP_NS
    SAU --> AHB
    IDAU --> AHB
    CPU --> AHB
    AHB --> TZASC
    AHB --> TZPC
    TZASC --> SRAM
    TZASC --> DRAM
    TZPC --> PERIPH_S
    TZPC --> PERIPH_NS
    AHB --> FLASH
```

## 4.2 TrustZone State Transition Flowchart (World Switch)

This flowchart models the complete state transitions during an SMC-based world switch, including the gatekeeper role of the Secure Monitor.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NSApp: BOOT
    NSApp: NON SECURE APPLICATION EL0 NS 1
    SecureApp: SECURE APPLICATION EL0 NS 0
    Monitor: SECURE MONITOR EL3 NS BRIDGE
    NSApp --> Monitor: SMC INSTRUCTION
    Monitor --> SecureApp: NS BIT 0 ERET
    SecureApp --> Monitor: ERET TO MONITOR
    Monitor --> NSApp: NS BIT 1 ERET
    SecureApp --> [*]: SECURE CALL COMPLETE
    NSApp --> [*]: TASK EXIT
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Bus Access Authorization

A logic-level flowchart of the TZASC decision process for every AXI transaction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["AXI TRANSACTION ARBITRATION"] --> B{"ADDRESS A MATCHES A TZASC REGION"}
    B -- NO --> Z["DEFAULT SECURE BUS ERROR SLVERR"]
    B -- YES --> C{"REGION STATE SECURE OR NONSECURE"}
    C -- SECURE --> D{"NS BIT 0 OR 1"}
    D -- NS 0 SECURE REQUEST --> E["GRANT READ WRITE"]
    D -- NS 1 NONSECURE REQUEST --> Z
    C -- NONSECURE --> F["GRANT ANY NS REQUEST NO RESTRICTION"]
    E --> G["DATA RETURNED TO MASTER"]
    F --> G
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question, PBCST504, Module 4]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Define ARM TrustZone. State any two key hardware components used to enforce it in an SoC.**

**Model Answer:**
ARM TrustZone is a hardware-based security extension of the ARM architecture that divides all SoC resources into two isolated execution domains: the **Secure World** and the **Non-Secure World**. This isolation is enforced at the hardware level using a single Non-Secure (NS) bit propagated across the AMBA bus.

Two key hardware components:
1. **TrustZone Address Space Controller (TZASC):** Dynamically partitions external memory (DRAM) into secure and non-secure regions based on the NS bit of the bus transaction.
2. **TrustZone Protection Controller (TZPC):** Declares the security attribution of on-chip peripherals (e.g., marking the cryptographic engine as Secure-only).

(Any other valid component like TZMA, SAU, or IDAU is also acceptable.)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**What is the role of the NS bit in TrustZone, and on which AMBA bus sideband signals does it appear?**

**Model Answer:**
The **NS (Non-Secure) bit** is a single-bit indicator that tags every memory transaction on the AMBA AXI bus to indicate whether the access originates from the Secure or Non-Secure world. A value of **0 indicates Secure**, and **1 indicates Non-Secure**. It is transmitted on the **ARPROT[1]** signal for read transactions and the **AWPROT[1]** signal for write transactions. Memory controllers (TZASC) and peripheral bridges (TZPC) inspect this bit to grant or deny access — without any software involvement — making the security enforcement deterministic and fast.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - December 2023 Style, PBCST504, Module 4]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**a) Explain the architecture of ARM TrustZone on Cortex-M microcontrollers. Describe the roles of SAU and IDAU in detail. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

ARM TrustZone on Cortex-M microcontrollers (e.g., Cortex-M23, M33, M55) is a lightweight hardware security extension that partitions memory and peripherals into Secure and Non-Secure worlds without the heavy EL hierarchy of Cortex-A.

**Architecture Overview:**
- The processor has a single mode bit (security attribute) that determines the current world's privilege for every bus access.
- The bus matrix (AHB) propagates the NS bit on every transaction.
- World switches occur via SG (Secure Gateway) instructions or hardware interrupt re-routing.

**SAU (Security Attribution Unit):**
- Software-programmable; up to **8 regions** can be defined.
- Each region is specified by a base address (SAU_RBAR) and a limit address (SAU_RLAR).
- Supports three security states: **Secure, Non-Secure, Non-Secure Callable (NSC)**.
- The NSC state is special: it allows Non-Secure code to *enter* a specific Secure function entry point, but cannot read/write the surrounding Secure memory.
- Used by the application to define custom memory partitions dynamically.

**IDAU (Implementation Defined Attribution Unit):**
- Hardware-fixed by the silicon vendor at design time.
- Marks critical resources — internal flash, OTP, debug logic — as Secure by default.
- Has **higher precedence** than the SAU: if IDAU says "Secure", no SAU setting can override it.
- Provides *fail-secure defaults*: even an uninitialized SAU cannot expose critical assets.

**Combined Effect:**
The effective security state of any address is determined first by the IDAU, then by the SAU, with Secure being the default. This dual-layer design ensures that security cannot be accidentally relaxed by misconfigured application software.

---

**b) With a neat diagram, explain the SMC-based world switch mechanism between Secure and Non-Secure worlds on a Cortex-A processor. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The world switch is orchestrated by the **Secure Monitor** code running in **EL3**, which is the only software allowed to modify the NS bit.

**Mechanism:**
1. The Non-Secure application executes the `SMC #imm16` instruction.
2. The CPU traps the SMC exception and dispatches to the EL3 vector table.
3. The Secure Monitor saves the Non-Secure context — general-purpose registers, SPSR, SP, and the PC — into a banked Secure-memory region.
4. The monitor sets the **SCR.NS = 0** bit, flipping the processor and bus matrix into the Secure world.
5. The L1 data cache is flushed of any Non-Secure-tagged lines to prevent leakage.
6. The Secure World context is restored from Secure RAM.
7. The monitor executes an `ERET` instruction, transferring control to the Secure OS at the saved PC.

**Reverse direction (Secure → Non-Secure):** Symmetric process; the monitor sets NS = 1, saves Secure context, restores Non-Secure context, and `ERET`s to the saved Non-Secure PC.

**Diagram (Mermaid block schematic):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    NSAPP["NS APPLICATION EL1 NS 1"] -->|"SMC imm"| MON["SECURE MONITOR EL3"]
    MON -->|"SAVE CTX"| SECMEM["SECURE RAM CONTEXT STORAGE"]
    SECMEM -->|"RESTORE"| SECAPP["SECURE OS EL1 NS 0"]
    SECAPP -->|"FINISH WORK"| MON
    MON -->|"ERET NS 1"| NSAPP
    SECAPP -->|"ERET NS 0"| SECAPP
```

**Significance:** The whole switch takes less than 100 ns and is atomic, making TrustZone usable even for latency-sensitive operations like fingerprint template matching on smartphones.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**a) List and explain the functions of TZASC, TZMA, and TZPC in a TrustZone-enabled SoC. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. TrustZone Address Space Controller (TZASC):**
- **Function:** Dynamically partitions **external DRAM** into multiple Secure and Non-Secure regions at runtime.
- A typical TZASC (e.g., TZASC-380) supports **up to 8 programmable regions**, each with independent base, size, and security state.
- Inspects the NS bit on every AXI bus transaction; denies mismatched access by returning a bus error (SLVERR).
- Used for protecting large memory buffers (e.g., decrypted video frames in DRM).

**2. TrustZone Memory Adapter (TZMA):**
- **Function:** Partitions **small on-chip SRAM** (typically the last 4 KB or 8 KB) into a Secure region.
- Simpler than TZASC — it is meant for low-latency secure scratchpad memory (e.g., cryptographic working buffers).
- The split granularity is fixed by hardware; the secure portion appears at the top of the SRAM address space.

**3. TrustZone Protection Controller (TZPC):**
- **Function:** Declares the security attribution of **on-chip peripherals** (UART, GPIO, AES engine, RNG, etc.).
- The TZPC has a set of configuration registers (TZPCR0size, DECPROT0, etc.) that software (typically the Secure Monitor) writes once at boot to mark each peripheral as Secure or Non-Secure.
- Once declared Secure, a peripheral is *invisible* to Non-Secure code — any read returns zero and any write is dropped.

**Summary Comparison:**

| Component | Protects | Granularity | Programmability |
|:---|:---|:---:|:---:|
| TZASC | External DRAM | Region-based (8) | Runtime |
| TZMA | On-chip SRAM (tail) | Fixed (4/8 KB) | Boot-time |
| TZPC | Peripherals | Per-peripheral | Boot-time |

---

**b) Compare ARM TrustZone implementations on Cortex-A and Cortex-M profiles. Why is Cortex-M TrustZone considered suitable for embedded MCUs? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Cortex-A (Application Profile) | Cortex-M (Microcontroller Profile) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Exception Levels | 4 levels (EL0–EL3) | Flat — Handler/Thread only |
| World Switcher | Secure Monitor at EL3 | Secure Monitor / Handler mode |
| Memory Attribution | TZASC + TZMA | SAU + IDAU |
| Typical software | OP-TEE, Trusty TEE | TF-M (Trusted Firmware-M) |
| Use case | Smartphones, tablets | IoT, automotive MCUs, sensors |
| Performance | High (Cortex-A53, A75) | Deterministic, low latency |
| Power | Higher | Ultra-low power |
| Footprint | MB of TEE memory | KB of secure memory |

**Why Cortex-M TrustZone suits embedded MCUs:**
1. **Determinism:** No cache or speculative execution side-channels — critical for real-time industrial control.
2. **Low power:** Secure peripherals can be kept in low-power states; world switches consume negligible energy.
3. **Small footprint:** The SAU is just 8 registers; the entire TrustZone logic adds < 10K gates.
4. **Fast boot:** Secure Boot can validate firmware in milliseconds — vital for IoT devices that wake from sleep.
5. **Real-time guarantees:** Secure interrupt handlers (e.g., motor-control crypto) can preempt Non-Secure code with bounded latency.
6. **Compliance:** Meets emerging IoT security standards like **PSA Certified Level 2/3** and **NIST IR 8259**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in TrustZone Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing TZASC vs TZMA:** TZASC is for *external* DRAM (large, region-based), TZMA is for *on-chip* SRAM (small, tail-split). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if swapped.
> 2. **Wrong NS-bit value:** Saying "NS = 1 means Secure" loses the mark. Always: **NS = 0 → Secure, NS = 1 → Non-Secure**.
> 3. **Omitting the SMC instruction:** Any world-switch explanation without mentioning SMC is incomplete.
> 4. **Forgetting EL3:** On Cortex-A, the Secure Monitor runs in EL3 — the *highest* privilege level — and is the **only** code allowed to change the NS bit.
> 5. **Neglecting NSC state:** On Cortex-M, the *Non-Secure Callable* (NSC) state is a distinct third state — not the same as Non-Secure. Examiners test this.
> 6. **Cortex-M has no EL hierarchy:** Do not write "EL1/EL3" for Cortex-M TrustZone — that is Cortex-A terminology.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TrustZone** = hardware-enforced isolation of SoC into **Secure World (NS=0)** and **Non-Secure World (NS=1)**.
- **NS bit** rides on **ARPROT[1]** and **AWPROT[1]** of every AMBA AXI transaction.
- **SMC instruction** is the only legal way (Cortex-A) to invoke a world switch; **SG instruction** is the Cortex-M equivalent.
- **EL3 (Secure Monitor)** is the sole authority permitted to flip the NS bit on Cortex-A.
- **TZASC** partitions external DRAM (up to 8 regions).
- **TZMA** partitions the tail of on-chip SRAM (4 KB or 8 KB).
- **TZPC** declares peripheral security (Secure / Non-Secure) at boot.
- **SAU** = software-programmable, 8 regions, lower precedence.
- **IDAU** = hardware-fixed, higher precedence, **cannot be overridden** by SAU.
- **NSC (Non-Secure Callable)** is a *third* state on Cortex-M allowing selective entry into Secure functions.
- **Default state at reset on Cortex-M is Secure**, ensuring fail-secure behavior.
- **World switch latency** is typically **< 100 ns** on modern Cortex-A cores.
- **Primary use cases:** Secure Boot, mobile payment tokenization, DRM (Widevine L1), IoT firmware integrity, automotive HSM.
- **Cortex-M TrustZone** is the focus of KTU Module 4 because of its relevance to microcontroller-based embedded designs.
- **No TrustZone ≠ No security:** TrustZone is a *hardware primitive*; it must be paired with a TEE (Trusted Execution Environment) like OP-TEE or TF-M for a complete security stack.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Trust Zone Architecture and Features
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Trust Zone Architecture and Features

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TrustZone Architecture** is a **hardware-enforced security extension** introduced by **ARM Holdings** that partitions a microcontroller (or system-on-chip) into two physically and logically isolated execution environments: the **Secure World** and the **Non-Secure (Normal) World**. It enables a **root-of-trust** in the silicon itself, ensuring that sensitive firmware, cryptographic keys, and intellectual property (IP) are isolated from the rich/normal-world application code.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus context (PBCST504 – Microcontrollers), the term *TrustZone* specifically refers to the **ARMv8-M TrustZone for Cortex-M33 / Cortex-M23 / Cortex-M55 / Cortex-M85** class microcontrollers, which is distinct from the older ARMv7-A TrustZone used in Cortex-A application processors. TrustZone-M brings the security paradigm of application processors into deeply embedded, deterministic, real-time microcontroller environments.

> [!NOTE]
> **TrustZone ≠ TrustZone-M.** The M-profile variant (introduced with ARMv8-M, 2017) is tailored for microcontrollers with deterministic interrupt latency, single-core operation, and a smaller silicon footprint. The A-profile variant uses a hypervisor mode and EL3 monitor.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a corporate office building with a **high-security server room** inside it.

- The **building** = the microcontroller chip.
- The **server room door** = a **hardware gate** (TrustZone-aware bus firewall, NS bit).
- The **server room** = **Secure World** (holds crypto keys, secure boot, secure firmware).
- The **main office** = **Non-Secure World** (runs the user's RTOS, application code, possibly an untrusted third-party app).
- **Employees** = software threads.
- **The access control panel on the door** = **SAU + IDAU** (Secure Attribution Unit + Implementation Defined Attribution Unit) — they decide *which corridors* are secure.
- **An airlock** with a one-way mirror = a **Secure Gateway (SG)** — the only legitimate way a Non-Secure caller can ask the Secure World to perform a function.
- **Security guards on the staircase** = **MPU regions** — fine-grained, software-programmable memory protection *within* each world.

Just as the building is one physical structure, the chip is one piece of silicon — but TrustZone divides it into two **logically disjoint** worlds that **cannot** see or modify each other's memory, peripherals, or registers unless explicitly allowed.

## 1.3 Why TrustZone Exists (The Engineering Problem)

Modern IoT/embedded devices face threats that purely software-based security cannot reliably defend against:

- **Memory dump attacks** — adversaries read firmware from RAM.
- **Rogue firmware updates** — malicious code replaces legitimate code.
- **Side-channel probing** — JTAG/SWD exfiltrates internal state.
- **Buffer overflows in 3rd-party libraries** — break out of the application and overwrite secure data.

TrustZone **raises the bar** by making these attacks require physical silicon modification rather than just a software exploit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard metric / state bit:** TrustZone is governed by a single attribute bit called the **NS (Non-Secure) bit** that propagates on **every bus transaction** in the system. The NS bit is forwarded on the **AHB5 / AXI5** bus, allowing memory controllers, peripherals, and bus bridges to filter accesses *in hardware*.

## 1.4 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Secure World** | World where the secure firmware executes; full access to all resources. |
| **Non-Secure World** | World where normal/rich application code executes; restricted visibility. |
| **NS bit** | A single bit indicating the security state of a bus transaction. |
| **SAU** | Secure Attribution Unit — software-programmable region attributer. |
| **IDAU** | Implementation Defined Attribution Unit — vendor-fixed region attributer. |
| **SG instruction** | Secure Gateway — the only legal entry from Non-Secure to Secure. |
| **TT instruction** | Test Target — returns the security attribution of an address. |
| **FU** | Function-call convention extension (B / BL → BXNS / BLXNS). |
| **TZB / MPCBB** | TrustZone Bus / Memory Protection Controller Block Builder. |
| **FNCRETURN** | Mark for return-from-secure-function markers. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Security States

TrustZone-M extends the Cortex-M **Handler / Thread mode** concept with a **security state axis**. Every execution context exists in **one of two worlds**:

$$
\text{Code State} = (\text{Mode}, \text{Security})
$$

The four possible combinations are:

$$
S_{state} \in \{\text{(Thread, Secure)},\ \text{(Handler, Secure)},\ \text{(Thread, Non-Secure)},\ \text{(Handler, Non-Secure)}\}
$$

**Stack pointers** are also duplicated:

$$
\text{MSP} \rightarrow \text{MSP}_{\text{secure}} \cup \text{MSP}_{\text{non-secure}}
$$

$$
\text{PSP} \rightarrow \text{PSP}_{\text{secure}} \cup \text{PSP}_{\text{non-secure}}
$$

The **CONTROL[2]** (SPSEL is bit 1, **nPRIV** is bit 0) is now extended, and a new bit **CONTROL[0]** of the banked CONTROL register controls stack choice, while a per-bank state machine tracks the security.

> [!NOTE]
> **Lazy stacking:** When the CPU transitions between worlds, the hardware automatically pushes `{R0–R3, R12, LR, ReturnAddress, xPSR}` onto the **opposite world's stack** for the **Non-Secure → Secure** transition. This is hardware-enforced, not a software decision.

## 2.2 Memory Attribution — The Heart of TrustZone

TrustZone's protection model is **attribute-based**, not list-based. Each memory address in the 4 GB address space carries an implicit **security attribution** decided jointly by:

$$
\text{Attribute}(A) = \text{IDAU}(A)\ \text{AND}\ \text{SAU}(A)\ \text{AND}\ \text{MPU}_{\text{active}}(A)
$$

- **IDAU** is **fixed by the chip vendor** at silicon design time (cannot be changed by software). It typically marks the **Secure ROM**, **Secure RAM**, **peripheral alias regions**, and **non-secure callable (NSC) regions**.
- **SAU** is **software-programmable** at secure boot, allowing the secure firmware to carve up the remaining address space into **Secure**, **Non-Secure**, or **NSC** regions.
- **MPU** in each world further restricts access (R/W/X, privileged/unprivileged) *within* the world.

### 2.2.1 Address Range Examples (vendor-specific)

| Address Range | IDAU Default | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| 0x0000_0000 – 0x000F_FFFF | Secure | Boot ROM |
| 0x1000_0000 – 0x1FFF_FFFF | Non-Secure | External flash (NS) |
| 0x2000_0000 – 0x2001_FFFF | Secure | Secure SRAM |
| 0x2002_0000 – 0x3FFF_FFFF | Non-Secure | Non-Secure SRAM alias |
| 0x4000_0000 – 0x4001_FFFF | Secure | Secure peripherals |
| 0x4002_0000 – 0x5FFF_FFFF | Non-Secure | NS peripherals |
| 0xE000_0000 – 0xE00F_FFFF | Secure | System Control Space (SCS) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NSC (Non-Secure Callable) regions** are the *only* memory regions that the Non-Secure world can **branch to** when entering the Secure world. They must contain exactly the **SG (Secure Gateway)** instruction followed by a function. This prevents code injection.

## 2.3 Interrupts and Exceptions in TrustZone

ARMv8-M introduces a new bit, **NVIC_ITNS** (Interrupt Target Non-Secure), to configure each individual interrupt as **secure-targeted** or **non-secure-targeted**:

$$
\text{IRQ}_i.\text{target} = \begin{cases}\text{Secure} & \text{if ITNS}[i] = 0 \\ \text{Non-Secure} & \text{if ITNS}[i] = 1 \end{cases}
$$

- A **Secure IRQ** is **always serviced by the Secure world**, even if the CPU is currently in the Non-Secure world. The hardware automatically performs a **world switch on exception entry**.
- A **Non-Secure IRQ** is **invisible to the Secure world** (registers such as `NVIC_ISER0` are banked — the Secure world has its own NVIC view).
- **NMI, HardFault, BusFault, MemManage, UsageFault, SVC, PendSV, SysTick** all have configurable security targeting in M-profile.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tail chaining** across worlds is supported — if a Secure exception is taken immediately after a Non-Secure exception, the CPU performs **lazy stacking** to the Secure stack pointer, with **deterministic latency** (no world-switch penalty in the normal case).

## 2.4 Bus Transactions and the NS Bit

Every bus transaction on **AHB5 / AXI5** carries an **HNONSEC** (or equivalent) sideband signal. Memory controllers, bus bridges, and peripherals **inspect this bit** and either:

- **Accept** the transaction (if the peripheral is configured as accessible from that world), or
- **Return a BusFault** (if the peripheral is marked Secure-only and the master is Non-Secure).

This makes TrustZone **distributed** — there is no single "gatekeeper" to defeat; every peripheral port and memory port enforces it.

## 2.5 The Secure Gateway (SG) Mechanism

The **SG** instruction is a 32-bit special opcode. It is **only valid in NSC memory**. When the CPU executes `SG`:

1. The hardware verifies the instruction is at the start of an NSC region.
2. The CPU performs a **Non-Secure → Secure world transition** (security state changes, registers and stack are banked).
3. The `LR` is set to a special value **FNCRETURN** so that on `BX LR` the CPU knows to return to Non-Secure mode.
4. **Vector fetches and stack pushes** are routed to the Secure world's stack.

The standard calling pattern:

$$
\text{Caller (NS)} \xrightarrow{\text{BLX}} \text{Entry in NSC} \xrightarrow{\text{SG}} \text{Secure Function Body} \xrightarrow{\text{BX LR}} \text{Back to NS caller}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Attempting to execute SG outside NSC causes a **SecureFault**. Attempting to branch to a *non-NSC Secure* region from Non-Secure world causes a **SecureFault**. These are **not** recoverable HardFaults.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

| # | Concept | Symbol / Symbolism | Value / Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of security states | $N_s$ | **2** (Secure, Non-Secure) |
| 2 | NS bit per transaction | $b_{NS}$ | 1 bit (0 = Secure, 1 = Non-Secure) |
| 3 | Banked stack pointers | $SP$ | 4 (MSP_sec, MSP_ns, PSP_sec, PSP_ns) |
| 4 | Address space | $A$ | **4 GB** (32-bit, ARMv8-M) |
| 5 | IDAU priority | $P_{IDAU}$ | Highest (vendor-fixed) |
| 6 | SAU priority | $P_{SAU}$ | Lower than IDAU |
| 7 | MPU priority | $P_{MPU}$ | Lowest of the three (within world) |
| 8 | SAU regions | $R_{SAU}$ | Vendor-specific, typically **4 to 8** |
| 9 | MPU regions per world | $R_{MPU}$ | Typically **8 to 16** |
| 10 | NSC region count | $N_{NSC}$ | Subset of Secure regions (vendor-decided) |
| 11 | ITNS register width | $W_{ITNS}$ | One bit per interrupt |
| 12 | FNCRETURN marker | $\text{LR}$ | **0xFEFF_FFxx** (special) |
| 13 | Interrupts banked | $N_{IRQ}$ | All NVIC registers world-banked |
| 14 | Default boot state | $S_0$ | **Secure** (so the boot code can decide) |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | TrustZone Usage |
|---|---|
| **Smart cards / SIM** | Immutable secure element for crypto key storage |
| **IoT edge nodes** | Secure boot + OTA update verification in Secure world |
| **Automotive ECUs** | Isolation between OEM firmware and 3rd-party apps (AUTOSAR) |
| **Payment terminals (POS)** | PIN handling, key-injection isolated from app layer |
| **Industrial PLCs** | Secure firmware update channel; ladder logic cannot access proprietary algorithms |
| **Medical devices (FDA)** | Tamper-proof logging of medical sensor data |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The cost of TrustZone:** A TrustZone-M core consumes **~15-25% more die area** than a non-TrustZone Cortex-M core of the same generation, but offers hardware-grade isolation that would otherwise require a separate secure MCU on the same board.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Resolving Security Attribution of an Address

The resolution of an address's effective security attribute follows a **three-level priority cascade**. The chip vendor freezes IDAU at design time. The SAU is programmed by secure firmware at boot. The MPU is programmed later by either world.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-step Logical Derivation

**Step 1 — IDAU (highest priority, vendor-fixed)**

The IDAU is a hardware decode of the upper address bits. It returns one of three outputs:

$$
\text{IDAU}(A) = \begin{cases} \text{Secure (S)} & \text{if } A \in \mathcal{R}^{\text{sec}}_{\text{IDAU}} \\ \text{Non-Secure (NS)} & \text{if } A \in \mathcal{R}^{\text{ns}}_{\text{IDAU}} \\ \text{NSC} & \text{if } A \in \mathcal{R}^{\text{nsc}}_{\text{IDAU}} \\ \text{SAU-controlled (—)} & \text{otherwise (escape region)} \end{cases}
$$

**Why does it come first?** Because the boot ROM and secure peripherals must be **absolutely guaranteed** to be Secure, even if the secure firmware is buggy and has misconfigured the SAU. The chip vendor encodes these regions in hardware, not in software.

**Step 2 — SAU (next priority, software-programmable)**

For the *escape* region (where IDAU yields "—"), the SAU takes over:

$$
\text{SAU}(A) = \begin{cases} \text{Secure} & \text{if } \exists r \in \text{SAU\_regs}: A \in r \land r.\text{type} = \text{Secure} \\ \text{Non-Secure} & \text{if } \exists r \in \text{SAU\_regs}: A \in r \land r.\text{type} = \text{NS} \\ \text{NSC} & \text{if } \exists r \in \text{SAU\_regs}: A \in r \land r.\text{type} = \text{NSC} \\ \text{default} & \text{otherwise (configurable to S or NS)} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Effective attribute (combination)**

$$
A_{\text{attr}} = \text{combine}(\text{IDAU}(A), \text{SAU}(A))
$$

where the combination rule is the **AND** of "Secure" and **OR** of "NS" — i.e., a region is Non-Secure only if **both** IDAU and SAU agree it is Non-Secure:

$$
A_{\text{attr}} = \begin{cases} \text{Secure} & \text{if IDAU} = \text{Secure OR SAU} = \text{Secure} \\ \text{NSC} & \text{if IDAU} = \text{NSC OR SAU} = \text{NSC} \\ \text{Non-Secure} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Why AND-OR?** Because the vendor wants to give the secure firmware a *safe default*: even if the firmware is completely absent (no SAU programming), the chip's hard-coded regions are still Secure. This is the principle of **fail-secure** design.

### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example

Consider a Cortex-M33 SoC with the following IDAU and SAU configuration.

**IDAU map (vendor-fixed):**
- 0x1000_0000 – 0x1FFF_FFFF → Non-Secure (external flash)
- 0x3000_0000 – 0x3001_FFFF → Non-Secure Callable (NSC)
- All other regions → "escape" (SAU-controlled)

**SAU regions (programmed by secure boot):**
- Region 0: 0x2000_0000 – 0x2001_FFFF → Secure
- Region 1: 0x2002_0000 – 0x2002_FFFF → NSC
- All other escape regions → default Non-Secure (SAU_CTRL.ALLNS = 0, but no region matches)

**Query: What is the effective security of address 0x3000_4000?**

Applying Step 1:
$$
\text{IDAU}(0x3000\_4000) = \text{NSC} \quad \text{(falls in 0x3000\_0000 – 0x3001\_FFFF)}
$$

Applying Step 2:
$$
\text{SAU}(0x3000\_4000) = \text{default} \quad \text{(no SAU region covers it)}
$$

Applying Step 3 (combination):
$$
A_{\text{attr}} = \text{NSC}
$$

**Conclusion:** Address 0x3000_4000 is Non-Secure Callable. The Non-Secure world can branch to code at this address (provided it is an `SG` instruction).

**Query: What is the effective security of address 0x2000_8000?**

Applying Step 1:
$$
\text{IDAU}(0x2000\_8000) = \text{escape}
$$

Applying Step 2:
$$
\text{SAU}(0x2000\_8000) = \text{Secure (Region 0)}
$$

Applying Step 3:
$$
A_{\text{attr}} = \text{Secure}
$$

**Conclusion:** Address 0x2000_8000 is Secure. A Non-Secure world read attempt would generate a SecureFault.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of the World-Switching Sequence (Non-Secure → Secure)

The CPU executes a `BLXNS` (or `BLX` if no FPU is involved) to a function in NSC. The hardware performs the following deterministic sequence:

**Step 1 — Prefetch and decode**

The CPU fetches the instruction at the target address. The target must be marked NSC by SAU/IDAU; otherwise a **SecureFault** is generated.

**Step 2 — Identify SG opcode**

The instruction is decoded. If it is the `SG` opcode, the world-switch sequence is initiated.

**Step 3 — Save Non-Secure context**

$$
\text{Stack}_{\text{secure}}.\text{push}\big(\text{PC}_{\text{return}},\ \text{xPSR}_{\text{NS}}\big)
$$

The CPU pushes the return address and the saved xPSR to the **Secure stack** (because once the CPU is in the Secure world, it must use the Secure stack).

**Step 4 — Update CONTROL and banked registers**

$$
\text{CONTROL}_{\text{secure}}.\text{SPSEL} \leftarrow \text{original value}
$$

$$
\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{MSP}_{\text{secure}}
$$

**Step 5 — Set security state**

$$
S \leftarrow \text{Secure}
$$

**Step 6 — Continue execution in Secure world**

The instruction *after* the SG is now executed in the Secure world. The secure function can do its work and returns via:

$$
\text{BX LR} \Rightarrow \text{CPU reads FNCRETURN marker} \Rightarrow \text{Security state} \leftarrow \text{NS};\ \text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP}_{\text{NS}}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: TrustZone vs Traditional Security

| Parameter | Software-only security (no TrustZone) | TrustZone-M (hardware-isolated) |
|---|---|---|
| Isolation mechanism | OS process / RTOS task | Hardware-level world switch |
| Bypass complexity | Read memory dump → defeat | Requires silicon invasive attack |
| Crypto key storage | In encrypted flash | In Secure SRAM behind NSC gate |
| Interrupt latency overhead | None | ~0 cycles (lazy stacking) |
| Die area cost | **0%** | **~15–25%** |
| Code size overhead | App + crypto libraries | App + secure stubs + veneers |
| Vendor effort | Low | High (IDAU definition, NSC design) |
| Failure mode | Software bug = total compromise | Software bug = contained within world |
| Root of trust location | Software (mutable) | **Hardware (immutable)** |

## 3.3 Derivation: SAU Region Count Trade-off

A typical Cortex-M33 has **8 SAU regions**. Each region requires a base register, a limit register, and a control register. The trade-off is:

$$
\text{Coverage} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (L_i - B_i + 1) \leq 4\ \text{GB}
$$

The number of regions $N$ is vendor-fixed, but the *placement* of them is a critical design decision. A standard partition is:

$$
N = N_{\text{code}} + N_{\text{data}} + N_{\text{NSC}} + N_{\text{periph}} \leq 8
$$

**Example partition:**
- 1 region: Secure flash (0x0000_0000 – 0x000F_FFFF) — **Secure**
- 1 region: Secure SRAM (0x2000_0000 – 0x2001_FFFF) — **Secure**
- 1 region: NSC stub area (0x2001_0000 – 0x2001_0FFF) — **NSC**
- 1 region: NS flash (0x1000_0000 – 0x1FFF_FFFF) — **NS**
- 1 region: NS SRAM (0x2002_0000 – 0x2002_FFFF) — **NS**

This uses only 5 SAU regions, leaving 3 for vendor-specific carve-outs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a TrustZone-M Microcontroller

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional block-level architecture of a Cortex-M33 (or similar) device with TrustZone enabled. Note that all blocks operate on the **AHB5** bus, and the **NS bit** propagates alongside every read/write signal.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["CPU Core Cortex M33"] --> B["Bus Matrix AHB5"]
    B --> C["Code Region A 0x00000000"]
    B --> D["Code Region B 0x10000000"]
    B --> E["SRAM Region 0x20000000"]
    B --> F["Peripheral Region 0x40000000"]
    B --> G["System Control Space 0xE0000000"]
    A --> H["SAU Secure Attribution Unit"]
    A --> I["IDAU Vendor Fixed Unit"]
    A --> J["MPU Bank A Secure"]
    A --> K["MPU Bank B Non Secure"]
    H --> B
    I --> B
    J --> B
    K --> B
    L["Secure Boot ROM"] --> A
    M["Non Secure Application"] --> A
    N["Secure NVIC"] --> A
    O["Non Secure NVIC"] --> A
    P["Secure Peripherals Crypto DMA"] --> B
    Q["Non Secure Peripherals UART GPIO"] --> B
    R["NSC Region Non Secure Callable"] --> B
    S["TrustZone Bus Filter on each AHB slave port"] --> B
    B --> T["External Bus Off chip memory"]
```

### 4.1.1 Diagram Interpretation

- The **CPU** is the only master that initiates transactions. Every transaction it issues carries the **NS bit** reflecting the current security state.
- The **SAU** and **IDAU** work in parallel to attribute every address. The **MPU** is also active, but is *banked*: there is one MPU configuration for Secure and another for Non-Secure.
- The **NSC region** is a small address range inside Secure memory, marked as Non-Secure Callable. It holds the entry veneers.
- The **TrustZone Bus Filter** sits on every AHB slave port and inspects the NS bit; if a Non-Secure transaction targets a Secure-only region, the filter issues a **BusFault** and the transaction is aborted.

## 4.2 World Transition State Machine

The following state diagram traces the possible transitions between the two security worlds. A transition can occur only via specific trigger events: secure interrupt, SG instruction, exception return, or reset.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    secstateA["Secure World"]
    nsecstateB["Non Secure World"]
    secstateA --> nsecstateB: BX LR with FNCRETURN marker
    secstateA --> nsecstateB: Exception return to Non Secure state
    nsecstateB --> secstateA: Secure interrupt taken
    nsecstateB --> secstateA: BLXNS to NSC executes SG
    secstateA --> secstateA: Internal BX BL within Secure code
    nsecstateB --> nsecstateB: Internal BX BL within Non Secure code
    secstateA --> secstateA: Hardware reset enters Secure
```

### 4.2.1 Trigger Categories

| Trigger | Direction | Stack Used | Latency (cycles) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardware reset** | (any) → Secure | MSP_secure | Reset sequence |
| **Secure interrupt** | NS → S | MSP_secure (lazy) | ~12 cycles |
| **NS exception to S-vec** | NS → S | MSP_secure (lazy) | ~12 cycles |
| **SG via BLXNS** | NS → S | MSP_secure | ~4 cycles |
| **BX LR (FNCRETURN)** | S → NS | Restored from saved context | ~4 cycles |

> [!NOTE]
> The reason "lazy stacking" makes this fast is that the CPU does not immediately push the entire exception frame; it pushes only a small "sentinel" frame, and defers the full frame push until the Secure world actually needs to use those registers.

## 4.3 SAU/IDAU/MPU Decision Flow

The following diagram illustrates the **priority cascade** described in Section 3.1.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    addr0["Address A"] --> checkA["IDAU Check"]
    checkA -->|Secure| resultA["Result: Secure"]
    checkA -->|NS| resultB["Result: NS"]
    checkA -->|NSC| resultC["Result: NSC"]
```
