```mermaid
    checkA -->|Escape| sauCheck["SAU Check"]
    sauCheck -->|Region match| resultD["Use SAU decision"]
    sauCheck -->|No match| defaultA["Use default ALLNS setting"]
    resultA --> mpuA["MPU Check within Secure world"]
    resultB --> mpuB["MPU Check within Non Secure world"]
    resultC --> finalA["Final: NSC"]
    mpuA --> finalB["Final: with MPU perms"]
    mpuB --> finalC["Final: with MPU perms"]
    resultD --> mpuA
    resultD --> mpuB
    defaultA --> mpuA
    defaultA --> mpuB
```

### 4.3.1 Reading the Flowchart

- For every bus transaction, the **IDAU** is consulted **first**. If it returns a non-escape result, the decision is final.
- Only when IDAU says "escape" does the **SAU** weigh in. The SAU either matches a region or applies a default.
- The **MPU** is the final filter, but it works *within* the world that the transaction originated in. It cannot promote a transaction's security — it can only restrict **RWX** and **privileged/unprivileged** access.

## 4.4 Memory Map (Example – Cortex-M33 with TrustZone)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    map0["0x00000000 to 0x000FFFFF Secure Boot ROM"]
    map1["0x10000000 to 0x1FFFFFFF NS External Flash"]
    map2["0x20000000 to 0x2001FFFF Secure SRAM"]
    map3["0x20010000 to 0x2001FFFF NSC Stub Region"]
    map4["0x20020000 to 0x3FFFFFFF NS SRAM alias"]
    map5["0x40000000 to 0x4001FFFF Secure Peripherals"]
    map6["0x40020000 to 0x5FFFFFFF NS Peripherals"]
    map7["0xE0000000 to 0xE00FFFFF System Control Space Secure"]
    map8["0xE0100000 to 0xFFFFFFFF Vendor Specific"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The exact map is **vendor-defined**. Different Cortex-M33-based microcontrollers (NXP LPC5500, STM32L5, Infineon PSoC 64, Cypress / Infineon PSoC 6, etc.) have their own proprietary IDAU maps. The KTU examination expects familiarity with the *principles*, not a specific vendor's map.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model Paper)]**

*List and explain the two security states of ARM TrustZone-M architecture. Mention the role of the NS bit.*

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** ARM TrustZone-M is a hardware security extension that partitions the microcontroller into two execution environments: the **Secure World** and the **Non-Secure (Normal) World**.

**Secure World (1 mark):** The Secure world is the privileged, trusted environment where sensitive firmware such as secure boot, cryptographic key storage, secure OTA update verification, and any IP that must not be exposed to the application runs. Code executing in the Secure world has full access to all memory, peripherals, and registers of the device.

**Non-Secure World (1/2 mark):** The Non-Secure world is the untrusted, application environment where the rich RTOS, third-party libraries, and untrusted user code execute. It has restricted access — it can only read/write/execute memory and peripherals that have been explicitly exported to it.

**NS bit (1/2 mark):** Every bus transaction on the AHB5 bus carries a single **NS (Non-Secure) bit**. The NS bit is 0 for transactions originating from the Secure world and 1 for transactions originating from the Non-Secure world. Memory controllers, bus bridges, and peripherals use this bit to filter accesses; a Non-Secure access to a Secure-only region is rejected with a BusFault / SecureFault.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – December 2023 (Model Paper)]**

*Explain the role of the SAU and IDAU in TrustZone-M. Why is the IDAU given higher priority than the SAU?*

#### Model Answer (3 marks)

**SAU (1 mark):** The **Secure Attribution Unit (SAU)** is a software-programmable, region-based address attributer inside the Cortex-M33 CPU. The secure boot code configures SAU regions at start-up, partitioning the 4 GB address space into Secure, Non-Secure, or NSC regions. Typical SoC has 4 to 8 SAU regions.

**IDAU (1 mark):** The **Implementation Defined Attribution Unit (IDAU)** is a vendor-implemented, fixed-in-hardware address decoder. The chip vendor hard-codes certain address ranges as Secure, Non-Secure, or NSC. The IDAU's decisions are immutable — no software can override them.

**Priority (1 mark):** The IDAU is given higher priority than the SAU to enforce the principle of **fail-secure**. Even if the secure firmware is buggy, absent, or has been corrupted, the vendor-defined Secure regions (e.g., boot ROM, secure peripherals, key storage) remain Secure. The SAU can only attribute regions that the IDAU marks as "escape". This guarantees a hardware root-of-trust that software cannot defeat.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 scheme End Semester Examination pattern, each Part B question is 14 marks. Below **Question A** and **Question B** are the two alternatives of a Module-4 internal choice.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Alternative 1

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Module 4 Choice Question)]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **TrustZone-M architecture** of an ARM Cortex-M33 microcontroller. Explain the role of the **NS bit**, the **SAU**, the **IDAU**, and the **MPU** in the security attribution of a memory address. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **Secure Gateway (SG) mechanism** used for invoking secure functions from the Non-Secure world. Describe what happens in the stack and registers when a Non-Secure caller invokes a secure function via a **veneer function**. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Block diagram (2 marks):** Draw a block diagram showing:
- **CPU core (Cortex-M33)** at the top
- **Bus matrix (AHB5)** as the central interconnect
- **Code flash, SRAM, peripherals, and System Control Space** as AHB slaves
- **SAU, IDAU, and two MPU banks (Secure MPU, Non-Secure MPU)** as configuration units feeding the bus matrix
- **NSC region** highlighted as the bridge between worlds

(Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the full diagram.)

**NS bit explanation (2 marks):**
- Every AHB5 transaction carries an HNONSEC sideband signal.
- 0 = Secure, 1 = Non-Secure.
- Peripherals and memory controllers check this bit and either accept the transaction or return a SecureFault.

**SAU explanation (1 mark):** Software-programmable; configured at secure boot. Defines additional Secure/NS/NSC regions outside the IDAU fixed ranges. Typically 4–8 regions.

**IDAU explanation (1 mark):** Vendor-hardcoded; cannot be modified. Guarantees fail-secure.

**MPU explanation (1 mark):** Two banked MPUs (one per world). Enforces RWX and privileged/unprivileged access *within* the world. Does not change security attribution.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Secure Gateway concept (1 mark):** The Secure Gateway (`SG`) is a 32-bit special instruction. It is the **only legal mechanism** for the Non-Secure world to call a Secure function. It can only execute from NSC memory.

**Veneer function (2 marks):**
- The linker generates a small "veneer" function inside NSC memory.
- The Non-Secure caller invokes the veneer via `BLXNS` (or `BLX` if no FPU is involved).
- The first instruction of the veneer is `SG`.

**Step-by-step world switch (3 marks):**

1. **NS world calls veneer**: `BLXNS` with LR set to the return address.
2. **CPU fetches the SG opcode** from NSC memory. If the memory is not NSC → SecureFault.
3. **Hardware pushes** the saved return PC and xPSR to the **Secure stack** (MSP_secure).
4. **Security state transitions** to Secure. SP is switched to MSP_secure.
5. **Secure function body executes** with full privileges.
6. **Return**: Secure function does `BX LR`. The LR contains the special **FNCRETURN** marker (0xFEFF_FFxx). The CPU recognises this and switches back to Non-Secure state, restoring the original SP.

**Why SG and not direct BX (1 mark):** Direct BX to a Secure address from NS would not bank registers/stack. SG forces the world switch and routing of stack to Secure world — preventing NS code from jumping arbitrarily into the middle of a Secure function.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative 2

**[KTU University Exam – December 2023 (Module 4 Choice Question)]**

**(a)** Discuss the **interrupt handling model** in TrustZone-M. How does the **NVIC_ITNS** register determine whether an interrupt is serviced by the Secure or Non-Secure world? Explain what happens when a Secure interrupt is taken while the CPU is in the Non-Secure world. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **priority cascade** for resolving the effective security attribution of a memory address when both SAU and IDAU regions overlap. Provide a numerical example. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Banked NVIC (2 marks):** The Cortex-M33 NVIC is **banked** — there is one set of NVIC registers for the Secure world and another for the Non-Secure world. A Secure interrupt is invisible to the Non-Secure NVIC, and vice versa.

**ITNS register (2 marks):** `NVIC_ITNS[i]` is a 1-bit register per interrupt source. If `ITNS[i] = 0`, interrupt *i* is **Secure-targeted**. If `ITNS[i] = 1`, interrupt *i* is **Non-Secure-targeted**. The default after reset for most interrupts is Secure (it=0), so the secure firmware retains control.

**Secure interrupt while CPU in NS (3 marks):**

1. A peripheral asserts an IRQ line. The NVIC checks the ITNS bit to determine target.
2. The CPU is in NS, but the interrupt is Secure-targeted. The hardware automatically performs a **world switch on exception entry**.
3. **Lazy stacking** is used: only a small sentinel frame is pushed; the full frame is deferred.
4. The CPU enters Handler mode in the **Secure world**, and reads the **Secure** vector from the **Secure Vector Table** (located via the banked VTOR — `SCB_VTOR_S` for Secure and `SCB_VTOR_NS` for Non-Secure).
5. The Secure ISR executes, services the peripheral, and on return, the world switches back to NS.

**Tail chaining across worlds (bonus 1 mark):** Supported and deterministic. Latency is the same as same-world tail chaining.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Priority cascade concept (2 marks):**

$$
A_{\text{attr}} = f(\text{IDAU}, \text{SAU}, \text{MPU})
$$

The order of precedence is **IDAU > SAU > MPU**. This means: if the IDAU says "Secure", the address is Secure, period. If the IDAU says "NS" or "NSC", that also sticks. The SAU can only attribute regions that the IDAU marks as "escape". The MPU can only add access permission restrictions *within* the world.

**Diagram (2 marks):** Use the flowchart from Section 4.3 of these notes (the Mermaid diagram showing IDAU → SAU → MPU cascade).

**Numerical example (3 marks):**

| Address | IDAU | SAU | Effective |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0x0000_0000 | Secure | not configured | **Secure** |
| 0x1000_0000 | NS | not configured | **NS** |
| 0x2000_0000 | escape | Secure (Region 0) | **Secure** |
| 0x2002_0000 | escape | NS (Region 1) | **NS** |
| 0x3000_0000 | NSC | not configured | **NSC** |
| 0x5000_0000 | escape | not configured, default = NS | **NS** |

**Worked explanation (1 mark):** Address 0x2000_0000 — IDAU returns "escape" because it is general-purpose SRAM and not vendor-fixed. SAU Region 0 covers 0x2000_0000–0x2001_FFFF and is configured as Secure. Therefore the effective attribute is **Secure**. The MPU can now further restrict access within the Secure world (e.g., allow only privileged Secure code to read).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks**
>
> 1. **Conflating TrustZone (A-profile) and TrustZone-M (M-profile):** Many students write about EL3 monitor, hypervisor mode, and S-EL2, which belong to Cortex-A. In PBCST504, you are expected to discuss **Cortex-M33 / Cortex-M55 with TrustZone-M**, which is single-core, has no hypervisor, and uses **lazy stacking**.
> 2. **Forgetting the IDAU-vs-SAU priority:** A common error is to say "the SAU always wins" or to describe the SAU as the primary attributer. **No — the IDAU wins first.**
> 3. **Saying the MPU can change security attribution:** The MPU is per-world and can only add RWX/privilege restrictions. It **cannot** promote a Non-Secure address to Secure.
> 4. **Skipping the lazy stacking explanation:** In Part B answers, students often say "world switch happens" without explaining the *mechanism* (lazy stacking, sentinel frame, MSP_secure push). This costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Confusing NSC and Secure:** A NSC region is **readable** by Non-Secure code (it contains veneers), but is *not* Secure in the full sense — only the SG instruction inside it can transition worlds.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention ITNS:** In interrupt questions, students often describe the banked NVIC but forget to introduce the `NVIC_ITNS` register, which is the *control* mechanism for interrupt security targeting.
> 7. **Using “M-profile TrustZone” in a vague manner:** Be specific — say "ARMv8-M TrustZone" and give the chip family (e.g., Cortex-M33, Cortex-M55, Cortex-M85).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **TrustZone-M is a hardware-enforced security extension for ARM Cortex-M33 / M55 / M85 / M85 microcontrollers** (ARMv8-M architecture).
- **Two worlds**: Secure and Non-Secure. Code in one cannot see resources of the other without an explicit, hardware-mediated channel.
- **The NS bit** is the single attribute that propagates on every AHB5/AXI5 bus transaction. It is the *only* signal the peripherals need to enforce isolation.
- **IDAU = vendor-fixed, highest priority.** It guarantees fail-secure behaviour.
- **SAU = software-programmable, next priority.** Configured at secure boot.
- **MPU = lowest priority, banked per world.** Adds RWX/privilege restrictions.
- **NSC (Non-Secure Callable) regions** are the only legal entry from Non-Secure to Secure. They must contain the `SG` instruction.
- **Secure Gateway (SG)** is the 32-bit instruction that performs the world switch. It is only valid in NSC.
- **BLXNS / BLX** is used by NS code to call into NSC. `BX LR` with FNCRETURN marker is used by Secure code to return to NS.
- **Stacks are banked:** MSP_secure, MSP_ns, PSP_secure, PSP_ns — four separate stack pointers.
- **NVIC is banked:** Secure and Non-Secure worlds have independent NVIC views. `NVIC_ITNS[i]` controls whether interrupt *i* is Secure or Non-Secure targeted.
- **Lazy stacking** keeps world-switch latency low (~12 cycles for interrupt, ~4 for SG call).
- **SAU regions** are limited (4–8 typical). Plan carefully.
- **The default boot state is Secure** so the boot code can configure the rest of the system safely.
- **TrustZone-M cost:** ~15–25% more die area; a *huge* improvement in security against software-only attacks.
- **Common exam keywords to know:** NS bit, IDAU, SAU, MPU, NSC, SG, FNCRETURN, lazy stacking, banked NVIC, ITNS, AHB5, secure gateway, veneer, root-of-trust, fail-secure.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Secure and Non-Secure Worlds: Configuration and Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Secure and Non-Secure Worlds: Configuration and Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **TrustZone — Secure & Non-Secure Worlds**
> In ARM architecture (Cortex-A and Cortex-M class processors), **TrustZone** is a System-on-Chip (SoC) and CPU level **hardware-enforced security extension** that logically partitions all system resources (CPU, memory, peripherals, buses) into **two parallel execution environments**: the **Secure World** (privileged, trusted firmware/TEE) and the **Non-Secure World** (Normal World — untrusted, rich OS like Linux/Android). The partition is enforced by an additional control signal called the **NS-bit (Non-Secure bit)** which is propagated alongside every bus transaction, address, and peripheral access on the AMBA AXI/AHB-AP infrastructure.

The two worlds are **mutually exclusive at any instant** — the processor core is physically in **exactly one** world at a time. Transition between them is **only** allowed via the **Secure Monitor Call (SMC)** instruction under tightly controlled conditions defined by the **Secure Configuration Register (SCR)** of CP15 system control coprocessor.

### 1.1.1 Key Terminology Used in KTU 2024 Scheme

| Term | Full Form | Meaning |
|------|-----------|---------|
| TEE | Trusted Execution Environment | Software running in Secure World |
| REE | Rich Execution Environment | Software running in Non-Secure World |
| NS-bit | Non-Secure Bit | Tag signal propagated on the bus |
| SCR | Secure Configuration Register | CP15 c1 register governing security state |
| SMC | Secure Monitor Call | Instruction to switch worlds |
| TZASC | TrustZone Address Space Controller | DRAM security firewall (Cortex-A) |
| TZMA | TrustZone Memory Adapter | SRAM partitioning (Cortex-M) |
| TZPC | TrustZone Protection Controller | Peripheral security gating |
| MPC | Memory Protection Controller | Generic name for TZASC/TZMA |
| BLX / BX | Branch with Link and eXchange | Used to enter Monitor mode |
| EA | External Abort | Routed based on SCR.EA bit |
| FIQ | Fast Interrupt Request | Routed based on SCR.FIQ bit |
| IRQ | Interrupt Request | Routed based on SCR.IRQ bit |
| HCE | Hypervisor Call Enable | Controls HVC trapping |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Bank Vault Inside a Shopping Mall**
> Imagine a massive **Shopping Mall** (your SoC) where the regular public area is the **Non-Secure World** — anyone can walk in, browse, and shop (your normal apps, OS, games). Hidden behind a heavily guarded, bullet-proof steel door inside this mall is a **Bank Vault** (the **Secure World**) containing cash, gold, and confidential data.
>
> - The **vault door** is the **SMC instruction** — the only public key to enter the vault.
> - Every **teller window, security camera, and door inside** has a small **red/green sticker** (the **NS-bit**) — green means "open to public," red means "vault staff only."
> - The **vault manager's rulebook** is the **SCR (Secure Configuration Register)** — it dictates which interrupts, aborts, and exceptions are answered by the vault or the mall.
> - The **public address system** is the **TZASC/TZMA** — it tells every corridor and storage room whether it's public area (Non-Secure accessible) or restricted (Secure-only).
> - When you want to withdraw cash (use a secret key), you go to the **teller (Monitor Mode)** who verifies your request and either lets you in or sends you back to the mall.
>
> This is **exactly** how TrustZone works: hardware-enforced, state-tagged, with strict world-switching controlled by a single privileged monitor.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Number of worlds:** **2** (Secure, Non-Secure) — strictly, no more.
- **NS-bit width:** **1 bit** added to every relevant bus signal.
- **TZASC max regions (typical ARM IP):** **8 to 16** independently configurable secure regions.
- **TZMA max regions (Cortex-M3/M4):** **up to 4** secure SRAM regions.
- **SMC instruction encoding:** `1110 0111 0111 1111` (T32) / `cond 0001 0110 1111` (A32).
- **Monitor mode privilege level:** **PL1 (privileged)** — never accessible from user (PL0) code.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a single NS-bit and not multiple security levels?**
> Hardware simplicity is the cornerstone of TrustZone. A single bit allows the SoC to add minimal logic to every bus master/slave while giving the operating system enough flexibility to build software hierarchies (e.g., Trusty OS, OP-TEE, Samsung KNOX) on top of the two-state foundation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The NS-Bit — Heart of TrustZone

The **Non-Secure (NS) bit** is the single most important signal in TrustZone. It is essentially an additional state line carried alongside every read/write channel, address channel, and sideband signal in the AMBA fabric.

### 2.1.1 Where the NS-bit Lives

- **On the CPU core (Cortex-A):** Held in CPSR/SCR as a derived signal.
- **On AXI/ACE bus:** `AWPROT[1]`, `ARPROT[1]` carry the NS attribute for write/read address channels.
- **On AHB-AP (Cortex-M):** `hnonsec` signal line.
- **In memory controller:** Each line in cache or DRAM may have a stored NS attribute.
- **In MMU page tables:** The **NS (Non-Secure) bit** in the **first-level descriptor** and **second-level descriptor** in the L1 and L2 translation tables.

### 2.1.2 Bus Protocol Encoding (AXI)

For the **ARPROT** and **AWPROT** signals, the NS-bit is encoded as:

| ARPROT[1] / AWPROT[1] | Meaning |
|------------------------|---------|
| **0** | **Secure access** (privileged or normal, in secure world) |
| **1** | **Non-Secure access** |

The other PROT bits (`[0]` for unprivileged/privileged, `[2]` for instruction/data) remain functionally similar to non-TrustZone systems.

## 2.2 The Secure Configuration Register (SCR) — CP15 c1

This is the **most critical configuration register** for managing world behavior.

### 2.2.1 SCR Bit Layout (Cortex-A)

| Bits | Name | Reset | Function |
|------|------|-------|----------|
| **[0]** | **NS** | **0** | **0 = Secure state, 1 = Non-secure state (read-only indication of current world)** |
| [1] | IRQ | 0 | 0 = IRQ taken in current world; 1 = IRQ routed to Monitor |
| [2] | FIQ | 0 | 0 = FIQ taken in current world; 1 = FIQ routed to Monitor |
| [3] | EA | 0 | 0 = External abort handled in current world; 1 = Abort to Monitor |
| [4] | reserved | 0 | — |
| [5] | reserved | 0 | — |
| [6] | reserved | 0 | — |
| [7] | SCD | 0 | SMC Disable: 1 = disable SMC instruction in Non-secure |
| [8] | HCE | 0 | HVC enable: 1 = HVC trapped to Hyp, 0 = treated as UNDEF |
| [9] | SIF | 0 | Secure Instruction Fetch: 0 = can fetch NS memory in S, 1 = cannot |
| [10] | reserved | 0 | — |
| [11] | nET | 0 | Not Early Termination |
| [12] | AW | 0 | A32 Warning bit |
| [13] | FW | 0 | F bit writable from Non-secure |
| [14] | nMD | 0 | Not Monitor Disable |
| [15] | EA_3 | 0 | 32-bit External Abort behavior |
| [16:31] | reserved | 0 | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most-examined bits in KTU 2024:**
> - **NS (bit 0)** — read-only world indicator.
> - **IRQ, FIQ, EA (bits 1, 2, 3)** — exception routing control.
> - **SCD (bit 7)** — locks the SMC door.
> - **nET (bit 11)** — enables early termination on bus errors.

## 2.3 World Transition Mechanics — The SMC Instruction

The **Secure Monitor Call (SMC)** is the **only** legal way to switch worlds from a CPU running in PL1 (privileged, S or NS) or PL0 in secure user mode.

### 2.3.1 Transition State Diagram

1. **NS → S (Non-Secure to Secure):**
   - Non-secure privileged code issues `SMC #imm`.
   - CPU enters **Monitor mode** (PL1, secure, AArch32/AArch64).
   - Monitor's handler validates the request via the **SMC Function ID** (bits [15:0] of `SMC #imm`).
   - If valid, Monitor sets NS-bit = 0 and jumps to the secure handler.
   - If invalid, Monitor returns to Non-secure with a return code.

2. **S → NS (Secure to Non-Secure):**
   - Secure code calls Monitor via a software convention (often a `SMC` with a designated "return to NS" Function ID).
   - Monitor sets NS-bit = 1, restores the saved non-secure context (banked registers), and performs an `ERET`-like sequence (`MOVS PC, lr` to non-secure PC).

### 2.3.2 Register Banking

The CPU provides **separate physical registers** for the two worlds in the following banked groups:

| Register Group | Banked? | Notes |
|----------------|---------|-------|
| **R8–R12 (FIQ state)** | **Yes** (Secure vs. Non-Secure) | Allows secure FIQ handler to use them without saving |
| **R13 (SP)** | **Yes** (SP_S, SP_NS, SP_hyp) | Different stack pointers |
| **R14 (LR)** | **Yes** (LR_S, LR_NS, LR_hyp) | Different return addresses |
| **SPSR** | **Yes** (SPSR_S, SPSR_NS, etc.) | Different saved PSRs |
| **CPSR** | **No** (single) | Contains A/I/F bits, mode bits |
| **R0–R7** | **Not banked** | Software must save/restore during SMC |

> [!NOTE]
> **KNOX, OP-TEE and Trusty OS** all rely on this banked register set to keep the secure and non-secure contexts in hardware without expensive software context switches for the high-use registers.

## 2.4 Memory Partitioning Components

### 2.4.1 TrustZone Address Space Controller (TZASC) — Cortex-A

The TZASC is a **DRAM firewall** placed between the bus masters and the DDR controller. It allows the secure firmware to define **N configurable regions** of DRAM as Secure or Non-Secure, with separate R/W permissions for each world.

**Region Configuration (typical):**
- **Base Address** (page-aligned).
- **Size** (power-of-two from 4 KB up to 4 GB).
- **Secure Read Access (SRA)** — 0 or 1.
- **Secure Write Access (SWA)** — 0 or 1.
- **Non-secure Read Access (NSRA)** — 0 or 1.
- **Non-secure Write Access (NSWA)** — 0 or 1.
- **Sub-region disable bits** (8 sub-regions inside each region for finer control).

### 2.4.2 TrustZone Memory Adapter (TZMA) — Cortex-M3/M4

The TZMA is a **lightweight SRAM splitter** used in microcontrollers. It allows the first **N KB** of SRAM to be Secure and the rest to be Non-Secure.

**Size Encoding (TZMA, 4 KB granularity):**
- The `SIZE` register holds a value `S` such that:
- **Secure SRAM size** $= (S + 1) \times 4$ KB.
- **Maximum secure region** $= 4$ MB (when S = 0x3FF, 10 bits).

### 2.4.3 TrustZone Protection Controller (TZPC)

The **TZPC** is used to set the **security status of peripheral devices**. It exposes a small set of registers (`TZPCDECPROT0..n`) where each bit determines whether peripheral `n` is:
- **0 → Always Secure** (non-secure accesses denied).
- **1 → Non-Secure accessible**.

Used for peripherals like UART, SPI, timers, GPIO — critical for things like secure keypad, fingerprint reader, secure element communication.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | **NS-bit propagation** | Every AXI ARPROT[1], AWPROT[1] = NS attribute | 1 bit |
| 2 | **SCR.NS readout** | `SCR.NS == 0` → Secure world; `SCR.NS == 1` → Non-secure | Boolean |
| 3 | **TZMA secure size** | $S_{\text{secure}} = (S + 1) \times 4$ KB | Bytes |
| 4 | **TZMA size field width** | $S$ is 10-bit → max $4$ MB secure | 10 bits |
| 5 | **TZASC sub-region count** | 8 sub-regions per region | Sub-region size = Region size $\div$ 8 |
| 6 | **Cache line NS attribute** | Stored in L1/L2 tag (per line) | 1 bit per cache line |
| 7 | **World switch overhead** | $\approx 30$–$200$ CPU cycles (context save) | Cycles |
| 8 | **Banked registers** | R8–R14, SPSR (per world) | 8 registers per world |
| 9 | **SMC instruction** | Only legal PL1 (and secure PL0) → Monitor | Single opcode |
| 10 | **SCR exception routing** | IRQ/FIQ/EA = 0 → in-world; = 1 → Monitor | Boolean per bit |
| 11 | **Page table NS bit** | L1 descriptor bit [3], L2 descriptor bit [3] | For TTBR0/TTBR1 |
| 12 | **TZASC region count** | 8 (TZASC-380) or 16 (TZASC-380T) | Configurable |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** Use `\vert` in any inline expressions inside tables to avoid Markdown table breakage. Example: `SCR.NS \vert = 0 → Secure`.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | How TrustZone Secure World Is Used |
|--------------------|------------------------------------|
| **Mobile Payments** | Apple Pay, Samsung Pay, Google Pay use Secure World to hold card keys, perform cryptographic transactions. |
| **DRM (Digital Rights Management)** | Widevine L1 in Android stores decrypted video keys in Secure World memory. |
| **Mobile Banking** | Banks use TEE for PIN entry, biometric matching, key storage. |
| **Firmware Update Verification** | Secure bootloader in Secure World validates signed images before allowing NS boot. |
| **IoT Edge Devices** | Cortex-M33/M55 with TrustZone-M isolate security-critical code from main application. |
| **Automotive** | Secure world hosts V2X key handling, ADAS sensor trust establishment. |
| **Anti-cheat (Gaming)** | Game protection systems (e.g., on consoles) check memory integrity from Secure World. |
| **Biometrics** | Fingerprint template storage and matching inside Secure World. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — TZMA Secure SRAM Size Calculation

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A Cortex-M4 microcontroller with TZMA has its `TZMA_SIZE` register loaded with the value `0x0A5`. Calculate the size of the Secure SRAM region and the address of the boundary between Secure and Non-Secure SRAM.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Identify the formula.**
> The TZMA size formula is:
> $$S_{\text{secure}} = (S + 1) \times 4 \text{ KB}$$
> where $S$ is the 10-bit value held in the size register.

> **Step 2 — Read the register value.**
> The given value is $S = \text{0x0A5}$.
> Converting hexadecimal to decimal:
> $$S = 0 \times 256 + 10 \times 16 + 5 = 160 + 5 = 165$$

> **Step 3 — Apply the formula.**
> $$S_{\text{secure}} = (165 + 1) \times 4 \text{ KB}$$
> $$S_{\text{secure}} = 166 \times 4 \text{ KB}$$
> $$S_{\text{secure}} = 664 \text{ KB}$$

> **Step 4 — Convert to bytes.**
> $$S_{\text{secure}} = 664 \times 1024 = 679{,}936 \text{ bytes}$$
> $$\boxed{S_{\text{secure}} = 664 \text{ KB} = 679{,}936 \text{ bytes}}$$

> **Step 5 — Determine the boundary address (assuming SRAM base = 0x2000_0000).**
> $$A_{\text{boundary}} = 0x2000\_0000 + 664 \text{ KB}$$
> $$A_{\text{boundary}} = 0x2000\_0000 + 0x000A\_6000$$
> $$A_{\text{boundary}} = 0x200A\_6000$$

> **Step 6 — Interpretation.**
> - SRAM from **0x2000_0000 to 0x200A_5FFF** → **Secure** (accessible only from Secure world code).
> - SRAM from **0x200A_6000 to end of SRAM** → **Non-Secure**.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — TZASC Sub-Region Disable Calculation

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

A TZASC region is configured with **base = 0x4000_0000** and **size = 16 MB**. Sub-region disable register holds `0b1010_0011`. Find which sub-regions are **enabled (accessible)** and the address ranges of the **disabled** sub-regions.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

> **Step 1 — Compute sub-region size.**
> Total region size = $16$ MB, divided into $8$ sub-regions:
> $$S_{\text{sub}} = \frac{16 \text{ MB}}{8} = 2 \text{ MB}$$

> **Step 2 — Build the sub-region address table.**
> Starting from base 0x4000_0000:
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \text{Sub 0} &: 0\text{x}4000\_0000 \text{ to } 0\text{x}401F\_FFFF \\
> \text{Sub 1} &: 0\text{x}4020\_0000 \text{ to } 0\text{x}403F\_FFFF \\
> \text{Sub 2} &: 0\text{x}4040\_0000 \text{ to } 0\text{x}405F\_FFFF \\
> \text{Sub 3} &: 0\text{x}4060\_0000 \text{ to } 0\text{x}407F\_FFFF \\
> \text{Sub 4} &: 0\text{x}4080\_0000 \text{ to } 0\text{x}409F\_FFFF \\
> \text{Sub 5} &: 0\text{x}40A0\_0000 \text{ to } 0\text{x}40BF\_FFFF \\
> \text{Sub 6} &: 0\text{x}40C0\_0000 \text{ to } 0\text{x}40DF\_FFFF \\
> \text{Sub 7} &: 0\text{x}40E0\_0000 \text{ to } 0\text{x}40FF\_FFFF
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 3 — Decode the sub-region disable bits.**
> The given bit pattern is `0b1010_0011`. Reading from **bit 0 (LSB) = Sub 0**:
> $$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
> \hline
> \text{Bit} & 7 & 6 & 5 & 4 & 3 & 2 & 1 & 0 \\
> \hline
> \text{Value} & 1 & 0 & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 1 \\
> \text{Sub} & 7 & 6 & 5 & 4 & 3 & 2 & 1 & 0 \\
> \hline
> \end{array}$$
> Convention: **1 = Disabled**, **0 = Enabled**.

> **Step 4 — Identify the disabled sub-regions.**
> Bits set to 1: **Sub 0, Sub 1, Sub 5, Sub 7** are **disabled**.

> **Step 5 — List disabled address ranges.**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \text{Disabled Sub 0} &: 0\text{x}4000\_0000 \text{–} 0\text{x}401F\_FFFF \\
> \text{Disabled Sub 1} &: 0\text{x}4020\_0000 \text{–} 0\text{x}403F\_FFFF \\
> \text{Disabled Sub 5} &: 0\text{x}40A0\_0000 \text{–} 0\text{x}40BF\_FFFF \\
> \text{Disabled Sub 7} &: 0\text{x}40E0\_0000 \text{–} 0\text{x}40FF\_FFFF
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 6 — Total disabled memory.**
> $$M_{\text{disabled}} = 4 \times 2 \text{ MB} = 8 \text{ MB}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3 — SCR Configuration for Monitor-Routed FIQ

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

Configure the SCR so that:
- The processor is currently in the **Secure world**.
- The **FIQ** is **routed to the Monitor** (not handled in Secure world).
- The **IRQ** remains **handled in the Secure world**.
- The **SMC instruction is disabled** in the Non-Secure world.
- **HVC** is **disabled** (treated as UNDEF).
- **SIF (Secure Instruction Fetch) is enabled** (Secure code cannot fetch from NS memory).

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

> **Step 1 — Build the target bit value for SCR.**
> Looking at the bit table from Section 2.2.1:
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \text{Bit 0 (NS)} &= 0 \quad \text{(read-only, Secure world)} \\
> \text{Bit 1 (IRQ)} &= 0 \quad \text{(IRQ in current world)} \\
> \text{Bit 2 (FIQ)} &= 1 \quad \text{(FIQ routed to Monitor)} \\
> \text{Bit 7 (SCD)} &= 1 \quad \text{(SMC disabled in NS)} \\
> \text{Bit 8 (HCE)} &= 0 \quad \text{(HVC disabled)} \\
> \text{Bit 9 (SIF)} &= 1 \quad \text{(Secure cannot fetch NS)}
> \end{aligned}$$

> **Step 2 — Compute the hex value.**
> $$\text{SCR} = (1 \ll 9) \mid (1 \ll 7) \mid (1 \ll 2)$$
> $$\text{SCR} = 0\text{x}200 \mid 0\text{x}080 \mid 0\text{x}004$$
> $$\text{SCR} = 0\text{x}284$$

> **Step 3 — Verify by binary layout.**
> $$\text{SCR} = \underbrace{0010}_{\text{bits 9–6}} \underbrace{1000}_{\text{bits 5–2}} \underbrace{0100}_{\text{bits 1–0}} = 0\text{x}284$$
> Bit positions confirmed.

> **Step 4 — Write the CP15 instruction to set SCR.**
> This is the A32 syntax to write to SCR:
> `MCR p15, 0, r0, c1, c1, 0`
> where r0 contains **0x284**.

> **Step 5 — Read-back verification.**
> To confirm, read SCR back:
> `MRC p15, 0, r1, c1, c1, 0` → r1 should equal 0x284 (with bit 0 forced to 0 since we are secure).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Bit-by-Bit Summary

| SCR Bit | Symbol | After Config (0x284) | Effect |
|---------|--------|----------------------|--------|
| 0 | NS | 0 (RO) | We are in Secure |
| 1 | IRQ | 0 | IRQ handled in Secure |
| 2 | FIQ | **1** | FIQ → Monitor |
| 3 | EA | 0 | Aborts in Secure |
| 4–6 | reserved | 0 | — |
| 7 | SCD | **1** | SMC disabled in NS |
| 8 | HCE | 0 | HVC → UNDEF |
| 9 | SIF | **1** | No NS fetch in S |

```mermaid
    appB --> rtosNS
    rtosNS -->|SMC instruction| mon
    secureApp --> teeOS

    classDef nonsecure fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
```

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — SMC Instruction Flow (Non-Secure → Secure)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant NSApp as NS Application
    participant CPU as ARM Core
    participant Mon as Monitor Mode
    participant SecOS as Secure OS TEE
    participant SecApp as Secure Service

    NSApp->>CPU: Execute SMC function ID 0x1234
    Note over CPU: CPU enters Monitor Mode<br/>Saves NS context to banked regs
    CPU->>Mon: Branch to Monitor vector
    Mon->>Mon: Validate SMC function ID
    alt Valid SMC
        Mon->>SecOS: Set NS bit = 0, branch to secure handler
        SecOS->>SecApp: Invoke secure service
        SecApp->>SecOS: Return result
        SecOS->>Mon: SMC return request
        Mon->>CPU: Restore NS context from banked regs
        CPU->>NSApp: Return to NS with result
    else Invalid SMC
        Mon->>CPU: Restore NS context immediately
        CPU->>NSApp: Return error code
    end
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is the role of the NS-bit in ARM TrustZone architecture?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **NS-bit (Non-Secure bit) is a single additional control signal that tags every memory bus transaction in a TrustZone-enabled SoC as either Secure (NS=0) or Non-Secure (NS=1).**

The NS-bit plays the following critical roles:
1. **Bus propagation:** It is carried on `AWPROT[1]` and `ARPROT[1]` of the AXI bus, on `hnonsec` of AHB-AP, and on cache tag RAM and TLBs.
2. **World identification:** Hardware uses it to enforce access control — a Non-Secure master cannot access Secure-marked memory.
3. **Peripheral gating:** TZPC uses it to block non-secure access to secure peripherals.
4. **Cache coherency:** Stored per cache line, allowing the same physical memory to be cached separately in each world.
5. **Single-bit simplicity:** It allows a two-state hardware model with minimal logic overhead.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for usage, 1 for AXI example)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: List the three TrustZone Memory Protection Controllers (MPCs) and state one difference between TZASC and TZMA.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **The three TrustZone MPCs are TZASC, TZMA, and TZPC.**

| Controller | Used For | Typical System |
|------------|----------|----------------|
| **TZASC** | DRAM (off-chip DDR) security | Cortex-A application processors |
| **TZMA** | On-chip SRAM security | Cortex-M microcontrollers |
| **TZPC** | Peripheral security | Both A and M class |

**One key difference between TZASC and TZMA:**

- **TZASC** allows **multiple independent secure regions** with **fine-grained, per-region R/W permissions** for both Secure and Non-Secure worlds, with **8 sub-regions per region** for sub-region disabling. Typical implementations support 8–16 regions.
- **TZMA** is a **simpler splitter** that divides on-chip SRAM into **one Secure block at the start** and **one Non-Secure block at the end**, with the boundary size encoded in a single SIZE field (10-bit, 4 KB granularity). It does not support per-region permissions — it is a single split.

**(3 marks: 1 for naming the 3 controllers, 1 for TZASC description, 1 for TZMA description)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Only ONE of the two choices A or B should be attempted.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — Choose this OR Question B

**Q (A): With the help of neat diagrams, explain the ARM TrustZone architecture showing Secure and Non-Secure worlds, the role of the NS-bit, and the Secure Monitor Call (SMC) instruction for world switching. Also explain the SCR (Secure Configuration Register) with its important bits. (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

#### Part (a) — TrustZone Architecture & SMC (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 (1 mark) — TrustZone Concept:**
> TrustZone is a hardware security extension in ARM processors that partitions system resources into two worlds: **Secure World** (trusted firmware, TEE) and **Non-Secure World** (rich OS like Linux). Partitioning is enforced by a single control bit — the **NS-bit**.

> **Step 2 (2 marks) — Architecture diagram (refer to Section 4.1 & 4.2):**
> The system has:
> - CPU core with NS-bit in its CPSR (via SCR.NS).
> - Bus fabric (AXI/AHB-AP) carrying NS-bit on `PROT[1]` / `hnonsec`.
> - Three protection controllers: **TZASC** (DRAM), **TZMA** (SRAM), **TZPC** (peripherals).
> - Cache and TLB with NS attribute stored per line / descriptor.

> **Step 3 (2 marks) — SMC for world switching:**
> The **SMC** instruction is the only legal way to switch worlds. From Non-Secure, it traps to **Monitor mode** (PL1, Secure), which validates the **SMC function ID** (`SMC #imm16`), saves Non-Secure context in banked registers, and either:
> - Calls the secure handler (if valid) and returns to Non-Secure afterwards.
> - Or returns immediately to Non-Secure with an error code.

> **Step 4 (1 mark) — Register banking:**
> R8–R12, R13 (SP), R14 (LR), and SPSR are **banked** between worlds. R0–R7 and CPSR are shared (saves time in R0–R7 not used by most SMC stubs).
>
> **Step 5 (1 mark) — Sequence diagram (refer to Section 4.5):**
> Show NS app → SMC → Monitor → Secure service → return via Monitor → NS app.

#### Part (b) — Secure Configuration Register (SCR) (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 (1 mark) — Definition:**
> The SCR is **CP15 c1 c1 0** register, accessible only from Secure PL1 (or Monitor). It controls the **current security state indication** and **exception routing** between worlds.

> **Step 2 (2 marks) — Important bits and their meanings:**
> | Bit | Name | Function |
> |-----|------|----------|
> | 0 | NS | **0 = Secure, 1 = Non-Secure (read-only world indicator)** |
> | 1 | IRQ | 0 → IRQ handled in current world; 1 → IRQ to Monitor |
> | 2 | FIQ | 0 → FIQ handled in current world; 1 → FIQ to Monitor |
> | 3 | EA | 0 → External abort in current world; 1 → to Monitor |
> | 7 | SCD | SMC Disable (1 = NS code cannot issue SMC) |
> | 9 | SIF | Secure Instruction Fetch (1 = S code cannot fetch from NS memory) |
> | 11 | nET | Not Early Termination (1 = wait full bus cycle on error) |
> | 13 | FW | F (FIQ disable) writable from NS world |
> | 14 | nMD | Monitor Disable (1 = Monitor mode disabled) |

> **Step 3 (2 marks) — Example configuration:**
> To **route FIQ to Monitor** while keeping IRQ in current world, with **SMC disabled in NS** and **SIF enabled**:
> $$\text{SCR} = (1 \ll 9) \mid (1 \ll 7) \mid (1 \ll 2) = 0\text{x}284$$
> Setting instruction (A32):
> `MCR p15, 0, r0, c1, c1, 0` with `r0 = 0x284`.

> **Step 4 (2 marks) — Significance:**
> - **NS** bit allows software to query the current world.
> - **IRQ/FIQ/EA** bits allow the secure firmware to **intercept critical interrupts** (e.g., to wipe keys on tamper).
> - **SCD** prevents non-secure code from spamming SMC requests to launch DoS on Monitor.
> - **SIF** prevents non-secure code from tricking secure code into executing non-secure instructions (return-oriented attacks).
> - **nMD** can permanently disable Monitor for ultra-secure bootloader modes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students confuse **SCR.NS** (read-only) with a **writable mode bit**. **You cannot write to SCR.NS** — the world is changed only by SMC, never by direct register write. Writing 1 to SCR.NS is **ignored**; the CPU stays in the current world. This is a common 1–2 mark loss in KTU exams.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q (B): Explain the TrustZone Memory Protection Controllers TZASC and TZMA in detail. Show how to compute the secure region size and sub-region disabling for a given configuration. (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

#### Part (a) — TZASC and TZMA Architectures (7 marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 (1 mark) — TZASC overview:**
> The **TrustZone Address Space Controller (TZASC)** is a **DRAM firewall** used in Cortex-A application processors. It sits between the AXI bus masters and the DDR controller and enforces per-region security and access permissions.

> **Step 2 (1 mark) — TZASC features:**
> - **8 to 16** configurable regions (varies by IP, e.g., TZASC-380).
> - Each region has: **base address**, **size (power-of-2, 4 KB to 4 GB)**, and **4 permission bits** (SRA, SWA, NSRA, NSWA).
> - **8 sub-regions** per region (with a sub-region disable register).
> - Region-based **address decode** — overlapping regions resolve by priority (lower region index wins).

> **Step 3 (1 mark) — TZMA overview:**
> The **TrustZone Memory Adapter (TZMA)** is a **simple on-chip SRAM splitter** used in Cortex-M3/M4 microcontrollers (e.g., NXP LPC43xx, STM32 L4+ with TrustZone). It splits SRAM into **one Secure region at low addresses** and **one Non-Secure region at high addresses**.

> **Step 4 (2 marks) — TZMA size encoding:**
> The TZMA holds a 10-bit SIZE register. The Secure region size is:
> $$S_{\text{secure}} = (S + 1) \times 4 \text{ KB}$$
> with $S$ being the register value. **Maximum secure region = 4 MB** (when $S = 1023$).

> **Step 5 (2 marks) — Comparison table:**
> | Feature | TZASC | TZMA |
> |---------|-------|------|
> | Target | DRAM (Cortex-A) | SRAM (Cortex-M) |
> | # of regions | 8 to 16 | 1 (split) |
> | Per-region R/W permissions | Yes (4 bits) | No (whole-block split) |
> | Sub-regions | 8 per region | None |
> | Region size granularity | 4 KB | 4 KB |
> | Maximum secure size | 4 GB per region | 4 MB total |
> | Use case | Mobile, application processor | IoT, MCU |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation of Secure Region & Sub-Region Disable (7 marks)

**Problem:** A TZMA has SIZE = 0x064. The SRAM base address is 0x2000_0000. Calculate the secure region size and boundary address. A TZASC region is configured at base 0x6000_0000 with size 32 MB and sub-region disable = 0b1101_0100. List the disabled sub-regions' address ranges.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

> **Step 1 (1 mark) — Convert SIZE to decimal:**
> $$S = 0\text{x}064 = 6 \times 16 + 4 = 100$$

> **Step 2 (1 mark) — Compute secure size:**
> $$S_{\text{secure}} = (100 + 1) \times 4 \text{ KB} = 101 \times 4 \text{ KB} = 404 \text{ KB} = 413{,}696 \text{ bytes}$$

> **Step 3 (1 mark) — Compute boundary address:**
> $$A_{\text{boundary}} = 0\text{x}2000\_0000 + 404 \text{ KB} = 0\text{x}2000\_0000 + 0\text{x}0006\_5000 = 0\text{x}2006\_5000$$

> **Step 4 (1 mark) — TZASC sub-region size:**
> $$S_{\text{sub}} = \frac{32 \text{ MB}}{8} = 4 \text{ MB}$$

> **Step 5 (1 mark) — Decode disable bits:**
> `0b1101_0100` (bit 7 to bit 0): bit 7=1, bit 6=1, bit 5=0, bit 4=1, bit 3=0, bit 2=1, bit 1=0, bit 0=0.
> Bits set: **Sub 2, Sub 4, Sub 6, Sub 7** are **disabled**.

> **Step 6 (2 marks) — List disabled addresses:**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \text{Sub 2} &: 0\text{x}6080\_0000 \text{–} 0\text{x}60BF\_FFFF \\
> \text{Sub 4} &: 0\text{x}6100\_0000 \text{–} 0\text{x}613F\_FFFF \\
> \text{Sub 6} &: 0\text{x}6180\_0000 \text{–} 0\text{x}61BF\_FFFF \\
> \text{Sub 7} &: 0\text{x}61C0\_0000 \text{–} 0\text{x}61FF\_FFFF
> \end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** A frequent error is **reversing the sub-region bit order**. Always map **bit 0 = Sub 0 (lowest address), bit 7 = Sub 7 (highest address)**. Reversing this costs full 7 marks. Another error is forgetting the "+1" in the TZMA size formula, which gives an off-by-4 KB boundary.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ⭐ **TrustZone = 2 worlds** (Secure + Non-Secure), enforced by **NS-bit**.
- ⭐ **NS-bit = 1 bit** on `AWPROT[1]`, `ARPROT[1]`, `hnonsec`, cache tags, TLB descriptors.
- ⭐ **SCR.NS (bit 0) is READ-ONLY** — world is changed only by **SMC**, not by direct write.
- ⭐ **SMC** is the **only** legal instruction to switch worlds; from PL1 (or secure PL0).
- ⭐ **Banked registers:** R8–R12, R13 (SP), R14 (LR), SPSR — separate for each world.
- ⭐ **Shared registers:** R0–R7 and CPSR — single physical instance.
- ⭐ **Monitor mode** is **PL1, Secure** — the gatekeeper between worlds.
- ⭐ **SCR key bits:** **NS[0], IRQ[1], FIQ[2], EA[3], SCD[7], HCE[8], SIF[9], nET[11], FW[13], nMD[14]**.
- ⭐ **TZMA size formula:** $S_{\text{secure}} = (S + 1) \times 4$ KB, max **4 MB** secure.
- ⭐ **TZASC** has **8–16 regions** with **8 sub-regions each** of size RegionSize/8.
- ⭐ **TZPC** controls peripheral security (1 bit per peripheral).
- ⭐ **SMC disable (SCD=1)** prevents NS code from issuing SMC — DoS protection.
- ⭐ **SIF=1** prevents Secure code from fetching Non-Secure instructions (prevents code injection).
- ⭐ **nMD=1** permanently disables Monitor — used in deepest secure boot modes.
- ⭐ **Page table NS bit** is in **L1 and L2 descriptors** (bit 3) for MMU-controlled partitioning.
- ⭐ **Cortex-A** uses TZASC for DRAM; **Cortex-M** uses TZMA for SRAM.
- ⭐ **Real-world users:** Apple Secure Enclave, Samsung KNOX, Android Widevine L1, OP-TEE, Trusty OS.
- ⭐ **Exception routing:** When SCR.IRQ/FIQ/EA = 1, that exception is **always** taken to Monitor regardless of current world.
- ⭐ **Cache coherence:** NS-bit stored per cache line allows the **same DRAM address** to have **two valid cache lines** — one secure, one non-secure.
- ⭐ **Transition overhead:** Approximately **30–200 cycles** depending on how many registers need saving.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Secure and Non-Secure Worlds: Configuration and Management**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implementing Trust Zone in STM32U575
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Implementing Trust Zone in STM32U575 # Module 4 — Implementing TrustZone in STM32U575 ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **TrustZone for Armv8-M (TZ-M)** is a hardware-enforced security extension integrated into the **Arm Cortex-M33** core (the heart of the **STM32U575**). It partitions the system into two physically isolated states: the **Secure World** (where all critical assets, firmware IP, and crypto keys reside) and the **Non-Secure World** (where the main application and RTOS run). The boundary is enforced by the **SAU (Security Attribution Unit)**, the **IDAU (Implementation Defined Attribution Unit)**, and the **MPU (Memory Protection Unit)**. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, TrustZone implementation in STM32U575 is taught as a **Hardware Root of Trust (HRoT)** mechanism that protects against software-level attacks, IP theft, and remote code injection in IoT edge devices. ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine a high-security government building: - The **Public Lobby** = **Non-Secure World** (anyone can enter, browse, ask questions). - The **Secure Office** = **Secure World** (classified files, restricted entry, keycard access). - The **Reception Window with a Bell** = **Non-Secure Callable (NSC) Region** (the only legal way to call into the secure office, with a special "Secure Gateway" bell). - The **Security Guard** = **SAU + IDAU** (decides which memory belongs to which world, prevents illegal entry). - The **One-Way Mirror** = **Hardware Bus Firewall** (data can flow out of secure world, but the non-secure world cannot peek back in). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU 2024 Highlight:** TrustZone is **NOT software-only**. It is a **hardware-level** isolation technique. Once a non-secure thread attempts to read secure memory, the CPU raises a **SecureFault** exception — the code never silently fails. ### 1.3 Why STM32U575 Specifically? The **STM32U575** belongs to ST's ultra-low-power **U5 series** and is one of the first Cortex-M33 MCUs that supports TrustZone-M with: - **8 SAU regions** (programmable). - Full **IDAU** support for fixed memory attribution. - **OTFDEC (On-The-Fly Decryption)** for encrypted external flash. - **Secure Hidden SSB** (Secure Storage Block) for OEM keys. - Operating voltage down to **1.71 V** with **< 1 µA** standby current. This makes it a textbook example for KTU Module 4 because it perfectly illustrates both the **conceptual** and the **practical** sides of TrustZone. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Pillars of TrustZone-M Attribution For any given memory address in the STM32U575, the security attribution is the **logical AND** of three sources: | Priority | Attribution Source | Type | Configurable? | Function | |----------|-------------------|------|---------------|----------| | 1 (Highest) | **IDAU** | Hardware-fixed (silicon-level) | No | Maps the **alias memory** of Secure SRAM/Flash to Non-Secure. Marks some regions as always Secure (e.g., option bytes). | | 2 | **SAU** | Programmable in firmware | Yes (8 regions) | The developer explicitly marks regions as S, NS, or NSC. | | 3 (Lowest) | **MPU** | Per-region access control | Yes | Refines read/write/execute permissions for the current security state. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The AND Rule:** A region is **Secure** only if **all three** sources agree it is Secure. If SAU says "NS" but IDAU says "S", the IDAU wins — the access is treated as **Secure**. ## 2.2 The Three Valid Memory Attributions A memory location can be marked with one of exactly three attributes: | Attribute | Symbol | Meaning | Allowed Access from NS? | Allowed Access from S? | |-----------|--------|---------|-------------------------|------------------------| | **Secure** | `S` | Trusted, protected region | **No** (SecureFault) | **Yes** (full R/W/X) | | **Non-Secure** | `NS` | World accessible to NS code | **Yes** | **Yes** | | **Non-Secure Callable** | `NSC` | The "entry window" into S | Only via **SG** instruction; otherwise SecureFault | **Yes** | ## 2.3 The Two Security States and Transitions ``` NS Code --[BL/BLX to NSC address + SG]--> S Code (entry function) S Code --[direct branch, no SG needed] --> S Code S Code --[B/BL to NS code via NS-VTOR] --> NS Code NS Code --[cannot call S code directly] --> SECURE FAULT ``` The state transition is **atomic** at the hardware level. The CPU automatically saves the security state on the stack as a single bit (the LSB of LR) when a branch with link is performed. ## 2.4 Key KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet Although TrustZone is mostly architectural, the following table is **exam-critical**: | Concept | Value / Formula | Unit / Note | |---------|----------------|-------------| | Number of SAU regions in STM32U575 | **8** | Programmable, max 8 | | SAU region size granularity | **32 bytes** | Smallest region = 32 B | | Secure stack pointer banks | **2** | MSP_S and MSP_NS (or PSP_NS) | | Vector table copies | **2** | Secure VTOR + Non-Secure VTOR | | NSC entry marker instruction | **SG** | 16-bit Thumb-2 opcode `0xE97F` | | TrustZone initial state on reset | **Secure** | Boot always begins in S state | | Option byte to disable TZ (TZDIS) | `0xC3F7` (FLASH_OPTR) | Setting this bit **destroys** TZ capability | | Cortex-M33 pipeline stages | **2** | Fetch + Decode/Execute | | TrustZone added gate area overhead | ~**3%** die size | Negligible cost | | Maximum SAU region address range | `0x00000000` – `0xFFFFFFFF` | Full 4 GB | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | Use Case | Why TrustZone? | |----------|----------|----------------| | **Smart Meters** | Tamper detection, OTA update verification | Protect crypto keys from physical probing. | | **Medical Wearables** | Patient health data | HIPAA-equivalent isolation of PHI. | | **Payment Terminals** | PIN entry, key storage | PCI PTS 6.x compliance. | | **Automotive ECUs** | V2X communication | Protect OEM firmware IP from after-market tuners. | | **IoT Sensors** | Cloud credentials | Prevent firmware dump via debug port. | # 3. Step-by-Step Implementation Walkthrough The implementation flow on STM32U575 is **strictly sequential** — getting any step out of order bricks the device (until next option-byte reset). ## 3.1 Step-by-Step: From Reset to a Running TrustZone Application ### **Step 1 — Verify Hardware and Option Bytes (OB)** Before any code is written, confirm in the **STM32CubeProgrammer** that: - `TZEN = 1` (TrustZone enabled) in `FLASH_OPTR`. - The device is in **Development Mode** (DBANK = 1, single-bank option not chosen). - `BOOT0` is configured for flash boot. > [!NOTE] > If `TZEN = 0`, the entire Cortex-M33 runs in **Secure** state with **no SAU visibility**. TrustZone is **irreversibly disabled** unless you perform a full option-byte reload. ### **Step 2 — Define the Memory Map (Plan, Then Code)** The KTU-practical canonical split is: | Region | Start Address | End Address | Size | Attribution | Owner | |--------|--------------|------------|------|-------------|-------| | Secure Flash (S) | `0x08000000` | `0x0801FFFF` | 128 KB | Secure | Secure bootloader + Secure services | | NSC Flash | `0x08020000` | `0x08020FFF` | 4 KB | Non-Secure Callable | Entry veneers | | Non-Secure Flash (NS) | `0x08040000` | `0x081FFFFF` | 1.75 MB | Non-Secure | Application | | Secure SRAM (S) | `0x20000000` | `0x2001FFFF` | 128 KB | Secure (alias at `0x30000000` for NS) | Secure heap/stack | | Shared SRAM | `0x20020000` | `0x2003FFFF` | 128 KB | Non-Secure | NS heap + shared buffers | | Peripherals (S) | `0x50000000` | `0x5FFFFFFF` | — | IDAU = Secure (default) | Crypto, RNG, Flash | | Peripherals (NS) | `0x40000000` | `0x4FFFFFFF` | — | IDAU = Non-Secure | GPIO, USART, ADC | ### **Step 3 — Initialize the SAU (8 Regions Available)** Each SAU region is configured by writing to: - `SAU_RNR` — Region Number Register (0–7). - `SAU_RBAR` — Region Base Address Register. - `SAU_RLAR` — Region Limit Address Register (also holds the enable bit and NSC flag). Pseudocode structure (register-level, C-style for clarity): ```c // Disable SAU globally while we configure SAU->CTRL &= ~SAU_CTRL_ENABLE_Msk; // Region 0: Mark NS Flash SAU->RNR = 0; SAU->RBAR = 0x08040000 & SAU_RBAR_BADDR_Msk; SAU->RLAR = (0x081FFFFF & SAU_RLAR_LADDR_Msk) | SAU_RLAR_ENABLE_Msk; // Region 1: Mark NSC Flash (NSC bit = bit 1 of RLAR) SAU->RNR = 1; SAU->RBAR = 0x08020000 & SAU_RBAR_BADDR_Msk; SAU->RLAR = (0x08020FFF & SAU_RLAR_LADDR_Msk) | SAU_RLAR_ENABLE_Msk | SAU_RLAR_NSC_Msk; // Region 2: Mark NS SRAM (alias at 0x30000000 via IDAU) // (Often left to IDAU; no SAU needed.) // Re-enable SAU SAU->CTRL |= SAU_CTRL_ENABLE_Msk; ``` ### **Step 4 — Configure the Secure / Non-Secure VTOR** After the SAU is live, you have **two vector tables**: | Register | Address | Purpose | |----------|---------|---------| | `SCB->VTOR` (Secure) | e.g., `0x08000000` | Secure vector table, used when in S state. | | `SCB_NS->VTOR` (Non-Secure) | e.g., `0x08040000` | Non-Secure vector table, used when in NS state. | The transition to the NS application is done by **secure code itself** — this is a security boundary; the NS app **cannot** promote itself to secure. ```c // Secure code, just before jumping to NS world SCB_NS->VTOR = 0x08040000; // NS vector table base __DSB(); __ISB(); // Ensure write completes // Configure NS Main Stack Pointer __TZ_set_MSP_NS(*((uint32_t *)0x08040000)); // First word = initial MSP_NS // Jump to NS Reset Handler (second word of NS VT) SecureJumpToNonSecure((func_t)(*((uint32_t *)(0x08040000 + 4U)))); ``` ### **Step 5 — Author the Non-Secure Callable (NSC) Veneer** The NSC region must contain only **Secure Gateway (SG)** entry points. An SG instruction is the **only** legal way for NS code to call into S code. ```c // File: secure_services.c — placed in NSC region via linker script __attribute__((cmse_nonsecure_entry)) uint32_t Secure_GetDeviceID(void) { return DBGMCU->IDCODE; // Read the unique device ID } // Equivalent assembly veneer (NSC region, first instruction MUST be SG): // 0x08020000: SG ; opcode 0xE97F // 0x08020002: B.W Secure_GetDeviceID // ; returns to NS world with BXNS ``` The `__attribute__((cmse_nonsecure_entry))` keyword instructs the Arm Compiler to: 1. Emit an `SG` instruction at the function start. 2. Mark the function entry as a branch target in the linker (so the linker enforces the NSC region). 3. Generate the proper secure-to-ns return code. ### **Step 6 — Configure the Memory Protection Unit (MPU) for Both Worlds** A common KTU 2024 exam question asks: **"Why is MPU needed when SAU exists?"** The answer: > **SAU answers: "Is this region S or NS?"** > **MPU answers: "Within that world, who can read/write/execute?"** Configure two MPUs: - **Secure MPU** — protects Secure RAM/Flash from rogue Secure code (e.g., a stack overflow in the secure bootloader). - **Non-Secure MPU** — protects NS Flash/SRAM from misbehaving NS tasks (typical RTOS sandboxing). ### **Step 7 — Validate the Configuration (Self-Test)** Always include a startup sanity check: ```c if (SAU->RLAR & SAU_RLAR_ENABLE_Msk) { // Count enabled regions uint32_t cnt = 0; for (int i = 0; i < 8; i++) { SAU->RNR = i; if (SAU->RLAR & SAU_RLAR_ENABLE_Msk) cnt++; } // Lock SAU if all OK SAU->CTRL |= SAU_CTRL_ALLNS_Msk; // Optional: mark all unmarked as NS } ``` If validation fails, stay in Secure state and blink an error LED. ### **Step 8 — Secure Boot Verification (Optional but Recommended)** For production, chain into a **Secure Boot** routine that: 1. Verifies the NS application's signature (ECDSA-P256). 2. Verifies the Secure services' signature. 3. Atomically flips to NS only on success. This forms the **Root of Trust**. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: SAU Region Math > **Problem (KTU Module 4 Typical):** Configure SAU Region 1 to cover the NSC flash from `0x08020000` to `0x08020FFF`. The RLAR encoding uses bits `0` for ENABLE, bit `1` for NSC, and bits `5..N` for LADDR. The region must be enabled and marked NSC. Compute the RLAR value if LADDR bit 4 is the lowest bit of the address field. **Solution (Exhaustive, step-by-step):** The base address (BADDR) is right-aligned and the limit address (LADDR) is also right-aligned but represents the **last** address of the region (inclusive). $$ \text{LADDR}_{5..N} = \text{Address} \gg 5 $$ For our NSC range: - **Start (BADDR)** = `0x08020000` - **End (LADDR)** = `0x08020FFF` Compute the LADDR field value (shifted right by 5): $$ \text{LADDR field} = \frac{0x08020FFF}{32} = \frac{0x08020FFF}{0x20} $$ $$ 0x08020FFF \div 0x20 = 0x00401007 \;\; (\text{decimal} = 4194311) $$ Now assemble the RLAR word: $$ \text{RLAR} = (\text{LADDR field} \ll 5) \;\vert\; (1 \ll 0) \;\vert\; (1 \ll 1) $$ $$ \text{RLAR} = (0x00401007 \times 0x20) \;\vert\; 0x01 \;\vert\; 0x02 $$ $$ \text{RLAR} = 0x08020FE0 \;\vert\; 0x03 = 0x08020FE3 $$ > **Result:** Write `0x08020FE3` into `SAU_RLAR` for Region 1. The base register `SAU_RBAR` holds `0x08020000` (or `0x08020000 & 0xFFFFFFE0` = `0x08020000`, already 32-byte aligned). The region is **enabled** (bit 0 = 1) and marked **NSC** (bit 1 = 1) — the only legal state for an NSC region. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Trap:** A common student mistake is to set the NSC bit on a region that is already mapped as `S` in IDAU. The result is a **SecureFault on the very first NS call**. Always cross-check the IDAU table in the reference manual for your specific STM32U5 die revision. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level TrustZone Architecture on STM32U575 ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CPU[Cortex-M33 Core] PC[Program Counter] LR[Link Register with Security Bit] ST[Security State Bit] end subgraph ATTR[Attribution Layer] IDAU[IDAU Fixed Hardware] SAU[SAU 8 Programmable Regions] MPU[MPU 16 Regions x2] end subgraph MEM[Memory Map 4GB] SECFLASH[Secure Flash 0x08000000] NSC[NSC Flash 0x08020000] NSFLASH[Non Secure Flash 0x08040000] SECSRAM[Secure SRAM 0x20000000] NSSRAM[Non Secure SRAM 0x20020000] PERIPHS[Peripherals 0x40000000] end subgraph FW[Firewall] BUS[AHB APB Bus Matrix] end PC --> ATTR ST --> ATTR ATTR --> BUS BUS --> SECFLASH BUS --> NSC BUS --> NSFLASH BUS --> SECSRAM BUS --> NSSRAM BUS --> PERIPHS LR -. sets .-> ST ``` ## 4.2 Memory Map Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FULLMAP[STM32U575 4GB Address Space] A0["0x00000000-0x1FFFFFFF
Code Alias"] A1["0x20000000-0x2001FFFF
Secure SRAM"] A2["0x20020000-0x2003FFFF
Non Secure SRAM"] A3["0x30000000-0x3001FFFF
NS Alias of Secure SRAM"] A4["0x40000000-0x4FFFFFFF
Non Secure Peripherals"] A5["0x50000000-0x5FFFFFFF
Secure Peripherals"] A6["0x60000000-0x9FFFFFFF
External Memory"] end F1["0x08000000-0x0801FFFF
Secure Flash"] -.SAU region 0.-> A0 F2["0x08020000-0x08020FFF
NSC Flash"] -.SAU region 1.-> A0 F3["0x08040000-0x081FFFFF
Non Secure Flash"] -.SAU region 2.-> A0 ``` ## 4.3 Boot and Transition Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB R[Reset Vector Cortex M33] --> SBL[Secure Bootloader] SBL --> SAUINIT[Initialize SAU 8 regions] SAUINIT --> CLKSET[Configure secure clocks and MPU] CLKSET --> VAL[Validate option bytes] VAL -->|Pass| JMP[Set MSP_NS and VTOR_NS] VAL -->|Fail| ERR[Stay in secure halt] JMP --> NSAPP[Non Secure Application] NSAPP -->|Calls NSC veneer| SG[SG instruction] SG -->|SecureFault if invalid| SF SG -->|Valid| SVC[Secure Service executes] SVC -->|BXNS return| NSAPP ``` ## 4.4 Secure vs Non-Secure Call Sequence ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant NS as Non-Secure App participant NSC as NSC Region Veneer participant S as Secure Service NS->>NSC: BL GetDeviceID NSC->>NSC: SG instruction NSC->>S: Branch to S function S->>S: Execute with S privileges S-->>NS: BXNS return value Note over NS,S: Any direct call to S address raises SecureFault ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **Q1. What is the role of the SAU in the STM32U575 TrustZone implementation? How many regions does it support?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Security Attribution Unit (SAU)** is a programmable hardware unit in the Cortex-M33 that decides whether a memory address is Secure, Non-Secure, or Non-Secure Callable. The STM32U575's SAU supports **8 programmable regions**. Each region is configured via the `SAU_RNR`, `SAU_RBAR`, and `SAU_RLAR` registers. The SAU works **in combination** with the fixed IDAU — if either marks a region as Secure, it is Secure. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for region count, 1 for IDAU interaction.)* **Q2. Explain the purpose of the SG (Secure Gateway) instruction in TrustZone-M.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Secure Gateway (SG)** instruction is a 16-bit Thumb-2 opcode (`0xE97F`) placed as the **first instruction** of every Non-Secure Callable (NSC) function entry. It is the **only legal mechanism** for Non-Secure code to enter the Secure world. When the CPU is in Non-Secure state and jumps to a function whose entry point is an SG instruction, the transition to Secure state is atomic. Without the SG, any direct branch into Secure memory raises a **SecureFault** exception. *(3 marks: 1 for opcode, 1 for NSC location, 1 for SecureFault consequence.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** ### **Question A: TrustZone Memory Map Configuration** **(a)** Draw the TrustZone memory map for an STM32U575 project with 128 KB Secure Flash, 4 KB NSC Flash, 1 MB Non-Secure Flash, 64 KB Secure SRAM, and 64 KB Non-Secure SRAM. Show the SAU region settings. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain step-by-step how the secure bootloader performs the transition to the Non-Secure application. List the exact registers and sequence of operations. **(7 marks)** ### **Question B: Secure Services and Inter-World Communication** **(a)** Write the C code (with appropriate attributes) for a Non-Secure Callable function `Secure_GetTemperature()` that returns an ADC reading from a Secure peripheral. Explain why the `__attribute__((cmse_nonsecure_entry))` keyword is essential. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe three security mechanisms enforced by TrustZone that protect against unauthorized memory access. Justify why TrustZone is considered a hardware root of trust. **(7 marks)** --- ### 5.2.1 Complete Model Solution for Question A #### **Part (a) — Memory Map + SAU Settings (7 marks)** | SAU Region | Start Address | End Address | Size | Attribute | Use | |-----------|---------------|-------------|------|-----------|-----| | 0 | `0x08000000` | `0x0801FFFF` | 128 KB | **Secure** | Secure firmware | | 1 | `0x08020000` | `0x08020FFF` | 4 KB | **NSC** | Entry veneers | | 2 | `0x08040000` | `0x0813FFFF` | 1 MB | **Non-Secure** | NS application | | 3 | `0x20000000` | `0x2000FFFF` | 64 KB | **Secure** | S stack/heap | | 4 | `0x20010000` | `0x2001FFFF` | 64 KB | **Non-Secure** | NS data | | 5–7 | — | — | — | Unused | Disabled | **Register Write Sequence (excerpt):** ```c SAU->CTRL = 0; // Disable SAU during config SAU->RNR = 0; SAU->RBAR = 0x08000000; SAU->RLAR = 0x0801FFFE | 1; // Enable, NSC=0 SAU->RNR = 1; SAU->RBAR = 0x08020000; SAU->RLAR = 0x08020FFE | 1 | 2; // Enable + NSC SAU->RNR = 2; SAU->RBAR = 0x08040000; SAU->RLAR = 0x0813FFFE | 1; // Enable, NSC=0 SAU->CTRL = 1; // Re-enable SAU ``` *(Marks: 3 for correct table, 2 for correct addresses, 2 for correct SAU code.)* #### **Part (b) — Secure-to-NS Transition (7 marks)** **Step 1 — Configure the Non-Secure Vector Table:** The NS app has its own vector table starting at, say, `0x08040000`. The first word is the initial MSP_NS, the second word is the Reset_Handler_NS address. **Step 2 — Set the NS stack pointer:** ```c __TZ_set_MSP_NS(*(uint32_t *)0x08040000); ``` **Step 3 — Set the NS VTOR:** ```c SCB_NS->VTOR = 0x08040000; ``` **Step 4 — Memory barrier:** ```c __DSB(); __ISB(); ``` **Step 5 — Branch to NS reset handler:** ```c typedef void (*nsfunc_t)(void); nsfunc_t ns_reset = (nsfunc_t)(*(uint32_t *)(0x08040000 + 4)); ns_reset(); ``` The CPU atomically switches state to Non-Secure, fetches the NS vector table, and begins execution. The transition is **one-way** until the NS app explicitly calls back via an NSC function. *(Marks: 1 each for steps 1–5, 2 for explaining atomicity and one-way nature.)* --- ### 5.2.2 Complete Model Solution for Question B #### **Part (a) — C Code for NSC Function (7 marks)** ```c #include "stm32u575xx.h" __attribute__((cmse_nonsecure_entry)) int32_t Secure_GetTemperature(void) { // Enable ADC clock (Secure peripheral) RCC->AHB2ENR |= RCC_AHB2ENR_ADC1EN; // Configure ADC1 in Secure state ADC1->CR &= ~ADC_CR_ADEN; ADC1->CR |= ADC_CR_ADVREGEN; for (volatile int i = 0; i < 1000; i++); // Wait for regulator ADC1->CR |= ADC_CR_ADCAL; while (ADC1->CR & ADC_CR_ADCAL); ADC1->CR |= ADC_CR_ADEN; while (!(ADC1->ISR & ADC_ISR_ADRDY)); ADC1->CR |= ADC_CR_ADSTART; while (!(ADC1->ISR & ADC_ISR_EOC)); return (int32_t)ADC1->DR; } ``` **Why `cmse_nonsecure_entry` is essential (3 marks):** 1. It tells the compiler to emit the `SG` instruction as the function prologue. 2. It marks the function entry in the linker map as a **callable veneer** — the linker will reject the build if the function is not placed in the NSC region. 3. It generates a `BXNS`-compatible return sequence, restoring the LR security bit correctly. *(Marks: 4 for the C code, 3 for the three justifications.)* #### **Part (b) — Three Security Mechanisms (7 marks)** 1. **Memory Attribution via SAU + IDAU** — Every bus cycle evaluates the security state of the initiator and the target. A Non-Secure initiator cannot read Secure memory. **(2 marks)** 2. **Non-Secure Callable (NSC) Regions with SG Instructions** — A single, auditable, narrow entry point into the Secure world. The Secure side can validate every parameter before acting, preventing the entire Secure attack surface from being exposed. **(2 marks)** 3. **Secure Fault Handling** — Any unauthorized access (e.g., NS code attempting to read Secure memory) triggers a **SecureFault** exception, which is handled **only in Secure state**. The NS code cannot catch, suppress, or skip the fault. **(2 marks)** 4. **Bonus: Option-byte lock + secure boot verification** — Once the TrustZone option bits are set, they cannot be modified without a full chip erase, providing a **Hardware Root of Trust** for the entire system. **(1 mark)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing TrustZone-M (Cortex-M) with TrustZone-A (Cortex-A).** They are **fundamentally different**. TZ-M uses SAU/IDAU and SG instructions; TZ-A uses NS bit on bus and a separate Secure Monitor. Writing TZ-A concepts in a TZ-M answer is a 2–3 mark penalty. > 2. **Forgetting the IDAU interaction.** The SAU alone is incomplete. If the IDAU marks `0x50000000` peripherals as Secure, the SAU cannot make them Non-Secure. Many students ignore this and lose 1–2 marks. > 3. **Misplacing the NSC region.** Putting the SG function in a Secure region (not NSC) causes the build to fail or the linker to warn. The linker script must explicitly place the NSC section between Secure and Non-Secure flash. > 4. **Confusing MPU with TrustZone.** The MPU is **per-world**, and does **not** enforce security attribution. Mixing up MPU and SAU roles in the answer is a 2-mark deduction. > 5. **Forgetting the `__DSB(); __ISB();` barriers** after configuring VTOR and SAU. The CPU may continue executing with stale attribution, leading to a SecureFault on the very first instruction. Examiners specifically look for this. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Points (memorize before the exam):** - **TrustZone-M ≠ TrustZone-A.** Cortex-M33 uses **SAU + IDAU + SG**, not the Secure Monitor of Cortex-A. - **STM32U575 has 8 SAU regions**, 32-byte minimum granularity. - **Three valid attributes:** Secure (S), Non-Secure (NS), Non-Secure Callable (NSC). - **The "AND rule":** Final attribution = IDAU ∧ SAU. IDAU wins on conflict. - **The SG instruction** is the **only** legal entry from NS to S; opcode is **`0xE97F`**, 16-bit Thumb-2, **must be the first instruction** of an NSC function. - **Two vector tables** exist after TrustZone is enabled: `SCB->VTOR` (Secure) and `SCB_NS->VTOR` (Non-Secure). Both must be initialized by Secure code. - **NS code cannot promote itself to Secure** under any circumstance — the transition must originate from S side (typically the secure bootloader) or via an SG call into NSC. - **SecureFault is raised** for any violation — handled **only in Secure state**. - **TrustZone is a hardware root of trust** because the option bytes that enable it (`TZEN = 1` in `FLASH_OPTR`) cannot be modified without a chip erase. - **The three security pillars are layered:** SAU/IDAU (attribution) + MPU (access control) + NSC/SG (controlled entry). - **MPU does NOT enforce security attribution** — it only filters access within the current world. - **The Cortex-M33 boots in the Secure state by default** — TrustZone never starts in Non-Secure mode. - **Reset behavior of TZ-enabled devices:** Secure-only RAM and flash are always accessible to S state; NS alias is gated by IDAU. - **Code on STM32U575** uses the `__attribute__((cmse_nonsecure_entry))` keyword — the linker + compiler combo enforces the NSC placement automatically. - **Avoid `|` (single pipe) in KTU exam math** — use `\vert` or `\mid` in any LaTeX. Examiners will mark answers using vertical bars in math expressions as confusing. - **Practical exam tip:** Always include `__DSB(); __ISB();` after SAU, MPU, and VTOR configuration — this is a frequently tested detail. - **One-way transition from S to NS** — once in NS, you cannot return to S without a `BXNS` from an SG-marked entry function. - **Secure SRAM alias** is at `0x30000000` for NS-side access (IDAU-mapped) — useful for shared buffers. - **On STM32U575, the OTFDEC and Secure Storage Block (SSB)** are *always* Secure — even with full SAU, you cannot expose them to NS.

topics.title: Advanced Debugging and Optimization: Code and Memory Optimization Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCST504 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Advanced Debugging and Optimization: Code and Memory Optimization Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Code Optimization** is the process of modifying a software system to make some aspect of it work more efficiently or use fewer resources. In the context of microcontrollers, optimization primarily targets three competing goals: **execution speed** (latency), **code density** (Flash footprint), and **power consumption** (energy per instruction).

**Memory Optimization** refers to the systematic arrangement, reduction, and strategic placement of data and instructions within the limited **Harvard architecture** memory spaces (Program Flash, SRAM, and EEPROM/FRAM) of an embedded microcontroller to minimize wastage, prevent stack/heap collisions, and ensure deterministic real-time behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 8051/ARM/AVR microcontrollers (covered in this course) have highly constrained memory (e.g., 8051: 4 KB ROM, 128 B RAM). Optimization is not a luxury but a **mandatory engineering requirement** to fit functionality into silicon constraints.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Travel Backpack

Imagine you are packing a small backpack for a 7-day mountain trek. You have limited space (memory) and a weight limit (Flash/ROM budget). 

- **Code Optimization** is like rolling your clothes tightly and removing the unnecessary book — you still have a t-shirt, but it occupies less space.
- **Memory Optimization** is like deciding what goes in the *top compartment* (frequently accessed items) and what goes in the *bottom* (rarely used items). You do not waste the limited inner pockets.
- If you pack poorly, your backpack bursts at the zipper (stack overflow) or you cannot fit the tent (program won't link).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Optimization Trade-off Triangle:** Faster code ↔ Smaller code ↔ Lower power. You usually cannot maximize all three simultaneously. The KTU examiner expects you to identify which trade-off applies in a given scenario (e.g., battery-powered IoT node → prioritize power; hard real-time control loop → prioritize speed).

### 1.3 The Three Optimization Axes in Microcontrollers

| Axis | Target Resource | Typical Metric |
|------|-----------------|----------------|
| **Speed** | CPU cycles / latency | Execution time in $\mu s$ |
| **Space** | Program Flash + SRAM | Bytes used |
| **Energy** | Dynamic + static power | $\mu J$ per operation |

Explicit KTU metric: **MIPS** (Million Instructions Per Second) and **Code Density** in **bytes per instruction** are the standard evaluation yardsticks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Optimization Pipeline (Structured Logic)

The optimization process in an embedded project follows a strict 5-step engineering pipeline:

1. **Profiling** — Measure first. Use tools like Keil µVision Analyzer, IAR C-SPY, or hardware trace (ETM/SWD) to find the *hotspots* (the 10% of code consuming 90% of cycles — the **Pareto Principle**).
2. **Source-Level Optimization** — Algorithmic restructuring: replace `for` loops with `while` decrement loops, use lookup tables, prefer `unsigned char` over `int` for loop counters.
3. **Compiler-Directed Optimization** — Select the appropriate `-O` level in GCC (`-O0` none, `-O1` basic, `-O2` recommended, `-O3` aggressive, `-Os` size-optimized).
4. **Assembly-Level Tuning** — Hand-craft critical ISRs (Interrupt Service Routines) in assembly, using register-specific instructions like `RR A` (rotate accumulator) instead of multiply/divide.
5. **Memory Layout Optimization** — Place time-critical code in internal Flash, frequently accessed data in internal SRAM, use `code`/`xdata`/`idata` qualifiers wisely (8051-specific).

### 2.2 Code Optimization Techniques (Deep Dive)

> [!NOTE]
> **A. Loop Optimization** — Highest yield technique. Tight inner loops dominate execution time in 8051/ARM.

- **Loop Unrolling:** Manually expand a small loop to eliminate the branch penalty.
- **Loop Invariant Code Motion (LICM):** Hoist constant expressions out of the loop body.
- **Strength Reduction:** Replace expensive operations with cheaper equivalents. `x * 2` $\rightarrow$ `x << 1`; `x / 4` $\rightarrow$ `x >> 2`.
- **Loop Fusion/Jamming:** Combine two adjacent loops iterating over the same range to reduce loop overhead.

> [!NOTE]
> **B. Function-Level Optimization**

- **Function Inlining:** Replace a small function call with its body. Saves CALL/RET stack overhead (8051: CALL = 2 bytes + 2 cycles; RET = 1 byte + 2 cycles).
- **Tail Call Optimization (TCO):** Reuse the current stack frame for a recursive call at the end of a function.
- **Dead Code Elimination (DCE):** Compiler removes unreachable code or writes to variables that are never read.
