### 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — HOME AUTOMATION FOCUS**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**  

**(a)** Design a **block diagram** of a microcontroller-based home automation system that can control **4 appliances** with both **automatic (sensor-based)** and **manual (Wi-Fi app-based)** modes. Identify and justify the choice of **5 peripherals** with their interface type. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the **firmware logic** in pseudo-code for the automatic lighting control mode, which must:
- Turn ON a light when motion is detected **AND** ambient light is below threshold.
- Turn OFF the light if no motion is detected for **30 seconds**.
- Display status on 16×2 LCD via I²C. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

**(a) Block Diagram + Peripheral Justification (7 marks):**

```
   ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
   │  [Smartphone Wi-Fi App]                 │
   │            ↓ UART                       │
   │  [ESP8266 NodeMCU]                      │
   │            ↓ UART                       │
   │  ┌─────────────────────┐                │
   │  │  STM32F103C8T6 MCU  │                │
   │  └─────────────────────┘                │
   │   ↓ ↑    ↓ ↑    ↓ ↑    ↓     ↓ I²C       │
   │  PIR   LDR   BTN   RELAY×4   LCD         │
   └─────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

| Peripheral | Interface | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **STM32F103C8T6** | Core MCU | 32-bit ARM Cortex-M3, plenty of GPIO/ADC/TIM, low cost (~₹180), KTU curriculum-aligned |
| **PIR HC-SR501** | GPIO + EXTI | Detects human motion up to 7 m; digital output; trigger on rising edge via EXTI |
| **LDR + 10 kΩ** | ADC1_CH0 | Voltage divider → ADC reads light intensity; threshold-based logic |
| **4-Channel Relay** | 4× GPIO | Switches 230 V AC appliances; optoisolated for safety |
| **ESP8266 (NodeMCU)** | UART (115200 baud) | Provides Wi-Fi connectivity; AT command firmware for app control |
| **16×2 LCD + PCF8574** | I²C1 (SCL=PB6, SDA=PB7) | Reduces GPIO usage from 6 data pins to 2 |

**Awarding (7 marks):** 2 marks for block diagram, 5 marks × 1 each for justified peripherals.

---

**(b) Pseudo-code Solution (7 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{\#include "stm32f1xx\_hal.h"} \\
&\text{\#define LIGHT\_PIN GPIO\_PIN\_0, GPIOB} \\
&\text{\#define PIR\_PIN GPIO\_PIN\_1, GPIOB} \\
&\text{\#define LDR\_CHANNEL ADC\_CHANNEL\_0} \\
&\text{\#define MOTION\_TIMEOUT 30000 \text{ ms}} \\
\\
&\text{volatile uint32\_t last\_motion\_time = 0;} \\
&\text{volatile uint8\_t motion\_flag = 0;} \\
\\
&\text{void HAL\_GPIO\_EXTI\_Callback(uint16\_t GPIO\_Pin)\{} \\
&\quad \text{if(GPIO\_Pin == PIR\_PIN)\{} \\
&\quad\quad \text{motion\_flag = 1;} \\
&\quad\quad \text{last\_motion\_time = HAL\_GetTick();} \\
&\quad \}\\
&\}\\
\\
&\text{void Auto\_Lighting\_Task(void)\{} \\
&\quad \text{uint16\_t ldr\_val = HAL\_ADC\_GetValue(\&hadc1);} \\
&\quad \text{uint32\_t now = HAL\_GetTick();} \\
\\
&\quad \text{if(motion\_flag \&\& ldr\_val < LDR\_THRESHOLD)\{} \\
&\quad\quad \text{HAL\_GPIO\_WritePin(LIGHT\_PORT, LIGHT\_PIN, GPIO\_PIN\_SET);} \\
&\quad\quad \text{lcd\_print("LIGHT: ON ");} \\
&\quad\quad \text{motion\_flag = 0;} \\
&\quad \}\\
\\
&\quad \text{if((now - last\_motion\_time) > MOTION\_TIMEOUT)\{} \\
&\quad\quad \text{HAL\_GPIO\_WritePin(LIGHT\_PORT, LIGHT\_PIN, GPIO\_PIN\_RESET);} \\
&\quad\quad \text{lcd\_print("LIGHT: OFF");} \\
&\quad \}\\
\\
&\quad \text{HAL\_Delay(100);} \\
&\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of Key Steps (2 marks):**
- `HAL_GPIO_EXTI_Callback` resets the 30-second timer every time motion is detected.
- `Auto_Lighting_Task` runs in the main loop and uses `HAL_GetTick()` for non-blocking timing.
- LDR threshold read via ADC; if light is already bright, motion alone won't switch ON.
- Status string written to LCD via I²C using `lcd_print()`.

**Awarding (7 marks):** 3 marks for logic correctness, 2 marks for register/function accuracy, 2 marks for timeout + LCD integration.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — SECURITY SYSTEM FOCUS (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)**

**(a)** Identify and explain **five real-world threats** that a small home/office security system must address. For each threat, specify **one sensor** with its operating principle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the **firmware flow** (as a state diagram + pseudo-code) for an alert-cascade that classifies threat severity into **4 levels** (Idle, Warning, Alarm, Critical) and triggers corresponding actions. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

**(a) Threat-Sensor Matrix (7 marks):**

| # | Real-World Threat | Sensor | Operating Principle |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Unauthorized door/window entry | **Magnetic reed switch** | Two ferromagnetic reeds close when magnet (on door) is within 1 cm → digital LOW → HIGH when door opens |
| 2 | Intruder walking in room | **PIR (Passive Infrared)** | Pyroelectric sensor detects change in IR radiation (≈ 10 µm, human body) |
| 3 | LPG/CNG gas leak | **MQ-2 sensor** | SnO₂ semiconductor; resistance drops when gas concentration rises; analog output proportional to ppm |
| 4 | Fire / sudden heat | **LM35 + thermistor** | LM35 outputs 10 mV/°C linearly; thermistor resistance drops with temperature |
| 5 | Glass break / wall impact | **Piezoelectric shock sensor** | Piezo element generates voltage spike when vibrated; analog → comparator → digital |
| 6 (bonus) | System power cut (sabotage) | **Voltage divider on Vcc** | ADC reads Vcc; sudden drop below threshold triggers tamper alert |

**Awarding:** 1 mark per threat–sensor pair (×5 = 5 marks), 2 marks for correctly stating operating principles.

---

**(b) State Diagram + Pseudo-code (7 marks):**

**State Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Level 0 Idle] -->|1 trigger| B[Level 1 Warning]
    A -->|2+ triggers| C[Level 2 Alarm]
    A -->|Gas or Fire| D[Level 3 Critical]
    B -->|escalation| C
    C -->|escalation| D
    D -->|reset by user PIN| A
    C -->|reset| A
    B -->|timeout 10s no further trigger| A
```

**Pseudo-code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{typedef enum \{IDLE, WARNING, ALARM, CRITICAL\} AlertLevel\_t;} \\
&\text{AlertLevel\_t current\_state = IDLE;} \\
&\text{uint8\_t trigger\_count = 0;} \\
\\
&\text{void Security\_State\_Machine(void)\{} \\
&\quad \text{trigger\_count = count\_active\_triggers();} \\
\\
&\quad \text{switch(current\_state)\{} \\
&\quad\quad \text{case IDLE:} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{if(trigger\_count >= 1) current\_state = WARNING;} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{if(is\_gas\_or\_fire()) current\_state = CRITICAL;} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{buzzer\_off(); led\_green\_on();} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{break;} \\
\\
&\quad\quad \text{case WARNING:} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{buzzer\_beep(1, 1); led\_yellow\_blink();} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{lcd\_print("WARNING");} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{if(trigger\_count >= 2) current\_state = ALARM;} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{break;} \\
\\
&\quad\quad \text{case ALARM:} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{buzzer\_continuous(); led\_red\_blink();} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{siren\_on();} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{lcd\_print("ALARM!!");} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{log\_to\_eeprom("ALARM");} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{if(is\_gas\_or\_fire()) current\_state = CRITICAL;} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{break;} \\
\\
&\quad\quad \text{case CRITICAL:} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{gsm\_send\_sms(OWNER\_NUM, "CRITICAL ALERT!");} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{gsm\_call(POLICE\_NUM);} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{siren\_on(); relay\_on(LIGHTS);} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{lcd\_print("EVACUATE");} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{break;} \\
\\
&\quad\quad \text{default: current\_state = IDLE;} \\
&\quad \}\\
&\quad \text{if(check\_user\_pin()) current\_state = IDLE;} \\
&\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Awarding (7 marks):** 3 marks state diagram, 3 marks pseudo-code correctness, 1 mark for GSM integration logic.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Project-Based Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting interface type:** When listing peripherals, students write *“PIR sensor”* but **omit** whether it is GPIO / EXTI / ADC. KTU examiners award **0 marks** for the peripheral if interface is missing. Always specify: `PIR → GPIO with EXTI (rising-edge trigger)`.
>
> 2. **No power budget:** Students often describe a security system with GSM but **fail to compute the 2 A peak current**. Examiners specifically look for the **$I_{peak}$ calculation** to test your understanding of real-world design.
>
> 3. **Polling instead of interrupts:** In security systems, polling the PIR every 100 ms wastes CPU and power. Always use **EXTI (external interrupts)** for event-driven sensors. Writing polling logic loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Missing non-functional requirements:** Don’t write only *“system should detect motion.”* Quantify: *“detect motion within 200 ms at a range of 5 m.”* This is a 2024 NEP-aligned marking rubric expectation.
>
> 5. **No state diagram for alarm cascade:** If the question asks for *“alert cascade”*, drawing just a flowchart is **insufficient**. You must provide an explicit **state diagram (IDLE → WARNING → ALARM → CRITICAL)** with transition conditions.
>
> 6. **Confusing ADC channels:** STM32F103 has only **ADC1 and ADC2**, with 10 external channels each. Students write `ADC_Channel_15` on ADC1 — wrong. Always verify the channel-to-pin mapping in the datasheet.
>
> 7. **No safety note for 230 V AC:** Any project involving relays MUST mention **optocoupler isolation, fuse, and proper earthing**. Examiners check for this safety awareness in home automation questions.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 Project Topics**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ A microcontroller project = **MCU + Peripherals + Firmware + Power supply + Communication**.
- ☐ Real-world problems are translated into **functional (FR)** and **non-functional (NFR)** requirements.
- ☐ Peripheral selection is driven by **interface type, voltage compatibility, and accuracy**.

**Home Automation Project:**
- ☐ Sensors: PIR, LDR, DHT11.
- ☐ Actuators: Relays (×4), motors.
- ☐ Communication: ESP8266 (Wi-Fi) via UART, or Bluetooth HC-05.
- ☐ HMI: 16×2 LCD + push buttons.
- ☐ Key formula: $V_{LDR}$ from voltage divider → ADC.

**Security System Project:**
- ☐ Threat model = door, motion, gas, fire, shock.
- ☐ Sensors: Reed switch, PIR, MQ-2, LM35, Piezo.
- ☐ Alert cascade = 4 levels (Idle, Warning, Alarm, Critical).
- ☐ GSM (SIM800) for SMS/call — remember **2 A peak current**.
- ☐ Power budget: $I_{peak} = I_{MCU} + I_{Sensors} + I_{GSM\,peak}$.
- ☐ Use **EXTI** for event-driven sensors, not polling.

**Voice Response Project:**
- ☐ Voice storage IC: **APR33A3 (SPI)** or **DFPlayer Mini (UART)**.
- ☐ Audio amplifier: **PAM8403** (Class-D, 3 W + 3 W stereo).
- ☐ SPI Mode 0 for APR33A3, max clock 1 MHz.
- ☐ Use **pre-recorded WAV at 8 kHz / 16-bit** for clarity.
- ☐ Address accessibility (visually impaired) and elderly users.

**Universal Engineering Practices:**
- ☐ Always draw a **block diagram before writing code**.
- ☐ Use **modular C** (separate .c/.h for each peripheral).
- ☐ Apply **state machines** for multi-condition logic.
- ☐ Compute **power budget** ($I_{avg}, I_{peak}$) explicitly.
- ☐ Include **safety isolation** (optocouplers, fuses) for mains-voltage projects.
- ☐ Document **test cases, expected vs actual results** in the project report.

**KTU 2024 Exam-Specific Reminders:**
- ☐ For 7-mark sub-questions, **always include a diagram** (1–2 marks reserved).
- ☐ For 14-mark questions, address both **hardware AND software** aspects.
- ☐ **Quantify every requirement** — vague answers lose marks.
- ☐ **Cite the peripheral interface** (I²C/SPI/UART/ADC/GPIO) — this is a 2024 scheme emphasis.
